label: anti-marriage
translation:
When I behold the bright rays,  
That fall from my Love's eye,  
I am again pierced by Love,  
Bacchus soon flees from my eyes,  
My Philis has even greater power,  
Each has their taste; this is my preference.  
Away with drunks, wiser than beasts,  
Yes, worse than the hellish spirits,  
You swore off drink immediately,  
Did you know how I wooed my Philis,  
While I rested on her bosom,  
And surrendered my love to her.  
I want to love my Philis forever,  
She who is mistress of my senses,  
And let another with the wine:  
When I am then drunk with love,  
By all the sweetness she offers,  
And I say, Dearest, your sweet countenance.  
When I then hear my Philis sing,  
Then I call cowardly young men,  
I love reason far above beauty,  
Reason will always last forever,  
Beauty lasts but a short hour—  
Time plainly shows this to us.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
When I behold the bright rays  
That from the bottle fall into the glass,  
I am soaked by Bacchus’ drink,  
Away Venus, away with your lustful eyes,  
My Bacchus has much more power  
When he sprinkles me with his drink.  
Away Venus with your lustful glances,  
I prefer to get drunk daily,  
And I swear to you immediately,  
If you knew how I kissed my Bacchus  
When I lay to rest on his barrel,  
And he gave his drink at its best.  
I want to love my Bacchus forever  
And banish Venus from my thoughts,  
I prefer to stick with the wine:  
When I am drunk from his cup,  
Then I quietly go to bed and snore,  
And wait again for the sunshine.  
Then I hear Bacchus sing again,  
Away Venus heroes, young men,  
Beware of Venus, and love my drink  
For know it will last forever,  
And Venus lasts for too little time,  
Therefore avoid her for Bacchus’ sake.  
Girl, may I once please you,  
Even to clean your chicken coop,  
Even with a broom of a single sound:  
No, no, you will not have me like that,  
That you will fill my chicken coop,  
Even if your broom were six feet long.  
Farewell daughters young of years,  
Do not want to mate in the chicken coop,  
For you will fall into great sorrow,  
Just as May went to play,  
Hup came in mourning with the dogs,  
Because she let them kiss her too much.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
I side with Wine,  
And laughed at the Game of Love,  
I side with Wine,  
Love that causes us pain,  
Tell me, what does Loyalty give?  
Nothing but worries:  
I prefer to remain a free Bachelor,  
Loyalty breeds torment.  
There comes a child, two, three,  
Daddy has to provide,  
There comes a child, two, three,  
Oh, what a burden!  
It’s a man, hey, pull the cradle:  
Our little Jan is crying,  
How the little guy weeps,  
He’s tearing out his eyes.  
The chamber pot over the house,  
The head is about to burst,  
The chamber pot over the house,  
There is great turmoil,  
Mama is in it,  
Like a dirty rag,  
The baby in the pram,  
Food for the lamb.  
I still say love  
Does not brew like foolish whims,  
I still say love  
Doesn’t fall like disasters,  
If it goes well, it’s just well,  
If once badly, it turns out,  
Then the brain is robbed;  
Alas! one loses one’s head.  
Happy then, when  
Papa goes to the East Indies;  
Happy then, when  
He attains such an honor,  
There sits Mama, gazing,  
Like Stephen towards Heaven,  
Thus love brews the sweet,  
With bitter gall and soot.  
Who is still crazy?  
And seeks to marry?  
Who is still crazy?  
To such a sad fate?  
As long as I live,  
I will cherish the Free State,  
Once again the Free State,  
That surpasses all.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
What use is it to lie with another,  
Is it so lovely and so sweet?  
And to take a breath from one another?  
I love the only one to lie with.  
I give no status or noble descent,  
For the offense of a bare foot:  
I cherish money and gold hems:  
I love the only one to lie with.  
I am assured that all my knowledge  
Faithfully remains in my mind;  
That no one speaks of my tears  
I love the only one to lie with.  
By day I know how to increase my status,  
Greeted well by all who see me,  
And reveal myself unbothered:  
I love the only one to lie with.  
If I find pleasure in yells and whistles,  
I never ask for a maiden’s procession;  
Whether I go hunting or sailing outside,  
I love the only one to lie with.  
If before my eyes come beautiful maidens,  
I think I always see you well;  
But still, I keep the freedom hedge:  
I love the only one to lie with.  
I am hindered by no dream  
That raves suddenly or rages wildly;  
No eager will diminishes my success:  
I love the only one to lie with.  
If I am cold, I let myself be covered,  
That I am as warm as a glow;  
If I want to get up early, I let myself be woken;  
I love the only one to lie with.  
If I once visit my friend,  
No one says, go in haste;  
I see no ghost with children’s cloths:  
I love the only one to lie with.  
I choose freedom here on earth,  
From freedom deprived is bitter soot,  
And I hold freedom in high regard:  
I love the only one to lie with.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Oh roads! Full of dangers and disaster,
Where we often embrace a deadly calamity;
At the service of vain illusion, born from a wind,
That is called love, but lovelessly blinds the eye.
Thus one misses, where one strives the most,
And as long as one keeps running in a fearful night,
One gains nothing but worry, sorrow, and discomfort,
Then the earth grants its tears, but virtue shares pearls.

Oh foolishness always seeks with Narcissus,
That which, although charming, is nothing but a shadow.
Sail freely with him your desire, and feed your zealous nose,
You seek a joyful life, but find a sad death.

As soon as you have reached your goal according to desire,
And you think you will rightly know what has disturbed you,
Reason returns again to folly as before,
Breeding hatred, killing love, and marring joy and glory.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Go away, go away, I laugh at marriage,  
I shun it like death,  
I choose wine over women,  
Then I escape distress.  
For every day and night,  
A wife with her stubborn head,  
Constantly to wait on,  
I give the nun the boot,  
That's certain, that's certain.

If I marry a sweet angel,  
Then I have the sorrow,  
That a gallant rascal  
Pipes through my reed,  
You cannot escape the horns  
With angel wings,  
If I marry an ugly fox  
I cannot even look at her  
Away, away, away, away.

If I marry a wife with plates (discs, maybe inflexible rules),  
Then I have the reproach,  
Go away, go away, what do you want to say,  
You stay where you are.  
And if the wife has no slices (parts, perhaps attributes),  
Then it is not good either,  
Who can stay unmarried,  
Is crazy, if he does not do it,  
That's certain, that's certain.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Although all is sweet in love,
That love's desire promises.
Yet I do not want to begin it,
It is not believed by me.
Yet I do not want to begin it,
Not begin.

I love generous freedom,
And own sovereignty
Shuts out the eager desires:
Decides my wish within me.
Shuts out the eager desires,
Eager desires.

The servants may talk,
Her talk I do not understand,
Her flattery I do not value,
Her actions I do not observe.
Her flattery I do not value,
I do not value.

If they promise with folly,
Her faith she does not bind,
Her love is but words,
Her words win nothing.
Her love is but words,
Are words.

Love is truly madness,
An imagined whim.
A child of lust and emptiness,
A playful voluptuous will.
A child of lust and emptiness,
Lust and emptiness.

As long as reason
Rules the brain,
Never in my young heart
Will that guest be welcome.
Never in my young heart,
My young heart.

For why would I wish,
That if all goes best,
I make myself of loose commands,
I bind myself to a marriage block.
I make myself loosely bound,
Yes, bound.

So let me safely flee,
The nickname of a woman,
Bought with brief pleasures,
Paid with long regret.
Bought with brief pleasures,
Brief pleasures.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays:  
For they keep for pleasure,  
Yes to say; no to mean:  
Each one suffers bruises,  
When one hopes for marital joy.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

2. Who would wish for the girl  
Whose gall is concealed  
In the sweetness of words;  
Whose learned glances  
Received the heart with love;  
But poison the blood.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

3. For as soon as you are married  
The spirited wife grows bold.  
She takes over the household management.  
She is Mistress of the keys:  
That you foolishly stay out late,  
She is glad because of this release.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

4. When you see good friends  
There is wine, but not for you,  
The wife holds the keys fast,  
You may curse, you may jump,  
She goes to sit singing for you,  
And invites neighbor Smul as guest.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

5. She tells how everything goes there,  
And how bad the crown looks on you.  
Well, says Smeersmul, well, one still has  
A comforting little Barneveld  
With our Teetje, for the money  
He spends on the wine.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

6. When the wife wants to go out  
You are left with the child;  
Whether it suits you well or poorly.  
You must sing sujo, sujo child  
Because someone wants to drink a pint elsewhere  
In the evening.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

7. Therefore, young men, never be so foolish  
And exchange your liberty to your mockery,  
Marry for your freedom.  
The carefree life of a suitor  
Will never be given back to you,  
Once you are lost to the wife.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.  

8. But one finds (do you still like marriage)  
Here and there a good woman,  
Of good morals, love, and virtue.  
If you manage to get such a woman,  
Otherwise, it would be better to remain silent  
And quietly bear your displeasure.  
There are so few pious men,  
Even fewer women nowadays.

label: anti-marriage
translation: No one approaches Red Eye love to serve her: Everyone tries to flee from her like the plague. No one lends her their eyes. That is the reason she cries.

label: anti-marriage
translation: My husband has gone out today. Come girl, immediately, Go around now And tell it to my friends. Well then, I wave the broom. Now his money is being spent by me I always have to sit - in the mood. And that bores me. Now I am frank and free.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Living alone is my purpose, marriage cannot please me,  
I live like a priestess; I nourish my soul with heavenly love.  
I avoid what can disturb my peace. Marriage cannot please me.

label: anti-marriage
translation: Sweet little darling, what nonsense. Have you really thought this through? A lonely life is not bad, a lonely life is not bad; but a well-married state surpasses the nuns. Have you really thought this through? have you really thought this through?

label: anti-marriage
translation: The human heart long spoiled, Since hatred was its dwelling place. For love, by them as if dead, Thus it descends with hatred into the grave. When people themselves drive me away. Whether I treat them unfairly, And whether they complain about it, Their favor shows itself to me again.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
Cupid, stay off my body!  
I am not driven away before my time.  
I am too pious and chaste;  
I have no desire  
To love,  
I am too pious and chaste,  
Knock freely on another door.

2.  
Where the god of love commands,  
One sees Minerva flee,  
And where one loves passionately,  
One sees things  
Escaping us,  
And where one loves passionately,  
One sees that time vanishes.

3.  
What is the maidenly sex?  
Something at which wisdom laughs.  
No. I do not like those apples,  
They bring death,  
They spoil,  
No. I do not like those apples,  
Full of bitterness and sorrow.

4.  
What is, spoken straight out,  
Love other than vanity?  
And if your love is disturbed,  
By a worthless one,  
By an evil one,  
And if your love is disturbed,  
Then just think once about how it ends.

5.  
You get horns on your head,  
And your pretty money runs out,  
I give love the sack,  
I rather let my desires  
Rest.  
I give love the sack,  
Love is too heavy a burden.

6.  
Away then love! Wisdom lets  
You be embraced. The god of love goes!  
I kiss Themis’s mouth  
I find there things  
That amuse  
I kiss Themis’s mouth,  
That is pleasant and healthy.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Away, away! Cupid with your glances!  
I do not value your power or arrows,  
You will never loosen my heart,  
Nor pull it under your command.  

Go away, rascal! You will not win!  
All that you attempt is but wind;  
Go, go; I do not love to love,  
I am simply not inclined to it.  

Should I submit myself under the yoke,  
And offer freedom as prey,  
Oh no, you will get me from your life  
But not into that gilded cage.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
Young men, if you intend to court,  
Do not let the girls deceive you,  
For they cause your heart and blood to wane,  
And your money, and your money,  
All to completely disappear.  

2.  
There is nothing to gain from the girls,  
If you go courting, you must pay for her,  
And while she soothes her throat,  
Thus you must, thus you must,  
Honor her with bare head.  

3.  
If you then marry one or the other,  
Well, then your misfortune is made:  
For by day she will come to bother you,  
And by night, and by night,  
Drive away your sweet rest.  

4.  
If you cannot heal her pains,  
Well then you may fear the strumpet,  
For she will adorn you with the laurel,  
And with the crown, and with the crown,  
Of Actaeon.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
Away, away with Bacchus and Silenus  
I prefer the pleasant Venus  
And this Goon  
With their boon.  
Who would not, with soul and mind,  
Love them wholeheartedly,  
While that sweetness nourishes joy.  

2.  
And what good is drinking to us,  
When everyone hates each other,  
It affects our body,  
But stiff and rigid.  
Where the lovely glances  
Gifted by a friendly eye,  
Soon free our heart from pain.  

3.  
Away then, drinkers, away sinners!  
Here is greater joy to be found  
The drink  
Only leads you onto the path  
Of many unrestrained morals  
Where all the pleasures  
Of love satisfy the mind.  

4.  
Who would want to lose that joy,  
And choose Bacchus over Venus?  
Who holds the bottle  
As a mistress,  
While the dear attractions  
Are always adorned by Klorinde.  
Where wine brings care and pain.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
I give the wine a kick,  
What use do you have for licking?  
One drinks its whole crop,  
And guts almost burst.  
No love can our heart  
With more pleasure, caress,  
If it gives us some pain,  
It can heal us again,  
Its yoke can never tire,  
If it is well borne.

2.  
Ah! Look at pain and sorrow,  
O lover, do not complain,  
Your reward will be greater,  
If heartache is endured.  
The pains themselves are sweet,  
They make our heart blossom,  
And through that glow,  
Make us even drunk with love,  
When the eye comes flashing at us,  
Whose shine makes us blush.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
Alas! How sweet it is to live in the cabbage,  
Sang the free nightingale in the field,  
When it hears a canary giving a sound,  
That echoes through wood and field with its  
language.

2.  
He does not have to gather his food with sorrow,  
He has good grain, a sweet renewal:  
He can sleep peacefully through the whole night,  
She follows, delighted, him wherever  
he skips.

3.  
Amintas, seeing a beautiful shepherdess;  
Thought to be at the height of all joy,  
If so dear, a maiden granted him  
her love,  
With whom he spent his best youth faithfully.

4.  
But in the cage and marriage there are many  
torments,  
That no one knows but those who are first  
in it,  
You who want to carry pure love for each other,  
Remain unmarried and always follow the sweet pleasure  
of love.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1.  
Friends, if you ask about the cause  
Of living alone for so long,  
And not giving my heart and hand  
To a good girl.  
I am not yet tired of life,  
And you ask why?  
I will tell you the reason  
What heavy obstacles  
Keep me back. Listen closely.  

2.  
First of all, I carefully ensured,  
That around her perfect body,  
There was some kind of layer,  
To devote his disinterest,  
To that sweet and inviting look,  
That from her face,  
Played so unpleasantly with me;  
That she might share it with another.  
Especially no Venus for me!  

3.  
I marry once an ugly wife,  
With purple horse-colored cheeks,  
And is not worth a penny,  
Can neither make love nor fire,  
Fie! There is no time to waste on such a wife,  
Because my desire for fiery kisses  
Would extinguish her face.  
Especially no ugly wife for me!  

4.  
I marry a clever spirit,  
Very polite and well-speaking,  
And who mostly breaks her head daily  
In books.  
So she wants by her reason  
To have the upper hand.  
She wants, by her charming speech,  
To do this and forbid that,  
To be master of the marriage bond.  

5.  
I marry again a bad dove  
Who can only talk nonsense  
About her cap and her tuft  
And children’s goods.  
It is only sadness without end,  
All her chatter.  
I would not value her at all,  
Thinking she is half crazy.  
If only I had her for a week.  

6.  
I marry once a wealthy wife,  
That I can live off her goods,  
And only our youthful bodies  
To each other's pleasure devote.  
Yet the matter is not good,  
See what you do.  
She will impress on your breast  
That you live prosperously  
On her income. Away with wives’ fortunes!  

7.  
I marry again a sweet  
Daughter of a poor burgher,  
So I must in adversity  
Be a miserable caretaker.  
Because we both, equally poor  
Of wealth,  
Would have to live poorly.  
What joy can marriage then give,  
When one keeps a sober meal?  

8.  
And if fortune should fall  
That Heaven would grant me  
A peaceful and sweet soul  
To live joyfully with.  
Yet the matter is still not well,  
But greatly troubled.  
You must care for your children,  
Day and evening, night and morning,  
Until you get a wrinkled skin.  

9.  
And if the marriage gives no children,  
Then there is no pleasure,  
For one desires fruits  
Of one's sowing and ploughing,  
And then your fate is  
That you are mocked.  
Neither virtue is yours then,  
You must suffer clacking tongues.  
Would I marry? I would be mad.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
I want to praise bachelorhood,
For it surpasses everything,
To it I want to give honor,
Above the married state:
But when you are married,
To such an evil wicked wife,
Bachelorhood you then lose:
Farewell then, bachelorhood,
Farewell then, bachelorhood.

If I want to tell a tale,
To a fair daughter here or there,
The fire of love begins to shine,
Already on the heart of a lover,
If this daughter doesn’t please me,
Then I go on my way again,
I wish her pleasure without sorrow;
Long live then, bachelorhood;
Long live then, bachelorhood.

When I go to drink a pint,
No woman in the house waits for me,
Then I sit without worries,
Sometimes the whole half night,
Hey, when I come home again,
I will not have to listen to;
The sad little wife’s reproach,
Long live then, bachelorhood:
Long live then, bachelorhood.

There’s no woman who can force me,
When I go to play cards,
Even if I play with dice,
I am not skilled at it,
And if I lose some money,
Hey, then I am content with that,
The sad little wife sits and cries,
Long live then, bachelorhood:
Long live then, bachelorhood.

When I have drunk a pint,
My belly full and round,
I go straight to my bed,
And fall asleep immediately:
In the middle of my rest,
No one will disturb me there:
The sad children cry:
Long live then, bachelorhood:
Long live then, bachelorhood.

Young men, remember this well,
All the sweet bachelorhood,
But if you ever come to marry,
Bachelorhood you then lose,
For when you are bound,
By the vow of marriage,
Bachelorhood you then lose,
Farewell then, bachelorhood,
Farewell then, bachelorhood.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Climeen, Where to  
But do your thoughts wander?  
I would love you:  
But never that I;  
Would mean to marry you:  
No, by my oath,  
I would not like that,  
I have a fear of it.  
I want  
In Love,  
And her gentle bonds,  
To offer you with willing hands  
Of my heart;  
But not in marriage:  
Because that becomes  
By time detestable,  
And brings great pain.  
Would you,  
With me,  
Live as before,  
And give your heart?  
Then I am at peace:  
But if that is not your will:  
Say freely no;  
I stick with Amarillis,  
Then go away, Climeen.  
Damon,  
What could  
Drive you so far?  
Saying that against whores,  
Do you think I would  
Always love you,  
As your lawful wife?  
Feel free to get that out of your mind,  
Or give me your vow.  
What can,  
A man,  
Give you more joys,  
Than that he spends his whole life  
Paired,  
With a nice girl:  
Sweet by nature;  
That one travels from journey to journey,  
Young Damon indeed.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Jan Sul the Friday,  
Jan Sul the Friday,  
After a sweet dear girl:  
Nuts, grapes, figs  
Liere-lette: liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To get her as his wife.  

She has a mouth,  
She has a mouth,  
Like the Wester market all around,  
Cherries and Morello cherries,  
Liere-lette: liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To tell her something.  

Eyes sweet,  
Eyes sweet:  
Like a big Quaker hat,  
Mangles and raisins,  
Liere-lette: liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
He is pining away.  

Her nose,  
Her nose  
Like the saber of a giant,  
Greens and carrot sticks,  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To bring her home.  

Her little neck white,  
Her little neck white,  
Like the blowpipe of a smith,  
Cinnamon apples, pears,  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
With his fine clothes.  

Small breasts,  
Small breasts  
Round and flat and also square,  
Waterbread with clouts,  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To bring her fine coins.  

Little hands quick,  
Little hands quick,  
Each a big flyswat,  
Salsify and radishes:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
Wants to prove his loyalty.  

The little body stands,  
The little body stands,  
Crooked like Calf street,  
Buns with syrup:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
Thinks she must be for sale.  

Something strange,  
Something strange:  
Can be found without candle,  
Beef tripe with lard:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
Foolishly hesitating.  

Little haunches shake,  
Little haunches shake,  
Each like a child's bottom,  
Pomposity well-behaved with eggs:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To impress her a bit.  

Little feet flat,  
Little feet flat,  
Like the shield of Goliath,  
Little wine with orders:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
To annoy her a bit.  

Bloody she treads,  
Bloody she treads,  
Like a goat eating beans,  
Crumbs of butter bread:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
On his skinny legs.  

Hey, she speaks,  
Hey, she speaks,  
Like a crazy devil preaches:  
Horseradish with sugar,  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
Like a foolish drunkard.  

But she hears,  
But she hears,  
Not a word from Jan Sul,  
Bacon with Pentecost nails:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
And is gibbering.  

Nods and bows,  
Nods and bows,  
Grunts and laughs,  
Snorts and rejoices,  
Pig carbonades:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
He runs after that sweet Susje,  
Yet it is too late.  

All suitors,  
All suitors,  
Be careful, but not too foolish,  
Herring on a grill:  
Liere-lette, liere-la,  
Yet no crippled suitor runs after her,  
Send her to a monastery.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
My Roosje, my Croosje, is sweet and dear,  
My Little Mouth, my Little Fool, whom I must praise,  
My Darling, my Thief, my Croosje, my Child,  
Her Beauty,  
On display,  
For me, who holds her mind  
Like sulfur sticks,  
Which the vermilion dulls,  
Very good for a Monsieur,  
Who is called Reprateur,  
Or for Crispijn’s Guests:  
No one more beautiful I have seen surprised on Earth.  
To walk to the girl,  
Every cheek is stiff from the wetness,

label: anti-marriage
translation:
In the sweet month of May,  
An opera came to play here,  
Whose entertainment was offensive;  
To share many guilders here,  
But the theater caught fire  
Because of Flemish misunderstanding,  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, go away now,  
You caused many to weep.  

What will the actors do now,  
Who were not even allowed to come there:  
Under the play of the Flemish hen,  
Their strong light has been extinguished,  
Their hands were crooked,  
Because they are far too dumb:  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, it is done,  
Want to go to Bruges or Ghent.  

Because of their clumsy, clumsy language,  
The theater is now lost,  
And they said repeatedly,  
We now see art born,  
And they praised its flat speech,  
To the foolish their entertainment,  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, silly blood:  
I don't know what you're still doing here.  

Your fox can be recognized by its hair,  
Your snout would betray you,  
Your cowardly Flemish heart  
Is seen to our detriment,  
You took much bread here,  
Many others were killed,  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, cowardly by nature,  
You are not worth a penny to us.  

Remember the burning of Loosduinen,  
Where you also destroyed a barn  
Before you came to the Amstel side;  
With your opera you defied us,  
Then everyone called out wisely,  
Do you want to go to Ghent again,  
Little Fleming? Little Fleming, go to Ghent,  
They called then, it is a long way.  

Because of the impure Dutch,  
And unsightly entertainment,  
The cat is watching Flemish mouse,  
To crack him like a nut.  
Go forever to your land,  
And take care of light and fire,  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, now it's over for you,  
Go now sing for a penny.  

The theater is now sold,  
And you dare to play in Haarlem;  
Have asked in Rotterdam,  
To steal money with your opera,  
And it has been allowed,  
To go there on fair days,  
Little Fleming, little Fleming, go away from here,  
Drink your little pot of Leuven beer.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Glimeen, where are you going,  
Yet your senses wander,  
I want to love you,  
But not that I intend  
To marry,  
No, by my oath,  
Do not trust that,  
I am afraid of that.  

2. I want to enter love,  
And its gentle bonds?  
You well offer your hands,  
Do it from my heart,  
But not in marriage,  
Because that would,  
Over time be dreadful:  
And bring us much pain.  

3. If you want, with me,  
To live again as before,  
And give your heart,  
Then I am at peace,  
But if that is not your will,  
Say freely no,  
I side with Amarilles,  
Go away, Climeen.  

4. Damon, what could,  
Carry your passions so far,  
Say that against your conscience,  
Do you think I would  
Always love you,  
As your wife,  
I leave it freely to your mind,  
Or grant me your faithfulness.  

5. Indeed, a man,  
Can give you even more joy  
If he keeps his life  
Spared,  
With a charming girl,  
Sweet of nature,  
Where one journeys on journeys,  
Young Damon’s beard.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
1. I was somewhat inclined toward loyalty,
I fear it like death,
I choose the wine for my lady
Then I live free from worry,
For every day and night
A woman’s stubborn head
Is constantly to be expected,
I give love the boot.             Away, away.

2. If I marry a sweet Angel
Then I have that sorrow,
That many a naughty rascal
Shoots pipes into my reed;
Even if I had eagle’s wings:
I cannot escape them,
If I marry a messy woman,
I may not even see her myself.             That’s for sure.

3. If I marry a woman with a temper
Then I have that blame,
By day and by night,
Away, fool, wherever you are;
If I marry one without a misdeed,
Then it’s not good either,
Whoever can stay married
Is a fool if he does not.             That’s for sure.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Friends want to hear this song:
What I am going to tell you,
I will tell it to you beforehand,
Listen to this strange story,
About two lovers you want to understand,
Who have gone on a spree.

2. He was a soldier who wanted to know,
And she was a clear winner in her trade,
See, I have forgotten her name,
And his as well, indeed,
Hear how late in the evening,
She walked heavily through the street.

3. From one playhouse to another,
And she jumped on the bed,
There they lay next to each other,
He slept by that beautiful portrait,
Then she got a strange whim,
He wanted to play rough.

4. Her cheeks are in a bad way,
That comes from Bacchus’ drink,
In her mouth she has no teeth,
It is so, yes I do not know why:
She is so beautiful and fair-skinned,
She resembled Galatea very well.

5. Then she sits that restless little thing,
Already with her lover on her side,
He gives her many a push,
And then she is also so happy,
Then she says, darling, push on,
I am not tired of it yet.

6. She is so well-made from suffering,
She has such a pleasant mouth,
Her face is so well shaped,
She looks entirely like a doll’s face,
And she thrusts a bit backward.
Is that not a beautiful Bride?

7. Farewell, oh fairest on earth,
Farewell, oh heart that dearly loves me,
You are my dearest flower full of worth,
I have loved you for a long time,
One more little kiss for the last time,
Because that tastes best of all.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
If there is any clarity with gossip,  
Certainly not with drunken drinking,  
With drunken raving and clinking,  
To the ruin of court and house;  
Therefore note what wise punishment  
That people of old times gave.

Whether it was brandy or beer,  
All those foolish drunken fools  
Had to roll and spin  
For the boys’ pleasure:  
‘Into the barrel with every rascal!’ –  
Oh, it will surely be reintroduced.

It has certainly been told to me,  
That the High Council of States  
Has already bought and paid for  
A million jenever barrels. –  
Drunkards! quickly, repent a bit!  
Or you will fall from the glass into the barrel!

label: anti-marriage
translation:
Oh, Jan-nie, don’t get married in your life,  
Then you will be in sorrow,  
If you marry an old man,  
He will die before you;  
If you marry a young man,  
He will play on your lap.  
If you marry a lame man,  
He jumps on one leg;  
If you marry a skeleton,  
He looks everywhere;  
If you marry a nightmare,  
You will sleep too cold;  
If you marry a fat man,  
You will sleep too anxious;  
Oh, Jannie, don’t get married in your life,  
Otherwise you will be in grief!’  
(Oral tradition.)

label: anti-marriage
translation:
The Pope is our Father,  
He is chosen by God,  
He redeems us all,  
No one remains lost;  
So if you want to be born free and joyful,  
You humans, be fearless:  
He will destroy Hell.  
He carries the spears.  

He has many kinds  
Of monks and priests,  
They have little provision,  
Otherwise they would sing and gape,  
Only they always sleep  
As long as it is day:  
Their works are made by God—  
He carries the spears.  

They seek no ducats,  
Gold guilders or crowns,  
They always drink moderately,  
And eat raw beans:  
I must excuse them somewhat,  
I have been asked about it:  
The Lord shall reward them—  
He carries the spears.  

They have no dominion  
Here or in any places,  
They are good in opinion,  
They cover their shame;  
Hear me evoke their virtue,  
Who despise no thing;  
Also they are without blemish—  
He carries the spears.  

Prince,  
Prince, you Sasserdotten,  
Want to preserve your honor,  
You are God's chosen,  
Who wants to spare you for a long time,  
With all your servants,  
You who thoroughly know scripture:  
God's Word will surely explain it—  
He carries the spears.

label: anti-marriage
translation:
A new little song of the Geuzen [1va]

¶ Do you want to hear a new song?  
Vive le geux!  
And that from Sint-Truiden has happened.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe dobbe dop. fa. lyrum. fa.

¶ It happened on a Monday, listen!  
Vive le geux!  
The Geuzen were already before the gate.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ The gate was closed, and they were not allowed in.  
Vive le geux!  
So they took a bad mind.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ They knocked with a determined courage.  
Vive le geux!  
Why doesn’t one open the gate?  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Then my lord came from Duras.  
Vive le geux!  
And asked if they were his lords?  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc. ut supra

¶ They said we are all from Nassau.  
Vive le geux!  
We want to hold our council here.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Those from Sint-Truiden stood firm,  
Vive le geux!  
And sent a messenger to the bishop.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ The bishop spoke these words:  
Vive le geux!  
‘Sint-Truiden is a wealthy city.’  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ The Geuzen had bad courage.  
Vive le geux!  
They rode to Leuven in a hurry.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ On Saturday Floyon came to help.  
Vive le geux!  
And let the monks come inside.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe.

¶ They swore by their noble courage,  
Vive le geux!  
That they would not harm anyone.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Then the Revalge called with force.  
Vive le geux!  
This is the day we have waited for.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Floyon said: ‘We know very well,  
Vive le geux!  
The king is already coming into our land.’  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ And if the king did not come,  
Vive le geux!  
Then the small monasteries remain in distress.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ They retreated into the monastery’s enclosure,  
Vive le geux!  
Where they sought loose counsel.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Heard counsel was soon revealed,  
Vive le geux!  
They destroyed statues and altars.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc. [1vb]

¶ They have given power to their promises,  
Vive le geux!  
They have not practiced Sint-Truiden.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe etc.

¶ Let us pray to the Lord of lords,  
Vive le geux!  
That they may convert the monks.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe.

¶ And he who has composed this little song,  
Vive le geux!  
Was a woman of noble light.  
Vive le geux!  
Dobbe, dobbe, dop. fa. lirum. fa.

When one called: ‘Vive le geux!’ throughout all lands,  
Then it happened that we saw churches and cloisters burning.

label: anti-marriage
translation: ¶ In the year 1566 this little song was first composed. Then the heresy was greatly suppressed. Look here to your benefit. For people sang it in the streets. About the great man. If you want to hear a new song, come to Hasselt, great man. What happened to the great man? Come to Hasselt, great, great. Come to Hasselt, great man. It happened on Saint Matthew's Day. Come to Hasselt, etc. That one saw on the way. Come to Hasselt, etc. Come up above. The children were very confused. Come, etc. They ran home in great heaps. Come, etc. Come to Hasselt, etc. When he came into Hasselt, Come to Hasselt, etc. They gave him a sandwich. Come to, etc. Come to Hasselt. They sat him down there on a block. Come, etc. They put a yellow coat on him. Come to, etc. Come, etc. When he was to go in the procession, Come to, etc. There he received a stick. Come to, etc. Come to Hasselt. When Wouter, my eldest son, heard this, Come, etc. He was truly so angry. Come, etc. Come to, etc. That great man was so severely hurt, Come to, etc. He died on the spot. Come, etc. [Come to, etc.] When one wrote one thousand five hundred sixty-six, Come, etc. Then was that Vaes Landmeters this business. Come, etc. [Come to, etc.] Herewith I hereby let this little song go. Come, etc. God willing, may he preserve our great man's soul. Come to, etc. [Come to, etc.]

label: anti-marriage
translation:
A new little song of the Geuzen  
The Spanish king has come to the land  
Vivere papist.  
The Romans act as if they are good Christians.  
Vivere b[a]rgoende, spiteful Hugonist  
Vivere papist.  
They follow their own whims.  
Vivere papist.  
They intended to break the Roman church.  
They have missed.  
Vivere burgoende et cetera.  
Calvin’s teaching is nothing but spite.  
Vivere papist.  
Their churchyard is whitened outside  
All that’s worthless.  
Vivere burgoende et cetera.  
They have deceived so many people.  
Vivere papist.  
They lie to me without shame with pride  
Through the devil’s trick.  
Vivere burgoede et cetera.  
With beautiful words they have deceived the people.  
Vivere papist.  
Hermannus has fled with that money  
That no one knew.  
Vivere borgoende et cetera.  
Orloff prince at this term.  
Vivere papist.  
Calvin’s teaching is nothing but spite  
Like stinking mist.  
Vivere borgoende et cetera.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
The day of 's Heyland’s birth  
Recently gave cause for joyful poems;  
Yours brings forth new ones again,  
And thus invites me to compose for you.

2.  
Since your birthday, o friend,  
Follows shortly after that of your Lord;  
Mark here the useful teaching,  
To learn your duties from it.

3.  
He who after His birth grows up  
In Wisdom and in Holy manners,  
Bids you here quietly but clearly,  
To shortly follow in His footsteps.

4.  
That is, that His example and Law  
Be your light and beacon for your Soul,  
That you constantly keep before your eyes;  
Follow Him who goes before closely.

5.  
He was born so that He  
Might free you from the power of sins,  
To stand under His lordship,  
Firmly bound to His service.

6.  
O sweet state of slavery  
Of the Soul bowed under God,  
Awaiting the coming freedom; which she  
Already sees with spiritual eyes.

7.  
Strive to imitate  
His earthly likeness in all your conduct,  
So that through His virtuous image  
He is spiritually reborn in you.

8.  
Then the Father (who restored His child  
To blessed life)  
Will accept you like Him in earthliness,  
Also in life by His side.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
What cause may God give,  
That He lets us prosper  
In peace for so many years?  
His desire for our well-being.  

2.  
This never-deserved blessing  
Produces in us every day  
The fruit of bearing here,  
By walking in God's ways.  

3.  
You can clearly see here,  
That never gaining virtue,  
But always keeping earthly desires  
Is dangerous.  

4.  
Oh, turn once inward,  
And is today  
Even better than the past,  
Your soul will find rest.  

5.  
This I hope from you,  
Not only but moreover,  
That you increasingly  
Are penetrated to walk to God.  

6.  
The moderate effort,  
Many think is enough:  
Thus youth goes astray too soon,  
And was (Alas) deceived.  

7.  
But so as not to stray,  
I wish that you may renounce  
Early the enjoyment of earthly show,  
And draw comfort from above.  

8.  
Oh! Whoever has grasped this well,  
By insight finding some pleasure,  
God creates in holy bearing,  
For no treasure gives such a treasure.  

9.  
When I deeply consider this here,  
Though I am saddened,  
That I still need  
To seek to strengthen you.  

10.  
It is true for you all the years,  
If God has also commanded,  
What the body needs  
To skillfully gather as food.  

11.  
The earthly, only perishable,  
Cannot nourish you, O human;  
The soul must be guarded  
Eternal and immortal.  

12.  
The most important thing here is,  
To follow the Spirit's counsel,  
And reject the flesh’s advice:  
Let this also weigh the heaviest.  

13.  
Gather such treasures,  
By which (though you forsake  
All that flesh once enjoys)  
You are embraced by God.  

14.  
Whoever wants to lose all this  
For my sake and life,  
Says Christ, to give for me,  
I choose to be my servant.  

15.  
Granted to you by God many goods;  
Feeds; Refreshes; Clothes; and Shelters;  
Visits; and comes unbidden  
To your imprisoned brothers.  

16.  
Thus if you here so generously  
Lay away against what is coming,  
A firm ground departing,  
You will find God gracious.  

17.  
In short, if you want to sow here so,  
Not to the flesh destruction,  
But to the Spirit; as inheritance  
You will reap eternal life.  

18.  
Grant that we may with confidence  
Oh! Lord, daily follow You:  
Gather Youth and Virtue:  
And afterward behold You.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
The Christian’s highest duty,  
Of which God’s word speaks,  
Can never be more perfect,  
Than that they steadfastly  
Sharpen the mind  
To do good,  
To fear God earnestly.

2.  
Is it always good, even more so,  
Now since the Lord  
Enriches your years anew,  
That you are mindful  
That with this new time,  
New virtue must also accompany.

3.  
This is my wish and prayer,  
That soul’s and body’s pain  
Never disturb your rest here,  
Which you find in God; and especially,  
If through the Spirit,  
You are reborn in every way.

4.  
No one is moved to this,  
But death of earthly lease,  
And anything that the flesh desires:  
Unless one violently  
Suppresses the flesh’s passions,  
The spirit here never truly rests.

5.  
Seek the heavenly, the earthly perishes,  
Learn to die before you die,  
So that when the hour of death  
Suddenly comes upon you,  
You do not then learn to refuse the evil,  
(that now is the time).

6.  
Flee foolish pride,  
And foolish vanity:  
Much rather be present  
In spirit, and go inward,  
For inner adornment  
Is precious in God’s eyes.

7.  
Never let unfruitful words  
Depart from your lips,  
They offend pious ears:  
But produce all that has edification,  
So that it gives  
Grace to those who hear it.

8.  
Narrow is the path of life,  
Nothing unclean has hold there:  
So do not seek space where it is narrow:  
God’s word is easily misinterpreted  
According to the flesh’s convenience:  
Whereby one becomes slow and lukewarm.

9.  
Is it then sinful  
To act joyfully?  
Oh no, I would not say that:  
Yet I find it necessary  
That joy originates,  
From God, I return to Him.

10.  
Now that the Spirit lives,  
Strive to celebrate the best,  
Endeavor to imitate Christ,  
So you are refreshed in Him,  
And keep watch for His coming,  
You will find heaven on earth.

11.  
Oh, sweet slavery!  
Who is freer?  
Than one who is bowed before God,  
Enjoys Him here in hope,  
Sees the freedom to come,  
With spiritual eyes.

12.  
This I now wish for you,  
That you grow weary of the world,  
Desire only God:  
And withdraw your mind  
From earthly goods,  
And for God, lack everything.

13.  
Ah Lord! grant that we  
May continuously lift up  
Our hearts and eyes to You;  
And watch in the Spirit,  
To be with You here and especially  
To enjoy You hereafter.

label: birthday
translation:
All-seeing One! who from Heaven's arches,  
Beholds all that stirs beneath the Moon,  
Look with your gracious eyes,  
You who make everything rise and fall,  
Mercy-giver full of compassion!  
Treader of hell and death,  
Who embraces yours with open arms  
And protects them from all distress!  
Receive, O High Father! once again  
That time, which from us has come to an end!  
It is yours, who makes everything rise and fall,  
Turn or stand still.

label: birthday
translation:
I.  
I sometimes like to imagine  
That I was rid of my Feeling;  
Until I stroked Cloris again,  
Then my heart first rejoiced.  
And from top to bottom  
Joy crept through my limbs;  
All the Feelings came together.  

II.  
But it is with strict Lords,  
Just as the proverb says,  
That they never reign long:  
So it was with my Ardor,  
And my initial great Joy  
Was fairly cold and dead;  
The purse light, and the head like a twig.  

III.  
I side with the Lord of Dusren;  
And unusual change.  
I side with where one can sit five, six hours  
Exactly in a circle,  
Breasts and buttocks tire me:  
No; it goes better here;  
Because here I see what I do.  

IV.  
Although the Feeling was dear to me,  
I have four others besides;  
I am also no stepfather to them,  
And that always brought jealousy:  
Because for this Sense alone  
All the others are married around.  
Was the joy not extraordinary?  

V.  
But here I taste, smell, hear,  
And it is fair for the Sight.  
But here I know, I lose them;  
The purse is heavy, the brains light.  
This makes the lungs and limbs strong;  
The other is mere peasant work:  
This is for the Court, and for the Church.  

VI.  
Priests, Monks, Clerks, Popes,  
Lawyers, Procurators,  
One might gape more at here,  
Than at the best market.  
The Heidelberg Vat  
Never had  
So many suitors,  
As Councilor Roemers has in their place.  

VII.  
It is so ardently prayed to,  
Because of its moist garment,  
Everyone feels, through all their limbs,  
The fire of this Love burn:  
That is never extinguished,  
However fat Love feeds it,  
But what of it in the end?  

VIII.  
By day, to quench the noble thirst  
Your dry throat is invited again;  
Afterwards you have pushed through a bartender or sausage  
With rye flour.  
Straight then to the suitor;  
Whom one never saw without jealousy;  
Secretly she gave a blue glow.  

IX.  
But quite otherwise is the situation,  
Another day with Breeches and Scarf,  
Breeches that slip off the paths,  
Although she served them Sweet Cake,  
With raisins and preserves;  
With good words, and reproaches,  
Breeches have no time the next day.  

X.  
Prince, who comes here to hear  
My feeling about the Feeling,  
Where time is too often lost,  
I withdraw, I sing, I rave, and stir,  
Now I add to it again;  
I hold, I prick, I carve, I cut:  
Farmer, now watch your Geese free.

label: birthday
translation:
I.  
I call all this singing  
Unnecessary things,  
Each does what he can best;  
But losing time,  
There my cries call out  
To ruin in response.  

II.  
Yet it is stipulated,  
That none unsung  
May cut from the Ham;  
I swear, by all sausages,  
I sang my heart out;  
So that I might have a share in it.  

III.  
What was I before this  
A mere hound,  
Of Archimedes' kind;  
Then, for their Differences,  
With Tubes and Glasses  
I was busy by day and by night.  

IV.  
Ptolemy lacked it;  
Copernicus guessed;  
I saw that Tycho Brahe  
As wise as his Mathematician:  
His Circles and Protractor  
Deceived just as much, or more.  

V.  
It all had to go their way;  
The Earth had to stand still:  
No, cried the others again,  
Let the Earth spin;  
But however they decided,  
Their brains spun even more.  

VI.  
I heard these Companions  
Tell old Phoebus,  
How he circled the World,  
How, before his walk,  
Twelve Taverns stood open,  
Which Jupiter created for him.  

VII.  
Cease your disputes,  
Or keep them away from me;  
It is Peace that I seek;  
And if you are oppressed,  
Turn, so that you are visited;  
But do not turn my Dish.  

VIII.  
And let my Glass stand;  
Thereon I can rely,  
More than on your Almanac.  
Here is, or I am truly lost,  
A certain Pole Star;  
Which never lacks its light.  

IX.  
Here I see Planets,  
Fixed Stars, Comets,  
Arrangement, all under  
Such gentle swaying,  
Like a pot with fish;  
All the household goods are now common.  

X.  
Thus I now go to the field;  
So, Farmer, that money  
Do as I have done;  
Thus all the brightness  
Of the Night is lost in the dark;  
I think the Day is also coming.

label: birthday
translation:
I.
Everyone sings firmly that he sweats:
I am born for the table,
I am for the, &c.
While I drink and eat,
No time is lost there:
That is no trouble for my throat,
Which swallows firmly without hearing.

II.
Courage, my dear Capoen,
You must not escape me;
You must not, &c.
I will make you daily
Crawl through my intestines,
Shoeless but barefoot,
And drench you with wine;

III.
And say; this is the grave
Of one doomed to death;
Of one doomed to, &c.
Because he gave no more
To Cantekleer’s widows and orphans:
And all his gear was off,
That could heal heads.

IV.
The body, rolled in a pancake
In place of linden:
Rolled, in place, &c.
Tart is found in every corner
In place of straw:
The dead is buried in Broek
With his friends.

V.
Little lions, by the dozen,
Go along to sing at the corpse;
Go along to the corpse, &c.
The Volunteers are
Mashed finches.
The youth, long once with wine,
To drink death in sorrow.

VI.
Ah me! how cold that is!
It sinks down to my toes;
It sinks down, &c.
My stomach surely has a hole,
The wine drips through it:
Come, offer something here,
Bring ready lime and stones.

VII.
What is thus kept here
In his dry vestments?
In his, &c.
Oh, it is worth the pain,
It is the calf he butchered:
Make sure you do not spare it,
Now everything is at its best.

VIII.
A glass in every hand,
And the song of joy started;
And the song of joy, &c.
The vestments are encouraged,
The enemy lies defeated,
By knights pious and valiant,
Therefore we burn Piktonnen.

IX.
Constables long are four of us,
And those laid muskets.
And those laid, &c.
That smoke nearly suffocates me,
It dries my nets too strongly:
Give me again a mug of beer,
That I will set there in between.

X.
Now everything is empty;
My belly taken out.
My belly, &c.
That once got news
To come again tomorrow:
I wager my mouth never silenced;
Even if I would dream of it at night.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
Fine man, Insensitive servant,  
Wouldn’t you be better served,  
If instead of chairs,  
You were given a sharpened stick?  
For you cannot feel it anyway:  
And your ass is nowhere aware.  

2.  
That your insensitivity,  
At feasts, was assigned  
A role among chapter sticks;  
With it, the truth was known,  
Whether it was true or false,  
That you are insensitive.  
Ask him how your wine tastes?  
He says, it cannot be late:  
Says that he must invite us;  
He asks, how much does that hat cost?  
What does such a fool on earth do,  
Who finds no pleasure in red cakes?  
Once Aeltje van der Plas  
Came humbly before him:  
Wouldn’t the fool have to smell  
In whatever land he was?  

3.  
Ovidius became a shameful exile  
Because he went too often to the stable with Julia,  
Because he with &c.  

4.  
Horace, the squinter, and exceedingly slimy...  
Was Caesar’s flatterer, and Maecenas’ sycophant,  
Was Caesar’s &c.  

5.  
Martial is satirical, but mocks the fool as well;  
He speaks of a stocking as if it were a piece of bacon,  
He speaks of a &c.  

6.  
Who has looked upon a greater fool than Lucan;  
Who with his pen has stabbed himself to death!  
Who with his &c.  

7.  
So that from today I still knew yesterday,  
That the spirit of madness resides in poets,  
That the spirit &c.  

8.  
Yet they welcome me with all their rough teasing;  
Because I once studied in a school with them,  
Because I once &c.  

9.  
They helped me speak; who would dare be dead?  
Here you see what faithful friends are in distress,  
Here you see &c.  

10.  
Be welcome then little fools, and drink freely;  
For whoever is not a fool here, must become one,  
For whoever here is not &c.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
That is just eight times a guest  
In the time of Seven days:  
Should one not rightly ask,  
If my Wambays still fits me?  
But who can hardly understand me,  
That I exchange ink for Wine:  
And there, by force, the People  
Once a Year want to celebrate?

2.  
Although my Rhyming desire is over,  
This will be the last, I may hope,  
To go about with Verses,  
But not about your Year-mark:  
That I wish another Sixty times,  
Without disaster, to come again:  
I would fetch no Apollo,  
I wish his Wisdom good day.

3.  
I now have for my Singing goddess  
Tarts, Flatbreads, and Pastries,  
That teach me to fly better,  
Than Horace, who in  
Lollius’ White breads  
Such Taste and footsies found;  
That he well with drooping Legs  
Could nicely sit before him.

4.  
If my Inkwell were as big  
As the Glass here of my Zyman,  
Who rightly can’t pass me,  
I would write myself half dead in your Praise  
And my Pen should be just so,  
As there just now was that flute,  
But who would want to read it, Droes?  
The book would not be finished in a Century.

5.  
Would I once come upon the Style  
Of Apples, and Reliefs,  
Of à minima the Grievances:  
Or how many Petitioners,  
And Defendants in Rough Action,  
You have saved Money and Time:  
Or how you preserved  
Many judgments from putrefaction.

6.  
If I had to make everyone understand  
Your honest and concise Pleas,  
Then in Rights, then in Facts;  
I was surely badly at it;  
Cleverer, or if I had a Trial,  
It wouldn’t seem it; it’s best,  
It seems to me, that I sheathe my Knife.  
Or I run out of eating last.

7.  
Pious Farmer, who so heavy,  
Like me, you are to get as guest,  
If you continue so with silence,  
Instead of One, you will get a Pair;  
That you will also put inside:  
I bet I will soon hear your Voice,  
That will not say,  
But; long live! long live! Mister Rem.

8.  
Drink up, the time is short,  
The salvation of those in twelve Months  
Will again (well understanding  
That this Day is not counted)  
Gather here for pleasure;  
For me, I say it surely so:  
If he doesn’t get tired of Celebrating,  
I would not get tired of his Tart either.

9.  
But holla! now I look around:  
To fill the body, to empty the glass  
Makes, even if they are straight paths,  
All my other Ways crooked;  
My intestines do Exercise,  
And the Hawk touches my heart;  
I became as blind as Lady Justice;  
I also see no Persons.

10.  
Excuse me;  
Now I depart from the Gym:  
May your Brotherhood  
Have great thanks for the charge.  
No Poems in the future  
I will give; for I have withdrawn  
The Muses: now I darken the rake.  
Enough for Today.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
Welcome possible Day,  
Welcome, double welcome, Candlemas;  
None may match you;  
You, whose name carries great weight.  

2.  
Thus cried the dry Gang,  
Always thirsty, always eager;  
Fed on Hare loins;  
Sharply toothed, like a saw.  

3.  
Jan, startled by this sound;  
I bet he would rather have seen a Ghost:  
He was well used at home;  
Where it is always Lady Feast day.  

4.  
It was also on the second of this month,  
That his Claertje had her birthday;  
Whom he courteously served, dear Reader,  
Don’t think otherwise, it was well arranged.  

5.  
That he had a young son,  
When he was Claertje’s Lord and Man;  
The child was as beautiful as a statue,  
And alabaster was what covered him.  

6.  
Don’t praise it, ask the Peat farmer,  
Who said to Cunis, “Oh Man,  
I wish that were in your marrow,  
What? that poor Akkerman.”  

7.  
Words fill no sack here,  
And even less empty stomachs.  
Oh, my legs are so weak;  
That they can hardly carry the empty body.  

8.  
I become so ill; where is the Table?  
Hold me a quart in front of my nose,  
Set wine here; and there a waffle:  
It’s good, even if it’s entre deux.  

9.  
Blooming hearts, it is quiet here;  
Besides, we are no talkers:  
When a person wants to say something,  
Suddenly it’s, well, a lot of chatter!  

10.  
I choke on my food;  
Give another a turn:  
Silence there; suddenly what morsels!  
Wonder that his stomach does not tear.  

11.  
But whatever everyone thinks or says,  
I still laugh just as much about it:  
And for some impudence,  
I drink one glass more for it.  

12.  
Don’t you think that best, Uncle Peter?  
I have the weariness of the lonely hermit;  
No, I find it here a bit better:  
He may go home if he wants.  

13.  
I am tired of porridge and beans,  
And the disgust rises from flatfish;  
No, it goes much lively here,  
That people drink until they are full.  

14.  
I bring you, Candlemas, says one;  
I thank you, Candlemas, says the other:  
Would a stranger not think,  
That people were brothers with each other?  

15.  
All of the same name,  
Birds of the same feathers;  
Et Cantare pares t’ zaem,  
Et parati respondere?  

16.  
If you want to go chat by the Neighborhood,  
Go out with new shoes;  
All the rubbish from the streets  
Jan has brought into his house.  

17.  
No, it’s not fished from him,  
And he might well wish for the crowd;  
By my reckoning, it is so,  
Truly a Lazymart of people.

label: birthday
translation:
What great favor shines upon me this day,
Which attaches a new Year to my life?
A favor for which I will give honor to You,
Blessed God, with humility and awe.

label: birthday
translation:
Sing and be merry now  
Phillis has a birthday, wish her a long life,  
To strive for heaven,  
For she is worthy of both.  
Sing and be merry now  
Phillis has a birthday.

label: birthday
translation:
Sings, sings aloud, Of Bas and Flute, For Fillis, Fillis is now a year old, And if I know the Girl correctly, She is by no means stingy, About her Liver Sausage and Sausage, And about Cakes, About her Liver Sausage and Sausage, And Wine for thirst, and Wine for thirst.  
And best of all,  
In this case,  
Is that she lets us smoke,  
That proper Venetian herb,  
Which we so gladly smoke,  
With the Cool wine,  
For she tastes to us;  
With the Cool wine,  
Like Marzipan.

Sing until it sounds,  
And, when you drink,  
Let it be to her health,  
And praise that sweet Young Maiden,  
Who can cure hunger;  
So, grab a glass,  
Cheerfully, cheerfully;  
So, grab a glass,  
Cheerfully, cheerfully;  
So, grab a glass,  
And quickly empty it.

Who is cheerful,  
And calm,  
Plays a little with his jaws;  
I see good Anchovies there,  
That will taste good with the drink;  
Blood! I am having such fun,  
And I want to cheer;  
Blood, I'm having such fun,  
I'm not going to bed.  
Come, then wish her,  
The Coming Year,  
No more illnesses to trouble;  
And that she drinks Tea and Coffee,  
As much as she may wish;  
For she may indeed;  
With a full belly,  
For she may indeed,  
Even if she is delicate.

Yes indeed Miss,  
I say it to you,  
I will not wish you again a happy birthday,  
Before I see you in honor and virtue,  
Sailing towards the Fool's Walk;  
For, it is not appropriate,  
Always to refuse,  
For, it is not appropriate,  
From such a Cup.

For, just so you know,  
I crooked Poet,  
Want to sing at your Wedding,  
Of cuddling, frolicking, and even more  
Entertaining things,  
Of which I gladly speak,  
Friendly Girl;  
Of which I gladly speak,  
Even if you get angry.  
I quit it,  
And, in conclusion,  
I wish you as many Suitors,  
As there are green Peas in the pod,  
And then choose from those fighters,  
Who please you best;  
According to your wishes,  
Who please you best,  
Oh beautiful Maiden.

label: birthday
translation:
Everyone who now honors Father Bacchus,  
Grabs a glass and a jug;  
And revels, by quaffing a pint,  
Always again from the start;  
Because know, he is yearly,  
Still as cheerful, generous, and fresh,  
Pour, pour,  
Pour, pour,  
Pour, pour;  
And just watch his sign.  
So shout: long may he live,  
Who can give the earthly wine,  
The Indian fell on the skin,  
And drove out his land borders.  
All of Silesia’s blood, what fun you had,  
What do you fight like a man,  
Not with rapier, dagger, bayonet,  
But with a full jug:  
Thus will we now also, in honor  
Of Bacchus, your sweet Lord,  
His sops, his sops, his sops,  
Bravely pour into the crop;  
And like the wine maidens,  
Bold and happily of mind,  
So that it sounds on land and sea,  
Keep shouting: Evoe!

Away with Bremen beer, away with thick mum,  
Just fools’ drink,  
You drive your lovers crazy and dumb,  
And their heads sick.  
Nothing beats Bacchus' noble wine,  
Which makes us joyfully drunk,  
But gently, but gently, but gently,  
I see dear waiting for us;  
So, fill your glasses full,  
And shout like noble masters:  
Away cowardly Venus, with your son,  
The wine god takes the crown!

Come, let's build an altar here;  
Come, stack with wisdom,  
Broken glasses on top of each other,  
Set wine leaves on fire;  
And offer Bacchus with a glass,  
The purse, where the money used to be,  
And show, and show, and show,  
That you are Bacchus’ son,  
And that the treasure of the Money God,  
Which everyone seems to want to grasp,  
Is less worthy to you,  
Than the purest wine.

Come host, give some horseradish,  
Some goat cheese and pomegranate,  
With salt and pepper, sprightly fast,  
Arrange for us some roast;  
Do not forget a Westphalian sausage,  
Blood, I am already thirsty just mentioning it,  
Pour in, pour in, pour in,  
And start again;  
As surely as we toast here,  
And drink without spilling,  
Everyone wishes that Bacchus lives forever,  
Who gives us these little tears.

label: birthday
translation:
My Calliope, my doll,  
So cheerfully lift once again,  
And sing with me by the glass and by the bottle,  
So cheerfully friends! come  
Now drink the glass once more;  
That’s for you, but if it should bother you a little,  
Tell us once; honestly and clearly:  
That’s clear with a sweep,  
To his long life and empty health.  
Now once steamed, Volcano in honor;  
That’s with the grape juice  
The flair and character.  
Thus we offer your feast,  
Joyful and merry in spirit,  
And celebrate, at the joyful altar of thanks,  
Your anniversary of twenty-two years.  
May you never lack again,  
And live as long as Hanneke the madwoman:  
And is there still something you wish for?  
I wish your youthful body  
A rich and virtuous wife.  
But, oh damn it!  
It is probably the last time:  
Time runs its swift course, perhaps  
We will never see you here again next year.  
Farewell my great mate:  
But hear one last word before you go:  
We will always remember you,  
Do the same for us overseas.

label: birthday
translation:
Courage! Go at it with gusto!  
To gather joy in the evening.  
Even if I now act sly as a fox,  
Oh Kaatje! I had to celebrate you:  
Prepare pies, bottles, and small glasses,  
Then we’ll sing and dance together;  
Only once a year, just this once.  
May your little hands at their time  
Only fold, smooth, and stiffen.  
Your loins, and limbs, and everything that goes with it,  
Externally, internally, more still belongs to you.  
May no discomfort ever come aboard. (repeat)  

7. Furthermore, I wish you pleasure in your life,  
Your cupboard full of golden ducats,  
Your cellar full of wine and beer,  
Your purse full of silver coins,  
Your shop full of customers, your attic full of wood,  
Your barrels full of bacon, and your pots full of lard,  
And much happiness and old age. (repeat)

label: birthday
translation:
My Kalkoop, my doll,  
So cheerful, come and get up,  
And sing with me, by the glass and the bottle,  
The birthday of my friend, come, Singer,  
I now have a cheerful mood:  
Monsieur Apol, with your smoky brew,  
Stand by and help me celebrate, it may count now;  
Let voice and strings loose.  
God Bacchus, follow this reel,  
And sweet melody;  
Grant Flute, Violin, bass, and voice,  
On this birthday once the power and emphasis:  
The birthday, as one knows,  
Is, without your tears, a bit  
Of spirit that makes us joyfully remember that we are a year older,  
Like the noble clear wine;  
So cheerful friends! come  
Drink a glass,  
That is for, though it is a little cheeky,  
Tell your story! honest and pure out loud:  
That’s clear with a sweep,  
A toast to his long life and health.  
Now once dammed, volcano in honor, that’s at the grape juice:  
The grace and the character.  
Thus we offer your feast,  
Cheerful and happy of spirit,  
And celebrate, at the joyful thanksgiving altar,  
Your annual celebration, of twenty-two years,  
Never lacking again,  
And live as long as Hanneke the Fool,  
And if you still have something to wish for; I wish your youthful body,  
A rich and virtuous wife.  
But, oh! the devil take it,  
It is probably the last time,  
Time runs its swift course, maybe,  
That we never see you here celebrating again.  
There, my great companion,  
But hear one last word before you go,  
We will always remember you, even overseas,  
Remember us the same.

label: birthday
translation:
Good evening dear Trui,  
Good evening little marble box,  
I am now in a mild mood,  
Ah! my freshly blossomed little rose,  
Round blonde curly head,  
Neater than a little hat,  
Girl, here comes Floris Knol  

And presents you with a Needle,  
Which you must stick through the hair,  
Darling, see how it shines,  
I have looked out for it for you,  
And while you at this time,  
Are just eighteen years old,  
This Needle is for you, oh! Girl.  

Further lives my Sunflower,  
Many years and less Floris,  
Yes, grows old and never bends,  
Follows Besje who is ahead by much,  
Sews and knits, and stops and stiffens,  
And so inherits the folk,  
Floris remains your Servant.  

Hey, what do you say dear Floor?  
Are you so inclined to me?  
Besje does not answer yet,  
She opposes our wedding day,  
Oh! She grumbles and mutters, and hits  
Me, when I talk about it,  
Yes, then she is quietly angry.  
Dear Truitje, come, I will ask her,  
Tomorrow for the ‘Yes’ word,  
I know we will be a couple,  
I will please Besje well,  
Here’s a kiss, here it goes,  
If she says Yes, I am happy,  
Otherwise, it’s a black eye.

The birthday poem steals a youthful heart,  
That was moved to love.

label: birthday
translation:
We thank You, O God!,
That You have given this Teacher
To our salvation and comfort so long in life,
His golden tongue, filled with heavenly counsel,
Is sweeter than the sweet
And all the treasures
Of earthly goods.
Give then, O Lord! that we rightly understand
What he continuously
Preaches to us from Your Word.
It is today eighty years
That he has lived; but
We are, alas! for this received good,
Not as grateful as we ought to be.
How many times
Has he, through his reasoning,
And refined language,
Brought us so far that we confessed
With our heart and mouth
That You truly sent him.
O clear trumpet of repentance,
Guardian of God's garden;
Who, just like a Jonah for our benefit,
Protects us from the plagues of Heaven (well deserved).
If Jonah was sent by God to Nineveh,
Then you are sent to us as well,
Therefore, O Lord! please open our mouths
To Your praise,
We lack no reason for it.

P.Y. van der Hof.

label: birthday
translation:
O Girl, in whose years  
One now counts three sixes,  
We come here gathered,  
Set to joy,  
To celebrate again  
The hour of your birth,  
With sweet things;  
Give my songs  
Then hearing.  
There is nothing more pleasant  
Than the flower in its youth;  
Also one finds nothing more fitting  
To taste the sweetest joy  
Than a Maiden,  
Who on her cakes bears  
Red Roses  
Whose little blushes  
Everyone delights in.  
For the flower, before that,  
The eye takes pleasure in,  
No one will cherish more  
Than when it is dry and withered;  
And Maidens, freed from art,  
Are without favor,  
Like just simple  
Wrinkle or crease  
That the forehead frowns.  
So Girl of three sixes,  
Before you have aged so much,  
That you resemble the grumpy Berries  
In appearance and nature,  
I wish that  
You soon become tired and satisfied  
With the solitudes,  
And tread  
A better path.  
I mean the path of marriage,  
So that, before this day  
We look back again,  
One may visit you,  
In the joyful gleam of a wedding.  
Who wishes this more than I?  
All together!  
I then say Amen;  
It must be so.

label: birthday
translation:
O Sweet Girl! It is,  
(If I am not mistaken)  
Again the day, That one saw  
Joy revived,  
In your birth.  
How it throbbed then in joy!  
Your Mother was delighted,  
Although her heart,  
At first with pain  
Was burdened;  
That pressure week after week.  
Your Father showed him,  
Through his delighted voice,  
At that time, Also rejoiced,  
And full of joy.  
Then laughter had a hold.  
Early women, and everyone,  
Who appeared nearby,  
Swam along, In a Sea  
Full of riches,  
For you, oh Maiden, alone.  
It is fitting then that we,  
Deprived of suffering,  
Also the taste, Of pleasure  
Once enjoy,  
And show ourselves glad.  
Surely it must be so:  
But first a glass of Wine  
To the bottom, Come, all around  
Emptied.  
Look there, that is for me.  
How strong is that Drink!  
I sing; but neighbor first takes  
That glass, In his own way  
Again poured,  
I have had it so.  
Now I sing joyfully out,  
Hey! listen to the sound  
Of my Song, Not very long,  
It is a tune  
That concludes a wish.  
First I wish this,  
Oh All Lovers know!  
For your youth, And your virtue,  
And the Purple  
That sits on your cheeks.  
That he who gives it all,  
As long as you live here,  
Remembers you, And grants  
Those gifts,  
That one here needs.  
That is food and drink, and beyond  
All that belongs to it:  
Rest and peace, Clothes too,  
And even more,  
Health continuously.  
And finally then  
A youthful virtuous Man,  
Who adorns you, And avenges you,  
And in everything  
Can satisfy you.

C.G. Kleyn.

label: birthday
translation:
Am I on the very spot,
Where a week ago,
According to someone among my companions,
Prayers were offered for this feast?
Do you say yes? Well, how has it changed today?
Was the crop then
Not dry, dull, and sorrowful?
Now again fresh, green, and fragrant?
Or was it a new spring!
Do I not see in flowers and fruit
That it is not fitting for us to mourn here?
Do I not smell a fresh air
Of the most pleasant scents,
Which one should know how to appreciate?
Do I not now feel my spirits
Thoroughly delighted and uplifted?
Do I not savor in full measure
Against the poisons of sorrow?
Do I not hear a sweet music
Of the swiftly winged creatures
Who united and alike
Chirp among the greenery,
To celebrate your annual season
In a pleasant manner?
Hear how sweetly they sing!
Each seems to compete
For the very highest prize.
Could the newly blossomed praise
Also reveal their joy so openly,
Without doubt your garden
Burst forth with joy, and the leaves
Sang happily on the birthday
Of their yearly gardener;
Who with so many tongues
Still would not have heard his praise fully sung.
Oh, how merry it is here!
Who among us will not honor this feast
With song and play,
(Signs of a joyful spirit)
Now that we learn from the birds,
Who through their lovely warbling,
Show that they are delighted,
How we now best amuse ourselves,
To taste the true fruit
Of such well-wished times?
What! What do you say? First wet the heart
With some cool moisture,
So the throat is more comfortable
To let this sound flow,
Now come on, I'll set the notes.
Sweet virtue, how tasty that is to me!
Are these Hippocratic drops
That make the soul jump within the body?
Or is it another drink?
Friends, hey, fulfill your duty
By expressing with a tune.
Or does this bore you too easily?
If so, play one or the other
That will not so lightly bore you:
Joyful before the time demands it,
Show yourself as a delighted person,
I will meanwhile with wishes
Do all that my duty requires.

label: birthday
translation:
This work, this labor is.
This is the power of the eternal covenant of salvation,
Sealed early with the blood of Christ,
This is no image, but truth;
No dark shadow, but light and clarity.

Let ear and lip remain uncircumcised,
As the heart, thus with little rain
The uncircumcision is slandered
By Israel, greatly corrupted.

Wickedness endures ahead, heavenly hand,
While its vengeance glows and burns,
Cuts short, slices through plague after plague,
Where land and church bear much hardship.

How here God's house, nor Canaan
Upright, no uncircumcised one can
Tolerate, God's word often proclaims,
Gilgal remains nameless and unmentioned.

Or is this work too bitter?
Oh think what Jesus suffered with pain
From the sharp circumcision, himself innocent,
But patiently for the guilt of humanity.

The law-bearer bore the burden of the law,
Which stains everyone with inherited guilt.
Who then avenges their yoke, where one lives holy,
Safe from disaster and inherited stain.
Appear, O Lord: Your Spirit appear
With salvation and help, no matter the pain;
The salvation, of which Your flesh scorns no seal,
According to Moses' strict rule.

That Moses' stone law, like a
Sharp circumcision, a conscience, of stone,
Your power hereby, Your blood and suffering,
Much evil, full of disgrace, teaches us to cut the way.

Thus we find our offense atoned for
By Your obedience and blood,
Poured early in drops, in streams
At the cross, to wash souls clean.

Thus wins a bride, without force,
As David served in the battlefield,
Nor flesh before from the Philistines,
But through Your first and last pains.

label: birthday
translation:
Now my Song-Goddess creates delight,  
To sing on that day of days,  
On which Amira saw the light.  
A day of great value to us,  
Since she now turned eighteen times:  
Which obliges everyone to joy.  

2. I bring no wreath to crown her head:  
The season forbids me to show a flower:  
What flower would stand so gloriously,  
Like the lilies and roses  
That blush on her dear cheeks,  
And do not fade through any wintertime.  

3. I wish that her beauty may bloom so long  
And always grow in full splendor  
To an infinite number of years;  
Yes, until she is tired of living here,  
She will then depart to the place  
Where all joy shall be perfect.  

4. And since she does not seem created  
To always sleep alone like this;  
I wish for her in short a Husband.  
This wish goes before all others,  
For there is nothing that can delight us so much  
As everything one can wish for.

label: birthday
translation:
How my Soul was moved on this day  
And as if lifted to Heaven with joy  
On this day: on which you were born,  
My second Soul, my chosen delight  
To be with us two, to be with us two,  
United in soul and mind,  
Through loving kisses, through loving kisses  
To nurture our love.  

2. You are the Beloved, whose first day caresses me  
A day that so pleasantly imagines to me  
The sweet pleasure of a thousand joyful days,  
What can, what can a Lover desire,  
Or satisfy more, or satisfy more,  
Than that he sees in youthful freshness  
Reborn, reborn  
The fountainhead of his joy.  

3. Your Son rose now twenty years ago:  
A Son whose radiance is worthily displayed,  
Adorned with soul-transporting ornaments.  
What mortal eye can tire of seeing it?  
I sing your Praise, I sing your Praise,  
O Worthy soul-Friend!  
But Song and Poetry, but Song and Poetry  
Fall far short there.  

4. I then only wish on your Birthday  
You as much blessing as ever a mortal saw,  
That your beauty may so grow,  
And year by year bloom in such splendor,  
That even Envy, that even Envy  
Must finally cry out,  
No Beauty on Earth, no Beauty on Earth  
Is like an Angel.  

5. Live long, live long, you who charm me alone  
And celebrate your joyful Birth.  
May you still long refresh me.  
Heaven will arrange for your days  
A long journey, a long journey  
Of years without number,  
Until we are, until we are,  
Where Eternal bliss shall be.

label: birthday
translation:
All who have come to the cheerful birthday cards with me,
Want to be cheerful. Their delicious wine
Is the medicine for worries.
One wishes him that the day
When the light first saw him,
May still be celebrated often.
Come, boy, quickly pour us
A large Italian glass.
Sir, with this drink
I wish you to remain young for a long time.
May cough and gout
Strike you late and rarely:
But may you drink the wine
Without harm before and after.

2. All the Italian glasses await further
On the cheerful card’s word,
From the cupboard, where they,
Washed, stood ready in pairs.
See friends, see how beautifully
I joyfully raise
The glass with the Raven.
Here comes the little Tulip now,
Friend, I bring it to you.
The glass without a foot,
I expect without a hat.
Come, pour it on my hand,
Once full to the brim:
It is for the good status
Of the dear Fatherland.

3. But I feel the power of the Rhine bird,
It boldly jumps into the bend.
My head, spinning wildly, spins like a top;
It’s close to me rolling over.
Those glasses are indeed many;
But for me, by my throat,
Half of them are too many.
I do want to stay
By the fire for half an hour longer.
But wine, believe me,
Must first be tried by a way.
Let us in peace and quiet,
Smoke a friendly pipe or two;
And with that
Quickly off to the tavern.

4. After this, I can (believe me on my honor)
No drop more.
Therefore, I ask, Sir, pour no more,
But give a small sip now.
I am not one to boast
For your sake,
Therefore I sing pure praise.
I am going to Bethlehem:
And with this, I thank Him,
Who has generously prepared
This joy for us.
May Heaven spare him
Eight times eight hereafter;
Then he is best to start
Of a pure hundred years.

label: birthday
translation:
O Girl in whose Years One counts three sixes,  
We come here gathered,  
To rejoice, To celebrate again the hour of your birth  
With sweet things, Give my singing Then attention.  
There is nothing more pleasant  
Than the Flower in its Youth;  
Nor does one find more capable,  
To taste the sweetest joy  
Than a Maiden Who on her cheeks carries  
Red Roses, Whose blushes please everyone.  
For the Flower, before that  
Gives delight to the eye,  
Will please no one more  
Than when it is dry and withered;  
And Maidens, after the art is lost, are without charm,  
As soon as simple, crease or wrinkle  
Furrows the forehead.  
So Girl of three sixes,  
Before you grow so old,  
That you resemble the grumpy berries  
In appearance and nature,  
I wish that  
You soon become tired and sated  
With Solitudes, and choose  
A better path.  
I mean the path of marriage,  
So that, before this day  
We look back again,  
One may visit you,  
In wedding's joyful splendor.  
Who wishes this more than I?  
All together, I then say Amen It must be so.

label: birthday
translation: Come, merry friends, come on! Tonight we must drink. We must drink to prosperity, from Kloris it may stand. That girl so kind celebrates this day. She is worthy of us, a cheerful laugh. Live long, O Kloris, rich, happy and old: and within ten months I wish you married.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
My very best friend!  
I find  
Your birthday is beginning;  
And therefore I come to sing  
A new birthday song,  
As you see.  
Oh, do not  
Take it badly, these are just things  
For joy, not for sorrow.  

2.  
You can see from the manner,  
How!  
The tune with good sense;  
In the biblical songbook,  
Or the little harp, well known;  
There you will find,  
This voice impressed;  
You will also find them, just search  
Only from the beginning to the end.  

3.  
You know very well, as I  
Consider and weigh, even at a glance,  
That year and wedding songs  
Are reheated food,  
As cold as ice,  
And low in value;  
Therefore add, with emphasis,  
This beautiful tune well.  

4.  
My dear lovely Kees!  
Read,  
Or sing it without fear;  
It surely will not bite you:  
It is neither Goliath,  
Nor hero of Gad,  
Who boldly stepped  
To frighten the Israelites:  
No! It is a defenseless leaf.  

5.  
Now finally to the point;  
I get  
Off track otherwise;  
I had to congratulate you, my friend!   
Why does a Philistine  
Have to be there?  
It does not seem  
He is dragged to it by the hair, oh my.  

6.  
Now listen to my words,  
Yes!  
I will make you understand them:  
I wish that we may celebrate many more birthdays for you  
Before death,  
Our foe,  
With his paw  
In the rotten grave takes us away.  
This I wish you, very sincerely.  

7.  
Now I am silent, quietly,  
Before I waste more ink,  
To tell of more fortune  
On such a Germanic lyre;  
It does not seem proper  
I let that flourish  
For all the other heroes  
Who wrote like a hammer.  

8.
