Still keep my love for my Phillis, my goddess,  
My all too faithful beloved hidden?  
Will she never know that I love her?  

3. Oh yes! My angel will know  
That through her charms  
She has long possessed my heart and mind:  
I live and die for her alone.  

4. What use is this fearful dread to me?  
I seek relief in my pain,  
Which has already reached its peak:  
If only she were once moved.  

5. For in a body so full of gifts  
It seems to me an impossibility  
That a soul is buried there,  
So full of insensitivity.  

6. Well then, I shall cease my complaints from now on,  
I think that she, through loving Phillis,  
Drives away all my former sorrow,  
And completely banishes it from my mind.

label: children
translation:
Farewell now, Clorenie,  
Since I see  
That I can never bend your mind to loyalty:  
It is for that reason, then,  
Why I banish you.  
Farewell then, Clorenie,  
For I do not know who  
Could love you so much,  
Who through her deeds shows  
That faithlessness dwells in her,  
While you rewarded me so.  

It is true that before  
You showed me much favor and love,  
But that time has passed,  
And your favor is also misplaced;  
I have learned from that.  

2. How little is it that one  
Can trust  
Probabilities?  
It seemed I was loved by you,  
But in the outcome, I find  
How blind I was:  
For your loose nature  
You paired  
With feigned morals,  
Which I did not see well before,  
Since I thought I was only  
Loved and no other.  
I now put aside  
Your love and all your flattery:  
What was once highly regarded,  
And carefully regarded by me,  
Is now despised by me.  

3. Your lot no longer affects me,  
Whether joy or sorrow,  
It cannot hurt me now;  
I have regained the golden freedom,  
Which I so foolishly once  
Laid at your feet,  
Through your evil conduct  
And faithlessness,  
My state was overturned,  
And I now think henceforth  
To guard myself well against that stallion,  
Upon which I have been betrayed,  
I am content with my fate;  
I taste the sweetness of freedom:  
I now know no love-sickness;  
I consider love a harmful herb,  
And say then, to conclude.  

4. Away, away now, foolish love,  
I banish you from my mind:  
Though before,  
I also was under the rule  
Of the blind god Cupid,  
I now disregard his arrow.  
Your sweetness is only sour,  
Your joy of no duration,  
What pleasure can it be,  
Where one always sits and sighs,  
And this is only the fruit  
Of your imagined contentment.  
How such a life is,  
Woven with discontent and disaster,  
Who flatters themselves with many favors,  
And then, through your conduct,  
Finds themselves misguided too late.

label: children
translation:
Elisa, I seek to offer my greatest treasure  
To you, who have moved my senses  
To love you,  
So often I wished for the fortune  
To prove my love  
And to praise your beauty.  
2. Well then! Receive my heart completely  
Bound to your virtues;  
It was willingly sent to you  
If only I had a more worthy jewel;  
But I find no gift so pure and clear  
To offer you in this New Year.  
3. May this gift not be scorned!  
Or has my soul ever moved you?  
This heart will always accompany your enchanting eyes  
Wherever you go,  
And show that my love alone  
Admires your radiance and no other.

label: children
translation:
My mother told me every day,  
That I must carry myself wisely:  
That I may not see any gentlemen,  
No matter how much they please me:  
But one of them appeals to me greatly.  
That is well done: That is well done:  
He kissed me six times one night. The last  
That was still the best: That was still the best.  

2. I lived a life according to my wishes,  
And am also quite content:  
I kiss, and see what I love:  
Do I have no reason for that?  
I drink my glass, then go to my lover:  
That is well done: That is well done:  
What happens there I leave to your guessing: Yet  
It is even better: It is even better.

label: children
translation:
Who came to wake me so soon  
Recently in the green grass,  
Where I in sweet rest  
Created my desire,  
I lay beside a clear stream,  
Where I had a sweet dream,  
Whose noble image  
Always caressed my senses.  

2. I thought I saw a joyful crowd  
Sweetly joking with each other,  
Where the Huntress Goddess  
Stood in the midst:  
And the Muses, three times three,  
Immediately joined in there,  
They sang in praise,  
It seemed like a paradise.  

3. Lady Venus came stepping in  
As if Apollo were playing the harp,  
God Bacchus with his mates  
Took the first place,  
Little Cupid flew around,  
Each Nymph shot an arrow,  
Thus I remained standing,  
And wondered how it would go.  

4. Among this joyful crowd  
I also noticed my beloved:  
My heart and soul ignited,  
When I spoke to her.  
I enjoyed my full delight,  
I kissed her lovingly,  
And this pleased,  
Also my second soul.  

5. When the joy seemed at its height  
It was then that she almost disappeared,  
Sleep took its flight  
Upon me:  
Under this melody,  
My dream soon passed as well;  
I stood and saw,  
And made this sorrowful lament.  

6. O delight full of cunning,  
Who are so hastily missed,  
You have enticed me in sleep,  
Out of pure spite.  
Are the dreams then so sweet,  
Just think how it must be,  
When one there can  
Taste the truths.

label: children
translation:
The Min warns me to think of something,  
Climeen, to give you for New Year  
On this joyful day:  
Where dare I firmly burden my spirit.  
2. For looking around in all corners,  
I do not know in the world anything suitable  
To seek out for your beauty,  
That can be pleasing to you.  
3. How then shall I please you?  
Come, Love! whisper softly in my ear  
What I shall carry to my Angel,  
Where dare I best enchant her soul.  
4. There he says: 'I have already found it,  
My heart, the most worthy of my possessions,  
Was sent in this paper;  
So that it may fall at your shining feet.  
5. Receive this offering worthy Angel,  
She awaits your favor at the altar of Love.  
I give you, for which I myself long:  
Oh grant me also such a New Year.  
6. Thus I became bold for my life,  
As you have honored me with your love,  
To give your heart and hand in faith,  
This is my only desire.'

label: children
translation:
My Phillis, Ah! who can  
Yet refrain from  
Being touched by your power?  
Whoever once looks at you,  
Immediately feels a loving glow  
And falls within your realm.  

2. The Scythe, oh God of Love,  
Cannot reach  
The heart if it does not see her eyes,  
With which she shoots glances;  
They prevent us: for her power  
Is greater than yours, Cupid.  

3. The Spring Rosebud,  
Which we see so delicately  
Blooming on your cheeks,  
Has ignited many a heart,  
In flames of love,  
Full of sweet love-sorrow.  

4. I am silent about your lovely mouth,  
Which has pierced me through  
With kisses from your lips,  
That I consider them all  
True love-tokens,  
They rival red coral.  

5. The curls of your head,  
Whose shine outshines gold;  
Those curling waves,  
Which mostly  
Have buried me in your tender embrace,  
Are Venus’ love-bonds.  

6. But your great spirit  
Keeps me above all  
Bound in your chains;  
Yet I still boast of the day,  
When I softly cast my freedom  
Into your beautiful hands.  

7. Then it is said not: one can  
Refrain  
From being touched by the power of love;  
For whoever sees my Phillis,  
Must immediately blaze with love,  
And honor her realm.

label: children
translation:
Rosaura, gentler than a Rose,  
Whose little blush glowed in the earliest times  
Soothed with lovely Ambrosia,  
Which can delight our little souls.  

2. It is the little flower that now on its leaf  
So cheerfully flaunts in its shoots:  
But if it is caught by time,  
It will pronounce its own sentence.  

3. It will die in its sorrowful green,  
And let flower and leaves fall:  
The Rose, which was the whole point,  
Loses its lustrous rays.  

4. Rosaura, what I may pray for.  
Oh! always keep this in your mind,  
Come grant me that joyful day once.  
The torch of joy of love.  

5. May your little flower never wither,  
Although your years may also increase:  
Continuously grant the counterbalance  
That the number of years has taken away.

label: children
translation:
If I could escape love,  
I would no longer sigh,  
Nor endure the burden  
Of my time in misery,  
But since I am born  
To be consumed by love,  
I remain who I am,  
As I know no other.  

2. What can I do then?  
There she must yield  
The goddess on her throne,  
To the power of Venus’ son:  
I will with offering hands,  
Burn passionate fires  
At the altar of love  
And the blonde foam-goddess.  

3. I then live in your bonds,  
You hold my heart in your hands,  
My precious little angel,  
Whom I only love:  
It is your sweet glances,  
Received by me in love.  
The rays of your eye  
Moved my soul to love.  

4. Those finely drawn bows  
Of your loving little eyes  
Grant me a sweetness  
That melts my heart:  
When the loving flutter  
From that azure heaven  
Strikes my little soul  
With a deep wound.  

5. The red coral tips  
Of your sweet little lips  
Often make me sigh  
Fainting from pleasure:  
When I press a moist kiss  
On the edges  
Of your ivory teeth,  
And pluck the fruits of lovers.  

6. But when I caress your bosom,  
Fragrant as the blossom  
Of pear or apricot,  
You blush like a rose:  
And show me how your cheeks  
Through love’s amusements  
Will one day be colored,  
If that fortune befalls us.  

7. Then let us, separated  
From all turmoil, both of us  
Enjoy ourselves here alone,  
In our love’s delight:  
That knows no hardships,  
Once the misfortune is suffered,  
Happiness comes again,  
Love grows in calamity and hardship.  

8. Come, let us once more kiss  
And quench our flames,  
Climene, come, oh come,  
Embrace your bridegroom:  
Want to entwine me in your arms,  
Sinking away in the sweetness  
Of chaste love's glow.

label: children
translation:
Dianier, joy of my life.  
Dawn, who gives me a light  
More beautiful than the Sun can give,  
Through your loving face  
You make me bathe in joy  
All my pain and sad sigh,  
As long as I live in your grace,  
Are turned into soul-delight.  

2. When I open my eyes to your eyes,  
It is as if my soul has flown away  
And is shown to Heaven:  
The body rocked as in sleep.  
A slumber comes to enjoy,  
That relieves my dull spirit  
And frees me from sorrow,  
Through that lovely sunlight.  

3. Sweet mouth whose tender tips  
Defy the most beautiful coral in color,  
I press your dear lips once,  
I smell a sweet Nectar fragrance;  
I taste the sweetest of honeycombs:  
So sweet that I die from it  
When the soul has left me,  
Sweeter life I acquire.

label: children
translation:
Well Cloris, how can you be so detached by nature?  
How have you changed your mind so much?  
At first you forced me to love you  
And now you regard my face as unworthy;  
Is it enough for you that you have  
Captured my heart in love’s net by your cunning?  
Since you now seem to fear my arrival:  
I hardly know what this will be,  
Or why you flee.  

2. If you were happy without me,  
Why then do you spend my freedom so fiercely?  
Why add so many winks and sweet kisses  
To all your kindnesses?  
Yes, right from the start,  
When I found myself in your loving company,  
You yourself gave these to me;  
And now that you have received me,  
You put me aside.  

3. Well then! Do you dislike it all?  
When I have given you no reason at all;  
Have I been driven out of your favor?  
If your changeable nature displeases me,  
I will not love you against my will,  
No, I demand a return for my love,  
And if I cannot win you thus,  
Then I will stop  
Loving this fairground doll.

label: children
translation:
Away Clorimeen, who before
Enticed me all day through your favor into your lap:
Away Clorine, for I flee
Your beauty, like a rock.
Away, Phillis, away,
Although you have deceived me before,
I will never be answered by you,
Thus my love is smothered:
Away, Phillis, away,
My dear Cloris who
Takes the prize of three.
2. Her beloved face
Gives sweeter light
Than Phoebus' love in Heaven's clear expanse:
A glance from my Cloris can
Dull all that shine.
Away altogether.
My Cloris alone holds the field
I am completely in her power,
Your might is defeated:
Away, altogether,
My Cloris, my goddess,
Alone rules my senses.

label: children
translation:
I am a good Welshman,  
Who has nothing, and cannot live:  
I have just recently come from Rome,  
And have brought my chest with me.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities:  

2. Now I open my chest:  
I say it is not for sale;  
The King of Sweden on his horse  
The Roman Emperor with his sword.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities:  

3. There is Czar Piet, the great Muscovite;  
The great Turk with all his suite;  
Prince Eugene with his army,  
Who drives the Turk back and forth.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities:  

4. The King of France in his carriage  
With Monsieur de Conti, look how proudly he rides;  
The Cardinal, and the Pope of Rome,  
They have on their heads the triple crown.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities;  

5. The King of England on his throne  
The Prince of Wales, his eldest son  
The Parliament, and the Exchequer  
The bill from the excise makes a great disturbance  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities;  

6. The King of Spain with the infant,  
The Prince of Brazil at his right hand;  
The Queen of Portugal,  
She is chosen by her spouse.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities;  

7. The Prince of Orange with his bride.  
Herewith my chest of rarities is finished.  
Cathryn, and Margriet, with their gallant,  
They go to bed hand in hand.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities,  
La belle Catharyn, Charming Margariet.  
Beautiful toys, and beautiful rarities.

label: children
translation:
I swear that Cupid’s power  
Could never affect my heart,  
Meanwhile day and night  
I pine for Climene’s eyes:  
While time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love,  
While time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.  

2. Proud in indifference  
I dared to defy the most beautiful,  
And I boasted vanity,  
To be free from lovesickness:  
But time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love;  
But time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.  

3. I thought it was foolishness,  
That I now call true love,  
And I considered it madness,  
That a beauty pierced me through;  
But time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love;  
But time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.  

4. From the moment I beheld  
My Climene’s beautiful eyes,  
I bowed to her authority  
And acknowledged her power;  
For time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love,  
For time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.  

5. It does not surprise me that Climene  
Can ignite me with love,  
And through her charms,  
Break my petrified heart;  
While time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love,  
While time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.  

6. Most beautiful, who pierces my soul  
Through your sweet glances of love,  
Once grant me your dear mouth;  
Your love can oblige me:  
While time shows me,  
That sooner or later one must love,  
While time shows me,  
That one must never swear anything.

label: children
translation:
When Thirsis found himself in the forest,  
He spoke to the one who had wounded him,  
I die, my sweet dear Maiden,  
I die, my sweet dear Child.  
Iris, however, take my sighs,  
For no one can heal me,  
But you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
But you, my sweet dear Child.  

For where can one ever find such a Maiden,  
Who has so pleased my soul,  
As you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
As you, my sweet dear Child.  

This is confirmed by the Rosebush,  
Which stands on the lovely cheeks  
Of you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
Of you, my sweet dear Child.  

The Ivory and Lily-white,  
That rests on the smooth Forehead  
Of you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
Of you, my sweet dear Child.  

No Carnation in the whole world  
Is more beautiful than the red mouth  
Of you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
Of you, my sweet dear Child.  

Much more beautiful than the Dawn,  
Is the soul-enchanting face  
Of you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
Of you, my sweet dear Child.  

If Jove had a human eye,  
He would surely take it from the Heavenly Bow  
For you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
For you, my sweet dear Child.  

No matter how much the noble wine is valued,  
I would much rather have clear water  
With you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
With you, my sweet dear Child.  

I smile at someone else's favor or pressure  
And let my happiness depend  
On you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
On you, my sweet dear Child.  

Many lovers seek money and wealth,  
But no one satisfies my desire  
Like you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
Like you, my sweet dear Child.  

Oh yes, my chaste Soul-Friend,  
I only strive for mutual love  
From you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
From you, my sweet dear Child.  

If only you were my only possession,  
I could not rest a minute  
From you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
From you, my sweet dear Child.  

Until I shall end my days,  
No one shall be my love, my All,  
But you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
But you, my sweet dear Child.  

When death has pierced me  
And that from too great love  
To you, my sweet dear Maiden,  
To you, my sweet dear Child.  

It will surely stand on my tomb,  
Thirsis has perished through love,  
Alas! For Iris, the sweet maiden,  
Alas! For Iris, the sweet child.

label: children
translation:
When I sit by my Phillis,  
I never think of my sorrowful ailments:  
When she lets her eye, as black as pitch,  
Full of soul-conquering fire, rest favorably on my valleys,  
Then I defy doom with my fate,  
Then I defy doom with my fate,  
Then my soul swims in little loves.  
If I give her a kiss once,  
She presses me with her lips,  
Thus Off' ...... ren,  
Thus Off' ... ren w'aan the God of Love.

label: children
translation:
I had promised to give you something  
For your Saint Nicholas, here beside  
Thus goes what I have bought.  
At first, I hardly knew what to think,  
What I best could give my Cloris,  
But finally, this is chosen.  

2. Here, Miss, then, a jar,  
That with its sugar-sweet little hand  
Holds the chamber pot for its lady.  
She, for the least as far as I can guess,  
Will pee as sweet as Spanish wine,  
Because she is made of sugar.  

3. Her sweetness is also inward,  
Her sweet humor is so steadfast,  
That though she may kiss or bite,  
She will never grumble against that,  
Not at all complain about it,  
She is very good-natured by temperament.  

4. Therefore I would not marry her  
For fear of bad or wicked women,  
I would rather dare to propose to her,  
That she would not deceive for long,  
But also to have such a sugary wife,  
Just as I have arranged today.  

5. But I will not fear any longer,  
I ask that you kindly accept  
Such a heart, just as I  
Present it to you now,  
And if this pleases you,  
Then I am very pleased myself.  

6. Since I have not delivered it sooner  
And you have waited somewhat long,  
Now you receive this little song,  
Because if you can learn it by heart,  
To confine this sweet wife:  
She is not soon tired of peeing.

label: children
translation:
O sorrowful suffering! that my heart  
Is constantly plunged into new grief  
And that my soul, with pain upon pain,  
Is overwhelmed by your force:  
Alas! what do I not suffer through your cruel power  
With which you oppress me.  

2. I miss all my peace with Phyllis:  
My desire has completely fled,  
My joyful fire is extinguished  
By the absence of her dear eyes.  
The most beautiful sunshine becomes darkness to me  
When I miss my beloved.  

3. I know no joy, except  
When I may be with my Phyllis.  
She can heal the sadness of before  
Again by the slightest favor,  
If I pluck a kiss from her dear lips,  
Then all my distress quickly flees.  

4. Then flee, sorrowful sighs! flee  
To Phyllis; cause of my lament:  
Witness what sadness and grief  
I must endure because of her absence:  
Witness how faithfully I, despite all my pain,  
Still persevere in her love.

label: children
translation:
Pan once sat in the reeds and crooned
Though he played of no delight,
But because
He could neither win his love, I mean Syringa,
Nor compel her,
To join him,
Or rather to sow too many seeds
In her little patch of mixed salad,
For she constantly scorned his love.

2. While he thus sat alone lamenting
His torments, she came hunting by,
Whereupon he, ignited again by news,
Assaulted her: And then, on the left side
Of his maid, after a preface,
He recited this speech,
With much grace.

3. Syringa, can Pan not please you?
Incline your ears properly,
But listen to
My complaint: if you would entirely torment me,
Not forgive me,
Would you remain undaunted?
Oh! how gladly, my sweet plump
Maid, would I prick you once:
It's a question.

4. Pan, well now? said Syringa,
To sing about these things here?
Leave such a game aside:
I will never allow it:
All your talk does no good,
Praise it freely,
Your seed, but just let it prickle:
For I consider all your speaking
As nothing.

5. I want that, said he,
I do not want to speak
Of your tricks,
I have it all in hand,
Keep your word, do not let it be broken,
Nor stop me,
I shall open
Now your gate,
But (oh me) before he learned the desired
Art, Jupyn transformed her.

label: children
translation:
What Duke can one find here on Earth,  
Who possesses so much wealth?  
Or what passion can be combined  
With a sweetness as love gives?  
Nothing soothes our senses,  
As lovely love:  
There is no sweetness  
That so satisfies  
Our soul:  
Than when Love binds two souls;  
When one loves and is loved in return:  
This is a matter that surpasses  
All the pleasures  
Of the world.  

2. Ambition was deemed  
To be the greatest power:  
But it seems to me that on the contrary,  
Love must be attributed  
The greatest power;  
Who can resist  
The weight of love?  
For everyone yields  
Before its might.  
Is it not true that many a prince and lord  
Lay down their crown and scepter,  
Before the altar of Love,  
When they have only tried their weapon?  

3. Just as ambition is never satisfied  
With a proud crown:  
So a lover knows no law or limit,  
For his desires.  
If he once gets his wishes,  
He is still not content,  
He hooks anew for another time;  
Yes even the more  
That he obtains what he asks for,  
The more his desire ignites,  
And in his breast, a new thirst  
Is born for more.  

4. This I notice daily,  
My dear Phillis: for  
If I happen to, just  
Press one or two kisses  
On your sweet hand,  
I wish to pluck one  
From your mouth:  
If that were permitted, I immediately feel  
That my desire soars higher,  
I wish you to give me one in return:  
If I were kissed, I feel that my pleasure  
Is not yet fulfilled.  

5. Yet not that I am ungrateful  
For your favors: No,  
My dearest Phillis, I acknowledge  
With gratitude all that  
I have enjoyed from you:  
The small as well as the great,  
And I will always, despite misfortune and regret,  
With all diligence show you that I am and always will be  
Your lover,  
Even though the cruel fate  
Separates me from you,  
For eternity.

label: children
translation:
How long does the changing of fashions last,  
Every day one sees something strange;  
New style seems to urge Philida,  
To take part in that oddity,  
It doesn't cost much, it's beautiful with one;  
I am quite pleased with it:  
I like a lot to change sometimes,  
In that I differ not from others,  
That passion seems common to us all.  

2. Philida increased the lovely gifts,  
Given to her by Nature,  
She makes all who see her into slaves  
And enlarges the fire of love  
Thereby in my heart at once:  
I am quite pleased with it;  
I like a lot to change sometimes,  
In that I differ not from others,  
That passion seems common to us all.  

3. While then, Philida! our whole life  
Consists of change,  
Give me your heart, I shall give you mine  
Thus we benefit each other  
And satisfy our desire at once:  
I am quite pleased with it:  
I like a lot to change sometimes,  
In that I differ not from others,  
That passion seems common to us all.  

4. Let us then steadily stick  
To inconstancy:  
Prevent me from coming to sorrow over it,  
Or from deceiving myself  
With only temporary hope,  
Then I am very well pleased:  
And like a lot to change sometimes,  
In that I differ not from others,  
That passion seems common to us all.

label: children
translation:
Do not think, Phillis: that my absence  
Will ever extinguish my flame:  
Never again shall I be so cowardly:  
I defy my misfortune,  
To uproot you from my heart,  
My loyalty stands firm  
Like a rock against the raging waves:  
And although I miss you,  
I will keep a heart  
That is always yours.  

2. If only you remain steadfast  
To a word once given by you,  
My love will also be endless,  
You who once enchanted me,  
Shall also remain my mistress,  
And my sole guardian,  
Although time, which smothers all,  
Steadily urges us to change,  
We shall forever,  
Equally fervently, love each other,  
Until death separates us.  

3. But what use is your care,  
While I, my angel,  
In my departure want to leave my heart  
As a pledge of my love,  
Then I also let you carry it with you,  
Cease your needless complaints;  
You reign forever in my mind.  
Phillis! then do not let my absence  
Dampen your glow,  
It will be punishment enough for me,  
That I must thus miss you.

label: children
translation:
Young lady, I pray, say, ta, la, la, &c.  
And ask me sincerely,...  
Do you not wish to marry me...? To be once...        Houp za za.  
2. I certainly promise it, ta, la, la, &c.  
Is that so? Come on, I will...  
Show you as a man,...  
That I can be decent        Houp za za.  
3. But how! Are you ashamed! Ta la la &c.  
That is something unbecoming,...  
Oh, oh, I can see it,...  
You know nothing of the game        Houp za za.  
4. Where it is familiar to you, young lady, ta, la, la &c.  
Just like me; you would...  
Say, come again...  
The sweet tune of        Houp za za.  
5. Do you think I deceive you, ta, la, la, &c.  
Or that I stand here and lie:  
No, certainly not [keep on]  
No sweeter joy than in        Houp za za.  
6. But you do not believe, ta, la, la, &c.  
And remain deaf to me,...  
You never taste the sweetness of love,...  
Which dwells in        Houp za za.  
7. Therefore see what you do, ta, la, la, &c.  
But do not reject the sweetness,...  
Which you, Leonoor,...  
Cannot obtain except through        Houp za za.

label: children
translation:
If Love has once captured us,
Whether through the eye of a blonde or a brunette;
The feeling of that pain
Always remains in our heart,
And one becomes increasingly entangled in the net of love
And very distressed.
Joy and laughter during the day are lost, and the night

Is often spent restlessly,
Most of the time is wasted in a fading dissatisfaction,
In vain tears, one seeks solace in forests and retreats,
Or in the air:
Awake like a man,
Love then takes control,
With my fresh wine, I choose the glass and the jug.
The love of a girl
Is indeed rightly said,
Nothing more than fantasy and pure vanity
Of vanity.

label: children
translation:
If Doris, now she is Cloris  
Might be found alone:  
He would tell the girl then a story,  
How love, that loose rogue,  
Has hurt him terribly in his health,  
Has shot and stabbed him;  
But I will catch that blind one,  
For those tricks,  
Just you watch.  

2. I am like the little boy,  
Who sees himself pictured  
In the almanac.  
Through his whims, full of bitterness,  
And my heart  
Full of bruising pains—no!  
I mean arrows, seem like  
A pincushion; meanwhile  
I sit admiring:  
I have patience and silence  
In my pain.  

3. Yet my Cloris, if you Doris,  
Do not soon come to your senses;  
Dear angel, that rascal  
Will still torment me  
With his torch:  
And then perhaps so on,  
Merciless and unruly,  
Burning like charred meat,  
Or to ashes; I’m willing  
To take part in this game.

label: children
children
translation:
O Freedom! Farewell forever;  
for I must leave you.  
I am carried away by sorrowful torment;  
They now force me to hate.  
I am conquered by love,  
Therefore I banish you from my mind,  
I must submit to destiny.  
Man often stands like a ball  
Before the blows of misfortune,  
And must content himself...  

2. A lover's heart is like a ship,  
Now low, then at the clouds.  
Often fearing a blind cliff  
And bottomless whirlpools.  
His mind is here, his heart is there;  
Now happy, then fearing danger,  
He wrings his pale hands.  
Tortured on the sea of love;  
Unknowing where I shall land...

label: children
translation:
O Freedom, which for a long time
Had to wander as an exile:
To you my heart is dedicated again,
Although I had to do without you for long.
I see you again at the end.
O Freedom! now that I return
From lovelessness without trace:
Now that you lie before me shining bright,
Illuminated, my sight brightens
And I feel joy from within.

2. A skipper in a heavy tempest
And countless winds,
Is slowly afraid of death,
Can find no way out:
But when he sees his own coast again,
He enjoys the rest once more,
The rest can please him.
Thus I find myself freed from sorrow:
Now that I behold your face again,
I see my joy reborn.

label: children
translation:
Hey Amarantje! tell me,
Why do you cover the rose tips
Of your perfect bosom still?
While you grant your cheeks and your lips
To your lover:
Or do you think that bare from dew,
No eye will be there?
2. Where does this change come from?
You often allowed me, during our loves,  
That hand and eye roamed freely,  
Around your ivory thighs;  
Or do you not know, beauty,  
That all your limbs are mine,  
As a lover's own.

label: children
translation:
At Somersche Days,  
In Bosschen en Hagen,  
Thus I try to flee  
The torments of Love;  
And if I have no mistress,  
I kiss the bottle,  
And if no glass helps, then I take ten,  
And if I kiss no girl,  
Then I smoke a pipe:  
No one shall ever see me sorrowful.

2nd Verse.  
For the wine cures my lovesickness  
When I pine for a fair lady:  
And the wine is my medicine,  
When I seem somewhat melancholy.  
But when I am  
With Phillis and,  
Seated kissing,  
My torment may be forgotten,  
Then I have never regretted it,  
Though I first suffered some grief and sorrow:  
I reckon, I reckon not.

label: children
translation:
I will then go away, Clorimeen,  
While you do not want to love me,  
I will then go away, Clorimeen,  
I am content with my fate;  
Although I remained faithful to you,  
All my sighs, all my words,  
Although I remained faithful to you,  
I know I can do nothing about it,  
It’s no use, because your hatred  
Lasts forever, to my regret;  
Therefore I banish you from my mind,  
All my desire is extinguished:  
Long enough kissed a block.  

2. I flee, the restlessness,  
It has been long enough suffered.  
To always live without hope,  
I flee, the restlessness,  
Clorimeen, I separate from you:  
You have used me long enough.  
Because your snares frighten me,  
And my heart, that with pain,  
For your cruelty lay trembling,  
Now frees itself like a man;  
And goes happily to dance.  

3. What is all this commotion for?  
I just want to speak plainly with you,  
What is all this commotion for,  
If I will not have you?  
I do not seek to force you:  
Much less do I want to overpower you;  
I do not want to force you: No!  
Because then I would act against reason.  
I will watch myself, and respect you,  
Although you have cruelly let me suffer pain:  
I thank you very much for that honor:  
Clorimeen, there are more of them.

label: children
translation:
To whom shall I turn?  
Where can I find comfort or healing?  
To whom shall I turn?  
Who will soften the blows?  
Love appoints me a tormentor.  
Love, which before  
Was a source of sweetness  
And pleasure to me,  
Has sadly deceived me.  
Who would ever think  
That you, my Rosemond,  
Would so violate loyalty?  
And break the bond  
That you once promised,  
When my sincere soul  
Believed your false tongue,  
And took your appearance for truth.  

2. Alas! How bitterly rage  
Love, hate, and regret,  
Alas! How bitterly rage  
Those fierce spiritual storms?  
Oh Heaven! Oh, what pain  
My soul has endured in bitter blows!  
Who will, who will carry my heart  
Through the triumph?  
Hatred? No! Despite the pain  
And the suffering I must endure,  
And those who so fiercely  
Oppose my soul from all sides,  
I love her nonetheless  
And will always love her;  
Oh Love! you remain always  
The master of my senses:  
My heart remains devoted to you.  

3. Return, Rosemond! Return,  
Ere I perish through sorrow;  
Return, Rosemond! Return,  
I kneel down full of respect  
And beg your mercy;  
Let once the sunbeams  
Of your perfect gaze  
Clear away the mists;  
You are the light of my life.  
Come, delight of my life!  
Your lover is ready  
To forgive all  
That you once did wrong to him.  
What can still frighten you?  
Let your charms  
Refresh me as before;  
Thus ends my lament.

label: children
translation:
Let your little lambs wander white from the pasture  
Through the green leaves,  
Fairest Nymph, who my complaint  
Sings and scorns in the wind.  
Let us go beneath the dense fruit,  
May my soul once catch its breath!  
Who then would measure my joy?  
If my Phillis became my bride.  
If... If... If...  
If my Phillis became my bride.  

2. Sit here down in the field,  
Under the shade of the trees;  
See how it swells with love,  
As Cupid sets his bow.  
See how the clear water flows  
And how gently the tender stream  
Kisses and babbles along its banks,  
And then dances before the wind.  

3. Hear how the nightingale flatters,  
To entice his Gayke;  
It seems he sings to us in his language,  
“Love now, dear hearts, it is the right time.”  
All love, whether human or beast;  
Otherwise, the whole universe would be lost,  
Venus was surely born for us  
From the wild, fierce sea.  
From... From... From...  
From the wild, fierce sea.  

4. Would a nymph now so beautiful,  
So perfect and truly virtuous,  
Not even listen to the gods  
And sometimes follow her deeds?  
Ah! My angel would be gentle:  
That those soft and tender limbs  
Might once bend to reason.  
Hey! Do hear me once, bee.  
Hey... Hey... Hey...  
Hey! Hear me once, my bee.  

5. When I gather honey kisses  
(Or love deceives me)  
My great happiness appears,  
When I press the red mouth.  
The little soul slips out to the bosom,  
And goes resting on the lips,  
When my powers fail;  
Ah! My voice loses its sound.  
Ah... Ah... Ah...  
Ah! My voice loses its sound.

label: children
translation:
Phillis, when you last went through your songs,  
The whole neighborhood was drawn to your little throat. Phillis,  
I was startled with fear,  
Almost overwhelmed, while you  
Smiled at me from the side. Wierd.  
  
2. That smile and glance of your eyes  
Had a great power over my soul;  
That shyness and modesty  
Brought delight to my desire:  
That mouth I would gladly have kissed.  
  
3. After that shyness had left my soul,  
My eye boldly looked at you:  
I then, free from  
The overly cruel restraint,  
Kissed one cheek, and the other cheek:  
  
4. Oh, what joy, in reading those roses:  
The little kiss made your cheeks both blush:  
My soul kept  
The guard at your mouth,  
Lest you make a single sigh.  
  
5. I will always praise your mouth and sweet throat,  
Like a bee loves honey more than flowers:  
Do not sing, or offer  
Me your mouth, or cheek,  
The little kiss adds to the song of love.

label: children
translation:
Might I now kiss my Phillis once more  
And quench my fire on her cheeks?  
Then no sorrow  
Would distress my heart,  
Which grew heavier from the suffering.  
2. Might I now see those rose-like cheeks,  
Once more move through songs.  
The sound, that came  
From her sweet throat,  
Gave new life to my flame.

label: children
translation:
Ah Phillis! Did you know what is now fighting within my heart,  
And what pain my soul has endured, now that I love you:  
My compassion nearly touched your soul  
As friend, or rather goddess:  
Then you would see how shame restrains my soul  
Always against my passion:  
Then you would see how highly I regard you,  
That I do not want to reveal my love's complaint  
To you thoughtlessly.  
Therefore, let one eye of mine,  
Like sunlight from above,  
Once fall upon me, oh, dry my tears,  
Then I fall again  
At your feet,  
And show that I am your long beloved tenderly.  
Oh! Grant me the honor.

label: children
translation:
Last I was in my solitude, (Bis)
I thought how best to spend my time,
Alone, Iris I swear to you.
Alone, Iris I tell you.

2. Now I thought at my first time:
Then again about the distinction
Of you, Iris I swear to you.
Of you, Iris I tell you.

3. Until it came back to me,
How once I held you in my arms,
My Love: Iris I swear to you.
My Love: Iris I tell you.

4. And that she did not take it badly at all;
But rather very gently my dear Lamb
What is this? Iris I swear to you.
Is it serious? Iris I tell you.

5. But alas! what good is this thought to me?
My beautiful Nymph, who laughs with me
Remains cruel: Iris I swear to you.
Remains cruel: Iris I tell you.

6. But know well that if you laugh with me
Your Cupid will gain power,
You will flee, Iris I swear to you.
No Arrow; Iris I tell you.

7. Fulfill my wish, dear Angel
And once grant me your Mermaid,
But softly, Iris I swear to you.
She laughs; Iris I tell you.

8. Well Lover as I know your love,
I blind myself to your will;
I give you, Iris I swear to you.
My Faithfulness, Iris I tell you.

9. Happy we are forever
And laugh at the bitter Envy,
Together, Iris I tell you.
Together, Iris I tell you.

10. Thus we always live uninhibited
And always love each other through struggle,
Together, Iris I swear to you.
Together, Iris I tell you.

label: children
translation:
Climeen! my Life-Sun, when  
Shall I enjoy happiness again,  
That your eye, as before,  
Shall shoot its rays upon me,  
When shall I, as before,  
See the end of my sorrows,  
When shall I, as before,  
See your sweet face, Climeen.  

2. Hardly had the sorrowful farewell  
Slipped from my lips,  
When my sad soul, too fiercely  
Stricken by grief and disaster,  
Wished again, as before,  
For your sweet face, Climeen.  

3. You, whom I alone love,  
Are the target of my efforts;  
You, my only idol,  
Enthrall me by your sweet power:  
Make it so that I, as before,  
See the sunlight of your eyes.  
Make it so that I, as before,  
See your sweet face, Climeen.

label: children
translation:
No one needs to find me strange,  
(Although I am often free)  
That I have not yet been seen to marry,  
It is only out of mischief:  
For the one I see last,  
I mostly offer my service,  
Now to one, and then to another;  
For I mean to change every time,  
Provided I flee marriage.  

2. Yesterday I saw a brunette,  
Who pleased me very much:  
I bowed to her laws,  
I called her my second soul:  
But when she was gone,  
That love was almost over:  
For I thought, there are still others,  
And I always try to change:  
The newest one pleases me best.  

3. Today I have again set my sights  
On a pretty blonde:  
But I will not love her long,  
She does not hold me by force:  
She remains in my mind for as long,  
Until I find another again.  
I often exchange one for the other,  
And today, I intend to change.  
I do not want to wither due to love.  

4. In the evening after dinner,  
I call her the dearest I love,  
In the morning I have forgotten again  
And go back to my own mind;  
Therefore she is not right,  
Who believes all that is said;  
I court one, and keep it with the others,  
Because I mean to change every time.  
One like this is far too bad.

label: children
translation:
A lover suffers many pains,  
But it does not touch his heart;  
For if he is once free between the two,  
He gets his old troubles back  
And complains of his sorrow from the start,  
So that I myself do not know  
What in love's marriage  
Is best: slave, or free,  
Or rather none of the two.  

2. I was lately reproached,  
And it grieved me deeply,  
That Rose no longer loved me  
And truly I did not want to know;  
But if that beauty herself says it to me,  
Then I go again to another girl  
And once more get a blue mark  
From spiteful Clorimene;  
Yet I am not willing to be content.  

3. Thus one toils in this life,  
Driven by hope or fear;  
But once one grasps Hymen's thread,  
The card is forfeited;  
There one trembles on all sides.  
Oh! Sad thought for the mind!  
Outwardly one seems content enough,  
But inside it is alas and woe!  
O Libera nos Domine.

label: children
translation:
Away foolish Love with all your sorrow,  
I live full of joy and pleasure, happiness as you see.  
I will never surrender myself to you,  
But honor Bacchus as long as I live,  
So I live here with pleasure;  
A fresh glass of wine,  
That is truly the meaning of my life:  
But not  
Like you, constantly mourning and sitting in grief.  

All your sighs are merely wind,  
You know no state in which one is when one is truly loved.  
One must live through loving;  
I gladly want to offer Bacchus’ drink to you,  
Yet here I am also inclined,  
To always serve a young lady by the cool wine,  
With whom  
We can settle our hearts well through kisses and caresses.

label: children
translation:
Is it true that you love me?  
As I must guess from your anger?  
Then Glory Rose, let the wind  
Of your love secrets  
Only reveal how you  
Then sigh beside me?  
Ah! Glory Rose Love, let your eyes  
Only reveal to me how you  
Then sigh beside me,  
Or love's great power.  

2. Because I, she said, love you fully  
And reasonably, my Floris,  
I fear once to stain the dear part  
That my Glory is:  
For honorable love easily becomes impure,  
When one values it lightly,  
See Floris, that is the reason:  
For honorable love easily becomes impure,  
When one values it lightly,  
Because of its allurements.  

3. Here she withdrew her hand from me:  
How, I asked, can my hand hurt you?  
No, she said, I fear my own fire,  
And I may rightly suspect it.  
My love has no return:  
But fire and straw should not be too close  
Or it lightly catches fire.  
My love has no return:  
But fire and straw should not be too close.  
Love flatters through your hands.

label: children
translation:
Hear my complaint     In this night,  
Whether my strength fails,  
While your Love, Angel, has enchanted my mind  
Through your Heavenly sight, That my young Heart delights  
And steadily in my Soul builds an Aethna...  
Give heed, Leonoor, Or I will surely be lost.  
Look at me kindly: For the fire  
Of your love, Field-goddess, scorches my soul and mind  
All my Weeping and my Groaning and my Stonings  
Do you love me, Like a Lizard loves a Spider.  
Let your Mind  
Once change this state,      While I will then eternally  
Praise you, above the great Heavenly Gods all:  
And you, my Field-goddess, honor with the love,  
That I find in my Heart for you:  
Place it then also in your Mind.  

2. Why mourn, Do I hear before  
My poem is sealed?  
Who wastes His time here  
Complaining about what he suffers  
Through great love-sorrow, Where his youthful heart  
Was fiercely bound as with chains...  
Was it true, What you say,  
You who call yourself my servant!  
Ah! do not think, That it happened  
Only for nothing,  
Nor out of mockery. No, my fate knows that the Love-God  
Gave me inside, in my senses, The command to love you?  
With which you only constantly drive the spite  
I have also come to you, Angel, for I pray for mutual love.  
Come my Shepherd I shall then let you come in:  
Come my Heart, Never with pain, Shall you again be distressed.  
So! ten twenty kisses that is your part,  
Before you become in my arms.

label: children
translation:
Dear little ones, little flatteryers,  
you hop along and demand something from me!  
Come! I will offer you a gift,  
which, for you, this Song Nymph gave.  
But you ask: ‘Who is this Song Nymph?’  
Is this name unknown to you? -  
I will let you get to know her better,  
once I have first kissed you.  
She is fatherly love,  
the very first love on earth,  
which in me is matched only by the love  
for your mother.  
May her sweet song give strength to these lessons,  
and be useful to you as well:  
Even more, may you learn her life,  
which is pure harmony.  
Her joy endures, my happiness remains;  
play freely, guiltless youth,  
create amusement, but teach, while playing,  
early on to aspire to Virtue.

label: children
translation:
This little tree, which grows so vigorously,  
And already blooms so full and lush,  
Was planted here at the same time as me;  
It lifts its tops,  
And is full of buds,  
Rich in fruit.  

It already rewards the diligent hand  
Of the gardener who planted it:  
And he will see his joy increase,  
When it once, mature,  
Surprises him  
And bears fruit!  

This little tree I am like:  
Also, like it, rich only in hope,  
I bloom, promising, in my youth;  
In riper days  
I will bear fruit  
Of Honor and Virtue.

label: children
translation:
The heart feels radiant with joy,  
That it always enjoys your influence,  
Caressing Innocence! your pleasure  
Bears neither torment nor sorrow.  
You make where there is pleasure tasted,  
Although delight flees from you.  

You scatter flowers, fresh with scents,  
On the path that I tread;  
You, you know how to cheer me up,  
You provide me with rest and peace;  
I know neither grumbling nor mourning,  
You share your smiles with me.  
My swift days of life  
You bring by untouched.  
No remorse, no dismal complaints,  
Nor worries torment me.  
You make effort lighter to bear;  
With you, I play freely and happily.  

Let my heart always praise you;  
And in later times of life  
Always call you my Friend,  
Who delights me in adversity.  
Let me always deem your amusement  
As that of which you are not the source.

label: children
translation:
Why do you bloom so secludedly,
Said the field violet, in the lonely valley?
Why do you hide yourself, reluctant
To show off among the many flowers?

And yet, as soon as you are picked,
You give us a lovelier fragrance
Than many, who entice the eye
Only by elegance and color.

You can be called the image of Humility,
Who dwells modestly in silence,
And everyone who knows how to appreciate him,
Rewards with devout gratitude.

label: children
translation:
Is one often not blinded by judgment?  
I hear you constantly call Fillis a beauty;  
Look at me! Surely, I find  
That my beauty surpasses hers.  
Yes, Galathé, your beauty is to be praised,  
You surpass Fillis, believe it freely!  
But even if Fillis is not so beautiful,  
She is not as proud as you. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
How joyful, how caressing,  
How sharing in gladness,  
In new attire  
With fresh ornaments  
May beckons us!  
The greenery adorns the bushes,  
The flowers bloom;  
Nature, reborn again,  
Invigorated, as before,  
Forest, Field, and Meadow.  
But should not the Being  
That creates all this,  
Be the first to be praised  
And gratefully sought?  
Certainly! certainly! these, these.  
Thanks be bound to the joy.

label: children
translation:
Many tears are seen for those who die,  
Who still lacked Wisdom,  
And whose lives, which they lacked,  
Never spoke through noble deeds.  
Unmourned for Virtue or Gifts,  
Is the complaint one makes:  
“They, who are finally buried,  
Have lived long enough.”  

Should I then not eagerly strive,  
To live early and always thus  
Through knowledge and virtue,  
So that, even if I die in my youth,  
Brave souls mourn my death,  
And this wish covers my grave:  
“Oh, that his days of life  
Had been extended longer!”  

(Da Cupo.)

label: children
translation:
Little Jan, who was recently picking flowers,  
Found, while he was busy,  
By a flower he bent down to,  
An apple in the grass.  
This sight pleased him,  
Greedily he seized his prize;  
"Ha!" he cried, "that will suit me!  
Indeed, it looks delicious!"

But no sooner had he bitten,  
Than, whimsically angry,  
He threw the apple away. "Who could have known?  
Ugh, there's a worm inside."  
Beautiful appearance had deceived Jan:  
But he learned, and this was good,  
That no finely decorated lies  
Must always deceive us.

label: children
translation:
Why, why do you leave me longing,  
Dear little Nightingale, your shrill tone,  
That in the forest, honoring Nature,  
Honoring Nature,  
Used to sound so sweet and beautiful? (repeat)  
Seated in a cage that shines,  
Well provided with tasty treats,  
Protected from the weather, always cheered up,  
Yet you do not sing, but fade and grieve. (repeat)  

What more could you desire for yourself?  
Or does it cause you pain, dear little creature!  
To be imprisoned here with me,  
With me imprisoned,  
And not be free as you wish? (repeat)  
Well then, fly away! I do not want to force you...  
What! Listen!.. Yes! I already hear you singing!  
And for the joy you enjoy  
Your exquisite song caresses and rewards me. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
A warrior thirsts for glory on the field,  
And believes that honor will accompany him there  
When his sword fells many men.  
As blood flows along his footsteps,  
He devastates land and cities,  
They call him Great and Hero.  
But no: he who fights himself,  
Honors Virtue, avoids doing evil,  
And with diligence, and with diligence, and with diligence  
As a friend of mankind always  
Wishes gladly to increase the well-being of his neighbor,  
And teaches by his example:  
To him be that name dedicated.

label: children
translation:
Laugh, good people, laugh freely!  
There now lies my little house broken;  
The wind blew unexpectedly from afar; and you  
You laugh! but now once spoken,  
You yourself, to whom this now seems a jest, } repeated.  
You yourself often build in the air. }

Is it the splendor and glory  
Whose shine can so enchant you,  
And which flatters your delusion and pride,  
Is it truly more valuable or fortunate?  
A moment, a sudden shock } repeated.  
Often suddenly disrupts our happiness. }

label: children
translation:
Why then does man wander, for money and great treasures  
On distant seas?  
As if not one world could contain him  
Nor still his desire and wish?  
If he ever obtains the most fortunate fate,  
Then death disrupts all his joy.  

It is not this good that one must acquire:  
A Wise one, who prefers it best,  
Seeks a treasure that can never perish,  
Nor lose it through time.  
Virtue is what prepares this salvation;  
It follows us into eternity.

label: children
translation:
The little fish plays and frolics,  
It follows its desire in this stream,  
And, as it carelessly struggles,  
It does not notice the fisher's strike,  
Who, as deceitful and false as flattering,  
Lures it with his bait on the hook;  
The little fish finds that bait appealing: (repeat)  
It darts eagerly forward and swallows.

Alas! What did you dare to presume?  
Poor little creature, I see you bleed!  
If only you had not bitten,  
Nor satisfied your foolish desire!.....  
Let me never foolishly desire,  
Nor give temptation access!  
Let me learn from your example, (repeat)  
To always flee the world's allurements.

label: children
translation:
See that little bubble glistening and floating!  
Who had expected something so beautiful?  
But a breeze touches it briefly:  
Away it goes, and all its splendor!  
It resembles the young gentlemen,  
Whose merit means nothing,  
And yet they are proud of their clothes:  
Take them off, then they are nothing. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
If I may dare to believe Swift,
(Perhaps he was not too generous:)
The people in Lelieput
Are not much bigger than my thumb;
They must therefore seem like mosquitoes,
If one wants to see them from afar!
If I were seen there unexpectedly,
I would be considered a giant!

Everyone would avoid and fear me!...
But if I were no wiser for it,
Would that prove my worth?
Certainly not! That small group
Possibly more talented for a reason,
Greater in intellect and morals,
Would surely then, unabashedly, shout:
‘Great in size, but small in spirit!’

label: children
translation:
Oh, poor fly! I see you hovering  
Around the light of the candle; but too thoughtlessly.  
That false glow costs you your life  
While you seek a fleeting joy.  
My heart! if ever, full of false allurements,  
Lust, though empty in appearance, invites me,  
Oh! then think that its bonds are chains,  
Its sweetness always deadly,  
(Repeat.)

label: children
translation:
In my youth I must strive,  
With zeal and desire,  
That when gray hair I may once live  
Content and at ease.  
I want to taste the joy of youth,  
Which humbly soothes heart and mind;  
But always shun those pleasures,  
Whose caress prepares regret.

label: children
translation:
I greet you, full of joy, oh glorious light!  
O Sun, before whom the darkness falls silent,  
And whose new radiance shines upon my sight!  
How great is the power of the wise Creator  
Who brought your majesty and splendor  
To appear in the sky’s vault for our salvation and joy!  
Through you strength is given  
To the lowly and the exalted,  
The oak, and the smallest grass;  
You grant to every plant  
Bloom, growth, and life.  
Just so does  
He who is wise and good  
Always nourish love for mankind;  
His hand is generous,  
His understanding enlightening:  
Might I strive to follow His example!  
(da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Consider this Tulip! how beautifully
It displays
Its colors! See! it can delight anyone!
But no, only beautiful in color,
Yet without scent,
It does not inspire a desire to pick.
It is pardonable that, through rich attire,
A fool commands respect;
He may adorn himself;
The fool is nonetheless scorned,
It cannot succeed,
The fool is nonetheless scorned.

label: children
translation:
A flirtatious sparrow, hopping and pecking,  
Once saw a turtledove and said:  
Man is foolish; why is it that  
He grants you more privilege, more ease?  
You are fed, I am driven away;  
And yet, admit it freely,  
That I, much craftier than you,  
Possess more cheerfulness and life. (repeat)

That is because, (the dove let it be heard:)  
You are a bold robber.  
I take only what is granted to me,  
And neither harm man in goods nor labor.  
Those who did not know their true duty  
Have never been loved.  
What advantage do wit and spirit give  
If one is unwilling to use them usefully? (repeat)

label: children
translation:
What gentle joy do your strings awaken in me,  
Sweet-sounding, caressing clavier!  
You can give me pleasure, refreshment, attention,  
In solitude I find delight here!  
How airy you sound when I am joyful,  
How plaintively you sing when I am sorrowful!  
You enchant at all times  
When in song I recognize God's salvation.  
(da Capo.)

Oh! May my heart nevermore open  
To the empty flattery of false desire;  
May I always resist enticing impulse,  
Never seek joy at the cost of my peace!  
As pure as your tone is,  
So pure is the pleasure I find in you;  
And always my whole life remains  
A sweet and gentle harmony.  
(da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
A friend who faithfully holds up a mirror to me,  
And does not cover my faults;  
Who dares to judge me freely  
When my duty fails:  
Is truly to be called a friend, } twice.  
Even though he does not seem a friend. }  
Even though he does not seem a friend. (twice.)

But he who always flatters me, and never reproaches anything,  
And always praises my words and my deeds;  
Who relieves me of complaints  
Before my remorse even appears:  
Is to be considered an enemy, } twice.  
However friendly he seems. }  
However friendly he seems. (twice.)

label: children
translation:
O Sleep! O Sleep! let me taste your sweetness,  
Rock me gently now into rest! but alas!  
If I were never to awaken,  
If my eye no longer saw the morning?  
O, then shines  
Out of that darkness  
The light, full of splendor,  
That never fades;  
And nights never await me  
Where the eternal light shines upon me.  
(da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Just as the rain, with drops together  
Is carried away through a brook in flight,  
So too, always in the sea of eternity,  
Unnoticed, one moment follows another.

The greatest pleasure, Time, cannot be missed.  
And oh! how quickly that time disappears, how fast!  
The moment that was present now,  
Is already gone, and will never return.

Of what importance, of what priceless value  
Must each moment be to man!  
If he loses it in futile illusion,  
He can never make up for his loss on earth.

Oh, how much time, driven by idle pleasures,  
Have I already wasted! those are gone,  
That henceforth every hour may be dear to me!  
Away with foolishness! I no longer want to live as a child.

label: children
translation:
I want to amuse myself once in this dark forest  
My lonely silence,  
And taste the sweetness of Spring  
Unobserved by anyone.  
Yes, now here my song  
The tone of the Nightingale,  
So soothing, so beautiful,  
Listening, I am replaced!  
I will... but... softly... I hear something!... alas!...  
What is that? what noise!...  
Yes... it’s coming... where shall I flee now?  
There it shows itself!  
What is it?... a Hare!

label: children
translation:
Little screamer! I would wish  
(Spoke to Philomel the Crow:)  
That I knew why to humans  
Your song still seems so fine?  
You often keep silent: I, on the other hand,  
Make my clapping heard loudly,  
And I cannot move a single person  
To praise my snapping!

Is it only about talking?  
(Replied the Nightingale:)  
Those who always let themselves be heard  
Mostly speak foolishly.  
You do not know when to be silent,  
But steadily repeat the same tone;  
And this can win no praise,  
No one finds your sounds beautiful.

label: children
translation:
Oh, what do I hear from each one  
My companion exceptionally  
To praise his diligence and zeal;  
To call him capable and clever,  
And to raise him above me!  
Above me!  
So that I almost envy him.  

But what use is my envy to me?  
Shall I then silence the rightful reproach?  
Will one because of that praise me more,  
Show him less respect?  
No; but if I strive to surpass him,  
To surpass him,  
Then surely they will also praise me.

label: children
translation:
I beg you, let us go away;  
Do not look at that beggar. (repeat)  
How starved his being stands!  
The skeletal frame frightens me!  
No; I am disgusted to see him. (repeat)  
Can it be  
That you can pay  
Such contemptuous attention to people? (repeat)  
No, I will not flee from him;  
I want to see all his misery. (repeat)  
He who does not learn to understand misery  
Can never truly appreciate  
The happiness that he enjoys, (repeat)  
Nor praise for it  
Him, who from above  
Favorably grants him that happiness. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
I saw a Gryzaart, stiff and bent,  
He crawled hunched, with slow steps;  
His eye, weakened by old age,  
Seemed to give him unsure footing:  
He fell; the Gryzaart fell! and alas!  
His fall caused a cruel laugh.  

How I pitied that man!  
Should his suffering not arouse some compassion in me?  
Who can mock old age,  
Show no respect for gray hairs,  
Does not deserve that youth now caress him,  
Nor that old age ever be his share.

label: children
translation:
Yes, I am beautiful! My beauty truly  
Is attractive and fresh;  
And this mirror clearly shows me  
That this is no illusion.  
See how expressive these eyes are,  
What a blushing fair face!  
And this mouth! These curved eyebrows!  
Nothing, nothing do I find amiss.

When I smile, my teeth  
Appear like ivory,  
And a dimple in both cheeks  
Creates new charms for me.  
The blonde hair, its wavy swaying,  
Is rightly praised as beautiful:  
Yes, charms adorn me,  
Who may be called fair.  
Brother, less than a year ago,  
I was as beautiful as you!  
Everyone praised my charms,  
Everyone flattered and caressed me.  
The fair skin, the black hair,  
The fresh face, the sparkling eye  
And my gentle features were  
Something that inspired everyone's admiration.

But that beauty has faded:  
Childhood illness quickly took away  
From my face, from my eyes  
What was once lovely!  
But this gives me no cause for complaints:  
No; I have learned because of it  
To value true beauty only  
As that which neither time nor chance harms.

label: children
translation:
Pleasant and full of usefulness,  
Praiseworthy diligence! is always  
The reward you prepare for the youth  
Who wish to dedicate their strength to you.  
That the yawning sluggard stands  
Complaining listlessly about boredom,  
Diligence, diligence makes the time  
Almost always pass too quickly.  

He who is cheerfully at work  
Seldom experiences illness or pain;  
Pleasure accompanies him at his table,  
Moderation never dulls his taste;  
At night, disturbed by no unpleasant dreams,  
He enjoys a rest that gently refreshes;  
And he rises, and he always rises in good spirits  
As soon as the sun begins to dim the light.  
Cheerful, healthy, and well at ease,  
He knows how to adapt to the times.  
Active in useful occupations,  
He finds pleasure in his youth;  
Respect and fame and dignity  
He finds prepared for himself later, as a man;  
Displeasure, displeasure, neither care nor sorrow  
Torment him in his old age.  

Everything delights him everywhere,  
To see the fruits of his diligence;  
Of his well-spent time  
He will never regret a moment.  
Still diligent in his old days  
He avoids useless sullen complaints.  
Diligence, diligence is his staff  
That calmly leads him to the grave.

label: children
translation:
Shy, and as if afraid of the light,  
Hides, in dark hollows  
Hides, in dark hollows  
The Owl from everyone's sight:  
Why does it remain hidden?  
Because what it does  
Can never endure the brightness, can never endure the brightness  
Of the clear light.

Let me flee every deed  
That can be called foolish or evil,  
That can be called foolish or evil,  
And leaves remorse behind!  
A pure good conscience  
Nourishes no hatred for the light  
And never fears, and never fears  
The scrutiny of an action.

label: children
translation:
Between tall-grown trees  
Stood a low crooked trunk:  
They, consumed by pride,  
Wished for the woodcutter to come  
To fell the stump...  
Look! the artist is already coming...  
Alas!... there! he fells them all, } repeated.  
And leaves the crooked one standing. }

Let us never again on earth  
Be proud of form or splendor:  
Vain outward value  
Has brought many down.  
Through arrogance and proud display  
Envy and hatred are stirred:  
But..... neither envy nor jealousy harm } repeated.  
Him whom the eye does not attract. }

label: children
translation:
The death knell tolls! For whom?  
Ah! It is for Dirce, who,  
Not even a week ago,  
Adorned with lovely little flowers.  
And full of charm  
Celebrated her birthday.  

Fair, like the dawn,  
Then shone her lovely face.  
I seem still to hear her:  
“See,” (she said:) what a punishment  
What disaster has befallen me?  
My flowers are falling off!”  
Ah! who then had thought,  
That in that very night  
She would fall, like her flowers?  
Alas! she, everyone’s joy,  
The most beautiful flower of all,  
Withered in early youth!  

She, who resembled Spring,  
Lies pale, like her burial garment.  
How quickly beauty has fled!  
She, once rich in gifts,  
What do our eyes see now?  
A cold, lifeless corpse.  

Soon will her bones  
Be given the last rites.  
I shall go to her grave  
With flowers, every spring.  
But will I still be alive then?  
Ah, who can know that!

label: children
translation:
How bright, how neat,  
How pleasant, how fragrant,  
Oh see! how fine and colorful  
This fresh Peach is!  
Yes, Sister, surely  
Its traits  
Arouse  
Your longing, if I am not mistaken?  
There! Do you want to accept it!  
Fulfill my desire:  
Gladly I give it, for I enjoy,  
When I bring joy,  
A caressing and double delight.

label: children
translation:
As well as wisdom keeps God's decree  
Hidden, which is distressing to us;  
Would that consciousness be a refreshment,  
Or would it be a punishment? Yes, certainly:  
For if I were to encounter prosperity,  
I would become too proud,  
And easily miss my goal,  
Through careless wrong feeling.  
Through wrong feeling.  

If I foresaw disaster, I would tremble;  
The impending pain, magnified by fear,  
Would spoil all the sweetness of my life  
Which I now enjoyed.  
I do not want to be foolishly curious,  
Nor fear misfortune far ahead,  
But rest content  
In the fate assigned to me.  
Assigned to me.

label: children
children
translation:
I saw him then  
The notorious man,  
Who can dance so skillfully on a rope.  
I saw him swing,  
Jump, spin;  
One freezes from it!  
It is an art, yes truly,  
That is learned with difficulty:  
But, hey tell me,  
(I am allowed a question,)  
What use is it for?

label: children
translation:
Dear, little innocent Lamb!  
You play carefreely, happily, and tame;  
No fear makes you shiver. (twice)  
Alas! He who strokes and celebrates you,  
Is driven by self-interest:  
He stands, he stands bloodthirsty towards your life.

To this red, beautifully shining cord  
They drag you to the slaughterhouse:  
You seem not to be alarmed. (twice)  
The hand that you now lick and kiss  
Will, out of merciless unconsciousness,  
Soon, soon make your guiltless blood flow.

Taste, innocent little animal, taste  
Taste calmly the sweet pleasure  
That you find prepared for you, (twice)  
Enjoy your short time now;  
For, alas! what good is it to you  
If you knew, if you knew what fate is destined for you?

label: children
translation:
That is certain.  
It is desirable  
To live rich and with honor,  
I will not strive against that,  
I give this testimony:  
But the greatest happiness can be here  
When we, free from appearance,  
Are worthy of the happiness we enjoy,  
Yes, even the greatest.

label: children
translation:
Little Johnny recently played in the field,  
And felt a sting from a bee:  
Soon he shouted angrily: "I will take revenge!"  
He threw stones, clods, sand;  
And, although the little bee had dodged,  
Yet his wild hand sought  
To break the bees' hive.  

What did he gain from his mad behavior?  
He soon found himself, timid, defeated,  
Wounded from sting after sting, driven away:  
The entire swarm then attacked him.  
This was his fault; he must not complain  
But learn, more calmly, from now on  
To patiently bear a small regret.

label: children
translation:
I saw a snowman being made recently;  
Oh, no one ever saw a finer one!  
But the sun began to shine:  
Soon he melted, just completed.  
He resembled a foolish show-off:  
Only from afar does he seem bold;  
But how quickly the young man melts  
Before sensible scrutiny.  
He shines outwardly,  
His splendor and power  
Are never lasting,  
And have no strength.

label: children
translation:
Glimmers from that weekend darkness  
Appears the silver Moon.  
With what gentle splendor  
Does she smile upon us kindly?  
Her light spreads on the tops  
Of the forest a fiery red.  
How grass and buds gleam  
On the earth’s broad lap!  
Yet her face, so open,  
So soft, seems to calmly invite our desire  
To peaceful silence and rest. –  
Thus wisdom also invites  
Him whom her influence guides,  
Always to rest, to peace,  
And to meekness.

The light of the sun’s rays  
Lends radiance to the Moon,  
Which would otherwise wander darkly  
And always be dull. –  
Thus virtue also lends value  
To wisdom here below:  
I seek wisdom on earth,  
Yes, but through Virtue alone.

label: children
translation:
Are your joyful tunes then  
Those that herald the arrival of Spring,  
By such cruel means acknowledging  
That the man who owed you reward,  
Craftily, craftily attacks you,  
And hunts greedily in his net?  

Dear Little Lion, compassion  
Breeds in me your danger and pain!  
No, I will never be so cruel  
As to try to cause your ruin!.....  
But what am I saying? I forget  
That just this afternoon I ate you.

label: children
translation:
Jolietje, my little dog, is willing,  
He does what I ask of him;  
But sometimes he is a bit capricious,  
Then I threaten him, the beast is meek and afraid.  
But sometimes he is a bit capricious,  
Then I threaten him, the beast is meek and afraid (repeat).  
And would it cause me sorrow,  
If, deservedly, I see myself punished?  
This would surely not be fitting for me:  
My dog would then shame me.  
Jolietje, my little dog, etc. (da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Oh! That I were a bird,  
That in the air could soar so swiftly,  
And strive over forest and mountains,  
Freed from toil and from danger!  
I would then fly here and there:  
I would then fly here and there:  
No tale, no tale would deceive me anymore.  
Would deceive me anymore.  
I would surely choose the fairest place,  
Where always Spring and Summer flourish,  
Where choice fruit and flowers grow,  
Where everything delights eye and mind!  
Now I would ascend to the skies,  
Now I would ascend to the skies,  
Then through song, then through song make the listening forest fall silent,  
Make the listening forest fall silent.  

I would... but hush! I hear a shot,  
There a bird falls bleeding down!  
I recall my foolish wish again!  
What good fortune that I did not enjoy it!  
How great—(God’s grace be always praised!)  
How great—(God’s grace be always praised!)  
Is the happiness, is the happiness, to be a human!  
To be a human!

label: children
translation:
There goes, there goes my shadow before me } twice.
Look, how great I am now! }
Surely, by this I shame
Those who carelessly belittled me too much:
Yes, come closer now, freely acknowledge
That I only seemed small to you,
But now it shows how great I am. (twice.)

There goes, there goes my shadow before me } twice.
Look, how great I am now! }
But how! As I hardly recognize my greatness,
My shadow vanishes through a cloud!
There stands a Lord who sees this, and
He laughs? Surely at me, I would think,
That I am so foolish and vain. (twice.)

But wait, but wait, who knows what can happen? } twice.
How great he appears and rises! }
When unforeseen disaster strikes him,
His golden shine quickly fades away:
Only then is his worth truly realized;
People will barely look at the man
Whom they once so highly exalted. (twice.)
(da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
See how splendid, lively, beautiful,  
What a glow, what lovely colors,  
Each to judge the most beautiful,  
This Butterfly now displays!  
The little creature, once of no value,  
Crawled on earth like a little worm,  
And nearly caused me disgust,  
Now flies happily and cheerfully.  
I see it reborn in a Chapel,  
That can delight our eyes  
And once attract the attention of connoisseurs.  
This must serve as a lesson to me,  
Never to draw bad conclusions  
From outward appearances;  
For it may well be,  
That he whom we now do not esteem,  
Rises higher than I can aspire.  
See how splendid, etc. (da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Bee, I regard your home!...
While the sun shines refreshingly,
You gather your treasure of honey,
Before the summer time disappears.
You extract from richly flowering orchards
The sweetness that you prepare with purpose;
Diligent industry and pleasure estrange
Idleness from your domain.

When hail, wind, and harsh gusts
Cause the fresh blossoms to wither;
When neither fields nor orchards bear green anymore
And everything fades in the dry season:
Then you sit in full granaries
At ease; and need does not compel you
To, often scorned, stand by grim neighbors
For support and help.
Your zeal, which you always show,
Ensures not only your own existence:
You also wish to enrich others generously,
And are useful to the public at large.
You gain much advantage from him
Who properly tends your hive;
He sees a return on usury
Who lends to you in times of scarcity.

May your lineage flourish!
May you never be denied shelter!
May no stranger trespass ever harm you
Nor disturb your esteemed citizenship!
I will show myself careful for you,
And plant flowers here in this place;
Your industry will reward me for that,
And one day share your honey with me.

label: children
translation:
Little noisy one, stop it now!  
Will that drumming, that has lasted for hours,  
Still last even longer?  
Although I cover my ears,  
It still rages through my head.  
That such annoying disturbance of peace  
Can please you yourself,  
I would certainly never have believed.  
Little wiseacre, vain show-off,  
It suits you well to scold me!  
Would you yourself want to hear  
That you waste your time?  
With a silly doll's booth,  
Playing this way and that,  
Shouldn't that get boring in the end?  
Is a doll so pleasant?  
Sweet relaxation and amusement  
Are also to be counted as duties;  
But I will trouble no one with that:  
Quiet toys are my business.  
Yes, a doll, I freely admit,  
Can give me much pleasure;  
I know how to live nicely with it,  
But a drum! Does that please me?  
What can I say? It must be so!  
One is worth as much as the other;  
You are just as wise and clever,  
In your eyes, as I am in mine.  
Everyone loves the play doll very much  
Where their heart mostly leans:  
Yours is the joy of dolls,  
Mine is for noisy drumming more.

label: children
translation:
Welcome, beautiful morning hour!  
Oh, how glorious you are!  
Everything, everything around,  
Shows itself invaluable.  
What delight, what pleasure  
Remains hidden from me,  
If I do not awaken in time  
With the joyful morning!

Everything my eye beholds  
Caresses me from all sides:  
Dune and hill gleam with gold,  
The grass with diamonds.  
The cheerful song in wood and air  
Seems to thank you happily;  
Valley, field, and hamlet  
Catch all those sounds.  
Drunkard, stupid from excess,  
Wants to yawn freely with indulgence,  
And, since you hate yourself,  
Sleep away the sweetest pleasure;  
Remain ignorant of all that beauty,  
What splendor, what brilliance  
The morning hour now displays;  
Buzzing in somber darkness!

Meanwhile I want to  
Amuse myself here in thought,  
And taste the sweet delight  
Of that rapture. –  
If already on the earthly valley  
So much joy has dawned for us,  
Oh, what salvation, what delight will  
Later charm us!

label: children
translation:
My restless horse keeps running,
When I want to whip him;
But if I stop urging,
Soon he lies idle still, -
Whoever learns to appreciate rest,
Often becomes slow to good:
But when misfortunes strike him,
Virtue and Courage often awaken,

label: children
translation:
Dear little brook, that, driven onward,  
Flows past me! How I cherish you!  
If only my whole life  
Could be like you, just as now!  
Though you do not raise golden sand  
Nor pearls grow on your bed:  
I see you swell with richer blessings,  
Where you water field, forest, and meadow. (repeat)  

If you ever flow along winding banks,  
Then you carve, meandering, your path;  
And though you wash along barren shores,  
Here and there a little flower appears!  
Your little waves babble freely,  
Never stormy, always gentle;  
As clear as they spring from the source,  
So purely they flow into the sea. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
Ha! That works now!  
Little checkmarks, I've got you!  
Here is the nest. I have finally surprised you!  
Beautifully you struggle,  
And you torment yourself,  
It is in vain; I hold you too tightly.  
But, how complaining,  
Panting, chasing;  
The older couple now chirps and wanders around!  
It seems to me they plead for mercy!  
Should I extinguish their hope?  
Did I not often feel joy growing  
Through their song, when I once found myself here? (repeat)  
As a reward  
I disturb their home,  
Built by themselves from moss and straw,  
And to which I  
(Ah, what am I doing!)  
Never gave anything!.. I feel that I regret it.  
What pain  
Has struck our heart  
If my parents were ever taken from me!  
No; there you have your young ones again,  
Dear singers! now descend;  
Soon, softly and tenderly,  
Their song, united with yours, will promise to me. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
What cruel deed have you committed, cat?  
How dared you endure it!  
What wrong did you do to innocence?  
My dear pair of Doves you took their lives!  
My Doves! It is over with you!

How much amusement I found,  
When you enjoyed your food from my hand,  
And gratefully showed your thanks!  
Now that cruel one has fiercely devoured you,  
In her claw you found your death.  
But, false cat, I will take revenge,  
As soon as you fall into my power.  
However much you try to flatter me  
With wagging and empty gestures:  
Know that the severest punishment awaits you...

But would that revenge really benefit me?  
Oh no: it would be done in vain.  
Mice are the duty of cats;  
They cannot easily refrain from snatching,  
And because of that, this evil is caused.

You followed your innate inclination,  
Which is always proper to you as an animal:  
My heart, as a human, surely teaches me  
A nobler passion! No threat of revenge  
But human duty: forgiveness.

label: children
translation:
You who flow through me purely through the air,  
Grant me strength, desire, and courage;  
You who can bring me pleasure and joy,  
Sharpen my senses, satisfy my taste;  
Who imparts grace to all things,  
And makes that joyful and full of changes  
Everything caresses me: everything caresses me:  

Health, highest happiness on earth!  
Through you the world becomes beautiful to us.  
You far surpass in value  
The greatest treasure, the richest crown.  
You sweeten our life,  
Reduce the burden, increase the pleasure,  
And by your side, and by your side, float  
Amusement and rest, amusement and rest.  
You, you give roses to the cheeks;  
Glow to the eyes; splendor to the body;  
To the breast you give gentle desire;  
Skill and strength to the limbs;  
To the soul capability  
For discerning truth or illusion;  
For the smallest happiness, through you we accomplish,  
To be sensitive, to be sensitive.  

If I could always, as now, enjoy you,  
And with that desirable blessed joy  
Pursue a good and useful goal,  
And be grateful to God for it!  
But should I ever offend you  
Through misuse: then I pray, be silent!  
Often sickness, ailments, and misery  
Teach man his duty, man his duty.

label: children
translation:
The dear season, so full of delight,  
We have lost; harsh winds  
Chase from the thundering north:  
Everything that lives shivers from the cold;  
Forest and meadow seem to wear mourning;  
The earth is iron; the creature trembles. (repeat)  
I must highly value my fortune,  
That I can withstand the cold:  
Peat and wood and warm clothes  
Protect me from discomfort;
