I lack nothing,  
Safely I rest under a roof.  
But how does he shrink in such times,  
He who must struggle with want,  
And suffers misery and poverty,  
Yes, even lacks the bare necessities,  
And yet wants to avoid shame  
That he begs for alms! (repeat)  
Hungry, cold, battle-worn by need,  
He stretches, sick and weak, his limbs  
On some straw: lies his prayers  
That his end may hasten!  
Alas! Alas!  
Feel his pain with compassion,  
Oh my heart! And let me try,  
Clumsily, to the best of my ability,  
To lovingly offer him help!  
Be moved by your neighbor’s disaster,  
Then you deserve to see salvation. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
What makes my cheeks glow  
So suddenly all at once?  
I feel the blushing grow,  
And lower my eyes.  
Here I stand, daring,  
Prepared for some mischief:  
Alas! and my conscience  
Advises me caution.

I will heed that admonition  
And be on my guard,  
Before anything might please me  
That causes gnawing and pain.  
Bloom, bloom at all times,  
O color of Innocence! bloom fresh!  
Oh, may desire for sin  
Never erase you from my face!

label: children
translation:
Whose desire would you not awaken  
Dear little Rose! softly tinged with blush!  
Half closed and half already open,  
You smile sweetly at me!  
Yet although you appear  
Still fresh, dew-kissed, gently met by coolness,  
You do not unfold,  
As if you are almost scorched by heat and glow.

In the morning of my life,  
Like the rosebud,  
I bloom driven by desire  
And always rich in wishes:  
But when the soul  
Opens to desire or the illusion of pleasure,  
That glow burns,  
And the youthful heart quickly withers in pain.

label: children
translation:
How I love you, O useful and beautiful Books! (repeat)  
How short time feels with you to me!  
Desire makes me willingly seek you;  
I feel this duty urged by pleasure. (repeat)  
The wise man's knowledge, highly valued,  
You share by instructing me;  
You make me sincerely understand  
What is idle appearance or truth.  

You, you teach me what is necessary to know, (repeat)  
And show why, and what I am;  
Through you I learn to measure duties  
To which I consider myself bound. (repeat)  
You clearly unfold to me the course of times;  
The rise and fall of kingdoms;  
What passions most torment man:  
Desire, ambition, lust, interest.  
You, you make me behold the beauty of Nature (repeat)  
And what wonders she spreads:  
The smallest worm and the dust unveil  
To me the Creator’s wise and good design! (repeat)  
And when I read your sacred hymns  
O Poets! who sing of Virtue!  
Then I feel a joyful desire  
Delightfully pressing into my heart.  

O Books! yes, you shall always remain my joy, (repeat)  
You shall be my best recreation!  
You help me spend time usefully,  
You shape my taste and make it refined. (repeat)  
How I thank those who first recognized your value  
To me, and whose diligence  
Teaching explained to me your use!  
To them be my best gratitude dedicated!

label: children
translation:
What sweetness does the gift give me  
From that poor man!  
I feel my heart blazing  
With joy that gently caresses me inside.  
That man! how moved  
With what visible tender emotion,  
With what grateful heart's excitement,  
He looked at me there weeping!  
Weeping!  
There, then, the benevolent compassion  
So much delight in itself contains.  
Oh, then often let me rejoice  
In that pure joy, which at all times  
Springs from kindness and mercy.  
(Da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Ah! this depicts the kind nature  
Of my Mama, worthy of praise!  
She is the one; oh yes!  
So kindly is her nature,  
As I, hopping happily, (thus I spin  
When I have risen in the morning,)  
Go to receive her kiss.  
Ah! this depicts the kind nature  
Of my Mama, worthy of praise!  
worthy of praise!  
She is the one; oh yes!  
But however beautifully art may adorn,  
It must yield to Nature:  
The image does not convey that tender language,  
It does not, as the principal,  
As the principal,  
Show tender love.  
Ah!... Ah!... Ah!... Ah!...  
This depicts, etc. (da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
A luxurious horse, whom freedom, pleasure, and play  
Flattered much more than yoke and labor,  
Broke free and fled to the open meadows;  
There he knew neither coercion nor torment,  
The amusement seemed there to bring him much delight (3 times).  
He lived happily,  
Free from worries,  
And kept running back and forth,  
Or lay down,  
As long as the days,  
So full of pleasure,  
Lasted of the Spring and Summer time. (repeat)  
But the harsh North  
Soon struck these regions,  
And the field lost all its splendor.

A luxurious horse (da Capo.)  
Winter came; and cold and discomfort  
The horse felt. In all directions  
He then fled; but all went against him,  
He found nowhere food nor shelter,  
And stood, but too late, then sorrowful and ashamed. (3 times)  
Alas! alas!  
He cried: I fool!  
Had I avoided some work  
In this short time,  
Then today I would have found myself  
Quite content  
With shelter provided, and feed supplied. (repeat)  
Oh, what suffering  
For short enjoyment!  
Who would not flee heedlessness?

Winter came (da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
I hear, I praise myself in my being; } twice.
myself in my being praise; }
People say: her beauty and her charm
Are seen daily rising higher!
But that is not what flatters me the most. (twice.)

I would, I would with more pleasure hear myself praised
For my intellect, and virtue, and morality.
and virtue, and morality.

Time, time can make me great and beautiful,
I owe my growth to its course:
But to guard the beauty of the heart
Such is accomplished much more by my care,
To perfect it. (thrice.)
Let praise be given to me
That I fulfill this duty,
And also above all (twice.)
My heart agrees to this,
Then I have rightly gained
That praise, that blessing.

label: children
translation:
Work freely, work, oh clever spider!
I will not oppose your* diligence.
Work freely, work, oh clever spider!
Your skillful weaving
Amuses my mind.
How soft, gentle, and courteous,
How orderly, skillful,
How soft, gentle, and courteous
Thread closes into thread!
No human being is capable
Of your art, worthy of admiration. (da Capo.)
Stay, stay free here in this corner;
I will not drive you out of your circle.
Stay, stay free here in this corner;
I will take care
Of evil visitors.
I want to unravel once
Through diligent observation,
I want to unravel once
What you gain?
With what intent or trust
Do you spin your web so skillfully? (da Capo.)

What! What do I see? Are you lurking for prey?
Your web hooks a little fly.
What! What do I see? Are you lurking for prey?
You steal her life
And suck her dry!
Your art is meant
Only to commit murder?
Away! She opposes me,
Though she seemed great.
What is art worth, however highly honored,
If its goal is evil and wicked? (da Capo.)

label: children
translation:
Your splendid crown loses its leaves,  
O Tree! already rustles, withers, your foliage;  
And what your top still seems to spare  
Hangs dry and pale, robbed by the wind.  
As beautiful as useful was your life,  
With honor you thus bow to rest:  
You have generously given us your fruits!  
Your shade gave us coolness and delight. (repeat)  

When the autumn of life may also  
Call me to the resting grave,  
What joy! if then I die in peace  
And am rightfully praised,  
That I have borne many fruits,  
And each willingly, according to my power,  
To be of use, to bear the burden  
And always strive to do good! (repeat)

label: children
translation:
Little sister! We walk elated!  
Here something lovely is felt everywhere!  
Cast your eyes upon those Doves:  
Oh see how they love each other!  
What delights one also gladdens the other;  
They float happily, well content,  
Always together, always together.  
Surely, they belong to each other  
As we do, so I believe: as we do, so I believe:  
For their Love is extraordinary.  
I would trust it as you do.  
But Brother! Look! Here is also a sign!  
Would you like to observe this vineyard once?  
Its tendrils, all rich with clusters!  
See how they wind around the trunk;  
One lives for the other's benefit alone.  
They appear one; they appear one.  
They surely are brotherly friends,  
As we do, so I believe: as we do, so I believe:  
For their love is extraordinary.  
Oh yes, they surely are! We love  
Each other too, just as they do.  
A harmony of our minds  
Binds me to you, and you to me.  
I can taste neither joy nor gladness  
If I miss your caressing presence.  
Oh! It is certain, oh! It is certain,  
A Love must bring happiness  
If it is like ours, if it is like ours:  
Our heart bears this testimony.  
Brother, in my pleasures  
You are, indeed, my playmate;  
But this is not the only reason  
That increases my love for you:  
Driven by inclination and nature,  
I feel a stronger bond binds me.  
Oh yes, I find, oh yes, I find  
That you, like my own life,  
Love my heart; love my heart;  
I am your friend, you are my friend.  
Let us always nurture that friendship,  
Sincere and loyal in joy and sorrow.  
May envy, interest, nor ill suspicion  
Ever creep into our loving hearts.  
No quarrel nor discord may separate us,  
But unity, duty, service,  
Make us ready, make us ready  
For mutual happiness for both.  
Through unity and affection  
Blessing is spread within companionship.

label: children
translation:
These days, so welcome, gave you life  
Which is the origin of our life;  
Allow, dearest guardian! that we give you  
A token of joyful gratitude!  
What a blessing that we received you as mother!  
Our heart already feels all your worth.  
The children of princes taste blessing,  
But not such a blessing as we do.  
Although a thousand hands, at their beck and call,  
Are always ready to serve them:  
We see ourselves granted a greater privilege  
In your faithful tenderness.  
They may freely welcome them fully  
With everything that merely pleases their taste:  
What can compare to the sweetness of the kisses  
With which you lovingly bestow on us!

Their uncertain childish steps  
Are constantly governed by a strange bond:  
But we, we dare to take the first steps  
Always safely by your faithful hand.  
You awaken our sleeping thinking ability,  
Bring our soul's power to light,  
Set limits to our eager attempts,  
And always tame our will through duty.  
You depict Virtue to our senses  
Gracefully, blessedly, and sweetly;  
And by truly loving us tenderly  
You kindle love in our hearts.  
Your watchful eye remains undaunted  
Always carefully watching over us,  
And the smallest happiness enjoyed by us  
Gives you the greatest pleasure.

Your lessons shape us for good,  
But oh! your example even more!  
May your care always watch over us  
Faithfully and tenderly, also in ripened time!  
Oh! may the richest fruit be constantly reaped in our lives  
From the seed that you, driven by hope,  
Now plant in our youthful hearts!

label: children
translation:
Come quickly out of bed;  
The rooster has crowed.  
Already the birds are singing;  
The morning breeze blows.  
How brightly the rays  
Of the rising sun shine:  
They cheerfully reflect  
In brook and spring.

Everything is already alive  
In the field and in the forest.  
Everything sings of joy;  
Everything gleams with gold.  
How cheerful, how lively  
Is our heart when tired.  
It brings, rejoicing, God's goodness  
A song of praise.

Your Name be praised,  
O Creator of the Light!  
That is what we and all  
O God! owe to You.  
Even children raise,  
In blessed delight,  
With so much pleasure,  
Your goodness, O God!

But, loving Father!  
The thanks befitting us  
Are not in words  
Or empty sound.  
No! We are thankful to You,  
When with diligence  
We attentively spend  
The precious time.

Your will be, O Father!  
Fulfilled most perfectly  
By being active  
With wisdom and strength.  
You gave us life,  
You bestowed the strengths; -  
Then come to the work -  
We obey.

label: children
translation:
Dear are you, pleasant days!
Free and frank from worries, fear, and torment;
Nature laughs joyfully.
Dear are you, best part of life!
God has given me nothing in vain:
Nothing, however brief it lasts.
A thousand other young dear little ones
Must mourn their lot without comfort.
I, free of sorrow,
May always taste joy through God's goodness.
Tears glisten on many cheeks;
And God blesses me.

Ah! how many orphans, how many poor
Pray for help, beg for mercy,
Cry loudly for bread;
I, I may eat my bread with joy,
Not stingily allotted with sorrow,
Neither fear nor need burdens me.

I hear a child chattering with cold,
Because it does not know where it will end up,
Sadly, alas! no bed.
I, on the other hand, cheerful at heart,
Am not disturbed in my sleep by cold, sorrow, pain, or grief.

Many wander on remote streets;
Father, Mother have abandoned them,
Cared for by no one;
I, on the other hand, see all the time, from morning
Until evening, parents care for me,
Always faithful and good.

Ah! how shall I, God! declare Your favor!
Ah! how shall I repay so much good,
As You do to me.
Every following hour of my life
I will always devote myself to good,
With a joyful mind.

Yes, early I sow good seeds,
And I walk, rich in good deeds,
On the path of virtue.
Oh, then I will close, without anxious complaint,
Once my eyes, even in old days,
Joyfully on my youth.

label: children
translation:
Rejoice and sing,  
Brothers! (Sisters) sing.  
Virtue and good morals  
Banish the semblance of sorrow.  
Let us all today,  
Be brave and cheerful.  
Rejoice and sing,  
Brothers! (Sisters) sing.  
Yes, even joyful children's songs  
Are received in God's favor.  
Rejoice and sing,  
Brothers! (Sisters) sing.  
Virtue and good morals  
Banish the semblance of sorrow.  
Let us all today,  
Be brave and cheerful.  
Rejoice and sing,  
Brothers! (Sisters) sing.  
It is on Earth  
Good and beautiful.  
But the best part of life  
Comes with care.  
How many tears are  
Not wept on Earth.  
It is on Earth  
Good and beautiful.  
May all who mourn,  
Experience peace and rest as we do.  
So come, sing,  
Brothers! (Sisters) sing.  
That we, driven  
By a tender heart,  
Always strive for salvation,  
At every sign of pain.  
Brothers! (Sisters) if we dry tears,  
God will also help us in need.

label: children
translation:
Come, Dear Youth! a cheerful Song,  
In honor of dear May!  
From clouds, full and mild, descends  
A stream of blessings.  

It rains flowers, herbs, and grass,  
How tender is every drop.  
There the sunlight breaks through clearly.  
The whole sky brightens.  
The bird choir, which sadly was silent,  
Bursts out in praise,  
The forest echoes with their joyful Song.  
There is joy everywhere.  

There bees settle on the flowers,  
Yes, with a soft humming,  
That means this industrious numerous swarm  
Welcomes the month of May.  

By us, our Children, also cheered,  
Yes, with a joyful spirit,  
And with a grateful hymn of praise  
Greet dear May.  

It teems already with activity.  
Nature lives all around.  
Come, let us welcome with new diligence  
The month of May.  

Renewed diligence, led by joy and delight  
In the practice garden,  
Proclaims to the good God  
Properly our gratitude.  

Yes, God! You call us to work,  
You call us, this hour,  
Then we obey Your voice, O God!  
And the counsel of nature.  

Well then! Mind, hand, and heart,  
Dedicated to work;  
For no better hymn arises,  
Than well-spent Diligence.

label: children
translation:
Come, children! gather around me,
In the corner by the hearth.
Do not be uneasy about Winter.
It deserves a hymn of praise.
A hymn, in a heroic tone,
Sound from your grateful chest.
Nature is always good and beautiful,
In summer dew and frost.

Now a warm, white carpet
Of snow covers the chilled field,
Which, in the mild springtime,
Melts into fruitful drops.

In the heart of the earth, Nature
Lives on infinitely active,
For she hears the will of the Universal Order
In the heart of the earth.

Already in the ground,
Grass, flowers, and herbs develop;
And the roots shoot around.—
See a sprout through the snow.

The snow barely melts in this place,
When from that sprout shoots
A flower stem, and arises a flower,
Which is called Snowdrop.

What’s more; the healthful nourishing grain
Grows, although Winter rages,
For the clockwork never stops,
That cultivates and nourishes all.

Thus Nature works, though she seems at rest,
She goes before us working;
Come, children! follow, follow with joy
The indicated path.

Work also in silence, where no one sees,
With wisdom and patience.
We already see the day in prospect,
That fulfills your hope.

When, at the height
Of your lifetime,
You harvest from silently shown diligence
The most beautiful fruits.

label: children
translation:
o How happy, how content,
Freed from fear and sorrow,
I went away cheerfully with my prize.
How joyfully my heart still beats.
This time I have succeeded
In winning a prize;
o I am still completely ecstatic.
My happiness knows no bounds.

How wonderful, sincere is my fate.
I dared not wait so long.
But thanks be to You, o God!
I received strength from You.

Your goodness has bestowed powers upon me,
So that, despite so much honor,
I remain humble above all,
And truly appreciate Your favor.

Then this prize, no matter how great or beautiful,
Will not awaken vain pride,
No! but as a sign of virtue and diligence
It will spur me on to a new path.

O God! it shall be, it must be so.
I will always strive earnestly,
And thus, o good God! not for myself
But to seek Your praise.

label: children
translation:
The air is lighter. - The thunderstorm passes by.  
Come, Brothers! Sisters! let us sing;  
Yes, let us sing a joyful song,  
For the salvation enjoyed by man and beast.  

How lovely smells the scent of flowers!  
How wonderfully shines the color of flowers!  
Everything brightens up. It is all refreshed.  
Praise be to the Power that has ordained this.  

While our hearts praise God's goodness,  
The breath ascending to Heaven  
Even more freely passes through the air, fresh  
And pure after the thunderstorm.  

O God! receive our thanks.  
Freely follows the thunder, hoarse in sound,  
On lightning beams, fierce with glow.  
You, great God! are wise and good.  

Come, let us always gratefully heed  
Your Wisdom, Love, and Power,  
O God! who, while the thunder roars,  
Sprinkles the field with streams of salvation.

label: children
translation:
Already awakening, we cheer,  
In the morning, at dawn;  
That our heart may then be joyful;  
Youth is no time for complaining.  
It all refreshes,  
Every spirit  
Everywhere 'joyful shouting.'  
In the morning everywhere.  
And no heart, full of feeling,  
Remains cool at so much joy.  
Even the secret noon hour  
Has approached for man.  
With the gifts of nature,  
Gathered through diligence,  
Rises the thanks  
Joyful in sound  
Everywhere 'joyful shouting.'  
In the afternoon everywhere, etc.

If the task is fulfilled with diligence,  
Then the sun may set,  
And the face shining through evening splendor  
Glows with pleasure.  
What a matter  
Then for praise.  
Everywhere 'joyful shouting.'  
In the evening everywhere, etc.

label: children
translation:
No! Brothers! Let no vain shame  
Hold us back at the Cemetery.  
Let everyone calmly come here,  
Our blood flow freely through the heart here,  
At least let no fear oppress us.  
Here rests, oh yes! in the earth's bosom,  
A number of fellow humans.  
Here lie corpses, small and large.  
God set for all at their death  
The limit of earthly wishes.

But let no sadness surprise us  
At so many graves.  
God, who took here, was He who once gave.  
Here then falls the wreath of life.  
Man perishes to ashes.

But in spring the seed  
Is entrusted to the heart of the earth:  
That seed, although it perishes before our eyes,  
Sprouts into wheat that covers the field,  
So rich in number and value.

Would God, in whom not a single grain of wheat  
Is hidden in the field,  
Not watch over man in the grave?  
Would man perish entirely there?  
No! God continues to care for him.

Although here the seed husk blows away,  
And leaves not even a trace.  
This is the field sown by God himself.  
One day man will rise again, and reap  
The fruits of his works.

label: children
translation:
Here, with attention,
We observe the spinning Wormpje.
Will the bed that it spreads
Ever come to the diligent creature?
But see slowly, slowly disappears
The Silk Worm from our eyes.
We still see it... No! now, now it seems
To entirely hide its face.
The creature has completely disappeared,
As if in a coffin of silk;
That now devotes itself here
To rest, the eager Wormpje.
Its diligence has earned this rest,
Its grave even honors it.
Yes, Youth! says here a Friend of Children,
May this example teach you.

Always spend your short, fleeting life
In useful activities.
Always want your strength to last
As given to you by God.
Let each be a symbol of diligence.
Would diligence not suit you,
Who are amazed by this Worm?
Would a Worm shame you?

No! no! you follow its activity;
And when, after much striving,
The grave of rest is prepared for you,
Then too you will be praised.
Then your display of diligence is still acclaimed,
Valued by posterity.
Oh yes, your example, great and beautiful,
Will then still establish usefulness on earth.

label: children
translation:
Comes with songs  
With a cheerful spirit  
Joyfully begun  
This hypocritical feast.  
A glow of contentment,  
Spread upon the parental face,  
Shows how their heart shares in the joy  
That delights the child's heart.  
Come, etc.  
Oh yes, even everyone in this circle,  
Whether citizen or stranger,  
Gives serious attention to the glory  
That diligence here expects.  
Come, etc.  
Here worthy displays of diligence  
Are seen after the hung crown of honor,  
That crown delights every eye,  
But alas! it hangs so high.  
Come, etc.  
Everyone who has done their duties  
Now feels their heart beat comfortably,  
And waits, freed from anguish,  
To see what their share will be.  
Come, etc.  
If the crown of honor is prepared for us,  
May it stretch a path toward diligence;  
And above all else, may far from us be  
The spirit of pride.  
Come, etc.  
If the crown of honor of diligence fails us,  
Then far be rancor, anger, and envy,  
No! Let a blazing zeal  
Ignite within our hearts.  
Come with songs,  
With a cheerful spirit,  
Joyfully begun,  
This hypocritical feast.

label: children
translation:
Hear, Friends! hear a true case,  
An act of honesty,  
Which I will briefly recount.  
It is about a poor girl;  
But one who clearly shows us all,  
How Honesty lives with Poverty.  
Oh, tell of a deed that shows us,  
How Honesty lives with Poverty.  
A girl, from a poor neighborhood,  
Was sent to a shop,  
To fetch two ounces of coffee,  
She was given the money along with it.  
The girl always walked so quickly;  
She returned like the wind.  
Yes, good girls are always quick  
And always come back soon.  
She now had the two ounces of coffee.  
“Come, put the coffee on,”  
Her mistress then called – “I’ll be with you shortly;  
Just prepare the half-eleven clock.”  
She shook out the pepper shakers,  
But heard a sound, a strange noise.  
Oh tell us quickly what it means,  
That strange sound, that strange noise.  
She then looked into the container, and saw,  
(She held it up to the light.)  
That something shiny was lying on the bottom.  
What an unexpected sight!  
It was a silver thimble,  
Guess now what the girl did.  
She found that silver thimble,  
Guess what the girl did.  
She quickly put it into her pocket.  
She was all alone,  
When she found it and stowed it away,  
Before her mistress appeared.  
She drank the coffee with her mistress;  
But do you think she said anything?  
Why would she, or think she would say anything  
To her mistress about what she found?  
The poor child had neither penny nor cent,  
But now a thimble.  
At twelve o’clock the girl went home.  
How she hurried!  
She went – easy to guess this –  
To the first silversmith.  
Yes, yes, easy to guess this,  
She went to the first silversmith.  
You guess wrongly – she passed by,  
Wherever she was wandering to.  
She flew straight to the shop,  
Where she always gets coffee.  
“There, madam,” she said very generously and quickly,  
“There is your thimble back.”  
How generously and quickly did the girl give back  
That silver thimble.  
But, Friends! such honesty,  
Was not left unrewarded.  
The woman immediately said: Good girl!  
You have shown your faithfulness.  
Receive as reward for honesty,  
This thimble, honest girl!  
Praise be to the virtuous poor girl:  
A striking example of Honesty.

label: children
translation:
Come, dear friends! Let us cheer!  
Who among us is not happy this hour.  
We warmly welcome every newcomer  
Into this circle.  
Be welcome in this place of practice;  
May Heaven’s favor support and strengthen you.  
Spend here the irrevocable, precious time  
With gratitude and diligence.  
Come, dear friends! Let us cheer!  
Who among us is not happy this hour.  
We warmly welcome every newcomer  
Into this circle.

We offer you our friendship,  
At the entrance of this place of practice;  
That low jealousy may never  
Be harmful to friendship here.  
No! Though our hearts blaze for honor,  
They glow even more with love and friendship.  
Where kind helpfulness  
Spreads quiet joy through the heart. Chorus from the beginning.  
Do not scorn the generous hand  
That offers help to each of us;  
And if we lead you for a while;  
We gladly show you the way.  
But the Teacher, our friend,  
Who loves you, who loves all children,  
Calls you and us to diligence;  
May his love be your goal and reward. Chorus from the beginning.

label: children
translation:
Creator! thanks to you.  
We are refreshed  
Generously and mildly by your gifts.  
Thanks to you, who disposes everything for us.  
Thanks, O Creator! thanks.  
From a deeply moved heart,  
The thankful joyful tone sounds sweet.  
Sanctify the Creator’s song.  
Creator! thanks to you.  
You grant warmth and rain  
By your power;  
Food you let grow,  
Rich in strength.  
Thanks, O Creator! thanks.  
It suits man, fed by you,  
That he praises your goodness.  
Sanctify the Creator’s song.  
Creator! thanks to you.  
You create clear springs,  
Generous in moisture;  
That thirst, so gently,  
Might be quenched.  
Thanks, O Creator! thanks.  
Food and drink flow down from you.  
Yes, your favor be praise and honor.  
Sanctify the Creator’s song.

label: children
translation:
Rejoice! The holiday hour has struck;  
We cease our daily work.  
Welcome, joyful leave days!  
Happy rest makes brave and strong.  
You are welcome. Now come, banish care.  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
You are welcome. Now relax the bow.  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
You are welcome. Now relax the bow.  
Rest, ordinary activities!  
Now our playtime is here.  
May we cheerfully step homeward;  
Joy brings forth new work enthusiasm.  
Rest and joy must be properly enjoyed.  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
You can yourself increase strength and diligence.  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
You can yourself increase strength and diligence.  

May we now not grow bored,  
Dull and lazy lying down.  
No! Let us show in our play  
Even ingenuity and activity.  

That we behave cheerfully, but not wildly;  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
No one may ever complain about you.  
You are welcome, Holiday time!  
No one may ever complain about you.

label: children
translation:
That I, with a joyful song,
Greet the rising sunlight,
And replace the birds' song.
That my delight takes pleasure
In this spectacle, beautiful and grand;
All rejoice at the morning red,
That, O God! Your favored one
May also meet You joyfully.

All nature is refreshed; revived...
Flowers, animals, humans. -
Creator! who blesses us,
Beyond our wishes;
May Your praise always be spread;
May the flower of gratitude,
Richly covered with dew of favor,
Neither wither nor fade.

With the climbing of the Sun,
Whom I soon see rising,
Climb, rise, source of goodness!
Your tokens of grace.
Everything works, full of strength and fire.
Life is entirely nature;
Until the glowing midday hour
Calls all to meals.

Then also songs arise,
To, with sharp sounds,
Go joyfully to the dining table,
To thank God, the Lord.
For a bite with spring crystal; -
Or if fate allows somewhat more,
Praise freely with acclaim
Fruitful vineyard branches.

May the work be resumed with diligence,
After the meal.
That diligence, in village and town,
May praise Heaven’s favor so.
Work waits in every circle
For Heaven’s help and blessing;
Rejoicing, beautiful the twilight,
Early or late, rise.

O how gentle, how good is God.
The evening dews with blessing
After a day of blessed joy.
God, affectionate to man,
Grants, where the evening purple shines,
The hour which determines his work.
And God flows, while the sunlight fades,
A new hymn of praise.

Yes, the songs do not cease,
Though beneath the horizons
The sunlight flees our sight. -
See the stately climbing
Of the silver-white moon.
Floating along Heaven’s path
She gazes comforting upon mankind....
Dreamers! Dream of shadows;

Dream of ghosts, superstition!
Hush your iron chains. -
Godly, deaf to your nonsense,
Rejoice in the nightly darkness.
God-fearing praises the silent night;
She sings God’s love and power
Shining in starry splendor
Joyfully through that radiance.

label: children
translation:
Sleep softly, sleep softly, oh dear child!  
What my heart so tenderly desires;  
I will watch by your cradle.  
Where you lie down without worries;  
Caring for you is my duty.  
Sleep freely. No harm will approach you.  
Sleep softly, sleep softly; no wild noise  
Suddenly bursts from the street into the house,  
To disturb your sleep with a fright.  
Sleep peacefully, dear innocence!  
Who would not be charmed  
By a face, as if covered  
With rosy well-being?

Sleep softly, sleep softly; a joyful smile,  
As friendly as I have always seen,  
Floats over your lovable being.  
Oh, you who are as innocent as you,  
Sleep, free from all sorrow.  
Why should innocence always fear?

Sleep softly, sleep softly, but what sound  
Breaks thundering up to the sky...  
A thunderclap! - I see fierce flashes...  
Sleep, child! unaware of any worry -  
Well then! I will draw the cradle curtain,  
To shade the light a little further.

Oh what a strike! - Naked danger lies ahead.  
Does the child not even notice this;  
Can nothing disturb his rest, his sleep?  
A light presses, so dreadfully bright,  
A sound presses, so icy sharp,  
Does it not reach his eyes or ears?

But softly I notice... yes! he awakens...  
Show yourself... show yourself... how he responds.  
Does anything affect his calm or joy...  
No, he, who knows no worry,  
Laughs - even plays under the cradle cloth,  
With the twilight glow of lightning flashes.

Play on in your happy fate.  
Oh, let my heart, at peace in God,  
As little as your troubled being. -  
Be, be calm and composed, my spirit!  
For he, who fears the Deity,  
Needs to fear no other fear.

label: children
translation:
I am grateful for the understanding granted to me….
I am very aware that I understand and think.
But may that gift not be taken from me for pride.
Should I be proud? – How! Of what? Of a gift?

Yet that gift, so great; given by God, –
Alas! one fall – one blow deprives me of it.
And is not understanding a gift, so highly elevated,
That no other gift can ever surpass it.
How then could pride swell my young breast,
For fleeting honor – like a bubble of water.
Or should I count myself great for wealth or lineage,
Yet many a fool is indeed great or rich.

Would a garment perhaps awaken pride in me?
A trivial garment, though stiff with silk and gold;
That might soon attract another,
By me and everyone seen as foolish or wicked.

But the shining beauty of face and limbs…!
Oh tender rose, that blooms for few hours,
And withers after one day, trampled in the dust….
I feel all too clearly how Beauty resembles you.

Does strength and robust physical power deserve it,
Perhaps that man exalts himself on that.
One little fever… and he, whom all consider Hercules,
Collapses – and dies. – Humbling realization!

But pride in Courage and Virtue, repeatedly shown,
Repeatedly sought… No! False images! Depart:
One weak gust; Courage has long fled….
Temptation beckons…. and our Virtue falters.

On nothing, oh God! may our hearts be raised,
That pride never seduces the weak man.
But that he, always calm in joy and sorrow,
May love and serve You, in quiet humility.

label: children
translation:
Again, again, a friendly farewell greeting.  
The time to part is here.  
This circle, this school we leave.  
Farewell! O dear circle of friends!  
Farewell! O brotherhood.

Again, again, a friendly farewell greeting.  
The hour of parting is here.  
This circle, this school we leave  
For a larger circle and society,  
Farewell, O sisterhood!

Again, again, a friendly farewell greeting.  
Our hearts are moved.  
O brotherhood; O circle of sisters!  
We leave your dear circle:  
The bell will soon ring.

Again, again, a friendly farewell greeting.  
Farewell! Enjoy with each other  
Pleasure, without quarrel or envy,  
May your common goal be virtue and diligence.  
Farewell! O circle of friends!

Again, again, a friendly farewell greeting,  
As the hour of parting soon strikes.  
O teacher, dearest friend!  
Who loves us so sincerely.  
May willing deeds follow your will.

Receive a grateful farewell greeting.  
Thanks for so much pleasure  
As was given to us in turn by you.  
We pray, with a grateful soul;  
May God grant your prosperity.

O God! Hear our farewell vow.  
The hour of parting strikes.  
Pour out, O good God and Lord!  
Your blessing on each of us  
Who leaves this school.

label: children
translation:
The flourishing young shoot must be bent,
When it is youthful and soft.
And with no hard, rough harness
Willingly yields to small force.
But if it stands for years in storms,
Exposed to the wild and to itself,
No gardener gives new shapes
To a shoot that has grown into a tree.
One does not trouble a gardener
To spend his art on it,
It remains a tree as crooked and knotty
As chance has made it.
So too, the person, who is let loose
And wild in their youth,
Will not benefit greatly
From the most striking teaching,
That wisdom gives them as an adult.

Step forward, children! Always willingly draw near,
When your teacher calls,
And thank the good God and Father,
Who grants you such a favorable lot.
Let Him bend your young hearts—
So surely later time
Will bear witness to your understanding and virtue,
When you are grown adults.

label: children
translation:
Look, Klaas! There goes my marble,  
But it bounces like a frog,  
Now it misses the little pit;  
"Good," says Klaas, "I will hit it,  
And it will please me even more  
If my game is won." –  
Yes, I know well, friend Klaasje!  
You are a master at marbles,  
But you do not hesitate to crawl,  
And this crawling makes me fear  
That my mother will be angry  
When she sees my clothes.  
"All that crawling and bending down  
Breaks the strongest things into pieces,"  
Says my mother as well, spoke Klaas;  
But who can ever learn to play marbles  
And always think about their clothes,  
You won't become a master at this work!  
I want to love the marble game,  
But winning my parents' favor  
Is worth more to me than marbles; –  
Dirty clothes, which displease,  
I do not want to wear for this game,  
Look, your pants are already full of dirt. –  
Yes, I can well believe that,  
But I cannot quench my desire  
To be a master at the game;  
Because with beating, scraping, sweeping,  
Clothes can be made clean again,  
Then everything is fine, right? –  
Well, I wouldn't dare say that  
No matter how you want to explain it to me,  
It remains unworthy of parental love;  
Whoever does not want to avoid dirty clothes  
Surely must suffer punishment,  
Which brings no joy but unrest.  
Now, I do not want to be a master  
At marbles, for fear  
That I often grieve my parents,  
No: I want, with my games,  
Preferably to share in their happiness,  
Because they love me too. –

label: children
translation:
My kite goes up,  
How strong the wind is! –  
Who would ever believe it,  
I pulled with all my might;  
I have gotten so much string,  
And it has been used;  
It has risen so high,  
That I can hardly see it.

I must tie my kite,  
Otherwise I will travel along;  
Where will they find me then,  
It surely would pull me into the sea! –  
I will wait for my father,  
So that he can pull it back;  
I have not discovered  
My small strengths in vain. –  
The wind has much power,  
Who can oppose it? –  
Here we see, with our own eyes,  
Yet nothing of its existence! –  
This has never been explained;  
God does not give that knowledge,  
Whom everyone must fear for His almighty power,  
Who also commands the wind. –

label: children
translation:
I have taken this little nest,  
Yes, I was in love with it,  
But, dear parents of these little creatures!  
Ah! who knows how this grieves you. -  
I will feed your dear little ones,  
And raise them, just like you;  
I will enclose them in a cage,  
But then they will never be free!  
Oh! had I left them lying  
In the warm sunshine;  
Would I now return them  
There, where they were born? -  
No: I first want to feed them  
In this narrow confined space,  
Until they, on their clumsy little wings,  
Can soar through the air.

Oh! what happiness it will be,  
When they again with their parents  
Joyfully jump on the branches,  
Sing to their Creator’s honor;  
I will grant them those pleasures,  
Since I too, just like them,  
Seek to taste the free life,  
Cheerful, contented, and happy.

The duty assigned to me is serious,  
Oh! do not forget your finches,  
So that no lack of food  
Causes them to die of sorrow;  
Ah! this would always grieve me,  
I would then be the cause of their death,  
Since I, from their storehouses,  
Have locked them in this prison. -

I quickly return to you, my dear little creatures!  
All your joy and freedom again;  
For this is what your wise Creator wants,  
I give you then what I desire. -  
I will never disturb nests,  
Rather be a joyful observer  
Of how these sweet forest dwellers  
Frolic and hop, without pain. -

Oh! then I want to sing with them,  
To my Creator’s honor and praise;  
The sweet pleasure of the morning,  
Also gives me, like them, cause for rejoicing. -  
Yes, who would not thankfully rejoice,  
If out of a dark night,  
He hears the sweet forest songs,  
With renewed life force. -

label: children
translation:
I got this doll, at the fair,
From my dear mother:
I have always been dear to her;
And my dear brother
Got the finest rocking horse,
To ride on it;
Much is mother's love worth,
That can delight us. -
I will show my mother,
Gratitude for the gift;
When I think of her love,
I will praise her goodness;
For she is not obliged
To give me so much,
The thankful heart directed at her,
Makes me live happily. -

Dear mother! what joy
Would you show me;
I will, through love and pure virtue,
Always reward your kindness. -
Grietje is grateful, truly peaceful,
Grietje will learn well,
Then she will also get on her birthday,
For her doll, many clothes. -

label: children
translation:
Never was a happier day born to me  
Than when I received this rocking horse;  
Nothing could ever please me more,  
I had saved my money for this;  
It could have suffered another year,  
My purse was still far too short,  
And now I can ride my little horse,  
Which increases my joy.  

Now I can call myself happy,  
I received this horse unexpectedly,  
Oh yes, I must praise my parents,  
They gave it earlier than I had thought,  
How grateful a child must be,  
When it has loving parents;  
They are never praised enough  
For the good that their love gives us.  
O Loving Parents! I feel your worth,  
How good you are to your little Jan!  
—  
You are my delight and joy on earth,  
Now my eyes always see your love.  

A child who tries to live gratefully,  
Commits to obedience,  
Can give joy to their parents,  
And is loved by God and man.

label: children
translation:
Pietje was once quietly playing,  
Building a little house, but out of cards,  
Oh! this seemed to him, in his eyes,  
More valuable than all treasures. -  
Once again he had to look at the house,  
And examined it everywhere,  
But, as we often learn,  
Seeing too much causes disaster,  
“The house,” he says, “cannot remain like this,  
Because it must be much taller;  
I will build two rooms on top of it,  
And then it will certainly be good.” -  
He continued and looked at it again,  
No more satisfied than before;  
“It is too narrow,” he says, “for the height,  
No, it cannot remain like this;  
I will make it much wider now,  
With a tower added,  
Then it will look like a church,  
With a beautiful parsonage!”  
He continued building again,  
Until he almost got his way;  
But, with building and rebuilding,  
The house collapsed, weak as it was. -  
Pietje saw his work broken,  
Cried sobbing loudly,  
And his father, who heard this,  
Asked: what does that cry mean? -  
“I have,” says Pietje, “bad luck,  
The card-house has collapsed;  
I do not know what to do,  
My understanding falls far short!”  
Father said: “My dear boy!  
I know and understand your reckless nature;  
If you had first calculated your goal,  
You would not have done it like this.” -  
Those who want to build houses,  
First lay the foundation,  
Measure width, length, height,  
So the house cannot fall. -  

If the foundation of our efforts  
Is not calculated beforehand,  
One receives, for starting carelessly,  
Misfortunes as punishment. -  
First one must calculate their plan,  
If one ever desires something great,  
This lesson, - let it be a teaching to all, -  
Has been taught to us by Jesus himself.

label: children
translation:
It is now only a short time ago  
That I placed this finch on the perch;  
I soon thought, upon seeing the difficulties,  
My little finch will learn through hardship and pressure. -  
Everything I did, he did not want,  
Learning seemed to cause him great distress;  
He constantly searched for a branch or twig,  
So that his string often broke. -

Oh! I then thought, how can it be possible,  
My little finch! I am not harming you,  
I only teach you, you have no need to fear suffering,  
Yet eventually he followed the string. -  
And now he performs exceptionally well,  
By skill, he rewards my labor;  
Where is a finch like this,  
That sits singing on its perch? -  
He can fly along a long string to the end,  
And never falls down tired,  
If I let him go, he would not deceive me,  
He would still return to his master;  
Then why let go? that was risky,  
Once he stayed away, I was grieved,  
And though I lured him with much patience,  
The creature stayed away, and I was to blame.

I will surely faithfully keep my little finch,  
Because he has learned diligently;  
I may, with joy, watch him fly,  
Until he returns to the perch;  
And when he grows tired, I let him rest,  
So he can again take flight,  
There he finds food, and also his drink,  
And I thank him for his agility.

I also want to learn my lessons diligently,  
Even if through pressure and adversity,  
And honor my teachers gratefully by my diligence,  
Just as this creature does his master. -  
Is not every person obliged,  
To accomplish either art or work?  
And is this ever in opposition,  
Joy often follows after weeping.

A child who always wishes to show diligence,  
Toward sciences in his youth,  
Will reward himself more than his teachers,  
And serve for the elders’ joy.  
But he who works sluggishly,  
And honors neither God nor man by deeds,  
Will, whatever he may become or seem,  
Be far more foolish than the finch.

label: children
translation:
I will make my little mill turn,  
Even if I have to walk against the wind,  
And, although my jaws are red from the breeze,  
No worries, playing strengthens a child;  
I can still control my mill,  
Even if the wind blows fiercely,  
Learning from adversity in time,  
Is also the purpose of a child's play.

The ship is driven before the wind,  
But the mill does the opposite;  
Who sustains more people in life?  
I think the mill, through the flour  
It gives us, and the land it dries,  
When it lies under water;—  
If adversity has this great power,  
Prosperity also points us to duty.  
If adversity can produce good,  
Prosperity has no less strength,  
When we always restrain the vice  
That sometimes is brought by it.—  
Thus, ship and mill can teach us,  
An ever-wise governance of God,  
Who, for or against, continues to reign,  
According to the holiest purpose, the fate of humanity.

label: children
translation:
Received the snap lock,  
Placed on the shoulder;  
The saber or rapier  
Attached at the side;  
The drum must roar,  
The whistle sounds sweet  
And is played cheerfully  
For zeal and courage.  
Now we will march,  
Come, friends! step forward;  
We must learn well,  
And march as it should be. —  
Turning right and left,  
Must be done neatly,  
Everyone watches, on a signal,  
Their wingman! —  

To live for the Fatherland,  
Also adds to youth,  
To give honor  
To the virtue of the ancestors. —  
So we must be vigilant,  
With the sword in hand,  
Fan our zeal,  
For the benefit of our Land. —  

This Land has cost  
Many brave lives;  
Thus the Batavians  
Were freed from tyranny. —  
We follow their paths,  
Along the Fatherland’s trail,  
They walked, by deeds,  
Heroically before us. —  

One must look to God,  
Who gave us this Land,  
We must preserve it,  
As the most precious pledge. —  
Freedom is sacred,  
Let us consider this,  
So everyone remains safe,  
In what they possess.  

To live for the Fatherland,  
Is good, dear youth!  
But also learn to strive  
For wisdom and virtue. —  
Sin has brought  
Strife into the world,  
And suffering was never eased  
By the fire of discord.

label: children
translation:
There goes my best top!  
It is a large ball,  
I know no better top! -  
But I must strike steadily,  
Or the spinning is done;  
Thus the whip exerts its effect sooner,  
Because otherwise it falls down. -  
I do not strike out of anger, no:  
But only to keep it going,  
Who ever punishes wood or bone?  
We are driven back and forth,  
Turn after turn,  
And sometimes, by our own compulsion, spin  
Unto our downfall. -  

Oh! that everyone could see from this,  
How one, day by day,  
May learn while playing;  
He who learns a craft,  
And lingers in the work,  
Is also, like the top, guided  
By striking, unto diligence. -

label: children
translation:
I have received a little cart,  
Made with a little goat and sturdy,  
With which I drive along streets and roads,  
And I would not like to miss it. –  
I play, as coachman, for my little sister,  
And she smiles kindly at me,  
She even gives me a little kiss,  
When I take a ride with her. –  
I can also ride on my little goat,  
Then I leave the cart standing;  
And nothing can please me more,  
Than to go out as a rider! –  
The little goat is strong, it can carry me,  
Brings me wherever I want to be;  
If it runs full tilt, chasing,  
I say, hey! – then it stops still. –  
I ride out, to garden or home,  
With my little sister behind me,  
I am then richer than a king,  
No one has as much as we do. –  
Away then with kites, tops, spinning-tops,  
Hoops, marbles, rocking horse,  
Dolls, foam from water balloons,  
Nothing is worth anything next to my little goat. –  
I now learn early to drive horses,  
And to ride with a pair;  
When I am grown, I will race,  
With two legs! – thus spoke Jan.  
But his father, who had heard  
This boasting from his little son,  
Said: ‘Jan! I must grieve,  
That you like riding so much. –  
I have now perceived your pride,  
I see that good deeds are harmful;  
I must restrain your arrogance,  
As the source of sorrow; –  
I will therefore sell your carriage,  
Marbles will do for you just as well,  
To avoid danger early on,  
That feeds the pride in you.  
It is not uncommon, in this life,  
That prosperity breeds pride,  
And one must give back again,  
What harms us in the end.  
Learn from this, my dear children!  
If you get more than others,  
Never mock those less fortunate,  
Show that you are thankful in silence.’ –

label: children
translation:
How can it be possible,  
That wind, and nothing but illusion,  
Can show itself so beautifully?  
How can it be possible,  
That water and some soap,  
Blown only from my pipe,  
Appear so splendid! -  
It is like glass,  
But I must marvel most,  
When I look at the color. -  
With green, and yellow, and red,  
This water globe shines,  
And climbs upward with brilliance,  
Provided it does not hit anything;  
But, alas! how short-lived,  
May I praise its beauty,  
I barely smile at it,  
Or its entire existence  
Is too little to mention for me,  
The air makes it vanish. -  

Man, so beautiful in splendor,  
Shows strong power,  
But sometimes quickly disappears,  
Like a shadow, night. -  
He is a water bubble,  
Who must borrow everything,  
This teaches us every hour,  
How short our duration is;  
Each day flies away,  
Like smoke before the blazing fire.

label: children
translation:
My hoop, which takes much effort,  
Through steady walking,  
Has never freed me from work,  
However, I had to buy it,  
Because it belongs to our game;  
So I walk on, playing with it;  
But, alas! how harmful is this game,  
By which I tire and torment myself. –

One grows tired and powerless,  
Like a fool and inexperienced,  
And if one does not, no one can give power  
To the rolling hoop. –  
Then, surely, everyone chooses their own,  
One loves the real, another the appearance.  
Let everyone be free in their choice,  
I do not favor any game with slavery. –  
I, who do love playing well,  
Prefer quite different games,  
That entertain me, to my liking,  
And do not torment the body so;  
The game is suitable for the youth,  
That spreads joy, and refreshes the heart,  
But when it is slavish or real,  
It becomes a source of sadness. –

The hoop adds to the children’s game,  
But this cannot benefit me;  
Everyone chooses their goal, I know well,  
I would rather leave this game;  
Because it tires me too much,  
Heats my blood, as if it glows;  
Who finds pleasure in his fate,  
From too real a spurious delight? –

So I quickly throw down my hoop,  
And will seek something else,  
Through which, untired, I learn something,  
I turn to my books;  
Oh! why should I toil so hard,  
Through a real game that promises nothing,  
The greatest joy is virtue,  
Which prepares the heart for true goodness.

What game a child deems worthy of himself,  
I cannot give him so much  
As the desire to learn, which grants pleasures  
For this and the next life. –  
The game gives, yes, sweet delight,  
But often leads us away from God;  
Who early seeks true wisdom,  
Is a gracious, virtuous child. –

label: children
translation:
Skipping rope can flatter me,  
Oh! this game can never bore,  
Early in the morning, and late in the evening;  
I can jump a hundred times:  
And then still sing beneath,  
As long as one keeps hitting steadily. -  
Yes, that game can also please me,  
But my breath cannot bear it,  
It is quickly done with me;  
Scarcely having jumped six times,  
I become tired, and then forced  
To leave the rope again. -  
It is not a game for all children,  
Chest and breath can  
impede,  
For the healthy it is good;  
One must also be cautious,  
Jumping has dangers to fear,  
If done thoughtlessly. -  
Once, I must tell you,  
I jumped more than a hundred times  
Back and forth in the rope;  
Strongly sweaty, and then drank,  
Glass after glass poured,  
And I fell down as if dead. -  
Shame on you! Jan! you should be ashamed,  
Who would approve such amusement,  
Where death can follow? -  
Would I, to seek fame,  
Pay such an honor so dearly?  
Dear girl! I tremble at it. -  
‘Child!’ thus spoke recently my Father,  
My faithful friend and adviser,  
Whom I will always follow;  
‘If you like skipping rope,  
Keep calmness beforehand,  
Thus you will guard yourself against accident!’  

Games suitable for children  
Are those whose pleasure  
Is never a cause of pain;  
Wild, loose, and playful games,  
That tire or bore,  
Torment the virtue-loving heart. -

label: children
translation:
Who is coming along to the bowling alley?  
There I have nine pins set up,  
And whoever can hit the King,  
Wins the game, provided that  
None of the others are knocked down,  
For which no points are counted.  
Come, friends! I will lead you all,  
But softly, the ball rolls through,  
And, alas! no pin falls down,  
How clumsy and dumb the ball is!  
“No, Jan!” thus says little Piet,  
“Do not blame the ball;  
Give me that ball, your strength  
Is not suited for that load.”  
And Piet threw, with faster speed,  
The King down, and calmly says:  
“Do you see now, little Jantje! well,  
By my strength, I win the game!  
Whoever does not direct the work according to his strength,  
Misses his goal, neglects his duty;  
How many people regret and see  
The fruits of their efforts not;—  
Whoever carries more than he can bear,  
Makes himself an unhappy man.”

label: children
translation:
Mietje plucked, with her little hand,  
Once a single little rose,  
And she laid it in her basket,  
When she set off for home;  
“I may,” she said, “oh rose!  
Pluck from my father’s garden,  
But now surely sister Koosje,  
If she sees it, will cry for sure.  
I will then pluck one more,  
For sister Koos, that dear girl,  
If only I succeed  
In not telling father.” –  
Mietje, scarcely home arrived,  
Looked at her father downcast,  
She began to feel ashamed as punishment,  
Knowing what she had done. –  
“Dear Mietje!” said her father,  
“Why do you look so downcast?” –  
Mietje stepped closer shyly,  
“I have plucked two little roses,”  
She said: “one for sister Koosje,  
And then also one for me;  
Oh, I wanted a rose so much,  
I hope it pleases you.”  
“Child!” then the father spoke again,  
“Now that you admit your fault,  
I am not angry, I love you tenderly,  
But still I am not used to it,  
That without asking me,  
You pluck roses for your own pleasure;  
If roses please you,  
Plucking is your father’s business. –  
The rose, the most beautiful of all flowers,  
Teaches everyone who plucks it  
To appreciate its scent and color,  
But whoever rashly pulls it  
By its stem full of thorns,  
Receives a painful punishment;  
If you want to value the rose highly,  
Praise the Creator who gave it to you.” –  
Now Mietje was quite satisfied,  
“Oh! How good my father is! –  
She said, “because, with gentle reason,  
He teaches me how I must act.” –  
“I will be obedient,  
No matter how the rose entices me,  
I will regard it, and always wait,  
Until it is given to me.” –  

This is the true childhood life,  
Whatever pleases them,  
Never to give anything to oneself,  
Nor be greedy with the eye. –  
From desire sometimes comes stealing,  
Which displeases God and man;  
If you want something, children, in your play,  
It is proper that you ask for it first.

label: children
translation:
Nothing here gives us beneath,  
A purer joy of the soul;  
Nothing else more soothes the heart,  
To unfeigned delight;  
Nothing can comfort us more,  
In disaster or adversity,  
Than the highest happiness in life:  
Peace of mind...  
Peace of mind.

label: children
translation: Come, dear Playmates! The day has come again; Come, let us wish each other good night while singing. May it happen to us again, To share with a clear mind, United in spirit, In this so joyful celebration.

label: children
translation: On a beautiful summer evening, gently dewed by balm fragrance, we were sitting peacefully in our little hut, when there was a knock at the door: knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock knock

label: children
translation: Let others grumble morosely. While fate offers them no more; Everyone must bear their burden, Partly joy, partly sorrow. But what is given to him, He always experiences: The enjoyment of earthly life, Rises through contentment.

label: children
translation: The air becomes mild, the ice melts away; Already the green buds on shrub and hedge, The Winter has fled. Nature revives Anew and has taken off Her mourning dress... taken off Her mourning dress.

label: children
translation: Oh how sweetly it laughs in the dawn. The Creation meets us! Every new morning hour Brings us new blessings: Every day, as the horizon descends, Shows how God's favor shines upon us, Shines through all beings. Shines through all beings.

label: children
translation:
Let us, let us sing joyfully,  
To the honor of God, Who again,  
Blessingly wants to surround us! Yes, companions  
Through this life, let our hearts, strange to sorrow,  
Give HIM honor again.

label: children
translation:
Silent brook, which gently flows there under the trees,  
Might my life resemble you,  
Though the storm-wind roars elsewhere,  
Might all the paths of my life,  
Free from worries, free from pain,  
Always mildly shaded by good fortune,  
Be as even as your plain!

label: children
translation: Who knows the hero of our Fatherland, with De RUITER, so great in battle! Who does not gladly sing a song for that Sea Hero, whose life was devoted to his country! He who, by strategy, still conquered his enemy, where bravery could not withstand the superior force.

label: children
translation:
Winter retreats; Spring shines Again  
with renewed strength. On meadow and field, along  
mountain and valley, In the stately forest, yes everywhere. She displays her beauty anew, With resplendent splendor.

label: children
translation:

label: children
translation:
The sun is rising, my little Rose! awake,
You slept so peacefully after the lively fun,
Come, stretch yourself once more, my child,
Thus you grow my little heart, my darling, my sprout.
Awake now, my little Rose! it is a bright day,
It is time to play... your innocent laughter,
Soothes your mother's heart, your friendly glance,
Always makes her feel that you are content.
Come play now, my little Rose! my darling! my treasure!
Every piece of toy is caught by you,
A precious wealth, a caressing delight,
Come play now, my little Roses! the game is your affair.
God’s blessing guide you, my little Roses! my child!
Growing while playing virtuously loved by all,
O comfort of my life, o joy of my heart,
May God keep you safe from sorrow and pain.
My little Rose! my angel! my delight and pleasure,
How you soothe my senses where my heart always burns,
Driven by joy and feelings of love,
If God watches over you I achieve my goal.
And when your days, my little Rose! rise high,
How then my reverence and gratitude reach their peak,
And when those days come to a year,
My heart beats so clearly with love.
Grow up then, my little Rose, in innocence and virtue,
That the hearts of father and mother may rejoice,
And gambol and play on my lap,
If God keeps you safe then you will soon be grown.

label: children
translation:
Who lives only for himself  
and does not give from his abundance  
to the poor and their suffering, to  
comfort them with his assistance,  
the good is only unworthy  
that God has granted him on earth.  
Although his fate may seem glorious,  
he will never be satisfied;  
he will never taste the pleasure  
that makes his heart shine,  
whose generosity, so mild,  
gives comfort and soothes suffering.

label: children
translation:
Dear God! I want to praise You,
Give thanks for the gentle,
quiet rest that I have received from You,
when I had closed my eyes,
carelessly slept, aware of nothing.
No one could then take care of me;
Although hidden
from everyone’s eyes, - yet You watched!
Yes, You grant me life anew,
Want to clothe me, give food,
And refreshment besides.

label: children
translation:
Dear Creator! I bow down,  
Before sleep closes my little eyes,  
Gratefully stammer my prayers  
Still in childish tones. Oh! Child, do not  
Despise that one who looks to your mercy.  
Ah! Forgive me all the evil  
That I have often thought or done,  
Also today again have committed,  
Although only You know it!  
For Your eye, that sees everything,  
Dear God! cannot be avoided.

label: children
translation:
O Spring! image of our youth, your arrival fills
every heart with joy, You awaken nature.
We see how everything comes alive again,
How everything strives for growth
And seeks to perfect itself, And seeks to perfect itself.
It is summer. See how everything blooms!
The bird sings; hear the cattle bellow,
See how the little fish splashes.
It is all life, all joy,
Nature celebrates, it is all delighted,
Everyone sings and plays and frolics.

Autumn is here. How richly laden,
One sees the farmer going home,
He thanks the Heavenly Father.
The sky turns gray, the weather harsh,
The days short, and every hour
Brings winter closer to us.

Winter rules; and shrub and tree
Stand bare; and more, river and stream
Are now frozen to ice.
The snow covers both path and land;
People skate on the ice in all directions,
Nothing seems to disturb their pace.

Thus spring gives ample joy,
Summer renews delight,
Autumn can also please;
Winter gives us ice fun.
Whoever thus criticizes the seasons,
I will gladly endure them.

label: children
translation: We sing, we cheer, oh lovely morning! To the honor of the Creator, the bird sings along. The cooing turtle dove, the fluttering little dove, the crowing rooster, all are joyful, each sending their songs every morning in grateful praise to the Creator.

His love, his goodness  
Created this wonderful place for us,  
Gave us all that sweetness of life.  
Thanks, O God! for all that good,  
For that rich abundance.  
For that rich abundance.

label: children
translation:
Welcome, Summer! You grant us joy.  
Come now outside, You, lively youth!  
Jump in the fields, Now cheerfully around;  
Sing a hymn to God, From a joyful mouth.  
Sing a hymn to God, From a joyful mouth.  

See the little cow grazing  
Here in the meadow;  
And the little horse trotting  
On the clay soil;  
There the lambs are skipping  
Back and forth;  
Over there, a bird of prey  
Soars through the sky.  
Over there, a bird of prey  
Soars through the sky.  

See how the little swan,  
There on the water,  
So proud and stately,  
Hurries away;  
I see ducks, geese,  
As if in a bath,  
Rinsing, washing themselves,  
In the flowing water.  
Rinsing, washing themselves,  
In the flowing water.  

Hear, hear the clattering  
Of the stork;  
And the sharp whistle  
Of the finch pair.  
Hear, hear the tones,  
The sweet song,  
Of lark, reed bunting,  
And nightingale.  
Of lark, reed bunting,  
And nightingale.  

Yes, the earth is again  
A Paradise:  
Everyone praises the Creator  
In their own way.  
We sing along,  
With joyful mouth,  
In praise of the Creator  
Of the whole world.  
In praise of the Creator  
Of the whole world.

label: children
translation:
Summer has disappeared;  
The sun loses its strength.  
Now Autumn has appeared,  
With fields, richly laden.  
Richly laden. Richly la-den.  
The gardener shakes the trees,  
Loaded richly with fruit;  
And brings us, along the streams,  
His fruit and produce.  
Fruit and produce. Fruit and produce.

The farmer reaps the field,  
And gathers grains,  
From which the diligent baker  
Bakes bread for the household.  
For the household. For the household.

The farmer loads his wagon  
With dairy, fresh and rich,  
Delivers to us, every day,  
His neat field banquet.  
Field banquet. Field banquet.

Thus Autumn brings its sweetness,  
Through field and farming.  
Leaf, creature! God's goodness,  
His love, care, and faithfulness.  
Care and faithfulness. Care and faithfulness.

label: children
translation:
Winter reigns, it is harsh and cold;  
Beck and stream are frozen.  
The wind howls bleakly through the forest,  
And naked stand shrub and tree.  
The last little flower has been cut;  
The snowflake blows around the ears;  
And the earth, wherever one casts the eyes,  
Displays a sad, deathly garb,  
But whoever reproaches or blames the winter,  
I hold him in high esteem;  
Winter brings me great pleasure:  
Then I skate over the ice,  
And swiftly, as on winged feet,  
Hasten to my closest friend;  
Break a trail with the fool’s sleigh,  
Through thick snow, the streets along.  

And if the rain pounds on roof and window,  
And streams flood path and street,  
Then we sit together by the fire,  
And shorten time with talk,  
With song or play in joyful times;  
Thus we taste the sweetest delight,  
And everyone shouts, joyful and glad:  
Welcome, winter season!

label: children
translation:
Little, little toddler!  
What are you doing in my garden?  
You are plucking all the little flowers,  
You are making it far too rough.  
Mama will scold,  
Papa will hit;  
Little, little toddler,  
You better get away quickly.

label: children
translation:
So pure friendship inhabits the heart, that is beautiful  
When it shows itself in every deed: that is beautiful  
When it does not care for itself alone,  
Does not calculate: zero I am keeping one,  
That is beautiful, that is beautiful, that is beautiful, b.

But many a truly friendly man says friend  
And yet does not mean a jot of it, it is wind,  
That sweet hand-shaking  
Is sometimes nothing but pretending,  
Then wind, then wind, then wind, b.

With many a friend things are not quite right; ouch, ouch,  
It goes for them like the cat with the mouse; meow,  
How sweetly at first it plays with the little mouse,  
It devours it when it tires of it,  
Meow, scratch, scratch, meow. b.

It goes for them like the dog with the bone: woof, woof,  
He sniffs inquisitively all around you: woof, woof.  
But if you take the bone away from him again  
He growls and bites as punishment,  
Woof, woof, woof, woof, b.  
Nor is this friendship known here -  
We avoid those who impose obligation: thus violates,  
With us the old friendship lives on,  
A handshake that one hears ring,  
One man, one word, one word, b.

Now fill the glass with wine for this, hooray,  
A red seal must be the mark: hooray,  
Then the motto friends, that from now on,  
Will be engraved in our hearts,  
They Amicitia, b.

label: children
translation:
Moderation is the lesson,
That grandpa Hippocrates
Has given us in Greek;
Who drinks moderately and eats moderately,
Can live without sickness, pain, or suffering.
Live well a hundred years, repeat.

From time to time a good glass of wine,
Will never cause harm to anyone,
But preserves strength;
And also from time to time a kiss,
Will surely do us no harm,
Then one may live a thousand years. repeat.

So whoever wants to live ten centuries,
Should always walk calmly and quietly,
As if on velvet shoes,
And savor with all honor and virtue,
The enjoyment of a generous table feast,
With red wine and kisses, repeat.

label: children
translation:
When Adam was in Paradise, oh dear,  
Still staring like a siskin, oh woe,  
And despite himself once pondered,  
How he could pass the time, oh dear, (chorus)  

Then he saw to his soul’s sorrow, oh dear,  
Only greenery and cattle, and nothing else, oh woe.  
That was all there was, and the poor boy  
Soon fell asleep from boredom, oh dear, (chorus)  
He dreamed quite improperly, oh dear!  
Was tormented by (you know what), oh woe,  
But innocence made the first man  
Take almost no notice of it, oh dear. (chorus)  

He quickly jumped up, and in a snap, oh dear,  
He placed his hand on chest and rib, oh woe,  
And sighed, have pity on my suffering,  
Oh Lord, I am no longer complete, oh dear, (chorus)  

He did not curse, that good fellow, oh dear,  
It was not yet known in Hebrew, oh woe,  
But lamented his misfortune,  
And the lost rib piece, oh dear, (chorus)  

And behold a lovely maiden, hurrah,  
Such as now are few, oh woe,  
Spoke kindly: if you wish,  
Then I will bring exactly what you lack, oh dear. (chorus)  

Then... but who doesn’t know our two, oh dear,  
How that goes with life’s love and sorrow, hurrah,  
A dear wife, a chaste maiden,  
Pleases eye and heart and soul, hurrah, (chorus)  

And though sometimes they cost us a tear, oh dear,  
There now needs no rib anymore, hurrah,  
Thus freely to the fair sex  
Our universal tribute is paid, hurrah, (chorus).

label: children
translation:
Greetings, beloved fields!  
And you, humble thatched roof!  
Here, Eliza, peace dwells,  
That so often the Prince lacked;  
In my little hut our life flows,  
Without sorrow, without pressure,  
Virtue and innocence are my treasures.  
Love and freedom are my happiness. (repeat)  

Should I, oh Princes, bow to you,  
No, for worries are my fate,  
If I were to inhabit a palace,  
Oh, there I would find no joy;  
In my little hut our life flows,  
Without sorrow, without pressure,  
Virtue and innocence are my treasures.  
Love and freedom are my happiness. (repeat)  

Listen; the thunder rolls, how frightful,  
Lightning flies over the earth,  
But it strikes the high spires,  
While it spares the humble hut:  
In my little hut our life flows,  
Without sorrow, without pressure,  
Virtue and innocence are my treasures,  
Love and freedom are my happiness. (repeat)

label: children
translation:
They say: we have come from giants,  
they lie about that.  
Turn around once, little giant, little giant!  
Turn around once, giant flower!  
So mother, put the pot on the fire!  
the giant is here.  
Turn around once, little giant, little giant!  
Turn around once, giant flower!  
So mother, cut a slice of bread!  
the giant is angry.  
Turn around once, little giant, little giant!  
Turn around once, giant flower!  
So mother, pour the best beer!  
the giant is greedy.  
Turn around once, little giant, little giant!  
Turn around once, giant flower!  
So mother, now stop the barrel!  
the giant is drunk.  
Turn around once, little giant, little giant!  
Turn around once, giant flower!

label: children
translation:
Martijn!  
turref in de murref in de moonlight,  
throw in the moss, throw in the wine!  
here lives Saint Martin.  
Martijn had a little pair of scissors,  
that wouldn’t cut,  
Martijn had a little knife,  
that wouldn’t slice,  
Martijn had a little rope,  
that wouldn’t knot.  
give me a little turref or a little piece of wood  
and let me walk with it.  
Here lives a rich man,  
who can give a lot,  
he can give a lot,  
he will live blessedly,  
he will die blessedly,  
he will reach heaven,  
God will reward him  
with a hundred thousand crowns,  
with a hundred thousand little robes on!  
here comes Saint Martin.

give me - a turrefie or a little piece of wood  
and let me walk with it.

who can give a lot,  
a lot he can give,  
blessed will he live,  
blessed will he die,  
heaven will he reach,

with a hundred thousand little coats on!  
here comes Saint Martin.

label: children
translation:
1.  
Saint Nicholas, little rascal!  
Put something in my little shoe!  
Anything that won’t fit inside,  
just put it behind it,  
behind that little glass!  
Thank you, Saint Nicholas!  

2.  
Saint Nicholas Bibabon  
Throw something in the rain barrel!  
Throw something in the houses  
for all the rats and mice!  
Give something, keep something!  
Another year yet again.  

3.  
Saint Nicholas, noble master,  
Bring something in my little shoe,  
An apple or a little lemon,  
A nut to crack,  
That will taste a bit better,  
A little beer to drink,  
That will sound a bit better!  

4.  
Saint Nicholas, good holy man,  
Put on your best robe,  
Ride with it to Amsterdam,  
From Amsterdam to Spain,  
Two little apples from Orange,  
Two little pears from the best tree,  
The child will have a golden crown,  
A golden crown of dock leaves,  
The child will sail to East India,  
From East India back to Sluis,  
Thus the child comes home again.  

5.  
Saint Nicholas, holy man,  
Put on your beautiful robe,  
Ride with it to Amsterdam,  
From Amsterdam to Spain,  
Three little apples from Orange,  
Three little pears from a pear tree,  
Saint Nicholas is my uncle.  
Hossebosse, little horse,  
With your flowing tail:  
This is how the gentlemen ride  
With their colorful clothes;  
This is how the women ride  
With their colorful sleeves:  
This is how the farmer rides  
With his little horse behind.

Hossebosse, little horse,  
with your fluffy tail:  
this is how the gentlemen ride  
with their colorful clothes;  
this is how the women ride  
with their colorful sleeves:  
this is how the farmer rides  
with his little horse behind.

label: children
translation:
Well then! You beautiful dawn!  
What do the birds tell us,  
Do you come with roses in your mouth?  
I would like to see that!

And do you pick from every boy or girl,  
Who sleeps longer than you,  
A little rose from the full cheek  
When you pass by?

And do you give to every boy or girl,  
Who sleeps less than you,  
Two little roses from your rich treasure  
When you pass by? -

Up, children! Up, it is already dusk,  
There comes the Dawn! -  
He must like to see pale faces,  
Those who do not get up now!

label: children
translation:
Children! It is morning!  
Get out of bed!  
Listen! The birds are singing  
Already with sweet sound.  
The little rooster calls you awake,  
Listen! He crows your name,  
And the tree branch taps you  
Against the windowpane.  

No longer sleep!  
Dip your head in the water,  
So that the fresh water  
Splashes around your ears:  
Much still needs to be started,  
Much is still to be done;  
Children! wake up;  
Already it is noon!

label: children
translation:
Oh little rose! Watch out for your bud,  
Or it will almost be day!  
Come, safely raise your little head,  
You have no harm to fear;  
Or don’t you see me standing before you,  
Dressed in all my best clothes?

Didn’t Mother bet yesterday  
That you would already be showing off,  
Before I, still dozing in bed,  
Would look out from my curtain! -  
Oh, slept in! I’ve been dressed for a long time,  
And you are still not half ready!

But I also went to bed early:  
That must be the reason  
That I was the first to get up now,  
And you are still standing there dreaming: -  
Oh, dear Mother! oh tell me now,  
Who did you think would win?

label: children
translation:
Oh! It is quite nice in my garden  
When the sun rises early in the morning,  
But I live right in the middle of a street,  
Right between walls, gray and brown: -  
Yet, as I read yesterday  
And as my Mother told me,  
Out there, in the open field,  
There, children! it must be wonderful.

Then, she said, the sky is still gray at first  
And here and there a star still shines,  
But slowly from afar glimmers  
Some rose-red and heavenly blue;  
Then you see white clouds drifting,  
Suffused with a purple tint;  
Then comes, as if it were a king's child,  
The sun, with all its golden rays. -

See! It is quite nice in my garden  
When the sun rises early in the morning,  
But I live right in the middle of a street,  
Right between walls, gray and brown: -  
Oh! whoever could behold that outside,  
Would surely kneel down with joy,  
And for our Dear Lord  
Would fold their hands a thousand times.

label: children
translation:
How gray your hair, how white your beard,  
You were, oh Winter King!  
We have stared at you fearfully,  
All shivering by the chilly hearth  
In our empty home!  

How terribly expensive the food was,  
How meager the drink;  
How bitterly harsh it was outside,  
How little fire there was inside,  
How scanty the clothes.  

Well then, little hearts! Be happy now,  
And sing once out of sorrow!  
There is the mild season  
With a thousand little flowers, row by row,  
With colors and with scents.  

You have come through that winter again  
And see the Earth dress herself anew:  
Thank God for it, in a joyful choir...  
And—though voice and heart froze,  
They will surely thaw!

label: children
translation:
April, April,  
Does as he wills,  
And gives, according to his pleasure,  
Us sunshine or hailstorms;  
Yet we sing with louder voice  
A song for him,  
And hope that these days  
Will be nothing but clear sunshine  
For us.

April, April,  
Does as he wills;  
But those who behave well,  
Now have no cause to complain;  
Those who have been careful all year,  
Know for sure  
That they will carry away a prize here,  
And with a half-ashamed smile  
May thank him.

April, April!  
Do as you will,  
And give, according to your pleasure,  
Us sunshine or hailstorms;  
Yet for all the good  
That is done to us,  
We will gratefully carry you in our hearts –  
And greet you, with sweet song,  
All our life long.

label: children
translation:
The trees are budding, the blossoms appear,  
The birds sing with lovely sound,  
The little sheep jump through the greening grass,  
I jump with them, as if I were one myself.

And do you know why I jump and sing like this?  
That Winter, I found it such a dreadful thing;  
Then I was cold in my duffel coat,  
And just think how cold a poor fellow must have been!

But now it is nice and soft out on the land;  
Away with coat and scarf! in the greenhouse by the wall;  
Come on, boys! outside, and be really brave,  
We call him 'lazybones,' who was the last one here.

label: children
translation:
Gentle, cool South Wind,  
Messenger! whom our heart loves,  
Do you not carry on your pair of wings  
The most beautiful time of the year?

Flower scents, fresh and sweet,  
You bring as the greeting of Spring;  
Birdsong, rustling leaves,  
Melt in the whisper of your wings.

Good friend, long awaited!  
Tell us! do you blow day and night?  
Then come at night without hesitation,  
And circle around us in the dream!

label: children
translation:
There were seven little frogs  
Already in a farmer's ditch,  
The ditch was frozen over,  
They were half dead,  
They did not croak, they did not croak  
From hunger and sorrow.  

The youngest, who was a wise guy,  
Said to his comrades:  
"Those silly nightingales,  
What a chatter they had!  
If only the ice would thaw first,  
We once sang so beautifully!"  

The gentle, dear Spring came;....  
They croaked the old wise one:  
If they call that singing,  
I wish them back on the ice;  
I give those frogs all of them  
For one nightingale.

label: children
translation:
Do you feel the coolness floating;  
Do you smell the sweet scent?  
Children! That is life!  
Spring is at the door again:  
Let her in, let her in;  
Spring love, whom we adore!

Do you hear that dragging whistle,  
That penetrates through all the windows?  
Nightingale sings outside;  
Hear how pure his song is!  
Let him in, let him in,  
The singer, whom we adore!

No! Open door and window,  
Hang hat and coat on the rack,  
But – to walk freely and pleasantly  
Through the fresh, tender grass:  
Spring! Linger, do not come inside,  
Outside we will adore you.

label: children
translation:
The birds jump through the hedge  
And sing out of sorrow;  
May scatters blossoms along the way  
And knocks on every door,  
And see! there comes with the joyful sound  
The young folk out of the houses.

You, children! sat all winter long  
Shivering and mourning;  
Now that lovely birdsong seems  
To cheer your hearts;  
Come, then sing and jump in battle,  
So boldly as if you were birds.

And sing low and sing high,
