And sing out of sorrow,  
And see the spring mists tearing  
At the arch of Heaven,  
Then think of our dear Lord,  
Who gives blossoms and spring weather.

My bird choir, my children's choir,  
That’s better than mourning!  
Sing freely and happily through your spring  
In sunshine and scents,  
And think – even when you are older –  
‘After winter day comes springtime!’

label: children
translation:
Whoever wants to be strong and tall,  
Wants to grow up brave,  
Just walk with your little head  
In the gentle rain!  
Do not be afraid of a splash,  
Jump in fresh,  
Do not fear a drop or two,  
Little boy!  

Just look at the little flowers,  
How they drink the drizzle!  
Look closely, how on the leaf  
All those drops shine!  
In that lovely, gentle wetness  
Lies Heaven's blessing;  
Therefore, little one! Hurry up,  
Walk boldly in the rain!

label: children
translation:
Green twig, sprouting from the earth,  
What will become of you as you grow?  
Will you become useless or harmful weed,  
Will you bloom like a rose or a carnation,  
Or will you bear delicious fruit? -

Young people! now still small and tender,  
What will become of you as you grow?  
Will you grow more and more into shame,  
Or will you bloom into a glorious ornament,  
And bear fruit, to God's honor?

Yet the little plant – whether bad or good,  
Oh! can only grow;  
You – can, according to body and mind,  
Bloom in strength and skill and piety...  
If only you put your best effort into it!

So when you walk in the garden  
And see plant and herb grow,  
Then give yourself ample reason to be thankful,  
That you, by free choice, can bloom  
To joy or sorrow – to shame or praise!

label: children
translation:
I have a small, small garden,  
There I grow flowers;  
And when my dear little sister comes,  
I sing happily with joy:  

- "Little toddler, little toddler!  
What are you doing in my garden?  
You are picking all the little flowers  
And making it far too rough!" -  

And when she then sees those plants,  
Grown with so much care,  
I bet that dear child  
Does not break any of the little flowers.

label: children
translation:
Green leaves, pointed tip,
Angular at the edges!
Tell me, how can you grow
On those bare walls?

- I lay discarded before the door,
Dirty and overflowing;
Found here or there a crack,
I crawled inside.

Every ray I caught
Gave me strength and life,
Every droplet that hung there
Made me strive higher.

Thanking our Dear Lord
For His sun and rain,
I grew daily more and more
In Heaven’s blessing. -

Whoever does not despise the smallest
That God wants to give him,
Can grow to his stature
And live contentedly!

label: children
translation:
Well, apple tree! How richly you stand,  
You look like a single blossom!  
Wherever I gaze, wherever I look,  
I see only, only blossoms;  
I see no branch, I see no leaf;  
It seems to me, you never had them!

And yet, it's only been a day or so,  
While I looked outside,  
That I saw your branches bare and smooth  
Winding in front of my windows,  
And I already asked the gardener’s wife,  
If you were also dead?

“No,” she said then, “no, dear darling!”  
And pinched my cheeks;  
“No, wait just a day or so more,  
Then it will stand like a sheet:—  
And if you behave, little man!  
Then you will get some apples too.”

label: children
translation:
Pick flowers, pick flowers,  
Pick flowers and grass!  
And rub them together  
Through the hair  
While playing around:  
Where else would grass and flowers grow  
And bloom!

And whoever makes a grumpy face  
While doing so,  
Or yawns,  
Or screams  
When we play around,  
He does not need to see how the little flowers grow  
And bloom.

He can just go home  
And crawl into the corner,  
The grump who complains  
And grumbles  
While we play around:  
For him, it’s not that the little flowers grow  
And bloom!

label: children
translation:
Now once jumped around,  
Jumped hand in hand,  
Now happily sung once,  
Summer is in the land;  
The blossom of the trees  
Has long ago flown far and wide;  
Summer has arrived:  
Now it is cherry time!  

How delicious they will taste,  
Those cherries, with wine inside;  
How we will crack them  
The hard cherry pit;  
Oh! if only we were already seated  
Before a bowl full to the brim,  
We would eat from it  
Even if it were a hundred times.

label: children
translation:
Cherries, cherries, soft and red,  
Hanging among green leaves;  
Oh! The tree is richly laden;  
Catch when picking, in your lap,  
Cherries, cherries soft and red!

Cherries, cherries, ripe and fresh,  
Isn't it enough to make your mouth water?  
Come, I pick with full hands,  
The most goes to who is the most skillful,  
Cherries, cherries, ripe and fresh!

Cherries, cherries of May,  
Every month the roses bloom,...  
All months you had to grow  
For the children’s sweet row, -  
Cherries, cherries of May!

label: children
translation:
Boys! There are starlings over there!  
Let's climb to get a better view;  
Look, they are watching over the nest:  
Listen to that young one crying out!  
The old one flies back and forth,  
As if it can guess what's happening.  

Oh! How defeated that creature looks;  
I find it a bit upsetting: -  
Boys! wouldn't it be better, really,  
Not to bother the poor animal?  
Just imagine if someone came,  
Who took us away from Mother like that!

label: children
translation:
They flew high, they flew low,  
They jumped and they sang,  
They quickly and gladly pecked worms,  
And brought them to their young;  
And the little ones in the nest called:  
“Peep, peep... thank you very much. - Peep, peep!”

And I thought, when I found that so nice:  
“Am I also such a grateful child?” -  
And I immediately  
Jumped around my little mother’s neck;  
And mother held me when I cried:  
“Peep, peep... thank you very much. - Peep, peep!”

label: children
translation:
Little bird, on a green branch!  
What a cheerful song you sing!  
In our entire book  
We cannot find such a joyful tune;  
Oh! Tell us, tell us, lovely creature!  
Who has been your master?

You sing so pure and so high,  
So perfectly in time,  
And our hearts brim with joy  
When your little throat sings;  
Oh! Tell us, tell us, lovely creature!  
Who has been your master?

Oh! Surely it is the good God,  
Who has entrusted you,  
So that you may announce His kindness  
To the ears of the blind;  
Oh yes! We know it, lovely creature!  
Who has been your master.

label: children
translation:
Little dove with your white feathers!  
Do you not fly through all weather,  
Can no rain shower harm you  
As you wander here and there?  
Your crest is always clean,  
Your little plumes, white dove!

- If the wind blows too hard outside,  
I can be home whenever I want;  
I find all my comrades there  
In the warm dovecote:  
But swiftly or at the strike,  
I clean my feathers every day!

- Preened and smoothed  
With some water from my pot,  
I am worthy to be seen  
In the air and in the coop:....  
Children! now that you know the trick,  
I pray you do not forget it! -

label: children
translation:
Robin, robin, witty thief!  
Have you ever sat there showing off like that?  
The little copper cage and the tray full of food:  
Lovely jumper! how I love you!  
Yet your little throat does not sing cheerful songs,  
Little bird! tell me, what has happened,  
That you let your feathers hang so limp,  
Robin! tell me why you mourn?

- Dear boy, dear boy! there was my cage,  
There was also my food, tasty and neat,  
Outside in the forest it is still so lively:  
To be free, darling! is better than beauty!  
Oh! let me go, let me jump in freedom,  
Over there on that tree, where no bars disturb me;  
My friend! then I will sing a song for you  
Like you have never, ever heard! -

label: children
translation:
Hear, children! What a clear sound  
Penetrates through the leaves over there!  
The birds have long been asleep,  
And so, surely no one sings like that.  
Who can sing so wonderfully?  
It’s true, my heart is beating from it!  

Quiet! – That is surely the nightingale,  
Mother said so much about it;  
I didn’t know it could be heard  
When we were put to sleep,  
Otherwise I would have long asked Mother  
If I might go to bed a little later?  

Oh! If I might ever be sick again,  
I wish spring would then begin;  
Then I would surely hear the nightingale  
When I could not sleep at night! –  
Say, Mother! Does that sweet creature  
Not mostly sing for sick children?

label: children
translation:
You, dear birds, shy and wild!  
How gently you sing your songs,  
How joyfully you warble!  
It is as if the whole forest trembles  
To catch the tones!  

The rustling and whispering of every leaf,  
As if it were full of ears  
And could hear the lovely song;  
As if it had a thousand tongues  
And sang along in your chorus.  

You, shy birds! are not afraid,  
Though voices join in your wild singing  
The children's pious melodies;  
We wish with you, often and long,  
To praise the good God.

label: children
translation:
I wish I were a bird,  
A little bird with feathers,  
What could harm me?  
I would fly high, I would fly fast  
And no one could stop me:  
No school or bed would matter anymore  
If I were a free bird!

- Say, little friend! Have you considered,  
How often the thrushes stay on the bars,  
How many finches are caught under the net,  
How many snipes have been trapped,  
And that many a falcon awaits  
That naive generation?

- Even if you flew high, even if you flew fast,...  
Those troublesome guns  
They would stop you:  
What good would it do if you were a bird,  
A little bird with feathers?  
Believe me (it still comes in handy):  
Who is wise remains what he was!

label: children
translation:
Just as the bee sucks sweet honey  
From flower and fragrant herb;  
You, dear children! do as it does:  
Let nothing pass in your springtime,  
Or draw a lesson from it.

And in her house of sticky wax  
She has gathered together  
All that she found in field or greenhouse; -  
The little flowers, children! dry quickly!  
Who is wise, preserves!

Do the same in your joyful youth,  
Where everything bears blossom;  
What a joy it is, later on,  
If in old age you still remember  
What you saw in your youth!

label: children
translation:
Lamb! Are you bleating so lonely  
Over the heath?  
How did you get here, so abandoned by everyone?  
Wouldn't you rather have stayed over there in the meadow?  
Lamb! Here no flowers or grass grow,  
There is no water you would like,  
There is no shade to rest under,...  
And if you weren't still so small!

- Children! I had been wandering and playing for so long  
Over there on the meadow;  
Always grazing began to bore me,  
I wanted to see what it was like on the heath.  
Oh! Now I have wandered farther and farther,  
I am looking for my mother, I am looking for my friends,  
I am looking to find some grass and some water:  
If only I were home, I wouldn’t leave it again! -

Little sheep! We will teach you the way  
Over the heath,  
Go with us and no harm will come to you,  
Certainly! We will bring you back to the meadow.  
But hurry up, or we will leave you behind,  
Mother surely is already watching where we are,  
Truly, I would not like to upset her so,  
As you have done to your mother today!

label: children
translation:
Out there in the meadow  
Go lambs, small and large;  
They graze from morning  
Until the evening glow.  
So that no harm may touch them,  
No lamb should stray,  
A watchful shepherd  
Keeps the flock devoutly and faithfully!

But even more than the shepherd  
Guards the little lambs,  
God watches over good children,  
That no harm befalls them;  
He looks down protectively  
From Heaven upon us,  
And when we wander unknowingly,  
He leads us back for good!

label: children
translation:
Lily, with your white robe!  
Rose, with your purple little hat!  
The poor boy and the girl greet you...  
And they indeed find  
That your attire suits you beautifully.

The boy sees his half-worn jacket  
Covered with many patches,  
The girl looks at her cap,  
Here full of slips, there full of fluff...  
Half saddened, they go home!

But when mother, over there, so happy,  
Sits sewing and stitching,  
To mend those clothes—  
Flowers! oh, then we find  
Ourselves happier than you!

Whether one has much or little...  
Each of us can please,  
If we wear skillfully and gratefully  
What the hand of love gives us;—  
Whether one has much or little!

label: children
translation:
Hunger, hunger! ugly word,  
When an empty stomach hears you!  
When the teeth water,  
And one stands with empty hands....  
When you boldly glance at full bowls or baskets,  
But the stomach remains vacant.

Yet—however troublesome you sometimes are,  
You also make us doubly glad,  
We will gladly welcome you  
When we eagerly sit down  
For a dish of tasty food....  
Eager tooth chews the meal,  
Earned by oneself and paid for oneself!

Therefore our happy song calls out:  
Dear Hunger! torment us long!  
Rich and sick would often  
(If it could!) pay you with gold—  
You will not fail us so easily;  
But if you desire thanks and praise,  
Come then (if you can!) armed with.... food!

label: children
translation:
There walked a girl along the road;  
Her little eyes were red:  
‘I know no hedge or path in the land,  
My father and mother are dead,  
My eldest brother is a soldier;  
Oh, if only I had work, then I would know what to do!’  

A good farmer’s wife saw that  
And said: ‘My dear child!  
Why do you walk barefoot in the cold  
And cry your little eyes blind!  
Whoever wants to work, always finds a way!  
I still have plenty for you.’ –  

She worked late, she worked early  
And served faithfully and diligently;  
A sturdy and wealthy farmer  
Brought her home as his wife;  
But even though she had abundance then,  
She still remained diligent, early and late.

label: children
translation:
If only it were just for giving,  
Then I would give every child,  
Who wished to be loved,  
A little heart made of the finest glass;  
Of glass... of glass?  
Yes indeed, of glass!

“I must take good care of it!”  
Thought each one then (no matter how small!)  
“Because, if I don’t keep it clean,  
One soon sees a blemish on the glass;  
On the glass... on the glass?  
Yes indeed, on the glass!”

And—if it first gets a crack or scratch  
They never go away  
And it makes a false sound  
When someone taps on the glass;  
On the glass... on the glass!  
Yes indeed, on the glass!

Oh! If only it were just for giving...  
But look! It is just the same  
When you, oh children! act  
As if your little heart were made of glass;  
Of glass... of glass?  
Yes indeed, of glass!

label: children
translation:
Hey! Good morning, Pear Tree!  
How richly loaded you are;  
I see, from whichever side I come,  
More fruits than leaves almost;  
It truly is as if leaf by leaf  
Had turned into pear by pear.  

And there you, dear Pear Tree!  
Stand so beautifully to show off,  
Father calls: - ‘Boy! now don’t stand  
Lazily looking underneath;  
Come on, get in - such a great treasure  
Remains great, even if it loses some!’  

So, dearest Pear Tree!  
I will no longer fear  
And without hesitation take  
A sturdy sack full of your pears;  
And I will give every neighbor boy something,  
Who does not have such a generous tree.

label: children
translation:
Celebrate, celebrate!.... backwards!....  
Will your kite be able to rise  
You must grant it the strings,  
Or the chance that is wasted:  
Give your kite free air,  
Give it space for its flight.  

Run, run, as fast as you can!  
If your kite wants to descend  
You must go fetch it again,  
With a tighter, stronger pull:  
Without effort no pleasure  
With that flying paper.  

Grip, grip, as much as you can!  
If you cannot keep it above,  
At least take care of your ropes....  
It is a lesson for old age: -  
Whoever can manage all that in time,  
Becomes a wise man.

label: children
translation:
Between Cologne and Paris  
Lies the road to Rome:  
All who want to come with us,  
Must understand our ways:  
Good manners,  
Sweet manners,  
Such are our manners.  

Whether you are small or great,  
You can always learn;  
If you wish to be loved,  
Just keep our manners as a friend:  
Good manners,  
Pious manners,  
Such are our manners.  

If you are not of Dutch blood,  
Do you not want to remain so?  
Then hold, children! where it fits,  
Then keep Dutch manners firmly;  
Old manners,  
Faithful manners,  
Such are our manners.

label: children
children
translation:
Little hare was in the ripening grain,  
Nibbling on the green herb; -  
Little hare, little hare! be careful,  
Didn’t a hunter’s horn sound there?  
Didn’t a horse’s gallop sound there?  
Didn’t a dog bark there?  

“One more leaf, one more herb,...  
I can run, fast enough,  
It’s, truly! still too early,  
Just that one little shoot....”  
Bang!.... there the gun fired,  
The little hare fell down bleeding!  

Little hare, little hare! I’m sorry for you,  
But your fate is deserved:  
If you had been less greedy, friend!  
The dog wouldn’t bite you now:  
Who doesn’t listen to good advice,  
Will regret it too late!

label: children
translation:
Jaapje wanted to shoot a partridge,  
And he hit his neighbor's sheep.  
Everyone said: What a foolish boy! -  
Jaapje did not let it upset him,  
And he loaded his gun  
For another time.

With that, he grabbed it in his hands,  
Shot and hit a fat hare;  
Everyone shouted: You are the boss! -  
Jaapje made them drool,  
And he happily brought it  
To his neighbor.

I have read in earlier times:  
'Hans suffers because of his foolishness!'  
That is not always true,  
It may be so now and then;  
But, when you make a foolish mistake,  
Make it good again quickly!

label: children
translation:
Look! October is in the land!  
Branches bend full of apples, sacks,  
Almost breaking, the thickest branches,  
Give a basket!  
No! Give a thousand, a thousand baskets!  
Pick and gather, with full hands,  
Now, from all this abundance,  
Fill your belly properly.  

But, remember, young heart!  
It is (no matter how much you may gather)  
Good to save an apple  
For thirst!  
When winter has come,  
You will find none on the trees anymore,  
And your overly eager tooth  
Bites lightly into an empty basket!  

So, if this applies to apple and pear,  
Dear child! — in your riper years  
You will see that saving like this  
Applies to more!...  
So start early and young  
Always saving from your gains,  
To ensure that you, when old,  
Keep apples for your thirst.

label: children
translation:
Behold, the moon shines through the trees,  
Friends! cease your wild noise;  
The glorious evening has come,  
The evening of Saint Nicholas!  
With anticipation our hearts beat,  
Who will get the cookie, who will get the rod!

Oh! What fun it will be to play  
With that colorful harlequin!  
Honestly, we will share everything,  
Sugar treats and marzipan;  
But, oh woe! what bitter pain,  
If for a cookie, we got a rod!

Yet I do not fear that we will complain,  
Father, Mother are too good!  
Even if not every day,  
Many times we have been good!  
So banish fear from your heart,  
I bet there is not a single rod!

label: children
translation:
Snow covers the lands:
Washes the chilly hands
So they tingle and burn,
Kneads the snowball firm;
Strongly the arm raised,
Quickly the ball driven:
Ha! that is a life,
That suits boys!

The cold-shivering may warm themselves
In their Mother's arms,
Cry out for mercy,
When the ball hits them;
Let the stove glow,....
From our roughhousing
The blood will surely flow faster,
Where the snowball cracks!

label: children
translation:
I like a winter evening like this,  
Even if there's a storm outside,  
Even if the hail lashes sharply  
And bangs against the windows;  
Inside here is a strong glow  
And chocolate, warm and sweet,  
And crisp rusks.  

We did not ask for these treats,  
But we enjoy them,  
And sometimes sing a cheerful song  
That can entertain children;  
And empty many a large dish,  
And hear many a strange tale  
Of all kinds of matters.  

But when the fun comes to an end,  
And the fire grows weaker,  
Then we cheerfully go to bed,  
And greet friend and mate;  
Then we lay down gratefully,  
And pray: 'O! Protect us, Lord!  
And awaken us in health!'

label: children
translation:
Little children! Aren't you tired yet? –  
Or do you already want to rub your little eyes,  
Look! They are closing so gently;  
Didn’t you ask to stay awake until later?  
If only you had thought of Klaas Vaak;  
Little children! Come, your bed awaits!

Isn’t it as if your little heart is sleeping? –  
Aren't your cheeks growing tighter already?  
Fie, how your little mouth is yawning!  
Sing along once, then you’ll wake up!  
Then get dressed and pray to the Lord;  
Little children! Come, it’s time and more!

label: children
translation:
Children! When you go to sleep,  
Pray with such a devout heart,  
As if it is for the last time  
And perhaps you will not rise again.  

Thank for the good that God has given you,  
Him who bestowed it upon you;  
Ask forgiveness from our Lord  
For the wrongs you have committed.  

The little eyes, burdened by sleep,  
May then go to rest;  
You will open them cheerfully  
In Heaven or on earth.

label: children
translation:
It is written in the Bible,
That God's Angels hover around us,
And watch over us in the night;
That no evil can harm us
And that all pious children
Sleep safely in their watch!

Often when I had prayed
It was as if upon my eyelids
A night kiss was pressed:
Could that not be an Angel,
I thought - and with holy reverence
I bowed my head to rest.

label: children
translation:
We, children, kneel down in thanks,  
And praise You, oh Dear Lord!  
Now that we awaken from sweet sleep,  
Your hand made our pillow soft,  
Your angels watched all night,  
So that no harm could befall us;  
And now You give us new strength: –  
Oh Father! Thanks be to You!

label: children
translation:
Now the church bell calls with a soft sound  
All the children out to their homes;  
To the House of the Lord go great and small,  
In silent reverence, attentively there.  

To thank Him, who does so much good  
For all people and children;  
To pray to Him, that He may forgive  
What each of us has done wrong.  

O Dear Lord, You, good God!  
O teach us to live according to Your command!  
And may every church visit be a feast for us,  
On which we praise You with a joyful spirit.

label: children
translation:
Hey! Are your little eyes still full of dew?  
Watch out, along meadow and field,  
The little flowers are waking up, -  
Awake, awake, awake!

Oh, little flowers! That was a mistake,  
You should know that!  
We had not forgotten  
That joyful, joyful celebration.

No, long ago we have been ready,  
From head to toe,  
To greet dear Master,  
To greet him pair by pair.

And when he enters the school,  
We will welcome him  
With songs  
And verses for the good he has done for us.

Then we pray to our Dear Lord:  
“Oh, might You grant us favor,  
To live for his joy,  
And for You, O God! for honor!”

label: children
translation:
“To school, to school! The clock struck eight!”  
Oh look! On all the roads,  
From sidewalk and stairs, along street and canal,  
A small group comes towards us;  
Quite cold, but clean of cheek and hand;  
Quite poor, but bright as a flame;  
And in their little eyes you can see,  
That they very much want to go to school.

First the happy crowd wrestles and laughs  
And jumps on sidewalks and stones;  
Then you softly see pair by pair  
Joining into a line;  
And if there’s a rascal here or there,  
They still don’t cause any mischief: —  
There may be a touch of fun  
For those who so gladly go to school.

But then to school, — and pay attention!  
The lesson must be read well,  
The notebook must be neat in the cupboard,  
The sum must be correct;  
And think about everything during the lesson,  
It is all for my own good: —  
Because if you go to school like this,  
You will come away wise and well-behaved.

label: children
translation:
Come now, my smooth skipping stone,  
I will throw you, sharp and flat!  
Come, hop over the water,  
And kis-kas along the surface of the wet,  
Until you, almost like a Pailjas,  
Make a jump into the green grass!

Hey, boys! watch out - he’s going!  
Kis-kas... one, two, three, four! kas-kis,  
Again... plomp!... Missed it, comrade! -  
Another... plomp!... that’s really off!  
Once more - what’s good happens in three!...  
There he jumps - hoop! - there he is... see!

Hooray! - as if he were a bird,  
That’s how he skimmed the mirror-like surface!  
And look! quite far in the grass,  
There he lies, over there, by that brown branch!...  
Thank you, my smooth skipping stone!  
If you jump I’ll - but to school now, go!

label: children
translation:
Hey! That’s something else, compared to school  
Sitting stiff on those hard benches,  
Looking at those black boards,  
And being sweet like dumb Jool!  
Come on, come on! Learning is just nonsense,  
I think I’ll still find my own way to earn a living!...

What do you want, man?... half a cent?  
I truly have none to give....  
But can’t you live from your work,  
And do you have to beg, poor guy?  
You look big and strong otherwise,  
How come – don’t you have work?

“Oh, dear child! When I was younger,  
I did not want to behave well  
And when the school bell rang,  
I went wandering in the green grass;  
I didn’t want to learn when I had to,  
And now I have to beg for my living.”

Oh, poor man! I’m shocked by that:  
I was just about to start working;  
But I’ll let your example convince me...  
Come by our place tomorrow: –  
If Mother has forgiven me,  
It’s certain that she’ll give you some buttered bread.

label: children
translation:
I wished to be like a little bird  
To fly and to jump like that;  
I wished to be like a little bird  
To sing a cheerful song,  
And I gladly told everyone  
That I am so happy today!

And do you want to know why?  
Oh, just listen for a moment!  
I did my lesson well,  
I wrote my notebook neatly:  
And Mother pulled me onto her lap  
And kissed my cheeks red.

label: children
translation:
Oh Springtime love, oh thief of hearts!  
How beautiful are your clothes,  
Embroidered with little white and blue flowers  
And little pearls of morning dew  
And colorful bird feathers!  
How beautiful you are, how beautiful you are,  
My darling! in that rich attire.

And God the Lord, who for his honor,  
Oh Spring! wished to clothe you so,  
Will surely pay even greater care  
To the poor child,  
Whose mouth praises Him, whose heart loves Him.  
His goodness grants the shy youth  
The attire of knowledge and virtue.

Rich season! that we  
May again show gratitude today  
(Though in worn-out dress and jacket),  
Now love wants to reward us  
For virtue and diligence  
In the Lord’s House;  
That love looks not at show nor attire,  
But thinks: ‘The hearts are beautiful after all!’

label: children
translation:
The brook thaws, the snow layer melts –  
And a thousand flower buds  
Raise their little heads again  
And look across the budding field;  
Half, yes! with contentment –  
But half with shy questions:  
“Say! Is Spring far enough along,  
That we dare take the risk...  
Or are we coming too early?”

You dear little flowers, white and red,  
You delicate daisies,  
Oh! be careful, little thieves!  
You were so safe in Mother’s lap!  
No winter could harm you...  
And as with downy feathers  
You were covered by the white snowflake spread:  
What more could you desire  
You flowers of the meadow?

“Desire, yes!... desire, no!  
But still, to look around  
That would seem nice to us!”...  
That’s fine! – I am content with that;  
I answered your little questions:  
Yet the sun still shines slowly –  
And snow and ice come again...  
Then, curious little eyes!  
Learn wisdom through shame and disgrace!

label: children
translation:
The winter chill has been driven away:  
Look! What flowers, everywhere,  
The garden, the hedge, the orchard bears.  
I hope it will no longer freeze!  
And that I see no other snow again,  
Than that  
Which falls as white blossom!

Or will the swallow soon arrive? -  
With straw and finely combed wool  
I made the nests, in the stable  
And under the gutter, full.  
I think: surely the faithful bird  
Will be pleased,  
That I love him so much!  
And when our stork returns again,  
The long-legged one with his long beak,  
Then it will be a joy! - quite a bit more,  
Than when he stood at his departure,  
And everyone sadly looked at the roof  
And said:  
‘He takes the summer with him!’

So it sounds, then, on every side,  
While we gather for a dance,  
‘Hurray! - Spring has come to the land,  
Weave her a mayflower wreath!’ -  
And Poverty calls out, as happily once again  
As we do:  
‘Cold and want are now past!’

label: children
translation:
In May,  
All the little birds  
Lay an egg!  
And where they sit and brood,  
There we will not bother.  

In May,  
A very small little bird  
Crawls out of the egg!  
Who would want to harm it?  
It doesn’t even have feathers yet.  

In May,  
All the little birds  
Lay an egg!  
And whoever hinders those little creatures,  
Those are truly naughty children.

label: children
translation:
I must praise you nevertheless,  
My little buttercup!  
Though the other flowers  
Still looked so sour about it;  
You stand so sparkling in the forest,  
As if you were made of pure gold.  

The little flowers in the pots,  
The little flowers in the cupboard,  
They are easily mocked,  
They are protected;  
You stand in all wind and weather  
And still grow and bloom just the same.  

I could learn from you,  
My Father told me:  
More than the finest clothes  
Contentment adorns us; –  
So that a poor and grateful child  
Deserves to be loved twice as much!

label: children
translation:
None of the little flowers still have their buds closed,  
The dear little sun smiles kindly,  
The playful birds jump high and low;  
Oh Lord! how beautiful everything is today!  

Oh Lord!... shame! I said that word thoughtlessly—  
And yet everything speaks of God's love and power;  
Yes, everything praises the goodness of the Lord,  
And blooms and laughs and hops in His honor.  

And I, who may enjoy so much good,  
Should not mention God's name with reverence,  
Nor think of the Giver of that good,  
Who so fatherly watches over my youth!...

Forgive me, O Lord! and may my love and thanks  
Praise Your goodness all my life long;  
And every day my soul says in prayer:  
Oh, dear Lord! how great and good You are!

label: children
translation:
Violet, soft in colors!  
You adorn my little garden,  
Violet, sweet in fragrance!  
I sing a song in your praise;  
When all the little flowers rest  
And slumber in their buds,  
Then I still inhale with delight  
Your lovely scents.  

So therefore, confidently  
Stick your head out of the grass,  
As if you were a little bud  
Of rose or lily.  
Oh, why hide away,  
My little sweetheart!  
Tell me, why should you sulk?  
I love you just the same!  

Who is lovely in nature  
And humble in spirit,  
(I read this recently)  
Is doubly beautiful and good:  
And so, modest little flower!  
I now sing in your praise,  
And honor you as the little flower,  
The little flower of my garden.

label: children
translation:
Ow, ow, my whole hand is burning!....
Or did a beast bite me?
No! That is that wicked plant,
What is it called again?
That naughty herb, -
It looked so nice though!

“Nettle, my dear child!
That’s the name of that herb,
And if you hadn’t been so hasty, friend!
It wouldn’t have bitten you:
That little pain
It teaches you to be careful now!”

“Everything you see for the first time
(You may remember this!),
Don’t grab, test, or smell it,
Because it could bitterly harm you:
So don’t dare anything,
Without first asking a wiser person.”

label: children
translation:
Bluebell, blooming by the stream,  
I believe your name is  
Forget  
Me not!  
If I knew that I dared to pick you,  
I would pin you to my chest;  
Then I would see  
Your splendid colors all day long!  
And I dreamed of it the whole night;  
Say, little flower—blue! say... may I?

"Dear girl! if you really want to,  
Pick me here for pleasure—  
But know,  
If you pin me to your chest,  
I will die there from heat and thirst!...  
If I am to bloom  
Under the fierce rays of the sun,  
Then the water from the spring  
Must sprinkle my little roots!"  
"Then leave me here, by the fresh water!...  
But wait, my child!  
I find  
Something else!  
Dig, around my little roots, a clod  
Of earth, very carefully,  
And place me,  
Watered, in a cool spot  
On the windowsill of your room,  
Then I will live for a long time—what do you bet?"

The girl gently dug out  
The roots  
Of the plant,  
And went home, and placed it  
In a perfectly beautiful little vase there  
On the shelf,  
By the small window—and week after week  
Blooms there the Forget-me-not by the brook  
And daily whispers: "Thank you!"

label: children
translation:
Poor little flower! How withered you look,  
Your petals shrivel on the stem;  
How pale they are, how yellow too!  
Tell me, little flower! Is it water you lack?  
Ah! Wait, let me carefully pour for you...  
That feels good, doesn’t it, little flower?

- Oh, dear child! How kind and good you are!  
I feel fresher again with every drop,  
And all my petals brighten up:  
Oh, it’s so sad to have to wither away,  
While a little water from the spring,  
If someone would give it, could help us.

And it gives you pleasure too, right? Doesn’t it?  
You feel your little heart beat happier  
Now that you have done me a kindness:  
Oh! Think of me when you see a poor soul:  
Sometimes helping the struggler in deep need  
With a sip of water and a bit of bread. -

label: children
translation:
Mother, how short-lived the little roses are!  
Yesterday, you know how beautifully they stood,  
I had just tied them to the sticks, -  
Now most are wilted and withered;  
Look how the wind now makes their petals flutter,  
Only the rose hips remain....  
Mother! I thought it would be nicer,  
If they only bloomed until late in the year!

- Dear! Everything has its turn and its day.  
How would seed grow in the rose hips,  
If all those little roses did was bloom?  
I bet that soon none would be seen anymore!  
It is just like the children and the roses:  
Think, if playing makes your cheeks blush,  
Always playing brings no benefit;  
Playing and working, each has its time! -

label: children
translation:
Oh! could it be true, playful creature!  
What Grandma told me just now,  
That you once were a little caterpillar  
(You, who now float around the field!)  
And had to crawl along branch and leaf  
Or hide in a chrysalis?  

How happy you must be, if it is true,  
Now that you may fly so pleasantly,  
And now, from roses and jasmine,  
You gather your meal every day...  
And now the little wings, on which you soar,  
Are adorned so exquisitely beautiful! —  

And in the arbor sat Grandma...  
She gently pulled me onto her lap  
And closed her eyes in prayer,  
And said: ‘So it shall be, after death,  
Freed from earthly struggle and pain,  
The good person shall be an Angel!

label: children
translation:
Dear little creatures in the meadow!  
Do you not find all kinds of things here?  
Flowers, clover, grass, and herbs,  
Each one seeks its own delight;  
On that green tablecloth  
There is something ready for everyone.

Listen to how that bird sings,  
Look how that foal jumps,  
The little cow mooing and the little sheep bleating,  
Each one speaks according to its nature,  
Each one gives thanks in its own language  
For that precious meal.

Shame on me that I must stand embarrassed,  
I who have begun to eat  
(Oh! Too often and for too long)  
Without praying, without giving thanks! –  
Listen! Now I will never eat again  
Without thanking our Dear Lord!

label: children
translation:
Little bird, so black with feathers,  
With your beak yellow as gold!  
I hear you daily chirping  
In the tops of the trees;  
High and dry you sing your song, -  
Come travel here... or do you not dare?

Don’t you see those red berries,  
Which you love so much?  
Look, they wind themselves like clusters  
Around the shrubs of the forest;  
Just look, taste, come here,  
Come, my dearest animal!

- Oh! you thought you could fool me,  
Boy!... but I keep deaf!  
I saw all too well the traps  
Hidden among the leaves:  
Foolish is he, who lets himself be caught  
For a treat: - I stay free! -

label: children
translation:
A nightingale sits in the tree,
That stands before my window;
He sings so early, he sings so late,
I still hear him in my dream at night;
And in the morning his clear song calls:
“How long you sleep, how long you sleep!”

Well, dear little nightingale!
Tell me, do you not sleep yourself?
And at night, who then hears your song?
Everyone sleeps!
Or do you perhaps sing for the guard,
Because he keeps watch at night?

The Master says, when winter comes,
Then you go far from here;
Oh, just come inside, dear creature,
If then there is a lack of deck or food!
I will shelter you by my side,
And in summer I will let you be free and unrestrained!

label: children
translation:
Did I hear birds warbling  
In the quiet, cool forest,  
I used to look at their feathers: –  
I thought that purple, blue, or gold  
Must play around the little throat,  
That could trill so heavenly beautifully.  

But – my dear nightingales  
Oh! they quickly taught me  
That a throat gray and pale  
Was fullest with beautiful songs,  
And with the most glorious sound magic  
Most modestly hid away in the foliage.  

Are you poor in clothes,  
Poor boy, poor maiden!  
I still want to love and honor you,  
If you show, in your heart, some courage:  
I could learn from the nightingale,  
That it’s not in the... feathers!

label: children
translation:
Well, dear little spider! How very small you are!  
How dare you walk like that, and all by yourself?  
Tell me, don’t you have a mother, a sister, or a maid,  
And no bed like mine where you lie down in the evening? –  
Well, dear little spider! If I took you with me,  
And gave you a rose petal for your evening bread;  
But would you then promise, when you grow bigger,  
That you won't bite those little flies so fiercely anymore?

label: children
translation:
Whether the wind tears your little web apart,  
Or a bumblebee comes brushing through it,  
And whether it happens again and again,  
Little spider! you never sit watching listlessly: –  
“It won’t be whole again, whether one grumbles or mourns!”  
You think so, and boldly get back to work  
And make your little web twice as strong.

Listen, I wish I were always like that,  
Even if my work sometimes gets stained or spoiled...  
That I could so cheerfully, so skillfully, so quickly  
Repair again what some air had corrupted;  
Truly, your example is just what I need...  
It proves again: there is no creature so ugly,  
But it has still been useful somewhere!

label: children
translation:
Naughty! Bad bee!
Why are you stinging me?
I meant you no harm:
I just took a moment to see how things are going
And how your little hive is doing,
And whether you have already gathered
Many a fat honeycomb
For the winter days.

- Yes, child! I work
Half the year
For my honey and my wax;
I roam and fly over meadows and ponds,
And seek the little flowers in the grass
And on the highest hilltops,
And suck my honey and my wax
From all the buds.

And when I then,
My little man!
Tiredly trudge home,
And I see there creeping, soft and quiet,
A boy who secretly wants to steal,
Then I do not forget to sting....
Because I believe that those who do not want to work
Should not eat either. -

label: children
translation:
Well, kitty! How soft your little skin is,  
Your paws, they seem like velvet,  
So white is your nose and so colorful is your coat,  
You purr so kindly when I play with you,  
Such nice little cats, there are not many: -  
What stops me from giving you a kiss!  
Because you know, my thief! I love you so much!  
But scratching, you must not do that!

- Well, friend! With my paws as soft as velvet  
I smooth my little hairs;  
I wash myself often, I wash myself a lot,  
Who would want to play with a dirty cat?  
And I have often had some of your milk too;  
Oh, go ahead and give me a kiss:  
And scratching, my friend! - You are a sweet child,  
That is what I would do to the naughtier ones. -

label: children
translation:
‘Lorretje, kaporretje, kapoe!....’  
Shame, I cover my ears, -  
What shouting and what yelling,  
Nasty animal!  
Oh! my whole head is tired....  
‘Lorretje, kaporretje, kapoe!’

- Yes, my child! even if you find it wrong,  
I was taught otherwise;  
But a child who knows better,  
And forgets  
How his actions hurt others....  
Then intentionally does twice as wrong. -

label: children
translation:
Top, top! Aren’t you lazy?  
If I don’t come to hit you with my whip,  
You’ll give up walking and spinning;  
Naughty boy, you’ll drive me crazy yet!  
Father! Oh, give me a top that stays standing,  
You know, with a string and knots on it,  
Then I’m free from all the flying and hitting!

- Boy, tell me! You talk so lazily?  
But if the teacher doesn’t come to warn you,  
You’ll give up on lessons and writing;  
Boy! It costs me sighs and tears;  
Truly, I fear—if it goes on like this longer—  
You’ll be shamed, mocked by the neighborhood,  
Lazy like your top, sent out with the ants. -

label: children
translation:
Hop, hop! my cheerful rocking horse!  
Now you must ride fast;  
For oh! I am in such a great hurry,  
I must fetch something from next door; -  
There, Mother, for the fun,  
Has placed a plate of cherries,  
And if you don't hurry along now,  
Perhaps the plate will be empty by then:  
So hurry, hurry, as fast as you can,  
Then you'll get a cherry too;  
For Father says: a diligent horse  
Is worth double the oats.

label: children
translation:
I read about Lazyland yesterday,  
Boys! it must be wonderful there,  
There you have canals of milk and wine,  
There you have streets that are edges of tea!  

To get there, you should know,  
You have to eat mountains of rice pudding:  
Once you're there, just lie down lazily on the ground,  
Eating and drinking, it runs into your mouth!  

Boys! – how nice it is to read,  
I think that laziness must be annoying;  
No! when you work with zeal and desire,  
Then the food tastes good, then rest tastes good!

label: children
translation:
Hunger is the best sauce;  
Dashing, slaving, toiling, sweating,  
Gives the true appetite for eating;  
Who has worked hard and well,  
Finds raw beans taste sweet.  

Hunger is the best sauce!  
Had you cakes and pastries,  
Had you the world's delicacies,  
Oh, what use would it be, my dear!  
If you had no hunger after all.  

Hunger is the best sauce!  
Sometimes it comes down to a morsel, -  
Think, what good is the best meal  
To a lazy gourmet.....  
Large dishes, small appetite!

label: children
translation:
Eating is a precious thing!  
But, if you want to enjoy the food, -  
Do not underestimate hunger,  
Which must make the sauce!  
Truly! - if I must choose,  
Either no hunger, or no food...  
Oh! then you should know,  
I choose hunger... short and sweet!

Resting is a precious thing!  
But, if you will welcome it -  
Do not undervalue work,  
Which makes you appreciate its price!  
Truly! - if I must choose,  
More work... more rest...  
Oh! despite all the sweetest pleasures,  
I choose work... short and sweet!

Wealth is a precious thing!  
But, if you want to value it properly,  
Do not underestimate being poor  
To teach you proper use!  
Truly - if I must choose,  
Always rich... sometimes poor,  
Oh! I say it without fear,  
I choose being poor... short and sweet!

label: children
translation:
Well, little birds! How silly you are,
That you all fly away when you see it!....
It is a doll and nothing else,
Wrapped in many old rags:
Look! whether you peck at it or hit it,
It remains a dead one, comrade!
And certainly does you no harm or sorrow.

- Well, young dear! Are we so foolish,
You’re not such a clever one yourself;
Because, if you see something at night,
You get scared, and run away,
And fear ghosts or other evil....
And if you just looked more closely, mate!
It was imagination, comrade! -

label: children
translation:
Good is good and bad is bad!  
Whether friend or foe does it;  
Therefore, boys! behave yourselves,  
Speak your mind honestly,  
Dare to speak the truth:  
“That is good, and that is bad.”

If you have a good companion,  
Whom you care for a lot,  
And he is sometimes angry or naughty,  
Tell him then: “My best mate!  
That is bad,” – or, “that is wrong!”  
I bet he will stop it further.

If you find someone strange,  
But you see, now and then,  
Something brave or noble from them,  
Give them your right hand  
And tell everyone around:  
“He is indeed a fine fellow!”

But think well of yourself,  
Before you speak to another,  
Whether you yourself lack something  
That you still need to fix: –  
Then honestly say, whatever you do:  
“That was bad, and that was good.”

label: children
translation:
A little, little lie,
Would that really be a sin?
I think, it does no harm....
But - little children grow up,
That I recently read;
And if they are a bit naughty when young,
I think one can guess for oneself,
What they will be like when older.

A little, little lie,
Even if it is such a thief,
Grows into a bad guy,
And grabs you and pinches you and holds you tight,
I'll bet you on that;
And many a good man or woman
Has regretted such a little lie
Their whole life, my dear!

label: children
translation:
Short and strong in doing good,  
Short and strong against evil,  
Calm in spirit in prosperity,  
Twice as brave when times go bad...  
If you learn that, comrade!  
You will see that—short and strong  
Is even more than rich and mighty.

Quiet and modest in doing good,  
Quiet and modest in the face of evil,  
Calm in spirit in prosperity,  
Twice as gentle when times go bad...  
If you learn that, girl friend!  
You will see how—quiet and modest,  
It best satisfies everyone's wish.

Boys, Girls!—Selected,  
Full of health and full of peace,  
The whole world could be,  
If everyone listened to my oath;  
But!—not everyone complies lightly,  
I wish you happiness, united,  
Quiet and modest, short and strong!

label: children
translation:
A patch here, a patch there,  
Wherever I can find them,  
I neatly sew them together  
From neighbors and from friends;  
And give in exchange for chits, – yes, for cotton,  
A hand, a smile, and sometimes a kiss.

A patch here, a patch there,  
I then cut them into diamonds,  
And neatly stitch them together,  
The prettiest side outward;  
And when all the corners are full,  
I line them with warm wool.

A patch here, a patch there  
Would otherwise have been lost...  
Now I am ready for many a winter  
And keep warm as I should: –  
I am doubly happy with that coat,  
Because I made it through my own diligence.

label: children
translation:
My Uncle Kees is a carpenter,  
There is no better one on the yard;  
He makes in the house and in the yard  
Everything you can see or imagine;  
His hand is rough, and his voice coarse,  
But I am not afraid of him because of that.  

He pinched my ear last time  
And said: "Well, if you study diligently,  
Honor your Father and your Mother,  
Then you will get something for Christmas!"  
And my ear tingled from it,  
Yet I looked at Uncle Kees kindly.  

And think what he brought?....  
A new pair of clogs, fine and neat,  
With silver toes trimmed,  
Lined with white sheepskin....  
And inside there lay a letter,  
On which was written: "For my dear Nephew!"  

And Mother told me, with a smile:  
Now you see, my dear boy!  
How the sweet children are doing;  
I hope you may stay like this now!  
And I gave my dear Mother a kiss  
And said: I will do my best!

label: children
translation:
Oh winter, harsh winter,  
How bitterly cold you are!  
I wish I had a guilder,  
Then I would buy turf and wood.  
I would build a little fire  
If our place were that big,  
And I would invite my poor neighbors  
For coffee and bread.  

How they would then feast  
In the corner by the hearth!  
For me, a soft little spot  
And also some bread reserved:  
Oh, if only I had a guilder....  
But when I asked Mother,  
She said: "Child! we have  
Just enough for ourselves."  

Listen, boys! when I grow up,  
You will see something:  
I want to save a cent  
From what I earn each day;  
Then I will have three guilders  
Saved up by New Year's,  
And I will invite my poor neighbors  
In the corner by my hearth.

label: children
translation:
What taps against the windowpane,  
As if it asks: open up! —  
If only it were that little bird  
That I saw walking nearby!  
Oh yes! there it sits, cold and stiff;  
How it chirps for some food...  
Oh, that I hadn’t eaten  
My sandwich now!

Or if only I had all the crumbs  
That Mother had to sweep away,  
Then the poor little creature would be saved  
And I wouldn’t be embarrassed! —  
Oh, Mother! help me out of this need,  
And I will never forget  
That not a single crumb of bread  
May be thrown away.

label: children
translation:
‘Why do you dance so cheerfully around the light,
Tell me, little mosquito! Do you have no eyes,
Or is your vision dimming?
So many have flown in,
Half-burned, struggling to get free,
And, I truly must fear,
It will soon happen to you as well!’

But what the housewife said or did
And whether she wanted to chase the little creature away,
It paid no heed to warning or command
But followed blindly its own pleasure: -
And it lasted only a second or two,
When look! As punishment for the frolicking,
There it lay struggling in the candle!

I wish a certain child
(I will not mention his name here!)
Could see that foolish mosquito, so blinded,
Lying in that hot candle! -
But the creature is dead before I find him....
Maybe it can still be saved,
If I am clever about it!

label: children
translation:
You crawl in vain into bush and hedge,  
Now that you have done wrong to the flax;  
You perhaps thought:  
‘It is dark on the road anyway  
And no one saw me during my flight!’ -  
Look up, at the sky...  
The Stars have seen it!

Now, in the evening, sooner or later,  
Before every bush and every tree  
You stand in fear and shame;  
For wherever you sit, or wherever you stand,  
There the Stars look at you  
And call out: ‘You have done wrong!....’  
And at night you still hear it in your dream.

But if your little heart is sadly weary  
And you feel true remorse and pain,  
Because you did it,  
Then the Stars smile at you,  
And say: ‘Never do it again,  
Then we will look kindly down again!’  
Sleep well now – as long as you never forget!

label: children
translation:
Whether you close door and window,  
Evil thieves, evil crooks,  
Who rob and sadden,  
You will not keep them out of the house with that!  
No! You must choose two guards,  
If you do not want to lose money or peace.

‘Labor’ faithfully stands watch  
To take care ‘outside the house’;  
Watching from early morning,  
Relieved only late at night; –  
‘Love’ holds, with calm senses,  
Devout and eager watch ‘from within.’

Where these two stand guard,  
And draw their white weapons  
When they discover crook or thief,  
You are safe, day and night!  
You can call out, without hesitation:  
‘Let the evil folk come!’

label: children
translation:
Children! look! the moon shines clear:  
Come a little here!  
Sing joyfully together,  
Sing for some pleasure!  
You know plenty of songs,  
I hear you late and I hear you early,  
Come now quickly here!

When the work is done  
And the task completed,  
A song may well be sung;  
It sounds so beautiful at night!  
Many a window opens,  
All the neighbors gather together;  
Sing now pure and soft!  
Sing first how a child  
Becomes teachable and sweet!  
Then a song, beloved,  
Of ‘Whose Dutch blood!’  
Then how one honors their parents,  
And how our dear Lord  
Is mighty and good!

Children! look! the moon shines clear:  
Now come all here!  
Sing joyfully together,  
Sing for some pleasure!  
Soon the watch calls: to bed!  
Therefore sing now for fun,  
Come quickly here!

label: children
translation:
Late in the evening, late in the evening,  
Then my Father comes, tired and weary,  
And often from streets and canals far away,  
From work to our little house;  
Then I stand before our small window  
Yearning to catch sight of him,  
And when he then turns the corner,  
I hurry to the door.

Late in the evening, late in the evening,  
When Mother has made coffee,  
I slide my Father's rocking chair  
Into the corner between the hearth and bed;  
I put his slippers on the stove  
And then, carefully watched,  
I get my Father's long pipe  
And his tobacco jar from the cupboard.  
Late in the evening, late in the evening,  
When my Father returns home,  
He gives me a sweet kiss  
And asks: "Well, have you studied well?"  
Then I throw my arms around Father's neck  
And say: "I did my best,  
Because I want to be so clever soon,  
To go bravely to work for you!"

label: children
translation:
Stars! I see you shining and standing,  
It has often crossed my little mind,  
Where do you come from in the evening,  
Where do you go again in the morning,  
Do you find your way all alone?

Stars! if you smile so kindly,  
I have often thought about it,  
Who lets you shine in the dark night;  
Humans cannot be the cause,  
Are you as great as we read?

Oh, it must be God, who is great and good,  
Who from His Heaven watches over the children,  
When they fear Him with devout heart;  
Teach us to thank and praise Him,  
Whether you may descend or rise.

label: children
translation:
Before we lay our heads to rest,  
Where our camp is spread,  
We want to express our gratitude  
To the good God  
For the blessing prepared for us:  
Will, O Father, forgive us  
What, in thoughtlessness,  
Has been done wrong by Your children: –  
Let us dream all night long  
Of Your love, of Your power!

label: children
translation:
Little sunshine, morning light,
When you sparkle on the windows
And shine again in our faces,
We fold our hands together
And thank, with reverence,
For this new, beautiful day.

Look! In the evening we lay our heads
Always so calmly down,
As if everyone believes:
Tomorrow the sunlight will return! -
None of us thinks about
That you might not appear again.

No one could possibly have thought,
That even if you might come again,
Perhaps no tomorrow awaits us
After our sleep, out of our dreams....
Therefore, beautiful morning glow,
Be greeted with joyful thanks.

label: children
translation:
Early up, early up, in all things,  
That makes a fool into a wise one,  
That makes something insignificant significant,  
That makes a youth from a gray-haired man,  
An old man from a youth.  

Early up, early up, in virtue and diligence.  
Early up, you maidens and you boys!  
Then you can, when you are weary,  
Go to sleep with a peaceful soul,  
Whether for once or forever.

label: children
translation:
When day comes and night recedes,  
Then awake to fresh strength,  
Clear of eyes and mind,  
Pure of heart and clean of hand.  

Call, with pious childlike faith,  
Then the Father's blessing in;  
He, who from His heavenly throne  
Gives labor strength and reward.  

And remember, whether - heavy or light,  
You perform your task and work:  
That no eternity returns  
The hour, lived unused here.

label: children
translation:
How early you are in the air,  
How you fly cheerfully back and forth!  
No matter how early I may rise,  
You, lion-bird! win every time!  
What drives you out of the camp so quickly,  
And what does your joyful sound mean?

- Well, child! when in the clear sky  
The morning light pours its rays,  
Then my bed no longer holds me  
And I rise quickly upwards;  
And, soaring toward the arch of heaven,  
My song also rises up to God.

Believe me, I know it, dear child!  
He surely has a good day every day,  
Who first greets the morning light  
And begins the day with God's praise...  
Although that song remains low on earth,  
God's angels carry it Heavenward. -

label: children
translation:
Who wants to wash his hands clean,  
Must wash them together;  
It does no good, try it,  
To wash them one by one:  
If you do not rub them together,  
I bet there will still be dirt left.  

If you wash one through the other like this,  
Learn from it: sooner or later,  
It also goes like this in the world:  
We must help each other! -  
Whoever only wants to work for wages,  
Will never get his hands clean.

label: children
translation:
Rye bread and white bread  
They belong together!  
If you eat one before the other,  
Oh! you bring yourself into trouble!  
Rye bread must also be eaten;  
If the white bread is gone,  
Then, you should know,  
You eat the rye bread alone.

Rest and work - and work and rest  
Belong together!  
If you do one before the other,  
Oh! you make a burden out of pleasure!  
Everyone's task must be completed:  
Who tries to rest too soon,  
Runs the risk that throughout their whole life,  
They never expect rest again.

label: children
translation:
Oh, look! How wet the little flowers are!  
It's as if they cried through the night!  
Look! There lies a whole load  
Of little drops on every leaf:  
Say, mother! - what could be wrong with them?  
Could it be because of the cold?

- Yes, dear! I don’t know for sure,  
What could trouble the little flowers;  
But, if I may say so, little one,  
Those are not tears you see;  
Those drops are good for the little flowers...  
It is dew that refreshes and nourishes the plant.

That will often happen to you too!  
You will see, only as you grow older,  
What blessings are poured upon us  
In many seemingly sad tears  
And how that dew, in our hearts,  
Makes the heavenly flowers grow.

label: children
translation:
Say, children! Have you heard it already?  
Spring has appeared again!  
She chased away the evil Winter,  
And he grumbled as he went away.  

Now she scatters flowers here and there  
And teaches the birds to sing,  
And calls the children together  
To play happily once more.  

Look! How the little animals here and there  
Now sing and now jump; –  
I think we should, for fun,  
Now sing a little song too: –  

Oh dear Spring! Greetings to you  
And teach us, as we praise you  
For the good that is done to us,  
To thank the Creator!

label: children
translation:
The sun shone warmly on the herb,  
Spring had arrived,  
And all the buds burst open,  
As if there were nothing to fear;  
They thought that the winter frost  
Would not return in the sunshine.  

But when the sun went to rest,  
Then came, laden with snow,  
Winter suddenly appeared,  
And snapped stems and leaves;  
And all the little flowers, white and red,  
Were sick or dead by morning.  

Sorrow usually dwells next to joy,  
The cold beside spring days;  
And those who rejoice the soonest  
Often have to grieve the longest;  
So trust, if I may pray,  
Trust no first day of spring.

label: children
translation:
Dear May is at the door,  
We are already calling: come in!  
She gives us flowers soft in color  
And sweet in scent,  
And clear minds;  
For whoever has done their best this year here,  
She always brings a little prize along —  
Come in!

That would indeed be a naughty guest,  
Who would dare to call: stay outside!  
I bet he surely wouldn’t have been careful,  
And would have thought for sure:  
I must whistle for my prize after all!  
But if there were such a rascal among us...  
May goes in, and he goes out:  
Stay outside!

But no! that is no danger at all;  
Hear, everyone calls: come in!  
And, prize or not, we just show  
What we might have gained this year  
In cleverness:  
Come, dear May! and hear the song,  
That gratefully flows from our hearts:  
Come in!

label: children
translation:
Blue little flower, my love, forget me not!  
How is it, when by the creek  
My eye sees you,  
That I, of all dear people,  
Do not dare to ask and wish:  
‘Forget me not!’  

Oh, little flower, my love, I know it well!...  
But I pray you, if I tell you,  
Forget it quickly...  
On many, many days  
I would have to ask with fear and shame:  
‘Forget me well!’  

Blue little flower, my love, forget me not!  
I promise you — when again by the creek  
My eye sees you,  
That I, of all dear people,  
Like you, dare to ask and dare to wish:  
‘Forget me not!’

label: children
translation:
“How quickly you bloom, how swiftly you wither,  
Blue flower of the green flax!  
The sun had just risen to midday height;  
This morning you stood in full splendor,  
And now already you are dry and pale  
And shriveled by the drought;  
Look! I say it plainly,  
If I had such a lovely little flower,  
Then I would surely bloom all year long!”

- Yes! that is very sweetly thought,  
If one only lives for pleasure and beauty,  
And not for work and to gain;  
The pretty little flowers you see  
Are not the most important to me....  
I make the threads for your linen  
And oilseeds for grease....  
And if I just keep on blooming,  
Then you will barely have a shirt on your body.  
When winter comes to the land,  
You will find many a vain plant  
Trampled on the manure heap;  
But I, even if I am twice dead,  
Then I still live for young and old,  
For linen becomes paper, ladies!  
On it everyone then reads the lesson:  
“Oh bloom a little less, work a little more...  
Being useful is the most precious honor!” -

label: children
translation:
That is growing out of sorrow,  
That is blooming, always ready!  
New buds, new colors,  
New flowers, new scents,  
As if there will never be an end to it.

When the little wind blows in the south,  
The moonrose blooms in full splendor;  
Hail showers plunder it...  
Before it lets anything wither,  
It blooms again in full strength.

Always flowers, always scents,  
Always buds, always more...  
Moonrose! may it happen to us,  
Amid cheering, amid sorrow,  
To bloom like this, to God's glory!

label: children
translation:
Lonely little flower in the forest,  
Deeply hidden beneath the moss,  
No one watches your blossoming;  
But you bloom just the same  
To God's honor,  
And you thank Him for His grace,  
Him, the Wise Lord of Creation!

Like a bee, lost in the woods,  
Settling on your little buds,  
You open your cups wider;  
And you think in your mind,  
How your sweetness  
Must now refresh  
The little creature, weary flown and weary crawled!  
O then you rejoice that God's hand  
Has planted you so lonely,  
To give your nourishment there,  
Where the bee on the thorny path,  
Weary and tired,  
Would surely perish from lack,  
If she had no refreshment. -

Pious one! who, in humility,  
Spreads salvation on the lonely path,  
Content in your fate and condition...  
Do not trade it for vain appearance,  
Little flower-line! -  
Blessed is he who, here below,  
May be the lonely little flower in the forest!

label: children
translation:
That little scent is beautiful enough,  
It looks so lovely... oh maiden!  
But do you know that early tomorrow  
The scent and color will have withered?  
Oh! that you did not pity more  
So many precious blossom petals!

If the little flower had stayed on the stem,  
It might have grown into fruit,  
And even with water in a glass  
It would have bloomed for days;  
But now—an hour or so—how short!  
And the poor little flower is withered.

So it is also with the joy of life!....  
Whoever desires too much at once,  
Will soon see, dear youth!  
How quickly it fades and decays!  
Do you long for fruit to come?  
Then do not break the flower from the tree.

label: children
translation:
Blue flowers in the corn!  
You look pretty among the grain;  
But a spot is lost,  
Where a rich stalk could have stood.  
You may be a charming ornament;  
But, if you grow too luxuriantly,  
I would (and with reason) fear,  
That you will soon be eradicated.

When the farmer has sown  
Valuable grains in the furrows,  
And, instead of nourishing corn,  
Mows nothing but blue flowers,  
He will call the land infertile,  
That gave him such meager harvest—  
And he rightly cuts your flowers  
Off as destructive weeds.

Therefore, flowers in the corn,  
Be an adornment, not a burden!  
The best of the field is lost,  
Where you grow too luxuriantly.  
Also wish to teach us this lesson:  
‘Usefulness must come before pleasure,  
And enjoyment in everyone’s life  
Should be a single flower in the grain!’

label: children
translation:
Little grain, that grows so luxuriantly!  
How are your stalks so thin and so spindly?  
I see them over there twice as sturdy,  
Although their length is not half that of yours;  
Yet you stand well in the clayey soil,  
And over there it is mostly all sand.

I fear, when the landowner harvests his grain,  
That he will not even have you bound into sheaves;  
That he will find only straw and stalks,  
The field and the harvesting and threshing worthless;  
That, dissatisfied with how little you gave,  
He might possibly throw your stalks into the fire with the chaff.

The fatness of the earth was granted to you,  
Provided you would also yield double the kernels;  
Not to live only for yourself,  
And to grow as tall as you can;  
Not to become useless straw,  
Had you that place and that little field.

Little grain, that grows so luxuriantly!  
May many from villages and towns  
Along with me walk the path beside your field,  
If only your example fits a few:  
For my part, that much is certain to me,  
I will consider how luxuriantly you grow.

label: children
translation:
Well, tree, how crookedly you have grown!  
It’s almost as if you are about to collapse;  
And how tangled all your branches are,  
As if you have never been pruned;  
It is true (I say it without shame)  
I find you ugly, crooked tree!

- Yes, child! It is as you say,  
I must also find myself ugly:  
Had I allowed myself to be bound earlier,  
Then I would not be crooked now, but straight;  
I might then have been a pride of the forest...  
Now I am only useless wood.

Just yesterday (I heard it with sorrow)  
A gardener said: ‘Had you been bent young,  
Then you would have stood as a delight to the eyes;  
Now you are firewood, nothing else!’  
You find me ugly, dear child!....  
Make sure no one else ever finds you so too. -

label: children
translation:
You, poor vine! Are you lying there  
So dirty, sprawling along the ground?  
How small and nasty are your grapes,  
Unripe, and certainly unhealthy!  
Come, if I tie you to the trellis,  
Then perhaps you would recover.  

- No! I fear, my child! It is now too late...  
If someone had done that  
When I was young and strong - and able  
To vigorously sprout my shoots,  
Then I would now be full of fruit,  
So sweet and fragrant as muscat.  

Oh! It happens to me like many a poor man,  
Who is not helped in time:  
That help sometimes comes later - but then  
Courage, desire, and strength are lacking...  
So support the vine before it falls,  
And the Poor One, hope it can still benefit. -

label: children
translation:
The little flowers on the heath,  
The little birds in the nest,  
They do not always have it easy either,  
But in the end they still grow up...  
The Lord of Heaven cares for both;  
And often a pious hand watches over  
Both little birds and heath plants.

Then they can grow carefree  
And draw strength from adversity:  
The bird’s song rejoices: ‘God is great’!  
The plant gives blossoms, white and red,  
And sweet scents during blooming;  
And both thank God the Lord,  
And Him who nurtured them, to His honor.

You boys, girls, lively little ones!  
You wild birds, tender plants!  
Heaven’s Lord protects you too;  
His Spirit descends into hearts,  
To grant you help and shelter.  
Oh, then sing and bloom to His honor,  
You wild birds – tender plants!

label: children
translation:
Rooster, rooster, cock-a-doodle-doo!  
What steps,  
What jokes  
You make, for a handful of food!  
I think, if I had to make such things,  
Before I had anything to bite,  
Before I could earn a living...  
That I would rather never eat again.

- Foolish boy, silly blood!  
All that talking  
You should stop,  
If your parents were not so good;  
I will, if in the evening or in the morning  
Your dish is not set,  
And you have to take care of yourself someday,  
See what kind of face you will make.

If you lose the parents once,  
Who give you  
To live,  
It will become clear what you choose;  
I think, then you will make, foolish boy!  
If you remain ungrateful and slow,  
Still other crooked jumps  
To fill your stomach. -

label: children
translation:
If you wish in your young years  
To gather wisdom,  
Come then, children, come here!  
See that quick, little ant  
Slaves, runs, gathers, saves,  
As if she did it for her pleasure...  
Boys! what a lovely creature!

To work like this, to toil like this,  
Is a pleasure  
To see and to do:  
To stock up, in the good season,  
To prepare for the winter together,  
When there are still green fruits....  
Children! I advise you to do the same.

Lazy girls, lazy boys!  
Yawning, sleeping.....  
If you do this in your younger days,  
I fear that when you’re older you will suffer hunger. –  
So if you don’t learn to gather stock,  
I tell you: you are (to my regret)  
Dumber than the ants.

label: children
translation:
The bee extracts from the sweet bud  
And from the bitter herb  
The purest and best juices  
And purifies them into pure honey:  
I hope no one is dumber,  
My child! than such a little bee!  

The good God gave you understanding,  
And bravery of spirit,  
A sharp-sighted eye, a quick hand,  
A fine hearing, a nimble foot....  
And what the bee, unaware, can do,  
You know the purpose and cause of.  

So—observe this charming little creature,  
And how it performs its task,  
Then do not be ashamed,  
But carry out your duty with double zeal;  
And whether you find sweet or bitter herb,  
Oh, always purify the honey from it!

label: children
translation:
The mosquito is dead!.... with your hand
You have, oh little boy, killed it;
But did you ever think, my young rascal,
How you yourself would feel,
If an elephant
Did the same to you, with trunk or tusk?

Yes, see! now it grieves you;
Now you mourn that you just now
Did not let that poor creature live.
For this time you are forgiven! -
But then do not do to another
What you do not want to be done to you.

label: children
translation:
You beautiful firefly, who in the forest  
At night shines so brightly through the trees,  
As if the lush green of the leaves  
Were laden with diamonds,  
Oh! tell me once (if I may know)  
Why don’t you also glow during the day?  
If I could give such beautiful light,  
I surely would do so my whole life,  
And not only that, that would be certain,  
When it is dark.

- Well, child! I tell you with pleasure;  
And, though I am just a simple creature,  
You might still benefit from it...  
When the warm sunbeams  
Make a stream of light, a stream of gold  
Flow over field and forest,  
I would fear to seem quite vain  
If I showed my weak glow;  
And even if I did... I bet that by day  
No one would see it.  
Oh! remember me, my dear child!  
If you find yourself among others,  
Who, like a sun, with warm rays  
Are proud of love, virtue, or knowledge;  
Then keep your mouth modestly closed  
And wait, just as I do myself,  
Until, when those others are silent,  
You too, in humility, may have your turn...  
Only if you create benefit or joy from it,  
Then give your light. -

label: children
translation:
Little swan, with your white plumes,  
With your wings large and wide!  
I see well how proud you are,  
When you make the water foam like that!  
All the small and delicate birds  
You chase away from brook and stream,  
And the shy little fish hurry  
Swiftly down into the depths.  

Beautiful, you are above many,  
Slender and stately beyond doubt;  
Precious down covers your body;  
But, what does that matter to others?  
See that little nightingale,  
Gray and poor in feathers—  
But how it can sing...  
Dear swan! Eh! that's more than display?  

Little swan! If you want to be humble,  
I bet that throughout the whole region,  
As the decoration of the brook,  
You are admired and praised:  
But if you remain so proud and proud,  
I say, when I hear you gabbling  
And see you waddling on goose feet...  
Oh, it’s really a nasty creature!

label: children
translation:
Stork, spoonbill!
I so gladly sit and watch you,
As you (carrying your sweat)
Glide through the clear air—
And know neither rest nor pause,
To have your nest, fully ready,
Proudly perched high in the oak tree.

Stork, spoonbill!
If you then still for many weeks
So patiently brood the eggs,
And—when the young break out—
Feed it with such faithful love,
See, then I find you so good,
That I can hardly express it!
- Dear friend, lovely child!
I thank you for your kindness!
But have you ever considered
What your parents did for you
Since you were brought into the world,
And how they always, day and night,
Still devote their effort to you?

Dear friend, lovely child!
Keep remembering this:
I care for only a few months,
See!.... but the parents who love you,
Have cared for so many years....
That is love and loyalty, indeed!
That far surpasses mine! —

label: children
translation:
Whoever wishes to rest in the green forest,  
Whoever wishes to rest with pleasure,  
He should choose a spot, deep in the woods  
And settle down to rest;  
A little pillow of soft moss,  
A cushion of fern  
And a curtain of leaves...  
Give a sweet midday nap in the forest.

The sky at the edge of the clearing  
Shines beautifully blue through the foliage,  
The bushes curl around the edge,  
The blossom hangs over it;  
The cool breeze whispers with the stream,  
The playful butterflies flutter,  
The nightingales warble...  
Isn't it a lovely lullaby?

And best of all: the green forest  
Refreshing you with coolness and rest,  
Requests no silver or gold from you,  
Even if you sleep until evening;  
It only asks: are you weary or tired?...  
The place to sleep is for everyone!  
And if it has suited you well,  
Then you will also receive the evening's gold!

label: children
translation:
Whatever you do or do not do,  
Brave boys, clever girls!  
Let Caution gently guide Speed by the hand;  
But always remember that you almost never leave Evil  
Too slowly behind.

Whatever you leave or do not leave,  
Clever girls, brave boys!  
Hastily give Cautious advice,  
Do not fall asleep in wickedness;  
Oh! always remember that you almost never do Good  
Too slowly.

Whatever you leave or do,  
Brave boys, clever girls!  
Do not be faster than necessary:  
But if someone tries to tempt you  
To do something evil—then say quickly:  
“I am far too good to do evil!”

label: children
translation:
Do you want cooling from the blaze
Of the fierce summer sun?
In the moisture of the spring,
In the stream, in the flood...
Young men! - in the fresh wells
You feel your strength restoring itself,
As the wet
Splashes around you!

Dive down and jump up,
Cut through the stream with strong muscles,
Turn and twist, swing and whirl....
Like an arrow from the bow
Young men! you may shoot forward;
It will pour strength into your veins,
As the wet
Splashes around you.

The coward remains on the shore,
The coward remains on the dike,
Waterland, Waterland,
You are our kingdom!
From the depths of the streams
Comes Old Netherland’s strength;
Retrieve that treasure
Again from the wet!

label: children
translation:
Upright in body, upright in soul,  
That is a stance to my liking.  
Whether you may wear a formal skirt,  
Whether you wear a coat or a jacket...  
Upright in body, upright in soul!

And if ever one bows their head or knee,  
Let it be only for God, the Lord!  
For anyone whom one honors as good,  
For anyone whom one sees as wiser...  
For those only does one bow head or knee.

But otherwise—upright in body and soul,  
In joy or sorrow, throughout our whole life!  
Not left, not right, but head held high,  
Whatever bows, whatever kneels...  
That is Dutch, in body and soul!

label: children
translation:
Almanac,  
Liar!  
Oh, it’s clear,  
You’re fooling us;  
Only one longest day in the year?  
If you wanted to ask me for advice,  
I would make a hundred longest days. -  

Whoever gets out of bed first,  
Makes the longest day;  
Whoever is the cleverest and most diligent,  
Makes the longest day;  
Whoever is the sweetest and kindest,  
Makes the longest day.  

You can do that every day,  
Three hundred sixty times,  
Yes, even five times more,  
Without asking the Almanac:  
If you did it well and with sense,  
Even if only a hundred times,  
Then let us repeat in chorus,  
Joyfully dancing hand in hand:  

Almanac,  
Liar!  
Oh, it’s clear,  
You’re fooling us;  
Only one longest day in the year?  
If you want to ask us for advice,  
There are a hundred longest days!

label: children
translation:
Do you know? - bitter in the mouth  
Is healthy for the sick heart!  
Do not shiver at that ugly drink;  
Swallow it boldly and skillfully,  
Let not a single drop be lost;  
I bet, in the end, you will say: thank you!

Do you know? - bitter for the mind  
Is good for the sick soul!  
Swallowing may be difficult,  
But if you will be better again,  
You will thank that medicine,  
Which has healed you so well!

label: children
translation:
Children, don’t burn your little mouths!  
It’s better to blow a little harder,  
Little bosses!  
And a bit more patience,  
Than to get, because of your fault,  
Here a blister and there a bump.

Children, don’t burn your little hands!  
It’s better to touch nothing,  
Little rascals!  
Than to notice afterward,  
To your shame and to your disgrace,  
That you have burned your little feet.

Children, don’t burn your little hearts!  
That I would find the worst of all,  
Little friends!  
That causes spots, brown and black,  
That causes patches, rough and hard,  
That causes eternal grief and sorrow.

label: children
translation:
Who eats the best and always gladly.....
That I know well and thoroughly: -
The one who daily from his full stomach
Keeps something for an empty one!

The best remedy for appetite,
That I have ever known,
Is: a man who suffers hunger,
Once tastefully eating!

So whether you have little or much....
If you truly want to feast,
Then let the half or a part
Of the poor hunger be satisfied.

label: children
translation:
Too much is unhealthy!  
Lieverts, will you not forget it?  
Your little mouth waters  
At a dish of delicious food,  
The branch hangs down to the ground,  
You may pick as you please....  
Remember, you eager stomachs!  
Too much is unhealthy.

Now Mother’s kind eye  
Will still watch over you with care  
(What a craving moves your heart)  
That you won’t overindulge;  
But when you are older,  
And have not learned as a child  
To keep measure in desire,  
You will lose pleasure and prosperity.

Too much is unhealthy!  
See, that applies to all matters,  
That once on this world-round  
Will make you greedy:  
Then watch over your eyes and mouth,  
Watch over your heart and senses,  
And always remind yourself:  
Too much is unhealthy!

label: children
translation:
I know a little monkey, empty and quick,  
A boss among the apes;  
A skirt covers his slender back,
