A little hat covers his temples;  
When he stands in front of his cage,  
You think you are greeting a Gentleman,  
But when he starts climbing,  
You see his bare feet: —  
And what you see beyond that,  
I won’t say....  
Or why should I waste words?....  
You know, Boys (Girls)! what one sees,  
When monkeys want to climb high.

You, nice little monkeys, small and big!  
Who wear those little hats and skirts,  
Oh! think again of your bare little legs  
And stay close to your cages: —  
Who wants to be higher than his rank,  
Or more than his equals,  
(You can be sure) he lets  
His bare legs be seen:  
And what you see beyond that,  
I won’t say....  
Or why should I waste words?....  
You know, Boys (Girls)! what one sees,  
When monkeys want to climb high.

label: children
translation:
Joyfully leapt through the lush dune,  
Joyfully sung on slope and crest;  
Hear, in our song echoes the song of the region:  
The roaring of the sea and the rustling of the brook.

Holland, sweet Holland! How our hearts swell  
Where your dune wall defies the sea floods,  
Where all the treasure of your fertile garden  
Borders the barrenness of the drifting dune.

Modest on the outside, but rich in your heart,  
You are, sweet Holland! encircled by dunes;  
What threatens you on the flood or on the land,  
Yields, like the waves before the shifting sand!

label: children
translation:
Come, boys and girls! now raise as one
The ground that carried your cradle;
Let your song sound through the meadows of the Fatherland,
That keeps its eyes on you:
You now honor and glorify that Fatherland,
Once, we hope, it will be proud of you.

In courage and knowledge, in piety and virtue,
It was once the adornment of the earth;
That glory faded; but it awaits your youth,
That you restore it to its value:
Then – if you honor and glorify it as you do now,
That Fatherland will one day be proud of you.

label: children
translation:
Brave in body, brave in spirit,  
Dutch boys  
Always have been!  
Come on, boy, don’t sleep!  
Exercise eye and ear and hand,  
Stretch your tendons, steel your muscles,  
Strive for knowledge and understanding! -  
Good morals, good manners,  
Sharp minds, strong muscles,  
Are the pillars of a country!

Fair in body, fair in soul,  
That was what Dutch maidens  
Always liked!  
Girl! - those who yesterday complained:  
‘It was only so in olden times!’  
Show them the present differently!  
Let those envious ones, to their regret,  
No matter how they stare, how they look,  
Red with shame have to admit:  
That you are without blemish!  
Oh! consider it, young generation!  
In your hearts, in your hands,  
Lies the strength,  
Lies the welfare of the Netherlands!  
So keep both brave and fair...  
The country in which you were born,  
You never give better thanks,  
For the good bestowed upon you,  
Than when we hear as your praise:  
Brave is the body, - the heart is fair!

label: children
translation:
Step by step, that still progresses:  
Think of it in good, think of it in bad,  
Think of it in advantage, think of it in damage:  
Usually, boy, you still forget it!  
Flying or standing still... you know no middle ground,  
Whatever I may talk and guess and pray.  

Drop by drop, wherever it can fall,  
Does make a little hollow in the heart of the stones;  
If the moisture flows over in a stream,  
Oh, it runs away and you do not notice it;  
Therefore, consider it, with all your beginnings:  
Saving makes progress… whoever persists, must win.

label: children
translation:
Boys! If an Enemy came  
To dominate our Netherlands,  
Every boy, I wager, would take  
(Since he found neither sabers nor rifles)  
Tongs or a pitchfork in hand  
To defend the Fatherland.

Girls don’t dare to do much either...  
I think that they (in such times)  
Would still fight for our Netherlands  
With a broomstick;  
Yes, even with a rag ball  
They would drive the scum away.

Tell me, children! - wouldn’t you?  
Now... then I will just tell you  
That my eye sees an Enemy...  
Who (with cunning schemes)  
Has long been stealthily  
Assaulting our poor Fatherland.

Do you want to know who he is?....  
The evil that sneaked into your souls!  
That, if it wins, surely  
Will one day destroy our Netherlands!  
Up then—help the country out of distress,  
Children! strike the Enemy dead!

label: children
translation:
Little dawdler! Little dawdler!  
Always, always plenty of time....  
Aren’t you ashamed when someone asks:  
Do you take after your father?  
Was your dear mother so slow?....  
Tell me, what do you answer to that question? —  
But, before the answer (I would like that)  
I still have to arrange it myself,  
Because in that, too, you are slow!.....  
It goes: — ‘If I don’t get to it today,  
Oh! then I’ll get to it tomorrow!’  

No, my darling!... with dawdling  
You won’t get to it tomorrow either! —  
It only brings you heartache  
That fiddling and dallying!  
As you approach the day,  
That has never seen a tomorrow,  
You will, in fruitless lament,  
Mourn the lost time....  
Darling! now while it still can be,  
Oh! make use of hour and day,  
Roll up your sleeves!  
Little dawdler, little dawdler!  
Otherwise you are sweet and good;  
But, I beg you, hurry up:  
Or that little string will have a tail!  
It still sounds like a fib now;  
But, poor child! for you and me  
There would be dreadful seriousness,  
If you were to need someone else’s help....  
So I plead: make me happy quickly!  
And — if there must be dawdling....  
Dawdle only... in grieving!

label: children
translation:
Little pebble, round and smooth,  
When you plunge into the clear water,  
And the water  
With splashing  
Pearling bursts apart,  
You have just slipped from the hand,  
Or you wiggle downwards.  

Yet, where the moisture has embraced you,  
A little ring appears on the water;  
It flows further,  
Grows for a long time,  
Before it bursts apart:  
It shows, no matter how quickly you sink, to all  
The spot where you have fallen.  

Everyone remember this always – it is the same  
With good and evil:  
Hours, days,  
Years, still carry  
The mark of your deed.  
Oh, remember this, friends,  
The spot can still be found for a long time!

label: children
translation:
In the green foliage  
Sits a little bird,  
Under the green foliage  
Sits a maiden;  
The little bird sings above,  
The girl sings below  
Contentment!  
And their sweet voices  
Softly blend together.

In the herbs listens  
All the woolly cattle,  
In the leaves whispers  
Every little breeze along;  
The little bird sings above,  
The girl sings below  
Contentment!  
And their sweet voices  
Softly blend together.  
The little bird’s song escapes  
To God’s honor,  
And the maiden’s lips  
Thank the Lord God;  
The little bird sings above,  
The girl sings below  
Contentment!  
And their sweet voices  
Softly blend together.

label: children
translation:
Has your little wing been broken,  
Bird, when the storm wind came;  
Did your wool, foolish Lamb,  
Stay caught in the thorn hedges?  
Bird, I beg you, then take shelter  
When the storm wind makes itself heard—  
Little sheep, watch out for the thorns,  
And find yourself another path!

Wild birds, wild boys,  
I see well how you are plucked bare....  
Oh! how many flakes you have already lost,  
Dear girls, innocent sheep!—  
I pray that you will listen willingly,  
That you will heed admonition in time;  
Sighing saves you and tears....  
Girls, boys, spread the word!

label: children
translation:
White flakes, fine and soft,  
Do your thousands and thousands  
Fall soon...  
The Earth has long been waiting for you.  

Cover her tightly and warmly,  
So that she may once rest  
From her burdens and desires...  
Oh! she was so bitterly weary.  

Has she not all year long  
Under care for flowers and fruits  
Sighed—  
As if there were never any rest time?  

At last, the poor sheep  
Fell backward  
Among all the withered foliage...  
And in deep, heavy sleep.  
There she lies now bare and bald,  
As Winter with its gusts  
Comes chasing—  
Then she freezes completely.  

Therefore, flakes, fine and soft,  
Cover her until she may awaken  
With your blanket,  
Cover her with your warm coat.  

When it is Spring again,  
She jumps out of your downy feathers  
Into the clothes,  
Just as strong and just as fresh!

label: children
translation:
Well, old tree! Tell me, are you not sorrowful,  
That you are the weakest of all,  
That you are grooved by so many cracks,  
And, only bark, almost threatens to fall?  
I see around you a whole avenue  
Of young, straight, healthy trees standing, –  
You, hollowed out on all sides,  
You, it seems to me, must surely envy their freshness!

- Oh no, my child! I have lived through much;  
I have borne fruit sixty times already,  
And I have seen what calm joy it brings,  
When you learn to die as gratefully as you live;  
Yes, even those hollows in my trunk,  
From which time took my best sap,  
Know – that they, like the arms of old men,  
Still protect and warm many.

See, in my crown, how I protect a nest,  
Where, calmly, the young brood can thrive,  
How, lower down, a wild swarm of bees  
Comes buzzing into the deep cleft with honey;  
How, at my foot, the plantain leaf  
Covers the hollow where the hare sat safely,  
And, in my root, among roses,  
The partridge has chosen a hiding place.

Thus old age also has its comfort and joy,  
Though the withered crown bends to the earth,  
When its heart still takes part in youth,  
Its ripe experience saving it from sorrow...  
So, child! as long as I may live,  
I rejoice in every beautiful day;  
And (must it be!) I will willingly fall,  
If I, dead and alive, may be useful to all. –

label: children
translation:
I saw a little rose blooming,  
No one could be more beautiful;  
I saw a maidenline playing,  
The dearest maidenline!....  
What was left at evening?  
A corpse... and withered leaves!

Oh! that the most beautiful flowers  
Are so fragile and tender! -  
Then do not boast so boldly  
You Rose, you Maidenline....  
But think how quickly your colors  
Fade and wither!

And - if we, at your passing,  
O Rose, O Maidenline!  
May have hope living in our hearts,  
That you will be worthy,  
Planted in Heaven’s Gardens,  
To praise God forever!

label: children
translation:
Good God! Your love and power  
Also shine in the winter night;  
Also in snow and hailstorm  
Your love and power speak to us.

You preserve the strength of the earth,  
Wrap the forest in a downy coat,  
Cover with snow, velvet soft,  
The seed that waits for Spring.

Then comes, after the winter night,  
Spring again, in pure splendor,  
Oh! how fragrantly and florally laughs  
That earth in fresh strength!

Once also for Our race will rise  
Heavenly light from Earth's night: -  
O that our heart may always remember,  
How an Eternal Spring awaits us!

label: children
translation:
Allegretto.
Jan, my man, would become a rider, if he could get on a horse; I take the brandy from the hearth, there Jan, my man, has a horse.
Hup-sa! Young master Jan, who can well become a rider.

2.
Jan, my man, would become a rider
If he could get on a saddle;
I break an egg, I give him the shell,
There Jan, my man, has a saddle.
Hupsa, young master Jan,
Who can well become a rider.

3.
Jan, my man, would become a rider,
If he could get hold of a bridle:
I take his shirt, I tear the hem:
There Jan, my man, has a bridle.
Hupsa, young master Jan!
Who can well become a rider.

4.
Jan, my man, would become a rider,
If he could get hold of a spur:
I break a pot and give him the spur,
There Jan, my man, has a spur.
Hupsa, young master Jan,
Who can well become a rider.

label: children
translation:
Witty.  
Little, little flower child! What are you doing in the garden? You are picking all the little flowers, you are making it much too rough. Mother will scold, father will strike. Little, little flower child, better be gone quickly from there.

label: children
translation:
Slow.  
Jan, my husband, is always sick, the whole week, the whole week; Jan, my husband, is always sick, the whole week, not on Sundays.

label: children
translation:
Allegretto.
The bridge that has fallen into the water, fal - de - ri de-
ri - re, fal-de-ri de - ri - re, the bridge that has fallen into the water, fal - de - ri de - ra.

label: children
translation:
Andante.  
In the evening, in the clear moonlight, the servant wants to be in the window. The moon that shines into the windows. What a wagon that is! Here is the bird and there is the fish, which is deceitful in the moon, in the moon.

2.  
This is short and that is long,  
And there is the carpenter’s bench.  
The short and the long, and the carpenter’s bench.  
The moon that shines into the windows.  
What a wagon that is!  
Here is the bird, etc.

3.  
Here is the plum and the apricot,  
And there stands Aunt Bello.  
The plum, apricot, and Aunt Bello;  
The short and the long, and the carpenter’s bench.  
The moon that shines, etc.

4.  
Here is the sausage and there is the grill,  
And there is the maiden’s comforter.  
The sausage and the grill, and the maiden’s comforter;  
The plum, apricot, etc.

5.  
Here is the pot, and there is the pan,  
And there is that silly Jan.  
The pot and the pan, and silly Jan;  
The sausage and the grill, etc.

6.  
Here is the cat and there is the mouse,  
And there is the waffle house.  
The cat and the mouse and the waffle house;  
The pot and the pan, etc.

7.  
Here is the priest and there is the brother,  
And Kaetje who sweeps the floor.  
The priest and brother, and Kaetje and the floor;  
The cat and the mouse, etc.

8.  
Here is the owl, and there is the finch,  
And there is that thin thing.  
The owl and the finch, and that thin thing;  
The priest and brother, etc.

9.  
Here is the skirt, and there is the wheel,  
And there is tall Machiel.  
The skirt and the wheel, and tall Machiel;  
The owl, etc.

10.  
Here is the waffle and there is the cookie,  
And there is the pastor’s pants.  
The waffle, the cookie, and the pastor’s pants;  
The skirt, etc.

11.  
Here are the cows in labor,  
And the little ships in the sea.  
The cows in labor, the little ships in the sea;  
The waffle, etc.

12.  
Here is the groom and there is the bride,  
And with that the song is also finished.  
The groom, the bride, and the song is finished;  
The cows in labor, the little ships in the sea;  
The waffle, the cookie, and the pastor’s pants;  
The skirt and the wheel, and tall Machiel;  
The owl and the finch, and that thin thing;  
The priest and brother, and Kaetje and the floor;  
The cat and the mouse, and the waffle house;  
The pot and the pan, and silly Jan;  
The sausage and the grill, and the maiden’s comforter;  
The plum, apricot, and Aunt Bello;  
The short and the long, and the carpenter’s bench;  
The moon that shines into the windows.  
What a wagon that is!  
Here is the bird and there is the fish,  
Which is deceitful in the moon, in the moon.

label: children
translation:
Allegretto.  
We are already rats and mice, we are once again together,  
we will not shine all night long, my soul no!  
And the grain is already being lost.  
There came a mouse running, a mouse like all others.  
It was to eat that grain; I granted that mouse right.  
The mouse and the grain are already being lost.  

2.  
There came a rat running,  
A rat like all others.  
It was to bite that mouse;  
I granted that rat right.  
The mouse, the rat, and the grain are all being lost.  

3.  
There came a cat running,  
A cat like all others.  
It was to catch that rat;  
I granted that cat right.  
The cat, the rat, the mouse, and the grain are all being lost.  

4.  
There came a dog running,  
A dog like all others.  
It was to bite that cat;  
I granted that dog right.  
The dog, the cat, the rat, the mouse, etc.  

5.  
There came a stick running,  
A stick like all others.  
It was to hit that dog;  
I granted that stick right.  
The stick, the dog, the cat, the rat, etc.  

6.  
There came a fire running,  
A fire like all others.  
It was to burn that stick;  
I granted that fire right.  
The fire, the stick, the dog, the cat, etc.  

7.  
There came water running,  
Water like all others.  
It was to extinguish the fire;  
I granted that water right.  
The water, the fire, the stick, the dog, etc.  

8.  
There came a cow running,  
A cow like all others.  
It was to drink that water;  
I granted that cow right.  
The cow, the water, the fire, the stick, etc.  

9.  
There came a butcher running,  
A butcher like all others.  
It was to kill that cow;  
I granted that butcher right.  
The butcher, the cow, the water, the fire, etc.  

10.  
And you who are crouching before me,  
And you like all others,  
Are already rats and mice too;  
And we all call out together:  
We are already rats and mice,  
We are once again together,  
We will not shine all night long,  
My soul no!  
We are already rats and mice.

label: children
translation:
You kicked, boy, on Mother's lap,  
Now you kick her in the heart!  
Naughty child!... her love and faithfulness were great,  
But greater is her sorrow.  
Oh! Surely, she would have grieved deeply,  
Had she lost you, boy;  
But suffering that tears the soul so,  
That was not destined for her!

You are still young!... The time will come  
For love and true union;  
Then: when you yourself are a mother,  
And joyful in your little ones,  
Then, as God the Lord says: -  
That dreadful parental pain  
Your children will return to you a thousandfold...  
And kick you in the heart!

label: children
translation:
Boy, take a rocking horse!..
Come, that game is reckless;
All that shooting with cannons
No longer suits Dutch nature;
Don’t you see it in your friend,
How he is trembling all too much!

It is a time of rest and peace;
Playing gently, speaking softly...
With those wild boyish tricks
We have little patience:
Here a stain, and there a tear,
Soon running under the door.

Yet (but tell no one!)
I would share in your brave play
With my whole heart,
If I were not a respectable man:
I must say it as it is—
I would rather have you than your friend!

label: children
translation:
Hear the clinking in village and city  
The fairground song far and wide!  
Of whatever rank or status you are,  
People, all join in:  
Big and small, poor and rich,  
Young and old, it’s all the same...  
Rejoice and laugh out loud the most,  
Children, today is your celebration!  

Poor wretches of poets,  
Dreamers who spend their precious time  
Napping in the attic,  
While your heart eats itself up,  
Because the people for whom you toil  
Greet you with mocking laughter...  
Rejoice and laugh out loud the most,  
Children, today is your celebration!  

Shrewd innkeepers,  
You who know by heart  
What little Napoleon eats daily,  
What’s going on with the Greeks,  
Yet never once look around  
To see what is happening here...  
Rejoice and laugh out loud the most,  
Children, today is your celebration!  

Flemings, who, forgetting your past  
Cowardly and nearly Frenchified,  
Dance to the Walloons’ tune  
And let yourself be trampled underfoot,  
Without crying out in pain,  
As if that’s how it ought to be...  
Rejoice and laugh out loud the most,  
Children, today is your celebration!  

Oh, where would misguidance  
Be, if I wanted to be complete?  
Take a good look at the human being  
(Just as others before me have said),  
And what you ultimately find  
Is— a poor, innocent child...  
So rejoice and laugh out loud the most,  
Children, today is your celebration!

label: children
translation:
Blessed New Year,  
Pull the cat by the hair,  
Pull the dog by the tail,  
Then you will have something all year.

label: children
translation:
Epiphany, Epiphany!  
Buy me a new hat,  
Mine is worn out;  
My father must not know,  
My mother has no money.  
Is that not unfortunate?

label: children
translation:
Palm Sunday, Palm Sunday;
A little rooster on a stick,
A hole through his skirt,
A piece of his left leg:
Now the whole little rooster is dead.

label: children
translation:
Sinterklaas, good holy man!  
Put on your best robe;  
Ride it to Amsterdam,  
From Amsterdam to Spain,  
Little apples from Orange,  
Plums from the trees;  
Sinterklaas will come.

label: children
translation:
Sinterklaas little rascal,  
Put something in my little shoe,  
Put something in my little boot;  
Thank you, little Sinterklaas.

label: children
translation:
Saint Nicholas  
He is a boss  
For children and for people;  
To him are all our wishes.

label: children
translation:
Hooks and eyes,  
Tikke-takke-togen,  
Gold paper,  
Tirelirelier.

label: children
translation:
Jantje,  
Wide, wadde, mitten,  
Wide, wadde, shop, tickle, auntie;  
‘Hey!’ said Jantje.

label: children
translation:
Pieter,  
Wide, wadde wieter,  
Wide, wadde, shop, sparkle, twister,  
‘Hip!’ said Pieter.  
etc.

label: children
translation:
Cuckoo,  
Faithful servant,  
Tell the truth,  
Tell me where:  
How many years  
Will I still wear that wreath?  
Cuckoo! How long will I live?

label: children
translation:
Stork,  
Spoonbill;  
Branch thief,  
Stork loves the little children.

label: children
translation:
Stork, spoonbill,  
With your long beak,  
When will you come home?  
When the mouse squeaks.  
Squeak, said the mouse,  
Stork will come home tonight.

label: children
translation:
Thirty days has November,  
April, June, and September,  
The others have thirty-one,  
Except for February alone;  
For it has four times seven,  
Leap year adds one more to it.

label: children
translation:
The days, they lengthen,  
The nights, they tighten there.

label: children
translation:
April sweet  
Still snows a white hat.

label: children
translation: March is stirring its tail.

label: children
translation:
A little snow on the mud,  
A frost on the dyke.

label: children
translation:
Snow on mudflats,  
Within three days ice, thin or thick.

label: children
translation:
Evening red  
Nice weather on board;  
Morning red,  
Water in the ditch.

label: children
translation:
A halo around the moon,  
That can be fine;  
But a halo around the sun,  
Women and children cry for that.

label: children
children
translation:
Naatje!  
Little parrot!  
What time is it?  
Eleven o'clock!  
Who says that?  
Little mouse!  
Where is it sitting?  
In its little house.  
What is it doing?  
It is working!  
For whom?  
For the little Poppedeine  
And the big Bombam;  
Good evening, player.

label: children
translation:
In The Hague, there lives a count  
And his son is called Jantje;  
If you ask: where does your dad live?  
He points with his little hand,  
First with his finger and then with his thumb;  
He is wearing a hunter’s cap  
With a hussar’s plume.

label: children
translation:
A B C,  
The cat goes along,  
The dog stays home:  
“Peep!” said the mouse in the front house.

label: children
translation:
Aunt Nans  
Sat on a goose,  
"Hop!" said the goose,  
And away flew Aunt Nans.

label: children
translation:
What does the little dog do? Woof, woof!  
What does the little cow do? Moo, moo!  
And the little sheep say: meh, meh!  
And the little goats say: baa, baa!  
And the little rooster: cock-a-doodle-doo!  
And the little cat: meow!  
Woof, woof, woof! Moo, moo, moo!  
Meh, meh, meh! Baa, baa, baa!  
Cock-a-doodle-doo! Meow!

label: children
translation:
Tikke-takke-tones,  
The little pig in the beans,  
The little horse in the oats,  
The little cow in the clover,  
The little sheep in the green grass,  
The little duck in the water puddle,  
The little fish in the net,  
The little child - up! in the bed.

label: children
translation:
Little horse, little horse, ride to the city,  
Bring cookies for the little child,  
Cookies with four corners,  
Equally narrow on all sides;  
Guess who will have them?  
The child will get those cookies,  
If it is naughty, nothing at all.

label: children
translation:
Gee, gee, little horse,  
With your fox tail,  
With your rough little paw,  
Jump over a little ditch,  
From the ditch into the reeds;  
The little horse doesn’t do that.

label: children
translation:
Gee, gee, little horse,  
To Brielle for fish,  
You don’t know what kind of weather  
It will be tomorrow;  
Give the little horse clover,  
Give the little horse oats,  
Give the little horse bran,  
Then it will trot along.

label: children
children
translation:
Sailing a little boat,  
Drinking tea,  
Picking flowers,  
Basket with crutches,  
Then we go to the Overtoom,  
There we drink sweet milk with cream,  
Sweet milk with curds,  
The clock strikes ten;  
And when the clock strikes ten,  
The bell ringer comes on the street,  
Eleven, eleven hours;  
The girls have to scrub,  
The boys have to fetch water  
At the neighbors’ back.

label: children
translation:
Boat ride, sea, sea!  
Child! will you come along, along?  
In that beautiful little boat,  
To grandpa’s?  
Grandpa has a spotted cow,  
Grandpa will milk her!  
The child will help grandpa.  
The child asked where the little boat lay,  
The little boat lay in the water;  
The little cow gave a bleat,  
The little cow gave a kick,  
Then all the milk was gone.

label: children
children
children
translation:
Little, little toddler,  
What are you doing in my garden,  
You are picking all my little flowers,  
And making it far too rough.  
Oh, my dear mommy!  
And don't tell daddy!  
I will go sweetly to school,  
And leave the little flowers standing.

label: children
translation:
Old Jan and young Jan,  
They would pump together;  
Old Jan broke his leg,  
And young Jan his clogs.

label: children
translation:
Uncle Jan  
Was sitting in a tree  
Waiting;  
Then the tree broke,  
Then Uncle Jan fell,  
And all the little cows  
Laughed.

label: children
children
translation:
Honeymoon  
In mother's lap;  
Peekaboo, my rooster is dead;  
My rooster with seventeen chicks.  
Wasn't that sung well?  
All that singing brings remorse.  
At night, all the kittens are gray.

label: children
translation:
Jan hit Lijsje,  
And Lijsje hit Jan  
Already with the frying pan.  
The frying pan in pieces,  
The pieces through the glass,  
And Lijsje went into a rage.

label: children
translation:
One, two, three,  
My sister is called Marie,  
And if she is not called Marie,  
Then she is called one, two, three.  
I give the farmer a hard push,  
So that he flew off the seesaw,  
From the seesaw straight into the ditch;  
Jan Dirks is dead.  
Where is he buried?  
Under the Delfshaven,  
Under the Delfshaven canal,  
Where you never see any people.

label: children
translation:
Sawing sound,  
Back and forth,  
Up and down,  
There and back.

label: children
translation:
In spring, the bend goes in;  
Out spray, the bend goes out.

Sharpen razor  
And the knife,  
One hundred and six.

label: children
translation:
In spring, the bend goes in;  
Out spray, the bend goes out.  

Sharpen razor  
And the knife,  
One hundred and six.

label: children
translation:
Black Willempje,  
Sat behind the curtain,  
She washed her hand with water.  
She dried it with the towel,  
She took one from the table.  
Black Willempje  
Sat behind the curtain;  
She put it on her hip,  
She took another one.  

I build a little house  
Of lime, a little safe  
Of lime and stone;  
There I throw my bullet.

label: children
translation:
Black Willemijntje,  
Sat behind the curtain,  
She washed her hand with water.  
She dried it with the towel,  
She took one from the table.  
Black Willemijntje  
Sat behind the curtain;  
She stuck it in her side,  
She took another one.  

I build a little house  
Of lime, a little safe  
Of lime and stone;  
There I throw my marble in.

label: children
translation:
Hein the ball,  
Who will volley,  
With a clap,  
With one hand,  
With two hands,  
Smack 'em against the wall.

label: children
translation:
Look for pins!  
I can't find any.  
Look for needles!  
I can't find any.  
Look for human flesh!

label: children
translation:
Sijmen the dear, the drunkard, the goat,  
How many horns are there behind?

label: children
translation:
Pieternelle! - Where do you live?  
In here.  
What do you know? - Reel and spin.  
What did you do yesterday?  
Ate a sandwich and went to sleep.  
What did you do the day before yesterday?  
That doesn't concern you.  
Who is knocking there?  
Anneken, Tanneken, Witch.  
What did you want to have?  
A piece of fire.  
I don't have that.  
I saw your chimney smoking.  
That was at the neighbor's.  
You're lying.  
May I come inside then? No.  
With my golden shoes? No.  
With my big boots? No.  
With my wooden clogs? No.  
With my glass slippers? Yes.  
What beautiful children do you have there.  
Would you like to have one? Yes!  
What will you feed them?  
Whatever they like.  
Where will you put them to sleep?  
On little feather pillows and little down beds.  
Rot and mice.

label: children
translation:
Free are the sons of these shores,  
gently sprinkled with Willem's blood,  
offspring of the people of the Seven Lands,  
great through Maurits' sword and courage.  
Let us keep through all times  
the old alliance faithful in honor:  
with Orange in joy and suffering!  
may the motto remain, whatever may fall or change.  
With Orange in joy and suffering!  
may the motto remain, whatever may fall or change.  

Strong as in the distant past,  
Honest, the noble ancestors worthy,  
Us in the ever-changing present  
Gathered around the ancient tribe.  
Never, when Prince and People dedicate themselves  
To each other’s welfare and honor,  
Shall they fall into the stream of times,  
Throne or freedom's sovereign down.  
Shall they fall into the stream of times,  
Throne or freedom's sovereign down.  

God of faithfulness! graciously  
Turn Your eye to our beloved land.  
Crown Your fatherly favor bountifully,  
Day by day our firm covenant.  
Grant that unity at all times,  
Inspire and strengthen us, O Lord!  
And in suffering and in joy,  
We are the Netherlands of yore.  
And in suffering and in joy,  
We are the Netherlands of yore.

label: children
translation:
There you wave again so free and happy,  
you Fatherland’s flag!  
So you wave – high, proud, and free –  
along sea and o-cean;  
So you often flew, in North and South,  
on shore and waterway  
before the glorious host of the Netherlands,  
you, Fatherland’s flag,  
before the glorious host of the Netherlands,  
you Fatherland’s flag!  

Your red attests to noble blood,  
Your white to pure virtue;  
Your blue shows God-dedicated spirit  
And sacred soul’s joy:  
O tricolor, – true, and good, and beautiful –  
You signify the people's character:  
A greeting of welfare and honor,  
To you, Fatherland’s flag!  
A greeting of welfare and honor,  
To you, Fatherland’s flag!  

Remain the Netherlands’ hope, remain the Netherlands’ pride,  
And still grant the late offspring  
The sweetest sip of festive delight,  
And courage, and support, and comfort.  
And where we – loyal to Land and Sovereign –  
Stand at the end of our path,  
Encourage us with your Heavenly Blue,  
O Fatherland’s flag!  
Encourage us with your Heavenly Blue,  
O Fatherland’s flag!  

Wave high then, wave proud and free,  
You Fatherland’s flag!  
Wave for centuries more so free and happy  
Wide over the ocean!  
Fly for centuries more, from North to South,  
On shore and waterway  
Ahead of the industrious people of the Netherlands,  
You, Fatherland’s flag!  
Ahead of the industrious people of the Netherlands,  
You, Fatherland’s flag!

label: children
translation:
Have you heard of the joyous celebration,  
The joyous celebration of today?  
How brave those Geuzen have always been,  
In the ancient past.  
The Geus! The Geus!  
Only Freedom was their motto! -  
They followed boldly and faithfully,  
They followed boldly and faithfully,  
The beacon of Freedom: the Prince of Nassau!  
The beacon of Freedom: the Prince of Nassau!  
See, it was just as well that the Englishman too  
No longer wished to harbor the Geuzen;  
They held onto some Spanish ships,  
And turned Spaniards into dwarfs. -  
Then forward, to the North,  
To the long-besieged place:  
Little Netherlands,  
Little Netherlands,  
For them, the holiest, the dearest pledge.  
For them, the holiest, the dearest pledge.

Yes, there the coast of the Fatherland rose again; -  
They knelt down reverently together,  
They bowed their heads reverently  
And spoke reverently: Amen. -  
Alva! Alva!  
For yours no mercy!  
The Geuzen advance,  
The Geuzen advance,  
It is over with your greatness and power.  
It is over with your greatness and power.

It was indeed then, the very first of April,  
- Now three hundred years ago,  
That Alva lost his precious spectacles; -  
The beginning of the battle was fought:  
Den Briel, Den Briel,  
Fell into the hands of the Geus;  
Covered in shame and scorn,  
Covered in shame and scorn,  
Duke Alva could go home.  
Duke Alva could go home.

Now let us sing joyfully the festive song,  
To proclaim the blessing of Freedom;  
The Geuzen, the Geuzen we do not forget,  
They truly were Dutch heroes!  
The Geuzen’s motto,  
Remains still all our choice;  
"For Freedom, Justice and Loyalty,"  
"For Freedom, Justice and Loyalty,"  
"And for the House of Orange-Nassau."  
"And for the House of Orange-Nassau."

m.a.b.b. (February 1872).

label: children
translation:
So in all the world’s places  
The people once found nothing good  
That did not consist of three,  
Today a quartet of words resounds  
Around the earth from mouth to mouth:  
Friends, let our motto be,  
With enthusiasm we sing:  
Fresh and cheerful, pious and free,  
Fresh and cheerful, pious and free!  
Friends, let our motto be,  
With enthusiasm we sing:  
Fresh and cheerful, pious and free,  
Fresh and cheerful, pious and free!

To be fresh in body and mind,  
Is that not a wonderful good,  
That everyone must strive for?  
Cheerfully one will then begin  
What causes the weak to quake.  
Friends, let our motto be, etc.

To be free is the right of all;  
Who feels free is pious,  
Knows no shame for what may come,  
Stands where slaves fall down,  
Straight and firm like a tree.  
Friends, let our motto be, etc.

Whether through thorns or through flowers,  
We go forward fresh and cheerful;  
Pious, that is what we are, as is fitting;  
And who would call himself free  
If he did not heed that word?  
Friends, let our motto be, etc.

label: children
translation:
Hold on, hold on,  
hold on bravely,  
you motto of our ancestors,  
pour strength and courage into the veins:  
- it goes against us, it goes with us,...  
hold on, hold on!  
Who then may lower their flag,  
Our Netherlands will not yield.  
It goes against us, it goes with us,  
it holds on, it holds on.

label: children
translation:
My mother tongue, my mother tongue,  
who or what clay may be,  
I love it like my fatherland,  
I stand for it with hand and tooth!  
O, my Dutch, my Dutch,  
I always hold it in honor,  
O my Dutch, my Dutch,  
I always hold it in honor!  
My mother tongue is the most beautiful language,  
And even if it were not,  
I would still prefer it,  
Because it is mine after all!  
O, my Dutch, my Dutch,  
Be forever praised by me!  
O, my Dutch, my Dutch,  
Be forever praised by me!

Frans de Cort.

label: children
translation:
The dawn breaks from the East,  
the light shines everywhere,  
how far away does the beloved know  
where I shall go—  
how far away does the beloved know,  
yes, the beloved.  

If all were my friends,  
that are my enemies,  
I would lead you out of the land,  
my comfort, my sweetheart,  
I would lead you out of the land.  

Where would you have me lead,  
bold rider, well met?  
All beneath the green linden tree,  
my comfort, my worthy greeting,  
all beneath the green linden tree,  
green linden.  

I lay in my beloved's arms,  
with greater reverence:  
I lay in my beloved's arms,  
bold rider, well met,  
I lay in my beloved's arms.  

Do you lay in your beloved's arms?  
If that is not true:  
Go beneath the green linden,  
defeated so he lies there,  
go beneath the green linden.  

The maiden took her cloak,  
and she went a way,  
all beneath the green linden,  
where she found him defeated,  
all beneath the green linden.  

Oh, lie here defeated,  
suffocated in your blood:  
This has made your fame,  
and your pride,  
this has made your fame,  
yes, your fame.  

Oh, lie here defeated,  
who used to comfort me?  
What have you left me,  
so many sorrowful days,  
what have you left me?  

The maiden turned around  
and she went a way,  
to her father's gate,  
which she found open,  
all before her father's gate.  

And is there no one inside,  
no lord or nobleman:  
who now can help me  
bury this dead man,  
who now can help me bury this dead one?  

The gentlemen were silent,  
they gave no sound,  
the maiden turned around,  
she went out weeping,  
the maiden turned around.  

With her golden hair  
that was stained with blood or paint,  
with her snow-white hands,  
with which she bandaged his wounds,  
with her snow-white hands,  
with her hands.  
With his shining sword,  
with which he dug the grave,  
with her shining arms  
with which she carried him to the earth,  
with her shining arms.  

With her shining hands  
that rang the little bell,  
with her clear voice,  
with which she sang the Vigil,  
with her clear voice,  
clear voice.  

Now I wish to enter  
a small cloister:  
and wear the black veil,  
in honor of my beloved.  
And wear the black veil.

label: children
translation:
Alas! I have lost  
The prince of my spirit,  
A knight born high  
Of clear and princely blood,  
Thus may I portray his passing well.

Now I shall no longer hear  
That lovely sound,  
That sweetly enchanted my soul’s ears,  
With kindly, loving speech,  
And fair pleas.

That mirror shall vanish,  
Where I so clearly saw my face shining:  
O dear and friendly light,  
Your splendor and your brightness  
Shall fade.

You are without feeling  
Of body and heart,  
Ah, if I could cool  
My extraordinary pain,  
Then I would not now weep,  
Nor mourn.

Now I want to withdraw  
Into a small cloister cell,  
And end there my life  
And secret pain,  
And shall mourn your death  
All my days.

But I shall build for you  
Here on this grave a house,  
Where I, with trust,  
In unity may confess my cross  
And my woeful sufferings.

Now I must go and wail  
And veil my face,  
And wear the black garments  
In honor of my dearest,  
I shall still honor him  
With mourning clothes.

g.a. Bredero.

label: children
translation:
Oh Leliana! all my goodness,  
That sets my little heart on fire,  
Because of your glances,  
Full of sparks,  
Sparks that from the droopy eye  
Shoot like arrows from a bow.

Beautiful one, where do you hide here in the greenery?  
I must quench myself with a kiss,  
From the tips,  
Of your little lips,  
Lips that, like rose petals,  
Are covered with a sweet dew.

The rising sun goes before me,  
Beckons my desires, to follow its trail  
And to lick  
The moisture of your little mouth sweetly,  
As he does from his Laura.

Now then my dear, strike the lute,  
Stretch out your head towards the greens;  
While waiting,  
My thoughts  
Fall into a deep reflection;  
Lily neck, that is not pure.

Or have you out of mischief  
Planned that you  
Would torment me a little,  
And once put me  
In a fire of impatience?  
I swear you will pay for it with me.

If I could just find you now,  
Dear nymph, then you have to endure it;  
A hundred kisses  
Are my desires  
Not enough for revenge:  
Then your neck, mouth, and jaw will pay.

label: children
translation:
It was morning, a girl was walking along the shore;  
a little boat, that was sailing, lay by the shore;  
and soon the friendly skipper was at hand,  
who said: "Fair child! Will you come with me?  
It is the right tide to sail,  
now the morning sun shines on the waves,  
take courage, fair child, and sail with me,  
take courage, fair child, and sail with me—!"  

The girl, with blushes on forehead and cheek,  
stood thoughtfully by the expanse of the sea;  
then from above sounded an enchanting song;  
then it murmured softly along the shore:  
"Go sailing, dear child! Go sailing!  
The morning sun shines on the waves:  
You carry happiness with you!"  
You carry happiness with you!"  

But tears moistened a motherly eye:  
the parting, the missing brings pain!  
And comfortingly again the song from above began,  
and the shore repeated more cheerfully:  
"Let the young couple sail! Let them sail!  
Equal in spirit and years,  
they cross a safe sea!"  
They cross a safe sea!"  

What did the girl do? She took the chance;  
and loudly the skipper shouted "hooray!"  
and the waves carried, with joyful dance,  
their little boat from the smooth shore.  
"Keep sailing, young couple; keep sailing,  
rocked on the bouncing waves;  
in the sunlight of prosperity and peace!"  
In the sunlight of prosperity and peace!"

label: children
translation:
Three side-drummers came from the East,  
three side-drummers came from the East,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
they came from the East, from rom.  
Say side-drummer, tell me what is your wealth?  
Say side-drummer, tell me what is your wealth?  
from rombom, what do I care,  
Tell me what is your wealth? from rom.  

My wealth is, I don't want to lie about it,  
My wealth is a drum with two sticks,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
A drum with two sticks, from rom.  

No side-drummer, you cannot have my child,  
No side-drummer, you cannot have my child,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
You cannot have my child, from rom.  

But old man, I forgot something,  
But old man, I forgot something,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
I forgot something, from rom.  

My father is Grand Duke of Castile,  
My father is Grand Duke of Castile,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
Grand Duke of Castile, from rom.  

Now side-drummer, you may marry my daughter,  
Now side-drummer, you may marry my daughter,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
You may marry my daughter, from rom.

label: children
translation:
Three oblique drummers who came from the East,  
three oblique drummers who came from the East,  
from rombom, what do I care,  
they came from the East, from rom.  
Say oblique drummer, tell me what is your wealth?  
Say oblique drummer, tell me what is your wealth?  
From rombom, what do I care,  
Tell me what is your wealth? from rom.

My wealth is, I won’t lie about it,  
My wealth is a drum with two sticks,  
From rombom, what do I care,  
A drum with two sticks, from rom.

No oblique drummer, you cannot have my child,  
No oblique drummer, you cannot have my child,  
From rombom, what do I care,  
You cannot have my child, from rom.

But old man, I have forgotten something,  
But old man, I have forgotten something,  
From rombom, what do I care,  
I have forgotten something, from rom.

My father is grand duke of Castile,  
My father is grand duke of Castile,  
From rombom, what do I care,  
Grand duke of Castile, from rom.

Now oblique drummer, you may marry my daughter,  
Now oblique drummer, you may marry my daughter,  
From rombom, what do I care,  
You may marry my daughter, from rom.

label: children
translation:
Aboard, aboard!  
The sailor's life vi - va,  
the sea vi - va, the sea,  
who will come with us,  
who will come with us?  
Vi - va the sea -!  
Oh Kaatje, don’t cry,  
there you have my best gloves;  
oh Kaatje don’t cry,  
there you have my bag,  
there was all my money,  
there was all my money;  
oh Kaatje, don’t...

label: children
translation:
There was recently a girl without a crew,  
she wanted to sail, she wanted to sail.  
There was recently a girl without a crew,  
she wanted to sail already as a sailor.  
But through storm and adverse weather,  
the sails flapped;  
the sails flapped;  
but through storm and adverse weather,  
the sails flapped down from above.  

Oh, little captain, do not hit me,  
I am your beloved;  
I am your beloved;  
Oh, little captain, do not hit me,  
I am your beloved as you see.

label: children
translation:
There was recently a girl alone,  
who wanted to sail, who wanted to sail.  
There was recently a girl alone,  
who wanted to sail already as a sailor.  
But through storm and adverse weather,  
The sails were struck;  
The sails were struck;  
But through storm and adverse weather,  
The sails were struck down from above.

Oh, little captain, don’t hit me,  
I am your sweetheart;  
I am your sweetheart;  
Oh, little captain, don’t hit me,  
I am your sweetheart just as you see.

label: children
translation:
There was recently a girl alone,  
who wanted to sail, who wanted to sail.  
There was recently a girl alone,  
who wanted to sail already as a sailor.  
But through storm and adverse weather,  
The sails were struck;  
The sails were struck;  
But through storm and adverse weather,  
The sails were struck down from above.  

Oh, little captain, don't hit me,  
I am your sweetheart;  
I am your sweetheart;  
Oh, little captain, don't hit me,  
I am your sweetheart just as you see.

label: children
translation:
How late it is getting! The clock strikes eight,  
Where or if my Doris is staying?  
It's hard when you wait for someone,  
And when fear drives us,  
To doubt his faithfulness;  
He may regret,  
That he asked a girl without money,  
Asked her to be his wife,  
Asked her to be his wife.  
Away, away with unfounded fear,  
I long for him with sorrow,  
So that I may read in his eyes  
The feeling of his heart.  
If Doris stays true to me,  
And I become his wife one day,  
Then there is no queen on earth,  
With whom I would trade.  
With whom I would trade.

No great status nor abundance  
Gives us true contentment.  
The love that satisfies the heart  
Makes one live joyfully:  
Then one shares joy and sorrow,  
Happiness and misfortune,  
And carries, comforted and cheerful,  
Together the yoke of marriage.  
Together the yoke of marriage.

But alas! My lover still does not come,  
Each hour feels like a day to me:  
What causes me grief in his absence,  
What could possibly harm him?  
I look out once again -  
But quietly... I hear a sound...  
It is he - oh yes! - how my heart beats,  
Come Doris, kiss your bride.  
Come Doris, kiss your bride.

label: children
translation:
Once there was a little farmer from Ouwerkerk,  
and a little farmer from Hazerswoude,  
and the little farmer's calf was dead,  
and therefore he was in mourning.  
Oh dear little farmer, don't cry,  
when the calf is dead it doesn't bite.  
The little calf with its fur coat  
lay yesterday dead in the calf pen,  
the little calf with its fur coat  
lay yesterday dead in the calf pen.

label: children
translation:
Co – line a good farmer’s son,  
the cream of the village lads,  
hardworking, well-built and handsome,  
thirsting to win Lisette’s hand.  
The girl, also a pretty farmer’s daughter,  
however, had sometimes been to the city,  
and because of this, her heart and mind  
had completely turned away from the fields and cattle,  
had completely turned away from the fields and cattle. –  
Thirsting to win Lisette’s hand.  
No, said the proud one, certainly no,  
no farmer will ever win my heart;  
Colijn then stormed off angrily,  
and went to court the gentle Filis.  
Now no single farmer dared  
to think of her, much less to ask;  
so she waited year after year,  
while no suitor appeared.  
While no suitor appeared.

Now she is forty years old,  
her beautiful face has completely faded,  
and most of what occupies her  
is speaking ill of youngsters.  
She burns with fierce pain and sorrow  
when she hears of Colijn’s happiness praised,  
because deep down in her heart,  
she must blame herself for her foolishness.  
She must blame herself for her foolishness.

O girls, who would gladly be married,  
let Lisette be an example to you,  
that you do not rely on any foolish hope,  
for you may also fear her fate.  
If you ask a good boy,  
then let him earn your hand,  
otherwise you lightly risk  
dying an old spinster.  
Dying an old spinster.

You tighten the skate, I’ll re-tie your straps,  
Should you fall, you will fall into my hands,  
Kaatje come on,  
Come onto the rink,  
Bravo! I thought it would go splendidly.

Willem was wise,  
For on the ice  
He chose no paths, ridden and gray;  
Far from the hustle by the tents and beacon,  
The pair knew how to enjoy themselves on the ice,  
Willem was wise,  
For on the ice,  
He won the heart of his Kaatje as the prize.

with a precious stone.

I give you this golden ring
And I know a lovely song,
that I sing while sailing.
And I know a lovely song,
that I sing while sailing.

Dear beauty! I do not ferry across
for a necklace, for a song,
No, for such trifles
you shall not step into my boat.
No, for such trifles
you shall not step into my boat.

Dear boatman! Ferry me across,
then tell me what pleases you,
And I will give you everything,
everything your kindness asks.
And I will give you everything,
everything your kindness asks.

Then let me press a little kiss
on your dear rose-like mouth,
For one little kiss, dear beauty,
I will ferry you around the world.
For one little kiss, dear beauty,
I will ferry you around the world.

The dear girl stood shyly,
bashfully lowering her eyes;
The blush turned glowing red,
and she sighed many times.
The blush turned glowing red,
and she sighed many times.

The blush soon disappeared,
that often colors the prettiest cheeks;
The dear girl was content,
and the boatman cheered up.
The dear girl was content,
and the boatman cheered up.

Sweetly the wind blew in the sail,
and the boat sped forth,
The girl sang a lovely tune
and the boatman sang along.
The girl sang a lovely tune
and the boatman sang along.

Both are now entwined
by the bonds of love;
For the mischievous little Cupid,
entered the boat with them.
For the mischievous little Cupid,
entered the boat with them.

- I beg you,  
please tell me something sometime.  

I have no time, I must leave,

Good evening! I’m going now,  
if you want some amusement,  
be at peace,  
stay alone,  
don’t hang on to me.

to love you;  
and my hand,

label: children
translation:
By the water’s edge—far from a swiftly flowing stream,  
A sorrowful girl was sitting;  
She wept, she cried from grief  
On the grass, wet with tears.  
She sighed and looked at him sadly,  
And spoke: ‘Oh noble man,  
Here stands a poor orphan,  
Whom only God can help.  

Do you see that green hill?  
There is my mother’s grave;  
Do you see the bank of this stream?  
There my father slipped away.  

The fierce current soon wounded him,  
He struggled and sank;  
My brother jumped in after him,  
Alas! he also drowned.  

Now I often flee from the orphanage,  
Where there is nothing but misery.’  
Thus she expressed her complaints,  
With sorrow in her heart.  

He said: ‘Do not complain, dear child,  
Your heart deserves no pain.  
I want to be your brother and your friend,  
I want to be your father.’  

He kindly took her by the hand,  
He, concerned with her fate,  
He gave her clothes befitting his own,  
Clothes for an orphan child.  

She ate his food, she drank his drink,  
Steadily, day by day,  
Give thanks, oh noble, kind man,  
For such good behavior.

label: children
translation:
Beautiful moon, tell me, do you see today,  
there where you survey the half circle,  
Yes, you see her, for no cloud  
covers your clear, shining eye;  
you can gaze unobstructed  
at the high arch of stars.  
Beautiful moon, tell me, do you see today  
my beloved Phillis?  
Yes, you see her, for no cloud  
covers your clear, shining eye;  
you can gaze unobstructed  
at the high arch of stars.  
Unobstructed! Ah! What am I saying?  
A roof or window might perhaps  
prevent you, O queen of the night,  
from seeing my zest for life.  
Envious roof, jealous window,  
let the eye of the pure moon  
through your tightly closed cracks,  
look upon my dear Phillis!

But if you, O delight of heaven,  
see my charming girl,  
then say that her faithful lover  
offers her his tender greetings.  
Say with a brightened little glance,  
through a more resplendent glow:  
“Beautiful Phillis, it is your lover  
who greets you through my rays.”

Night illuminator, nurturer of silence,  
do you see my Phillis now?  
Perhaps she sees, tenderly thinking  
of her lover, now on you!  
Perhaps she thinks, looking upwards,  
there where you survey the half circle:  
“Beautiful moon, tell me, do you see today  
my beloved Damon?”

Perhaps she sends a sigh  
of longing towards me.  
Catch this sigh, shoot it back  
to me with your rays below.  
Daughter of heaven, awakener of desire,  
remain the gaze between us both,  
and let your gilded rays  
ever be our messengers!

label: children
translation:
Here Ro-me-mont has separated me;
here, by this tree, which we were guarding.
Where can the fair one have been so long,
that she is not on time?
But oh, how long the hours are now!
Every glance back surely lasts a year.
At eight, the arrival was expected:
I estimate it is well past nine.

Val, evening. Would you not know your duty?
Or is time out of wings?
Or has Apollo forgotten his whip,
That he thus rides so slowly toward the sea?

Day leader, may this please you:
Shorten the day and lengthen the night.
Hurry forth: you will rest by Thetis,
And I by the girl I await.

translation:

To sea, to sea, to sea,
to sea, to sea, to sea. -
The company is already going ashore, - ha, ha!
And does not want to continue with us to the seahouse, - ha, ha!
The young ladies do not like it,
To sail to Japan.
Oh, no! oh, no! oh, no!
Oh, no! oh, no! oh, no!

They greet the guests with rumbling, - boom, boom!
They look anxiously at the shooting, - boom, boom!
Yes, wave your hat,
There will be many more farewell greetings.
Boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom!
Boom, boom, boom, boom, boom, boom!

Look, that hums nicely along, - hooray!
A fresh breeze blows around the ear, - hooray!
Farewell! oh fatherland coast,
Enjoy constant happiness and peace.
Hooray, hooray, hooray!
Hooray, hooray, hooray!

My dearest Betje certainly can, - oh no!
Not see how far her Louw already is, - oh no!
But when I come home with some money,
Our wedding day will be set.
Oh, yes! oh, yes! oh, yes!
Oh, yes! oh, yes! oh, yes!

Despite all the girls, small or tall, - luck!
I will love you until my death. - luck!
And if I return to the homeland,
Then shout to me also welcome:
Luck, luck, luck!
Luck, luck, luck!

The brook that flows past its roots,  
babbling in tune with the song.

But alas! I am alone in the house;

label: children
translation:
I sat in front of my door, spinning and singing;  
a young man heard my songs,  
he was so handsome – he lied to me,  
and red glow – those were his cheeks,  
I looked up once, but spoke no word,  
I sat ashamed and kept on spinning.  
He kindly greeted me good day,  
stepped up to me and seemed to be shy;  
I became so afraid... the thread broke off,  
and the heart... it had never beat like this before;  
I fastened the thread as best I could,  
I sat ashamed and kept on spinning and spinning.

Fondly he took my hand,  
and gently laid it in his,  
He had never seen a hand so fair,  
so pale, so soft, as mine –  
how much that praise won my heart,  
I sat ashamed and kept on spinning and spinning.

He then leaned on my chair,  
while he praised the fine thread,  
and with a heartfelt sigh,  
he confidingly called me his girl:  
He looked at me, I spoke no word,  
I sat ashamed and kept on spinning.

His cheek came closer to mine,  
where the clear eye looked even more tender…  
Accidentally my head touched his,  
that gently nodded beneath the spinning,  
He kissed me, I spoke no word,  
I sat ashamed and kept on spinning.

Seriously I rejected the young man;  
this seemed only to make him bolder,  
rashly he flew around my neck,  
and kissed my cheeks red as fire…  
Oh! tell me if it could be true,  
that in the end I kept on spinning.

Finish the whole today,

makes us forget all suffering.  
Creates joy in life, etc. etc.

Friendship dries tears,  
And scatters flowers for us to the grave;  
It makes the dark nights light,  
And shines everywhere.  
Creates joy in life, etc. etc.

It is the most beautiful, strongest bond:  
Come, brothers, give each other your hand!  
We want to be happy, content  
Until our blessed end.  
Creates joy in life, etc. etc.

Of sjarum, etc., etc.

Of sjarum, etc. etc.

Of sjarum, etc. etc.  
Sees there a miller - wiedewiedewiet -

They live there in love and faithfulness,  
Of sjarum, etc. etc.  
They live there - wiedewiedewiet -  
They live etc.

Of sjarum, etc. etc.  
And with this - wiedewiedewiet -

label: children
translation:
I am a young sol-da-ier  
of twenty-two ye-ars,  
born on the Sand, fal-de-ra,  
born on the Sand, fal-de-ra,  
born on the Sand, -  
that is my father-land.

translation:
The hunters of 'Van Dam',

and they eat some themselves. -
Move forward,
shout the people in times of war,
- lots of talk and little money,
they do not like that.

How do the soldiers get to heaven?

Captain and lieutenant,

Surely the devil takes them.
Captain, lieutenant, etc.

label: children
translation:
Who wants to know from us,
where the Netherlands are?
By the Lei or by the Nethen,
by the Maas or by the Rhine,
by the Maas or by the Rhine?
- There, where in South and North,
the old Low German is heard,
- the old Low German is heard.
Pious and virtuous as the fathers,
is the Dutch lineage;
the blood that flows through its veins
does not admit tyranny;
does not admit tyranny;
and the older's own manner,
reigns throughout the Netherlands.
Reigns throughout the Netherlands.

Cut off by no imperial border,
erased by no dialect,
it reigns until the grave of times,
the Netherlands' untouched maiden,
the Netherlands' untouched maiden,
in her vast domain,
that has been called the Netherlands for centuries.
That has been called the Netherlands for centuries.

Ah! what memories
bind us to each other!
Against Spain's hirelings,
we once fought eighty years.
We once fought eighty years.
Seek if you can still find peoples,
who love freedom as here.
Who love freedom as here.

Freedom! was the motto of the ancients,
and the namesake still wants it so;
all had remembered it,
until the field of Waterloo!
Until the field of Waterloo!
There, by the cannon's glow,
the Dutch blood flowed.
The Dutch blood flowed.

Though cut apart by fate,
the people's nature did not split;
here and there God is worshipped,
in the moving Low German song.
In the moving Low German song.
Here, and on the opposite bank
There and here is the Netherlands.
There and here is the Netherlands.

Brothers, you all must know,
where the Netherlands are;
by the Lei and by the Nethen,
by the Maas and by the Rhine;
by the Maas and by the Rhine;
and, as far as one speaks unblemished,
unadulterated Low German.
Unadulterated Low German.

label: children
translation:
A drummer, with a red mouth,  
found the most beautiful rose in the forest,  
that someone can find,  
ra-ta-plan,  
that someone can find.  
He quickly pulled it off,  
and pinned it in his buttonhole,  
ra-ta-plan, ra-ta-plan, ra-ta-plan, plan, plan, plan, plan,  
plan-, ra-ta-plan, ra-ta-plan, plan, plan.  
No flower more beautiful on earth;  
The drummer was worthy of the flower.  
Rataplan, etc.

She received it from a maiden:  
“Well friend, give me that beautiful rose,  
like a gentleman,  
rataplan,  
like a gentleman.” —  
“That beautiful rose I will not give,  
unless you offer me your faithful hand.”  
Rataplan, etc.

“My faithful hand I will not give,  
unless you command a regiment.  
Come back then, ask me then,  
rataplan,  
come back then, ask me then.”  
A year passed; one early morning  
it seemed to her as if a drum beat again.  
Rataplan, etc.

An officer, with red mouth,  
came, bowing deeply to the ground;  
He seemed like a gentleman,  
rataplan,  
he seemed like a gentleman.  
“See there the little rose I give,  
so that your little heart will think of me.”  
Rataplan, etc.

And happier than the greatest prince,  
he pinned it on her pale breast.  
Her little heart beat from it,  
rataplan,  
her little heart beat from it.  
There was never a more beautiful, dear bride,  
the drum beat to the festive sound,  
Rataplan, etc.

label: children
translation:
As many songs as sound in the bird-rich forest,  
As many pearls as shine on the green moss,  
As many stars as float above the trans in dense procession—  
That many times, my love, my life,  
That many times, be greeted by me.  
Nothing of all that on our earth  
Eyes and mind and heart delight in  
Is of higher value to me  
Than a glance from you, a word.  
Let others strive for treasures,  
Seek no fame or honor,  
Your love, my love, my life,  
That is all I desire!

If I may rarely come to you,  
If we are so far apart,  
It is blissful to dream of you;  
Ah, my heart is where you are!  
Again I must leave you,  
Weeping I go from you,  
Yet soon, my love, my life,  
Only death shall part us!

Frans de Cort.

label: children
translation:
"Ei - lie - ve, Niece! what must I hear?"
- Says Tante, a maiden of sixty years,
"Do you let yourself be beguiled by the menfolk,
and already long for the wedding altar?
A child, barely out of school,
already thinks of marriage! ..... Wicked times!
I fear, if one does not take care,
soon one will be making love in the cradle."
How! a portrait? a male being,
Set in a gilded frame?
Maria! I must fear the worst,
It is truly of a Lieutenant!"

"How do you come to that? Those epaulettes
Now tempt some young girl!
But I will forbid that golden fool
To ever see you again!
Like Argus I will watch over you,
And if he comes here, that sly fox,
He may then make his will;
I will unleash the bulldog on him."

A fiery glow colors Maria's cheeks:
She trembles with fear, like a reed,
Hides a note, just received,
For fear that Tante might see it.
She begs God Amor for mercy,
Who, deeply moved by the child,
Listening compassionately to her moaning,
And silently blinds Tante's eyes.

Thus she could not discover the note
The love messenger of the hero sent, -
Which, written in blood-red letters,
Announces the hour of the meeting.
Who, girls! will win the battle here?
Do you bet on Maria's hand?
Yes, fate favors those who love faithfully:
The niece gets her Lieutenant.

label: children
translation:
One, two, three,
My sister's name was Marie,
And if she wasn't called Marie,
Then she was called one, two, three.
I gave the farmer a hard push,
So he flew off the seesaw,
From the seesaw straight into the ditch;
Jan Dirks is dead.
Where is he buried?
Under the Delft harbor,
Under the Delft canal,
Where you never see any people.
Who is knocking there?
Anneken, Tanneken, witch.
What do you want?
A piece of fire.
I don’t have that.
I saw your chimney smoking.
That was at the neighbor lady’s.
You’re lying.
May I come inside then? No.
With my golden shoes? No.
With my big boots? No.
With my wooden clogs? No.
With my glass slippers? Yes.
What beautiful little children you have there,
Do you want to have one? Yes!
What will you feed them?
Whatever they like.
Where will you put them to sleep?
On pillows of feathers and beds of down.
Rats and mice.

Where you never see any people.  
Who’s knocking there?  
Anneken, Tanneken, Witch.  
What did you want?  
A piece of fire.

I saw your chimney smoking.  
That was at the neighbor’s.  
That’s a lie.

With my big boots? No.  
With my wooden clogs? No.  
With my glass slippers? Yes.  
What beautiful little children do you have there,  
Do you want to have one? Yes!  
What will you feed them?  
Whatever they like.  
Where will you put them to sleep?  
On little feather pillows and downy little beds.  
Rats and mice.

I build my little nest in the reeds,  
And no one sees it.  

10.  

Stork with your long legs,  
Takes the frogs out of the ditches,  
From the ditches onto the dike;  
Is that like a stork?  

11.  

Stork, stork, how long your legs are!  
Yes, yes, little frog,  
Equally thick above and below,  
Your calf is on your shins.

label: children
translation:
I know a lovely little song,  
That I already sang in my youth;  
The song of a little bird,  
That jumped into my room.  

It had such a friendly little voice  
And feathers, oh, so beautiful!  
I caught the lovely little bird  
And I put it in a cage:  

A cage with golden bars,  
With water, bright and clean,  
And with a little dish, filled to the brim;...  
But alas, it all seemed so small!...  
Out there beneath the green leaves  
So many songs resounded  
Of little birds, so free and happy,  
Yet mine did not sing.  

It screamed and called, as sharp as it could,  
And it flew around confusedly;  
Its little eye sparkled and it searched,  
Whether it could find a way out...  

And my heart full of sympathy  
I gave it back its freedom;...  
And in my small, narrow cage  
No bird ever came again!...
