With his little horse before his wagon -  
That's how Sinterklaas comes riding up.

label: st_nicholas
translation:
Five toddlers dance hand in hand,  
Five toddlers dance hand in hand,  
Because Sinterklaas is back in the country,  
On the beautiful, white horse.  

Joepla; now we turn around;  
Joepla! now we turn around;  
Holy Bishop, come, oh come,  
Now sprinkle for all the children!

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
It is now more than five years ago  
That I sailed to the East Indies,  
With my praised Silvia  
Who would be my sweetheart.  
Choosing the wild sea,  
Before I saw the desired cattle.  

2.  
I have also seen Japan,  
And Ambon as well.  
Along the clear water streams,  
Rich in water and birds,  
Where that black, fertile land  
Feeds her eager desires.  

3.  
From Japan all the way to Malacca  
They welcomed us well.  
In Suratte we stayed.  
There the sailors have the fame:  
Drinking the leftover wine,  
Merrily from the terracotta pot.  

4.  
I have not yet forgotten  
When I sat with the Akkerman,  
On his cart in the field.  
And we drank merrily,  
From Malacca all the way to Goeree,  
With lambs and doves too.  

5.  
Now I have changed my mind.  
I want to go to my love,  
And leave the black women.  
For I have a love letter  
From my sweet Silvia  
That I understand thoroughly.  

6.  
Since her uncle passed away,  
And the estate is so large:  
She has remained in possession  
Of house, garden, a high orchard,  
And of all kinds of fruit,  
Also of parsley herb.  

7.  
I want to sow that land again  
As I did before,  
To reap the fruit therefrom—  
Let the ships sail on the sea—  
Choosing the harsh ocean,  
And a green shepherd’s staff.  

8.  
How will my Silvia look  
When she sees Coridon  
Coming from the East Indies riches,  
And then welcomes him,  
With a newly tailored dress  
As the East Indies sailors do.  

9.  
I will ride sweetly with my beloved  
Along the beach in a carriage,  
And let my horses gallop  
So they puff through the sand.  
Let us drink the wine all around,  
For I am the bridegroom!

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
All those who cannot win their wages on land,  
Must go sailing by the sea.  
There they will be taught to climb upwards,  
Hoisting the sails right before the ship.  

2.  
“Haul cable, haul cable, wind up the anchor!”  
Thus the helmsman calls out so firmly.  
Then you see them running like the blind:  
One runs closely behind the other.  

3.  
As soon as the anchor is wound in,  
We must go back up again.  
Then the boatswain calls at every moment:  
“Here’s a chance to rest, aren’t you tired?”  

4.  
Then we steer from aft to fore.  
Wherever we go, we are tightly packed.  
The devil would not half listen  
To what goes on aboard a warship.  

5.  
It is scraping the mast, preparing it,  
Or caulking the ship, or tending the boat:  
However it goes — you may take note —  
Jan Hagel is disturbed from his cage.  

6.  
So that we cannot enjoy any rest.  
Jan Hagel makes everyone complain,  
They say: “It would vex the devil,  
To not be allowed to eat with rest.”  

7.  
The lookout from above, you may believe it,  
Calls out: “I see a sail in the sea!”  
Our helmsman, hearing this, hastens to the fore,  
Calls: “Men, make your sails fast!”  

8.  
Want the blind to help, Bram loosens the sails,  
Hoist up your stay sail, then you’re done!  
Then it’s: “Your belly girth and don’t be wild!”  
Then a lad must go up top.  

9.  
Our worthy captain calls: “Men, be ready,  
And make your boat fast and prepared.  
We want to row for a bit moderately  
For water and beer, all in all.”  

10.  
As soon as we are not seated,  
The provost calls out with no small voice:  
“Men, and if you haven’t eaten yet,  
Our boat must be unloaded.”  

11.  
You young fellows, keep to my best advice,  
What is loaded on me the whole day  
Is to tighten the sheet, or fasten the bowline,  
Or: “Reef your sails back to the mast!”  

12.  
You young fellows, speak freely about this,  
But don’t let yourselves be tormented by such appearance:  
Although sometimes the ship may heel somewhat,  
Remember, it won’t be a seven-year voyage.  

13.  
The one who first wrote us this new song  
Was a lad from Soetendal,  
He set it up aboard the ship Schiedam,  
And sang it. He does not lie at all.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
There will sail a little ship from the poles  
Of Amsterdam, to fetch pepper,  
As far over the sea,  
As far over the sea:  
Those who want to keep watch may still join.

2.  
Those who want to keep watch must choose  
To sail or shamefully lose their honor:  
The best of two,  
The best of two,  
Those who want to keep watch, etc.

3.  
All who do not ask for father or mother,  
Must endure hardships and blows there.  
Even if it pains them,  
Even if it pains them,  
Those who want to keep watch, etc.

4.  
If there is anyone left before we set off,  
Who wants to sail with us, they may still speak:  
Our little ship is ready,  
Our little ship is ready,  
Those who want to keep watch, etc.

5.  
With this, our sails seem to swell,  
With this, the ship goes to the helm:  
This goes to the sea,  
This goes to the sea,  
Those who want to keep watch, etc.

6.  
Farewell, young men! Those who can abstain  
From drinking and playing with beautiful women,  
Live here in peace,  
Live here in peace.  
Those who want to keep watch must go to sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
We who navigate the wild waves  
And sail the sea,  
Bumping along,  
Towards a desired shore,  
And through the fierce roar  
Of the waves once back home,  
Long to return,  
To go back,  
Without a cross:

label: work_sea
translation:
The life of the sea folk is,
As they, full of tempests.
When their fish is caught,
They bring it into the towns.
When they also go out fishing,
They must endure many storms,
Before the little Pentecost returns.

When they come ashore,
With their small light ships,
They lay the fish on the beach,
Which they then haul into heaps:
This gives the seaman joy,
If the fish still has breath!
But it does not live long.

When the fish is then sold,
They lay it in their bins.
It is brought into the city.
From youth they get used to it,
That they carry this fish:
This is two miles away,
Through dunes and wilderness.

Also, a hundred and fifty pounds,
A strong fisherman can carry:
You can well understand this,
That sweat must pour from him?
When they walk in the city,
They must satisfy their walk:
Many miles around that is.

When they draw fresh fish,
They do this with nets and horses.
The shrimp croaking is among them,
For profit, also high in value:
If no fish is caught,
They lack some money,
Which brings them to catch.

When the sea strikes fiercely,
They cannot catch fish then.
The folk indeed go to their doom,
When they then go their ways,
To catch its fish:
Where the sea is nearly full of it,
In its fury.

They care much for their village,
They would not part from it,
Even if their share could be
In another better time.
When they sail from the coast,
They fish also with pleasure,
Even if they have no rest.

The great sea also sweeps wildly,
As they sail on its waves:
Wild is also the wild beast:
So is the sea folk in their life.
Flocks is their daily work.
When they come out of church,
They mostly drink strong.

Lying is also their nature,
When they deal with your trades.
When they are joyful,
They speak many foolish words.
It seems they are free
From thievery in selling,
Although they overpromise.
Net weaving is the work,
For those who do not want to catch fish.
They also do not play very strongly,
In their reckless ways?
They go out fishing for herring.
They like whale hunting,
No matter how rough the sea may be.

Can a seaman be pious?
Why should it not be possible,
When they sail on the stream,
That they would not fear God?
Fishermen can be pious,
Though there is little appearance of it,
That their mind is pure.

When the storm rages very long,
This gives them bad times:
God the Lord does this lightly,
To lead these people to Him:
If they had understanding of this,
They lightly sailed from the shore,
To the eternal Fatherland.

label: work_sea
translation:
Skippers, steersmen, sailors all,  
Listen here quietly,  
and want to hear freely this song,  
what sorrow has happened to us,  
I hope God will preserve you  
From barbarians.  
We sailed freely from Livorno,  
We had many passengers aboard,  
All were to go to Rome,  
All willing to understand with our ship,  
Saint Anthony was the name,  
But we came.  
About thirty miles from the land,  
An Algerian approached us there,  
Equipped with fifty pieces of artillery,  
Five hundred Turks well trained,  
to destroy the Christian souls.  
He let the blood-flag wave,  
He thought this would be their sorrow,  
and we saw well on his banner,  
the great Turkish crescent moon,  
He called, surrender to Algiers,  
With manners,  
Our skipper brave like a pious hero,  
Says I pay no attention to Turkish violence,  
but I still have powder and shot here,  
and twenty-four large pieces,  
with that I will drive you away,  
From our lives.  
The Turk who hated this haste,  
Fired at us shot after shot,  
and we also boldly attacked him,  
did all that good could be done,  
to chase him away like that,  
With much complaint,  
Our captain gave us courage,  
Says men fight for Christian blood,  
And do not let your strength fail,  
I will assist you with body and blood,  
and God will fight with us,  
In these times.  
In this fight jumped by our side,  
five Dutchmen out of Turkish slavery,  
who had defended their manlike stature,  
their praise must be forever increased,  
no one wanted to be the least  
of these.  
The shooting went heavily back and forth,  
and lasted well eight hours long,  
Then our ship caught fire there,  
the Turks on all sides  
Threw hand grenades,  
But it did no good.  
We were hurt quite severely,  
Although many fell,  
Up to twenty-two in number,  
From whom blood streamed,  
But the greatest burden,  
Still came nearer.  
We already took on water,  
about six feet more or less,  
But the Turk was also not spared,  
certainly had eight feet of water,  
which caused him to complain,  
To torment us.  
We saw of the Turks also,  
Two hundred jumped into the sea,  
This still pleased our pain,  
Although our ship lay very wrecked,  
And saw no way,  
To govern us,  
But alas this touched us to the heart,  
And it was for us a great sorrow,  
That our ship still stood in the fire,  
That had lasted two hours long,  
No escape was there near,  
Through our lives.  
As the powder caught fire,  
The ship exploded with all its walls,  
Four of them died altogether,  
The cook and clerk in number,  
two sailors with one,  
And none else.  
Their pious, brave, and good conduct,  
Cannot be expressed,  
How bravely they stood,  
Against the Turkish dogs,  
Oh! God wants to give them now,  
Eternal life.

label: work_sea
translation:
Do you abandon my exalted soul,
Do you choose the wild waves for the plain,
Do you not trust a weak keel,
The goal of thousands of dangers,
Alas what sad pain,
When I must miss you,
I feel it in my heart,
Now that I must miss you,
I die in a blaze of love.
It is true I leave the Fatherland,
And you my Love my second life,
But do not judge my precious pledge,
That your Lover will forsake you,
Though my body travels at sea,
It is but half of me,
My soul does not travel with it,
Though my body travels at sea,
It is but half of me,
My love remains with you.
But read my sighs for nothing,
The wind carries you out of my eyes,
I miss you, Love, your soul's sorrow,
Ah, ah, how do I find my deception,
Or do you think my face,
Was not as beautiful as before,
Do you change love into hate,
Or do you think my face,
Was much more beautiful before,
May it never happen again.
My precious beauty, may the Winds
Carry you flying away from your sight,
So the Gods punish me freely,
If I sought to deceive you,
Believe then not
That ever my faithful Love,
Can turn into hate,
Believe then not
That ever my faithful Love,
Will leave my mind.
I shall stand by your side,
In times of storm and War gusts,
And fight with you through the Enemy,
To bear the oppressed,
My heart that laments and sighs,
Roams with you through the Sea,
Like Birds in the air,
My heart that laments and sighs,
Roams with you through the Sea,
Ah, turn and take me with you.
Ah Angel, cease your mourning,
And wish only to dry your tears,
I shall be eternally faithful to you,
And as soon as I have the power,
I shall be with you again,
Then give me for the farewell,
One more kiss, my dearest,
I shall soon be with you.
I beg you, do not cry anymore,
Or all my courage will end.
Alas, there is a sad kiss,
Given hastily for the last time,
And fear that I thus,
Now also give it for your life,
Oh Love, my precious pledge,
My heart breaks into pieces,
I will die from grief,
As soon as you are away from Land,
My heart breaks into pieces,
Leave me in distress.
Oh heart, and sad parting then,
That I must leave you,
Ah comfort yourself as much as you can,
And take Heaven to your aid,
And although by necessity,
As I now flee from you,
Death overtakes me,
Think that hard blow,
Happens by God's will,
And will never be seen again.
Go well then in the Lord's name,
I hope He will be your Guide,
And carry you everywhere easily,
I shall behave as before,
Always faithful in love,
As long as you are mine,
Farewell my Angel now,
I shall be faithful to you,
Until death separates us,
Farewell for eternity.
Farewell my Love, I shall trust
In the midst of the War celebration here,
That the favor of my Goddess,
Can well preserve me in great danger,
My Angel weeps no more,
Ah cease your sad sigh,
Your Lover returns soon again,
My Angel weeps no more,
Ah cease your sad sigh,
Before my Soul flees.

label: work_sea
translation:
Aurora’s journey day has come  
My love, I must now leave you,  
The drum beats with joy,  
On board, on board, for everyone,  
I am going to see the coasts.  
Already from the rich land of Asia  
That I hope to behold soon,  
My love, raise no objections,  
Although my body sets out on the sea,  
Across Nephtunis’ waves.  
I will, like a lover fine,  
Keep you in the heart of mine,  
Therefore cease all your complaints  
And dry away your flood of tears,  
I will act according to your wishes.  
Write to me how it goes with mine  
And think steadily early and late,  
Of you, my soul’s guardian,  
Whose beauty and perfect life  
Have wounded my eyesight.  
When I think of your rosy mouth  
And your round little breasts,  
Yes, your lovely being  
In which your goodness and virtue  
Are deeply inscribed,  
Then parting becomes a pain to me,  
Yet nonetheless it must be so now,  
My time has come,  
Farewell, my love: farewell goddess,  
Do not weep for my sake.  
When five or seven years have passed  
I will come to you again,  
Let faithful love continually dwell  
In your heart, oh beautiful maiden!  
I will come soon about that.  
So soon I will come back  
As I bow respectfully for you, oh young flower!  
Heaven remains your support, Calom:  
And here as our witness.  
I now depart for the Indies  
My darling, cease your sorrow,  
The time will quickly pass,  
When I shall behold my bride,  
Farewell, my chosen one.  
Batavia, completely pleasant,  
Provided with gold and diamonds,  
That I wish to see,  
Yes, Banda and beautiful Ternate,  
Their widely renowned princes.  
Japan and China highly esteemed,  
From where news is regularly brought,  
Cotton and silks very beautiful,  
Yes, porcelain and tea,  
The cloves adorn the crown.  
Where the Indies find their pleasure  
And eager love is steadily refreshed,  
That I am now going to see,  
No land or country under the sun  
To compare with it.  
Farewell at last my worthy pledge,  
Once more kissed, I leave the land,  
See flags and pennants fluttering,  
The anchor up, the sail set fully,  
Farewell, my exalted love.

label: work_sea
translation:
You Ionckmans hear my reason,  
Listen to my explanation,  
How I recently went deaf,  
Six or seven years ago;  
I left my homeland,  
For I was eager soon,  
To see the rich Asia.  
I arrived safely in that land,  
Raised with precious stones.  
There I did a lot of trading,  
I never forget it in my life,  
I saw many young men,  
Walking with black women,  
Whom they kept as a gift.  
But I despised this kind,  
I behaved like the pious,  
Until I completed my time,  
And returned  
To my very beautiful fatherland,  
There I saw girls on display,  
Above black women they took the crown.  
When I arrived in Zeeland,  
I left the palaces;  
I took my journey to Amsterdam,  
There I got into the revelry,  
With a girl, home and fine,  
She had to be a little Venus,  
For she liked soups and cool wine.  
She brought me to a cellar,  
I have forgotten the name,  
Phijool and Bas joyfully sounded there,  
We sat down,  
And we drank joyfully,  
But the wine overcame me:  
Oh hear how it happened to me.  
The innkeeper was a shady guy,  
With his company too,  
They surprised me asleep,  
And began to undress me,  
Then they carried me all together,  
Across the street over four men,  
And I knew nothing of ejaculation.  
The cold made me wake up,  
I opened my eyes,  
For I was in my underwear,  
So I found myself deceived,  
I called out to every girl, how is this,  
That one sits here so dark,  
But I lay on the street instead of the bed.  
Take here an example,  
You guests who hear this,  
I had to go home naked,  
Nor was I decently shorn,  
Therefore, everyone watch yourselves,  
So you do not fall into sorrow,  
As happened to me through drunkenness.

label: work_sea
translation:
Are you from Sintes or Paris,  
Young and old sailors,  
Do not fear the bears:  
The winter here is so cold,  
Later we will sail together:  
After Spekjan or the noble Jan,  
Though we sail boldly; we sail for salt,  
To measure the ground; and sail back again:  
It is too cold here.  
First we rest a while at Calais,  
Then we head towards the strait,  
There you find the foreign coasts,  
First the beautiful inn,  
Then they shout viva la Chase Sinjoor;  
I carry the key to their office,  
Set something down, Sydse my lord,  
I will offer it to you:  
Something to drink:  
At your request.  
Come quickly bring us some lemon,  
Pour us the mulled wine:  
What else do you have?  
There are one or two,  
And each row puts its doll on it,  
That’s how people spend their money dryly,  
But watch out: for the brown skin,  
Smear or burn;  
Watch your hands,  
The fire is fierce.  
I almost forgot,  
Livorno the beautiful street,  
In Bocchevelin if you want to know,  
There you will still find something,  
There the brothels stand house to house,  
For skipper merchants,  
Jan hagel in the safe,  
They shout in Spanish, Martillians,  
They shout, they wave,  
Something to drink,  
In Italian.  
The prostitutes of Alicante,  
When the money is spent,  
Then you find the light Dante,  
There is no prostitute or harbor  
Who can help us out of trouble,  
Because Jan Credit is already dead,  
Throw the scoundrel out the doors,  
His body full of torment,  
His skin full of wounds,  
There lies the scoundrel.  
Farewell sailors all;  
Who sail to the strait,  
And do not want to be fools,  
For whoredom is evil,  
Much rather keep your bakery tidy,  
Jan Pieterman dry,  
And your throat wet:  
And drink the wine,  
With fine young ladies,  
The street gang,  
Causes no trouble,  
So be it.

label: work_sea
translation:
In Vlissingen lies a ship ready,  
To sail to the East Indies,  
Upon which rests a Cupid,  
And plays on his gilded strings.  
O Cupid, little Venus’ child!  
How your gilded strings sound,  
They are curled in her lap,  
How pleasant it is to sail there.  
The first maiden who asks about it,  
I thought I saw the dunes shining,  
It was the island of the woman;  
From joy, my young heart sank.  
What kind of island can it be?  
What kind of island can it be?  
It was an island of the woman,  
That was being pressed by the lover.  
The moon shone, the sun disappeared,  
It is the stars that light us,  
The mast torn, the sprit broken in two,  
And yet we did not want to give in.  
Listen and understand at the Cape,  
Where the girls reside daily:  
Already in the house, the blue rooster,  
Where the wise converse daily.  
When we then arrive in the Indies Land,  
How joyfully shall we behave then:  
In the morning freely going outside,  
Against the Black skirmishes.  
When we then return to the Fatherland,  
How merrily shall we then dance,  
In the morning with brandy,  
And daily with good beers.  
Farewell, all you Utrecht girls,  
And remember this little song well,  
But we are not parting from here yet,  
A little glass we want to drink first.

label: work_sea
translation:
All those who cannot win their basket on land,  
They must go sailing by the sea,  
There people will learn to climb upward,  
Pull the sails straight before the wind.

Haul cable, haul cable, ready to wind the anchor up,  
So the helmsman calls out, all so stiff,  
Then you see people walking like the blind,  
One walks into the other’s body.

As soon as the anchor is wound up,  
Then we must go up again,  
Then the boatswain calls at all times,  
Here is something to rest, aren’t you tired?

Then we steer from aft to forward,  
Wherever we go, we are in the pinch,  
The devil would half invent it,  
What is going on on a warship.

It is scraping the mast, rigging the sail,  
Or caulking the ship or boarding the boat,  
How it goes, you may pay attention to,  
Ian Hagel was disturbed in his little loft.

So that we can enjoy no rest,  
Ian Hagel makes everyone complain,  
They say it would only annoy the devil,  
That with peace they may not eat.

The lookout from above, you can believe it,  
He calls out, I see a little sail at sea,  
Our helmsman hearing this hurried forward,  
Come men, make your sails ready.

Set the blind sail, loose the jibs,  
Hoist your staysail, then you’re done,  
Then your bowlines must not falter,  
Then one must go up to the mast.

Our captain calls out worthily, men be ready,  
And make your boat ready,  
We want to start rowing and quickly hurry,  
For water and beer, as everyone well knows.

As soon as we have not yet sat down,  
The provost calls with no small voice,  
Come men and have you not yet eaten,  
Our boat must be unloaded.

You young fellows, mind me the best,  
Because I was burdened the whole day,  
It is pulling the sheet or fastening the bowline,  
Or braiding your sails again before the mast.

You young fellows want to tell this freely,  
But do not torment yourself in such illusion,  
I myself will no longer trouble about it,  
It will not be a trip of seven years.

He who first wrote this new song,  
Was a boy from Zoetendaal,  
He has sailed on the ship Schiedam,  
He sang it, he is not lying.

label: work_sea
translation:
O Lord! We now prepare ourselves,
To sail to the North Sea,
And to leave our Fatherland,
Therefore we call upon You,
That You may show us Your assistance,
And free us through Your strong Hand,
Give us good Weather, and a favorable wind,
Show that we are those whom You love.
Let us sail to Your glory,
On the stormy Ocean,
Protect us from the power of the waves,
Which often rise to Heaven's heights,
Preserve us (Lord) from cliffs, sand, and shore,
And from the violence, and deceit, of the enemy,
Protect us from an unexpected death,
And assist us in all our distress.
Guide us through Your Holy Angels,
Let those be with us, night and day,
And so faithfully hang around us,
That no harm may come near us,
Protect us through Your outstretched arm,
From sorrowful lamentation, and miserable suffering,
Lord, do not let us perish in the flood,
Where many a man must lose his life.
Let us enjoy Your comforting Salvation,
And steer our course rightly,
And when we then set our sail,
Grant us a desired catch,
Just as You did with Peter,
When he had gone out fishing,
Give us likewise, from Your generous hand,
A double rich blessing in our sail.
Will You keep us (on this journey),
And will You deliver day and night,
And will You also spare in health,
Our Wife, and Children, and family,
Let all sickness, and disease, flee from them,
And let us see them again with joy,
Bring us healthy and fresh, with them to Land,
And let us flourish in a good state.
Farewell, the time of parting comes
Adieu, my Wife, and little children,
Father, and Mother, both of you,
And all my Friends in general,
My Neighbors and my Townsmen all together,
Farewell, farewell, we go in God's Name
To the wild land of Neptune's briny flood,
Where each of us must pursue his trade.
I hope that God will not allow
That we come into danger,
But that He will bring us back
In good health to each other,
I hope that He will free you from death
And give assistance in all your need,
And bind us by Love's strong bond
Until we come to the promised Land.
Now then, O Great Lord of Lords,
Who was Noah's Sea-Convoy,
Will You also always convoy us.
And be the course of our Compass,
Steer our Ship, of souls, in the Road
Of Salvation out of the world's evil Sea
Guide us all together, (through Your Mercy and love)
With Soul and Body, into Heaven’s harbor.

label: work_sea
translation:
O King of all Seraphim!  
I come to appear before your Throne,  
With songs of praise, and with thankfulness,  
Although with no human tongues,  
Rightly can be sung forth  
The glorious praise of your Majesty.  
Praise be to the Lord of Hosts,  
Because you have protected me,  
As long as I have lived here  
Through your praiseworthy wondrous works,  
Like an eagle with its wings  
Hovering over its dear young.  
Especially on this journey  
You, glorious Paleysen,  
Have so graciously cared for us  
That everyone, with confidence,  
May approach your Throne of Grace  
And in distress come to you.  
O Lord! the mighty floods  
Have come raging upon us,  
And brought us distress,  
But O you great Hero of heroes!  
(Who set up the stakes of the Wild Lake)  
You have faithfully stood by us.  
As soon as we began the voyage  
And went to the great North Sea,  
To do our work there,  
You deadly sent your angels,  
And let them daily hover around us:  
To mock our misfortune.  
You controlled the Sea and Wind  
So that their power could not overwhelm us,  
Nor bring us into great danger,  
For when you say to them to be quiet,  
Then they must, at your will,  
Be ready at your commands.  
Thus, O Lord of lords,  
You have brought us back fresh and healthy,  
And guided us by your hand  
And without harm or hindrance,  
You have brought our wife and also our children  
Back to the Fatherland.  
Who can rightly recount your praise now?  
Or Lord! what shall I repay you!  
For all this kindness?  
My weak tongue is too little  
To worthily sing out  
The glorious praise of your Majesty.  
But Lord, will you support me,  
And grant a pleasure,  
In my humble song of praise,  
Which with my (imperfect) tongue,  
Are sung to you, O Lord!  
Nearly all day long.  
Well then, my soul, praise the Lord,  
And honor his great name,  
With a sincere and grateful heart,  
Praise be to you Lord, Yes, highly praised,  
May your mercies be,  
Because you have delivered me from pain.  
O God, I will all my life,  
Give honor to your Name alone,  
And magnify your deeds,  
And please continue to preserve me  
So that no harm may burden me,  
Until I shall die the death.  
And when I then shall die the death,  
So let me, Lord, inherit your Kingdom,  
And prepare my weak Spirit,  
To with the Hosts of Heaven,  
Declare your Glories  
With songs of praise, forevermore.

label: work_sea
translation:
O Lord of Hosts! You King of all Lords;
You great and mighty God who for your glory and honor,
Have formed the universe, and set it in order,
And still reigns now: I call upon You today.
Lord, still this storm, which makes everyone tremble,
And do not strike us down because of our foolish whims,
That go against Your will, the head trying to rise above,
Ah, will You calm Your anger, and also calm the ocean?
You have brought a great tempest, Lord, upon us,
So that everyone is fearful, full of anxiety and dread,
For behold the streams of water that roar violently,
And have taken away from us joy! O heavenly Hero.
The ship rocks at the top, and soon again sinks down,
Ah! Stand firm today, O God full of tender love;
O peace-bringer of every soul, look upon us mercifully,
Control this heavy storm, or else we shall perish.
Will You, by Your power, protect us from the violence of the waves,
And their strong force, shelter and preserve us,
So that they do not weigh us down or bring death?
O Lord of hosts, deliver us from distress.
O God, You would have with rain, because of us (through our sins
And great abominations) consumed us
In the depths of the abyss,
And completely destroyed us,
But Lord (for Jesus' wounds) Ah! Will You still help us.
Remember Your grace, remember Christ's suffering,
How He had to struggle for us on Golgotha,
Remember those times, that were so bitter for Him,
And will You free us, only for His sake.
Bring us back to the land through Your all-wise goodness,
And tame the raging flame, and terrible fury,
Which now brings the flood of water, with which He torments us,
Show us Your sweetness, O Hero of all heroes.
Then we shall make Your Name great and honor it,
And all together turn away from sinful evil,
And increase Your praise, with a joyful spirit,
Come then O Lord of Lords: and still this heavy tempest.

label: work_sea
translation:
The day is gone: Yes Lord vanished,
The day is gone: And gone away,
Therefore we prepare ourselves:
To shoot the Wand into the Ocean,
And to spread out in Your Name,
And if You then kindly will stand by us,
O Lord of Lords! fulfill our desire:
O Lord of Lords! grant us assistance,
Let us enjoy Your blessing,
Through Your glorious gentle hand,
Now we go shooting in Your Name,
Give us now many Herrings in our Wand.
Give us Your Blessing: Like ripe rain;
Give us Your Blessing: And make us glad,
Grant that our Wand (when we draw it)
May be laden with Your blessing;
And let Your great goodness extend
To the salvation and blessing of our Fishery.
And when Your goodness has so favored us,
Then let us also thankfully praise You;
And let Your Name be magnified by us:
Let us praise You, Lord above,
Because we have tasted Your favor.
For this trade is our livelihood:
For this trade we must engage,
To thereby earn our living
And feed wife and children likewise:
And all our entire households,
Hereby escape pressure and poverty.
The Merchants also, Who provide for us,
The Merchants also: thereby sustain themselves,
And without Your special Blessing
They must surely perish:
For everything depends on this:
And everything hangs upon Your blessing.
Will You then turn, O Lord of Lords,
Will You then turn, To our counsel;
Let us receive Your Blessing
Upon the work of our labor,
And always let our hope rest
On Your faithfulness and mercy.
Well then, we cast, Now our Nets,
Well then, we cast, our Wand into the Sea;
On Your word, and in Your Name
Just as the Apostle Peter did:
Oh! if Your goodness would together
Also so shine upon us in full peace.
Lord, will You draw us, Out of earthly places:
Lord, will You draw us, very kindly:
Through the net of Your word out of this world;
Deliver us from the devil's wicked practice:
And let us be forever confined,
In Your glorious Heavenly Kingdom.

label: work_sea
translation:
O Lord of Hosts!  
You who are the strongest in power,  
who cause the wind and raging waves  
to roar with great force,  
and hastily calm and gently  
turn them back in their place,  
O Lord! please in my watch  
restrain the wind and waves,  
so that they do not harm us.  
Restrain my eye from sleep,  
so that I may look down attentively,  
Let no storm overtake us,  
but flee far from us,  
Please grant us your mercy,  
and be our Steersman;  
then no disaster will happen to us  
or at least cause fear.  
Ah! hear me now in this,  
protect us on this journey  
with your all-good strong hand,  
make us constantly consider  
the joy of the Heavenly Fatherland,  
so that we flee all disgrace:  
and honor your Name;  
Please enlighten and increase our understanding  
to this end,  
O Great Lord of Lords.  

And now that I must watch here,  
and that I measure weather and wind,  
so remind me in my thoughts  
that I must watch for your salvation,  
and keep my eye on the sail,  
to live piously,  
O let your spirit from the highest peak  
hover over my soul,  
and grant the powers of virtue.  

O Lord, my times are passing,  
and see, my hourglass is almost empty,  
assure me in hope  
that I, through David's noble offspring,  
have been purified from the weeds,  
from the cursed snares of sin,  
and that I now as his Bride  
am firmly united with him  
through the blood of his wounds.  

O shoot of all Branches,  
O Green Bough! highly esteemed,  
sprouted from David’s stock:  
and yet eternally brought forth  
from your Father mighty in power;  
O please strengthen me,  
and keep the watch over me,  
work powerfully in me,  
make me mindful of your salvation.  

Guide me in earthly life  
through your Spirit and Word,  
You who are exalted on high;  
Steer me in your Father's path,  
and please cause me to enter  
Heaven's Gate  
after my death;  

Be Lord over me, never disturbed,  
let me gain your joy,  
and inherit the Kingdom of Heaven.

label: work_sea
translation:
Dear great God, how have You been dishonored,
By unrestrained, human kind,
For Your name is being
Heavily slandered and brought to disgrace because of our sins;
Oh! what greater evil does one see on many ships,
Where evil is eagerly embraced,
And where everyone,
As if a virtue, drags it forward with joy.
What greater evil does one hear being mentioned,
Slandering and cursing, has become very common;
Many want to repay evil with evil,
Or their mouth is full of light songs,
Of lewd talk, and of drunkenness,
And of venomous gossip,
In this wicked attempt,
Everyone seeks the highest ranks.
Much of their talk is full of horror,
Many must hear much lewdness,
Sometimes they mock the prayers,
Yes, they grieve over it when it is done,
For it is too long to be at Prayer for even a small half hour, oh wicked custom!
Where at the revelry,
One could last a whole day.
Brothers, do you not think if you dishonor God the Lord
Like this, that He will also,
(According to your deserts) torment you with punishment,
In the comfortless sad valley of tears,
Where a bitter woe, with gnashing of teeth,
The evil people will strike most severely,
In the hellish kingdom,
Where it will burn eternally.
But this is not said to you all,
As if God’s names are by everyone
Thus godlessly and slanderously with blasphemies
Dishonored and trampled underfoot,
For there are many who greatly dislike this,
And (for the love of God) complain about it
Serving the Lord,
And also uphold His honor.
But this is said to the open,
Worldly servants, who (as if it were a virtue)
With their tongue continually slander the Lord of Hosts,
With pleasure, and joy,
And with shameless cursing, and horrid swearing,
They go about dishonoring Him most grievously,
And whose heart, stubborn and
Does not turn away from this wicked deed.
Yet I pray you all, flee such works,
Take heed to God’s Word,
Keep His Law,
Serve Him as He teaches you in His Churches,
Turn away from evil, and pursue good,
Then God will also protect and guard you,
From the violence of the waves
In your journey,
Storms, and sorrow
Will then not burden you.
If you serve Him thus, and evil comes your way,
Then His mercy will abundantly
Be your help, He will not abandon you,
If you cry to Him in distress,
But He will then hear your brief prayer,
And you shall never be lost,
For God has sworn
This covenant through the mouth of His Prophet.
He will give you comfort and help in your pressure
So that you shall not perish through it,
And over that He will, after this life,
Forgive you all your debts out of grace,
And when you come to die,
You shall obtain the throne of Heaven
Through His good favor!
And His glorious kingdom
You shall also graciously inherit.

label: work_sea
translation:
All who must sail  
On the wild Element,  
Learn, however, who can protect you,  
Be not unaware of this,  
It is necessary for you to know,  
Therefore see who it is,  
For if you forget Him,  
Eternal darkness awaits you,  
But if you receive knowledge  
From your Maker, your Lord,  
Want to step boldly into the ship,  
Then do not fear the roaring Sea,  
But want to firmly trust in God,  
With a pious sincere heart,  
Then He will surely preserve you,  
If only you do His will.  
Always want to practice this,  
To know and to do,  
Then He will well repel evil,  
And protect you from all harm,  
Always want to be thankful  
To the One who created you;  
With a childlike fear,  
Yet trembling before His Majesty.  
Then you can wander safely,  
Where God’s weather and wind guide you,  
No matter how you come to die,  
It will still serve your salvation.

label: work_sea
translation:
You spit on Nepthunis race,  
On the field there salt pond:  
With so many pious souls;  
On a fatherland’s keel,  
Of captain and little soldiers’ goods!  
And brave little sailors,  
Who love the fatherland:  
To overcome the enemy,  
To the last drop of blood.  
The ship Rozendaal valiant,  
Captain Breel also well manned:  
Will lead us with good courage,  
On Saint Ubes’ tide;  
Through the brackish waves,  
Dearest love, do not weep,  
When we come home:  
We shall without shame,  
Make of us two almost one.  
See our pilot is on board,  
Everything now as it should be;  
With a lucky and safe voyage,  
And five shots as requested;  
See our anchors before the Bazaan,  
See the round sails stand;  
See our little rudder is turning,  
Let the blue flag wave:  
To sail out to sea thus.  
To Lisbon is our desire,  
By the great God and Lord,  
Who is our chief helmsman;  
And he is sure of everything,  
I hope he will protect us:  
Be it from storm or lee shore,  
From sands and from beaches  
From the violence of the enemies,  
By his great goodness all.  
It seems to me Saint Ubes’ salt,  
That shines on us like gold:  
Through all the rays of the sun;  
On the field  
of the salt source,  
And then we go inland:  
Thus Portuguese Bogaart in,  
Full of apples certainly,  
Full of oil, figs, wines,  
Just as we desire and wish.  
And then we go hand in hand,  
And stroll all through the land:  
Through the vineyards and the grapes,  
With a lady high of plume,  
Or a Portuguese maiden,  
Who pleases my heart and soul:  
Who would then want to stay home,  
Always hearing nagging fights,  
From his wife undaunted.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
My sorrowful sighs are of no use,  
Alas, I must go away,  
My fate calls me back to the sea,  
Ah! if you had pity on my complaints,  
You would soon alleviate my misery,  
And give me your love along.  

2.  
If I could be certain of that,  
My tenderness would be without fear,  
If I could please myself with your love,  
No heavy storm or thunderbolt,  
Or whatever disaster came to plague my soul,  
Would ever remove you from my mind.  

3.  
Even if I came to foreign lands,  
No beauty would captivate me there,  
Even if she were like Venus herself,  
I could not be swayed by any treasures,  
No gold would ever taint my love,  
I would leave you for no kingdom.  

4.  
Ah, if you only knew how I waited for you,  
What complaints I made on the bitter nights,  
Chattering with teeth from the cold;  
You, O Phillis, showed compassion,  
And did not allow my eyes,  
For this time, to be overcome with tears.  

5.  
Cupid, bound in your love's bonds,  
I willingly give my heart and hands,  
Ah! see what force joy compels.  
If my lover's heart is bound to mine,  
Then he becomes mine, and I become his,  
Our love is nourished by your glow.  

6.  
Cupid’s feathers carried me on my back,  
Aeolus blew into my light clothing,  
Thus I saw you in a short time;  
But all my wishes I must cease,  
I can no longer reach you now,  
My heart full of love is ready for you.  

7.  
Mercury, you know how to accomplish much,  
Bringing through the power of your wings,  
My lover this faithful confession.  
You are the god of love,  
You help lovers in their courtships,  
Speed your wings as you fly.

label: work_sea
translation:
Dear Friend,  
I must go sailing at sea again,  
Dear Friend,  
I must go back into the sea,  
But do not let the journey trouble you,  
We are going to Turks and Barbarians,  
Dear Friend.  
When I am at sea,  
You are always in my thoughts,  
When I am at sea,  
I always carry love with me.  
It alone can ease my sorrow,  
In ugly weather and dark nights  
And stormy sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
The Batavians are coming soon
Want to sing early and save
The Lord Jehovah;
Praise, glory and honor,
Who now remembers you again,
Gives such victory
And wounds your enemy,
More and more,
Who so courageously came to attack you
Full of high pleasures,
On his coasts,
So many men.
February eighteen,
Sixteen hundred and thirty-nine, they saw
A battle;
Before Duinkerke on the Rhee,
Of two admirals,
the Holland and Duinkerke together,
The same day
Even that collar, over three years

In Zeelandic greetings,
No forgiveness,
But heavy sadness.
This was still on her mind,
As she thought to win back
With cross and edge,
On a Friday at eight
o'clock, she with her power
Came, but her expectation
Was, a Herp,
Who came to play with Zarwat,
To thank her,
But though the banks,
Fell to her heart.
Well Admiral Michiel,
Wonder how you liked
This Batavian skirmish?
That you went to say adieu
For the motto,
Of a blue nose,
And left it to the Geus.
Two new ships,
And your vice admiral through necessity
Completely burned,
Or did you go ashore,
In Charon’s boat.
Praise Herpet Admiral,
And Bonkers who for the steel,
Your enemy many times,
To the ground fallen;
Praise captains together,
Unknown to me by name,
Your honor will through fame,
Be told,

As long as Phoebus shines on Earth
Sailors, soldiers
Will be, through the talking
Your praise recounted.
Princess, oh Lord! the praise,
Comes especially from you here or,
Because we are but dust,
And our power
We assign with this work
To you who are very strong,
For us are a bulwark
Day and night,
Against our crafty enemy's pack,
Therefore your singing
We bring the honor to
Because the flesh is weak.

label: work_sea
translation:
Are you of mind to sail,  
Young and old sailors,  
Do not fear the births,  
Winter is so cold here,  
Come, let us sail together then  
To Spekjan or the Indian,  
We sail boldly,  
We sail for salt,  

To sound the grounds,  
And to sail back again,  
It is too cold here.  
First we rest a bit in Calis,  
Then we head towards the Strait,  
There one finds on foreign coasts,  
For the first time, the fair hostess,  
Then they shout, Viva la Chasen Sinjoor,  
I carry the key of the comptoir,  
Set some down,  
Say, my lord,  
I will serve it to you,  
What would you like to drink  
According to your desire.  
Come fetch us some lemon,  
Pour us the mulled wine,  
What else do you have done?  
There, is there a piece or two  
And everyone there sets his doll on it,  
Thus one spends money dryly;  
But beware,  
For the brown skin  
Pain or burning,  
Watch your hand,  
The fire is fierce.  
I almost forgot it,  
Livorno, the beautiful city,  
In Boekwelm you want to know,  
There you can still find some shelter,  
There live the whores house to house  
For sailors, merchants,  
Rough fellows in hiding,  
They shout in Spanish,  
Martilian,  

What would you like to drink,  
In Italian.  
The whores of all sides,  
If the money is now spent  
Then you find the light dancers,  
There is no whore or servant,  
Who can help us out of trouble,  
For Jan Credit is then dead,  
Throw out the scoundrel,  
Out the doors,  
His body full of plague,  
His skin full of blows,  
There lies the scoundrel.  
Farewell sailors all,  
Who sail to the Strait,  
And do not go crazy,  
For whoring is bad;  
Much better keep your bakehouse smooth,  
Jan Pieterman dry, and you in the wet,  
Drink the wine,  
With fine young ladies,  
The street gang  
Causes no misery,  
It may well be so.

label: work_sea
translation:
Consider GOD'S greatness, with deep awe,
You all who are used to sailing with ships!
There many signs will become apparent,
That show you what the LORD is capable of!
WHO commands the word, where the Sea and Wind,
Keep quiet, or rage so terribly,
That all mankind together, its strong waves,
Can protect as little as the weakest child!
I pray to You, O FATHER, in the SON!
That WORD, through whom YOU formed everything in the world;
May Your SPIRIT teach me the right way to worship,
When I approach Your high THRONE!

label: work_sea
translation:
O LORD of the Sea!  
And of the dry land too!  
GOD, who dwells on high!  
How blessed is the one who knows You;  
Who relies on You;  
And walks before Your Eyes!  
O benefit to You, the LORD!  
But the good that YOU work,  
Or that which YOU strengthen in me,  
Shall be to Your Name's honor!

label: work_sea
translation:
Here I stand now, on my watch;  
The work of others is completed:  
My GOD! may I also stand before You!  
Behold me, in my poverty.  
The Devil is often on the move:  
He conquers so many!  
Ah, may I be girded by You,  
So that He never becomes master!

label: work_sea
translation:
Who would not fear Your NAME?  
Formidable KING! mighty GOD!  
Especially since You so show in these,  
The fickleness of our fate!  
Would also, O LORD! bless these journeys,  
On which we are now about to go,  
So that we may say,  
GOD has truly helped us!

label: work_sea
translation:
O JESUS, at whose word the Sea and Wind,
How greatly angered, and as if unleashed,
(So that no effort or labor could help)
Were once calmed so swiftly!
Above all, let that treasure, our spirit,
(As once it was Your will that we should strand,)
Forever be entrusted in Your Hands,
And may this hope make us unshakable!

label: work_sea
translation:
How can we not speak of GOD’S wonders!  
When the storm, so fierce,  
Made the water boil beneath us;  
And the spray flew! so fast!  
How terrible must that CREATOR be:  
[The GOD who lives forever]  
Before whom the elements tremble,  
That everything cracks and quakes!  
When His EYE sees us falter,  
So it also closed;  
The water rose heavenwards,  
GOD calls as from above,

label: work_sea
translation:
- - It is allowed to sail safely through the waves
Sails.
No fleet of Josaphat, no Holy,
Like Jonah, finds itself safe at sea.

O Almighty, who buried the sea,
When at Your voice the earth’s flood fled,
With dunes and shores confined it,
With door and bolt firmly closed:
Teach us not to serve You bewildered,
Than ebb and flow, or wind and tide.

Protect us from the stormy raging
Of the seething element, and restrain
Your swift harness of wind and tides:
Grant that we, your wonders, free from fear,
Seeing Your power here, never to fathom,
May sail the harbor, to Your praise.

The world, horribly inflated
With pride, makes a wild sea,
Where storm upon storm rages fiercely.
Chief helmsman, steer us to the roadstead
Of the bliss above here,
So that in full rest we may praise You.

label: work_sea
translation:
Daughters of Holland,  
It is no disgrace to me  
That I have gone sailing,  
All the way to the East Indies,  
But think, if you are that one,  
How you spend your time there,  
In the blooming of your years,  
Wasting away in misery.  
Three months clearly,  
You get in a year,  
And he must still stand,  
Like a beggar,  
And then you get rations,  
It is three times a week,  
It provides a meager table,  
When one has no money.  
The water-pot  
Is the best lot here,  
And with that to eat,  
Like pigs in a shot.  
And if I say a word,  
The helmsman gets annoyed,  
And then he mutters and grumbles,  
Or you will be murdered.  
Friends listen,  
If I want to have a drink sometimes:  
A little sip,  
It is not allowed to rise,  
Or a bottle of sour beer:  
It is far too bad a way  
For anyone who is used  
To drinking wine and beer.  
If I drink myself drunk once, then I must immediately spin,  
Close my slates,  
Already very wet in the Galley,  
And in the morning enduring,  
Then I must, undisturbed,  
Appear before the helmsman:  
There I am questioned.  
If it is then squandered,  
Then I am subjected  
To five six hundred lashes,  
On my pants in front of the mast,  
But if he is obliged to me,  
By friendship in regard,  
I get my skin full of lashes,  
Or a lousy poem.  
Rice, that is our bread,  
In our great hunger,  
If one then has a dry little fish,  
Then there is still no need,  
A drink of water alongside,  
Then we are out of misery,  
But sometimes only eight sparrows  
In a day's meal free.  
Who would not want to be,  
In my place,  
To work every day  
And by nights certain,  
Because in this heat it is hard,  
Sometimes truly in the open,  
I would rather that East Indies,  
Were for the devil, truly.  
Alas, I lament,  
My youth every day,  
I will mourn to death even more,  
Here steadily in East Indies,  
But once I return a fine gentleman,  
Healthy again in Holland,  
I will surely say about Indies,  
I despise your state.

label: work_sea
translation:
If a King is well seated  
At his table to eat obediently,  
We are not the ones,  
Who bring it so, I mean,  
We are those who are willing to risk body and life,  
Bring it all  
To the shore,  
According to his desire and pleasure,  
With permission,  
What is a court,  
Does one not feed there or what.  
We who sail with our ships,  
Over restless waves  
On the brackish sea,  
We know we bring everything,  
That almost a person can imagine or name,  
Would one then  
Man by man,  
Not boast of our deeds,  
Because one must  
In the fleet,  
Bravely taste sour and sweet.  
We who watch with much zeal,  
Although masts and yards creak,  
And the wind  
Roars, and devours everything,  
Then we must avoid low shore and cliffs,  
Each in rank  
Bold or scared,  
Is a master in fighting,  
Night and day  
With endurance,  
Shows what we are capable of.  
That we all greatly fear,  
Neptune’s wild currents,  
The trade,  
Which in all countries is weak,  
We make the stock exchange  
Chest and cash and everything bloom,  
Seafaring is surely,  
That the countries come to be sprinkled,  
Time after time,  
Yet the Lord,  
Comes alone this honor.  
Though the winds blow easterly,  
Or those fall in the south,  
Or are heard,  
In the west or north,  
We know how to furl our sails there,  
And with one  
On the beam,  
To look out at the beacons,  
Exactly on time  
That our ship,  
May not hit a rock.  
If the supreme lord on high,  
Wants to give us a safe voyage,  
We each are a support,  
And useful to all countries,  
Sun, moon, and stars are our lights,  
On the waters  
The compass  
And many other sights,  
For the eye,  
Sextant and bow,  
Must serve us for the dry land.

label: work_sea
translation:
O Ye Maas-Sluys! highly exalted,  
I must bring forth your Praise,  
And with a Song give honor,  
For I have abundant material:  
My mind and my thought,  
To tell your Praise,  
Which shines up to Heaven.  

2 Since the time of fifty years,  
You have triumphed so greatly,  
That I can scarcely explain it,  
But experience teaches well:  
For luck and prosperity,  
You have in abundance, very much:  
Besides it used to be,  
Is it not a great difference.  

3 You used to sit in the Ashes,  
For your number was very small,  
You seemed to be like a Marsh,  
And became a very small band,  
But since this time,  
You have greatly increased, the number  
Of people has grown,  
Praise God with joyful sound.  

4 Your industry is seen to increase greatly,  
From your corner and herring trade,  
Which also honors many a man,  
Throughout the world it is renowned:  
You feed on every side,  
The whole Fatherland, very well  
Besides many other cities  
Thus you wear the crown.  

5 What also amazes many a man  
Through God’s favor and great blessing,  
Many Hoekers are counted more than hundred,  
There are also many herring-boats,  
Which through God’s blessing boldly  
Do their trade there, very well  
Where much money is seen gained,  
From many a boat companion.  

6 On the Maas stream you are situated,  
Very skilled for your shipbuilding,  
Which also wants to bring you to the edge,  
Herring and fish very pleasant:  
Whether fresh or salted,  
More than can be unsalted, if not  
A very praiseworthy blessing,  
That you receive from God.  

7 You also have besides this trade,  
A law and ecclesiastical government  
Granted to your benefit by God,  
Through which you live in peace:  
God’s pure Word and Doctrine,  
Stands planted in your midst,  
Lovers of Religion,  
By blessing of God’s hand.  

8 O highly exalted Maas-Sluys,  
Would God praise the Lord with song,  
Would give Him praise and honor,  
For what He has done to you:  
Fall to God the Lord’s feet  
And thank Him with humility, surely  
For to the Lord’s blessing  
It all depends.

label: work_sea
translation:
Listen, friends and understand the wildlife,
I will explain to you,
What one sees going on at sea,
Of those who sail for herring:
Who venture onto the waves,
To catch the herring:
Back and forth,
And how their business goes on in hardships,
Hear the explanation.

2 When our ship is prepared
With all its equipment,
And the sailors are aboard,
We take our places:
To go to sea quickly,
When the time has come,
Which we consider good:
The convoy and favorable wind taken,
This well anticipated.

3 We sail with common hand,
Taking a North-North-West course,
In the North Sea up to Hitland;
We sail without hesitation:
And there arrive at a good spot,
Bressem or Leverwijke,
Or Mouse-hole too,
There we anchor without fail,
On a good roadstead.

4 When the time of Saint John comes,
We leave the roadstead again,
Each behaves bravely like a man
And shoots his wand peacefully,
Make ample roadstead, sailor good,
Everyone diligently attends to it,
With cheerful heart,
Set out in the Name of the Lord,
Who must provide.

5 When our wand then stands at sea,
And fishes by God’s grace,
The cook prepares our meals:
With which we satisfy ourselves:
Herring roasted to our liking;
Eat and then go to sleep,
Until our night’s rest,
Five or six hours then we wake again,
Hauling with eagerness.

6 The spool-runners go to the spool,
There one must not fail,
The wand taker also stands ready,
To haul in the wand:
And lets it lower to its place,
In the hold without deceit,
The cook must come along,
To properly cradle the wand there,
Ready and prepared.

7 The nearest foreman also stands prepared,
Reaches hands out of sleeves,
And shows also diligence and zeal,
To haul lines and nets ashore;
To loosen off from the rope see:
The ox-handler at this,
Keeps or with vigor,
There must also be a rope shooter:
Who shoots the rope.

8 So goes our work in swing,
And all our affairs,
If we then enjoy a good catch,
We go to the caking:
The layers prepare their vats,
To lay in herring,
Wand in-taking but
Gives cakers herring, I will tell you,
To cure there.

9 When our ship is full and sweet then,
Well laden with herring,
And according to our wish the wind runs good,
We do not scorn the convoy:
By God’s grace and the convoy’s hand,
We then sail quickly
Back to Holland,
With this blessing abundant,
Very abundant.

10 When we then come ashore,
In the safe stream,
Each one offers us his hand,
And welcomes us,
We go ashore with good accord,
The merchants then come,
To us aboard,
The herring is taken from us,
That is proper.

11 The main thing then must be done
Is that we clothe ourselves,
That through our guilty duty
We offer God thanksgivings:
And prepare our lives:
To always praise God,
With diligence,
And show thanks for our journey,
In eternity.

label: work_sea
translation:
In your Name well,
O Lord! we go here to fish
Grant that we in abundance,
Enjoy a rich catch:
At your Word, Lord of Lords,
Thus we cast our nets,
O God! may it increase,
With abundant herring.
2 We go here to shoot,
And set it before God's Eyes,
O merciful God!
Would do so by your power:
Send us herring fish,
Very many in great number,
So that for that purpose,
We may all praise you.
3 Let us in this Night
Have no storm or fierce winds,
Sent by you,
To devour us together:
But would you keep us safe:
From bad weather or wind,
From storms or waves,
O Lord God beloved.
4 Lord steer through your hand
For us, at all hours,
Much herring in our nets,
Moves the creatures;
That they without restraint,
And without resistance,
May swim into our nets,
By the guidance of your hand.
5 Grant that early tomorrow
We catch a good catch,
And also a good enough,
Fulfilling our desire:
Herring is all our wishes,
Herring is our desire,
Herring delights many people,
Delight us also, O Lord.
6 Grant that my heart,
May always fear you, Lord,
And that we without pretense;
May be grateful to you,
When it is obtained by us,
What our heart longs for,
For it always depends
On the Lord's blessing the catch.

label: work_sea
translation:
The Buys man who now has caught a good catch  
Lifts up his heart to God, and praises Him with songs,  
And praises Him very joyfully, with sweet sound and resonance,  
The voice and the tune of the Hundredth Psalm.  

Believe, Thanks oh Lord! highly praised,  
May your name be exalted among us on high  
Because of your mercy,  
Which you, Lord, come to show us today,  
And now give us reason to praise,  
And to glorify in eternity.  
2 That you have given us a good catch;  
In this night, which we have passed,  
And blessed us, Lord, in abundance:  
That you have blessed our boat with herring,  
And with your favor and goodness have rained down,  
For which man must be grateful to you:  
3 The sincere prayer, Lord, raised by us,  
That is, that is, driven to your ear,  
And heard by you, merciful God;  
Lord, do not disdain our prayer,  
Therefore we must earnestly desire,  
That praise and thanks now overflow from us.  
4 And Lord, do not let any robbers or enemies,  
Or any evil even approach us in the least,  
But let us always continue forward and onward:  
In you alone, oh Lord, is our trust,  
What you give us, Lord, let us retain,  
That we bring it home where it belongs.  
5 Let such a catch continue with us,  
And may our ship increase with your favor,  
That it be blessed, whether one hauls or shoots:  
And that the boat, by your word, is launched,  
A good catch then enjoyed by us,  
For without You, there is no fishing.  
6 So grant us, as we long for it so,  
And if our ship (with God) is full of catch,  
Incline our weak flesh then to thanksgiving,  
And bring us after the long-lasting toil,  
At last into a safe harbor,  
And thus give us a preserved journey.  
How one hauls or shoots,  
If God offers no help,  
There is no catch.

label: work_sea
translation:
Listen all of you people,
Who dwell at Maas-Sluys,
What I will briefly explain to you,
That has happened in our time:
And occurs in our days,
Alas, it is truly to be lamented.

2 In the year they have recorded
Sixteen hundred, wild understood,
And fifty-one down from there,
Many were sadly seen departing:
That they lost their goods and blood,
Alas, had perished at sea.

3 In the fifth month of the aforementioned year,
It is true, hear my message,
A heavy storm-wind arose,
That everyone well saw happening,
But no one could tell,
Of the seafaring shipmates.

4 Six ships, hear my declaration,
Have been lost in this storm,
And entirely swallowed by the waves,
And completely shattered in the sea,
The people also here besides,
All together perished in the sea.

5 Well sixty or seventy souls,
Remained on these ships there,
The sea destroyed everything,
And none escaped from it;
And if any fled or survived,
Alas, everyone may well fear.

6 Everyone is saddened by this,
And very distressed in their mind,
Indeed it could not be otherwise,
For the reason makes everyone mourn:
And truly has double cause,
To voice their complaints with bitterness.

7 Each one complains with sorrowful thoughts,
As bitterly as he can,
Who will now earn a living for us:
Now that we live without a man?
For whom can this not seem a wonder?
Because everyone misses their own.

8 Commend your husband into God's hands,
You widows who cry sadly,
He will hear your tears many times,
As he has said:
He is and remains your faithful Father,
Rejoice here in all gatherings.

9 Just as He takes care,
Yes, even for the very smallest cattle,
So He will also care for you who are defeated,
And still much more besides:
He will certainly fulfill His word,
Because He cares for the women.

10 Think that the Lord raised your husband up,
Loved him much more than you,
For now in a better life,
He has been taken, be glad for this:
Commend your cause to God and the Lord,
He will make it well for the best.

11 Think how you pledged with each other,
And gathered yourself in marriage,
Yet you had still to part once,
And knew it would happen:
Take comfort rightly with this,
And live henceforth in peace and quiet.

label: work_sea
translation:
Maas-sluis I must sing,  
With a joyful spirit,  
And bring forth the Fishermen's Praise,  
Of which our Place has the most,  
Here enjoys so much welfare,  
For as a reward // This abbey,  
Shall now be declared by me.  

2 O you Neptune's earth,  
Praised Fisherman,  
I do not praise you undervalued,  
Can fully honor you:  
For this I am unqualified,  
For my wit // Almost drowned,  
If I were to mention your Name.  

3 The Sea he is going to build,  
With the winged Wood,  
He puts his trust in God,  
And hopes for many a fault,  
And then thus this Man sails,  
On the Brackish Sea // to and fro,  
According to his will and wish.  

4 Though he is struck high and low,  
With this ship of his,  
Beaten by Rudder and Weight,  
As God puts him before the Rock:  
See then all is well,  
In such sorrow // And it does not harm him,  
The waves angry and fierce.  

5 When Storm and Waves strike him,  
So thinks the Fisherman's child,  
If God wants to protect me,  
I do not fear bad Weather nor Wind,  
Then it harms him the least,  
He thinks God's hand // Can, as on Land,  
Help us out of sorrow.  

6 Though I must often move,  
In this stormy Sea,  
I must fulfill my calling,  
The Blessing of the Lord:  
I must always expect:  
For as they say // After bitterness,  
God surely gives the sweet.  

7 So although you must suffer much,  
Praised Fisherman,  
You will still endure,  
When you once arrive,  
In the Harbor of Maas-Sluis,  
They say again // Then welcome to you,  
When you come home.  

8 You bring us here on Land,  
Also pleasant Fish,  
Many and various kinds,  
To crown our Table:  
Is it not a good thing?  
Also fine Herring // That outshines all,  
To mankind's great delight.  

9 But you Praised Fisherman,  
How often were you scoffed at,  
Come tell what the reason is?  
Why are you not crowned?  
From the Trade of Maas-sluis  
Why are you despised,  
If it goes wrong for you,  
With envy and scorn.  

10 Notice how he feeds many People,  
From every side,  
So that their desire for Wealth,  
In the least has no need or penalty:  
Therefore give him praise and honor,  
Wish him prosperity // That whatever he does,  
Receives Blessing from the Lord.  

11 Excuse me, good Fishermen,  
With this I am done,  
I wish that your Ship prospers,  
Fully and sweetly well laden,  
May it arrive to your joy,  
And that you then // With everyone,  
May gratefully give thanks to God.

label: work_sea
translation:
Maas-Sluys People  
All of you,  
I must wish you,  
Here a New Year,  
That you, That you,  
May live in prosperity,  
And that everyone,  
Rejoices in their profession.  

2 Whether Political,  
Gentlemen of Maas-Sluys,  
Or Ecclesiastical,  
Servants of God's House,  
I wish, I wish,  
Them much happiness together,  
Every person,  
A blessed New Year.  

3 I wish the Lords  
All the mayors,  
Who govern here,  
In the Maas-Sluys valley.  
That they with pleasure,  
Govern beforehand and afterward,  
With peace and quiet,  
In the blessed New Year.  

4 The magistrates together,  
You who administer justice,  
I wish that you together,  
May rise to Heaven:  
That peace and quiet,  
May shine clearly with you,  
I also wish you,  
A blessed New Year.  

5 The very honorable,  
Gentlemen over me,  
The commissioners,  
Of the fishermen,  
I wish you prosperity,  
And complete peacefulness,  
That may meet you,  
A blessed New Year.  

6 You all citizens,  
I wish that God the Lord,  
May grant blessing upon you,  
More and more!  
That every person,  
Carries out their trade and business???  
According to their wish,  
In the blessed New Year.  

7 Former fishermen,  
And very widely renowned,  
Whose fate must also be,  
Declared by me:  
God grants that you,  
May all sail,  
Safely and free,  
In the blessed New Year.  

8 Permission for the last,  
It is time to part,  
Keep it for my best,  
I will not go far here:  
Whoever you may be,  
This is my wish together,  
Become new in virtue,  
In the blessed New Year.

label: work_sea
translation:
All that man does,  
Here in this life,  
If God gives His blessing well,  
To whom it comes,  
On whom it falls like a fruitful rain,  
How highly he is regarded,  
In the human race,  
Who keeps that Word in mind,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

2 Even if the carter sails,  
Or goes to travel,  
If God’s blessing sails  
In his burdens,  
Surely then the work is altogether successful,  
For then one clearly sees,  
That it is manifest,  
That the Word is true,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

3 Even if the fisherman goes,  
To the sea to fish,  
If he then misses God’s blessing there,  
What disaster would befall him!  
Then one rightly sees,  
How that Word is above all rights,  
As it was said to us,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

4 Whether Neptune’s roar,  
And hollow waves,  
Bring you into the Meuse,  
If the burden,  
Of God’s great favor does not come against it,  
It was all nothing,  
But if it happens so,  
Then one truly sees,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

5 However you cast your rod,  
Or come to haul,  
Nevertheless it is not all,  
If the streets,  
Of God’s blessing are not inclined to you,  
As the sun shines on you,  
And falls into your nets,  
It may rightly be told,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

6 The waves, high and great,  
Overcome you well,  
If in such distress,  
The Lord’s blessing,  
Did not give support or let you be distressed in trouble,  
Certainly your standing,  
Would be without God’s hand,  
Not altogether, for  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

7 The fish that you bring us,  
At Sluys by land,  
Gives us God’s blessing,  
For from His hand,  
He gives us abundance, for all ways,  
Thus one sees that present,  
Our God’s blessing sends,  
For in that it was made known,  
It depends on God’s blessing.  

8 This is the nail, then,  
On which all depends,  
Happy is the man,  
Who truly desires:  
And has received this blessing from God,  
Which is brought forth here,  
That is with sweet accord,  
Sung upon the Word,

label: work_sea
translation:
I must explain my sorrow  
What recently has happened to me,  
I sailed off from Maas-Sluys,  
With a Hooker heard my purpose,  
And all this to catch plaice,  
But the Flemings frightened us.  

2 With a Stierman heard my companions  
Pieter Komen was his name,  
As we understood each other,  
They boldly came at us,  
But we did not yield to them,  
As was clearly shown at the time.  

3 Our Stierman without fear,  
He said, "Oh guests, you must know,  
I will risk my life,  
Even if it costs me body and blood,"  
And no one made him yield,  
As was also clearly shown.  

4 They came eagerly to fight,  
Like wolves around the prey,  
Who watch over a little sheep,  
And made us deaf to shooting,  
So we almost without hesitation,  
Noticed their arrival immediately.  

5 Then we shouted without hesitation,  
Captain, where is your ship?  
He named Bottenburg,  
That struck us hard,  
But later as we understood,  
They were sent from Ostend.  

6 Then we shouted without hesitation,  
Why do you shoot so foolishly,  
Are you from Ostend,  
Then we are from the Som,  
We sailed from Maas-Sluys,  
So we are all friends.  

7 They did not want to yield easily,  
For they still shot boldly,  
And said wildly, "Strike your mainsail,  
Let your sails fall on the wood,  
Or we will go at you without mercy,  
And shoot you until you sink."  

8 A musket bullet without failing,  
Brought our Stierman to death,  
That made us all anxious,  
For we were in deep mourning,  
As he lay wounded in his blood,  
Oh Lord, protect his soul with swords.  

9 When the Stierman was shot dead,  
They came with the boat aboard,  
But it did not please them,  
Everyone saw him slaughtered in his blood,  
A stone hart would have mercy,  
But they showed no compassion.  

10 The one who composed this sad song,  
Was a fisherman from Maas-Sluys,  
He did not take it lightly in his heart,  
A cross fell on his heart,  
That he saw his Stierman before his eyes,  
Dead, bent down on his knees.  

11 With this I hope to end this song,  
And still take it from me with thanks,  
For my memory is very small,  
And I falter in my speech,  
With this I will leave off writing,  
God strengthen us, poor weak vessels.

label: work_sea
translation:
Listen, everyone,  
To the story I will tell you,  
Those who go sailing by the sea,  
Listen, people in great numbers,  
And country folk here on land,  
Listen along to this little song.  

2 I will tell it to you,  
In praise of the fishermen there,  
And how their God guides them,  
When they set sail indeed,  
And how quickly at sea,  
And how their God protects them.  

3 Just like a herder too:  
Who tends his sheep out in the field,  
So God peacefully guides  
The fishermen well-counted,  
When they sail on the sea,  
With their ships so ready.  

4 God does indeed test us,  
With storms and thunder,  
He knows what we need,  
When we are on the lake,  
When the sea becomes unruly,  
Then we do not know what to do.  

5 Then we also lay down;  
We understand our anchor well,  
When the waves with reason  
Almost wreck our ship,  
And then we see death,  
Steadily large before our eyes.  

6 Then we cry out very strongly,  
And with such weeping too,  
Our Almighty Lord protects us,  
That we do not sink in the lake,  
That we do not drown,  
In our sins with sorrow.  

7 We must all endure that,  
Whatever comes against us,  
God can deliver us well,  
From the storm henceforth,  
Land people consider this,  
When you go to sleep very gently.  

8 Therefore, do not despise,  
O fishermen, be very careful,  
By day or by night,  
Death is not far from them,  
You, land people, understand this well,  
When their ship nearly goes under.  

9 And you lie in your rest,  
With your children also,  
And whatever else you may desire,  
You get that here on land,  
God can find you too well,  
And death also very quickly.  

10 Therefore, do not boast,  
Of your life here so lightly,  
He can come quickly:  
With his sails also very large,  
Therefore do not scorn the seaman,  
He brings fish of every kind.  

11 What else must he suffer?  
Before his journey is over,  
Before God makes him happy,  
And brings him ashore from now on,  
He is silent about the waves,  
And then he gets his wish there.  

12 Do not despise the land people either,  
For that is not well done,  
Because God has peacefully  
Assigned each his work,  
For the one is the hand,  
The other the foot of the land.  

13 Now farewell for the last time,  
And take this song with thanks,  
And consider it my best,  
Even if it is not very long,  
I wish all people  
Good pleasure and blessing in peace.

label: work_sea
translation:
Renowned united Batavian lineage,  
Firmly fastened with arrows:  
You have, by Divine power,  
And manly fought,  
The heroes of our Fatherland,  
Who are witnesses here,  
You who by fire, sword, and flame,  
Have made two crowns bow,  
They also grant us peace,  
By God's grace,  
As it is now decreed,  
Within the City of Breda.  

Though Mars showed himself fierce and cruel,  
To oppress us down,  
Vulcan, who forges the iron,  
Now ceases his work again,  
The gods ride together,  
Each seeming embraced with joy,  
Rejoice Holland’s folk, rejoice all:  
And offer your songs of praise,  
That may extend to God’s honor,  
And the heroes piously,  
So that no strife may rise again,  
Or never any war come.  

Oh! see the Sister agree,  
Embracing peace sweetly:  
But see what love produces,  
Notice what peace does,  
It throws the sword from your hand,  
And seizes again green olives,  
The shield is loosened and set aside,  
From the works of war,  
Hold the arrows of unity firmly in a knot,  
Strive for peace,  
Through loyal love, faith, and hope,  
The sword never again leaves the sheath.  

Neptune, who had no rest or honor,  
For swimming in human blood,  
Mars brought Neptune into a bloodbath,  
Many people on her flood,  
Who came to their death, alas;  
Through the metal blades,  
Though Neptune lamented bitterly,  
Never did she bring forth,  
Here the mother, son, or friend is missing,  
The other her husband,  
Or brother-in-law, uncle, or nephew, or servant,  
No healing can occur.  

Oh see the bourgeois man with desire,  
He hoists and builds,  
Oh golden freedom, you come again,  
Much worthier than gold,  
Sailors who on merchant ships  
Used to sail restlessly,  
Now settle the unrest on one side,  
Plowing Neptune’s shores,  
No one robs ships of our strand,  
Oh see now freely,  
Neptune’s fleet preserved,  
And brings us goods.  

The craftsman who is delighted,  
For he is delivered,  
For see, it has well pleased him,  
How he was jostled and beaten  
On Neptune, and that he may  
Again gain his bread on land,  
Be welcome, welcome day of peace,  
I with my heart and thoughts,  
Have long desired you indeed,  
Our tears stilled,  
Of widows and small orphans,  
Peace shows your kindness mild.  

Praise be to God for your goodness,  
She who bends hearts,  
And brings us again into a free state,  
Therefore altogether rejoice,  
Praise the admirals in general,  
Praise faithful captains,  
Praise those who fight for our freedom,  
And diminish our enemy,  
Show gratitude and be glad,  
Praise God on foot,  
That we may live always in peace:  
For experience creates taste.

label: work_sea
translation:
Listen, maidens, to my song,  
After my lament,  
A sad song must I sing to you,  
And go sorrowfully,  
For I have cause to complain  
In this great distress:  
For my love has been struck down  
By the great waves.  

An honorable and good fisherman  
Often asked,  
And begged me very humbly  
With many sweet words,  
To always speak with me  
In honor and virtue,  
Which I allowed him:  
For it was my joy.  

Then it went so far,  
He offered me his faith;  
I willingly accepted  
To become his wife:  
He showed me love beyond measure,  
This young man, my sweetheart,  
And swore not to leave me  
Until death separates us.  

When he last set out,  
And left me,  
To fish on the sea waves,  
We had many  
Sweet words between us:  
About our love,  
But now it was time to part,  
For he had to go sailing.  

Farewell now, my beloved,  
He often said,  
May God spare you in life  
With good health:  
He kissed my red cheek,  
Out of peculiar love,  
But my heart was so fearful  
When he left me.  

Sad days and nights  
Did I spend all the while,  
Waiting for my bridegroom  
From the sea with diligence:  
But I have soon learned  
This sad news,  
That my love would not return,  
Oh! it struck me so hard.  

Then I went home  
And wept bitterly:  
For my heart was so fearful  
In my tender body;  
See, I had reason to mourn,  
For my faithful love:  
For it could not happen to me  
To become his wife.  

Oh my love, my chosen  
Certain part,  
You do not now hear me,  
How much I weep  
Because your body is devoured  
By cruel fishes;  
And I sit here, soaked with cries,  
With many hot tears.  

Farewell now, my beloved  
Bridegroom, certainly,  
Farewell until the other life,  
In this heavenly realm,  
Farewell now, my chosen,  
Faithful dear love,  
The sea has drowned you,  
And I remain in mourning.  

Farewell all maidens,  
Honorable playmates,  
The Lord will spare you in life,  
With your love indeed,  
I wish that what happened to me  
May not happen to you,  
For he who must secretly mourn  
Lives in heavy sorrow.

label: work_sea
translation:
If all my Companions come here safely,  
Let us fall to the Lord on our knees,  
And pray for His goodness,  
That He may guide us on this Journey,  
And be our Convoy with His Angels,  
Then our Journey will be prosperous.  

2 O Lord! You have ordained,  
That Man is humbled on Earth,  
So that he would earn fine  
His Bread by the sweat of his face,  
By Land or Sea, on the Ocean,  
As it pleases You, O Lord.  

3 Thus You, O Lord, kindhearted,  
Have called us here to Fish,  
And we are again prepared,  
To Fish with cheerfulness,  
For we are as You wished, Lord,  
Come here again to the Sea.  

4 We also know that it will not help us,  
Our labor, toil, and sorrow,  
Unless You, O kind Lord,  
Bless the work of our hands here!  
Then our toil and labor  
Are not a vain idleness.  

5 Therefore we pray, send Your Blessing,  
And be with us through Your Spirit,  
And keep us from all need,  
From Storms and great Waves,  
So that we do not, O good Father,  
Drown in the Sea-flood.  

6 Protect us from Sea-robbers, Lord,  
Or any other Enemies,  
So that no one does us harm,  
To our Ship, Body, or Goods,  
So that we complete this Journey swiftly,  
In peaceful safety.  

7 We pray You free us indeed,  
From quarrels and discord at all times,  
But would join us together,  
With love and pleasant peace,  
Here among our fine Companions,  
Who are with us in this Ship.  

8 Also make us all understand,  
Your Law and Word pure and clear,  
So that we also live rightly,  
As the Apostles holy,  
And walk our Path as is required,  
Always at Sea by Your Word.  

9 Protect our Household, Lord,  
Our Parents, Wives, and dear Children  
So that we may see their faces,  
With joy and rejoice together,  
And give thanks for Your goodness,  
In Your Temple full of Holiness.  

10 Whoever thus accepts their Journey,  
Such a Seaman is truly worthy of praise,  
And God, who is very kindhearted,  
Will bless the work of his hands here,  
And keep him from all need  
And dangers, even from death itself.  

11 Farewell now, skilled Companions,  
Who sail the Sea all together,  
For which God has called you,  
I wish that He grants you His Blessing,  
Here and hereafter keep the Journey,  
In Heaven after this voyage.

label: work_sea
translation:
Here come the East Indies voyagers well,  
Who today must part;  
And sail through the wild Flood,  
God, I hope, will guide you;  
Now may He also grant you all,  
Health, prosperity, blessings:  
Freed from storm or danger,  
And shipwreck on all sides.  
God wills all four ships,  
And all the people spared,  
The captains and officers,  
And all who sail with them;  
The first ship, and hear soon,  
The admiral with praise,  
That is the Rebecka Iacoba,  
And then comes Keukenhof.  
The ship Amerongen is heard of soon,  
And Ruyteveld wild to hear,  
These are four well-endowed ships,  
All born for the sea;  
They are all prepared today,  
And are expected from you,  
At Pampus and in Texel there,  
Go well then with care.  
Now all together but on board,  
Freed from worry and fear,  
And do as is fitting for you:
