And think it must be so,  
Take leave of all your friends,  
Everyone wishes you health and peace,  
And go with joy and glad cheer,  
From the city of Amsterdam.  
Sailors who love the girls,  
Do you still have money or pay:  
They call you their best friend,  
But otherwise it is piracy:  
Leave that beautiful city pleasant,  
The girls, beautiful women,  
And set your course for the Indies land,  
And think of no troubles.  
Go on board, wild little sailor,  
And keep your reins tight;  
For when your purse is emptied,  
You might as well scratch your head:  
Go together on board like a hero,  
But do not imagine too much,  
For it is with you as it is with me,  
A poor purse inside.  
Greet also your hospitable ones all,  
Plantations, gardens, and inns;  
And part with joy from the shore,  
On board live with pleasure:  
And find the innkeeper everywhere at home,  
Remember this well,  
So that all your toil and cross  
Disappears without trouble.  
God give you blessings at all times,  
He wills to open the way for you,  
That you with joy completely delighted  
Shall soon come to the Indians  
With your little ships soon,  
And then God willing will give you  
His blessing always early and spare,  
And many more years of life.

label: work_sea
translation:
Oh! What has happened to me,
On my last journey that I made,
Every man should shudder at it,
If I shared the story with him:
Scarcely had we sailed,
Three days out at sea,
When a storm on the waves
Broke the mainmast in two,
The ship that traveled, the ship that sank,
Eventually took such a blow,
That it ran aground on a rock
And broke into a thousand pieces.

Then it was, men, gather your lice,
Swimming was the very best,
I too had to move like a duck,
Disturbed in its nest;
But when I swam through the water,
Ah! I will never forget that,
Almost a shark with its snout,
Had bitten me in the arm:
I swam toward the whale
And thus escaped the danger,
Soon I reached the shore
And from there went on foot.

What shall I do now,
Wet and cold in this state,
Stomach and purse are empty inside:
Ah! How do I get off the street:
If the innkeeper would now credit me
And only serve drinks on credit,
Then I would be free of worries
And I would think no sorrow:
No hardship for this one,
I used up the money there;
I bet he won’t be so angry,
That he won’t give me credit.
I do not give up yet,
Hey courage, that’s the way:
Softly! The innkeeper may hear it,
He surely sang the Baboquivari Song:
"If you want to leave here,
Or I will cover you on your pension,
With a broomstick,
That you do not look like any man;
There is the wife, she is much gentler,
She has blood in her nose,
I bet she calls me right away behind,
Hey there she already gives the signal."

Well friend, what do you want,
Well friend, what do you want,
Make me tell it quickly,
For the people inside await me there,
I haven’t long on my journey,
Why do you look so sad and bad,
Come tell me.

Well how now Miss Griet,
Well how now Miss Griet,
Have you forgotten me so,
Look well at me, don’t you know me,
I am your old client, your Piet,
Who spent and gave much money here,
Don’t you remember?

Well Piet, I never saw you,
Well Piet, I never saw you,
So poor and so needy,
I swear you have wasted your money and goods,
Spoiled, neglected as you do more,
Therefore, just leave here nicely,
You naked blood.

Well Griet, what talk is that,
Well Griet, what talk is that,
Is it serious or madness,
I say I don’t want to leave here,
Before I speak to your husband or brother,
Go pour immediately a jug of old beer,
And call her here.

Well rascal, now I do not want,
Well rascal, now I do not want,
To call my husband or brother,
You
will not enter my house,
I notice you have no money nor coin,
Therefore, make no noise here,
Away naked lice.

Do not be so hasty,
Do not be so hasty,
I will tell you my journey,
But it went very badly
And left me standing here on the street:
But who comes there in rich attire?
Ha! It is my mate.

Let us be joyful,
With the girls and the fresh wine;
Hey! awake at the glass,
Grab lively quickly,
Let the wine go down,
Come pour once more
And let us toast with cheerful spirits.

label: work_sea
translation:
What great pleasure I enjoy once again,  
Now that I may find my rest  
On this Coast as well:  
I find—though I wander from shore to shore,  
No place more beautiful according to my Reason,  
Than your O Glorious Fatherland,  
Which with heart and mind,  
I love the most.

2 The life at sea indeed bears no sorrow!  
But I advise a Man,  
Who can otherwise,  
Not to live unnecessarily,  
One is his own master on land;  
But not at sea, where time and again  
The sailor is trained like leather,  
Even in the worst distress!  
On the shore of death.

3 Shall I then not be delighted now,  
Thanks be to fate:  
No reef or sandbank  
Can hinder my joy,  
I am now thinking of sails, March, still a walk,  
I take pride in foresail or yard intelligence,  
There will be plenty of time when I must  
Leave the land again due to lack of bread,  
Forced by necessity.

4 Here no Ship’s Officer shall,  
In sweating boldness,  
With curse upon curse,  
Drive me here upwards,  
I will not be struck by any ruined fool,  
Who runs wildly on the deck,  
And is beaten for my mouth;  
I am as good here as he,  
And equally frank and free.

5 I then go to my friends,  
To be with them over wine,  
Delighted and well tired,  
A welcoming drink can do no harm,  
And is never again despised,  
While one merrily beats inside,  
Adding joy to happiness,  
After hardship and strain.

label: work_sea
translation:
1 What strange rumor does one not hear!
In these last days;
Where many are concerned!
It was mutiny violence,
That many have alarmed!
Hear what this song tells, &c.

2 Where does Satan not lead?
Man by his cunning,
So that he, by transgressing
Against law and duty;
Performs the devil’s evil,
Hear what I have composed, &c.

3 The ship Nyenburg manned with people,
Sailed from Hoorn,
Towards the land of Java;
With goods
And money, well prepared;
Hear what evil it all does, &c.

4 Hardened and harder than stone,
So the mutineers
Turned against the ship.
And stubbornly
Committed: Murder and evil!
Like a wicked seed, &c.

5 Looted greatly for gold and goods;
Thus they set out to flee
Seawards with a boat:
Arrived at Cayenne,
Where they were captured,
For their evil deeds, &c.

6 They were taken to Lisbon,
Bound in chains and fetters,
Then all tragedy began:
But those once revealed,
Were punished after a year,
So reports the sorrowful song, &c.

7 Four perished by the noose,
And hang as spectacle,
Before Ravens who eat them,
Three more in the same state,
For their robbery and murder,
Chastised as it was heard, &c.

8 Ten were seen at Helder’s place,
Tied tightly with hemp ropes,
Thus evil swells at sea:
That mutiny did,
And it serves as a mirror too,
Of sailors at sea, &c.

9 Before the scaffold was fixed,
One saw sorrowful eyes!
Disturbed within:
They called upon the Savior,
With prayers and tears,
Forgive us our sins, &c.

10 Who knows how the penitent,
Showed their wickedness,
Cried: O Preacher
Pray for us at the throne,
That God may yet forgive us,
By the blood of His Son, &c.

11 When everyone saw that punishment,
How deeply moved they were,
At Kijkduin it was severe!
Each prayed in their heart!
Forgive this evil brood,
And grant them eternal good, &c.

12 Praise the sovereign justice,
Of the sea council’s supreme heads,
The evil must by God’s word be said
Receive punishment according to guilt,
Cease from evil deeds,
Then God will go with you, &c.

label: work_sea
translation:
My darling, I hear the drum beating,  
On board, on board, so every man calls,  
One more voyage, my love, I want to complete,  
Before I can fulfill my desire with you.

My dearest sweetheart, I fear the voyage:  
I tremble already for the wild sea,  
For the violence of those restless waves,  
For rocks or shore, yes many a strange reef.

God, who is the helmsman of all ships,  
Calms the angry waves according to His will,  
He will guide our ship and many brave souls  
Safely to shore as well.

Safe ashore, my love, that is so long  
Before this sorrowful journey is brought to an end;  
And suddenly you are captured by the Turk,  
And sold as a slave in his land.

The Turkish crescent cannot oppress us,  
We are equipped with a well-defended ship,  
And also many metal pieces  
To chase the enemy at close quarters.

My love, will you go to war,  
War is a constant flood,  
Whom will you serve, my love, say what lords  
Will you risk your body and blood for?

Our Most Mighty Lords the States,  
For whom many a brave war hero,  
For the fatherland, I will give my life,  
And bravely fight on Neptune’s field.

It is true, my love, war is brave,  
Each one ought to stand loyally for it:  
But alas, the fate of war is so bloody,  
That everyone should despise the same.

We usually entertain ourselves on Sundays,  
Here outside in the summer greenery;  
You must now suspend that a little, my love,  
Until we do so again in health.

Farewell, my sweetheart, my beloved,  
Farewell my love, I go, it is my time:  
I hope I will find you again,  
Despite all the dangers that it will bring.

label: work_sea
translation:
In Vlissingen lies a yacht prepared,  
To sail to the East Indies;  
Upon it rests a Cupid,  
And plays on his gilded strings.  
O Cupid, little Venus’s delight;  
How your gilded strings sound,  
They are curled in her lap,  
How pleasant it is to sail there.  
The first maiden who then inquires,  
I think I saw the dunes shining:  
It was the island of the woman,  
With joy my young heart sank.  
What kind of island could it be;  
What kind of island could it be;  
It was an island of the woman  
That was praised by lovers.  
The moon shone, the sun disappeared;  
It is the stars that light us.  
The mast in pieces, the spar in two,  
And yet we would not yield.  
At the Cape, the sound was heard and understood,  
Where the girls daily mingle:  
Like the blue rooster in the house,  
Where we daily converse.  
When we then arrive in the Indies land,  
How cheerfully we will behave there:  
In the morning going freely outside,  
Against the black skirmishes.  
When we then return to the homeland,  
How cheerfully we will then revel,  
In the morning with brandy,  
And daily the good beers.  
Farewell, you girls of Utrecht all,  
You will well remember this little song,  
But we are not parting from here yet,  
We want to drink a glass once more.

label: work_sea
translation:
Oh day! Oh long-awaited day,  
that many a person may wish for,  
Our little ships are coming in,  
It’s for Hellevoet or Rotterdam:  
that my beloved came home;  
I parted with spinning.  
I kicked the spinning wheel aside,  
I’d rather set it on a light fire;  
I kicked it with my feet:  
I want to go look at the boat:  
If there my beloved is coming out:  
How lovingly I will greet him.  
Who do I hear knocking at my door,  
Are you the beloved servant,  
Welcome, you must be so.  
How lovingly was the first letter,  
that I wrote to you, my love,  
Have you read it well?  
Oh yes! my precious pledge?  
The sweet writing from your hand,  
that was for my amusement,  
But when I received the last letter;  
From you, my sweet love,  
I first understood the matters.  
Oh dear, what do you have in mind,  
Do you want to go out to sea again,  
Or do you want to row with me,  
So pull in your sails quickly?  
Do as you please:  
then you don’t have to sail.  
A woman is no longer a virgin,  
She is no master of her honor,  
She may not love a young man:  
But when the yes word is said,  
Then the knot is tied,  
That cannot be undone.

label: work_sea
translation:
Come to me, come to me, come to me, On the Bear,
You are my beloved,
I will be your friend,
If you want to go sailing,
The ship lies at anchor,
Young man, do you want to come with me;
You shall be at peace,
If you want to go to sea,
At Neptune's place,
You will quickly find,
Now that fortune has changed.

2. Do not fear, do not fear, Do not fear any fierce storms,
You are all my pleasure,
Without hesitation again,
I will be at your side,
You shall be honored,
And respected,
Much more than you desire,
For the money and goods;
Follow quickly:
In favor and in abundance.

3. Come to me, come to me, Come to me in the fields;
I am the God of Love;
In you I have joy,
Young hero of heroes,
I have tirelessly pursued
For six years,
After a pure maiden,
Who bears you no hatred,
With a pure love,
Sets your heart and mind
On the beautiful goddess.

4. Rosetta, Rosetta, Rosetta's locks hang their pleasant fragrance,
And lovely color,
Of her noble cheeks,
Her snow-white hand,
Eye like a diamond,
Her alabaster teeth;
She is the most beautiful of our land;
And her limbs are well-shaped,
Not too coarse or too small.

5. What shall I do; what shall I do; what shall I begin,
Sailing is all my desire;
Yet I love,
Your beautiful goddess,
Must I then forever
Take my farewell from you,
Heaven, for my heart bursts with regret,
Take my heart then with you,
The ship lies at anchor,
My love is going to sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
Friends, listen once to my tale,  
How we with three ships,  
Have all gone to Surat:  
With God, to sail the sea?  
On the fourteenth of August,  
We sailed from Batavia  
Into the Sunda Strait, eager to know.  
Our admiral's ship, Lands-Kroon,  
Went ahead, willing to be heard,  
A man very skilled in deeds  
As if born for the sea,  
His name is Godtfried Wargyn:  
He is in his command  
Prepared for whatever may come.  
The second is named Aschat,  
The young captain by years;  
Yet very capable in his affairs,  
He can still keep pace.  
Willem Salvester is his name,  
He sailed along under sail  
On all the journeys to Surat.  
The third is the ship Velthoen,  
That ship that sails well,  
That went with us on the voyage  
And all without sails,  
The captain, a capable man,  
Hendrik Mulder is his name,  
So we set out sailing.  
We sailed from the mainland,  
Out of the strait with joy,  
All to Surat’s golden shore:  
Where God guided us,  
With good weather and good wind,  
As desires a seaman’s child,  
Then we rejoice.  
On the 19th of November, I understand,  
Let us praise the Lord,  
We came there to anchor;  
Thank God above,  
In twelve days more or less,  
Three months from the start,  
We arrived there.  
Farewell lovers of the sea:  
Who always with longing,  
Always seek a good anchorage,  
If you start it with God;  
Then you certainly and surely  
Will arrive where your desire is,  
I make an end here.

label: work_sea
translation:
How fortunate is the landman indeed,  
Where the seaman is a slave to,  
Storm, thunder, harsh nights!  
A seaman must always expect,  
Where the landman lies still and calm,  
With peace he kisses his beloved.  

2. Steep cliffs, dryness, and sand,  
Hidden lowlands behind land,  
Steep cliffs, heavy fires,  
Feared by many a shore,  
All misfortunes lie in wait,  
Fortunate is the landman in the highest rank.  

3. More breaking than a stay or a sheet,  
Then the seaman is in great distress!  
Heaven, what misfortune,  
Spars and masts break;  
Then the little ship goes to the bottom,  
Then one sees its last moment.  

4. Well landman, however bold,  
Where the seaman heads for land,  
Could you landman truly exist,  
If it did not come to wreck,  
The seaman brought no treasures,  
From East and West and North Sea.  

5. Gold, pearls, diamonds,  
All come from the Indies land.  
Fine silks and cotton,  
To maintain his fashion,  
The country's prosperity as a support,  
The laborer himself to benefit.  

6. Well landman, and try no more!  
The seaman alone gives the honor.  
Gold, silver, precious goods,  
A seaman must sail for them:  
As long as the seaman fares well,  
The more you landmen will prosper.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. The East India cows are inside:  
Together with the year,  
Let us begin late,  
To wish each other,  
Welcome my beloved,  
Welcome all darlings,  
I have succeeded in finding you,  
Here on the Amstel shore.

2. The Amsterdam landlady:  
She is again full of spirit;  
The post ship is inside,  
It is a pleasure for her,  
Even more the prostitutes,  
With their feigned caresses,  
They lurk and stare,  
And wish her share.

3. Now the prostitutes sit,  
With open throats,  
To transport you,  
Through her sweet caress.  
They know East India,  
The name carries it,  
For your money, I love you,  
My friend, be at peace.

4. The East Indian sailors,  
When they are ashore,  
They go courting  
And that everywhere.  
By night and day,  
And early morning,  
One comes asking for her,  
They are in the tavern.

5. Also the officers:  
They live brilliantly:  
They go out swinging,  
With ladies of fashion,  
Brothels and billiards;  
They keep it going:  
They do not let themselves be teased;  
It can get serious.

6. No eating at noon,  
No being at home in the evening,  
One must also know,  
That there is coin and cross,  
As long as it is with her,  
So just pour it in:  
So host, serve us quickly,  
That suits our taste.

7. Then one can find them  
Very late in the evening  
With a wench or beloved,  
One walks on the street,  
One hears sweet darling  
Do you want to go with me  
(Say sweet dear thief,)  
Not too far here.

8. But before the last,  
East Indies voyagers all,  
I advise you for your own good,  
When you are ashore.  
If you want to show something,  
And not live like that,  
Or you will soon come again,  
To another ship’s boat.

label: work_sea
translation:
The blue flag that waves,  
And our ship that leads and sways,  
The wind blowing in our sails,  
And our anchor ahead of the bow,  
Is it not a joy for us.  
Listen up, sailors all,  
What I shall tell you,  
How I recently sailed,  
On an excellent little ship,  
Through the wild, rough waves,  
To Bengal well known.  
And who would not be eager,  
To see the  
Bengali coast,  
The prostitutes beautiful women  
Always in Spanish wine,  
It is a pleasure to behold,  
When we are with the whores.  
The noble Prince's flag,  
Of the country was waving,  
And we fired four or five shots,  
The pilot came aboard,  
And we sailed undaunted,  
Up the river as was proper.  
When we arrived at the little land,  
Listen to what we heard there;  
The whores came to meet us,  
And made such a commotion,  
I will be your servant,  
For I am such a fine whore.  
In the middle of the Rea,  
We dropped our anchor,  
As our anchor was lowered,  
The skipper shouted loudly,  
So sailors all,  
Now climb up.  
Then we went up,  
To furl the sail,  
And immediately we would quarter,  
And go ashore with the boat,  
To enjoy ourselves a bit,  
With the whores abundantly.

label: work_sea
translation:
My dear Mietje, see I languish,  
Eighty in sorrow my time:  
I am going to war,  
And England's proud envy  
To soothe with gunpowder and lead.  

2. How much an Englishman enjoys,  
He does not grant us light in the eyes,  
And wants to skin us alive,  
Because we no longer wish  
To endure the heavy sorrow.  

3. The patience of our state is gone,  
Which for that reason also a war decision  
With wisdom has made  
Now let that people go on robbing and plundering.  
How will it fare with them,  

4. A Batavian is just as good as  
Three of the English breed:  
Let it come to fighting,  
Our fearless lion-hearted courage  
Will then settle the dispute.  

5. Lord Hartsinck, who as Admiral  
Must give our fleet the greatest splendor  
Through his flag,  
Commands boldly an invincible steel,  
That will make London tremble.  

6. Kingsbergen will with great pleasure  
In the Fatherland's rightful cause,  
Whether today or tomorrow,  
Deliver a just and complete revenge,  
Certainly, to us.  

6. The state has even more heroes  
Who tirelessly sacrifice their lives  
For the country,  
Like Piet Hein who gave his life  
To quell the enemy's hatred.  

7. My brave Dedel, the war hero,  
Lives only on the waterfield,  
And braves the wildest disasters,  
His mind is set solely  
To cling to the ship.  

9. And the land still has a larger crowd  
Of heroes, who by the war drum  
Know no retreat;  
But by the enemy's powder and bomb  
The horsemen keep their footsteps.  

10. Farewell Marie, the drum beats,  
Give me the robe of our state;  
You need not at all be shy:  
I hope tomorrow before daybreak  
To come aboard the dear ship.  

11. For our worthy leadership,  
Whose fatherly and wise policy  
All Amsterdammers honor,  
I go willingly and prepared  
To brave with fire and sword.

label: work_sea
translation:
The Dutch People follow the Fleet,  
Follow its brave Officers!  
I fear no gunpowder, I fear no shot,  
Is the language of the Batavians,  
I fear no barking of a Dog;  
Holland's old Lion still calls  
Come on, come on under my paw,  
Faithless Fur Companion.  
  
2. There have been worse times,  
Yes, over a hundred years ago:  
Whoever reads the country's history,  
No longer lets a Brit live in peace;  
Who violated the most sacred Covenant!  
For the whole World’s shame;  
Dogs, dogs, and insults to our Land,  
Away with that Brotherly Bond.  
  
3. To a new remembrance,  
One sees again his old habits,  
It seems he is the offspring of Kajans,  
Yet his scheme will not succeed;  
He sings a very high tone!  
Such songs we can well  
Sing, Sing, on our Fleet;  
But I mean with gunpowder and shot.  
  
4. Thus one day pride shall  
Be forever chained.  
And will visit the Thames again,  
To see what it has to say;  
For our Supreme Admiral,  
Fears no fire, no shot nor steel;  
But will surely show, will show;  
That he is the blood of the Great Father.  
  
5. Farewell then Heroes, keep your word,  
Come proceed on your Keels,  
You still have Trompens on board,  
Ruyters and many brave souls,  
Fear no barking of a Dog.  
Holland’s Old Lion still calls,  
Come on, come on, under my paw,  
Faithful our Ally.

label: work_sea
translation:
I was walking past the court,
A little country captain who spoke to me
Who immediately asked me,
If I had any desire to go sailing,
I let my name be written down,
For gold coins.

2. I put that money into my hat,
Thereupon I got a fresh courage.
I sat there at my ease,
And smoked a pipe of tobacco,
Without paying attention,
I lit a shot.

3. And from there I went on my way,
Right after my business in Halsteeg,
But listen, when I came upstairs,
Hear what I heard there.
My boss began to scold,
Where shall I stay?

4. My boss said, young man, what is this?
Will you go sailing on a warship?
How come you are so ill at ease,
Or have you been with the girls,
You will regret it,
In short time.

5. Farewell my Annaetje, my beautiful flower,
In eighteen months I will return,
And if you are still single then,
I will marry you all the same,
I will marry you, my beautiful child,
And hold a wedding.

6. Whoever wrote this song,
There were three lads whose hearts were light,
One was white, and the other was black,
The third had no beard at all;
They were three idle fellows,
Who told me this.

label: work_sea
translation:
Where does one find Fatherly Loyalty:
Than that which animates the State of the Country!
The Pillars of our Statebuilding,
Before which Envy trembles and kneels.
Each one, whose heart burns with love,
Now sings for them and the Fatherland.
Hurrah! Hurrah! Hurrah!

2. Their care for their Subjects,
Their vigilance for the Country’s honor:
Makes us gladly go to battle,
And quickly seize the weapon,
For love awakens heroic courage,
We willingly risk goods and blood
For the Land, for the Land, for the Land.

3. What a fine reward is promised to us,
When our brave fist,
The Sea brotherhood, that plunders unstoppably,
Conquers or crushes.
Quickly one sees how the sailor
Gropes to the elbow in the Targets,
Through loot, through loot, through loot.

4. And if we count on foot or hand
Maimed or badly wounded,
The song of the beloved Fatherland
Makes us healthy.
There our State, as long as one lives,
Provides brave targets for support,
For reward, for reward, for reward.

5. But love for the free state
Motivates us more than money
Each of us curses British hatred,
Each curses their wicked violence.
Come comrades I must go at it,
Let us blindly and cripplingly strike him
For the Land, for the Land, for the Land.

label: work_sea
translation:
Let us, as Dutchmen,
Seated in a circle,
Be united and well pleased,
Loyal with heart and mouth:
Loyal to the honor of the Fatherland.
Each take the glass in his hand,
And pour and pour it full.
2. Behold, my little glass full of my wine,
Even filled to the brim:
Well then, it must be emptied,
To the health of the Fatherland:
One calls out, one shouts, one yells loud
“That nothing closes the dear prosperity”,
Of this blessed place!
3. That nothing breaks the strong bond of unity
Of the Batavians!
Oh no, their knot shall hold forever
In freedom’s firm stretch!
Each calls to the other around:
“This pillar, grounded in the blood of our fathers,
Is the Netherlands' Sanctuary!”
4. Whoever sincerely wishes well for this free state
May that blessing grow for him!
Whoever means harm we hate as the plague
And must perish!
Whoever sows even the smallest seed of discord,
They are cursed by every Patriot
Down to their descendants!
5. Pour in, pour in, oh friendship!
Pour, Patriots! pour:
Now pour this great cup freely,
Completely full, while Freedom beckons.
“Drink up,” (thus they say,) “oh troop of heroes.
“We wish that Batavian heroism
Shall again shine with glory!”
6. If ungrateful England threatens us,
Where unity dwells,
Soon her warlike hand shall be,
Too long provoked, displayed.
It is clear, whoever truly means well with the Netherlands,
Unites his fate with the Netherlands;
And dies or conquers.
7. Once more; pour in, for the Fatherland,
Let us drink the health of the States.
We wish them wisdom and understanding,
And blessings beyond measure!
Let us swear (this oath never forgotten),
“We stand with our authority,
Until the very last breath!”
8. Come, follow us, noble seed of Batavia!
In this binding resolution,
On this golden armor plate
England’s pride is stopped;
Go, ask Spain freely for proof,
They withdrew us from their domination;
Tremble, England, before her power!

label: work_sea
translation:
Come young Mates! Come Men!  
Help me banish the brood of robbers  
From their caves.  
Come, if you have resolute courage,  
Then you must fight with us;  
The Brit sells off Peace;  
Who can endure their greed?  
Come cheerfully with me to Sea!

2. When Tromp left his grave  
He immediately flew to the shore,  
And shouted: ‘To Sea, Soldiers!  
It is for our Fatherland!’  
We are his brave Sons,  
And every Captain  
Will prove by his deeds  
To be a valiant Tromp.

3. See each ship with pain  
Being chased by the Britons;  
We will make them pay dearly  
For this pursuit.  
We shall, as the forces of the country,  
Be united in going to Sea.  
With equal strength,  
We will quickly smash them to powder.

4. Come young Mates! Come Men!  
Come now help the brood  
That plagues us by banishing,  
Show your heroic courage  
To the Fatherland now;  
We fear neither distress nor death;  
The Brit will have to yield  
To the herb and shoot of the Netherlands.

label: work_sea
translation:
Do not be surprised,
Sa, Sailor, keep courage!
If the Brit is raging,
The old Dutch blood
Will, through the thunder of the cannon,
Show him what he started.
Long live Hoezée!
Long live the Sailors, Hoezée!

2. Do you hear how Volbergen,
The Eritrean people greet,
Then also turn the torment
Of your heroic courage!
Make the Brit, through the ship's cannon,
See what began so falsely,
Long live! Hoezée!
Long live Sailors, Hoezée!

3. Although that hero had to strike down
Before their superior force,
He nonetheless showed
His courage and strength.
Ship by ship his cannon
Made the foam see what it started,
Long live! Hoezée!
Long live Sailors, Hoezée!

label: work_sea
translation:
Now England sings freely in lower tones,  
No prouder than before; now Peace is at hand;  
But no! He will not dishonor our Flag by this trick,  
Our Thunderous Cannon will drive them from the Sea.  

2. As on Dogger Bank, where Zoutman has won,  
Yes, the British force is driven from the Albion shore.  
For Parker fled from the fire of his Cannons,  
Where Zoutman bravely planted the Laurel of Honor.  

3. There you, by the trick of Peace, no Brit, we know your deceit,  
Here your faithlessness is all too clear,  
No Merchant will so lightly risk his Treasure at Sea again;  
You shall not lay your Pirate's claw upon the trade.  

4. Now you shall tremble before the likes of Ruiter, Tromp, and Van Galen,  
Tremble now, proud Brit, you surely shall.  
Our cannon through the Schaa will assuredly tell tales of you,  
As when at Chatham they saw our flags waving.

label: work_sea
translation:
Good Evening Griet?  
That you see me,  
Is now for the last journey,  
Although I love you,  
My Angel!  
I must part from you, Girl!

2. You have heard it,  
I must go aboard;  
I cannot stay any longer:  
But, what is that,  
That I do not understand? ...  
The Girl begins to quarrel.

3. How, Farmer Neighbor!  
Do you look so sour?  
No, I will never leave you:  
I will come back soon,  
With Booty and Honor,  
Then you will speak differently.

4. If I become your Man,  
I say, I can  
Surely make you happy;  
Yes, I will make you  
At least a Lady,  
Even if it seems a strange thing.

5. I am a Sailor,  
And if I get angry,  
I will fight like a Devil;  
How my blood boils;  
I swell with courage;  
Hear, Girl, those are the rights.

6. But I still remain silent;  
While I first want  
To show who I am, through deeds.  
If I behave well,  
Thereby pleasing,  
I surely expect to be rewarded.

7. I will come back then,  
As a great Lord,  
There must be War for that:  
One does not know yet,  
Before one sees it,  
Whose courage can be praised!

8. It was no strange  
Hundred years ago  
To rise to Admiral  
From a bad Sailor;  
And I got angry;  
Could one not get that now too?

9. Because the Dutch Lion,  
Has in this Age  
No less courage or strength.  
From the Sea people again,  
You can, as is proper,  
Expect Tromps and de Ruyters.

10. Captains full of courage,  
We also have good ones:  
I just do not name them.  
Their War strategy  
And Bravery  
Will shame even the Elders.

11. Come dear Girl!  
Now to farewell,  
Give me a little kiss,  
Oh, if only you would!  
I must go to Sea:  
It is a pleasant Life after all!

label: work_sea
translation:
Who wants to sail with us to the Cape,  
And seize loot on Neptune’s wild seas  
The ships are made ready,  
Come heroes, who will sail with us?  

2. No thunderbolt or fire will frighten us,  
Though the proud Brit shows fierce looks,  
We long for that dog,  
Deceitful and full of treachery.  

3. Come men, here is shining gold to be taken,  
A rich profit will repay all danger,  
Neither fire nor steel are spared,  
For valuable coin metal.  

4. Come, let us strive to earn glory,  
No need, we must all die once,  
Come, die then early or late,  
For the welfare of the State.  

5. Gather courage, for the chance of war will favor us,  
We shoot all that resists us to pieces,  
Fear proud Brit, your blood  
Cools our heroism.  

6. And if we then return safely inside,  
We will happily go again to those girlfriends,  
Who provide us, besides love and pleasure,  
Gentle rest.  

7. There sweet amusement will reward us again,  
When we show the darlings the trophies,  
Won with prudence,  
And true bravery.  

8. Then we will together cheerfully drink,  
And to the dear Prince’s welfare,  
Who gave us, as punishment to the enemy,  
Freedom to the Cape.

label: work_sea
translation:
Comes friends with joy,  
Would you like to go once:  
Aboard with great joy,  
To see the life of war,  
Now we must be here from the shore,  
No use in arguing here,  
Do not let me fail,  
It is everywhere here,  

2. Now we must quickly,  
Leave the Amsterdam girls:  
How many children we leave,  
To heartache for this little journey:  
Since we have been near,  
Who knows how many yards:  
Thus we must roam;  
But alas, it is over,  

3. Forget your sweet loved ones,  
Want to step into the lighter,  
See that goes very well,  
We are soon on the road;  
Farewell dear Susies all,  
And do not mourn for us:  
It will happen more often.  
When we are ashore,  

4. Now we are before Amsterdam,  
For Texel we must lay;  
Here we are no boss,  
We have little to say,  
Sharpening is the pastime:  
And wandering with one,  
And rubbing hard stones  
That suits us,

label: work_sea
translation:
How long that journey also takes;
It is now the fourth year;
How sadly fearful that time falls upon me!
I took an East Indies sailor,
A sea dog, fresh, healthy, and strong,
Greatly loved by the ship's crew,
Also by his Officers, as I notice:
A man like a child.

Thoroughly learned in ship work,
A truly experienced man:
Who neither fights, nor swears, nor drinks, nor curses,
Who can speak fiercely
When it is necessary, and otherwise not.
Oh, if I could see him again,
There would be an end to my sorrow;
How welcome he was!
Look, whether I say it or stay silent
I am very uneasy,
Because I get no word or answer...
Who knows on which coast
The ship has come to grief! Perhaps
It perished with man and mouse...
Oh Bram, shall I never see you again?
Won't you come home to your wife!

Who knows where he travels and wanders!
Whether he suffers from hunger!
And is sick and sighing, even dying.
I may have already lost him.
It is already far beyond the year
Since I last saw his spirit;
I sat with the child, and he stood there,
By bright clear day.
He looked quite well,
So handsome, so well made:
It is certain that this means something good;
He looked earnestly at the child.
There he stood, in his long trousers,
No six feet from me;
I saw very clearly the silk cloth
That I had given him as a gift:

Naturally, tied around his neck.
How immensely happy I was!
I had long hoped for this sight.
I thought, he is thinking of me.
My Bram was never a frivolous fellow,
Says everyone who knows him.
He has a valuable intellect;
Oh, he is such a good man!
I got this child when he was still
Less than eight weeks at sea.
He was well-made in body and limb,
It went well with him.
People suspected I served for Min
Regarding that:
But I had no intention of that,
I declined the Lady.

I give my child what God gave me
For the little blood.
I also promised it to my husband, and he
Left me on this.
Come to mother, sweet Jan;
Oh, if your daddy were here!
If he saw you, I know the man
Would cry with joy.
Yes, if you only knew, my dear Bram,
How day and night,
I cared for our little lamb;
How I fulfilled my duty!
He left my sight not once;
I earned my bread with the needle.
I feared gossip more
Than the pale death.

But why do I torment myself so
And worry, and sigh, and cry.
The man probably has it well;
I can, after little time,
See him again, healthy and fresh.
That would be a joy!
‘Then it’s a new fair again’:
So says my neighbor Styn.
And see, she already knows this of old;
That I can well guess:
Sailors always have good wives,
That is all we do.
They visit us so cheerfully,
They don’t look stern or angry:
Hear, it doesn’t grieve me that for myself
I chose a handsome sailor girl.

What is the noise in the street?
It is already flying towards Westdyk...
It’s as if guns are firing too!
I’ll go and look as well;
So I get the right information;
Come with me, mother, Jan.
There lies the ship; look, Jantje, there.
Oh Lord, there stands my husband.

label: work_sea
translation:
Ha! That’s back in the Fatherland again;  
That’s my house, here lives my wife,  
My Susan. Hey! Holla! Zantje,  
Don’t you know me, pastime?  
I am Kees! Come, give me a hand.  
Be happy now that I stay with you.

Is that little witch my Grietje,  
Whom I saw in the letter,  
Which you wrote through my neighbor Pietje  
When I stayed in Texel,  
That you were saved? That Mietje,  
That’s a girl like a flag.  
Hear, my wife, it will not hinder us,  
Everyone now gets a golden penny.  
It won’t reduce our goods,  
Even if I did not come for three years.  
I certainly love children very much:  
I have not squandered my monthly money.

Must there be no bottle now,  
Zanne dear, what do you say to that?  
Listen, I have always praised you;  
I know you cannot pretend.  
I can read from your face  
That you are happy with your man.

I let myself be cleverly settled  
On the shore; I walked, yes flew,  
(No one could prevent me,)  
Out of everyone’s sight.  
And I held to the old laws.  
Child, I tasted neither wet nor dry.  
Would you go to a pub and swagger,  
No, Kees, I said, don’t be mad.  
You were never drunk;  
Own hair, and own stable,  
And drank there with the wife,  
Look, that has much better effect.

I must, before anything else, kiss you once,  
It has not happened to me in three years;  
Can we do it younger?  
I have grieved for you so long!  
And it will be, I would guess,  
Not disapproved by you either.

I’m so happy, I can’t eat...  
Heavens! How can that be possible!  
I have almost forgotten my boy.  
Zanne, where is Boudewyn?  
Would he not know of my arrival?  
Dear wife, that has no appearance.  
But who is rattling so at the door?  
It is certainly he!  
How would he cry out with joy,  
If he knew my homecoming;  
I’m very eager to see the boy.  
I have missed him for so long too.

Hello, my Boud, here is your father,  
Honey, are you so upset?  
I never had it worse on the trip  
Than when I told mother:  
Come, my boy, come a little closer...  
This is for you; yes, yes, it’s money.

Come, my wife, he must have a drink,  
It can be allowed on this trip,  
Let us clink together once;  
Boy, take the glass.  
Look how his eyes sparkle;  
He is so moved!  
Go, you are too soft baked,  
Sailor’s son. If England  
Should come to attack us again,  
Then show that you, in my style,  
Dare to cut in cheerfully;  
Cowardice is the greatest shame.

Now, I don't want to sadden you,  
I am as soft-hearted as you,  
I am an enemy of boastfulness,  
I don’t like chatter;  
What I dare to taste, I gave,  
Standing at the Constables’ side.

Bout, do you also want to go to war  
As your father does? Come on.  
Tomorrow we will arrange that.  
The Prinsen Hof... have you understood?  
Hear my wife, there are enough men  
In his years to go along.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. Would I now not be welcome,  
After the victory of the nation's fleet,  
At Anna, my chosen one?...  
See how you kiss me to death:...  
Yes, Zoutman, the brave Hero  
Has restored our nation's glory.          (repeat)

2. The first Battle brings us glory,  
England's pride is halted,  
Parker flees, Hooray! Victory!  
All that boasting is now over:  
For Zoutman, the valiant Hero  
Has restored the nation's old glory.          (repeat)

3. Zeeland and Maas men cannot be found,  
They place hope on lost plunder,  
Ships, men, cannons and sails,  
Parker was ahead in everything;  
Nevertheless, Zoutman, the Hero,  
With God's help restored the nation's honor.          (repeat)

4. Yes, although Russia might fool us,  
With delay, with Swede and Dane,  
We could beat the enemy,  
The honor remains ours alone now:  
For Zoutman, the valiant Hero,  
Has restored the nation's old glory.          (repeat)

5. Red or Rinsch cannot help now,  
No, the greatest triumph demands  
Bourgonje for the welfare of the States,  
And for the Supreme Admiral:  
For Zoutman, the valiant Hero,  
Has restored the nation's old glory.          (repeat)

6. Zoutman especially deserves a drink,  
No one served the country more,  
He's no loudmouth but a leader,  
England feels it as its disgrace,  
For that old-fashioned Hero  
Has brought down his superiority.          (repeat)

7. I have conditions by dozens,  
But a seaman knows his measure:  
This is for the Captains,  
For those pillars of the State,  
Through whose help and courage the Hero  
Zoutman has restored the nation's glory.          (repeat)

8. Now for the Amsterdam Fathers,  
Without whom it would not have happened;  
They are the Prince's best advisors:  
Advice of strangers is no good:  
Through their language of deeds the Hero  
Sailed out and restored the nation's glory.          (repeat)

9. See how faithfully they oversee the wounded  
In the hospital,  
They give everything for the best,  
Who is not moved by love?  
They with Zoutman, our Hero,  
Have restored the honor of the country.          (repeat)

10. Had we not fought fiercely,  
Amsterdam would have been oppressed:  
But despite those who sought that,  
That wicked plan failed:  
For its treasure has been faithfully secured by the Hero.          (repeat)

11. Now send the Prince rich ships,  
Under Zoutman, the Brit  
If he dares to stand, so gingerly,  
That he asks us for peace:  
For the very name of that Hero  
Spreads fear of his power.          (repeat)

12. It is the sea indeed which for this one,  
Has brought the country to greatness,  
Without being a Count or Prince,  
A Citizen shows his strength there:  
It is clear from Zoutman, for that Hero  
Has restored the nation's honor at sea.          (repeat)

13. Yes, the sea is truly my life,  
Tomorrow I go back aboard,  
I will give you a sweet farewell,  
Dear girl, help me quickly forward,  
And wait for spoils, as our Hero  
Is placed at the head of the fleet.          (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
1. Let Dutch People Wear this Emblem  
As long as the British cursed plagues  
Bind trade and seafaring,  
We will still wave with Zoutman's Ribbon,  
And will keep waving it for as long,  
Until we celebrate at sea like Zoutman.  

2. Away, Henri-Quatre! with your fringes,  
Melon color and pale Orange:  
Here I have à la Zoutman's Ribbon,  
Which binds itself to the Netherlands,  
Must wear this new fashion,  
Or no patriot will be pleased.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. What joy, my dear Mietje!  
I come home safe again,  
And sing a cheerful little song  
In honor of our sailor.       Dear Sus!  
I give you this kiss as a welcome.  

2. Now I really have courage!  
Who would have ever thought this?  
Two months of captivity for nothing! ---  
This purse! --- I see you smile.       Dear Sus!  
A welcome home --- Yes indeed! I buy that fast.  

3. See here a sailor’s ribbon!  
There is the Dogger Bank:  
There the fleet fought, my child!  
There we won, thank God!       Dear Sus!  
Wear it on your chest, or lay it on your bosom.  

4. I thank you, dear Klaasje!  
For the ribbon, and I accept it,  
You are a noble mistress:  
You have done your best.       Dear friend!  
This little kiss shows how your heart loves me.  

5. One may hope something of you,  
Since you fought like a seal;  
I also wanted to buy you something,  
Since you conducted yourself so bravely.       Dear friend!  
A welcome home from her who loves you.  

6. The Henriquatre days  
Are over --- Give me your foot here;  
Now you must wear the sailor's ribbon:  
Those anchors suit you well.       Dear friend!  
Wear it in honor of her who loves you.  

7. You have a sailor’s ribbon!  
I fasten it in such a way,  
Now once pranked, child!  
Over there on the outside.       Dear Sus!  
Come, let us go. But before we go --- a kiss.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. We shall sail contentedly,  
With Kindsbergen our Captain,  
If an enemy comes to confront us,  
No one among us will be faint-hearted;  
Our Commander is like our Father,  
What greater fortune can there be,  
We wish him nothing but the best,  
Long live the Captain, long live the Captain,  
Long live the Captain, yes our Captain.  

2. Now let a wreath of laurel leaves fly,  
In honor of this Hero;  
We, as children, approach him,  
Faithfully stand firm against violence,  
And risk our lives with him,  
Whoever means this sings with me,  
With Kindsbergen all our days,  
Live in honor as Captain,  
Long live the Captain, yes our Captain.  

3. Our Captain, a man of honor,  
Widely renowned throughout the world!  
He must always triumph,  
Never retreat from his enemy,  
His heroism has been proven,  
And also his wise counsel,  
We will not fall short either,  
We are all ready to fight,  
Long live the Captain, yes our Captain.  

4. Let us just engage in battle,  
For we are eager to fight;  
Our Captain will manage it well,  
His wise counsel reassures us,  
And he can expect from us,  
That we with lion-hearted courage,  
Will combine all our strength,  
Until the last drop of blood is shed,  
Long live the Captain, yes our Captain.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. To board, to board; we must go!
Yes, Comrades!
No talking helps here;
One must fulfill their duty
For the welfare of the Fatherland;
Fighting for that is no disgrace;
But honor, like the Fire of War burns.
Come on, come on, come---on!
The time wants us to go.

2. Just hold on in true Dutch style,
Then one can still
Make the British Dog
Crawl back into his hole.
Or Nest, with tail between legs;
A Lion is not made for any Dog.
What is an Englishman worth to us?
Not a penny, not a penny, not a pen---ny,
But Bullets, Fire, and Gunpowder.

3. One does not fear a cowardly Robber;
A brief consultation
Of our State
Grants us the Sea territory,
As long as, without quarrel or envy,
One goes to battle united and bravely,
To the regret of all British sympathizers.
Then will, then will, then wi---ll,
Our salvation be restored soon.

4. If every Fatherland City
Took precaution
Like Amsterdam,
And had such Fathers,
Then no unruly Englishman
Could ever do what he can now;
Then there would be no proof of
His evil, his evil, his e---vil,
Hated even by the Turks.

5. Oh, if only all the Bastardry,
That British scum
Brought into our house,
Were once put aside,
Then one would see the true Civic Benefit,
Protected by Burgervaders’ Loyalty,
Supported by Freedom’s genuine glory;
And everyone would then offer their ha---nd,
For the Welfare of the Fatherland.
But let whatever may happen,
The City on the Y
Will turn its value
On the Pivot
Of Power, no matter how strongly it is opposed,
So that it does not lose its authority,
But even increases its prestige.
Then that will never hann---pen:
One will soon see it differently.

7. Come, Amsterdam lads! come
To board, to board;
We must move forward!
Who is it you are ashamed for?
Fight finally for your own City,
Which never had braver Fathers,
Than now, however much defamed by Envy;
By Envy, who before it be---gins,
Will see its power thwarted.

label: work_sea
translation:
Amstel’s Girl, highly esteemed,  
Sailor’s delight and love,  
Fame of the Amsterdam lineage;  
I must now take my leave of you,  
Hoping that I may,  
Benefit you a little,  
Now I must leave my dear Ley’,  
All my comfort behind.  

2. Oh, who comforts me in my pain,  
Now that I must lose you,  
With much love and tender pain,  
A sailor must wander,  
The wine, however, soothes the sorrow:  
On the wild waves,  
But love, the very best treasure,  
Fears no dangers.  

3. Oh, how bitter and heavy  
That parting is,  
Like a sweet beloved pair,  
Must part for a long time  
From the sweetest thing on earth,  
Always to be found,  
And venture into danger,  
From sea and stormy winds.  

4. But my love gives me good courage,  
The time will surely come again:  
That we, with a joyful mind,  
Live without sorrow,  
With the least danger possible  
That I now must suffer,  
And then nothing will separate us,  
But death alone.  

5. With this hope I now, freely,  
Will take my leave,  
On this journey pleased and glad,  
And on the waves soar,  
With the nation's ship d’Argo proudly,  
We shall sail away,  
And that with great pleasure,  
Who knows how many miles.  

6. Yet if duty and honor,  
Must be more than love:  
Our only desire is  
To fight without fear:  
For the State and the Fatherland  
To risk our body and blood,  
And with an armed hand,  
To drive away the enemy.  

7. Farewell then, my hope and delight,  
Life of my life,  
You kissed me for the last time,  
You shall forever soar,  
Within the depths of my heart,  
Though I am left behind,  
To my greatest sorrow,  
I cannot be with you.

label: work_sea
translation:
1. Now I will write,  
While I must stay,  
Ashore in loneliness,  
I was supposed to marry,  
Like other women,  
But I have lost my sweetheart,  
My husband has gone sailing to sea,          repeat.  
My husband has gone sailing to sea,  
sea yes sea yes sea because of the sea because of the  
Who now wants to go along with him.

2. Now I can show off,  
With all those gentlemen,  
Who are only kind to me,  
To flirt with them,  
Like other creatures,  
And I think I am alone now,  
My husband has gone sailing to sea:          repeat.  
My husband has gone sailing to sea,  
sea yes sea yes sea because of the sea because of the  
Who the devil sails along with him.

3. He must now fight,  
Like brave lads,  
I am fine with that,  
Even if he has to climb,  
Leap up above,  
I think I am on the right track,  
My husband has gone sailing to sea:          repeat.  
My husband has gone sailing to sea,  
sea yes sea yes sea because of the sea because of the  
I send him a little coat along.

4. Farewell, ladies,  
Do not mourn,  
If you lose your husband like that,  
If you want to have fun,  
With other fellows,  
Then you get out of the trouble,  
And think he had to go to sea:          repeat.  
My husband has gone sailing to sea,  
sea yes sea yes sea because of the sea because of the  
And be content with that.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.
Friends, come listen to this song,
About what recently happened,        bis.
Of a girl of twenty years,
Who intended to go courting:
Because her sweetheart was a captain,
She wanted to be a little sailor.        bis.

2.
It was in the city of Amsterdam,
Where she came to a soul-seller,        bis.
There she enlisted for seven years,
And she feared no dangers,
To climb up the mast,
To fix the sails and ropes tight.        bis.

3.
She went aboard with her chest,
As a good sailor should:        bis.
And they set sail at sea,
A few miles on the waves,
Then the weather surprised her,
She had to climb up the mast.        bis.

4.
But because of the storms and bad weather,
The sails went back and forth,        bis.
And then she was in danger,
Because she could no longer manage:
And she stepped on the yardarm,
From which she fell down from above.        bis.

5.
Immediately the captain spoke,
Saying she must be flogged,        bis.
She was tied up at the mast,
Hands and feet both bound:
And there she had to remain standing,
Until a quartermaster would beat her.        bis.

6.
Immediately she called, "Captain,
May I speak a word to you alone,"        bis.
She said, "My lord, do not strike me,
For I am your darling as you see,"
Immediately she was released,
And received a sweet kiss.        bis.

7.
Farewell girls, whoever you are,
Who are courted by a young man,        bis.
Become a sailor, a good soldier;
Never abandon your sweetheart,
For when this ship came ashore,
There was already a young sailor girl.        bis.

label: work_sea
translation:
O Moon! O starry lights,  
How beautiful is your glow,  
Before which my eye must yield,  
As I behold the transit,  
Of the entire heavenly sphere.  
That keeps watch over me here,  
Always connected to each other,  
Companions on this night.

When I hear the waves,  
The roaring of the sea,  
Neptune with his strings,  
Who accompanies me speedily,  
Then I think in the darkness,  
O Sea of abundance,  
Who through your fame and glory,  
Always flows toward her tide.  
I am a sailor who supports life,  
The farmer beneath,  
I must follow my path,  
Where storm or wind drives me,  
When I am in dark nights,  
Deprived of sight,  
Always watching the wind,  
To where I direct my course.

Then I think repeatedly,  
O sailor who guards the sand,  
Near the fatherland’s poles,  
Always holding steadfast,  
To land and farming,  
Through your coming alone,  
The baker and the brewer,  
Must remain on their feet.

Four glasses already struck,  
My watch is halfway done,  
With which I gladly,  
Whistle the joyful end,  
But what do I see from afar,  
Rising through my vision,  
Those dear morning stars,  
And the pleasant daylight.

That here in my eyes,  
Shines like a silver sun,  
Which through its great power,  
Travels from its clear source,  
But surely it is eight glasses,  
My watch is already finished,  
Neptune does not want to rage,  
For I want to go and rest.

label: work_sea
translation:
What greater joy comes to us before,
When one sees on every side,
Just as everyone comes to hear,
All through the writing of the Newspaper:
That there is money to be won again,
For the Girls here in this place,
Now the Ships come in,
Bringing many Sailors with them.
Now they will dress up again,
Wearing a Hoop skirt:
And their Bosoms nicely with ribbons,
To come to the Man,
They go swaying in a certain style:
They love a lot of fun,
Jan despised the black Country:
White Girls can be found here.
I would like to sail to the East Indies:
But Holland is so beautiful,
As if there were no Black women there,
The white Girls take the crown;
We can still entertain ourselves with them,
With a Chintz or Porcelain:
Sailors are all strange fellows,
They get the girls by the dozen.
Now a Farmer has nothing to say,
Now the Ships arrive
It seems the sailors now put forward
One must now stand behind,
Gerrit was nearly out of his mind;
I used to go to my girl:
But now I cannot win her over,
I see no joy in it anymore.
I thought she would never leave me,
She took me for her own man:
I would like to sell her at the ferry;
But I think no one will want that.
I recently sat in a Tavern,
I presented this to everyone,
I found myself cheated by the matter,
She was known publicly.
Now the Ships come in,
Well provided already with the loot,
Everyone may well love their sweetheart:
Otherwise they will lie you out a little,
Then they go swaying with Sailors:
Who now all come to the shore,
Who leave behind their black animals,
White girls you find everywhere here,
Who would not now make glory,
Now the Ships come home:
They talk about many lost matters,
Because the City comes full of bustle,
Now of those Boat companions:
Coming from the black Land,
One likes to hear some news
You are welcome, friend, on every side.
One sees the Inn now flourishing,
Building up good courage there,
Sailors live just like gentlemen:
If you have no money spend it well,
Just want to be merry and happy,
If the money is now spent;
Then we do like these,
The throat is supposed to be finished.
Mirror yourself here all Sailors,
Now you come back to the shore:
Do not sway overboard too much,
Think once of your storm and fall,
When you go to squander your money,
They will help you again to your place,
Thus you leave the Venus animals,
And go back to the black Land.
I will remember the Song well,
Because I had never thought it:
When I came to see the black Land
What I have gone through there,
I thought of the white Girls:
I was back in the Fatherland,
I never made a trip to the East Indies,
I would rather sail along the Coast.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
You famous Amstel residents,  
Pride of the free state:  
Rejoice now on your silver waves,  
Gysbrecht’s fortresses as ornament,  
Be cheerful, exuberant;  
Showpiece jewel of the seven states,  
Which benefits the foreigner.

2.  
Leave Pantagie’s green linden trees,  
Do not remain long in your harbor,  
The elite of Amstel’s princely households:  
Proceed towards the Admiralty,  
Young comrades take your girl,  
You truly have another voyage:  
From your sweet love, good to go.

3.  
If the War God commands all coasts,  
Of beautiful Europe,  
Bellona, stirrer of unrest,  
Away restless goddess,  
Let God Mars share his bitter grapes:  
To partake in this pleasure,  
I side with the Lion, that suits me.

4.  
Rejoice, you Prius-offspring axes:  
Admirers of the Orange lineage,  
Celebrate your sails on the Y Goddess,  
Splendor of our Amsterdam,  
Free from war’s compulsion and chains,  
See your sail fleet, full of splendor,  
In honor year after year.

5.  
That Y Goddess, through all the commotion,  
Casts her waves like a sea?  
Through the fruit she bears on the water;  
Hear the call of Hoesee,  
Driven by song and joy,  
May this harbor remain long happy,  
And extend towards a harbor.

6.  
All who love the Y stream,  
Be cheerful, all of you,  
See there your fleet guardian arrives,  
And the Vice Admiral:  
Schout by Nacht: here he comes nearer,  
Wind and current steer him to the rudder;  
To the joy of Amstel’s Lion’s dale.

7.  
Bloom long, oh head of the city,  
Adornment of the crown of our state building,  
Bloom still through favor of peace,  
Seldom harmed by sorrow or grief,  
Long live the Amstel citizen fathers,  
Adorn their heads with myrtle leaves,  
Long live the beloved house of Nassau.

label: work_sea
translation:
Thank God, I see the homeland,  
I feel my heart burning with joy,  
What a joy it will be for my wife,  
If I appear so unexpectedly.

I am still in the strongest prime of my youth,  
Although sometimes rough, I lack virtue;  
I always think of my house and wife,  
And care for her as for my own body.

There you have such a long Piet,  
He says, "I don't think about marriage,  
If I marry a wife, I'm tied down,  
I now keep hens on perches.  
When I come across a doe,  
Then I join the bucks.  
If I don’t escape marriage,  
Every friendly girl is my wife.

And if things go a bit rough at sea,  
I don’t torment my mind over a woman,  
If I had a wife, I would think of her,  
And be more anxious in danger.

I think that’s nonsensical reasoning,  
Because when one returns home,  
Then I feel, dear friends, here  
Such great pleasure in my heart.

Then I think, how is Janneman doing,  
Does my Piet already have the pants on,  
My wife surely prepares a cake,  
If she only knows of my homecoming.

Then it’s over with father and mother,  
My wife does not stink like a whore.  
I love honest fun,  
Our bed then looks like a wedding bed.  
And if it sometimes gets bad at sea,  
Which I handle as well as Pieter does,  
I eagerly take up the work,  
The longing for wife and child is strong.

And if it once seemed like a shipwreck,  
Then I think, if this keel broke,  
And everything here exploded to splinters,  
The Man of the Winds lives in high esteem.

label: work_sea
translation:
I no longer see the homeland.  
No towers – it’s over,  
I feel it, and does that not honor me?  
Truly, a tear in my eye.  
I turn my hat around once more,  
My homeland, how are you!  
I hope soon to see my people healthy  
And happily again:  
That is now near Greenland's coast,  
Behold the whales! behold,  
We are already uneasy on board,  
If the blubber still has its scent.

I just dreamed there a moment,  
But was disturbed in it,  
How already a great number  
Of fish lay on board.  
I stood working on the fish, while  
A shark clung to it.  
Then I struck that blubber thief  
On its head with a small axe.  
I said, you greedy thief!  
Go to your comrades:  
Tell them if you liked that snout blow,  
Then he sank like a stone.

Then they just called me out of my cage,  
And then I saw clearly.  
For truly that dream was beautiful.  
The good Heaven wishes  
That it may only become true,  
I already long in my heart  
For a joyful monster day.  
Oh, that is wonderfully sweet!  
When a ship full of blubber arrives  
At the current,  
Then there is no lack of cheering,  
Everyone welcomes us well!

The shipowners come in a boat  
With their accountant on board,  
I think I already hear their shout.  
They look at every man,  
As if we just pulled the fish  
Out of the sea.  
And the shipowner said, ‘That fish weighs heavy.  
How do you catch it?  
With a net or fishing rod?  
That was a clever man.  
I said and laughed at him as he traveled,  
You guess it about right.  
Harpoons! lenses! wait your turn,  
The lines are ready.  
A little fool is the one who mourns  
All the deadly danger.  
Although everything that is in Greenland  
Stands by us to live,  
We know very well that a fish  
Is like an island,  
And if I were to stay now,  
Still, I have no shame.  
As I consider myself,  
I still lived quite devoutly.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.
Well, what is better than living,
Than being a Sailor?
They always live contentedly,
And they go about cheerfully!
With the girls from the district?
As soon as they come ashore.

2.
First, they arrive in formation,
At the Outer side harbor,
There you see the girls standing,
Who then greet their friends kindly,
Welcome, my dear friend:
See, we have been waiting for you.

3.
Then you see them walking happily,
In the Chapel or Nieuwenbrug alley,
To the Cellars quite properly,
Where no one knows sadness,
They dance to violin and lyre,
Always with the girls for pleasure.

4.
The corner near the Vineyard street,
There they come very playful,
Find there ladies by the shore,
Who want to say "Come inside",
Demand a little bottle to your liking,
And then also a kiss.

5.
The French Half-alley and Kolk want to hear,
They pass there as one,
Wardens stand without crying,
Who also received them so well,
And they keep an eye on their money,
That is their purpose.

6.
They say don't forget,
The Knight and the Squire street,
The College one then strikes,
They do not leave the New Market unattended,
At the Mom Cellar harbor,
They must also go to the Kempies.

7.
The Devil’s corner also entered,
There you find the greatest fun,
By Cross-eyed Griet or Antinet;
By Mother Traenoog with her complexion,
And by Father Menke Agril,
There they play Honnebil.

8.
Then you see them promenade again,
Through the Nes toward the Bierkade,
There sit the girls very fine,
Dressed all in the latest fashion,
In the alleys all three,
It is all the rage of fashion.

9.
I almost forgot the Nieuwendijk,
They pass there like a hero;
Because we still have honest money,
They must be in the Karremelk alley,
By Mother Gort or Pissege Lys,
Who loves a long radish.

10.
The Mouthaen alley, last of all,
Yes, they must also be there,
To drink a bottle of wine,
If they then behave properly:
By Jannetje and by Neeltje Tuyt,
And then back out the Zeegat.

11.
Farewell then sailors all,
Who have written this little song:
It was a sailor whose heart was light,
At Cape the Good Hope,
He composed all out of his head,
At renowned Mother Schepop.

label: work_sea
translation:
Eylaas, what a sad fate!  
What has happened to me?  
How have I become so mad,  
That I have enlisted,  
That I have set foot on such a wooden clog,     (repeat)  
My body began to fail me.  

2.  
I have my youth,  
And in my spring days,  
So many times I have refused;  
No service can please me,  
But I see that no one knows,     (repeat)  
What disaster is prepared for us.  

3.  
Can a little gold  
Overcome my freedom?  
Does money not spoil  
Where are my free thoughts?  
As I, blinded by money,      (repeat)  
Am shackled by an iron chain.  

4.  
Who is the cause?  
Whom shall I blame?  
Love or the can,  
Shall I lay the blame on;  
For Love and Wine,          (repeat)  
Must be the cause of it.  

5.  
To Ale and Wine,  
I was too much bound,  
I thought it was medicine,  
Yet I have found out  
That much use of it,     (repeat)  
Can harm youth.  

6.  
My mother's image,  
Could please me too much,  
I could not endure my love's distress;  
Strong enough to withstand,  
And I was not well at ease,          (repeat)  
When I had to sleep alone.  

7.  
Had I listened more  
To the advice of friends;  
Then I could have driven away  
All that evil from my neck,  
One commonly covers the well,     (repeat)  
When the calf has drowned.  

8.  
Had I eaten fish or meat  
For this one well,  
Now only the bone or sting;  
That I have thrown away,  
I attacked it with appetite,         (repeat)  
As if it were the sweetest confection.  

9.  
Now my delicate mouth:  
Must be filled with barley and pea soup,  
What dried cod and what bacon,  
Is that not a fine treat,  
Butter, cheese and ship's biscuit,     (repeat)  
With some blows on my skin.  

10.  
I could also on land,  
Gather the joy of rest,  
I knew no "everywhere"  
When I lay sleeping.  
I slept in bed at my ease,     (repeat)  
Until the dear day broke.  

11.  
Now evening comes,  
I hear the bell toll,  
One must go to the bunk;  
But if there is wind or rain,  
Immediately all must make haste,     (repeat)  
Thus one is disturbed in one's rest.  

12.  
I lived on land,  
Always quite content,  
I knew no reef or bank,  
Or other hardships;  
I feared no fire of war or sword, (repeat)  
Never feared but well protected.  

13.  
Who knows in what state,  
We will come further,  
Maybe cross-eyed or lame,  
Or suddenly taken away;  
As I, healthy and well-made,     (repeat)  
Have boarded the warship.  

14.  
In all my poverty,  
In all my bitter suffering,  
I still receive adversity;  
Now some more is coming,  
It is better on a ship the body,     (repeat)  
Than married to a wicked wife.  

15.  
You Dutch all,  
We now go to fight for you;  
To the glory of our country!  
To free you so,  
From the Kaiser's evil tyranny,     (repeat)  
To make you bold and free.

label: work_sea
translation:
What sorrow does a sailor already feel,
When he sails to foreign lands;
But on his journey he does not care,
As long as he comes ashore,
Then his journey is with haste,
To my dearest sweetheart,
He brought her much money and goods.

I had a girl dearly loved,
Very elegant in features,
With two brown eyes like a gite;
Her features, look well below,
Neither too short nor too long,
Elegant is her gait;
There is no one more beautiful in the whole land.

But then I took my farewell
Of her, that sweet angel,
I said goodbye to her
With tears full of mingling,
What did she then give me as pledge?
It was on her right hand, a ring.
I gave her a little kiss and then departed.

When I had now completed my journey
On the sea with a thousand currents,
I dreamed of her many nights:
I have returned home
From my journey with haste
To my dearest sweetheart,
I also brought her much silver and goods.

Then I heard that my bride
Had chosen another,
My heart burst, I shout aloud
Oh, I was never born:
I say well, my dear Sarah,
Are you heavy with another?
Do you leave your faithful lover so?
Well, love was your loyalty
Then you forget me so quickly,
When I took you for a maid,
With prayers and with begging,
Dear, who has so dulled you
That you believe in chatter;
Your maiden flower has been stolen.

Oh love, you slayer of the Moor,
You turn with all winds,
Much promise, little action,
As I have experienced.
Dearest, lament your own disgrace,
Now quench your love-fire free,
For your fortune is now lost.

Now I will, for my farewell,
Go to comfort my young heart
Even with a fresh goblet of wine,
Then sail again to the East,
There I live without care or pain,
Farewell, separated love of mine,
I hope God will be your guide.

label: work_sea
translation:
Behold some new cheerfulness,  
Has happened to us today;  
For which we, with great diligence,  
Have faithfully fulfilled our duty,  
It was quite long but easy for us,  
While we performed our service.  
It was Saturday, just past three o’clock,  
Then it was called out with louder voices!  
“Come on boys, run upstairs quickly,  
They will read happy tidings;”  
It was the eighteenth of June,  
When I was quickly discharged from service.  
The Argo was our first ship  
On which we took the helm,  
It was Captain Staring, full of insight,  
Who taught us to make the English tremble,  
As everyone well knows,  
That we chased the English away with one shot.  
From there we moved on,  
To Gelderland with great pleasure;  
Where we then went to sea,  
To traverse that vast expanse,  
We sailed to the Turkish land,  
All with the great ship Gelderland.  
After we had seen those places,  
We then sailed to France,  
To make repairs we had to;  
Our stiff mast and our sails,  
So we set to work,  
To make everything properly fit.  
When our sails were ready,  
We went to visit Zeeland,  
Though we arrived in good peace;  
From all sorts of strange corners,  
We went ashore there,  
Where the girls received us.  
In Zeeland we lived in peace.  
In the Arms of that place we wished to know,  
How many girls we kissed there,  
As long as our money lasted;  
Then we went again to Spain,  
Where they received our love.  
There we lived in good peace,  
With all those dear Spanish girls;  
Though we were Hollanders,  
We were allowed, for a trip,  
To go once cheerfully with those girls,  
Until we had to leave again.  
So we went back to sea;  
But what a guest did we see then,  
Who had been there on the route,  
With her worn paintbrushes;  
Why the Doctor smiled kindly,  
And expected money from those fellows.  
So we entered Texel,  
Though we were on guard with great desire,  
To go visit Amsterdam,  
Though the girls longed after us;  
And we longed with great diligence  
One for his wife, the other for his maid.  
Now we are freed from service,  
And do not want to write more of it,  
Although the writer bursts with regret,  
Because he still had to remain in service,  
For all his pain is great sorrow,  
As everyone can see before their eyes.  
Now I end with the poem;  
Because see, my pen no longer wants to write,  
And now wants to stay in Amsterdam  
By the girls until my last penny,  
And drown the last penny,  
That I sing to you as the conclusion.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Be cheerful in spirit,  
You citizens of Amsterdam,  
Now we must fight with lions,  
For the Fatherland to go to battle,  
Our ship is already ready,  
At Texel by the shore,  
Sailors of Amsterdam,  
Make joyous and happy sounds.  

2.  
Aboard, aboard, aboard,  
The ship lies ready,  
The country's service has captivated us,  
That service is worthy of us,  
Sailor, you must be at peace,  
Aboard, aboard, aboard,  
Our ship lies at anchor,  
The country's service has captivated us.  

3.  
Everyone jumps into the bow,  
Pack up and stow your bales,  
But think above all about the bird,  
Which has chosen us for service,  
It gives the seaman strength,  
And makes him a hero,  
Warms him in the night,  
When the country's prosperity prevails.  

4.  
Pack up, pack all the goods,  
The lighter cannot close,  
The tide is there, it is ebb,  
Now set the work to screws,  
We must all assemble,  
Aboard, aboard, aboard,  
Take one last sip,  
The country's service has captivated us.  

5.  
Is it ready, then call out quickly,  
Dear parents, I wish you the best,  
O lady and love, farewell.  
Farewell, oh people and fortress,  
The long farewell pains you greatly,  
Embrace and call out quickly,  
The hardest heart becomes tender,  
Farewell, farewell always well.  

6.  
Jump, jump into the lighter,  
The farewell has been given to me,  
Change your mind,  
From the previous free life,  
Think only of your service,  
Thus extends this new status,  
The duty you must uphold,  
To the honor of yourself and the country.  

7.  
Cast off, cast off, strive forward,  
It must happen eventually,  
The country's service has captivated us,  
We must provide assistance,  
Think like a sensible man,  
Away with fear that restrains the heart,  
Arrange yourself as best you can,  
The sea must also be preserved.  

8.  
There must be a ship, sailors must,  
What would you refuse,  
He who fulfills his duty,  
Can make happiness all around,  
The world wherever you lie,  
Everyone’s craft is good.  
It is only serviceable,  
If one fulfills his duty.  

9.  
Hurrah, hurrah, hurrah!  
We will strive courageously,  
Luck always sails with us,  
Where brave souls live;  
God who is a helmsman?  
A helper everywhere:  
He will surely bring us  
Back safely to shore.

label: work_sea
translation:
Amsterdam girls,  
Will you join the voyage,  
To take a little trip with us:  
To the warm land  
Like a charming little creature,  
That is with us on board  
Who wanted, for a little pleasure:  
To sail along with us.  

2. A maiden of 20 years,  
Also wanted to join the voyage  
To sail with us,  
All the way to the Ape Land  
Her name is kept secret,  
When she was still freer,  
She was called something quite different:  
What do you think of that Bag?  

3. She was a sweet angel,  
Isn't that a strange trick?  
What do you think of that rascal,  
Delighted by the rumor  
Of our merry bunch,  
Those strange snakes they are  
Because they wished very much,  
That half would be like that.  

4. I will not name the ship,  
Where this happened  
There is enough to praise,  
In the content of this song,  
And this charming girl,  
In Texel where it happened,  
A piece worthy of attention,  
Who is sad because of that.  

5. She is a charming girl  
Born in Holland,  
Therefore she is worthy  
To go on the voyage with us,  
But she may not come now  
As it is openly known,  
With us on this trip  
Although our little ship is ready.  

6. This makes us very sad  
While we are lovers,  
To shoot birds  
Among such fine girls,  
Yes, we would much rather  
That there were fifty of such kind,  
To everyone's liking  
Were on board with us.  

7. He who wrote this little song,  
He was a strange snake  
He was also of the light-hearted,  
And liked entertainment,  
And such little tricks,  
They were to his liking  
He is always peaceful:  
To play the game of love.  

8. That all girls were  
We would be pleased  
And would not even stir  
Hear this fine girls,  
Yes, we could endure it well  
Because we are lovers,  
To sleep with both of us  
Until Aurora shines.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
The East India sailors are home!  
Together with the Year,  
Come, let us begin,  
To wish each other:  
Welcome, my beloved ones,  
Welcome, all sweethearts,  
I managed to find you  
Here on the Amstel embankment.  

2.  
The Amsterdam waitresses  
Are full of charm again;  
The ship’s afterparty has arrived,  
It is a pleasure for them;  
Even more the prostitutes,  
With their feigned caresses,  
They are lurking,  
And wish their share.  

3.  
Now the prostitutes sit,  
With open throats!  
To transport you,  
Through their sweet caresses.  
They know East India,  
The name speaks for itself,  
For your money, I love you,  
My friend, be at peace.  

4.  
The East Indian sailors,  
When they are ashore,  
They go courting  
And that everywhere.  
By night and day,  
And early morning,  
People come to ask for them,  
They are in the tavern.  

5.  
Yes, also the officers,  
They live brilliantly!  
They go out to have fun,  
With young ladies of the trade,  
Brothel and billiards;  
They keep things busy again,  
They don’t let themselves be trifled with;  
It might get serious.  

6.  
No lunch in the afternoon,  
No dinner at home in the evening,  
One must also know,  
That money and coin is still there,  
As long as that is with them,  
Then the tavern is just open:  
So Hospes just pour for us,  
That suits our mood.  

7.  
Then you can find them  
Very late in the evening,  
With a girl or beloved,  
They walk the streets.  
You hear sweet darling say,  
“Will you go with me?”  
(Say sweet dear thief)  
Not far from here.  

8.  
But yet for the last time,  
All East India sailors,  
I advise you for the best,  
When you are ashore.  
If you want to control yourself a bit,  
And not live like this forever,  
Or you will soon come again,  
To another ship’s side.

label: work_sea
translation:
For him who desires to sail,  
In service of the Fatherland;  
For him I stretch the tough strings,  
And give him the hand as a brother:  
I let Neptune guide me,  
I live content and happily weary;  
I will never part from seafaring,  
But sail until the end of my life. (repeat)  

One can live joyfully on board,  
One knows no adversity there,  
To everyone this joy is given,  
When one only fulfills their duty:  
I let Neptune guide me,  
I live content and happily weary;  
I will never part from seafaring,  
But sail until the end of my life. (repeat)  

One can also visit the ladies,  
One has access to the salon there,  
For those who fear no misfortune,  
They spread the gentle bed of love.  
I let Neptune guide me,  
I live content and happily weary;  
I will never part from seafaring,  
But sail until the end of my life. (repeat)  

When one then drops the Hope,  
I lower the anchor into the sand;  
Then you hear cheering from the ramparts,  
Welcome home in the Fatherland.  
I let Neptune guide me,  
I live content and happily weary;  
I will never part from seafaring,  
But sail until the end of my life. (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
I already see the blue dunes gleaming  
There lies the dear Fatherland,  
Now I may breathe more freely,  
Soon we will set foot on that shore;  
After a voyage of four long years,  
I leave, Neptune, your wild seas,  
And seek on Batavia's coast  
For love and gentle rest,  
And gentle rest.

Wherever one may wander around the world,  
One does not find the sweetness of the Netherlands;  
You, fair girls, I would have to give up,  
This causes me the greatest sorrow.  
But now I will live by your side  
Giving my heart to the dearest little heart.  
I seek on Batavia's coast,  
For love and gentle rest,  
And gentle rest.

Hurray! There the anchor is lowered,  
They furl the rounded sails tight;  
Now generous happiness lives again,  
I see the pleasure on the face  
Of all the mates and sailors' eyes,  
Hurray! Now joy is reborn.  
I seek on Batavia's coast,  
For love and gentle rest,  
And gentle rest.

Now quickly the heavy boat into the water,  
Brisk men, fall out with the oars!  
Thus we sail through the clattering waves  
Into the harbor, with joyful ringing  
Of the clear Hurray! there greeting us,  
Already meeting us from Helvoet’s head;  
I seek on Batavia's coast,  
For love and gentle rest,  
And gentle rest.

Welcome, men, ashore!  
Here a fresh drink can be raised;  
Come, host, now pour full pitchers,  
The long journey is at an end:  
The distant voyage of four long years,  
Farewell now, swift keel and oars;  
I seek on Batavia's coast,  
For love and gentle rest,  
And gentle rest.

label: work_sea
translation:
Oh day! Oh long-awaited day,  
That many a person may long for,  
Our little ships are coming in;  
It is for Hellevoet or Rotterdam:  
That is where my love came home;  
I parted with spinning.  
I kicked the spinning wheel to one side,  
I would rather set it on fire;  
I kicked it with my feet  
I want to go see the boat!  
If my love comes out of there:  
How dearly I will greet him.  
Who do I hear knocking at my door,  
Are you the beloved servant,  
Welcome as you may be.  
How dear was the first letter,  
That I wrote to you my love,  
Have you read it carefully.  
Oh yes, he said, my dear pledge?  
The sweet little letter from your hand,  
That was for my pleasure,  
But when I received the last letter;  
From you my sweet love,  
I first understood the matters.  
Ah love, what are you thinking,  
Do you want to go out to sea again,  
Or do you want to go with my horses?  
Then pull in your sails quickly,  
Just do as your lovers wish:  
Then you won't have to sail.  
A woman is no longer a lover,  
She is no master of her honor,  
She may not love a young gentleman:  
But once the "yes" word has been said,  
Then the knot is tied,  
Which cannot be undone.

label: work_sea
translation:
In Vlissingen lies a ready boat,  
To sail to the East Indies,  
Upon which rests a Cupid,  
And plays on his gilded strings.  
O Cupid, little Venus’ weight:  
How do your gilded strings sound:  
They are curled in her lap,  
How fitting it is to sail there.  
The first maiden who asks about it
