Thinks she saw the dune shining,  
It was the Island of the Lady?  
With joy, this made my young heart sink.  
What kind of island can it be:  
What kind of island can it be,  
It was an Island of the Lady:  
Praised by lovers.  
The moon shone, the sun disappeared:  
It is the stars that light us,  
The mast in pieces, the spar in two:  
And still we did not want to yield.  
At the cape hear and understand  
Where the girls daily gather,  
Already in the house the Blue Cock,  
Where we daily converse wisely.  
When we then arrive in the Indies land:  
How lively we will act then:  
In the morning going ashore freely:  
Against the Black skirmishes.  
When we then come to the Fatherland  
How lively we will act then,  
In the morning with brandy  
And daily with good beers.  
Farewell, you girls from Utrecht all?  
And would well remember this little song:  
But we have not yet parted from here,  
A glass we first want to drink.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
I would make a last day:  
A Vision and Complement;  
But a Woman’s image said to me,  
Whether I was content with her;  
But I made my reverence,  
Took my stick and I went away,  
Now I see my dear doll,  
Am I in your way here.  

2.  
And I must still tell you,  
What has happened,  
And what tricks the Girls use,  
And I am not mistaken,  
That they like to transport young men,  
And lead us astray;  
But we will not disturb ourselves,  
After that highly raised fun.  

3.  
Well, what do you think dear Girls?  
Do you think I am very stupid?  
Do you think the Sailor’s girl,  
Grows like a wild Tree,  
[...] away with all your thoughts,  
You get no hearing from me,  
If you want to despise a Sailor,  
Then you have strayed from the right path.  

4.  
I will never seek girls  
Who greatly delight in pride,  
But I will investigate,  
What sweet love carries.  
I will praise all the girls,  
Who possess only one virtue,  
Those who are worth praising,  
And they are my goal and aim.  

5.  
See, I am embarking on one more journey,  
Yes, out on the wild Sea,  
There too many a Girl,  
Has also had her Beloved;  
For see the Sailor’s life,  
Her pleasure is on the Sea,  
Floating between Earth and Heaven,  
Until we arrive at the Reef.  

6.  
I would give myself the time,  
Then the very first Tribe?  
Adam drove a plough,  
Eve sat in garden toil,  
Clothes were given to her,  
And that from a sheep’s skin,  
Everyone must live contentedly,  
Then it is always well.

label: work_sea
translation:
I hear with joy the hollow drum,  
That proclaims to me: Go aboard,  
But oh, I have heard it more often;  
At first I was frightened by that rumble,  
But now it is a joyful drum to me:  
I step so contentedly from the shore,  
As if I were going to my wedding;  
Long live the sea!  
Long live the sea!  
I go with it until my death,  
I go with it until my death,  
One sees him mock danger;  
He is the flower of the Patriots:  
He remains faithful to his fatherland,  
Leaves his girl or his wife,  
The welfare of the country,  
The welfare of the country,  
Is a much dearer pledge to him,  
Is a much dearer pledge to him.  
She will one day crown you with laurels,  
Farewell, my dear Batavians!  
If I once return to you,  
One may perhaps find no tyranny anymore;  
God grant you peace!  
With this oath,  
I go calmly to sea,  
I go calmly to sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
Karsou,  
I have looked at you many times:  
But all your useless gestures  
Do not appeal to me,  
All your useless gestures  
Do not please me,  
Therefore I want to step  
Where I come from.  
A kiss  
Can make  
The whole night last,  
Then one does not hear quarrels:  
From our Officer,  
Thus we begin to stray,  
As drunk as a pig,  
Our ship moors at the stakes,  
We must be on board.

label: work_sea
translation:
Now I depart from Africa,
And the Cape of Good Hope,
And steer my course with joy,
To a land where I am called,
Where people speak as they think,
And fight for the Truth,
Where love and loyalty remain unbroken,
Until death do us part.
In an equal line,
The servant and maid equal to their master,
As long as they remain obedient,
The beggar still recognizes the master’s right!
There one judges on solid grounds.

label: work_sea
translation:
Must the ship Leiden now depart,
And leave the Cape River?
Oh, what sorrow it will cause;
For we must now go to sea,
Not understood by us humans,
We long for the Fatherland,
Where one has what one can wish for,
Better than on the Cape shore.
If one can follow the Moods of France,
So does everyone;
But if one cannot begin that,
Then one is not respected,
For they torment their minds,
Only with moods and splendor.

label: work_sea
translation:
All those who have the desire to sail  
In the service of the dear fatherland /  
For him, I stretch my tough strings  
And, like brothers, offer him my hand /  
I let myself be guided by Neptunis  
Be content and happy with me /  
I will never part from the Sea  
But sail until the end of my life / repeat.  
But sail until the end of my life repeat  
but sail until the end of my life repeat

label: work_sea
translation:
1  
Oh, what can a sailor hope for,  
When the wind of misfortune blows,  
For there is no more comfort open,  
Where love sails passionately,  
It is a life without living,  
It is constant painful dying,  
If two deer cling together,  
Parting must be deadly. (repeat)

2  
Unfortunately, I must part from you,  
Oh, what disgrace and what regret,  
Where we both,  
Love each other sincerely,  
I swear to you by my death and life,  
That I will never give my true faith,  
To another’s hand,  
Than to you my beautiful noblewoman. (repeat)

3  
Cease your lamenting, noble lover,  
Your faithfulness is known to me,  
Ultimately you will be the winner,  
If you remain steadfast,  
Separation is bitter indeed,  
From you my dearest pledge,  
But after suffering comes consolation,  
True love always endures. (repeat)

4  
Oh, who comforts me in my lament,  
Who understands my soul and pain,  
Who has mercy on my complaints,  
Since I was overwhelmed,  
Must I leave you,  
Oh my most beautiful soul mistress,  
Only life of my life,  
Dear object of my love. (repeat)

5  
Do not grieve, it cannot be otherwise,  
Your departure is firmly decided,  
Always show your faithfulness to her,  
Keep her love always close,  
Nothing will drive you away from her,  
Although everything goes against you,  
She will remain steadfast to you,  
Sail safely to the Japanese Land.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Oh, what can a seaman hope for!  
When the wind of misfortune blows,  
There is no comfort left,  
Since love sails passionately,  
It is a life without living,  
It is constant painful dying,  
Where two hearts cling together,  
The parting must be deadly.  

2.  
I must unfortunately part from you,  
Oh, what sorrow! Oh, what pain!  
Since we both love each other,  
In sincerity,  
I swear to you by my death and life,  
That I will never give my true loyalty,  
To another hand,  
Than to you my fair young woman.  

3.  
Cease your complaints, noble lover,  
Your loyalty is known to me,  
In the end you will be victorious,  
If you remain steadfast,  
It is better to part,  
From your dearest pledge,  
But after suffering comes joy,  
True love always endures.  

4.  
Ah! Who comforts me in my lament?  
Who touches my soul and pain?  
Who takes pity on my complaints?  
Since I was overwhelmed,  
I must leave you,  
Oh my dearest soulmate!  
Only life of my life,  
Precious object of my love.  

5.  
Do not grieve, it cannot be otherwise,  
Your faithfulness is firmly established,  
Then show your loyalty to this one,  
Remains unimpaired by her love,  
Nothing will drive you away from her,  
Though she opposes everything,  
She will remain steadfast to you,  
Sail calmly to another land.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Oh! what has happened to me,  
On my last voyage that I took,  
Every person would hesitate for that:  
If I were to share the story with him.  
Scarcely had we sailed,  
Three days out at sea,  
When a storm on the waves,  
Broke the main mast in two!  
The ship that traveled, the ship that sank,  
Finally got such a blow,  
That it went astray on a rock,  
And shattered into a thousand splinters.

2.  
Then it was: men! secure your luggage:  
Swimming was the very best,  
I too had to move like a duck,  
That was disturbed in its nest;  
But when I swam through the water,  
Oh! I will never forget that  
Almost a shark, with its snout,  
Bit me on the arm,  
Then I swam to the shore,  
And thus escaped the danger:  
Soon I climbed onto the land,  
And have walked on ever since.

3.  
What shall I do now?  
Wet and cold in this state,  
Stomach and wallet are empty inside,  
Oh! how do I get off the street?  
If a landlord would now lend me,  
And just serve on credit:  
Then I am free of worries,  
And I think of no sorrow,  
Oh, no trouble there,  
If I have spent the money,  
I bet he won’t be so angry,  
That he won’t give credit now.

4.  
I have not yet lost courage,  
Hey! courage! that is the way,  
Soft! the landlord might hear it,  
Who surely sang a bawdy song,  
Scram! do you want to leave here,  
Or I will cover you on your behind,  
With a broomstick:  
So that you do not look like a human.  
There’s the woman, oh! she is much softer,  
Blood! she hit me on the nose:  
I bet she calls me right away behind,  
Hey! there she already gives the signal.

label: work_sea
translation:
Tune: O sun! at Heaven’s trans.

My husband, my dearest friend,
Who loves me so sincerely,
Wrote me this letter from Texel:
My husband! my dearest friend!
You see yourself to the power
Of storm and sea protect;
I thank the Heavenly power.
Think how Doortjen waits for you,
She waits for you with open arms,
Think how Doortjen waits for you.

O pure marital love!
Bring him into my dwelling.
How his heart will beat,
O pure marital love!
When his eye discovers
How, within a few days,
His joy will still increase,
Come, Jacob! come ashore,
My only delight!
Come, Jacob! come ashore.

Ei stand faithful by me
And do not leave my side,
When the hour will come,
Ei stand faithful by me.
Hurry your arrival;
Hear me, the All-preserver,
Soon we see each other, dearest friend!
Of a beloved child
You the father and I the mother
Of a beloved child.

Who knocks, who knocks there?
I feel my heart beat so,
Ah Jacob! might you be the orphan;
He knocks, he knocks there,
He greets his travel companion,
Meanwhile we recover;
That joy compensates the pain.
I wait for him at this heart,
With pure love kisses.
My friend! fall upon this heart.

A.L.Pz.

label: work_sea
translation:
All who have the desire to sail  
In service of the dear fatherland,  
For them I stretch my tough strings,  
And rich as brothers I give them my hand,  
I let Neptunis guide me,  
Be content and happy, my friend,  
I will never part from the Sea,  
But sail until the end of my life, repeat.  
When one then arrives in foreign lands,  
There a Sailor is highly esteemed,  
Both by high and low ranks,  
Much more than the countryman ever thought.  
I let Neptunis guide me,  
Be content and happy, my friend,  
I will never part from the Sea,  
But sail until the end of my life, repeat.

label: work_sea
translation:
What a joy has been born to us,  
In our dear Fatherland,  
Now that Peace is being heard of  
Between us and England,  
Who have already determined our freedom,  
To be delivered from the fire of war,  
We shout vivat Colonials,  
We are Hunters of pleasure.

2.  
One is already taken,  
With thoughts of departure,  
To achieve our aim,  
Of going east and west,  
To the island of Suriname,  
Or to the Cape of Good Hope,  
And then to Demerara,  
And also to Essequibo.

3.  
Oh! How one shall flourish there,  
In the land where Sugar grows,  
Apple and Cinnamon down below,  
Nobles and Figs in abundance,  
Coffee, Tea, all that has a name,  
And Cotton for fine clothes,  
We shout vivat Suriname,  
If only we could be there once.

4.  
How they will delight us,  
The black Girls of that Land,  
They are playful and full of love,  
For a white man from Holland;  
They are beautiful of limbs,  
Fine of hair and soft of skin,  
And well shaped underneath,  
Their breasts are quite large.

5.  
One will feel no cold there,  
For the winter does no harm there,  
It is always full summer,  
Lovely and delicate,  
What joy one will experience there,  
In that warm and dear Land,  
Where Heaven gives its blessing,  
Also granted on every side.

6.  
Then let us part with happiness,  
From our Parents and Fatherland,  
And from all dear Girls,  
Whom we leave behind in Holland,  
Adieu then Fatherland and friends,  
Adieu Girls much esteemed,  
We hope to find you again,  
Maybe in about eight years.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Ah, what can a sailor hope for,  
When the wind of misfortune blows,  
Then there is no more comfort,  
As love sails fiercely,  
It is a life without living,  
It is steady dying pain,  
Where two hearts cling together,  
Parting must be deadly.  

2.  
Alas, I must part from you,  
Oh what disgrace, oh what pain,  
When we both love each other,  
In sincerity,  
I swear to you by my death and life,  
That I will never give my true loyalty,  
To another hand,  
Than to you, my fair maiden.  

3.  
Cease your complaints, noble lover,  
Your loyalty is known to me,  
Finally you will be victorious,  
If you remain steadfast,  
It is better, then, to part,  
From you, the dearest pledge,  
But after suffering comes joy,  
True love always endures.  

4.  
Ah, who comforts me in my lament,  
Who touches my soul and pain,  
Who has mercy on my complaints,  
When I have been overwhelmed,  
Must I leave you,  
Oh my fairest soulmate,  
Only life of my life,  
Precious object of my love.  

5.  
Do not grieve, it cannot be otherwise,  
Your loyalty is firmly established,  
Show your faithfulness then to her,  
She remains silent through her love,  
Nothing will drive you from her,  
Though she opposes everything,  
She will remain steadfast to you,  
Sail calmly to another land.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
What dreadful crying  
Now sounds in my ears.  
What fright startled me  
When I just came to hear this.  
Is then, mighty God!  
The one who tames wind and waves,  
Is then the tragic fate  
Destined for so many people.

2.  
I saw, my heart still tears,  
Amidst towering waves,  
A ship distressed by storm,  
In a thousand dangers.  
It is the Vreede, of which  
I observe a scene of disaster,  
On which so many men  
Were torn away from their wives.

3.  
I immediately discovered this  
By the drifting of many barrels,  
Tossed upon the ground,  
Where no help could avail,  
A beautiful young woman  
I saw thrown onto the shore,  
Completely naked, stiffened by cold,  
Overwhelmed by waves.

4.  
Another soon had to share  
The same fate.  
Tremble before the arm of God,  
His judgment strikes many.  
Many goods, weapons,  
I saw drifting ashore.  
O mankind! fear, and bow  
Before the Lord’s hand.

5.  
It was a Dutch ship,  
As far as I first discovered,  
Which shortly laid its course  
To the Indies.  
Oh yes, it is the ship Vreê  
What judgment from God’s hand,  
What dreadful lament and woe  
Fill my Fatherland.

6.  
The ship was shortly after  
A wreck, entirely shattered.  
The entire ship’s crew was  
Consumed in this storm.  
I counted corpse after corpse,  
Up to twenty-one.  
O Heaven, what a sign  
Of your scourging rod.

7.  
The mast fell overboard,  
Whereby eight souls  
Drowned in the ocean,  
With help entirely denied  
To those who on the deck  
Faithfully and courageously  
Had hastened to row out,  
Snatched away by the flood.

8.  
Well over a hundred in number  
Still clung to life,  
And hoped that the wreck  
Would have drifted ashore.  
But alas, a high sea  
Soon took that courage away,  
And suddenly carried them off  
To the abyss in the flood.

9.  
Many officers, who faithfully  
Honor their oath and duty.  
Many children, men, and women,  
I saw turning in the waves,  
All washed ashore,  
A girl, eight months old,  
Pressed in her mother’s arm,  
Torn from her protection.

10.  
Only eighteen souls  
Were saved in total,  
The others became the share  
Of those overtaken by fate.  
One hundred and twenty-five men  
Found their death in the sea,  
Whom one cannot save  
From storm and great distress.

11.  
Learn, learn from the sorrowful sight  
That must strike your soul,  
Learn from this sad message  
To recognize God’s greatness.  
His power, His thunderous word  
Breaks forth with a great sound,  
He brings forth the storm wind,  
Which nothing in its rage restrains.

12.  
Preserve our Fatherland  
From well-deserved plagues.  
Let your Almighty hand  
Carry both ship and crew.  
Guide you the keels forward  
Along a tamed sea,  
So that from another land  
They bring us riches.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Peace then calls us aboard,  
Where Dutch pennants flutter;  
It again steers the ship’s rudder  
To distant shores.  
O blessed peace!  
You safely guide us from this course  
Along the currents;  
Your gentle hand  
Leads us from land,  
Will bring us as wished  
To the fertile Cape shore.  

2.  
Come, companions! Be joyful,  
Let us live in unity;  
We must, through generous joy,  
Give each other pleasure.  
No care, sorrow, nor pain  
Are fellow travelers here.  
No! Let us sing happily,  
And praise  
The merchant fleet,  
A source of blessings  
For the trading company.  

3.  
May it, after so much suffering and hardship,  
See its prosperity bloom again!  
May it, at the height of happiness,  
See its treasures grow again!  
Let us, as loyal allies,  
Faithfully offer it support,  
And stand bravely against  
Anyone who disturbs it,  
Driven by envy;  
For from its welfare and blessings  
Flows our prosperity.  

4.  
O dear girls! Do not weep so!  
Do not let farewell trouble you:  
Hope that you will see us again one day,  
As leading gentlemen;  
O blessed peace!  
You safely guide us from this course  
Along the currents;  
Your gentle hand  
Leads us from land,  
Will bring us as wished  
To the fertile Cape shore.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Now the anchor is laid,  
And the sails are set,  
With the favorable wind  
We go to sea.  
We fear neither disaster nor adversity,  
When the heavens' favor  
Always watches over us on the open flood,  
Unless a storm overtakes us.

2.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
We are brought to the place  
Where the Good Hope awaits us,  
Who, on her pleasant shore,  
Will welcome us well.

3.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
What joy appears on the face  
Of both sailor and soldier,  
While each sees their wishes fulfilled  
By soon setting out on a journey.

4.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
Soon we leave, reassured,  
The dear fatherland coast,  
In hope of, as prosperous people,  
Seeing it once more.

5.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
No war trumpet calls our fleet  
To resist the country’s great distress;  
But peace, which charms every heart,  
Drives our keels onward.

6.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
But if the dear fatherland  
Should ever, unexpectedly, be attacked,  
Then we serve it, with loyalty and courage,  
At the cost of our blood.

7.  
Now the anchor is laid, etc.  
Farewell, oh fatherland soil,  
May your prosperity grow from moment to moment!  
We shall see, upon our return,  
Your utmost happiness again!  
Now the anchor is laid,  
And the sails are set,  
With the favorable wind  
We go to sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Oh, how beautifully we see from the horizons,  
The morning sun climbing with splendor!  
It illuminates, with radiant shine,  
The surface of the sea and the sky’s expanse.       (repeat)

2.  
It will, along the calm waves,  
Quickly clear the faint horizon;  
Everything the eye meets  
It adorns with its purple glow.       (repeat)

3.  
Far from green arbors,  
One hears no birds singing here;  
But the gentle rustle of the winds  
Caresses our ear, by the sea’s roar.       (repeat)

4.  
In these uninhabited regions  
One now hears a sign of joy:  
The ship’s song sounds on all sides,  
From the deck, or from the side.       (repeat)

5.  
Blue mountains, green edges,  
Are seen alternately along the edges;  
But, where all escapes the eye,  
One sees nothing but sea and sky.       (repeat)

6.  
Cheerful, men! Be of good peace!  
We fear no hardships:  
By his skill and deliberation  
The helmsman shows us the way.       (repeat)

7.  
He who wishes to land safely  
Will willingly not let us beach,  
But travels freely, by his compass,  
Along the wide expanse of water.

8.  
If he is faithful in his duties,  
Then we also perform ours:  
Brave warriors are always  
Devoted to their oath and duty.       (repeat)

9.  
Yes! If we behave bravely,  
Then our days of salvation  
Will shine just as beautifully as the morning light  
Now rises before our sight.       (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Bright Moon, you spread your rays,  
Across the calm arch of the sky;  
You now silver the waves,  
And your glow lights our eyes:  
It glides along the white sails,  
Shines on bow, rudder, and hull;  
It appears, at twilight,  
Dimly on the distant shore.

2.  
By the soft lapping of the waves,  
And the rustling of the wind,  
We sail, freed from worry,  
While neither storm nor rocks are found.  
Oh, how lovely is this evening!  
Oh, how peaceful is nature!  
In this favorable, clear weather  
The journey appears short to us.

3.  
The farmer tastes true pleasure  
On his fertile arable land;  
What pleasure does the sailor taste  
In a calm evening hour!  
One tills his quiet field,  
The other plows the wild sea  
Both are now secured,  
By the delight of sweet peace.

4.  
Sweet peace! Yes, through your blessing  
This merchant fleet sails again;  
And no raiders' war thunder  
Brings the keels into distress anymore;  
It is now friend, instead of foe,  
Whom one meets everywhere;  
The Dutch flag, hoisted at the mast,  
Again proudly flies along the flood.

5.  
Peaceful evening; through your radiance  
You are the likeness of peace,  
Who also, through her quiet radiance,  
Soothes every eye and mind.  
May her blessed power  
Last long, yes, forever!  
Thus may the prosperity  
Of the Batavian seafaring steadily increase.

label: work_sea
translation:
Friends, come listen to this song,  
Which briefly tells what happened once more.       (refrain)  
Of a girl twenty years old,  
Who intended to sail,  
Because her love was a captain,  
She wanted to be a little sailor.       (refrain)  

2. It was in the city of Amsterdam,  
Where she came to a slave trader,       (refrain)  
There she took service for seven years,  
And they feared no dangers,  
To climb the mast,  
To secure the sails and ropes tightly.       (refrain)  

3. She went aboard with her trunk,  
As a good sailor ought,       (refrain)  
And they went sailing at sea,  
A few miles on the bay;  
Then the weather surprised her,  
So she had to climb up the mast.       (refrain)  

4. But due to the storms and bad weather,  
The sails went back and forth,       (refrain)  
And then she was in danger,  
Because she could no longer manage it,  
And she stepped on the yardarm,  
That she fell down from above.       (refrain)  

5. Immediately the captain spoke,  
As if she must be whipped,       (refrain)  
She was tied to the mast,  
They bound her hands and feet,  
And then she had to stay standing,  
Until a quartermaster would strike her.       (refrain)  

6. Immediately she called, "Lord Captain,  
May I speak a word alone to you,"       (refrain)  
She said, "My dear, do not hit me,  
For I am your love as you see,"  
Immediately she was released,  
And with a kiss he received her.       (refrain)  

7. Oh, leave girl, whoever you are,  
Who is courted by a young man,       (refrain)  
Become a sailor or a good soldier,  
So that you never leave your love,  
For before the little ship reached the shore,  
There was already a young sailor.       (refrain)

label: work_sea
translation:
Farewell my dear Soul friend, farewell!  
I strive into the sparkling sea, farewell?  
I leave the Fatherland’s shore,  
The voyage demands my help and hand,  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

Should I, who since my tender Youth, farewell!  
Upon the wave’s wet my greatest joy, farewell!  
And my absolute desire found  
No anchor after that joyful state.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

Once kissed again, farewell my Bride, farewell!  
Your Lover sails out from Texel, farewell!  
And will return soon, nearly sooner,  
With ringing Rijksdaalders again.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

I live or die for God mercuur, farewell?  
Trade calls me at this hour, farewell!  
I soon sail to the murderous coast  
The voyage is all my delight.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

Dry your salty tears, farewell!  
God who gave us the voyage, farewell!  
Will protect it with His hand  
Thus the beloved Fatherland blooms  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

Behave now as a Heroine, farewell!  
Remember our pure love, farewell!  
I will return as your Bridegroom  
Soon with Gold and honor again.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

Through voyage grows the free State, farewell!  
Who is there who hates the voyage, farewell?  
I sail in the service of the Fatherland  
Receive my heart and hand in loyalty.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.  

No note, no thundering sea battle, farewell!  
No high and fierce agitated bear, farewell!  
Discourages the heart of your Friend  
Who loves you until his death.  
Farewell, farewell, farewell.

label: work_sea
translation:
My husband, my best friend,  
Who loves me so dearly,  
Wrote me this letter from Terschelling:  
My husband! my best friend;  
You say you want to protect yourself  
From the power of storm and sea;  
I thank the heavenly power,  
Thinking of how Doortjen waits for you,  
She waits with open arms,  
Thinking of how Doortjen waits for you,  
Oh, pure marital love!  
Carry him into my dwelling,  
How his heart will beat,  
Oh, pure marital love!  
When his eye discovers,  
How, within a few days,  
His joy will still increase,  
Come, Jacob! come ashore,  
My only delight!  
Come, Jacob! come ashore.  
Ah, stand faithfully by me,  
And don’t leave my side,  
When the hour comes,  
Ah, stand faithfully by me,  
Then hasten your arrival;  
The Almighty hears me,  
Soon we will see each other, dearest friend;  
Of a lovely child,  
You father and I mother  
Of a lovely child,  
Who knocks, who knocks there,  
I feel my heart beat so,  
Ah, Jacob! if only you were here:  
He knocks, he knocks there,  
He greets his travel companion,  
Meanwhile, let us recover:  
That joy compensates the pain.  
I wait for him at this heart,  
With pure love kisses.  
My friend! fall upon this heart.

label: work_sea
translation:
Friends come to listen eagerly,  
And listen to this wonderful song,  
What I present to you first,  
These are girls from the neighborhood,  
They cross back and forth in the evening,  
And then again here, and then my lord,  
Come, you all, come inside once,  
Come, you all, come to me once.  

The little sailor says, where to?  
You have nothing to sell, sell, sell,  
Are you then all alone?  
That is no hope for me,  
Come rest here by me,  
Then I'll get some more, more, more,  
Come, just sit down here,  
Come, just sit down here.  

I sat there quite a few hours,  
The girl approached,  
With two or three others,  
She said, "Are those then?"  
Choose as you like,  
Then I will go in with you, in, in!  
For I cannot live with you,  
For I cannot be with you.  

He chose a girl  
Who pleased him as he wished!  
But he really wanted a journey,  
Once stepping into love,  
And she was afraid of that,  
Because she got him as a husband,  
She thought to herself,  
He is my lamb for a while.  

They married there together,  
Very quickly all in bed, bed, bed,  
He was quite skilled too,  
He did it just so,  
For nine months later, beautiful,  
That child grew quite well,  
He gave her this as a reward.

label: work_sea
translation:
Haju, then dear soul, I must go aboard,  
Haju, for the last time, I hear the drum beating,  
Ah! tender beloved treasure, what has come over me!  
Haju, my comfort, my Love, my desire, my life,  
My heart is very disturbed, how will it calm down again?  
But ah! how will it go, when I am away from you?  
And my soul, sorrowfully tortured by sorrow,          (repeat)  
Must alas! cross the wild sea,                            (repeat)  
Remain my faithful one, remember our dear vows,  
Which you seriously made to me,  
Then accept no hand or heart from anyone,              (repeat)  
But want to give your love only to me.                 (repeat)  
The sea will take me away, devour me by its waves,  
If I should ever be unfaithful to you too,  
No, dear treasure, you are and remain mine,            (repeat)  
I will keep my love also undefiled,                     (repeat)  
The pain that I feel, I cannot describe,  
My heart is breaking, Haju, I cannot go on,  
Haju, then dear soul; Haju, I embrace you tenderly,   (repeat)  
I will always remain your faithful lover.            (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
How joyful it is to sail at sea,
If only God is our helmsman,
Then we do not fear wind or waves,
Yes, rocks and banks we then avoid,
Why should the seaman be afraid,
The sea is nothing but sorrow,
We have safely returned,
All previous sorrow is gone,
So the sea is not so bad.
Then you will be my true wife,
And remain faithful as long as I live.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Last day I was going to make,  
The Vision and Compliment,  
But a woman figure said to me,  
If I was content with her:  
But I made my reverence,  
Took my little stick and I went away,  
Thus said I my dear doll,  
Am I in your way here.

2.  
And I still have to tell you,  
What has happened,  
And what tricks the girls use,  
And I am not mistaken,  
That they like to transport young men,  
And lead us on the way;  
But we will not disturb ourselves,  
After that highly raised fun.

3.  
Well, what do you think dear girls?  
Do you think I am very stupid?  
Do you think the sailor's girl’s flesh,  
Grows like a wild tree?  
No, away with all your thoughts,  
From me you get no hearing,  
If you want to despise a sailor,  
Then you stray from the right path.

4.  
I will never seek a girl,  
Who greatly enjoys pride:  
But I will examine,  
What sweet love carries:  
I will praise all the girls,  
Who possess only honor and virtue,  
Those who are worthy of praise,  
And those are my goal and aim.

5.  
See, I still make a journey,  
Yes, on the wild Sea,  
There many a girl has also,  
Her beloved also with her:  
Because see the sailor’s life,  
Her pleasure is on the Sea.  
Floating between Earth and Heaven,  
Until we arrive at the Sea.

6.  
I want to dedicate my time,  
From the very first tribe!  
Adam drove a plow,  
Eva sat in the Garden and Spin,  
Clothes that were given to her,  
And that from a sheep's skin,  
Everyone must live contentedly,  
Then it is always well.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
How happy the Countryman surely lives,  
While the Seaman is a slave to it,  
Storm and thunderstorm, and dark nights,  
What does the Seaman not have to endure,  
While the Countryman sleeps quietly and peacefully,  
He joyfully kisses his Silvia.  

2.  
Well Countryman, how boldly you speak?  
While the Seaman sees all lands,  
Storm and thunderstorm, and dark nights,  
What does the Seaman not have to endure,  
While the Countryman sleeps quietly and peacefully,  
He joyfully kisses his Silvia.  

3.  
If then a shock or a shot breaks out,  
The Seaman is almost immediately in distress again,  
Storm and thunderstorm, and dark nights,  
What does the Seaman not have to endure,  
While the Countryman sleeps quietly and peacefully,  
He joyfully kisses his Silvia.  

4.  
Fine pearls and diamonds,  
They all come from the East Indian lands,  
Fine chits and cotton,  
It is meant to please the girls,  
They also bring treasures,  
From the East and West Indian Seas.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
Oh! What has happened to me,  
On my last voyage that I made?  
Every person would be ashamed of it:  
If I were to share my story with him.  
Scarcely had we set sail,  
Three days out at sea,  
When a thunderstorm on the waves,  
Broke the main mast in two!  
The ship that traveled, the ship that sank,  
Finally took such a blow,  
That it ran aground on a rock,  
And broke into a thousand splinters.  

2.  
Then it was: men! save your lice:  
Swimming was the very best,  
I too had to move like a duck,  
Disturbed in its nest:  
But when I swam through the water,  
Oh! I will never forget that  
Almost a shark, with its snout,  
Had bitten me in the arm.  
I then swam to the shore,  
And thus escaped the danger:  
Soon I climbed onto the land,  
And have continued on up to now.  

3.  
What shall I do now,  
Wet and cold in this state?  
Stomach and purse are thin from within,  
Oh! how do I get off the street?  
If such a landlord now would vouch for me,  
And give me credit:  
Then I would be free of worries,  
And I would think of no sorrow.  
How, no difficulty for this,  
Have I spent the money,  
I bet he will not be so angry,  
That he will not now give credit.  

4.  
I have not yet lost courage,  
Hey, courage! that is the way,  
Softly, the landlord might hear it,  
Who surely sang a bawdy song,  
Scram! wish to leave here,  
Or I will cover you on your backside,  
With a broomstick:  
That you do not look like a human!  
There is the woman, oh she is much gentler:  
Damn! she has me in the nose:  
I bet she calls me at once back,  
Hey! there she already gives the signal.

label: work_sea
translation:
Is a king truly seated  
At his table to eat obediently,  
We are the ones  
Who bring it, so I believe.  
We are the ones willing to risk body and life,  
Bringing it all  
To the shore,  
To his pleasure and satisfaction.  
With permission  
What is a court  
Do they not bring everything there, eh?  
Whatever a person can think of or name in haste,  
Should one then  
Man by man  
Not boast about our deeds,  
Where one must  
On the tide  
Bravely taste both sour and sweet.  
Though the winds blow easterly  
Or they fall from the south  
Or are heard  
In the West or North,  
We know how to trim our sails there  
And with one  
On the reins  
Look out towards the beacons  
Exactly so,  
That our ship  
May not strike a rock.

label: work_sea
translation:
Who died the greatest heroic death? van Speijk,  
Who indeed has spilled Dutch blood? van Speijk,  
Who performed that great deed,  
For which a monument of honor is erected? van Speijk. (repeat)

Who is equal to van Speijk in courage? Hobein.  
Who gave a striking example of this? Hobein.  
Who once saved the beloved Dutch flag,  
While he feared neither flood nor fire? Hobein. (repeat)

Which people deserve such heroes? It is us.  
Who rally so around their King? It is us.  
Who, no matter the slander scolding and maligning,  
Are admired throughout all of Europe? It is us. (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
Farewell then, parents, always well,  
I must go aboard,  
I have hired myself as a ship’s mate,  
And now I undertake the voyage,  
I am heading for Batavia,  
Seeking safe harbor there;  
If I return early or late,  
I will bring you something!  (repeat)

Fare well my son, always fare well,  
God is always with you,  
He remains your faithful traveling companion;  
Constantly by your side;  
When the rolling waves rock your ship,  
Swaying back and forth,  
If it should be cast upon a rock or reef,  
Steadfastly trust in your Lord!  (repeat)

If wind or storm may strike,  
Your wooden clogs on the sea,  
Fix your steady gaze on Him:  
Until you reach safe harbor,  
After enduring fearful nights;  
He has searched out for you,  
And happily the land will then smile on you,  
After this great journey!  (repeat)

And when the cargo is unloaded,  
Your ship has obtained and dried out new freight,  
The voyage again sweet and gentle,  
Set sail, toward the dear fatherland,  
So let courage remain with you,  
The God who stood at my right hand,  
Is now also with me!  (repeat)

My father, that I never forget,  
The lesson you gave me;  
God is faithful in love and suffering,  
Faithful until the grave,

Then sail with God’s help,  
The great ocean,  
He, the disposer of our fate,  
Will then watch over you!  (repeat)

Once again then, dear mother,  
Farewell until death,  
My father, you remain always her husband,  
Do not leave her, in distress,  
And if God will let my effort,  
Flourish with joy:  
Then my work will not stand still for you,  
That rejoices my whole soul!  (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
Hooray! I am going to sail the Ocean  
With haste,  
And let Holland’s old banner  
Always flutter on the flood,  
There where the Rider, Tromp, Piet Hein,  
Van Galen may be their glory,  
There I hope, my dear girl,  
To once see if my luck endures there. (repeat)  

The vessel is already ready,  
And tomorrow sets sail,  
Do you sometimes feel heartache?  
I pray you stop your weeping,  
It is for the prosperity of the country,  
That we to the farthest shore,  
Ensure peace and true tranquillity,  
Come, dear girl, we are going to sea. (repeat)  

To sea, it is on the saltwater,  
Where a three-day battle,  
The Brit and French lost their compass,  
And course of their policy.  
Where Zoutman, proud with lion’s courage,  
Contested the Brit on the flood,  
At Dogger Bank fought the peace,  
Come, dear girl, we are going to sea. (repeat)  

Imagine how the Silver Fleet,  
Won by Piet Hein,  
Was brought within Holland’s lap,  
Such a boast I want to be;  
And where Capello forced Algiers’ wall,  
To peace with cracking fire,  
Gave freedom to the slaves who took it,  
Come, dear girl, we are going to sea. (repeat)  

The seed of our perfect happiness,  
That is what I strive for,  
Nothing torments me, nothing weighs me down,  
I go joyfully, though weary,  
And though sometimes storms or thunder roar,  
I do not fear, my breast glows;  
Forward, everyone’s happiness in village or town,  
Come, dear girl, we are going to sea. (repeat)  

And if I return, I swear faithfully,  
My word and honor pledged,  
That you will be my true wife,  
Hereupon I give you my hand,  
Hooray! this life offers me gold,  
If virtue is then entrusted to me,  
Live then pleasantly and in peace,  
Farewell for the last time, we are going to sea. (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
There was recently a girl unmarried,  
Who wanted to sail, who wanted to sail,  
There was recently a girl unmarried,  
Who wanted to sail as a sailor.

She enlisted for seven years,  
Because she feared, because she feared,  
She enlisted for seven years,  
Because she feared no danger.

Then she brought her belongings on board,  
Just as a good, just as a good,  
Then she brought her belongings on board,  
As a good sailor ought to.

She had to climb the mast,  
Make the sails, make the sails,  
She had to climb the mast,  
Make the sails and tie the ropes.

But through the stormy weather,  
Those sails fell, those sails fell,  
But through the stormy weather,  
Those sails fell down from above.

Now she was tied to the mast  
With her hands, with her hands,  
Now she was tied to the mast,  
With her hands and feet fastened.

She cried: Captain! don’t hit me,  
I am your darling, I am your darling,  
She cried: Captain! don’t hit me,  
I am your darling, as you see.

But before the ship reached shore,  
There was the young, there was the young,  
But before the ship reached shore,  
The young sailor girl was already gone.

When she then came back to the city,  
Where she still had one, where she still had one,  
When she then came back to the city,  
Where she still had a mother.

She cried: Mother! don’t be angry,  
I have sailed, I have sailed,  
She cried: Mother! don’t be angry,  
I have sailed as a young sailor.

With one who loves me truly,  
I have this little, I have this little,  
With one who loves me truly,  
I have this small innocent child.

But before Pentecost returns,  
I will be his wife, I will be his wife,  
But before Pentecost returns,  
I will be his wife, that is certain.

label: work_sea
translation:
The rose was still covered with dew,
Like balm scents on the flowered herb,
When William, moved by deep sorrow,
Brought his last farewell to his dear bride,
Farewell, now dear years, that grieved so sore,
Though love drives me back to the land,
As my heart remains my faithful love
Until death takes me from this time.

At a certain time, nearly twenty years ago,
I joyfully boarded my slender fishing boat,
Cutting through the vast heaven-high waves,
A storm arose threatening death.
Oh cry for help, oh last distress of life,
That moves the heart of the poor sailor,
Are you given in vain to others,
Who once carry the true heart of a seaman to you?

I saw on a keel, battered by the waves,
A man, prey to the raging element,
And prayed to God to make a way for him,
Held on, weary, bound by the force,
Soon courage replaced the cries of despair,
Glowing with thanks I held on beside the sailor,
And suddenly saw an infant in his arms,
This child, friend! the saved child was you.

He goes aboard, his heart recovered again,
The ship speeds away in bird-like flight,
Evening falls with more watered streams,
The lightning falls flying from the sky.
Forgive, forgive, I am on the wild waves,
Here be my grave for what I decide,
When William, moved by deep sorrow,
Brought his last farewell to his dear bride.

It was one evening, what did I then see drifting?
It was a piece of a broken ship,
Where William, my tender love,
His name arose with dreadful fright.
Then I cried out, will you find death here?
Then there is also a firm decision for me,
Thus I will then destroy myself here,
And pray to God my last echo forth.

O William! you were my dear beloved,
For you alone my heart was always ready,
O William! where shall I find you again,
Only with God there are we rejoiced.
Farewell, farewell, now dear earth,
My love is just, God knows that,
For twenty years a mother who bore me,
Think once more, surely it is my fate.

label: work_sea
translation:
Oh, mother! Why have you driven me from your side?  
To the ship's crew so rough on the wild element,  
Who care nothing for a mother's sorrow or a child's tears,  
But mocking as my foot cautiously turns toward them,  
You, mother! taught me to be patient in sorrow,  
Your loving voice made me meek,  
And under your care, I had nothing to fear,  
Tell me, mother! tell me, what harm did you do your child.

We had a simple meal in our humble home,  
But by your hand prepared, it tasted to me like ambrosia,  
Sitting on your lap, happy as a king,  
Love was with us, and water tasted like wine,  
But here no gentle feeling, no human compassion,  
Only curses on the child who looks on in fear,  
No motherly hand to wipe away tears,  
Tell me, mother! tell me, why do you neglect your child.

But what a wild uproar again comes to disturb me,  
A gust of storm flies through the rigging that will destroy everything,  
I hear the sharp whistle, it sounds dreadful in my ears,  
The captain's order calls thunderous all around!  
People whisper anxiously, I hear the masts creaking,  
The waves boil fiercely, the storm muffles every cry,  
The sky is nothing but fire. But God wants to watch over us,  
Tell me, mother! tell me, what harm did you do your child.

label: work_sea
translation:
The sailor's life knows no worries,  
He washes them away in the sea foam,  
Come, boys! then drink, for already tomorrow,  
We will set sail again from the roadstead.  
Poured out,  
Let us drink,  
To the merry sailor's song,  
Let the cups cheerfully clink,  
God does not abandon the sailor  
Empty the glass.

Separated from beloved places,  
When melancholy sometimes makes us mourn,  
Hope strengthens us, that upon landing,  
The reunion sweetens all our sorrow.  
Poured out, etc.

In East and West, in South and North,  
In every region of the Ocean,  
Our courage brings in all places  
Trade's rich treasures.  
Poured out, etc.

Even if the sailor must wander for months,  
On the boundless, great sea,  
Whatever he may lose on the waves,  
He carries hope as his anchor.  
Poured out, etc.

If a storm threatens us, or wild cliffs,  
Death grins at us in the distance,  
Still the prayer flows from our lips,  
O God, do not abandon the sailor.  
Poured out, etc.

label: work_sea
translation:
Who wants to hear a new song?  
Listen, I will explain it to you  
about an East Indies sailor and his love,  
how they were both in sorrow.  
“Sweet love, when you sail to the East Indies,  
how long will the journey last?  
Might I see you again after a year,  
and have such pleasant adventures?”  
“Sweet love, that cannot be:  
set me aside for two and a half years,  
and steadily bear love for me  
and look out to sea!”  
“Even if I look out to sea,  
I may not see or speak to you.  
If you carry your purse full of dollars,  
will you then also forget me?”  
“Dear love, do you really trust me with that?  
Would I do like the unfaithful?  
I have no one dearer on earth than you,  
hey! the flower of fair young women!”  
He who has composed this new song,  
he has sung it so well;  
he wrote it all about his love  
and defies all envious tongues.

label: work_sea
translation:
The cheer is raised,
Thunderously rolling along the shore,
Good and blood and dear life,
We dedicate to the fatherland;
Let it sound across the waves,
Hail to Fatherland and King,
Though sea foam splashes in our hair,
Dutch hearts glow in the chest.
Brave sons of the sea!
Sing your songs while sailing,
All the brave sing along. refrain.

2. Our brothers bear the weapon,
Uphold Holland’s ancient authority,
We are made for the sea,
And protect our flag;
The steel is sharpened on shore,
That withstands force and deceit,
It is the thunder of our ships,
That strikes the hearts of the wicked;
Brave sons of the sea!
Boldly lit the fuses,
All the brave fight along. refrain.

3. On the banks of the Scheldt,
A heavy toll was paid,
You, Van Speyk! made Holland count,
Defying a shameful superior force;
Gloriously the cry was heard,
‘Better to die as spoils,
Than to lose flag and honor,
And you put out the fire in the powder.’
Brave sons of the sea!
If this fate is also ours,
All the brave die along. refrain.

4. Yes, by God! we all swear this,
However treason may bend its waves,
Fighting, we may fall once,
We will never strike the colors.
Let it sound across the waves,
For a future fellow countryman,
That there once were seamen,
Who did not fear death;
Brave sons of the sea!
Sing a Dutch song while sailing,
All the brave sing along. refrain.

label: work_sea
translation:
The cables loose, the sails up, That's how you sail! Although we were gentlemen ashore, Our hearts lay in the waves; A Dutch child, that is known, Finds his element in the sea, Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, Finds his element in the sea.  
And we are no longer bums, As in days gone by, When everyone for the East India Company Had struck a flicker; Yet we sail on every sea Just as well as the best, Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, Just as well as the best.  
Hurrah then, boys! to the rigging! Hands out of the sleeves! Let the Germans, Norwegians, and Englishmen Not climb your ropes: Then you can sail without a course, Even if the nigger blows in the sail, Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, Even if the nigger blows in the sail.

label: work_sea
translation:
Sturdy boys, lively lads,  
Shame, how dull you stand there,  
Are you not well made,  
Are you not ready by six?  
Be ashamed, boys, come along,  
To the sea, oh to the sea.

That is a life full of pleasures,  
That is a life of firmness;  
To roam around the world,  
On the deck or in the mast;  
To be at home on every roadstead,  
Come along, come to the sea.

Let them drizzle, let them sulk,  
Let them wander on the land,  
Hide in factory smoke,  
Or also dig in the sand,  
We for ourselves, we don't join in,  
Our choice is the sea!

Free and cheerful to roam,  
On the wide ocean,  
Hoisting sails, reefing sails,  
Boys, don’t you like that?  
Come then, hurry and come along,  
The best life is at sea.

What, what would our Netherlands be,  
What trade without the sea;  
Everything has risen from the sea,  
The ship brings its treasures,  
From the East and West Roadsteads,  
The flourishing of the Netherlands is through the sea.  
Come then sturdy, quick lads,  
Let us enlist now quickly,  
For the sea we are made,  
Come now swiftly aboard.  
The ship is already waiting for us on the roadstead,  
Our happiness is at sea.

label: work_sea
translation:
Farewell my heart’s friend,  
The wind is east, I must go to sea,  
I will always love you,  
Just give me your portrait,  
And then a sip will ease my mind,  
That will make everything go well,  
For the tap must remain open, ) repeat.  
When I’m dead, everything is over. ) repeat.

Always be wiser,  
Oh, always keep your senses,  
You have dangers to fear,  
Besides, you are going to a strange land,  
So never let yourself be tempted,  
Never go to get drunk,  
Keep the tap open, ) repeat.  
Then it will soon be over for you. ) repeat.

Don’t be afraid of Schiedam liquor,  
I know too much, that’s not good,  
Don’t be worried,  
Do as I do, have good courage,  
Everywhere, there must be a bit,  
It can’t be that dry after all.  
Keep the tap open, ) repeat.  
If I am dead, everything is over. ) repeat.

Constantly you speak frivolously,  
You think of your Cornelia,  
Oh, I love you deeply.  
If you once fell out of the window,  
Or if you, with the black girls,  
Might sometimes forget me,  
Because the tap is left open. ) repeat.  
Our love would always be over. ) repeat.

Dear child, don’t think like that,  
I remain entirely faithful to you,  
Time will beckon us,  
That you will become my wife,  
Then the nectar will flow for us,  
And we will go singing together,  
Keep the tap open. ) repeat.  
When we are dead, everything is over. ) repeat.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.

Have you heard of the Silver Fleet,  
The Silver Fleet of Spain?  
It had many Spanish rats on board  
And little oranges!  
chorus.  
Piet Hein, Piet Hein,  
His name is small,  
His deeds are great:  
He captured the Silver Fleet!

2.

Didn’t Piet Hein say then, with a proud word:  
"Well, Boys of Orange,  
Come climb aboard this and that Spanish ship,  
And roll up that mat from Spain!”  
chorus.  
Piet Hein, Piet Hein, etc.

3.

Didn’t the Boys climb like cats in the rigging  
And almost fight like lions?  
They put the Spaniards to a terrible shame,  
Their shouts echoed all the way to Spain.  
chorus.  
Piet Hein, Piet Hein, etc.

4.

If such a Silver Fleet should come again,  
Say, would you still be able to beat it?  
Or would you feel safe, outside the line of fire,  
Just quietly staying in your hammock?  
chorus.  
"Well! Dutch blood  
Still has courage!  
Even if we are not great,  
We would still win a Silver Fleet!”

label: work_sea
translation:
1.

I have been singing about a brave Ruyter,  
But not about a rider on horseback;  
Yet he was indeed faster than English racers,  
He did create French craftsmanship.

2.

He already rode on his wooden horse  
Around the Sea in the blink of an eye,  
And Lands and Shores trembled before the horse,  
When it snorted with its brass mouth.

3.

And whoever did not quickly jump aside  
And whoever did not bow to Old Holland,  
That horse struck with its hoof so that blood  
Flowed from their nose and ears.

4.

Back then, our Land was so bold and free,  
Back then, it was so powerful and great,  
But the horse was gently nailed though old,  
The Ruyter, the Ruyter is dead!

5.

Oh Vlissingen’s Michiel, brave Ruyter,  
We do arm our horse now,  
But when will it gallop and snort at Sea,  
Like when you were a rider on it?

label: work_sea
translation:
1.

Cables loose, sails up,  
That’s how you sail;  
Though we were gentlemen ashore,  
Our hearts lay in the waves:  
A Dutch child, that is known,  
Finds his element at sea,  
Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho!  
Finds his element at sea!

2.

And though we are no longer musketeers  
As in the days of old,  
When everyone for the East India Company  
Had struck a match:  
Still, we sail every sea  
As well as the best,  
Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho!  
As well as the best!

3.

Hurrah then, boys: aloft!  
Roll up your sleeves,  
Don’t let German, Norwegian, or Englishman  
Climb your ropes,  
Then you can sail without fail,  
Even if the black man blew into the sail,  
Yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho!  
Even if the black man blew into the sail!

label: work_sea
translation:
Chorus.
On foot
Is good,
On horseback is better;
But what suits a Dutch fellow
Best,
Is... in the mast!

1.

Treasure of East and West shore,
Jan-maat fetches them, without rest:
See that nimble, quick crew,
Gold mine for the Fatherland,
Living bulwark of our coasts!
Netherlands' glory and support is he
In war or in merchant navy.
Chorus.
On foot
Is good, etc.

2.

No matter how the storm wind roars,
No matter how the bullets murderously whistle,
The Netherlands' proud tricolor flag
He lets wave night and day,
Lets it wave untamed,
And he wavers, yields nor retreats,
As long as Death does not strike that flag down.
Chorus.
On foot
Is good, etc.

3.

Stay so brave in body and soul,
Jan-maats! Hollanders or Zeelanders:
Think of Grandfather Michiel
On the deck and in the keel;
Pray like lambs, fight like lions: -
Heart of gold and arm of steel,
I still greet you as Admiral!
Chorus.
On foot
Is good,
On horseback is better;
But what suits a Dutch fellow
Best,
Is... in the mast!

label: work_sea
translation:
1.

Strong boys, tough lads,  
Fie, how foolish you stand there!  
Are you not well-built,  
Are you not born ready?  
Be ashamed, boys! and come with me  
To the Sea, to the Sea!

2.

That’s a life of pleasures,  
That’s a life of steadfastness,  
To roam the world around  
At the top of the mast:  
To feel at home everywhere...  
Come with me, to the Sea!

3.

If you’re a coward... go to War,  
(Alas! mostly just for fun...;)  
I know a very good Office still  
If you want to become a Merchant;  
But, whether you seek War or Peace,  
You’ll find both at Sea.

4.

Let them sulk, let them drizzle,  
Let them take shelter on the beach;  
Jan Salie stays on his heels,  
Jan Courage takes to the schooner: -  
Ahoy, Boatswain! everything ready?  
We’re going to the Sea!

label: work_sea
translation:
Geertemeû! There lies my ship: -  
All those ropes and those lines  
(Can you keep an eye on them?)  
I can name them all in a snap:  
And at the very top of the mast  
No one climbs after me!  

If a stoker blows around the mast,  
It should be a fellow  
Who made me fear for my life,  
If even one rope came undone:  
Oh, you learn so quickly and well  
If you want... everything you must!  

It is my life and my pleasure  
To always wander around:  
Meûtje - if you come to die  
I will never come back to the coast:  
Well, goodbye! - take care…  
That’s bound for the East Indies!

label: work_sea
translation:
Far from home or close to hearth,  
It all means the same to me;  
Wrong or right, or pomp and show,  
It all means the same to me;  
Rich as Croesus, poor as Job,  
It all means the same to me,  
As long as on the open sea  
I may make the enemies of the Netherlands tremble  
And whoever means harm – it all means the same to me –  
May be struck on the head!

Meager fare, or fine display,  
It all means the same to me;  
Milk or water, wine or beer,  
It all means the same to me;  
White bread or rye bread,  
It all means the same to me,  
As long as in joy or distress  
I may have an honest life,  
Or – it all means the same to me –  
Or a firm death!

label: work_sea
translation:
A little ship lands in the harbor  
Filled with spices;  
And many a fine young man  
With purses, full to the brim,  
And hearts full of joy.  

Well-scented spice, well-gleaming coin,  
Where will you wander now;  
It seems you have no end,  
For from every corner and point,  
Each one of you comes to be taken.  

But... when the ship has been emptied,  
The Merchant and the Shipowner  
Find their spice paid with gold:  
Yet—if your silver has gone astray,  
Sailor!... where will you find it again?  

A little fun, after sorrow and toil,  
Who would begrudge you that?  
But—do not fill the sails too full...  
Come on! reef the sail where it fits:  
There might be a storm someday!

label: work_sea
translation:
The wind blows from the East:  
To the East I must sail;  
And those who bear me otherwise,  
The white-foamed waves,  
Now delay my ship:  
I must comfort myself!

At anchor I want to turn  
Good-naturedly and without complaint:  
Whoever cannot do otherwise,  
Must bear patiently,  
If he does not want to run aground...  
It will once blow Westward!

Contrary in the currents,  
Contrary in the winds,  
So it is, by night and day,  
Our dear life, friends!  
But... waiting and enduring  
That is what gets us through!

label: work_sea
translation:
If the wind is against you, my friend,  
Let a skipper then teach you  
How to tack:  
Act as if you did not seek it  
To reach that distant shore,  
But sail on with wind and currents...  
And—once you are far enough,  
Then turn to the other tack!

If the wind is ahead, my friend!  
Learn from helmsmen, when sailing,  
Always to gauge:  
The water sometimes seems deep enough  
And there is hardly any beacon...  
But it is an art to get off the shoal  
When either wind or current too soon  
Chases the little ship ashore.

Upwind or downwind, my friend,  
Learn to work—learn to wait  
Day and night!  
Hurried, always arrives too early,  
Know-it-all, will know the least,  
Foolish, has always forgotten something,  
Slow, never has enough time...  
So always choose the right tack!

label: work_sea
translation:
Sailing along the shore  
If one knows neither wind nor currents,  
Will save you from running aground: -  
Who recklessly steers the bow  
To the middle of the waves,  
Has only themselves to blame  
If their boat is broken to pieces.  

Oh! I saw on the wild currents  
Of the deep sea of life  
Many sail without fear,  
Who will never justly arrive  
In the harbor, at the quay: -  
Those who still have to learn  
The art of navigation,  
Keep tacking along the shore!

label: work_sea
translation:
Herring! Dutch Seafood Delicacy,  
In the past, you came in much larger piles:  
It seems that has passed,  
Have the beacons been properly reset?  
Oh, I fear the young mates  
Still fish in the old place!

In the past, a skilled sailor knew  
Where to reef and take bearings,  
Where one could sail ‘all filled,’  
Where one safely chose space;  
Where, with a single maneuver,  
Half a catch could be seen.

I pray you, men at the helm,  
(It concerns the prosperity of the country!)  
Do not steer recklessly and disgracefully,  
If you have never sailed for herring: -  
If you want glory and profit,  
Make sure you are experienced!

label: work_sea
translation:
1.

Three nice boys who sailed at sea;  
They had to leave their Sweethearts:  
“Oh! then give us a Keepsake!”  
Would it help?

2.

One gave him a ring with a lock of hair;  
One gave him her portrait, lifelike;  
The third wanted—whether he found it good or bad—  
To give him nothing.

3.

The ring fell into the sea—and the portrait into the flood,  
Even before they reached the East;  
Then the boys had to console their sad hearts  
Somewhat.

4.

Two then sought comfort in bitter grief,  
Which pressed on them more and more,  
With little brown and black girls... there is nothing else—  
And it worked!

5.

The third, who received nothing from his Sweetheart,  
Had also lost nothing;—  
But still his heart (although he had neither portrait nor ring)  
Belonged to Her!

6.

So, dear girls! if your suitors sail to sea,  
And must suddenly leave you,—  
Oh! give them as little as possible with them...  
It may help!

label: work_sea
translation:
Chorus.  
There went a girl along the side,  
leaning, leaning, Outside side,  
So nice and in the manner!

1.

‘Do you have to sail, do you have to sail?’  
Called everyone, louder voices,  
Who were with each other,  
And they whispered: ‘how odd!’  
Jolman, skipper, ferry passenger  
Held the pretty girl tight.  
Chorus.  
There went a girl, etc.

2.

Mate said: ‘beautiful girl! come along  
With me to East India?  
In my hammock, sweet face,  
There is surely room for two!’  
The girl, color on her cheek,  
Said: ‘that trip is too long for me!’  
Chorus.  
There went a girl, etc.

3.

‘Say, girl! do you want to sail?’  
Called a jolman happily tired:  
‘Don’t be afraid of wind or waves,  
I'm just going to the Tollhouse!’  
The girl, picking at her apron,  
Said: ‘that trip is too short for me!’  
Chorus.  
There went a girl, etc.

4.

But a skipper at Enkhuizen  
Said: ‘my dearest girl!  
Do you see how by the locks,  
There my little ship’s sail rests?  
It’s just waiting for a skipper’s wife!’ –  
And she said: ‘I’ll sail with you!’  
Chorus.  
There went a girl, etc.

label: work_sea
translation:
The little rooster on the tower turns,  
It turns again and again:  
The wind that blows from the west  
Brings me my dearest back;  
Three days and three years  
He has now been sailing  
All around the seas.  

Many young gentlemen, wonderfully clever and rich,  
Have asked me to be their wife;  
I said: "Like should be with like,  
I remain faithful to my dearest!"  
For three years and three nights  
I have been waiting,  
And yearning.  

Though he wanders on the wide sea  
Driven by all winds,  
Even if it were seven years,  
Still I keep my word to him: –  
And even if it were all my days,  
I would bear him faithful  
With mourning and lamenting.  

Well, little rooster! then turn as best you can:  
A Dutch heart remains true;  
The little wind blows from the west after all,  
Then we are man and wife: –  
And Death!... if You would separate us,  
Oh! then hear my crying,  
And take us both!  
I sit early in the morning and late at night...  
The messenger does pass along the street,  
But, whether he knocks on everyone's door,  
To me, poor one, he brings none.  

The little grapes turned white and blue,  
The girl looked just as pale; –  
The girl who already cried and prayed,  
Sat in vain by the window.  

The leaves yellowed before and after:  
There comes a letter through the bars...  
But it was not from her dearest's hand,  
And the edge was mourning black.  
And many a pretty courting couple  
Wanders in the evening under the shade,  
And speaks of faithfulness until death  
And thinks of no sorrow or trouble.  

Yet - when the sweet nightingale  
Goes to sing and lament,  
There is many a loving couple  
Struck by something sorrowful in the heart.  

The young man is silent, the girl sighs,  
Her eyes run over...  
They rest where a helmsman’s love  
Is buried beneath the leaves.

label: work_sea
translation:
It was night, a storm was coming,  
The waves rose up to the mountains.  
Piet Bruyninckx pushed his pipe aside,  
And spoke to Jan Verschaeren:  
A fierce northwester is blowing, Jan!  
The waves, it’s as if they are at a ball...  
Oh, how I pity the people,  
The people who are now ashore!  
Oh, how I pity the people,  
The people who are now ashore!  
They envy us and sigh now—  
I think they are not crazy!—  
In such a storm it is better at sea,  
Much better than ashore, isn’t it?  

And the workers then, who all day long  
Toil for their daily bread,  
Who now, instead of sleeping, must  
Comfort wife and child!…  
The pots fly, the chimney falls—  
That must be a shouting and clanging…  
But we, we lie peacefully on deck,  
And laugh at those who are ashore!  

Yes, dear boy, they have it hard,  
And we must not scorn them,  
For storm and theft, murder and fire,  
Threaten them with so many disasters!  
And if I named a thousand to you,  
It surely would not be all yet…  
Thank God that we sailors, Jan,  
Are rarely ashore!

label: work_sea
translation:
There was recently a girl loose,  
Who wanted to sail, who wanted to sail,  
There was recently a girl loose,  
Who wanted to sail as a sailor.

She enlisted for seven years,  
Because she was afraid, because she was afraid,  
She enlisted for seven years,  
Because she feared no danger.

Then she brought her belongings aboard,  
Like a good, like a good,  
Then she brought her belongings aboard;  
As a good sailor should.

She had to climb the mast,  
Make the sails, make the sails,  
She had to climb the mast,  
Tie the sails with ropes.

But through storms of the weather  
The sails were blown, the sails were blown,  
But through storms of the weather  
The sails were blown down from above.

Now she was tied to the mast,  
With her hands, with her hands,  
Now she was tied to the mast,  
With her hands and feet bound.

She cried: Captain! don't hit me,  
I am your sweetheart, I am your sweetheart,  
She cried: Captain! don't hit me,  
I am your sweetheart as you see.

But before the ship reached shore,  
The young one, the young one,  
But before the ship reached shore,  
There was already the young sailor.

Then when she came back to town,  
Where she still had a mother, where she still had a mother,  
Then when she came back to town,  
Where she still had a mother.

She cried: Mother! don't be angry,  
I have sailed, I have sailed,  
She cried: Mother don't be angry,  
I have sailed as a young sailor.

By someone who truly loves me  
I have this little, I have this little,  
By someone who truly loves me,  
I have this small innocent child.

But before it is Pentecost again,  
I will be his wife, I will be his wife,  
But before it is Pentecost again,  
I will be his wife, that is certain.

label: work_sea
translation:
Gladly raised the sea tone,  
Evening rolls along the beach;  
Good and blood, the dear life,  
We dedicate to the fatherland!  
Let it sound across the waves,  
For a future countryman,  
That once there were seamen,  
Who did not fear death,  
O brave sons of the sea!  
Sing a song while sailing;  
All the brave sing along. (repeat)  

Our brothers carry the weapon  
Staffs of old Dutch authority.  
We are made for the sea,  
Serving under the Dutch flag.  
Let it sound across the waves,  
For a future countryman,  
That once there were seamen  
Who did not fear death.  
O brave sons etc.  

On the shore of the Schelde  
A costly task was accomplished,  
You, Van Speyk! made Holland count.  
Despite the disgraceful overpowering;  
Gloriously the cry was heard,  
Rather devoted to death  
Than losing the flag and honor,  
And the fame of Holland lost,  
O brave sons etc.  

Yes, as seamen we all swear,  
Always adorned with courage and loyalty:  
Fighting we may fall someday,  
Lowering flags we never do.  
Let it sound across the waves,  
For a future countryman,  
That once there were seamen  
Who did not fear death.  
O brave sons etc.

label: work_sea
translation:
The cables loose, the sails up,  
that's how it goes on a

label: work_sea
translation:
Farewell, my dear wife, our ship is stranded on the reefs,  
We are in mortal danger and cannot escape,  
My prayer rises heavenwards with calm on my lips,  
Farewell, my dear wife, farewell, my precious children.  

Should I then leave my wandering life on this shore,  
Where I so much wanted to speak to you once more,  
My dear Marie, should I leave you thus,  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.  

The storm wind howls through the rigging and the ropes hiss,  
My plea rises up: oh Lord! please save us!  
God grant that I may see you once again.  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.  

There our ship breaks apart, death is foretold to us,  
In the hour of death, oh Lord, hear my supplication,  
Yes, we are lost beyond rescue, lost with man and mouse,  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.  
Oh, what sorrowful pain, now that I must part from you,  
Now after a long journey, as I was rejoicing  
To see you soon again, and now must part so sadly.  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.  

A joyful hope still gently shoots through my mind,  
I loved you so much and saw myself loved in return,  
Yes, at this moment I feel soul-pain within.  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.  

Yes, one day we shall both be reunited,  
The long fear of the future will then be gone,  
Yes, with that firm hope I go away with trust,  
Until we meet again, Marie, until we meet again, my children!  

Oh God! It is hard for me to part like this,  
What will my wife and my children perhaps suffer,  
The mourning dress is long, I let myself slide into the waves.  
Farewell, my dear wife, etc.

label: work_sea
translation:
Farewell my Fatherland, the ship cleaves through the waves,  
The eastern wind blows, we count no dangers;  
My eye can hardly discern your shining dune anymore.  
Farewell my Fatherland, my dear Fatherland.

Perhaps I will find peace on foreign soil  
With a scorched face, farewell then to all my friends,  
Farewell dear Maria, whom I loved so much.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

Once my heart was at peace when on your paths  
With my Maria I could live in sweet delight;  
May fortune return her to me once again,  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

Oh, may faithfulness unite us both once more,  
And may the fearful dread of the future disappear,  
With this joyful hope I sail away confidently.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

And you, dear Maria, will you always love me?  
For you possess my heart, my love and my senses,  
Stay true to me, we will arrive soon.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

Then sweet delight will water our heather heart,  
Then true happiness will atone for misfortune,  
Then on the thorny path beautiful roses will bloom again.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

And you, Neptune, safely carry us across the waves,  
You, God of the sea, protect us from storms and dangers,  
Then my sailor’s song will sound, then my strings will tinkle,  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

Stay true to me Maria, I love you as my life,  
Your name remains in my heart as if written in a book,  
You are my hope, my beacon, never abandon me.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.  
Farewell then Maria! receive my last greeting,  
May the time come again when we meet each other,  
And united by faithfulness, we shall never part long again.  
Farewell my Fatherland, etc.

label: work_sea
translation:
The cables loose, the sails up,  
That is how you set sail;  
Even if we were gentlemen ashore,  
Our heart lay in the waves:  
A Dutch child, that is known,  
Finds his element at sea,  
yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho, yo-ho!  
Finds his element at sea!

label: work_sea
translation:
I = C. Jolig.  
In a blue checkered jacket  
he turned the big wheel  
all day long;  
but Michieltje's boy's  
heart  
suffered unbearable pain,  
ah! ah, ah, ah!

2.

As a sailor quick and neat,  
he set foot on board,  
that was proper.  
To the East Indies, to the West,  
boys, that goes excellently!  
Ho-jo, ho-jo, ho-jo!

3.

There stands the Dutch Admiral,  
now a man of fire and steel,  
the terror of the sea.  
He is a Rider by nature;  
gloriously he sits on horseback!  
Hurrah, hurrah, hurrah!

label: work_sea
translation:
I = C. Fairly fast and cheerful.  
Whoever goes along, goes along o - across the sea?  
Keep the helm straight!  
Freshly blows the wind along the reef,  
Will you stay  
in the nest, in the nest with the rest?  
Keep the helm straight!  
The sea seems best to us!  
Whoever wants to become something, well, they do not sit still,  
No, they pull – the sea gate out,  
See, a rich loot awaits them.  
At hand, at hand for the land!  
Keep the helm straight!  
So it sounds loudly from all sides,  
Ahead out of sight and upwards,  
Keep the helm straight!  
So that no storm may surprise you!  
With an eye on the sail and safe from anyone,  
The seaman steers the swimming horse,  
Never for anyone made.  

A hooray, hooray for the sea!  
Keep the helm straight!  
Boys of Holland, shout along!  
Here is the field, the field for the hero,  
Keep the helm straight!  
Here the man shows his worth,  
Under the sailor’s jacket courage is still at home,  
In his fist lies his whole fate,  
He fears no one but God.

label: work_sea
translation:
I = Verse. Not too slowly.  
The wind – you are already blowing – again from the East!  
Mates, forward, the sails are flying!  
Forward to sea!  
The farewell pains us,  
Out there awaits us the  
stormy sea,  
Out there awaits us the stormy sea.  

The little wind now makes the sails swell!  
Mates forward, let go the sheets!  
Forward to sea!  
The farewell pains us,  
Over there greets us the light green sea.          (repeat)  

The little ship will again scrape through the sea foam!  
Mates, keep courage, though dangers threaten,  
Forward to sea!  
The farewell pains us,  
The sparkling sea beckons us laughingly.          (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
I = Especially. Boldly and not too quickly.  
Fer - me boys, stubborn lads,  
Fie! how dull you stand there!  
Aren't you well brought up?  
Aren't you ready by six?  
Be ashamed,  
boys, and come with me  
to the sea, to the sea!  
Be ashamed, boys, and come with me —  
to the sea, to the sea!

2.

That’s a life of pleasures,  
That’s a life of steadfastness,  
To roam the world  
At the very top of the mast.  
To feel at home on every journey ...  
Come, come with me to the sea!

3.

Let them pout, let them drizzle,  
Let them hide on the beach;  
If Jan Salie walks on his heels,  
Jan Courage chooses the main course —  
Holla, boatswain! everything ready?  
We’re going with you to the sea!

label: work_sea
translation:
I = F. Jolig.  
The cables loose, the sails up,  
that’s how you set sail;  
As if we were gentlemen ashore,  
our hearts lie in the waves.  
A Dutch child, that is well known,  
finds his element in the sea,  
Joho, jo-ho, jo-ho, jo-ho,  
finds his element in the sea.  

2.  

And we’re no longer rovers like in days past,  
when everyone for Jan Company had struck a match;  
Still, on every sea we sail as well as the best,  
Jo-ho, jo-ho, jo-ho, jo-ho!  
As well as the best!  

3.  

Hurrah then, boys: aloft! roll up your sleeves,  
Don’t let German, Norwegian, or Englishman climb your ropes.  
Then you can sail without fear, even if the devil blew in the sail,  
Jo-ho, jo-ho, jo-ho, jo-ho!  
Even if the devil blew in the sail!

label: work_sea
translation:
I = C. Not slow.  
Wake up, young men, Dutch pride!  
What our  
heart can endure,  
If you dare to risk your swift body  
in the raging wave turmoil;  
Who fears neither danger nor death,  
If you can rescue from  
distress;  
Who fears neither danger nor death,  
If you can rescue from distress.  

The sturdy tube for a strong chest,  
Covers a heart full of mercy,  
When you carry a drowning person in your arms  
Through the wild surf,  
When you forget wife and child,           (repeat)  
At the shipmate's fearful cry.          (repeat)  

It is a piece worthy of Old Holland!  
Brave men in the land,  
Faithful watchers on the shores,  
May you be spared for a long time!  
Even if no one knows your names,           (repeat)  
I think God does not forget them.          (repeat)

label: work_sea
translation:
THE FAREWELL OF A SAILOR

To a cheerful tune
Oh listen young girls! It should be to your sorrow,
We are now taking a little trip, it is not forever,
We are going to the East, for the prosperity of our country.
I say, my sweetheart praised, give me your right hand.

Farewell liberty, farewell liberty,
I have courted you, until eternity.

Oh youth, please calm down and put that out of your mind,
You surely do not want to sail, and stay with your girlfriend,
Be always faithful to me... where shall I go?
If you want to sail, then I am in tears.

Farewell liberty, farewell liberty,
I have courted you, until eternity.

The girls hear the complaints as long as you are with her,
But on the fair days, they are quickly seduced,
Therefore, take your pleasure in whatever you can have,
I see the flags waving, we are about to set sail.

Farewell liberty, farewell liberty,
I have courted you, until eternity.

Love now gets stuck, see what deceit does,
With all vain tricks they come into your mind;
Until you are seduced, then they leave you in sorrow,
The young men want to court but do not take the girls.

Farewell liberty, farewell liberty,
I have courted you, until eternity.

Oh girls! Make no complaint and it is almost over now,
And if you do not want to wait for me, want to go with another,
Then you have your way, and are ready for marriage,
And I will cheer myself up with a black-haired girl.

Farewell liberty, farewell liberty,
I have courted you, until eternity.

Farewell then, dear friends, the ship is ready for us,
We sail through the waves and fear no danger,
Like a fisherman and company as a sailor should,
And if someone is to die, they throw him overboard.

Forever, forever,
Until eternity.

label: work_sea
translation: There was recently a girl loose / Who wanted to go sailing, who wanted to go sailingThere was recently a girl looseWho wanted to go sailing as a cabin boyinc. 2She had to climb the mastinc. 3But through storm and adverse weatherinc. 4Oh, little captain, don’t hit meinc. 5She had to come into the cabin

label: work_sea
translation:
All those who want to sail to pirate / Must be men with beards  
All those who like rancid flatfish  
All those who smoke fancy pipes  
All those who kill the walrus with us  
All those who do not fear death and the devil  

Chorus:  
Jan, Pier, Tjores and Corneel  
They have beards, they have beards  
Jan, Pier, Tjores and Corneel  
They have beards, they sail along

label: work_sea
translation: Sailors' Song Curaçao, I have looked at you so many times / But all your empty tricks, I do not like them No, all your empty tricks, I do not like them Therefore I am going to leave, where I come from inc. 2 I came passing through the Herestraatje inc. 3 A kiss can make one stay the whole night inc. 4 Come, let us happily clink the glasses inc. 5 Cast off the cluster, the fore and aft ropes

label: work_sea
translation:
In the harbor  
A small ship lands in the harbor / Hojo 4x  
Filled with spices  
Hojo 7x  
And many a sturdy young lad  
With bags, filled to the brim  
And hearts full of joy  
Hojo 6x  
Including 2  
Well-scented spice, well white coin  
Including 3  
And, when the ship is unloaded  
Including 4  
A little fun, after sorrow and burden

label: work_sea
translation: Everyone who wants to go to Iceland / To catch cod and fish with desireTo Iceland, to Iceland, to IcelandUp to thirty-three trips they are not yet tired!inc. 2When the time of the fair comes to usinc. 3When the north wind blowsinc. 4When the east wind blows!inc. 5Along the lee shores past the shoalsinc. 6Finally then we arrive at Iceland

label: work_sea
translation:
To the sea  
Brave boys, tough lads /  
Shame, how stupid you stand there!  
Are you not well-formed?  
Are you not ready at six o'clock?  
Be ashamed, boys! and come along  
To the sea, to the sea!  
inc. 2  
That's a life of pleasures  
inc. 3  
If you're a coward... go to war  
inc. 4  
Let them sulk, let them drizzle

label: work_sea
translation:
A song of the sea  
Who will come along, come along across the sea / Keep the rudder steady!  
The wind blows fresh along the reef  
Will you stay in the nest, with the rest?  
Keep the rudder steady!  
The sea seems the very best to us!  
Who wants to become something  
Well, they don’t sit still  
No, they head out the seaway  
A rich prize awaits them  

Chorus 2
