At the ready, at the ready for the land!  

Chorus 3  
A hooray, hooray for the sea!

label: work_sea
translation:
Pirate Song

The mightiest king of storm and wind  
Is the eagle, mighty and great  
The birds tremble and flee in fear  
At his beak and clawing foot  

When the lion raises his roar at night  
He frightens the animals with it  
Yes, we are the rulers of the earth  
The kings of the sea  

If a ship appears on the ocean  
We throw ourselves onto the enemy ship  
And thus we win our final battle  

Chorus:  
Tiralala 7x  hoi, hoi  
Yes, we are the rulers of the earth  
The kings of the sea

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
Come on, march calmly  
And cheerfully,  
Soldiers, to the field!  
Do not tremble  
Before a bullet.  
Follow Marlborough, that hero,  
Who does not remain in a seraglio, deterred by the chance of war,  
But for freedom  
And religion  
Bravely risks his life.

2.  
Let us run,  
You brave ones,  
And defy death!  
Storm the walls,  
Endure  
Fire, water, and loot,  
Your commander leads you like a brave man.  
Protect your cities,  
Act with reason:  
Each does what he can.

3.  
Brave masters,  
The French madness  
Does not frighten us!  
If he wants to endure  
He will lead us  
On foot and on horseback:  
Then march together undaunted, draw your sword from its sheath.  
Make the French  
Dance  
By tasting your bullet.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
In Hellevoetsluis there stands a house  
It is not a house of honor,  
House of honor,  
There the girls open the windows,  
There the soldiers hang out.  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

2.  
The mother spoke to the daughter:  
‘You must say some pretty things,  
Pretty things,  
And then stand in your doorway,  
Expecting the soldiers.’  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

3.  
The father spoke to the mother:  
‘You are not a woman of honor,  
Woman of honor,  
That you teach your youngest daughter  
Such things.’  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

4.  
The mother spoke to the father:  
‘She is not deceived,  
Deceived,  
If she gets a sting in her kitchen,  
It is far from her brown eyes.’  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

5.  
Then she still wears a little skirt,  
That is of Red Scarlet,  
Red Scarlet,  
Somewhat short in the front, somewhat long in the back,  
It is easy to pick up.  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

6.  
Then she still wears a little blouse,  
That is made of Cambric,  
Cambric,  
Underneath it hides a little red rose  
Where the soldiers search.  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.  

7.  
Those who composed us this light song,  
They were three soldiers,  
Three soldiers,  
They had slept by her side,  
They could not leave her.  
La, la lette ziante la coure,  
La, la lette zian belle mie,  
Siante la coure couragie, etc.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Cheerful, calmly in battle,  
March, march, march, what note is sounding?  
Drum, trumpet, and kettle drums;  
Hear the heavy artillery rumble!  
Fight, ://: ://: ://: ://: stab,  
shoot, up, up, up, the enemy flees:  
the enemy flees.  
Follow with drawn swords,  
Urge your horses to fly;  
Turn with lightning and thunder,  
the abyss above, the kingdom below,  
Close, ://: ://: ://: ://: together,  
Break through the enemy's forces;  
Sound your drums and trumpets,  
Your sword enforces every law.  
Where bravery commands,  
No one fears powder nor lightning.  
://: Rejoice ://: ://:  
Sing, dance, jump,  
Capture, burn, kill, force,  
Beware of plundering and looting,  
Janus' temple stands,  
Temple stands wide open.

Da Capo. Cheerful calmly.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Cheerful, calm in battle,  
March, march, march, what notes fall?  
Drum, trumpet, and kettle drums;  
Hear the heavy artillery  
rumble! Fight, ://: ://: ://: ://: stab,  
shoot, up, up, up, the enemy flees:  
the enemy flees.  
Follow with drawn swords,  
Urge your horses to fly;  
Turn with lightning and thunder,  
the abyss above, the realm below,  
Close, ://: ://: ://: ://: together,  
Break through the enemy’s forces;  
Sound your drums and trumpets,  
Each of you sets laws with your blade.  
Where bravery commands,  
Gunpowder nor lightning are feared.  
://: Cheer ://: ://:  
Sing, dance, jump,  
Capture, burn, kill, compel,  
Beware of plundering and looting,  
Janus’ Temple stands,  
Temple stands wide open.

Da Capo. Cheerful, calm.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Cheerful, calm in battle,  
March, march, march, what need is there to fall?  
Drum, trumpet, and kettle drums;  
Hear the heavy artillery rumble!  
Fight, ://: ://: ://: ://: stab,  
shoot, advance, advance, advance, the enemy flees:  
the enemy flees.  
Follow with drawn swords,  
Urge your horses to fly;  
Return with lightning and thunder,  
the abyss above, the realm below,  
Close, ://: ://: ://: ://: together,  
Break through the enemy's forces;  
Sound your drums and trumpets,  
Your sword commands all laws.  
Where bravery rules,  
One fears neither powder nor lightning.  
://: Rejoice ://: ://:  
sing, dance, leap, capture, burn,  
kill, compel, Beware of plundering and looting,  
the temple of Janus stands, the temple  
stands wide open.

Da Capo. Cheerful, calm.

label: work_soldier
translation:
What do I hear? What a fire,
is Mulciber coming down? Is Mulciber
coming down? To forge murder weapons ://:
in the service of cruel Death?
Or does Phaethon set
the world on fire again,
or does the Abyss spit out fire again
from the smoldering, from the smoldering
inside? Thunder threatens, the
Thunder threatens, The
Death threatens, The Thunder, The
Thu-
nder threatens to tear the Earth apart,
Nature seems pale from fright to mourn,
Nature seems pale, seems
pale from fright to mourn. Fire and
Sulfur scorch the air; All
Mankind sighs; Or was Lucifer
cast out? No! It is
the Allies! Who with their
thunder clubs,
rage against the French brotherhood,
which drowns,
which drinks itself under, in its own blood. Rejoice ://: ://:
://: ://: laurel twigs fly: the unconquered Batavians
write
France before the law,
with their own
blood in the sand, of the trembling, of the trembling, Malplaquet.

label: work_soldier
translation:
That a wise man can be a soldier,
Is incomprehensible to me,
To fight without being wicked;
Always fearing the aim of Death,
To risk oneself at the murder weapon,
For a piece of bread or fleeting honor.

If one could get a job anywhere,
To go fight with Ham and Wine,
I would be one of the boldest;
And with my Fork skewer the Innkeeper,
For I care less for the sound,
Of the war trumpet, than a full Flute.

Bacchus I would make my Captain,
A slick Rogue my Lieutenant,
A French Cook for my Sergeant;
Who could bravely fight with his jaws,
A young Girl who carries the Banner,
And does not ask much for a duel.

My Drummer would be a Pickpocket,
With a Traffic Sign at his side,
Pipes and good Tobacco with it;
My Sentinel would have nothing to fear,
For he would always stand on Shield Duty,
By the spit where I let my Meat roast.

My Regimental coat I would wear,
Would be a drunken Pig's Sword,
All around studded with Vine leaves,
And my proud Military Wagon,
Would be a Rinkhows Feed barrel,
Filled to the brim with Grape juice.

For when we shall be buried,
It will be, farewell you delicious drink,
Therefore I flee from Bellona's sound;
And try to quench my thirst in peace,
And shun Death like the plague,
For there is no tavern in its realm.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Under arms.  
Everyone shows themselves as a brave soldier.  
Sa, sa, march,  
The war drum beats  
Shield and mortar fire and flames burst.  
Taf! taf! taf! taf! taf! Bang! bang! bang! bang!  
Cut down all, small and large!  
Plunder, for the loot!  
Prince Eugene has gone ahead.

label: work_soldier
translation:
We live worse than the Slavs,  
The drink that refreshes us,  
It is not beer but wine,  
It flows from a fountain,  
They sometimes treat us badly,  
The service is so harsh to us,  
Will that last any longer?  
Hey, so we descend into the grave.  
The greatest hardship that befalls us,  
Those are the sentinel duties,  
Through hail and snow and cold,  
And sometimes faint from hunger,  
Thus we live in distress,  
Our bellies not half full of bread,  
It is barely that we receive,  
Hey, a handful of rye syrup.  
Now we go again with pleasure,  
Set out to rest,  
So the day comes to an end,  
Then we get a new commandment again,  
And all that without bread,  
Well supplied with herb and twig,  
Thus we serve the king,  
Hey, all in our great poverty.  
My trousers no longer close,  
And my legs are like flutes,  
Always at the same pace,  
They look like a single box,  
My body is disfigured,  
Like a flower standing in the field.  
They should well set me  
For a shadow in the field.  
Then we must prepare ourselves,  
At night we go to work,  
And all that without money,  
It is not worth telling,  
But ask the old Line,  
They have seen it more often,  
In the year ninety-six,  
Hey, well seen before Bruges.  
And there we lay on marshes,  
And it was not forbidden to us,  
It was on knoll land,  
Where a hundred thousand were planted,  
We plucked just as fast,  
They cut us a penny off,  
The devil may take  
Who gave the first order.  
We are very naked of garments,  
And we have nothing to spend:  
It is a great sorrow,  
Always without money,  
And sometimes without bread,  
Well supplied with herb and twig,  
Thus we serve the king,  
Hey, all bare in our poverty.  
Now we want to go and see,  
With joy settle in Holland,  
To join the service,  
To see if it is better there,  
We then make a request,  
The Provost puts us under arrest,  
And the shackles all around the legs;  
Is this not a heavy plague.

label: work_soldier
translation:
If you wish to be a Warrior,  
A Camper, a Knight of the Lord,  
Let yourself be marked, put on arms,  
You must learn to fight and battle.  

2. For we have here no struggle  
Against flesh and common blood:  
But against the pride of the wide world,  
We must fight, great and small.  

3. Against Kings and great Princes,  
All who govern darkness,  
We must fight unto death,  
Upright with pious Christ’s banner.  

4. Cunning, worse than venom,  
Of spirits who work in the air,  
We must also be fighting,  
Noting their wickedness and deceit.  

5. They can perform signs and wonders,  
And by an appearance lie the truth,  
Those who do not hold firmly to Christ  
They will deceive all of them.  

6. Let us hope with good patience,  
Leaving behind evil manners,  
So that God on the evil day,  
May well govern through His Spirit.  

7. In a body subject to sin  
Does not dwell God’s Spirit, free from discord,  
Nor does He enter any soul  
Disobedient and evil of will.  

8. But he who seeks Christ early and late,  
To serve without ceasing,  
Shall receive and be free from shame,  
If he does not temper Him.  

9. He will put on their strong weapons,  
(With which he has conquered all)  
And must give, for it is his work,  
Victory he will grant them.  

Pause.  

10. Thus stand, be pious in your fight,  
Gird your loins with truth,  
Put on the breastplate well and upright,  
Righteousness without hardship.  

11. Be shod on your feet well  
With the Gospel of peace,  
To run swiftly as a messenger,  
Messages of God's mercies.  

12. Take faith as a shield  
Against the devil’s unruliness,  
Whereby all his fiery darts  
Are quenched and silenced.  

13. The helmet of salvation you will wisely  
Also place upon your head,  
Grasp the sword of the Spirit, which is God’s word,  
To strike down your enemies.  

14. Bear Christ’s sign, the red cross,  
Willingly go out to the field with Him,  
Suffer shame, pressure, fear, death with confusion,  
Thus take His kingdom with power.  

15. Wish to walk with patience,  
Enduring, without any longing,  
In the fight God leads you,  
Thank, praise, and glorify Him with joyful songs.  

16. Wish to beg, pray in the Spirit,  
And keep good watch at all times,  
Do not flee from suffering or tempest,  
You will be found a true Knight.  

17. For those who flee from the fight,  
Will not please God:  
But those who fight steadfastly  
Until death, without despairing.  

18. They shall wash away their tears,  
Their suffering will be healed,  
They shall put on shining clothes white and clear,  
And be joyful.  

19. A crown (with greater melody)  
Christ shall also give upon their head,  
That will be their wage and their soldier’s pay,  
Here above in eternal life.

label: work_soldier
translation:
How does Mercury come flying so fast,  
Bringing us news of the battle:  
One sees the joy in his eyes,  
Jupiter is on his way,  
Mars shows his strength as best he can,  
Everyone wants to go to the fields.  
Around, bang bang, the drums are beaten,  
The trumpets sound troop calls,  
And the heavy cannon can be heard rumbling,  
Every soldier's saber gleams,  
Thus now come all to the fields,  
For there they sing with joy.  
Tienen, Leuven, and Mechelen too,  
Will soon be on our side,  
The Brabandt girls, sweet of body,  
Take a soldier by the hand,  
It is lively there today,  
The Brabant flower lies in the sand.  
My Margootje, good in color,  
Whom I have well known before,  
And knew well the Dutch scent:  
For she was more accustomed to it,  
Viva soldiers, it is the flower,  
To play with roses excellently.  
One sees them drinking tea together,  
Morning coffee accordingly,  
And Mechelen’s beer poured with joy,  
The violin plays abundantly,  
Viva soldiers of the States,  
They now have the upper hand.  
Antwerp, Brussels, Vilvoorde,  
All the decent girls  
Who captured many a soldier’s heart,  
It is to have a kiss,  
We pay them with sweet words,  
It is everyone's pleasure to do so.  
Cupid fears no weapon,  
Nor Venus any sharp gun,  
One sees many a rose bloom,  
That will gladly fall before you,  
Now is no time to sleep much,  
The drum beats, now we go again.  
It is now a carefree soldier,  
Everyone is equally delighted,  
Our generals do not want to guard,  
They seek the enemy in battle:  
The trumpet sounds early in the morning,  
So it always goes with us now.

label: work_soldier
translation:
So, so, pious soldiers,
You must now go early to the field,
For the country and for the States,
Against the enemy’s violence,
For they are coming to appear,
All with their great forces,
And fear no enemy,
Spare neither powder nor shot.
Although France and Spain,
Are now completely united,
The Emperor and Britain,
Of whom you say you are allied,
And the King of Prussia,
Will also show his power,
Neuburg there beside,
Will show all its strength.
Lunenburg as well:
They are preparing themselves:
They are at peace with us,
Resting until the fight:
And the Landgrave of Hesse:
He shows himself ready early:
To drive out the enemy:
And to make them flee there:
And to make them flee there.
See here all preparing,
Mars hangs on his weapon,
And wants to go forth,
Like a brave warrior,
With all his soldiers,
On foot and also on horse,
He is ready to fight,
And knows the white sword,
And knows the white sword.
Triton blows his horn,
Neptune readies the fleet,
And wants to pierce the sea,
And sees no danger,
But lets his flags wave,
From the very top,
And wants to mow down his enemy,
From foot to head,
From foot to head.
Rejoice, Batavians,
Now comes the summer time,
For through your war-like ways,
You are known in the fight,
Among Greeks and Romans,
There you are highly respected,
Where your lion appears,
There is great awe,
There is great awe.
Your seven bloody arrows,
In a band with praise,
You have bravely obtained,
And there is not yet,
Wanting to fight further,
For you have it well,
And go fighting with blood,
Against your enemy must,
Against your enemy must.
Do not let yourself be distracted,
You brave lions by nature,
Not to pluck any feathers,
Preserve them with your sword,
You seven free lands,
Who wage heavy war,
To terrorize your enemy’s shores,
If you but come near them,
If you but come near them.
One hears the trumpet sound,
And also the drum beating,
And one sees all sails shining,
Ready before the ocean,
On which the sea-lion powerfully:
Is fully mustered
Now in time
Hollanders and Zeelanders mighty,
Come, it is now time again,
Come, it is now time again.
Heaven wishes to crown you,
With a good victory,
So that we may live,
In peace and prosperity,
And that the sword may strike,
Again in its sheath:
And through that we gain,
A desired peace,
An Etc.

label: work_soldier
translation:
To arms: everyone shows themselves as a brave soldier:
Sa! sa! march, the drum of war beats,
Rouw douw, douw douw, douw rouw,
Rouyw douw, douw douw, douw douw,
Now blow harquebuses and field trumpets;
Everyone wants to take position in battle order
Tran tan te ran tan, tran tan te tan
Pike and oar, on the shore, turn only left and right
Step into the field, as a courageous hero.
The drum is beating, lively as faithful heroes in battle
Sa! sa! attack, fight for Prince Charles’s right:
Sa!! sa! sa! sa! sa! sa! tha! tha! tha! tha! tha! tha!
Taf! taf! taf! taf! taf! paf! paf! paf! paf! paf!
Cut down all, small and great, loot for the plunder
Prince Eugene has previously trained.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Although my money and I must go on,  
If there is no peace for a short time,  
We eat the good potage:  
Twenty-eight stuivers marbleu,  
Still good courage, my boy;  
Still good courage.  

Monday is our payday,  
Then we spend a feast:  
Sometimes our half pay,  
Twenty-eight stuivers marbleu;  
Still good courage, my boy,  
Still good courage.  

When Monday is over,  
We all must make do;  
Dry bread is our potage,  
Twenty-two  
stuivers marbleu;  
Still good courage, my boy,  
Still good courage.  

When we come to our camp,  
That’s when the devil spoils you:  
Then we seek adventure,  
Because twenty-eight stuivers marbleu  
Is a soldier’s pay, my boy,  
Is a soldier’s pay.  

We rarely drink ourselves completely drunk and out,  
Because often we have coin or money,  
Whether it’s through adventure  
Because twenty-eight stuivers marbleu:  
Is a soldier’s pay, my boy,  
Is a soldier’s pay.  

When we then go on marauding,  
Then we must have the farmer’s girls;  
We eat the good potage,  
Twenty-eight stuivers marbleu,  
Still good courage, my boy,  
Still good courage.  

If we then get a pretty girl,  
We kiss her till the cap slips off;  
And eat the good potage,  
Twenty-eight stuivers marbleu,  
Still good courage, my boy,  
Still good courage.  

When we then walk through the Spitsroe,  
If we get anything, we have it;  
Still good courage,  
Twenty-eight stuivers marbleu,  
Still good courage, my boy,  
Still good courage.

label: work_soldier
translation:
So says my comrades,
The drum that beats, Make yourselves ready,
And join with pleasure, Your Officer,
To go aboard,
Outside the True Gate, fa la, etc.
There lie so many ships,
There together, We shall go,
And leave Utrecht, Sail through the Vecht,
To Muiderstee,
And then the Zuiderzee, fa la, etc.
So Hospes wants to offer,
Jenever beer, With good cheer,
Gives us brandy, We want to be merry
Smoke a pipe of tobacco,
And a bottle in the bag, fa la, etc.
We must take our leave
From great and small, All in general,
Who have all together,
Supported us,
And drink with pleasure
Another bottle, three or four, etc.
Farewell, High Mighty Lords,
The States and The Magistrate,
By your command, We depart,
Very happy in spirit, And well-disposed, fa la, etc.
We shall cooperate well,
The Officer and the Soldier,
So that also for the honor,
Of Holland,
To defend the country,
For the King of England, fa la, etc.
We shall patrol well
Along the shore, in England,
So that neither Spaniard nor Frenchman,
Dares to take the risk,
To place the Pretender’s son,
On the throne, fa la, etc.
When we then return,
We shall be together,
And drink the welcome with great pleasure,
For Jenever and beer,
Drive the enemy away, fa la, etc.
Farewell Utrecht Girls,
Be at peace, Although we go,
So the unrest
Returns to rest,
So we shall faithfully,
Make you our wives, fa la, etc.
Is there here or there a girl,
Or sweet maiden, Who complains,
Who goes there heavy-hearted,
Think of the lover,
Oh don’t scold us,
It has happened many times, fa la, etc.
Farewell sorrowful hearts,
Wife and child, Beloved by us,
We want to part, With a sad tear,
Oh keep well,
We shall return soon, fa la, etc.
Farewell all together now,
Farewell Catrijn, farewell Fransijn,
They call us aboard,
They distribute the bread,
Farewell once more Utrecht,
And farewell oh beautiful Vecht, fa la, etc.

label: work_soldier
translation:
A warrior hears the war machinery,  
The rushing and the rum - - - mling,  
Of weapons and drums,  
The sounding of trumpets,  
And the shining of helmets,  
And gleaming sabers with shouting;  
And hopes when he returns, ://:  
That he will be highly honored. ://:

label: work_soldier
translation:
Girls of Vlissingen, be joyful,  
We will soon have another time,  
The Prince's children are almost away,  
Now we carry another sweetheart on our arm.  
Drum and flute just play on,  
And bring the Prince's children to Zierikzee,  
To Zierikzee in the garrison,  
Where we find beautiful girls according to the new fashion.  
If it must indeed happen,  
That I must part from my sweetest love,  
Give her then a little kiss on the mouth,  
With which you make her young heart healthy.  
Pen and ink are good hope,  
Then write a letter, just as big,  
Write then a letter straight from your heart;  
It will make all her pain and sorrow disappear.  
Come Prince's children, and you must go,  
For all your questions are now done,  
A brave soldier and has only one love,  
But wherever he goes, there he finds his comfort.  
Come then, little child,  
The Prince's children will surely be your fathers,  
The Prince's children leave Zierikzee,  
The girls remain sitting with the best boys.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Who ever saw a poorer creature,  
Who cares for nothing,  
Than a Soldier full of war-fire,  
When he has his monthly pay;  
He drinks and indulges and bullies,  
And does not stop until it’s consumed:  
Rom don: dom de rom dom.  
Then he must go naked back to the field,  
And risks his meager hide,  
For powder and lead placed on hope,  
But sometimes gets no loot;  
Then he chews ammunition bread,  
And drinks a little wine from the ditch;  
Rom dom:  
A piece of old moldy bread,  
Strengthens his venom,  
Sometimes an old crow’s foot,  
Instead of a young chicken:  
And though he gets something from the Farmer,  
It is often paid back with the shoulder,  
Rom dom:  
A rotten sail strengthens his house,  
A fox old straw his bed,  
He is the plague of many lice,  
Wherever he sets his feet,  
He has two friends he celebrates,  
That is poverty and filthy vermin,  
Rom dom:  
His breath never stinks of him again,  
From food that’s too fatty,  
He finds his grave at the end of his service,  
Deserts his post,  
Must stand guard at powder and lead,  
And walk at a foot’s pace after his death,  
Rom dom:  
Wherever he goes he acts beastly,  
And puffs with his courage!  
He thinks everyone fears him,  
How cowardly he is by nature,  
But as soon as he hears the calfskin again,  
The poor fighter must immediately move on:  
Rom dom:  
And when necessity drives him to battle,  
He often claws his head,  
And is in a short time,  
Robbed of arm and leg:  
Then there is no help left,  
When out in the city they go sniffing;  
Rom dom:  
But if he comes cheerfully from the dance,  
That is all he sees,  
Then dances and jumps in his finery,  
And knows no sorrow,  
But if caught in battle from outside,  
Then he is the old servant again:  
Rom dom:  
If he is brought to the Garrison,  
He stands as if in a stable;  
And sometimes paints night after night,  
In rough weather on the rampart,  
While another with his wife,  
Hands her hair over to him for pastime,  
Rom dom:  
If he has a penny and his pants,  
The Passbaan is his delight,  
He spends his money on the first search,  
Because before honor there is no rest,  
When the money shines in his face,  
He carries the rifle not half so lightly:  
Rom dom:  
And if he finally ages,  
Sickness attacks him,  
Which no pomade or shackles can help,  
But forces him to the Hospital,  
Then he dies like a Donkey dead,  
And is only good for the worms,  
Rom dom:

label: work_soldier
translation:
Farewell my love, we must part from each other,  
To the sound of drums and pipes,  
The trumpets blow just as much,  
Farewell my love until I return.  

2. I will not stay long in the army,  
Without writing to you, my love, my angel,  
If your heart is like mine,  
If two paired lives are to be united.  

3. I will never, ever leave you,  
Spite to all those who hate us,  
I depart now with dignity,  
From here again to another garrison.  

4. Although I travel three hundred miles from here,  
Love shoots with all kinds of arrows,  
Death follows us everywhere,  
Wherever we turn or go.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Well, what is better life than to be a Soldier?  
From early morning until late at night  
We always live in luxury,  
But I never had more pleasure  
Than when I became a Musketeer.  
My first Garrison you want to know,  
That was Breda, that beautiful City,  
Where I had much amusement,  
Especially with the girls of Breda,  
But the joy was almost over,  
Then we had to go march.  
Then you could see the girls crying,  
Because we had to leave,  
For they were heavily burdened,  
Several with their big bellies,  
And they made a sad noise,  
"Lad, will you not come back here?"  
We said dearest girls,  
You will have Soldiers here again,  
To do as you wish,  
Because they carry the same Sabers,  
To stab you once,  
That does not hurt the girls.  
I think I already hear the music raging  
In the most beautiful city, Namur,  
There we will live without sorrow,  
Merrily dancing with the Hostesses,  
Both day and night,  
Satisfied with their desire by our strength.  
Soldiers, do you want to take your gear?  
The time is coming and we must leave,  
Pretty girls, do not cry,  
We cannot stay with you,  
It is Orders from the State.  
So do not be angry with us.  
Be cheerful, faithful chamberlain,  
We are already coming to the Walloon coast,  
Set your youth at ease,  
There life is very affordable,  
With our money, we make pleasure,  
And drink wine and beer.  
On our journey, we happily sing,  
Play on drums and flute,  
Make such a sweet sound.  
Our Major rides a horse  
And we soldiers all together  
Follow behind.  
Already in Namur our Garrison,  
The girls stand at the Gate,  
They spoke many friendly words,  
And gave us so sweet winks,  
Our heart seemed through the love fire,  
Almost about to faint.  
How can life be better,  
Because we have it as we wish,  
Dressed just like a banner Lord,  
And with so many young girls,  
Whom we have for pleasure,  
Because they love Soldiers.  
All with leave, we now step,  
Back again to our beautiful Holland,  
The girls take the prize,  
Those are the ones we will marry,  
Because above all lands fair,  
Holland takes the prize.  
He who sang this little song  
Was a lad from The Hague,  
He liked the girls very much,  
He made this little song,  
Right out of his head and love glow,  
Because he was full of Soldiers' blood.

label: work_soldier
translation:
A Dieuw Bergen op Zoom,  
We are going to leave you,  
By order of the States,  
To another Garrison,  
Our assignment has come,  
From Smitshart Regiment:  
That I now make known  
To you girls of Bergen.  

2.  
Farewell proud girls of Bergen,  
If you want to say goodbye,  
My love we are departing,  
I wish you much pleasure,  
If you then take delight,  
With another in decency;  
Who will come to you,  
In this garrison.  

3.  
What do you say young man,  
Should I join myself  
With another's delight,  
That does not suit me;  
I have given you,  
My honor as pledge,  
To live together,  
Even if I remain in disgrace.  

4.  
You say long live disgrace,  
Though we are leaving now;  
Therefore do not weep,  
Keep yourself constant,  
Eight months without blame,  
Let us remain faithful,  
Then we will come again;  
And marry you my bride.  

5.  
The girl who says,  
Must I leave,  
My love beyond measure;  
The Lord guide you.  
God will guide you,  
To Sas van Gent.  
You have stayed there,  
And are known there.  

6.  
There we are patient,  
And see what is there:  
No one is guilty,  
Healthy and fresh,  
To go to Sas;  
What does that lead us to,  
There we will live,  
As well as one can.  

7.  
On the twentieth of April,  
Smithart will march,  
Crossing the sea maneuvering,  
Against the girls’ will,  
Farewell stop complaining,  
For parting is hard,  
Will you then comfort yourself,  
With another lover.  

8.  
Farewell then for the last time,  
With a toasted glass:  
And drink once again,  
That tastes best to us!  
And then one more kiss,  
For her red mouth,  
That I tell you Susje,  
Farewell and be healthy.  

9.  
Our pleasant little ships,  
They will soon lie at anchor:  
To swing their anchors,  
Smitshart’s banner planted,  
And the wind favorable,  
The fair water well known;  
Then we sail prosperously:  
All the way to Sas van Gent:  

10.  
Whoever has composed this little song:  
Everyone may well know it,  
Do not forget two kisses,  
From their dearly loved.  
From Smitshart’s Swiss,  
Are accustomed to singing,  
And also the Grenadiers,  
Who make it known.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.
What does one not see in these days?
Everyone sits lamenting in fear.
The sword of war is up;
The bloodshed is now imminent,
As the newspapers tell us,
It is a terror for everyone.

2.
All kings and potentates
Are busy making ready:
To send their fleets to sea;
That is what our own allies do,
With whom we were firmly allied,
We were in such peace and quiet.

3.
All around is full of misery,
The course of the world is now at an end;
Has anyone ever seen such bloodshed?
Of all kings and powers:
As is now described,
It now seems like the last day.

4.
One sees merchants lamenting,
As their ships daily
Are struck down by the enemy;
The seamen are seen mourning,
Thinking of which tears the heart;
As the newspapers tell us.

5.
He took our ships at his desire,
And captured them at his command,
As one now hears daily,
The island Stazles is taken away,
Which makes many people here shudder,
That alone has grieved their hearts.

6.
Yet I hope that the Orange Blood,
Will avenge him quickly:
And the States of our Netherlands,
We will reclaim it with the sword:
O, may God protect our fleet!
We give flesh and blood as struggle.

7.
Oh, please kneel before God the Lord!
And pray for all faithful souls,
Who now sail on our fleet;
That God may cover them with His hand,
And grant them heroic courage.

8.
Oh God, please preserve that fleet!
Who risked their lives with us,
Give the authorities good wisdom,
If God the Lord is with them.
Then they suffer neither fear nor dread,
Though they walk through fire and flame.

9.
Oh England, what can move you?
Ah, ah, reconsider your sword!
That you have so disturbed the sea:
That you have caused us war,
When we were in such peace and quiet,
You were our very ally.

10.
True loves are rarely seen,
As I shall tell you here,
Just think of Absalom:
Who fought against his own father,
Think just once entirely,
He hung from a tree.

11.
Might we, with the lost son,
Bow before the throne of grace,
And act as the people of Nineveh,
And truly repent of our sins:
As is clearly found in God's word,
Then we might yet have peace.

12.
For where God is, there is peace!
We pray to you, O God the Lord!
Protect our beloved Netherlands:
So that no sword of war may harm us,
Keep it far from all Holland,
Reign gloriously by your hand.

label: work_soldier
translation:
There sounds the war drum,  
Now heroes gather around Haroud!  
How the trumpet blares,  
The fire is shown everywhere,  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
Now towards the enemy.  

Grasp saber and musket,  
According to the warrior’s duty and law,  
Each at his post, with proud spirit,  
Who is there that fears the enemy?  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
No warrior shows cowardice.  

The money of freedom’s honor!  
Brothers-in-arms to the weapon!  
Spare no bullets, powder, or lead.  
Who falls, dies the death of heroes.  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
The enemy found his grave.  
Now the bayonet strikes,  
Set upon the hero’s steel,  
And so against oppressors on.  
Who can resist this hero’s drum?  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
Now towards the enemy.  

Be heroes! hold firm:  
There flows the enemy’s blood.  
Fight undaunted, the fatherland  
Has pledged your loyalty its salvation.  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
The bloodthirsty retreat.  

There they flee alive!  
We rush after the enemy.  
Thus the glory of the fatherland boasts,  
The loot they gave us on all sides.  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
The enemy found his grave.  

Freedom is saved!  
Oppression crushed!  
Come brave heroes, let us sing;  
The dear fatherland is free!  
Bang, bang, bang, bang,  
The saber is laid down.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
In Amsterdam there stands a house,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
There is an old grumpy fellow at home,  
Toesoer, Toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer, toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

2.  
Then in the evening they come to her door,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Then the old grumpy one comes forward:  
Toesoet, Toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer, toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

3.  
He sometimes comes over to her,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Because he is afraid she will get heavy (pregnant),  
Toesoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

4.  
Even if you have such a thick neck,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Nevertheless, you must join the dance,  
Toesoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

5.  
The painter teaches her on her back,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
The painter gave sighs upon sighs,  
Toesoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

6.  
She must go to the officer in the shop,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
The soldiers must stand before Father,  
Toesoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.  

7.  
Then she goes in the evening to the main guard,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
She keeps the soldiers from the sentry,  
Toesoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer, toesoer,  
Toelemalette, lamoer.  

8.  
He who wrote this little song,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Was a soldier whose heart was light,  
Toesoer, toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer,  
Lamoer toesoer,  
Toelemalette lamoer.

label: work_soldier
translation:
My lover is a young man,  
Molded for battle,  
His arm dedicated to the Fatherland,  
His love and heart devoted,  
He calls upon his pride,  
He swears: I will save you, Fatherland,  
Or I will perish with your downfall.  
Of my noble hero,  
I think I already see my lover,  
Placed at the spearhead of war.  
Victor in the fight,  
Then our wedding feast  
Will be prepared by the dear Fatherland.

label: work_soldier
translation:
When I deserted,  
I came to Mechelen,  
The French have caught me,  
I thought something would come of it there.  
To pass the tribunal.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The fourth half Brigade proud,  
Still in need of men,  
Boys come with great pleasure  
The enlistment bounty is offered to you  
Just join the Batavian Fusilier,  
A Blue Coat with white lapels  
A Hat added to it,  
You can get girls, three or four of them,  
As long as you are a Batavian Fusilier,

label: work_soldier
translation:
Long live Batavia, let us sing,  
To honor and glory of the Fatherland,  
No Briton or Emperor can force us,  
Oppression is broken among us,  
We return and bring with us,  
The long desired peace of ours.  
And risked our blood for the Fatherland,

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
Who ever saw a poorer creature?  
One who is nowhere to be found,  
Than a Soldier full of warfire,  
When he has his monthly pay,  
He drinks and indulges and dominates:  
Rom dom, dom de rom dom.

2.  
Then he must go naked once more to the field,  
And risk his skinny skin,  
Bet on gunpowder and lead,  
But sometimes gains no loot:  
Then he chews Ammunition bread,  
And drinks a little wine from the ditch,  
Rom dom, etc.

3.  
A piece of old moldy bread  
Strengthens his Venison,  
Sometimes an old crow’s foot,  
Instead of a young Hen:  
And though he takes from the Farmer,  
It is often paid back with the shoulder,  
Rom dom, etc.

4.  
A rotten sail provides his house,  
A bundle of old straw his bed,  
He is the prey of many lice,  
Wherever he sets his feet:  
He has two friends whom he celebrates,  
That are poverty and filthy vermin,  
Rom dom, etc.

5.  
His breath never stinks anymore,  
From overly fatty food:  
He finds his grave at the end of his service,  
When he leaves his post:  
He must stand firm for powder and lead,  
And walk step by step to his death,  
Rom dom, etc.

6.  
Wherever he goes he plays the beast,  
And shows off his courage:  
He believes everyone fears him;  
How cowardly he is by nature,  
But when he again hears the Calf-skin,  
The poor fighter must immediately move on:  
Rom dom, etc.

7.  
And when necessity pushes him to the fight,  
He often claws at his head:  
And in a short time:  
Is robbed of arm and leg;  
Then no help is possible anymore,  
When the moustaches leave the city;  
Rom dom, etc.

8.  
But when he comes cheerfully from the dance,  
You see a Soldier:  
Then he dances and jumps on his French tune,  
And knows no sorrow:  
But when he returns to the fight,  
He is again that old servant,  
Rom dom, etc.

9.  
If he is brought to the Garrison,  
He stands as if in the stable:  
And sometimes paints night after night,  
In rough weather on the Wal,  
While another with his Wife,  
Plants horns on him for pastime,  
Rom dom, etc.

10.  
If he has a penny in his pants,  
The Pasbaan is his delight,  
He spends his money first,  
For honor has no rest:  
When the money shines in sight,  
Rom dom, etc.

11.  
And if he finally fades away,  
That sickness attacks him,  
He no longer gives muzzle to any beard:  
But forces him to the Hospital,  
Then he dies as a man does,  
His Body then feeds the worms,  
Rom dom, etc.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Lovers of Malprade,  
Now make a joyful noise,  
If you want to meet with joy,  
The patents are already here;  
All to go marching,  
To Sluis and Flanders;  
There we will bravely parade:  
With girls abundant.  
People of Malprade full of honor,  
What do you make known to us:  
That you are going to march,  
Oh! what great misery  
That I cannot forget  
How it affects my heart,  
Now I am in trouble  
In this sorrowful state.  
Sweet love, do not hesitate,  
Just keep good courage,  
We will come again,  
And dry your flood of tears;  
And that will not help you,  
I cannot always be with you,  
These are orders from the States,  
They must be observed.  
Sweet love, are these the bonds  
That you have made with me,  
Now I must go into disgrace  
Before the whole world.  
Oh love, come to my aid,  
And do not let me be shamed,  
I will never leave you,  
Offer me your hand in loyalty.  
It has been nine months ago  
That you came into the garrison,  
Then I consigned myself  
To a Malprade man of decency,  
Who knew how to steal my heart  
Through his pleasant speech,  
Then I was won over by sweet play  
With a little child.  
Sweet love, you must know this,  
That is how we have become so accustomed,  
So that you will not forget  
Malprade’s regiment.  
I have had a son by you,  
And when he is able,  
Send him to Malprade,  
He will make him a soldier.  
The time has come  
That we must march,  
My heart is full of hesitation,  
I hear the drum beating,  
And must go  
To my captain’s quarters.  
Adieu, exalted sweet love,  
I wish you much pleasure.

label: work_soldier
translation:
My lover is a young man,  
Created for the fight,  
His arm is devoted to the Fatherland,  
His love and heart dedicated.  
He says: can I in my Fatherland,  
See myself perish humiliated.  
My heart, my eye calls: Edward!  
But my tongue remains silent.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Vivat Bataave, let us sing,  
To the honor and glory of the Fatherland,  
No Briton or Emperor can force us,  
Dwinglandy is bound to us,  
We return and bring with us,  
The long-desired Peace of ours.  
Pursued her on all sides  
With saber and pistol in hand.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The fourth half Brigade proud,
Still has troops in need,
Boys come with great pleasure
The pay was offered to you
Just enlist for the Batavian Fusilier.
Fight for your dear Fatherland
A white cap is planted in my heart,
As long as there is one in our Land.

label: work_soldier
translation:
When I deserted /  
I came to Mechelen /  
The French caught me /  
I thought something would come of it.  
And when I came to the Tribunal /  
What was read out to me there /  
Eight years in the irons, whether willing or unwilling /  
I thought that couldn’t be accepted.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
The Colonial Artillerymen full of courage,  
They also still have people in need,  
Come lads with great pleasure,  
The enlistment bonus is offered to you with pleasure,  
Just enlist as a Colonial Artilleryman.  

2.  
How well that uniform suits us,  
A cap with a red plume,  
There with a red linden leaf attached,  
You can get girls, three or four at a time,  
If you are a Colonial Artilleryman.  

3.  
When we are stationed in the garrison,  
The girls are quite content,  
An Artilleryman can satisfy her,  
And then they give her heart and hand,  
And they pledge her honor as a guarantee.  

4.  
Our brave Officers full of courage,  
They also risk their lives,  
When we go to the East,  
And if we lack or are in great need,  
They give us gin, powder, and lead.  

5.  
For nothing is greater pleasure,  
Oh sir, than the Colonial Artillerymen,  
There you can live in great pleasure,  
So young lads, come from all around,  
Just enlist for our brave Battalion.  

6.  
Brave heroes, stay constant,  
For I will cease writing,  
Fight for your dear Fatherland,  
An Artilleryman is planted in my heart,  
As long as there is one in our country.  

7.  
All who wrote this little song,  
They are two Amsterdam girls,  
And their little heart was light,  
And they gladly enjoy pleasure,  
With a Colonial Artilleryman.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
What will our Gunners drink,  
When they are with those black girls,  
Arak from silver mugs,  
Will our Gunners drink,  
Captain, Lieutenant,  
Ensign, Sergeant,  
Drummer, Corporal,  
All Gunners,  
Soldiers, Comrades.  

2.  
How will our Gunners fight,  
When they are with those black girls,  
Yes, with swords with silver hilts,  
Will our Gunners fight,  
Captain, Lieutenant, etc.  

3.  
How will our Gunners fire,  
When they are with those black girls,  
With bombs and mortars,  
Will our Gunners fire,  
Captain, Lieutenant, etc.  

4.  
What will our Gunners wear,  
When they are with those black girls,  
Blue jackets with red collars,  
Will our Gunners wear,  
Captain, Lieutenant, etc.  

5.  
How will our Gunners flourish,  
When they are with those black girls,  
Yes, like the greatest Lords,  
Will our Gunners flourish,  
Captain, Lieutenant, etc.  

6.  
Where will our Gunners sleep,  
When they are with those black girls,  
On a little bed with satin sheets,  
Will our Gunners sleep,  
Captain, Lieutenant,  
Ensign, Sergeant,  
Drummer, Corporal,  
All Gunners,  
Soldiers, Comrades.

label: work_soldier
translation:
We are patriots,  
We serve the Fatherland,  
We camp in the barracks,  
By the water's edge,  
We have a company,  
Of fifty-five men,  
We serve for adventure,  
All for our Fatherland.  
As soon as we fall ill,  
Then we must go to prison.  
Come, let us now decide,  
It is the will of God,  
One cannot object to it,  
For see, it is our fate.  
Therefore, my dear friends,  
We will go to fight,  
And we will willingly commit ourselves,  
To be under the Emperor’s power.

label: work_soldier
translation:
I used to be a Student,  
But now I am a Soldier,  
Who fears neither hell nor devil,  
Much less my mate,  
I did not like studying at all,  
So I left it behind,  
I have never had more fun,  
Than among the Soldiers.  
The drum beats in the quarter,  
Sweet love, we must part,  
Because they expect me there,  
I can no longer stay.

label: work_soldier
translation:
There was a young soldier,  
Not yet twenty years old,  
Who, after first arriving for service,  
Was to take up his third watch,  
Unexpectedly stood at his post,  
Singing a cheerful song.  
And kindly greeted him with good day,  
“How long will you remain at this post?”  
“In about an hour I will be relieved,”  
They described a place to speak to him.  
He was brought before a court-martial,  
But found no help to his benefit,  
His sentence was pleaded,  
He would be punished,  
Shot dead with a bullet.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
Up, up, Sons of the Netherlands!  
Now is the right time,  
Now you can first show;  
For whom you are;  
Fight now for Orange,  
That is the patriotic courage.  
Up, up, then banish,  
That French brood,  
To arms now, heroes!  
Gird yourselves for battle:  
Now you can first show;  
For whom you are.  

2.  
Show yourselves Batavians!  
And follow their trail,  
The flags that wave,  
The brave go first;  
Let heroic courage appear,  
The French scum,  
Must once succumb;  
Then you return home:  
Then you find, what glory!  
Wherever you are,  
Orange’s victory,  
And the country’s safety.  

3.  
It is not for the Emperor;  
No tyrant’s yoke,  
No harder than iron,  
Gnaws at your happiness:  
You are not led away,  
Guided by murderousness;  
No, that is disappearing;  
So show yourself ready,  
The country calls to arms;  
Your own happiness,  
Come on young lads,  
Break the yoke.  

4.  
Orange! – the blessing;  
The glory of our country,  
Received from God;  
Reaches out to you again,  
You see yourself rewarded,  
Your fatherland free,  
With laurels crowning you,  
And you will be happy again;  
Just as before;  
Now William commands,  
No longer fear tyranny,  
That eternally flees.  

5.  
Come on! brave mother,  
Speak courage into your son!  
Who does not get angrier,  
Thinking of the insult;  
Of the murder and the robbery,  
By the plunderers' rot;  
Revenge comes from above,  
It is the Dutch God;  
Who will fight with you,  
Wherever you go;  
Visibly guiding you;  
Defeating your enemy.  

6.  
Come on, brave women!  
Would you always remember your husband,  
The fatherland?  
No, that cannot be;  
He fights for your life,  
Your property, and your offspring;  
And this must give you,  
A double comfort,  
Then rouse him to fight;  
And once you will see,  
The whole of Holland freed;  
From French tyranny.  

7.  
Up, up Sons of the Netherlands!  
The fatherland speaks,  
Come, now show,  
What courage is in you;  
Orange must live!  
To the blessing of the country,  
Drive away the French;  
Thus bind the bond,  
Of unity, rightly together,  
So Holland blooms;  
Which the French once took away,  
Once again to its growth.

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.  
The men take new courage again,  
The men take new courage again,  
Showing that the old heroic blood,  
Still flows through your veins,  
Still flows through your veins,  
Hurry to drive away,  
The French brood,  
On the footsteps of your Fathers.  

2.  
If ever, then it is now the time,  
If ever, then it is now the time,  
To fight with the ancient bravery,  
As vigilant warriors,  
As vigilant warriors,  
Full of heroic courage,  
All the French brood,  
To battle advantageously.  

3.  
Then take up arms with courage,  
Then take up arms with courage,  
And join the Orange banner,  
Drive away those wicked hordes,  
Drive away those wicked hordes,  
Expel for good,  
All the French brood,  
You will soon see them flee.  

4.  
Orange awaits it from your hand,  
Orange awaits it from your hand,  
That you, for the good of the Fatherland,  
As brave and courageous men,  
As brave and courageous men,  
Fulfill your duty,  
To banish the French brood,  
From the Netherlands.  

5.  
And whoever cannot follow the war,  
And whoever cannot follow the war,  
Show himself a noble-hearted man,  
By cheerfully contributing,  
By cheerfully contributing,  
To drive away swiftly,  
The entire troop,  
Of the French brood.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The joyful cheer is raised,  
Thunderously it rolls along the shore,  
Good and blood and dear life,  
We dedicate to the Fatherland;  
Let it sound over the waves,  
Hail to Fatherland and King,  
If sea foam splashes in our hair,  
Holland's heart glows in the chest,  
Brave sons of the sea,  
Sing your songs while sailing,  
All the brave sing along. (repeat)

Our brothers bear the weapon,  
Maintain Holland's old authority,  
We are made for the sea,  
And protect our flag,  
If steel is sharpened on the shore,  
That resists force and deceit,  
It is the thunder of our ships,  
That strikes in the heart of the wicked;  
Brave sons of the sea,  
Boldly grasping the fuses,  
All the brave fight along. (repeat)  
Yes, on the banks of the Scheldt  
A costly toll was paid,  
You from Speyk made Holland prevail,  
Proud against a dreadful overwhelming force,  
Gloriously the cry was heard:  
Better to meet death as booty,  
Than to lose a flag and honor,  
And you extinguished the fire in the powder;  
Brave sons of the sea,  
Is this fate also reserved for us?  
All the brave die along. (repeat)

Yes, by God! that we all swear!  
However betrayal may twist its cunning,  
Fighting we may one day fall,  
We will never lower flags!  
Let it sound over the waves,  
For a future countryman,  
That once there were sailors,  
Who did not fear death  
Brave sons of the sea,  
Sing a Dutch song while sailing,  
All the brave sing along. (repeat)

label: work_soldier
translation:
No rank grants to man  
That joy, that happy life,  
Which he, a good soldier,  
A true hero can give.  
The warrior who in the field,  
Serves and honors his King,  
Fears no danger or hell,  
Or whatever else may harm him.  
Schlenredin, Schlenredin, Schlenredin.

No splendid house or castle  
Do I want as my dwelling;  
In my tent on straw  
I sleep like a king;  
My head rests on my property,  
The cloak is my cover,  
And I sleep peacefully and softly  
Until the drummer wakes me.  
Schlenredin, etc.

When the enemy’s army advances,  
And thunders around us,  
Then for us comes the hour  
Of true joy appearing,  
One advances in the wide field,  
One fights for land and king.  
And no power of the enemy  
Can liberate the free heart.  
Schlenredin, etc.

When I have died,  
Then let me be buried  
With drum, trumpet, and song,  
Followed by fifty brave men.  
Three salvoes are given to me  
At last on the hero’s path,  
And this soldier’s honor  
Leads me to the kingdom of heaven.  
Schlenredin, etc.

label: work_soldier
translation:
To battle, my brothers! To battle we go,  
What would make our hearts tremble?  
To battle, my brothers! The hour breaks again,  
The signal of the fight has been given.  
No fear grips the free chest,  
It concerns the honor of Fatherland and Bond.

To battle, my brothers! To battle we go,  
For parents, for maidens, or home;  
To battle, my brothers! We must defeat  
The ungrateful ones of Land and King.  
To battle, the heart calls for revenge,  
To battle, it is a sacred cause!

To battle, my brothers! To battle we go,  
We must win or die;  
To battle, my brothers! That, even in our deeds,  
Earns the praise of posterity.  
The sword, my brothers! In the hand,  
You fight for Prince and Fatherland!

label: work_soldier
translation:
Forward be our only pursuit,  
Forward comrades! in the field;  
Never shall courage abandon us,  
Forward always the true hero.

label: work_soldier
translation:
O Lord! Where may my lover be,  
Who has cherished me for about six years?  
Who can heal all my sorrows,  
Simply through pure affection;  
Who, in the prime of his years,  
Had to march for his Majesty,  
Oh, what a regret.  
Who can restore my misfortune?  
Oh sad day, oh sad hour!  
When he came to say farewell to me for the last time,  
Farewell, goodbye, my precious creature!  
Farewell, my love, my valuable treasure on earth,  
The law calls me, I take up arms,  
For I must go.

At his departure, he called out sorrowfully:  
Receive this kiss of farewell and tears,  
I thought the earth was splitting open,  
When he disappeared from my sight;  
Now he hovers, now stands before my eyes,  
My dear friend, who disturbs my sleep,  
Disturbs my sleep.

No day or night can pass,  
Without seeing him standing before my eyes,  
And by day he must drill,  
And at night he must keep watch.  
I will mourn his misfortune,  
Until he returns with consent,  
I am content.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Lovers! Hear how a Flemish girl,  
For Napoleon his Majesty,  
Went to risk her blood;  
She said: I also have the courage  
To carry a rifle,  
With a cockade on her hat.

Her lover fell in battle,  
Since he was eighteen years old;  
With a heart full of sorrow,  
She said; sweetheart! Do not be alarmed,  
We must part,  
I am going with you to the battlefield.

As her lover had gone away,  
She immediately put on men's clothes,  
Without hesitation,  
Followed her lover to the city of Ghent,  
Presented herself  
As an unknown cadet.

She served as a hunter for about three years,  
Completely unknown to her lover,  
They had to march,  
With the army going to Germany,  
Her suffering,  
The girl endured it constantly.

She attended many battles,  
And showed her bravery,  
Like a lioness,  
She cried, I must help win  
On the battlefield.  
For I am a great hero.

Before Austerlitz, that famous battle,  
She was wounded and brought back;  
They went to dress her wounds  
At the same camp,  
There it was found  
That she was an honorable daughter.

Her lover said: what can this be?  
Is that not the dearest of mine?  
Whom I see dying?  
No, she said: there is no danger,  
If only I could win  
Your heart before my death.

It was announced to the General,  
Everyone praised her courage completely;  
How can it be,  
That a woman shows her bravery,  
Speak, do not fear,  
So that your courage may be rewarded.

My General! I have now for three years  
Served out of love for my lover,  
May I dare ask you  
For my lover’s passport?  
I was free from harassment,  
We became a pair together.

The General said: dear child!  
Because you truly love him,  
I will agree,  
Live then happily with your bride,  
And I will honor you,  
A wedding celebration to conclude.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Napoleon! where is your glory?  
Your name is no longer Napoleon;  
Your heroic voice has vanished into darkness,  
Your fame sets like the sun.  

You were not satisfied with France,  
Spain also had to give up its crown,  
And all the German powers,  
Had to give way before your carriage.  

Your ambition, restrained by no power,  
Wrote all kinds of laws,  
Your Eagles, winged with glory,  
Penetrated the heart of Russia.  

The proud Moscow already saw your banners  
Planted on the great Kremlin,  
A path to St. Petersburg to carve for yourself,  
That was not for your mighty hand.  

Yes, that you with your castles in the air,  
Your proud riders and cannons,  
Like a water bubble saw playing,  
That did not occur to you, Napoleon.  

That Holland would dare to venture,  
The Prince, Prefect, and Adjutant,  
To drive away Customs and Overseers,  
That all came through your folly.  

That Bavaria’s brave armies,  
Generously awarded with crosses of honor,  
Would withdraw from your side,  
That you had never imagined.  

That Prussia would recover its lost states,  
Would win again in a battle,  
And Saxony would leave your party,  
That was a thunderclap for you.  

Emperor! you lay down your arms,  
Omnipotence is now on your side,  
Returned to your honor and duties,  
Made people free from your yoke.  

You had shed enough blood now,  
Dragged out the war long enough,  
And on the list of Frederick’s greats,  
You earned a heroic name.  

You let your sword rest forever,  
Offered your conquerors peace,  
You saw foreign people on France’s coasts,  
Oh Emperor! it is over with you.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Oh, what a beautiful and sweet delight,  
When fate offers me its hand,  
Ah, just help me onto the wagon,  
But above all, do not break my little bottle;  
I hold it in great esteem,  
More precious than my fairest bride,  
This little bottle is my treasure on earth,  
It constitutes my entire wealth.  (chorus)

What do you have hidden in there?  
It is after all not drink or wine?  
A glass bottle causes you much concern,  
What could be better than that?  
My friend! I will tell you:  
This little bottle is worth everything to me,  
It cannot be bought with gold,  
The good Prince who was thirsty.  (chorus)

At Waterloo, I saw him fight,  
Brave as the greatest hero,  
I saw that he had to suffer great thirst,  
I was already next to him on the field;  
Then I quickly offered, without hesitation,  
My little bottle, quite black with gunpowder,  
He took it from me,  
The good Prince drank from it there.  (chorus)

A bullet struck him down,  
Motionless he lay on the ground,  
They carried him from the battlefield,  
The blood flowed from his wound;  
He cried: do not break the little bottle,  
For I cherish it as my bride,  
Keep it under my blanket,  
The good Prince drank from it.  (chorus)

If I should die from that wound,  
Then place the little bottle by my side,  
Even in death I shall not lose it,  
Even in the grave it stays with me.  
The traveler will tell you,  
This little bottle is always worth everything,  
It cannot be bought with gold,  
The good Prince was spared.  (chorus)

label: work_soldier
translation:
I used to be a student,  
But now I am a little soldier,  
Who fears neither hell nor devil, (repeat)  
Much less his mate.  

I never liked studying at all,  
So I left it,  
I never had more fun, (repeat)  
Than among the soldiers.  

I stayed for a very long time in the city of Ghent,  
In garrison,  
And my captain was quite content, (repeat)  
He had no complaints about me.  

I also exercised diligently,  
Presenting the rifle,  
And a beautiful sweet young girl, (repeat)  
I can caress.  

I caressed her all night,  
The sun came through its rays,  
And Venus with a fierce blow, (repeat)  
Descended from Heaven.  

The drum sounded in the quarter,  
I had to part from my sweetheart,  
Because she awaited me here, (repeat)  
It could not be tolerated any longer.  

There lives a girl in the city,  
Who was to my desire,  
I had long had a fancy for her, (repeat)  
I have stayed attached to her.  

I asked her, sweet from love,  
To kiss her cheeks,  
She said: young man! a young soldier, (repeat)  
That is now my desire.  

The doctor told me,  
That I must kill the drink,  
Caressing the girl, (repeat)  
He also forbade me.  

If I must abandon the drink,  
Lose the dear girls,  
I said; Doctor, greetings to you, (repeat)  
I would much rather die.  

The drink makes my heart healthy,  
But no apothecary’s trinket,  
I said: Doctor, go to the ground, (repeat)  
Because you think you will kill me.

label: work_soldier
translation:
I am a Dutch soldier  
(This I once swore,)  
Who, averse to riot and betrayal,  
Serves the fatherland,  
And will defend the king’s throne.  

I swear it still with heart and mouth,  
God, let that oath endure!  
House of Orange, Dutch soil,  
That soil where once my cradle stood,  
I will remain faithful always.  

Yes, I want to dedicate  
My youthful life to king and fatherland,  
And if this heritage is ever attacked,  
Then faithfully, true to my oath,  
I will fight for land and king.  

When the Dutch army goes to battle,  
With William’s valiant sons,  
I want to be a brave hero,  
Who boldly faces the enemy,  
And proves worthy of them.

label: work_soldier
translation:
What will our Patriots eat,  
when they are in the army?  
Roasted chickens with pastries  
will our Patriots eat.  
Captain, lieutenant, ensign, sergeant,  
drummer, corporal –  
Patriots, comrades.  

Where will our Patriots sleep,  
when they are in the army?  
On a bedcover with fine sheets  
will our Patriots sleep.  
Captain, lieutenant, ensign, sergeant,  
drummer, corporal –  
Patriots, comrades.  

With what will our Patriots play,  
when they are in the army?  
With cards and beautiful masks  
will our Patriots play.  
Captain, lieutenant, ensign, sergeant,  
drummer, corporal –  
Patriots, comrades.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Here he hears the sorrowful complaints,  
Of a son against his mother,  
When he had to go to the battlefield,  
The sister weeps for her brother,  
The father, full of objections, said my dearest child,  
In your childhood years, where have you deserved it,  
That you must part from us for your life,  
My heart sighs, my heart sighs,  
You are sadly your mother’s first fruit.  

The mother who called out, oh dearest child!  
Must you go among the soldiers,  
I have loved you very tenderly from a young age,  
Must you now leave your mother,  
I have nursed you tenderly with my breasts,  
It will cost me my life,  
My own flesh and blood,  
At eighteen years you must bear arms,  
Oh what sorrow, oh what sorrow,  
That many a mother sees from her children.  

Had I in your youth,  
Rather given you the earth,  
Then I would have been freed from that burden,  
I feel my blood and veins trembling,  
Must I see you march,  
With your child’s mind,  
Risk your life, against the enemy,  
Fight in the field, whom I loved so dearly,  
My son still small, my son still small,  
Must you be separated from your mother?  

Mother! It is my sorrowful fate,  
That I must leave you, oh parents!  
Think that it is the will of God,  
Sighing and complaining will not help,  
Take comfort dear mother and father! Have patience,  
Sister pray for your brother,  
If you consider it,  
Then I will ask for the blessing,  
In my thoughts, in my thoughts,  
That God will grant me courage and strength.  

Child, depart in the name of God,  
I will give the benediction,  
And be patient in your fate,  
Heaven spare your young tender life,  
Will you ask your father,  
For the blessing for the last time, As you have been obedient every day!  
Farewell then my first little son,  
You remain our child,  
Of eighteen years, of eighteen years,  
Depart with the other youth swiftly.

label: work_soldier
translation:
I am a young soldier,  
Of twenty-two years,  
Born in the country, traldera,  
Here lies my fatherland, traldera,  
Born in the country,  
Here lies my fatherland.

He went into battle then,  
Had to risk his life there,  
He was wounded there, traldera,  
He fell to the ground,  
He was wounded there,  
He fell to the ground.

Come doctor? surgeon?  
Heal me from the pain,  
I am not yet ready,  
For the final battle, traldera,  
I am not yet ready,  
For the final battle.

I lay down my weapons,  
With my fine clothes,  
I lay down my weapons,  
Even with my sidearm, traldera,  
I lay down my weapons,  
Even with my sidearm.

If I have now died,  
Then they must bury me,  
On the grave of honor,  
A grave of marble stone, traldera,  
On the grave of honor,  
A grave of marble stone.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Cartridge pouch, saber, and rifle,  
That alone does not make a Soldier:  
A heart full of courage, a heart full of honor  
That is what it really comes down to,  
My brave comrade!

A clear eye, a quick hand,  
A fearless spirit,  
A soul that burns for Freedom  
And love for the Fatherland: -  
There, boys! that's what counts.

Remember this, mustachioed corporal!  
When you are drilling: -  
Even if his copper and steel are dull,  
Even if his leather gear is somewhat faded...  
Such a Shooter is a Soldier!

label: work_soldier
translation:
1.

Three dice and a card,  
That is on Vreê our coat of arms;  
In war it is lance and sword,  
One long and sharp, one sharp and short:  
Mercy neither in the game nor in battle,  
That is what the Landsknecht is allowed.

2.

A beaker full of Rhine wine,  
That is what pleases us in peace;  
In war it may be water,  
Provided one rests in the enemy’s tents:  
But wine in peace and in battle,  
That is what the Landsknecht is allowed.

3.

A girl who can kiss playfully,  
That is the best booty in peace;  
In war let it be a stallion’s team  
Decked with gold and silver:  
Good catch in peace and in battle,  
That is what the Landsknecht is allowed.

4.

But what he does not desire in peace  
That is: to die in his bed!  
Death, which he most bravely honors,  
Calls in the clatter of the trumpet!  
He falls on the field and in the battle:  
That is what the Landsknecht is allowed!

label: work_soldier
translation:
Lovers! hear how a Flemish girl  
For Napoleon His Majesty  
Went to risk her blood;  
She said: I also have the courage  
To carry the rifle  
With a cockade on her hat.

Her lover, who fell in battle,  
Since he was eighteen years old,  
With a heart full of suffering,  
She said: Sweetheart! do not be distressed,  
We must part,  
I am going with you to the battlefield.

As her lover had left,  
She immediately put on men's clothes,  
Without hesitation  
She followed her lover to the city of Ghent,  
Presented herself  
As an unknown cadet.

She served as a rifleman for about three years,  
Completely unknown to her lover,  
She had to march,  
Go to Germany with the army,  
Face troubles,  
The girl endured them steadfastly.

She attended many battles,  
And showed her bravado,  
Like a lioness,  
She cried: must I on the battlefield  
Help to win,  
For I am a great hero.  
Before Austerlitz, that famed battle,  
She was brought back wounded,  
Her wounds were to be dressed,  
And at the same time, it was found  
That she was an honorable daughter.

Her lover said, what could this be?  
Is that not my dearest,  
Whom I see dying? -  
“No,” she said: there is no need  
If I may win  
Your heart before my death.

It was announced to the General,  
Everyone praised her bravery altogether,  
Wondering how it could be  
That a woman showed her courage,  
He said, do not fear  
So that your courage may be rewarded,

My General, I have now for three years  
Served out of love for my lover,  
May I ask you,  
For the passport of my lover;  
Then we would be free from troubles,  
They would then become a pair.

The General said: dear child!  
Because you love him sincerely,  
I will agree:  
Live happily then as his bride,  
And honor you  
With a wedding feast to conclude.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The four—the com—pany, it does not like to polish,  
and that is why it is so fat, yes, yes.  
The four—the com—pany, it does not like to polish  
and that is why it is so fat.  
The blowing only concerns that com—pa—ny,  
which has 4 as a digit or number.  
The good ones of that company are here  
called by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The four - the com - pany, she does not like to clean,
and that is why she is so fat, yes, yes.
The four - the com - pany, she does not like to clean
and that is why she is so fat.
The blowing concerns that com - pa - ny,
which has the 4 as figure or number.
The good ones of that com - pa - ny are called here
by 1, 2, 3, 4.

translation:
The fourth company doesn't like to clean,
and that's why she is so fat, yes, yes.
The fourth company doesn't like to clean
and that is why she is so fat.
The blowing only applies to that company
that has the number four.
The good ones of that company have been
called here by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The fourth company, it does not like to clean,
and that is why it is so fat, yes, yes.
The fourth company, it does not like to clean
and that is why it is so fat.
The blowing concerns only that company,
which has the number four.
The brave ones of that company are here
called by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The four - the company, they don't like to clean,
and that's why they are so fat, yes, yes.
The four - the company, they don't like to clean
and that's why they are so fat.
The blowing concerns that company only,
which has the number four.
The well-behaved ones of that company are here
called by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The four—the company—doesn't like to clean,
and that's why she is so fat, yes, yes.
The four—the company—doesn't like to clean
and that's why she is so fat.
The parade is for that company,
which has the number four.
The brave ones of that company are here
called by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
The Fourth Company doesn't like to clean,  
and that's why it is so fat, yes, yes.  
The Fourth Company doesn't like to clean  
and that's why it is so fat.  
The blowing only concerns that company  
that has the number four.  
The good ones of that company are called here  
by 1, 2, 3, 4.

label: work_soldier
translation:
You brave war heroes!  
Behold such a delicate, young maiden,  
As I come here to tell you,  
Who carries rifle and weapons. She risks her life!  
Her brother had to march, to go to the army,  
She heard him lament with a sad tear,  
She said: oh brother be content,  
With our parents' consent,  
I will go to the regiment for you,  
Unknown.

With the hand of father and mother,  
She put on her brother’s clothes,  
And for her brother,  
She went to the city of Ghent, listen,  
Where she served for 18 months with much courage and bravery,  
In the 17th division of His Majesty;  
But see, the ship was anchored,  
They sailed across the sea,  
To the colony with him,  
Quite peaceful.

In military service and parade  
She was praised so much on all sides,  
Whereby she rose to the ranks  
Of corporal and sergeant. Full of understanding.  
Everything she performed was as wished and beneficial,  
She fulfilled her duties as a good soldier.  
But see, the Moors, well manned,  
They started a revolt,  
She struck the enemy  
With sword in hand.

Our Dutchmen quickly  
Calmed the rebellion with courage;  
But our sergeant bravely,  
Was wounded in her chest, severely wounded,  
And her wounds were soon treated,  
They immediately discovered that it was a daughter,  
She was healed from her wound.  
The general at once  
Wrote a report to the king,  
Clear and prompt.

His Majesty exalted,  
Praised her virtue and great bravery,  
They gave her leave,  
And pension trained her, forever.  
Annually she received 110 Dutch guilders,  
She returned home with much pleasure, happily with her parents.  
Learn here, war heroes, who you are,  
Follow such a dear girl,  
In courage and bravery,  
At all times.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Hear, men! Hear the drum and the trumpets,  
It is no alarm, but simply a roll call;  
We shall now occupy a peace camp,  
Prince Frederik commands over us.  
As field marshal, so upright in virtue and morals,  
He is beloved, honored by everyone.  
You know how Prince Frederik has fought,  
So men, forward! when he commands. (repeat)

Come on, comrades-in-arms! Let us march bravely,  
And move forward to the camp near Zeist;  
A good soldier must also camp once in a while,  
It is William III, the King, who demands it.  
We must not neglect our duty there;  
Come, grasp your weapons firmly in hand,  
For if danger threatens our beloved land,  
Then we shall fight for free Netherlands.

And when we have happily arrived there,  
Tired from walking and also somewhat covered in dust,  
You will soon have received orders,  
Here is your tent and polish your weapons;  
And then quickly, like Dutch soldiers,  
With sponge and brush eagerly at the task,  
While another comes once again to talk  
About the garrison and his dear girl.  
Evening falls, the tattoo is sounded,  
And every soldier then goes to his tent,  
After inspection, the raging stops,  
Lights out, as has long been known.  
One sleeps soundly on the long feathers,  
Not as soft as in the garrison,  
But in the field one must do without some things,  
The Prince of Orange consents to that as well.

The dark night enveloped by the black floor,  
Is soon over, the morning breaks,  
The signal is given to us by the morning shot,  
That we must start picking up the straw,  
When that is done, quickly sling on your gear,  
Grasp the rifle, your knapsack and your hat,  
The sergeant of the week then says as he wishes,  
Come men, hang your bag and knapsack well.

And then: attention! but quickly march forward,  
Head held high! the command has been given;  
We do our best to parade properly,  
For otherwise one gets turns by the boat.  
But no, here we want to fulfill our duty,  
So that the exercise may go well,  
And when the drum beats, we do not delay,  
With William III we will go to battle.

And now finally a Dutch song is sung,  
Long live the King! and also the House of Orange!  
May it flourish long and remain unscathed,  
From tyranny and also from foreign rabble,  
The Dutch blood flows freely through our veins,  
That remains the feeling, we have pledged ourselves;  
And should the enemy unexpectedly pursue us,  
Then we fight for Prince and Fatherland.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Rich. Thiele.  
So many a young soldier who is almost desperate,  
While he is lying in quarters in Naarden, what pleasure!  
He thinks, that young Bram,  
If only I were in Amsterdam;  
And with the first train, he quietly slips away.  

Both the infantryman and the cavalryman  
He amuses himself madly and then stays on the roll.  
That lasts a short while,  
Then he ends up in the box,  
Now complain and lament those  
From the infantry.  
Us from the cavalry,  
We are not hindered by such a thing!  

Now complain and lament those  
From the infantry,  
Us from the cavalry,  
We are not hindered by such a thing!

label: work_soldier
