We hear of twenty-five years,  
Accompanying each other in care and joy,  
Hardly can one believe this; (repeat)  
But what is written everywhere  
Is undeniably true, although  
It surpasses our understanding, (repeat)  

But no, not only the written page,  
Witnesses speak more of what  
You can point to here: (repeat)  
They adorn your Wedding Feast,  
While their joyful tone, loud and fresh,  
May rise to honor you. (repeat)  

Though many a royal crown is displayed,  
A sparkle of precious stones shown,  
That can dazzle the eyes: (repeat)  
Still your crown shines with a brighter gleam,  
For pearls, in a silver wreath,  
You may find on your Marriage Coach. (repeat)  

A line of friends sincere,  
Feels your joy and rejoices in it,  
And praises the blessed day; (repeat)  
Oh may you, after this happy state,  
Always jubilant in new marital bonds,  
Enter your path with joy. (repeat)  

Happiness always accompanies and crowns,  
Your parental love, your care, and diligence,  
Always rewards you through love; (repeat)  
And may, after twenty-five years,  
Love and friendship, worthy Couple!  
Crown your crown with gold. (repeat)

label: wedding
translation:
We do not want to separate yet,  
it is still a few hours too early,  
we want to wait  
from now until early tomorrow.  
Where has my love been for so long?  
For her sake  
I have been so deeply distressed.

label: wedding
translation:
Now let us all be grateful  
to our Lord, our God, our Father,  
who here has satisfied us all  
with bread, with wine,  
with flesh, with fish, with other food,  
prepared in many different ways!  
Love Him in this life!  
He will not abandon you.  
The Son of God be blessed,  
whom we also want to praise,  
who has freed us from sins,  
when we were lost,  
who has so mercifully satisfied us,  
and in distress does not abandon us,  
place your trust in Him,  
you will not regret it.  
Thus we also thank the Holy Spirit,  
for His visitation,  
for had He not been with us,  
our joy would be tribulation;  
He is our comforter in sorrow,  
without Him we can do nothing.  
Prepare your heart for Him,  
He will not leave you.  
Thus we thank our dear bride,  
that she wants to stay with us,  
and we will drink this cup once more,  
the Lord wants to write it all upon me.  
The bow must not be kept drawn,  
I hope we will fare well,  
now is not the time to part,  
we want to avoid going.

label: wedding
translation:
What a wonderful life it is,  
To be the Groom and Bride,  
And to drink the cool wine,  
And to drink the cool wine,  
I think one would marry on purpose for that,  
To also have a wedding trip,  
Playmates follow the Bride;  
If you want to do well, then don’t argue.

2. The first man and Eve,  
Who married each other,  
If you want to do well, then follow her,  
If you want to do well, then follow her,  
For if our parents had not done that,  
We would not have even gone to the Wedding,  
Playmates follow the Bride,  
If you want to do well, then don’t argue.

3. I am amazed by this,  
That you waste your youthful time,  
So wasting it in loneliness,  
So wasting it in loneliness,  
Your mother brought you into the world,  
It is fair that you also try for that,  
Playmates follow the Bride,  
If you want to do well, then don’t argue.

4. Those kisses and caresses and strokes;  
The embracing in bed,  
That prevents the cold in winter,  
That crushes the fleas in summer,  
Therefore it is very well said,  
It isn’t right that you live alone,  
Playmates follow the Bride,  
If you want to do well, then don’t argue.

label: wedding
translation:
1.

Maartje’s expensive pill box,  
(Maartje’s silver purse)  
Had a diamond pen;  
And I believe that on that occasion  
It was full of golden coins.  
But it lasted only a few years,  
Before the coins were spent,  
And she kept no red coin...  
The lively girl had never heard  
Of saving!

2.

Guurtje received a stone piggy bank  
(Everything godmother could give!) —  
But it did not stay empty for long;  
Because she knitted and sewed and spun  
And gathered what she earned.  
And after a few, few years  
The piggy bank became so full  
That it burst with fat:  
The diligent girl knew about saving,  
What a joy!

label: wedding
translation:
I know a lonely little spot,  
That lies behind the church,  
The grasses and the herbs  
Grow there so densely.  

He, who nurtured my youth  
With kindness and understanding,  
The good Master rests there...  
The kindest in the land!  

And in the middle of that spot  
When I went, my hand  
Planted a rosebush  
In honor of the dear man.

label: wedding
translation:
Lime and stone that does build well,  
Soft lime and hard stones:  
It suits and fits and quickly applies  
To a firm and faithful union...  
Bride and Groom! Did you know it well?

If you were both as soft as lime,  
If you were both as hard as stones,  
Oh! it would have been in vain attempted,  
You would never unite...  
Wedding guests! Did you think about it well?

Blond and brown of cheek and hair,  
Soft and strong natures,  
Together make a precious pair,  
Join to form solid walls...  
Wedding guests! Come on, just try it!

label: wedding
translation:
1.

Klaartje was leaning over her front door,  
Japik, her neighbor, was standing there talking to her; –  
Was it about calves, was it about cows,  
Was it about a wedding or the fair?…  
I stood a bit far away, and they spoke softly;  
But she shook her head no,  
And she called out: ‘Would you stop that!’  
And she laughed with him.  

2.

I swear it was Easter time!… The nightingale sang,  
When I saw a bridal couple entering the church;  
Everyone gave smiles, everyone gave greetings…  
What was the pastor asking the bride?  
I stood a bit far away, and he preached softly;  
But she nodded yes,  
And she looked down,  
And she cried afterward.

label: wedding
translation:
1.

When you wash fine linen clean,  
Dear ladies! put it to the side,  
Know this, with a quick hand,  
Fold edge to edge just so,  
Pleat band on band just so,  
You bring the corners inward thus,  
So it makes a perfect square.

2.

But what is good with linen,  
Is even better in marriage:  
Oh! consider this, young woman!  
What in marriage might hinder you,  
Sharp edges, sorrow or grief,  
Strike them boldly and neatly inward,  
Lay them in the finest fold!

label: wedding
translation: Thrice blessed is the man who, after twenty-five years spent in marriage, still sees his wife smile and can boast that, although his hair is turning gray, he loves her as much as when she gave him the first kiss!

label: wedding
translation:
What does Amsterdam suffer for the unusual widening  
Of the Evening Star's radiance? Or do you imagine Heaven's voice,  
Which threatens the subjugated Earth with novelties?  
So it was accustomed: but this journey governs the earth.

It is that the Star, enticed by the loving gathering  
Of two beloveds, whom she comes to see to bed,  
Closely attending their service in the chamber to reveal,  
Sets more than a common torch in the bright West.

That Maiden guides it, who your Fame of Women  
Hippolyta on both Earths, and fearfully before flight  
First gave heart to trust her wings,  
And moves above the eagle’s head in the air.

O brave Mothers, brave Daughter, in the sense  
Of the Noble Youth smitten, Ida, Ida, Quekel’s blood!  
Go forth, go, comfort your Servant’s sorrow, and Love  
To atone, wisely, with long-desired good.

Flaming Bridegroom, see how the tender cheeks  
Of the well-born Bride burst with redness,  
This is your Evening Star, who the chamber bids to nakedness,  
Mixing sweet vexation with bitter friendship.

label: wedding
translation:
Dear Mistress of dominion,  
Do you see that maiden on the IJ,  
In the first days of her life,  
Whom your mother bore,  
Blushing from cakes, and the stroke  
Of that lovely smile?  

What worries does she have?  
Mind works, hands go.  
Her eyes are busy.  
Throat and lips are restless.  
Can a youth, fresh with years,  
Stand against so much effort?  

If she takes a diamond, a scratch  
Does the mute glass speak.  
See that thumb, with golden threads  
Grinding costly fabrics:  
Fingers wield pen, pencil:  
Knuckles tickle the many.  

See once, that tender little mouth,  
With the nuts, up and down:  
The eye glues itself to the letters:  
Mind submerged in the rhyme:  
Tongue bends in the sound  
Of the Roman and the French.

label: wedding
translation:
Lift up, my Muse, to delight us wonderfully,
With noble tone, the inseparable pair
Of the best-matched pair, that love rewards with love.
The world is in their circle. It is a festive celebration.
The noble headdress of the Amstel maidens
Is adorned; and the crown of the maidens is crowned.

label: wedding
translation:
Why do you sulk, Amsterdam, and stand as if distressed,
By the unusual brightness, and rare ornaments
Of the blazing evening star? Or do you imagine it to be the heavenly voice,
Which, through this glow, once more caused its rays,
Threatening the subjugated earthly realm with novelties?
It pains so. But this journey governs the earthly domain itself.

It is that the star, lured by exceedingly bright sparks
From two souls each given to the other,
Thus appears magnificently with flames without blemish;
And, out of desire to bring those loved ones together,
Duly fitting to declare their service in due time,
Sets a lamp more than common in the clear west.

O mother of Love, who does not shrink
From the fiftyfold pomp of worldly dominions,
To Junus or her consort, neither standing by staff nor crown,
But content with the state of the spiritual realm,
Who guides the souls' conduct, Pope of the heavenly songs,
Descends freely from above; here you find a better throne.

label: wedding
translation:
Fizz, fizz, cham-pag-ne!  
Fizz to bride and bridegroom honor!  
Stretch their tooth image,  
Stretch them to leather.  
That never their marriage cup contains?  
A troubled, dirty and muddy wet  
But always clear as the sea wine  
May their lives be.

label: wedding
translation:
Now wishes to prosper
Fruitful enterprises
You cities in Holland,
In Zeeland, Men and Women,
Engage
Fruitfully on every side,
To honor the exalted Prince
His noble wife Edel vermaert,
Whom God has granted grace
And faithfully preserves.

She has come to Brielle
Already with great triumph,
This the burghers have heard
Many shots were fired
For the Prince, the pious one,
They took her by both hands
They welcomed her well
Already in the Prince’s land.

When the Prince heard this,
He was delighted from his heart,
He sailed without hesitation,
To Brielle very swiftly,
His longing was for her,
The noble Prince’s treasure
He received his bride,
And greeted her kindly.

label: wedding
translation:
Brothers, want to praise the Lord,  
Rich and poor, young and old,  
For the blessing that from above  
Now passes over these lands,  
As this appears, listen to the reason  
At this very courageous wedding  
That took place, at that moment  
Wish to observe  
In the Court of England.

Great joy could be observed  
Of the princely lord,  
Count Palatine of the Rhine born,  
For he came with great desire,  
With so many allies  
And many lords he invited,  
They would go, understand well, to marry  
His princess out of great love.

Through Holland it was heard,  
That he traveled to England  
Where he was very welcome,  
Abundant with much joy.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Come around here, Shoemakers all,  
I must praise you,  
Who in the Holland valley  
Hinder themselves.  
Do not take offense from me,  
It is done to your honor.  

2.  
Saint Crispin has, for pleasure,  
Given us a Monday,  
So that we might drink wine and beer  
And moreover  
Be free from work that day  
As long as we live.  

3.  
There is no more joyful day  
For us on this earth,  
Which one may rightfully celebrate  
And hold in esteem.  
Thus, on this day  
One sees us accepting joy.  

4.  
First of all, near the Bottermarkt  
One sees us in whole groups  
Walking together like one heart  
To buy this and that.  
Meanwhile, there is also  
Merry drinking.  

5.  
Then from there to the Plantje  
Or to the Overtoompje,  
In the Prut-Gang near Marrie  
Or by Cornelis Oomtje,  
There one takes whatever one likes:  
Sweet milk and cream.  

6.  
Then onwards to the Doubeltjes tavern,  
Is this not a joyful life!  
This lasts from early morning  
Until evening without cease.  
Why not? Crispin has  
Given us that Monday.  

7.  
Then to the city, the Copper Mountain,  
There one goes to socialize,  
Or to the Dijkstraet in the Burg,  
That one must also see:  
As long as the money lasts,  
We will not yield to anyone.  

8.  
Had our Crispin, the old blood,  
Made every fifty ducats  
Already from his inheritance,  
Had he left that behind,  
Then we would drink without worry  
From stoups and barrels.  

9.  
The Els truly takes the crown  
And should rightly be praised:  
He helps us with food and wages,  
Shoemakers, right alongside,  
Through the whole world  
Wherever we want to be.  

10.  
Permission, Shoemakers, please handle your work  
In peace and quiet,  
So that on Mondays you may  
Merrily grease your bright chest.  
I wish that you may together  
Increase in joy.

label: work
translation:
I.  
The herb camomile is very twisted,  
Big and double and more,  
Many matters are often  
Also improved just as much,  
Through pressing, through beating,  
This even farmers know,  
Who deal with farmers.  

2. See how they move the herd,  
And swell through the plough,  
By which they bring from the earth  
Grain very good, much, and enough,  
But if the earth remains unquilted,  
Not ploughed, and not turned,  
It will be filled with all weeds,  
Which then are not seen as weeds,  

3. The plough iron often glistens,  
If it is well maintained  
Otherwise the rust will consume it,  
The work of the iron is shining,  
See the wings stand the grains,  
Because the grains stand,  
See the press produce the grapes,  
Through pressing comes the wine  

4. Through pounding spices,  
And pepper gains power,  
See the cloves, and the blades,  
Like the soul that does not despise,  
And patiently wants to endure,  
The soul will gain strength,  
Through pressing and beating,  
Heaven's wine and grain protect.  

5. Sweet comfort, wine, and grains;  
Glory, splendour, strength, and virtue,  
Bear the cross, and sad tears,  
Sorrow brings true joy,  
When the cross begins to plough the soul,  
It bears heaven’s fruits,  
Whoever wants to attach it to the cross,  
That pleases God the most.  

6. No one here is free from suffering,  
Neither the King, nor the Pope,  
Only those who rejoice joyfully,  
Who have patience for the sauce  
Patience, sauce, is very powerful,  
Patience, cross, is very good,  
This is certain and true,  
That it brings the greatest good.  

After suffering comes joy,  
Suffering is a great profit if one endures it all patiently.  
From necessity comes virtue,  
So you will be exalted in the end.

label: work
translation:
I.  
No state on earth is as noble here  
As the state of the farmer,  
Adam, the first lord, was a farmer just as well,  
The first man who in sweat of his face turned the plough,  
Then all kings and lords come after him,  
Yet no one is more than a farmer.  

2.  
Noah also, lord of the world, and father Abraham,  
The patriarchal line,  
Saul, and David, and the kings after them,  
Most of Israel were here farmers,  
Yet the farmers are mostly despised.  

3.  
The pope and bishop, emperor and king,  
And all those of stature,  
And practitioners alike, and also the merchants alike,  
All must always live on the farmer’s labor,  
Thus the noble farmers ought to be respected,  
Yet still the farmers are mostly despised.  

4.  
In times of war they are mostly plagued,  
Plundered and driven away,  
The farmer pays for everything, the farmer must suffer everything,  
Good farmers create courage still: it is God  
Who does all this,  
God wounds and heals; the more your pain here is,  
The more your joys hereafter shall be.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Who would not be cheerful and merry with me  
With fine, handsome, and fresh French wine  
And glasses filled to the brim!  
I feel my spirit kindled in cheerfulness,  
No worry disturbs my joyful and merry heart,  
That defies all disaster and sorrow.  

2.  
Long live the Printing and Book Selling,  
In Holland's Crown, the pearl on the IJ,  
So that we may gather here again in joy.  
Long lives the Art, found by the Spaaren  
That gives us the fine and tasty little Wine:  
Long live that art by which Lady Pallas lives.  

3.  
So, drink then, one for the other,  
With glass upon glass until the New Year,  
Spite those who envy us this cheerfulness.  
Thus, we live in these sorrowful times,  
In hope that hereafter, through the Press  
And Heaven's favor, we will gain more happiness.

label: work
translation:
Here come my friends already, in number,  
Hear what I shall sing to you,  
It is about twelve trades with joy,  
That I have learned in my youth,  
Yet there is not one that is righteous.

I made baskets strong, in the field,  
Early and eager at the work,  
That was my first style to recognize,  
That I have done in my life,  
I soon let that go.

I went with a coppersmith, shortly after,  
The hammer was too heavy,  
I had to strike there the whole day,  
I rejoiced when I saw the evening,  
Then I complained greatly.

I went to learn weaving, faint and quick,  
So I sat on my rope,  
Clack, clatter and it no longer suited me,  
I let the shuttle go,  
I moved on to another.

Shoe-making seemed good to me, in a hurry,  
I went to sew with a good spirit,  
All day I toiled so that sweat  
Broke out, it grieved me from my heart,  
The waxed thread I quickly broke.

I went forging like a hero, well set,  
I had to strike all with force,  
The sparks flew so fast,  
On my clothes and onto my skin,  
I thought that I was in hell.

After that a tailor, somewhat severe,  
I almost gained courage,  
I sat with my legs crossed,  
The master made me work neatly,  
I said quickly goodbye to the fleas.

I went to a mason, just from there,  
The mortar bucket was too heavy,  
The mortar flew from my trowel,  
In my backpack mostly,  
There I stood completely disheartened.

I walked with a carpenter master, like a hare,  
There I was late for a day!  
I had to also hammer and strike there,  
In the evenings lie down with tools,  
And then go home.

Then I learned to make nets, like a set,  
Picks, swords and muskets,  
To go to the war,  
I always feared, I don't know how,  
Then I became tired from work.

I went learning shell decking, with a stroke,  
Every shoemaker was the fool with me,  
I courted a beautiful girl or two,  
From that came nothing but pain and woe,  
Then I made a voyage at sea.

I went begging in the land, like a pauper,  
With a big stick in hand,  
Among thousands of lice and fleas,  
My naked skin was scratched,  
They wanted to harm my body.

Now I must go singing, if you understand,  
That suits me much better,  
All the profit and great gain,  
Runs down my throat,  
That goes so well according to my mind.

label: work
translation:
THE farmer's complaint, I have brought
In verse close to my liking,
How they live in pain,
It is truly to be lamented,
Because one asks everywhere,
Some in great numbers,
And the farmer falls,
The receiver wants to cheat, fully,
The farmer stands swollen,
The bailiffs cruel, completely,
Like dogs that stand growling,
The farmer who stands, when it is too late,
With a lawsuit, without excess,
Must bear the costs.

He is summoned and questioned,
Much more than he can give,
Alas! the poor farmer,
How could he possibly live?
He is often in pain,
Everyone calls: bring money to me,
And the farmer sells a pig,
His horse or other animals, they are spirits,
They take almost everything,
The attorney, who also comes,
All complaining and storming,
With a volume, put quite large,
It is all there, that dirty paper,
Pays that with the first.

The farmer very cruel, must beastly
Also pay slaughter money,
The tithes of his grain,
Or one would take the goods away,
It barely brings in anything,
Two bailiffs or a noose,
Will catch you by the head,
Even without long delay, lay you,
Until you will surrender,
In the prison, without abuse,
To the regret of those who oppress,
It is all about the money, that one torments you,
Brings upon, I will free you.

If the farmer is sick, he takes a drink
Straight from the apothecary,
The doctor also comes,
Prescribes warm cups,
Syrup for the leaky pot,
They give it to him, and it is not bad,
They mock the farmer,
They can tear and smear him well,
The farmer neither sees nor hears it,
Takes into his mouth, even if it were dung,
And dares not swear on it,
Tears his beard, quite undaunted,
Is it not to tear?

If he comes to town, to buy this or that,
Clothes to put on his body,
Someone promises him half,
Still is seen letting him go,
He is just a ragged farmer,
To rob him is now our turn,
I am sorry that he travels well,
So they pinch him, undervalue,
Is it not to be horrified,
Is it too dear for you, go free from here,
They are but small mice,
Leave my goods, quickly done,
I will show you the door.

Abbots, prelates, and advocates,
Counselors held high,
Clerks and attorneys,
It must all live off the farmer,
Craft guild and druggist,
Beggars who waste time,
Soldiers who seek strife,
With other flies that lie,
And also cheat the farmer,
Lord and lady, they very quickly stand,
With strikes, flouts, nods,
Can get the farmer, without uproar,
Also well caught in the net.

The farmer must finally
Play bankruptcy in the long run,
Is beastly and turns away,
No goods but many debts,
That the attorney has done,
Has badly caused him the process,
Now he must go for pennies,
Therefore wants to learn and study land law,
And no longer proceed,
Do my advice, no more and go,
To attorneys or gentlemen,
Pay your debts, and you will
Reign with joy.

label: work
translation:
Here comes our Yarn girl again, as usual,  
Look, dear, a little trip,  
What you can find with her, as fine as yarn,  
Of all kinds of colors  
And all kinds of needles,  
You will find the finest with her;  
Also laces of many kinds  
And beautiful hatbands,  
Thimbles and sewing rings,  
Cases and needles therein,  
Acorns two and fourfold,  
Each according to his taste.

Hear her throat stir, she calls this a bargain,  
For citizens and farmers  
And calls many people together;  
Four ells for a stiver, as fine as hatbands,  
Netted and pure acorns,  
Strands of all kinds of yarn;  
Now prepare your handkerchiefs  
And choose as you like,  
Also mousebands and cords,  
A case for a penny,  
A lace for an ear,  
All two ells long.

A thimble for two pennies,  
Two needles for a penny;  
She peeks out your heads,  
She looks together at citizens,  
A strand of fifteen times,  
An ear, the money is small,  
Who would desire this here,  
To be embarrassed?  
Now friends, would you like a trip,  
Supplied by the yarn girl,  
It may never hurt you,  
She is not always here;  
Buy well for holes and tears,  
It is bread earning.

label: work
translation:
Walk on, yes men, just give it up!  
Walk on, today I am in a mild mood,  
From Emperors and Kings) presents  
My deeds and glory, with pen and hand,  
I travel the world from land to land,  
Nearly everywhere. Is there anyone  
Who wants to pull  
My bold mind,  
And pleasant style, by tooth  
Or molar, to try?  
The one there, (I swear it to you by my will)  
I will gently lay with this spindle.  
Or, if you are troubled with sinkings,  
I will drive them away for little money.  
3.  
But quietly. I sailed there west and east,  
This Poujer I call the Woman’s Comfort,  
Because she helps the man on horseback,  
And the woman very lively under the earth:  
She cools and stokes the wind; she hastens  
The work, when one rows to full extent,  
So that the Sheriff at night can sleep without trouble;  
And she gives the Woman new strength,  
That ball, Can almost  
Drive away the filthy spots,  
(I mean from clothes) that pill, mixed with beer,  
Broken and warmed on the fire,  
Drives away the torment of worms:  
There crawl a thousand in a quarter hour.  
That balm, melted, and man or woman  
Rubbed on nipple, on knee or joint,  
For about five hours, Before the fire, very tight;  
Is a great remedy,  
And of a strong operation in the body.  
Consumption, catarrh, humor, bad breath,  
And teething or biting, it takes flight;  
Runny sores, the yellow and dropsy;  
Even if you are as thick as a barrel, and swollen  
With water; you pee buckets full!  
4.  
Now then curious Lovers, yes!  
Yes! buy supplies before I go;  
For gout, lameness, gout, with a  
For all kinds of fevers, gravel and stones;  
For deafness, ringing and buzzing in the ear,  
For running eyes, bad stomach, and for  
All mucus, which causes bad constipation,  
And prevents the churning of the stomach.  
For cold, For thrush,  
For stinking throats and dirty mouth,  
For cuts and stabs, swelling and wound,  
For bites from fleas and mad dogs,  
For pimples in the face, purple and multicolored,  
For sores and ulcers, it immediately makes,  
Bad sores and cancer healthy and clean,  
It drives away scurvy and the flare-up.  
It is a medicine, it is a medicine,  
It is a medicine  
For all kinds of sovereign pain!  
Colic and pleurisy must go away quickly.  
Therefore Lovers, all of you who are lacking,  
Buy now before you are cheated;  
Throw away, are you tired of your torment and suffering?  
But everyone a glove or handkerchief too.

label: work
translation:
One now hears every day,
Throughout the land, in boat, on wagon,
Complaints about bad weaving;
Therefore I sit very dejected,
And must through these afflictions,
Wear robbed clothes,
And live soberly:
For everything here,
Is very expensive and dear,
Be it flax, be it linen, be it wool,
Be it meat, bread, beer,
And moreover other goods,
How can a person thrive,
Who weaves; and with bad yarn
Is constantly plagued;
Is it a wonder that he complains;
For linen and woolen cloth,
To make something new again,
And I do not know how to get
The goods I once knew,
To make a garment for myself,
I wished for no better thing
Than to turn my meager cake
Against hunger,
Oh, if only my eye that day,
That joyful time once saw,
That I was in sweet company:
As cheerful as I used to be,
In fine company might be,
So sweet as before;
If I could read the planets,
I would once see:
Whether such things would happen.
My thoughts are in vain,
I would through these complaints,
Despise weaving greatly,
And thus gape my mouth!
Let me beware of that,
While every night,
To lighten the body,
I try to sleep in linen;
No craft is as good,
As beautiful as weaving is,
Although one steps on it with the foot,
One lies lighter before the hat:
For no one without madness,
Can wear a fine shirt,
Or shall uncover his head:
But he who makes it,
Is scorned and blamed.

label: work
translation:
Come here, lovers of stockings,
Do not pass by my shop,
Here is for Turks, Jews, and Gypsies:
For burghers, farmers, or dandy,
My stall is well stocked,
Whatever a count or noble desires;
No countryman can come here,
Whether he pretends any color,
Whether single or double-layered:
No matter how he might dream it.
If you seek a decent little stocking,
Pleuntje will please you,
Step freely into my shop, brave one,
Here are both soft and stiff:
Here they are very finely picked,
Darned, embroidered, and fringed;
Entirely rough and also very smooth,
For Germans or French, Moors or Scotch
Who ride on horseback or go on foot;
For Scots and for Walloons.
No color on earth is to be named,
Or is obtainable here,
Do not pass by my shop briskly:
But step in without hesitation;
Ash gray, bright blue, and thunder green,
That I have in the newest fashion,
Pure white, also red, and gray,
I have them open with a hole,
Whether elongated, round, or flat,
For fools and for wise men.
My stockings just made with stitch and needle,
With buttons according to fashion,
For boatmen or for soldiers,
Whoever needs them,
Look at the color, look at the thread,
See inside the leg, how fine the seam:
Where one can aspire,
Step in, gentlemen, here you have it all,
Do you want wide, narrow, broad, or slim?
Pleuntje is waiting for you.

label: work
translation:
The Farmer and Countryman must always be honored,
And praised for his honest state,
He is poor and sometimes in bad clothes,
Yet rightly he surpasses all,
Popes, Prelates, and Potentates,
Noble by birth, do not disdain the Countryman.
As Adam worked and Eve labored,
Tell me, where was the Nobleman then,
The Farmer alone spread mankind's seed,
Therefore I praise the Countryman wherever I can,
He must give everything, so everyone can live,
For all good comes from the wise Countryman.

3. The Farmer must dig and sow day and night
And plow the land, preparing the fields,
To harvest his grain during the harvest,
He feeds both people and animals everywhere,
Princes and the Brave must also be nourished by him,
They bow to him, Long live the good Countryman.

4. Popes, Cardinals, Bishops, Prelates,
Abbots, stewards, pastors and monks,
Chaplains, sextons—in fact all states,
Doctors and even Canons,
Must be fed by the Farmer, he is worthy of praise,
They bow to him, Long live the good Countryman.

5. How could the King wage war
And go to battle against his enemies,
If all the money and goods did not come from the Farmers,
All trade would stand still,
All the soldiers, Germans, Croats,
The good Countryman also must feed them.

6. How could Princes and Noble Lords
Get so many delicate foods
With the ladies cheerfully banqueting,
Oranges and the best must,
Chickens, capons, delicious lemons,
He must provide, Long live the good Countryman.

7. What would all the merchants do,
The innkeepers, brewers, and shopkeepers,
Without the Farmer they could manage nothing,
How could they sell wares without the Farmers,
No one can stir without the good Countryman,
Who must feed us all together.

8. The miller with his wife, maids, and servants
Would starve and perish of hunger,
Not a single soldier could fight
Where he spends three or four months without grain,
The Farmer must bring it in, they must grind,
They bow to him, Long live the good Countryman.

9. What would the Baker and Brewer do,
If they could not buy grain, barley, and malt,
Without the Countryman no one can succeed,
Because no one can bake without wood,
Brewer and Baker live from the field,
Tip him the foot, Long live the good Countryman.

10. Finally, a salute to you Farmers and Farmer’s wives,
I have praised you here in general,
I hope you all shall win Heaven,
Take good care and it is a principle,
Always worthy of praise, do not be proud,
Live in humility, always praise sweet Jesus.

label: work
translation:
Wits and learned people, I must praise you,  
And all who handle the noble art of the pen,  
I must show you worthy respect and praise,  
Singing your praise that soars to Heaven,  
No art of the world is more esteemed,  
Than wisdom is, long live those who must write.  

2. Councillors highly esteemed and such,  
The Advocate and State Prosecutor,  
Clerks and other men of practice,  
All who use ingenuity with the noble pen,  
I must honour you and respect you,  
Long live the man who can write properly.  

3. The art of the pen is very highly regarded,  
For without it faith would perish,  
I praise all who strive for learning,  
I consider fools those who scorn the art,  
All traffics must give way,  
Before this art, long live those who first began it.  

4. The praise can never be fully written  
Of those who first discovered the art,  
Reason and wisdom would have been lost,  
It is the noble pen that gives beautiful treasures,  
Writing and reading are worthy of praise,  
Therefore, youth, learn all earnestly.  

5. The art of the pen cannot be compared,  
To riches, wealth, or great power,  
For one sees well human shortcomings,  
And by the pen one is brought to stature,  
Blessed be the lives of those who possess it,  
For in practice, they are honoured and rich.  

6. No one can blame the art of the pen,  
For its powers are wonderfully good,  
It often makes from knaves great lords,  
Who do their duties and their best,  
For those who are clerks must work their minds,  
The pen flows if you but foster it.  

7. In all matters the pen is capable,  
Makes from a virtue-less a handsome man,  
Through it they gain honour, praise, and fame,  
But it is a fool who cannot,  
I must confess, that the noble pen,  
Serves many people as a faithful friend.  

8. If one wishes to speak of all arts,  
And give each praise according to its value,  
Then the pen, for all its wise strokes,  
Must be placed on the Highest council,  
Therefore do wise, what is to be praised,  
Teach your children to be honoured as one.  

9. Princes, emperors, kings and counts,  
Barons, knights, and other noble people,  
And more who are given to the noble pen,  
Popes, cardinals, and church bishops,  
Merchants, factors, engineers,  
Handle the pen, which governs the whole land.  

10. This is in honour of all the letter-learned,  
To the praise of those who use the noble pen,  
Made to spite those who blamed this art,  
Will you writers accept this in thanks,  
Receive this youth, brought forth by art,  
Through my pen, with which I serve you.

label: work
translation:
If you are Boerkens from the countryside,  
You are truly to be pitied,  
Without good money or property,  
Your livestock and your grain,  
My lord forces you to sell,  
To keep him satisfied.  

2. Burghers and noblemen,  
They must live off the farmer,  
No one can comfort him,  
Alas, the farmer faces great distress,  
Instead of enjoying the plumage,  
He ends up with straw.  

3. The tax collectors in the city,  
They come every sitting day,  
It's always this or that,  
They barely sit for the whole day,  
The farmer must pay,  
Or falls into misery.  

4. The millers for the work,  
They have remained poor for so long,  
They reach deep into the sack,  
Toll collectors who are honest and loyal,  
Eagerly take the best pieces,  
Cut from his sacks.  

5. The farmer has no rest  
Until he is buried,  
Everyone pulls him with eagerness,  
The farmer is like a nutcracker,  
They pull and they shake,  
All of them on him.  

6. The landlords want to understand,  
If they want their amusement,  
They go to the tenant,  
The farmer still harnesses his horses,  
And must pay in full,  
Not a penny less.  

7. Whether it is feast or fair,  
City lads and bold rascals,  
Set themselves at the table,  
They come by a whole cartload,  
They feast and they spread,  
Always at the farmer’s expense.  

8. Priests and sextons as well,  
Must be supported by the farmer,  
Quarters from the city,  
Bacon, chickens, pigeons, small game,  
That becomes all too much,  
Granted by the farmer.  

9. The farmer is in distress,  
He must pay it all,  
Even if he has no bread,  
The livestock yields money, milling,  
With many more burdens,  
From excise taxes.  

10. All of it pulls on the farmer,  
Beggars and soldiers,  
If he knocks on their door,  
And does not treat them well,  
They wish the tenant,  
To be struck by the devil.  

11. The guards great and small,  
They can also write,  
Two mugs of beer for one,  
Still they do not fill the pot,  
It is to make the farmer  
A simpleton, fool, and madman.  

12. Clerks and attorneys,  
Know how to pluck the farmer,  
Lawyers, reporters,  
They drain all his purse,  
The farmer finally ends up,  
With the legal case in the hole.  

13. Then the farmer stands and sees,  
With his mouth full of teeth,  
Is it not a great sorrow,  
Alas, still with the hat in hand,  
At the attorneys and lords,  
Is it not a disgrace?  

14. Farewell Boerkens for the last time,  
Who stand here in the circle,  
I advise you for the best,  
If you must pay injustice,  
Show the attorneys your heels,  
And declare bankruptcy.

label: work
translation:
Weavers and make no more sorrow,  
Sing here a little song to your honor,  
They are weavers, whether woman or man,  
I have described your praise,  
How no one can live,  
Unless there is weaving, chit chat,  
Unless there is weaving.  

2. For all fabrics, coarse and fine,  
They all must be woven,  
Nuns, Quakers, Lords, Ladies,  
Also all Priests' clothes,  
They all come from the loom,  
The Weaver has the honor, chit chat,  
The Weaver has the honor.  

3. The Miller cannot grind grain,  
Unless the weaving goes on,  
The sacks in which the grain is put,  
Come out of the Weaver’s hands,  
I must praise the weavers,  
Here and in all lands, chit chat,  
Weaving is no disgrace.  

4. Skippers with their ships at sea,  
They would remain at anchor,  
If there were no weavers,  
The skipper would not sail,  
So lively country folk,  
Bring yarn for the weavers, chit chat,  
Bring yarn for the weavers.  

5. What is also used in the Church,  
Is also all the weavers' work,  
For all the spiritual habit,  
And worldly clothes,  
Are woven and always,  
One must honor the weavers, chit chat,  
One must honor the weavers.  

6. Tents and banners that one sees,  
Where the cornels show respect,  
Banners and standards planted there,  
Clothes of fool and wise,  
Come all from the Weaver’s hand,  
I must praise the weavers, chit chat,  
I must praise the weavers.  

7. Princes and Counts, high in state,  
Dressed in costly attire,  
It all comes from the Weavery,  
The Weaver has woven it,  
Of the craft I am truly glad,  
I must give them the honor, chit chat,  
I must give them the honor.  

8. Painters and sailmakers wise,  
Would remain without goods,  
Where there is none without clothes,  
Therefore I want on all sides,  
To sing this to their honor, chit chat,  
To sing this to their honor.  

9. Many fine thoughts, as one hears,  
Come from the Weavers’ work,  
Weavers are honest people,  
Though they are rarely rich,  
I have nevertheless seen weavers,  
They had fine clothes, chit chat,  
They had fine clothes.  

10. Weavers I praise your craft greatly,  
I have made this to your honor,  
Though some consider weavers bad,  
Both men and women,  
I praise both masters and the servant,  
Long live loyal weavers, chit chat,  
Long live loyal weavers.

label: work
translation:
The praise of the Smiths that I have described here  
I commend their skill and their craft greatly,  
For without them no one could live,  
Therefore I give them much praise and honor,  
In houses and churches // the most beautiful works,  
Are made by their hand, created with great understanding.  

2. Cities, villages, parishes, and castles,  
Are all surrounded and adorned with smithing work,  
Monasteries, chapels, cells, and pavilions,  
It is worthy that one learns such a craft,  
No ships sail // nor cross the seas,  
The smith fully must work their iron.  

3. Kings and emperors can wage war,  
But without smiths, they would very soon be stopped,  
Not even one countryman could be a farmer,  
Ploughing and sowing, it is made by them  
Cape knives, axes // hammers and files,  
Locks and latches // long live the noble smith.  

4. The carpenter would not work,  
Chisels and saws all come from the smith,  
You find no knives nor clerks,  
To properly and neatly cut a pen,  
Masons’ trowels // shears, crowbars,  
Locks and latches // I praise the clever smith.  

5. Gold and silver in villages and towns,  
Many fine jewels in churches and cloisters,  
I myself the money, the smith must forge it,  
From what he makes, he has the least share,  
I must praise him // and show honor,  
There is not one man who can live without him.  

6. The gardener would not be able to sow,  
Had he no hoes, rakes, and spades,  
No farmer nor countryman could mow,  
If one had no sickles to cut the grain,  
The smith makes sickles, also lady’s axes,  
Baskets and shoes // the smith must do it all.  

7. Grates and tongs come out of his hands,  
Shovels and hooks of all kinds,  
In soils and earth he makes iron teeth,  
For every countryman who must plough,  
Bridles for horses // of greater value,  
Ship anchors strong, that is all smith’s work.  

8. Furthermore, there is something remarkable to note,  
He makes the swords for the noble people,  
Muskets, sabers to skirmish,  
Pikes and lances, who can wield it all,  
Many weapons of war, for the soldiers,  
Also bayonets // long live the noble smith.  

9. For surgeons he makes the instruments,  
Lancets, blades, and cutting knives well,  
I, at the presses that print the books,  
Must work with a clever skill,  
Torches, lanterns // I go about many affairs,  
The clear lamps // I hang before the altar.  

10. The smiths will never deceive anyone,  
Their craft itself is justice and righteousness,  
They strike the hot iron so that sparks fly,  
Then beyond the fires they forge eternity,  
Permission then smiths // Endeavor with your members,  
And labor, to attain salvation.

label: work
translation:
If one were to thoroughly examine  
The state of every man,  
Of princes, and of kings,  
I think he could say this:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
Comfortable in a stable,  
Than to be raised high once,  
And await a heavy fall.  

Though the king wears on his head  
A crown of pure gold,  
Though he lives in a beautiful palace  
Very richly built:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
With a simple hat,  
Than be raised in such great worry.  

Though the bishop and prelate  
Wear a golden choir robe,  
Though he may walk with the golden staff,  
And with the miter too:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
Hand always on the plow,  
Always having to care  
For souls, late and early.  

Princes and nobility,  
Though they make fine feasts,  
With Rhine wine and wild roast,  
And also with costly beer:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
And eat buckwheat porridge,  
Than go to war with them,  
To hold the sword at their side.  

Though my lord the pastor owns  
Peat and fire,  
Though he wears long robes,  
The muffles on his hands:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
Though sometimes I lie cold,  
Than preach and study so,  
And live without a wife.  

Though a procurator earns much money,  
And also the lawyer,  
Only with pen and his mouth,  
To write good and evil:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
With my spade in the field,  
Than to win so much money  
From a quarrelsome farmer.  

Though the merchant lives in the city,  
Very mighty and rich,  
Though he walks quietly on his mules,  
Though he does not tread in the mud:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
Out here without grandeur,  
Than to worry strangely,  
And write day and night.  

Though the shopkeeper is supplied  
With abundant goods,  
Though the trade is still so good,  
Though he earns by the hand:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
Busy with my basket,  
Than to watch early and late,  
To serve every man.  

Though the rider has great pleasure  
In drums and trumpet,  
Though he goes with the slicer on,  
A helmet on his head:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
And always sleep well,  
Than stand at seven hours in the night,  
At St. Nicholas' Eve.  

Though the skipper has good fortune,  
If only he can come,  
Loaded from another part  
Of the world, to Amsterdam:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
With my cart on the hay,  
Than be in the midst of the wild sea,  
With a ship or galley.  

With your permission, farmer's wives,  
And all farmers,  
Consider yourselves fortunate in your state,  
And say in general:  
I would rather be a farmer,  
And live comfortably in the land,  
Than a king, prince, or nobleman,  
Full of worries on every side.

label: work
translation:
We Farmers, men and women,  
We work day and night:  
We plow and we spin,  
And we sing with all our might:  
Dear Lord, food and clothing,  
Heaven’s kingdom, and then no more.  

We dig and we hoe,  
All day long,  
We sow, and we mow,  
In great joy with this song:  
Dear Lord, food and clothing,  
Heaven’s kingdom, and then no more.  

We eat every day,  
In the morning buckwheat porridge,  
Thus we fill our stomachs,  
And we sing just as quickly:  
Dear Lord, food and clothing,  
Heaven’s kingdom, and then no more.  

We eat sweet butter,  
Milk is for our thirst,  
Then we become much more devotional,  
And we sing from the heart:  
Dear Lord, food and clothing,  
Heaven’s kingdom, and then no more.  

We seldom lay the table,  
A piece from our hand,  
That tastes to us like a waffle,  
And we sing along the side:  
Dear Lord, food and clothing,  
Heaven’s kingdom, and then no more.

label: work
translation:
When Father Adam spaded,
And Mother Eve spun,
Where did the Lords find work,
Or also the Nobleman?
Hear Princes and Prelates,
You who are called wondrous;
Hear great Potentates,
Noble by birth;
Hear all people,
For Adam is your Father,
And why then the Peasants,
So wondrously despised?
The Peasants and the Lords,
It all comes from Adam’s seed.

2. The first Peasant was Adam,
And also the first Lord,
Why then do people
Despise the Peasants so much?
The Peasant must provide,
So that everyone may live;
And where the Peasant is not busy,
From early morning,
With spading and digging,
We would soon perish.

3. How would great Princes,
And all the nobility,
So joyfully feast,
Always in full splendor,
With chickens and capons,
With delicate food,
With wine and lemons,
And with the best must (new wine):
The Peasant must toil,
To give to my Lord:
He must plant the vineyard,
And also press the wine,
And fatten the capons,
With which they are merry.

4. How would the young Squire,
Carry out his estate,
With laces and spurs,
Shining along the street?
How would he woo a daughter,
Adorned with pearl necklaces,
The Peasant must bring discs (coins),
Or the Squire would quarrel,
It all comes from an estate,
That he has rented,
And thereupon he dominates,
With all this splendor.

5. What would the Merchant do,
And also the shopkeeper,
How would he sell his goods,
So expensively?
How would he entertain,
His goods to every man,
Decorate his house and person,
As often as he can?
The Brewer and the Baker,
Live off the Peasant’s field,
The Baker would become,
Very poor without the Peasant,
He would have to sell,
The oven and his stake.

6. How would the Young Children,
Grow up so fast,
If they were not allowed to eat,
The sweet milk porridge?
How would sick people,
So quickly regain their health?
For what they can desire,
To please their palate,
Various eggs, or a little hen,
A pea or a bean,
A well-roasted chicken,
And also some small bones,
A finch or a lister (a type of bird),
That the Peasant provides them.

7. How would the old greybeard,
In the corner of the stove,
Be able to warm his little feet,
He would perish from cold;
How do old women who now have teeth,
Stay so long in life,
If they were not at hand,
And had book-sized cookies,
Where would they seek those?
It all comes from the Peasants,
The Peasant fetches the wood,
The Peasant cuts from the peat bogs,
The turfs manyfold.

8. With your leave, you Peasant women,
And all Peasants,
Though I have praised you,
It is only in general,
Though you are worthy of praise,
Though you must be respected,
Do not be proud therefore,
But keep yourself without splendor,
Humility wants to adhere,
Always live in virtue;
Be grateful to the Creator,
And praise Him at this moment,
And say also every day,
Forever Deo gratias (Thanks be to God).

label: work
translation:
ACh Friends, listen after my sorrow,
Listen to what I will tell you,
It is already about
my Mussel cart,
Which I must carry with great pain,
and call out when there are Mussels,
Won't you come out; You empty fellow,
Just once to ask.
That Kampen is good, noble and sweet,
All as salty as brine found:
I will proclaim it to you,
All my Mussels are excellent,
which I open for you as a present:
Buy some, my dear man,
It will please you.
Surprised, what noise do I hear,
Trijntje who would buy mussels,
She knew how to pile the baskets so nicely;
mit came our maiden certainly:
Oh Jaske, your Mussels are getting a bit small:
Where shall I, poor man,
Go walking.
Now I have thought of something else again,
Only to earn my living with honor:
I want to start something else:
I want to become an innkeeper,
in the timber rooster, where I tap good beer,
there you will find me. Just as happy,
again.
Just think how it goes with me, in my beard,
When I have to tap a pint,
the first sip I must snap out:
then it is already "excuse me Sir Sir",
I become so fat like a Bear:
That I almost roll: head over heels:
Down the stairs.

label: work
translation:
Be joyful already, farmers and country folk,
For what I have made for your honor,
I will proclaim your glory throughout Flanders.
Therefore, stop all your sorrow and sadness,
I will day and night
Praise you,
Bringing to light
Your office that sustains us all.

First of all, I find written in a book
How Adam was the first farmer,
Now exalted with a thousand more saints,
Yes, Abraham, as I read in God's Scriptures,
Isaac worked in peace,
Did the farming work,
And at this stage
Jacob was a shepherd.

Laban was also a farmer, as I came to read,
And Moses a shepherd in the house of Midian,
Joseph very wise and always full of fear of God,
He worked in the field like a brave man
And wishes to observe
The works of Gideon,
And Job very good,
All farmers wise with courage.

David was also a praised shepherd,
Anointed by Samuel, God's prophet's servant,
And raised to the throne of Israel,
A man also good and upright after God's heart,
Abigail full of love,
Was a farmer’s wife,
From whose lineage
His princes were born.

One sees with joy that every year the farmers,
In sweat, in pain, after hardship and labor,
Bring the ripe fruits into the barn,
And praise God who prepares their reward,
Farmers, farmer’s wives,
Who win those fruits,
Never stand idle,
And let God never perish.

As Adam spade and Eve spun,
Tell me, where would all the noble line be,
We are descended together from their seed,
Therefore, let us rejoice together with God,
The shepherds together,
Came to Jesus,
In Bethlehem,
Awakened by the angel’s voice.

So then, farmers, adorn yourselves highly,
You nymphs weave each a beautiful wreath
That must be woven of green laurels,
Rejoice together women and men,
No folk on land,
Great of understanding,
As much as each can,
Exalt the farmer.

label: work
translation:
Here I have described the worries of the world,
How everyone strives after money and goods
As if one had a hundred years to live,
Which, when one dies, all must be left behind,
Everyone is seen to worry,
Always about tomorrow,
No one who lives here
Has no worries.

One worries and strives for high status,
Another to be beautiful and rich,
The third wants to leave earthly goods,
The fourth to have much splendor,
Others also strive
For great shows,
Much for a wife,
Who is beautiful and faithful.

The tailors worry about sewing,
And the barbers about having a patient,
The farmers to sow their land,
The painters to work artfully,
Brewers to brew,
Girls to marry,
Bakers to bake bread,
In times of need.

Lawyers to deceive the farmers,
Therefore the wise advocate also worries,
Singers sometimes to add something,
To earn money so the soldier worries,
The blacksmiths to forge,
Field commanders for towns,
The innkeeper
To sell wine and beer.

The drunkards always worry about drinking,
And the priests about sobriety,
The guardians to fill the glasses,
The nuns for their salvation,
Printers to print,
Pickers to pick,
A sailor’s child
Worries about a good wind.

The millers worry about grinding,
Carpenters to make good work,
Carriers to deliver merchants’ goods,
Pastors and sacristans worry about the church,
Writers to write,
Men for women,
Hunters after the game,
And are almost never satisfied.

The fishermen worry about catching fish,
And beggars about getting their food,
A thief who steals until he is hanged,
Scamps to grease their chests,
Butchers for animals,
Patcher to patch,
The weaver cool,
After the shuttle and weaving‐rod.

The gardeners to sow and plant,
Ship carpenters who make ships ready,
The shopkeepers worry about their customers,
The privateers to capture at sea,
Sawyers to saw,
Mowers to mow,
Always worrying,
Each according to his profit.

The cloak cutters worry about selling,
Hat makers to make a hat,
Judges to hang thieves,
And all lovers for their sweet beloved,
The cooks to cook,
Ghost‐tellers to tell ghosts,
Coopers for wine,
Which must be well cared for.

The menders worry about mending,
Chair-turners to turn a chair,
Players for cards or sometimes for dice,
The shooters to shoot at the target,
Makers of glasses,
Blowers to blow,
The apothecary,
About sicknesses many and severe.

Doctors to order many prescriptions,
The farmer to fleece the spirit of the operator,
Schoolmasters to teach the children well,
The dyers to dye beautiful colors,
Women’s traits,
Much evil to talk,
Always,
But not toward the kingdom of heaven.

label: work
translation:
Who would call life a burden,  
It is a life full of joy  
Where the little birds amuse themselves,  
The deer of the young youth,  
Through the pleasant singing,  
Of the sweet nightingale  
One hears the sorrowful sigh restrained  
To a lovely sound.  
Although Mars has cut the harvest  
That the farmer has sown,  
It seems he is born for the work,  
When he once gathers his fruits  
His sorrow is forgotten,  
For his work he then receives payment,  
With good drinking and good eating,  
The burden is relieved.  
One sees wonderful joy happen  
When Bellona flees the field  
And when Mars goes away sadly mourning,  
Ceres is put to rest,  
Then one sees the farmer sway  
In the leafy green forest,  
Where the birds warble,  
He and they are no longer anxious.  
If one wants to bake or brew  
See the farmer is prepared  
To cultivate the fertile land  
He fears neither cold nor heat:  
For the reward strengthens him  
That he will once enjoy,  
For his servants and his work,  
The reward sweetens it all.  
See the women with pleasures  
Skillfully know with their hands,  
Sweetly singing of the fruits,  
Of the flax field on the land  
Preparing for everyone,  
For use of small and great,  
One must spread their praise,  
Who help us out of distress.  
Then praise all the farmers  
Who through skill from earth and dust  
Raise the means of life,  
It is praise beyond life,  
That the farmer did not sow,  
The weather treated us so well for a long time,  
Not even a rooster crowed,  
It would have perished through hunger and distress.

label: work
translation:
1.  
The herb Camomile is very rejuvenating,  
Great for dice and more,  
Many ailments are also  
Greatly improved,  
Through stirring, through mixing,  
Through pressing, through beating,  
Even the farmers know this,  
Who deal with farmers.  

2.  
See how they stir the earth,  
And swell through the plow,  
By which they bring good grain from the soil,  
Very good, abundant, and enough,  
But if the earth remains unswelled,  
Neither plowed nor broken,  
Weeds will fill everything,  
Which you will not see as weeds.  

3.  
The plow iron often glints,  
When it is well cared for,  
Otherwise rust will consume it,  
The work is the iron’s shine,  
See the flies strike the grains,  
Through striking the grains finely,  
See the press crush the grapes,  
Through pressing comes the wine.  

4.  
Through pounding spices,  
And the pepper gains strength,  
See the cloves and the blades,  
Like the soul that does not despise,  
And patiently wishes to endure,  
The soul will gain strength,  
Through pressing and striking,  
Heaven's wine and grain are sought after.  

5.  
Sweet comfort, wine, and grain;  
Glory, shine, strength, and virtue,  
The cross bears, and sorrowful tears,  
Sadness brings true joy,  
When the cross comes to plow the soul,  
It bears the fruits of Heaven,  
Those who want to join it to the cross,  
Are most pleasing to God.  

6.  
No one here is free from suffering,  
Neither the king nor the pope,  
Only he who rejoices blissfully,  
Has patience for suffering,  
Patience in suffering is very powerful,  
The herb of patience is very good,  
This is certain and truthful,  
That it brings the greatest good.  

After suffering comes joy,  
Suffering is a great benefit if one endures it patiently.  
From hardship comes virtue,  
Thus you will be exalted in the end.

label: work
translation:
1.  
No state is as noble here on earth  
As that of the farmers,  
Adam, the first lord, was a farmer just as much,  
The first man who sweat to the plow’s work,  
From him come all kings and lords,  
No one is therefore more than a farmer.  

2.  
Noah, too, was lord of the world, and Father Abraham,  
The patriarchs’ lineage,  
Saul, and David, and the kings after them,  
Most of Israel’s rulers have been farmers here,  
Thus the noble farmers should be esteemed,  
Yet the farmers are mostly despised.  

3.  
The pope, and bishop, emperor and king, indeed,  
And all those of status,  
And all practitioners, and also all merchants,  
It must always live by farmers’ labor,  
Thus the noble farmers should be esteemed,  
Yet the farmers are mostly despised.  

4.  
In times of war they are most afflicted,  
Plundered and driven away,  
The farmer pays it all, the farmer must suffer all,  
Good farmers still create courage: it is God who does all this,  
God wounds and heals, the more here is your pain,  
The more your joys shall be hereafter.

label: work
translation:
If an intelligent person were to look through the state of every man,
Of Princes and of Kings, I think he could say,
I would rather be a Farmer peacefully in my stable,
Than highly exalted once and await a harsh fall.
2. Although the Pope of Rome is great and wonderfully respected,
Though everyone kisses his foot, though he has all power:
I would rather be a Farmer here unknown,
Than accountable for every soul to give an answer.
3. Although the King wears a Crown of pure Gold on his head,
Though he lives in a beautiful Palace, very richly built:
I would rather be a Farmer with a poor hat,
Than to take all the goods from the poor Ploughman.
4. Although the Bishop and Prelate wear a golden choir cape,
Though he may walk with the golden Staff and the Mitre,
I would rather be a Farmer with my hand on the plough,
Than always having to care for souls late and early.
5. Princes and Nobility, they make good cheer,
With Rhine Wine, and wild roast, and also with Diester’s Beer,
I would rather be a Farmer, eating Barley Porridge,
Than to go to war with them, the sword at their side.
6. Though my Lord Pastor is supplied with turf and fire,
Though he wears long robes, the muff on his hand,
I would rather be a Farmer though I sometimes suffer cold,
Than preach and study so, and live without a wife.
7. Though the Procurator earns much money, and also the Lawyer,
Only with pen and mouth, to drive matters evil,
I would rather be a Farmer, with my scythe in the Field,
Than to rip off poor Widows of all their money.
8. Although the Merchant lives in the city very powerful and rich,
Though he walks softly on his Mules, though he doesn’t step into the mud,
I would rather be a Farmer here outside without splendor,
Than to worry and write marvelously day and night.
9. Although the Winckelier is supplied with abundant wares,
Though the guild is so good, though he earns easily,
I would rather be a Farmer and busy with my basket,
Than lying and cheating with the poor craftsman.
10. Although the Horseman has great pleasure in Drum and Trumpet,
Though he rides with the Serjeant, a Helmet on his head,
I would rather be a Farmer and always sleep well,
Than stand seven hours at night at the Sinterklaas celebration.
11. Although the Skipper has good fortune if he can only once
Come loaded from East India to Amsterdam,
I would rather be a Farmer with my cart on the island,
Than in the middle of the sea with a Ship or Galley.
12. With your leave, you little peasant women and peasants all,
Consider your luck in your state, and speak in general,
I would rather be a Farmer here outside within Meir,
Than King, Prince, or Nobleman to die of despair.

label: work
translation:
Come along, yes men, see the noise!
Come along, I have a mild purchase today,
Yes, old and young, come, walk, walk, walk,
I give you my herbs at a crazy price.
This little box of balsam, these herbs and ointment,
I now give together for a sixpence,
For a blue penny, and that pill with one,
And then also that balm, and white stone,
With that you have this little oil,
Useful for phlegm and gravel;
The powder for kidney stones (by my throat!
All small stones) as fine as flour,
And it makes the intestines like velvet,
Tested on Theunis the Farmer and Neel!
How many old and young have I,
The virtues of my herbs and ointment (perhaps
For a year or ten, for a year or ten,
For a year or ten,
Or more) have shown the power of it!
I am famous everywhere I go,
No better doctor here on earth,
Come, then show if life is worth it to you,
That you are lovers of your health,
And buy my herbs and balsam.
Just carry the box in your pocket,
In a week or two, without pain or woe,
Therefore now everyone take a box with you,
I have many donkeys promoted to doctor here,
To Leiden and Leuven, long associated,
With men of learning and knowledge,
These letters, and seals, honor me,
Presented by emperors and kings
My deeds and glory, in writing and hand,
I travel the world from land to land,
Almost everywhere. Is there anyone,
Who wants to test my brave skill,
And agreeable manner, in removing
Tartar or molars?
Look there, (I swear it to you by my buttock)
I lie gently with this pin,
Or if you are troubled with dizziness,
I will drive it away for little money.
And it gives the woman new strength again,
That balm can do all,
Almost drive away the filthy spots,
(I mean from the clothes) That pill made with beer,
Broken, and warmed on the hearth,
Drives away the torment of worms, knees,
There are thousands in a quarter of an hour,
That balsam melted, and man or wife,
Rubbed on nipple, on knee or disc,
For about five hours, very stiff from the fire,
Is a great remedy,
And of a strong effect in the body,
Consumption, catarrh, humor, bad breath,
And spleen or liver, it takes flight,
Red run, jaundice, and dropsy,
Even where you're as thick as a barrel, and swollen,
With water, you pee buckets full!
For blows and stabs, swelling and wounds,
For bites from fleas and mad dogs,
For pimples on the face, purple and spotted,
For sores and ulcers, it works at once,
For bad sores and cancer, healthy and pure,
It drives away scrofula and phlegm,
It is a medicine, it is a medicine,
It is a medicine
For all kinds of pain and soreness!
Colic and pleurisy must go quickly,
Therefore lovers, all those who are unwell,
Buy now before you are deceived,
Quick, are you tired of your torment and suffering?
But everyone gets a glove or handkerchief too.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Well, master of the Stone Grinding  
Why does the Bryn-Kas treat you so strangely?  
Or is it because it’s starting to pinch  
The Key keeps talking to me all the time?  

2.  
I would almost curse the Grinding  
And I fear it will get even worse,  
Because the work is already hard to find  
And the best wages come after the Moon.  

3.  
It is the same, dear friend, with the Cutting!  
For thirty stuivers now not ten;  
Who can endure that hail of wages  
When one sees the businessmen during the day?  

4.  
I swear to you these are fatal matters  
That one must strike, that’s the fun;  
What a figure I make  
But the money is gone, now silence to bed.  

5.  
One must just continue to play the lord  
It’s allowed to go as it wishes then  
If one can no longer pay their debts?  
Then we take the wages also after the Moon.  

6.  
And I would rather go to the East Indies  
Then it stays best still in decency?  
Because I know for sure, friend, there you will find  
Much more freedom than to do here.  

7.  
East Indies, what nonsense you talk!  
No: never think I will be so crazy,  
I would rather leave the work to others there  
I desire no gruel or bacon for so long.  

8.  
I hope we will get work again,  
Let us wait for it patiently  
We will ... but I better keep silent  
Dear friend, we are content with it.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Oh, what did I have to sew  
On those dear Little Sprigs,  
To twist them together.  
I say honestly, it is something strange,  
They were plucked,  
And also torn,  
Into a hundred pieces;  
They were plucked  
And also torn.  
I broke my head well  
Before I guided the Caps,  
To stitch them back together like that,  
And spread them after the Flower and stem;  
Then with a Thimble,  
And Needle in haste:  
Went to the Finches  
Then with a Thimble  
And Needle in haste.

3.  
What do Misses think of the Sewing  
Could you spot a stitch?  
Would anyone do it better,  
Could Mooyer ever do it?  
So nothing is spoiled  
Even if it was torn  
It’s in order again,  
So nothing is spoiled  
Even if it was torn.

4.  
If you might be missing a Seamstress  
To sew this or that,  
Please just contact me,  
Because I sew and darn very well,  
I am not even shy  
Or ever afraid;  
Of big tears  
I am not even afraid,  
Or shy either.

5.  
I don’t need to praise myself  
You can see that I know my craft,  
Miss, if you want more proof,  
Come take another sample then,  
Because I am quite certain,  
That I sew very neatly,  
And also darn nicely  
Because I am quite certain,  
That I sew very neatly.

6.  
Therefore, if it should happen again  
That by blowing my Nose,  
Something rips,  
I will then also generously  
Very neatly on a thread,  
After flower and petal  
Skillfully darn,  
Very neatly on a thread,  
After flower and petal.

label: work
translation:
1.  
You can see from my feathers well  
What kind of bird I am;  
I am no noble grave son,  
Nor also a prince’s child,  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.  

2.  
I want to help you as requested,  
For what you had me do,  
I want to sew for you again and again,  
Slippers and the shoe,  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows,  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.  

3.  
If a young lady’s dress is soon torn  
She may just come to me  
There is nothing lost at all  
I want to sew for you again,  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows,  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.  

4.  
My fishing line is neat and clean,  
And the embroidery is fine,  
But my Elsje outshines them all,  
Oh, she is much neater I think!  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows,  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.  

5.  
If a young lady closes the door in doubt,  
So that I cannot sew,  
Try once before you go,  
And then judge it,  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows,  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.  

6.  
I poke, I sew, I push very gently  
If you had once seen it,  
You let me sew here day and night,  
Come, let me earn something,  
But but my father is a man,  
Who can no longer help young fellows,  
Therefore, drum, drum, drum, I am his dear son.

label: work
translation:
Who would not be cheerfully joyful with me,  
With tasty, fine, and fresh French wine:  
And glasses filled to the brim,  
I feel my spirit bloom in cheerfulness,  
No worry disturbs: No worry disturbs,  
My glad and cheerful heart,  
That all misfortune, that all misfortune,  
And sorrows erase.  
Long live the Press, and Book selling,  
In Holland’s crown the pearl on the IJ:  
So that we here again may gather in joy,  
Long live the art bound to the Savings,  
That gives us the fine: That gives us the fine:  
And tasty little wine:  
Long live that art: Long live that art,  
But read through Lady Pallas.  
So drink then to one another,  
With glass upon glass into the new year,  
Spite to those who envy us this cheerfulness,  
Thus we live in these sorrowful times,  
In hope that, in hope that,  
Hereafter through the Press,  
And heaven’s favor: And heaven’s favor,  
We obtain more happiness.

label: work
translation:
HY stretches out his withdrawn hand,  
Whoever desires it eagerly.  
After the fate of his fortunes,  
We shall soon lift the heavy burden  
From the heart, if you wish to impress  
Our happiness upon the breast.  
What are you still standing there for, curious people?  
We will, like an interpreter,  
Tell you all your hidden matters,  
Which the high fate  
Of Heaven hereafter  
Will cause you to marvel at.  
And do not think that we are overly tame  
Deceivers, although we bear the name  
Of Egyptians:  
We also praise the most beautiful beauty,  
In which we calmly keep  
Our greatest reward.  
Yet swiftly flee from our Fatherland,  
And planted here on the shore,  
We rob the peoples:  
But otherwise not at all,  
Than what the Mingod commands us  
From his heavenly clouds.

label: work
translation:
That everyone has kept their little fire,  
And puts out their candle,  
And closes their door with a bolt,  
So that no thief or bride can enter,  
They then peacefully go to bed,  
Sleeping makes people fat,  
We carry rattle,  
Sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.  
We are ready at your service,  
In our Amstel city,  
And always inclined towards you,  
If you had any trouble,  
We are immediately there,  
And support  
all mutiny,  
With our rattle,  
Sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.  
Although it thunders, rains, or freezes,  
We show our duty,  
While no one loses any honor,  
Even if the houses are locked,  
Even if the moon does not shine by night,  
Monsieur the rattle-watchman still walks  
And carries his rattle,  
Sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.  
War boasts a warrior,  
The sea a good sailor,  
A drunkard on a full jug,  
We always boast,  
Of our service and loyal path,  
Although we often have to go,  
Provided with rattle,  
Sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.  
That we together once a year,  
For our loyal duty,  
Offer to the Amstel citizen,  
A New Year's poem,  
You do not take offense at that, for  
We always watch over people and country,  
With our rattle, sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.  
Heaven spare the citizenry,  
And gentlemen altogether,  
In Gijsbrecht’s city there, the ship-rich Y,  
Achieves much praise and fame,  
So that we gladly remain many times,  
Armed for everyone’s service,  
With our rattle, sword, and stick,  
The clock strikes ten hours.

label: work
translation:
I have here a box with powders, plasters,  
With balm, pills, and tincture,  
For wives, women, widows, maidens,  
Whether cool or warm by nature,  
Sweethearts, here stands  
Doctor good advice,  
Who wants to heal you sooner or later.  

I have here a little roll with which I soothe wounds,  
No matter how deep they are,  
Ripens and heals; I have bandaged many  
From whom I took the pain in one night,  
Ladies, here stands  
Doctor good advice,  
Who wants to heal you sooner or later.  

Here is a box with golden pills,  
With which you, in an hour or five or six,  
Can calm the most terrible heat  
And drive it out of your purse,  
Sisters, here stands  
Doctor good advice,  
Who wants to heal you sooner or later.

label: work
translation:
1.
A man has come,
With little waves for sale;
Who wouldn’t dream of that?
He had a whole bunch:
Many people came together,
He called out one time three, four
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

2.
He approached a beautiful girl,
He said, my dearest child:
Your little pale flesh,
That was loved by me,
Tell me what your name is:
Aren’t you a chambermaid?
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

3.
What is that man talking about?
Did she speak or is he crazy?
And here on the streets,
I say you should leave!
You should be ashamed,
You unpleasant creature!
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

4.
How can you despise me like that?
You are a strange girl;
I had different thoughts!
But I see I was deceived,
And I am quite capable,
As if it were one time three, four
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

5.
Are you such a rascal?
Said the girl sweetly:
I guess I’ll take a chance with you,
If you do it quietly,
Come, let’s go together then,
I don’t live far from here;
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

6.
That’s how all the girls are,
And it’s all just talk,
They so love the nonsense
And soon fall on their back,
You don’t have to be ashamed?
And don’t make a fuss either,
Voelam Plaisier de Dame,
Voelam Madam Plaisier.

label: work
translation:
Grinding the bases of the stone,
Why do you hold yourself so stiffly?
Or is it because the work is starting to pinch?
That guy speaks to me so strangely.
I almost wanted to curse the sharpening,
I fear it will get even worse:
The work is very hard to find,
And the best pay is hardly enough.
So it goes, friend, also with the cutting,
For thirty stuivers now only ten;
Who can bear that hardship?
People are constantly seen suffering from it.
I sweat these are fatal matters,
That one must stop such enjoyment:
What a spectacle we usually make,
But the money is gone, only after bed.
One must continue to play the lord,
And then it wants to go like that;
If one can no longer heal our debts,
Then again with the pay hardly enough.
And I [...] rather to East Indies,
There one best remains in decency:
I know for sure, friend, you will find there,
Much more business than to do here.
East Indies has some bad reputation,
Don't think I will be that crazy:
I want to leave the work to others there,
As long as I don't fancy groats with bacon.
Well, I hope we will get work,
Let us wait patiently,
We will just to the D...., let me be silent,
Oh mate, we will be together.

label: work
translation:
I would curse the cheap carpentry;  
And the walking also after corner and bridge,  
To fetch a few buckets of fodder,  
That really pains my back.  
If only we still got work,  
Then it would all be nothing,  
But we are shy about the matter inside,  
Our heads are as if crazy.  
The foremen are now sullen of mind,  
Damn, they may speak to them,  
There is no money to be earned anymore,  
I do not want to go back to the Shipyard.  
We can no longer get golden wages,  
While the work is at a standstill:  
We also cannot pay tin,  
I just have pennies here for tobacco.  
What do you think now of those Fools,  
They wear an undersuit of velvet,  
And with their bald shaved necks,  
I think it is somewhat criminal.  
When they are on the Shipyard again,  
They wear pants full of tar,  
But when you see people sometimes in the Church  
They really look like an English Lord.  
They do not want to look for other work;  
They rather suffer to their last penny,  
And when the nut comes to visit them,  
Then they must go out into the sea hole.  
I hope we will get work,  
Let us wait patiently,  
And let us be silent for now,  
Yes, but we are fed up with it.  
The women also do not act as if quarreling,  
From morning until evening;  
But make sure they come to write here,  
Otherwise, I must go to Ian-uncle.

label: work
translation:
1. I have come here,  
To live in this neighborhood,  
A pharmacist from Rome,  
Studied in Harderwijk;  
I come to summon you,  
For all the people,  
And I show here my skill,  
Requesting your favor.

2. If you also want to purge,  
Feel free to come inside,  
For the ladies and the gentlemen,  
I have a good start:  
Rhubarb and fine herbs,  
Sweet almonds on display,  
Salap and clean carrots,  
For the common person.

3. Syrup to swear by,  
Elderflower and chamomile bloom,  
If you have no taste in eating,  
I pray you come to me,  
Or if you have a quick fever,  
Use my quinine well,  
You will be healed,  
Without pain or ache.

4. If you have cough or consumption,  
Or if you have a bad stomach,  
Here you see my trade,  
Step in and do not delay:  
Also, I have head plaster,  
For ladies and maidens,  
Also ointment for burns;  
Balm for the winter hand.

5. With this I want to conclude,  
You have seen my shop,  
But now I wait for coins,  
That I have earned;  
I wish you all,  
Much blessing in the year,  
That you may be blessed,  
That is my wish.

label: work
translation:
1. And then is it not to be wondered at!  
How one sees the girls here daily,  
Catching on to each other,  
Almost as soon as they come together.  

2. But principally early Saturday morning,  
When one is going to scrub to one's satisfaction:  
And when the master and wife still rest,  
Then the girls are eager to chat.  

3. Then the girls come before the door,  
With scrubbers and brooms without sorrow:  
The beginning is a morning greeting,  
That Leisje gives to Aaltje her neighbor girl.  

4. And Grietje calls again to the other side,  
Neighbor girl, you’re already early there,  
Yes child, I must start early,  
If I want to have my work done.  

5. I give it praise, said the others there,  
Neighbor girl, it is too much,  
Ours is only half as big,  
Nevertheless, I work myself half to death.  

6. After that, they set the brooms together,  
They lean on them as if they were a peddler,  
Then it begins to strike,  
Over all that may almost happen in the house.  

7. And when they have talked this out,  
Then Tryntje says to Kaet:  
Well girl, what a neat corner you have,  
Set firmly in front of you.  

8. That is only about the suitor, says the other,  
Who was still with her last night;  
When you were going to the baker,  
Neighbor girl, I saw he spoke to you.  

9. That was my cousin, how crazy are you,  
No girl, his kisses fell well too good;  
Well, neighbor girl, how boldly you talk,  
That then makes one hold the belly fast.  

10. Then they sweep the street up to the gutter:  
That is like the first blow;  
They also scoop the water and let some remain,  
Then the chatting starts again.  

11. Is it a neat scout or a snitch, my child?  
Who watches you like a friend?  
If something comes up, I advise you,  
It is difficult to serve, it is better to be your own woman.  

12. If I must speak from my heart;  
Serving is great misery:  
It's better to have dry bread and be a lady,  
Than always commanding, even Tryn.  

13. Then the woman who has gotten up comes;  
Listen girl, haven’t you done it yet?  
Then they come up with this or that excuse,  
Then one must scrub and continue in the house.  

14. So it goes among the girls;  
Especially on Saturdays;  
The pious do not concern themselves with this song;  
It is just about a proper girl.

label: work
translation:
There beats the drum!  
Rob de dom, rob de dom!  
There beats the drum, viva!  
These are the Wervers for the Fleet,  
Rob de dom, viva!  
No lively fellow fears death,  
Rob de dom, viva!  
He laughs at the enemy’s powder and shot:  
I go! I go!

The Fatherland,  
Demands my hand, demands my hand,  
Rob de dom, viva!  
I am so strong, so fit;  
Rob de dom, viva!  
Like ever a fresh sea dog was:  
Rob de dom, viva!  
Come Jan, and Kees, come Hein, and Jas,  
I go! I go!

No much talk,  
Be alert mates! alert mates!  
There beats the drum, viva!  
Well, do you have a man’s heart in your body,  
Rob de dom, viva!  
Then fight for the Land, for child, for wife.  
Rob de dom, viva!  
But whoever hesitates or whoever stays,  
I go, I go!

label: work
translation:
I must get up; it is getting light.  
I have new strength  
From You alone, O dear Lord!  
I give to You, in my thoughts,  
The praise of it, for I can no longer.

I find myself immediately in the midst  
Of children, in great turmoil.  
I commend us to You: this is my prayer;  
You are most cherished in my heart.
