I have no prayer room, I have no books,  
I have no time to search  
All my duties alone,  
But always do my work with joy.  
Although I cannot close a room  
As others do for their prayer;  
I exclude everything that is not good  
From a heart that kneels before Your law.

I will joyfully earn my bread,  
For myself and for my household;  
I will begin the day with You:  
I expect my gain from Your blessing.

Last evening I read again:  
(Happy if I pay attention here!)  
“To be content is to be grateful;  
Trust is the right prayer.”

I am well in spirit, I feel no worry,  
I thank You for my favorable lot;  
And think, in all my earthly slumber,  
Of Death, of Eternity, of God.

label: work
translation:
Yes, mates, call me a true Blood,
Laugh at me brightly once;
Call me, to the point of madness, good:
I stick to my decision.

I do not want to beat the Horses like that,
They also feel pain;
I care deeply for the Beasts,
And always will.

If the Lord drives them from the mouth,
May I do it for that reason?
That madness never became natural to me,
I am of no decency!
Hard galloping is real fun
For Plugs, for no Lord;
(Or even though one does not pay attention there.)
And the Horse receives the honor.

I am now but a teamster’s servant,
But it troubles me, it pains me,
When the Horse is overloaded,
And toils for cart or sleigh.

The dumb Beast comes before a lock,
Wants to, but cannot proceed:
People curse, rant, make noise,
Purely as if it belongs to it.

They whip, they strike its body,
My heart shrinks from it!
And what is the end of that business?
The Beast dies for their punishment.
The scripture says: ‘A merciful man
Has compassion for his Cattle.’
I am glad that I can read something;
Although I do it rarely.

I know that God, who commanded us
The dominion of the Animals,
Surely will judge our tyranny
Regarding their punishment.

I often think: Bles is just like me;
Give me a friendly word,
Then in a moment
I do almost everything as it should be,

But if the Master is unreasonable,
Scolds me, teases, and torments,
Then everything goes wrong with me too,
Then I also become hardened.
Man, always strange in what he does,
Demands from a reasonless Beast,
More reasonableness, more virtue, more goodness
Than he prefers for himself.

I behave, though everyone does,
No injustice to my Horse.
A Plug, a godless man is cruel,
Whether he is poor or rich.

In the slavish profession raised early,
I come neither to monastery nor church;
Yet if I do my duty,
Do I not also do religious work?

label: work
translation:
1.  
Friends listen attentively;  
What I am going to sing to you,  
About the sawmillers here in town,  
How they manage their mill;  
But everything depends on the wind,  
In which they take great pleasure,  
Sawmillers here in town,  
Keep your little mill on its track.  

2.  
Early in the morning we get started,  
As soon as the wind begins to blow,  
Then we set our sails,  
Let our little mill spin skillfully;  
Then we place our beam in position,  
We drink a nice cup of tea:  
Sawmillers make sure your saw is taut,  
And do your work thoughtfully.  

3.  
If ever a saw fails,  
Then a support stick is taken in hand,  
Which is placed between the saw,  
So it does not shame the saw,  
Or they pull the saw a bit out:  
They sing a tune to conclude,  
Friends listen and hear it,  
How things must go with the sawmillers.  

4.  
And if there is a shooting stick,  
They want to insert it quickly,  
So you are not hindered:  
And so the wood can break quickly;  
That’s how we do it with the hand saw,  
So sawmillers, don’t be lazy:  
Sawmiller, fix your beam firmly,  
Before sawing a warp or tuft.  

5.  
And if there is a small stroke,  
Do not let your planks slip free,  
And push your sled back, sled back:  
Sawmillers, don’t let it upset you,  
And then they sharpen the saw a bit,  
So it will cut better through the wood;  
Carrier takes your planks away,  
So you can stack them in the meantime.  

6.  
If then the wind blows sharp,  
And the sail or high line yield,  
As you see fit:  
Or however the wind may dictate,  
They lay the sails along the beam,  
Sawmiller, don’t be worried about yielding,  
Sawmiller, pull up your vang,  
So the mill goes up and running again.  

7.  
Carrier must remove those planks,  
If sawdust is out of the windows,  
Unload gently without a bump,  
It may be done politely,  
And place a little bench on the side,  
And then brush it off properly like sand,  
And shorten it as the block is rather sturdy,  
It’s one and a half inches in the sawmiller's pants.  

8.  
Friends, listen attentively,  
How the sawmillers grumble,  
When they are in the mills,  
They hear their saws hitting the nails,  
And then they pull out the pole,  
And grumble like half mad,  
They chop, they chop the nails out,  
And say the work is spoiled nonetheless.  

9.  
If we then have a quiet day,  
We must lay out wood or sawdust:  
For we always have the cargo,  
The millers know how to manage their workers:  
Now with one thing and then with another,  
Oh, those millers are so strange;  
You millers keep your saws busy,  
So that you may rest once against the way.

label: work
translation:
Easily the Livelihood is won,  
Here in our Amstel city,  
Everything is invented here,  
One does this, another that,  
For although times are bad;  
No one must suffer poverty here;  
Although the city is very large,  
Everyone still gets their bread.  

2. Those who know no trade,  
Play tricks behind the scenes,  
Thus both large and small are deceived,  
It is the fashion of the land,  
Then there are the Masters Brokers,  
Often dealing in Crunier wares,  
Brokers of rye bread,  
Some thirty in one loft.  

3. One can also be an appeals agent,  
If one cannot handle affairs,  
If one can read and write,  
Then one is a respectable man,  
Yes, then one is also deceived,  
By those who do not know it,  
For a lord of high status,  
Although the purse strikes no coin.  

4. One can also play broker,  
If one happens to be in need,  
It is better than stealing,  
Thus coming to one’s bread,  
Or as keepers of animals,  
That roam at night in the playhouse:  
Then one earns the livelihood with diligence;  
And spends it in joy.  

5. Prostitutes and madams,  
Are here in the highest degree,  
Although they earn money by shoveling:  
If business goes well,  
All the prostitutes are their slaves,  
Who dig up the money for them,  
So that from the prostitute's sweat,  
They can strut about dressed finely.  

6. If a prostitute earns three guilders,  
It is a crown for the madam,  
And so she keeps her bottom full of debts,  
And the madam has great gain,  
But if he must advance money to her:  
Then he comes steadily to suffer;  
For three guilders a ducat:  
And so he maintains his state.  

7. What many harlots one sees running:  
On the cross street late in the evening,  
To steal a sixpence or two;  
From one or another mate,  
Whom they have served once,  
One cannot describe everything,  
Here and there with a shrug,  
On a doorstep or under a bridge.  

8. Chambered nymphs one finds many.  
Money sweetens labor,  
Therefore it doesn’t matter to them,  
Who lies down beside them,  
Even if he is half a fool,  
They can always settle it,  
Money only found in blood;  
Their faults are not counted.  

9. Soul sellers are there too,  
Who go to buy the person,  
Suffer and then so expend,  
That they tire after the job,  
Before they truly know;  
Where they have settled down,  
Or in what land they are,  
Remember this lesson of mine.  

10. Rather sell all your property,  
Or be stripped of it,  
Work the nails of your hands,  
And thus honestly earn your bread,  
Then on all your paths,  
You will find rich blessing,  
For it benefits your money and goods,  
If it does not harm your soul.

label: work
translation:
Now I have something new in my hands
Invented by the house carpenters,
Who, with the girls abundantly,
Live according to the carpenter’s way,
No craftsman can live like him,
Who wouldn’t like to go carpentry with him,
How sweet is carpentry,      repeat.
We live without fear.

When we come to the shop,
They ask where the boy is,
Who then fetches something for our mouths,
Taking a bite is healthy,
Thus we live like gentlemen,
Who on earth wouldn’t want to learn carpentry,
How sweet is carpentry,      repeat.
We live without fear.

And when we finish work,
We look at the prettiest girl,
With whom we keep up a conversation,
That’s the essence of a carpenter,
Then we get another lesson,
It’s from my master’s little bottle,
Then we drink,      repeat.
To the health of the girl.

When we come into the kitchen,
We live again without shame,
A sandwich immediately,
And a kiss for her mouth,
Then she fetches something from the cellar,
A little bottle that is somewhat clear,
Then we drink,      repeat.
To the health of both of us.

When we come home from work,
We live again without shame,
If we see someone,
We ask if the coffee is ready,
They ask if you want to drink along,
Or they give us hints,
From their master,      repeat.
Or from their ladies.

The girls say it all alike,
There’s no better person on earth,
Than a carpenter of worth,
Because they are always ready,
Therefore, don’t hesitate,
Always give a signal to a carpenter,
That is a proper man who can kiss you so sweetly,
So sweetly.

label: work
translation:
Miss, I come here to sell,  
All those kinds of Savonets,  
I have to walk with a crutch,  
I don't even pay attention to that:  
I have many kinds of waters,  
To wash the chatterboxes,  
I have beautiful Savonette,  
I have beautiful Savonet.  

2.  
Voulez-vous de l’eau d’Orange,  
Say to me, I have it good,  
Or voulez-vous de l’eau d’Almange:  
That is as clear as the sun,  
It is made in Paris:  
It makes all the girls wise,  
But even more my Savonette,  
But even more my Savonet.  

3.  
I have beautiful Queen’s water,  
Also Pumade for the Madam,  
To get a young son,  
I still have some in my stall,  
And that is so delicate,  
It has in the throat no more pain,  
And that is the Savonette,  
And that is the Savonet.  

4.  
Which mustaches do you want to buy from me  
To put on your chin:  
Take this little box, open it,  
There are all the mustaches in it:  
Mais vous prenez rein de tout,  
Dittes moi que voulez-vous,  
Mais je croi mon Savonette,  
’t Is van die Savonet.  

5.  
Here you all have it,  
What I have in my stall,  
I only have two of these balls,  
There you have them now madam,  
But I don’t use too many,  
It breaks sour at my throat,  
Because it has the Savonette,  
Because it has the Savonet.

label: work
translation:
It is warm here on summer days,  
The pans seem glowing hot,  
But should I now complain about my fate,  
While I sweat at my work,  
It seems to me that would be ungrateful,  
I feel some prickling on my skin,  
I think it’s the lazy sweat, that must come out.  
I have no fear of lacking bread,  
I do not fear that, I do not fear that,  
And that is the greatest household sorrow.

The farmer must sweat by his mowing,  
I was once with my Teuntje’s mother,  
That time I will never forget,  
A week at home with Kees the farmer,  
It was haymaking – look! – that I called work  
From early morning until late at night.  
I was not capable of that work,  
And now I belong to the strong.  
I will not exchange, no! I will not exchange, no!  
I am content with my shuttle.  
The sun will also feel the pitchfork,  
I have heard from Heinnef,  
People are frightened at sea by wet feet,  
Even if it is very hot on board;  
And loaded with a heavy gun,  
Like a soldier marching,  
Or standing for hours one after another  
And almost roasting through the sun:  
I will not exchange, no! I will not exchange, no!  
I am content with my simple [life].

It seems to me the clock struck twelve hours,  
The stomach is already making it difficult for me,  
Well wife! how can it take so long,  
She thinks that hunger is healthy.  
Indeed! I hear the pan sizzling.  
I think, what must many a man,  
Who can still boast of his welfare,  
Not miss my domestic happiness?  
I fly downstairs, I fly downstairs,  
I am content with my little wife.

label: work
translation:
There comes, there comes the last load,  
How grateful is my soul,  
Who had expected such a precious weather,  
During the best of the haymaking  
Almost not a single drop of rain fell.  
I thank Heaven for that treasure, that treasure, that treasure, that treasure.  
The flag that adorns this cart,  
I see with joy.  
Now the mowing folk must be paid,  
The beer has been brought in  
In the middle of the month of March.  
The mower’s wages are well deserved.

There men! There, there is the money,  
And the barrel stands ready.  
Come, everyone, now drink like a hero,  
Who works like this, certainly enjoys something,  
Soon my wife will also provide the feast.  
And then after eating, to the dance.

label: work
translation:
Yes, the day is dawning again,  
I hear the rooster crowing,  
It’s my time to get up,  
And what would keep me in bed,  
I am as healthy as a fish.  
Come, I’ll get my buckets,  
Because I’m already waiting for Teunisvaêr,  
And otherwise it would stink. -  
See my milk buckets shine,  
I am very proud of them, and that doesn’t  
Do any milkmaid harm.  

I already see the cows from afar,  
Now that I call unusual,  
As soon as they see my buckets,  
They begin to low.  
And I like to hear that so much.  
I saw a young lady last evening,  
That person was first afraid of cattle.  
I don’t know how that can be,  
Who would fear these little animals,  
They are the gentlest livestock in the country.  
See how they lick my hand.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Early in the morning with the day,  
That is how one must work at night,  
And never does anyone see in the forest,  
So here we already stand in thought,  
We Kappen diligently continue daily,  
To earn a piece of bread;  
But when we have done the work,  
Then we must go back home again,  
Come, let us first drink something together,  
And then we will be cheerful,  
Pour some in and drink,  
Before the rascal starts,  
We will go to work,  
Before the boss arrives.  

2.  
What noise do I hear,  
It seems the village is in turmoil,  
That you do not work and drink yourself full,  
That is already going wild here,  
Come, then work diligently.  
Because we have almost finished,  
Here you have your money from me,  
Your money that lies here counted out,  
Come, let us first drink something together  
And then we will be cheerful,  
Pour some in and drink,  
Before the rascal starts,  
Our work is done,  
We must go home.  

3.  
What a beautiful voice, what a lovely sound,  
Touches my heart and sounds so sweet in my ears,  
Whoever you may be, do not vanish,  
But let your voice be heard,  
Although I see your fair face,  
That you compel me through your singing  
To love you sincerely,  
For I mean to love no other.

label: work
translation:
1.
A painter of the first rank,
Who made everything from life,
Paints houses, trees, or land,
He knows how to give everything its color,
Paints flowers or herbs,
Everywhere his art shines forth,
Painter, painter, where is your palette,
Where those colors were placed.

2.
If he makes a portrait of a girl,
With a pair of brown glittering eyes,
Many are seduced by her;
And then such a painter is moved
He paints her coral mouth,
And a little round apple chin,
Painter, painter, where is your palette?
Where the colors were placed.

3.
If he goes a bit lower,
Paints her swollen bosom,
Oh then he is by no means slow!
When he quickly makes those round balls,
For which so many admirers long,
Then he paints with pleasure,
Painter, painter, where is your palette
Where you put your colors.

4.
Then if you come again to Venus’ grove,
He must paint and copy,
Oh how his senses loosen,
When he must lift her belly,
With a little piece of love,
Then he paints to his liking,
Painter, painter, where is your palette,
Where you put your colors.

5.
And then he makes a farmer,
Who sits talking with his beloved,
Yes, many a fresh girl,
Who must yield her rosy kisses,
That is a life without sorrow,
Such a life others do not have,
Painter, painter, where is your palette?
Where the colors were placed.

6.
Having done his work,
Then cheerfully goes dancing,
Yes, then it can start again;
And then he sings faldera songs,
A painter jumps through it,
Never has a painter cried,
Painter, painter, where is your palette?
Where those colors were placed.

7.
If he has no money left in his pocket,
He just makes some pretty pieces,
Then he could at his ease
Pick some money from a lover,
That is a life without sorrow,
A painter has no heartbreak,
Painter, painter, where is your palette?
Where those colors were placed.

8.
Where else can one find greater art?
Esteemed by princes and lords;
They give us their favor,
And spend their money on us,
But if sometimes unexpected,
A painter might be honored;
Painter, painter, where is your palette?
Where those colors were placed.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Love by the fire is naked,  
Happy is he who blazes with love,  
Who with such great power  
Came to Pluto, the God of youth,  
Sees that voice is more than virtue,  
Can I steadily gather joy!  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Strike the iron while it glows.  

2.  
Girls who go to work out there;  
Always take good lads,  
I washed myself by the streams!  
Young men who are lurking there,  
See that they assign the girl in due time,  
Then you will soon be a winner,  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Strike the iron while it glows.  

3.  
I am just a poor blacksmith, see,  
Who must earn his living by smithing,  
I lost my parents’ house out there,  
I did not let myself be forced in Amsterdam;  
But now I just keep walking on,  
And steadily without worries,  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Forge the iron while it glows.  

4.  
I am just a poor coachman, see,  
Who must earn his living by driving a coach?  
Yet when I drive my horses around,  
I know how to control my horses,  
But with the people from the opera,  
I can steadily gather joy,  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Forge the iron while it glows.  

5.  
I am just a poor grinder, see,  
Who must earn his living by grinding,  
When I work with the stones:  
Then I know how to hold that wrench so well  
In the manner of handling,  
I know how to take good care of my business,  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Forge the iron while it glows.  

6.  
I am just a poor turner girl, see  
Who must earn her living by turning,  
When I work with the lathe,  
Then I know how to control that disc so nicely,  
Everyone who knows what controlling is,  
And with that I must entertain myself,  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Forge the iron while it glows.  

7.  
I am just a poor singer’s wife!  
Who must earn her living by singing,  
When I go around with my songs!  
Then I must force my voice out of poverty,  
Therefore buy a song from me,  
If you don’t want to be deceived.  
Clang the tat, strike something, strike hard,  
Fire your hammers,  
Forge the iron while it glows.

label: work
translation:
I drive with my wagon,  
Through the whole country,  
I drink with pleasure,  
A glass by hand,  
I sharpen scissors, knives,  
That are very good bargains,  
Therefore young daughters,  
Hurry on your way,  
Round, round, round,  
So go my wheels,  
Cheerfully every moment.  

One can well notice,  
From the heaviest work,  
I am not afraid,  
Like a young daughter,  
Addresses herself to me,  
Then I am much happier,  
And sincerely glad,  
Round, round, round, etc.  

Often in the cities,  
One finds at all times,  
A scissor-sharpener,  
Who cheers there gladly,  
And when they get married,  
They think no differently,  
Than to give some work,  
To the scissor sharpener;  
Round, round, etc.  

I do not like to work,  
Where I drink something,  
In all my misery,  
I wet my throat,  
I keep my wheels turning,  
And that for my Catrien,  
I have never in my days,  
Seen a cleaner jest,  
Round, round, etc.  

I sharpen scissors,  
That in the new fashion,  
It is to harvest money,  
That I do the craft,  
For the old women,  
I sharpen without zeal,  
But for young daughters,  
My wheel always turns,  
Round, round, etc.  

Our old sharpener,  
Pays me with his deed,  
Since I am a sharpener,  
Who earns his bread on the street,  
With the old people,  
I have lost money,  
But with young daughters,  
I have already spent it,  
Round, round, etc.  

Friends of honor;  
Who rejoice the heart,  
Ladies and gentlemen,  
Who hear it with joy,  
How a scissor sharpener,  
Earns his bread on the street,  
And with his wheels,  
Goes on his back,  
Round, round, round,  
So go my wheels,  
Cheerfully every moment.

label: work
translation:
Always diligent, day by day,  
I make the main ornament of women:  
Always diligent, day by day,  
I do not mind,  
That with pleasure,  
With style, spirit, and grace,  
I create the Caps,  
With style, spirit, and grace  
The ladies' adornment.  
The Cap is worthy of everyone's respect,  
If it is clean, neatly folded. (the Capo)  
Laziness looks bad on a girl,  
It always brings contempt from the people,  
Laziness looks bad on a girl;  
I want to avoid it,  
I work with enjoyment,  
Nothing can tire me,  
I see the work steadily grow,  
Nothing can tire me,  
It gives me honor;  
Thus, I see my prosperity flourish,  
I win more and more customers. (the Capo)  

I dress neatly, according to the fashion,  
Well, who could begrudge me that?  
I dress neatly, according to the fashion,  
Yes, those who can win,  
It does not look out of place.  
I sing while working,  
This can strengthen me,  
I sing while working,  
This supports my diligence.  
Pleasure gives wings to our hours,  
If one passes them working. (the Capo)

label: work
translation:
Let me sing a little tune,  
it makes the work a bit lighter,  
and if the children are to be fed,  
something must be accomplished well;  
I sit all day pulling,  
and tapping on my knee;  
I keep gaining new strength  
when I see the wife and little ones,  
when I see the wife and little ones.

label: work
translation:
When I play the wooden hammer,
Or the shaft,
I often think of the State,
That barrel strikes many,
Also an untiring hand,
Yet it does not remain standing,
Yet it does not remain standing:
With my well-fitted hoop,
I keep the whole game together as one,
And firmer, I believe,
Even water stands for the seams,
Where the barrel of the State,
Lets many things pass,
Lets pass,
Lets pass.
Why is there no unity!
Unity gives the greatest power,
That has been known since ancient times;
Unity, it seems to me, is a cooper,
Who around the barrel of the State,
Keeps hitting a hoop again and again,
Hits a hoop,
Hits a hoop.
And freedom would not complain,
About tyranny and scorn,
She sat laughing on her throne,
She sat laughing on her throne:
But when one wants to walk to the right,
Another goes off to the left,
Then unity cannot exist,
And freedom must well complain,
It is sad, yes certainly,
That there is no unity,
There is unity,
There is unity.

label: work
translation:
Well, knoll, you do that quite well,  
You immediately stand like a wall,  
The pliers don’t need to be on your nose,  
And the work is not hard for me;  
But if you hit like that,  
Then we have trouble,  
Yes, receiving is a bad blow,  
And who will compensate us,  
And who will compensate us?

label: work
translation:
Life goes quite smoothly,  
one rides at ease,  
the other trots like a post horse,  
barely under a roof at night;  
This one feasts on chickens and partridges,  
hotchpotch is the other's specialty;  
This one drinks wine from fine glasses,  
the other water from a jug:  
the other water from a jug.

label: work
translation:
I am sitting on the chimney above,  
Clean but covered with soot and dust.  
I still sing like a gentleman –  
Pulling the net up and down.  

I always do my work with pleasure,  
After my labor I go to rest.  
I always think so well in my mind,  
While the reward suits the work.  

Recently a kind girl called me,  
Chimney sweep, here’s a little trip,  
If you sweep well, come inside quickly.  
I asked her where the chimney was.  
Come, she said... but into the kitchen.  
It seemed this girl was a bit nervous,  
It’s a chimney that I know,  
Because I am the kitchen maid.  

Yes, I want to believe that,  
And quickly I climbed up,  
Through the lower chimney hole,  
Until I reached just the right height.  

Then he sat up in the top,  
Sang clearly from his throat,  
And mockingly teased the girl,  
Who had gladly seduced him.

label: work
translation:
Method: Hitting the Steel with the Hammer, etc.

To work, diligent fellows!  
Are the lamps nearly done?  
Can I go deliver them?  
Today I didn’t want to fast,  
But prefer to receive my money,  
Which is earned from the work; repeat.  
This way I pay the gentlemen.  
I finish my work on time;  
Cheerful lads striking away:  
Tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick.  
Now I dare to defy London,  
Tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick,  
To hammer sheet metal in a new way,  
Better than in England,  
In England, repeat.

We must try everything  
That can be done abroad,  
And avoid dull sluggishness,  
That will honor the Fatherland;  
Thus everyone labors diligently,  
Constantly for the common good, repeat.  
It’s starting to look like it,  
Everything comes out neat,  
And I run at full speed,  
Tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick,  
To show off the work,  
Tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick, tick,  
Hammering sheet metal in a new way,  
Can also be done in the Netherlands,  
In the Netherlands, repeat.

A.F.Sz.

label: work
translation:
I.
One day I went out to sweep chimneys along the street;
I saw a young lady in front of the windows;
Her fine appearance was her pride and status,
I called out, "Miss, may I quickly
Clean your chimney?
I am a master who, quickly,
And very well, does his work,
You can see it by my machine,
That I will serve you very well."

2.
"Let us not argue long,"
I said directly to her,
"Let us rather come to an agreement,
My equipment is all ready,
Let me with one sweep inside your chimney
Make your chimney pleasing to you.
I pull up and down my rope,
That goes very fast, believe me, Miss,
If I am not mistaken, to be sure,
Your chimney is full of soot."

3.
I will clean it for you thoroughly,
Miss, see my machine here,
You need not pay me anything,
If I do not serve you well;
Please grant me your favor, try my skill,
Miss, you may believe it,
You will see it, and perhaps even
Three, four times, or even more,
Miss, listen, consider carefully,
Let us come to an agreement."

4.
I will concentrate on your chimney,
Chimney sweep according to your wishes;
But you must try for yourself,
Whether my equipment fits in there;
Because believe me, the gap is small,
I first want to stick the head in,
If you recognize it in my chimney,
All the way to the end, I am content,
Climb up quickly, and sweep well,
You know where you have to sweep."

5.
Miss, I find myself not embarrassed,
Because it is very easy for me,
To clean your chimney,
Even though the gap is still so tight,
Ask very gently, I just sweep in,
You will experience
How my broom will sweep,
As if nothing, I am already going there,
I called, "Miss, what do you say now?"
The Miss said, "That will go very fast."

6.
Ladies now want to hurry,
Grant me your favor and don't walk away,
Want to buy that lovely song,
Of the chimney that I mean,
That I have so thoroughly swept,
By this Miss I want to know,
Who will then be served by me
Who calls quickly, hear here my friend,
Then I come there, and sweep very well,
With my machine, your chimney is ready."

label: work
translation:
I.
I sit upon the chimney above,
Though covered with soot, dusted,          bis.
Still I sing like a lord,
While going up and down.

2.
I always do my work with pleasure,
After labor, it’s good to rest:          bis.
I always think, with a good heart,
While the reward sweetens the work.

3.
Recently a nice girl asked me:
Chimney sweep! Tell me a story,           bis.
Are you quick at sweeping already?
She asked where the chimney was?

4.
Come (she said) into the kitchen,
(It seemed the girl was a bit itchy,)
It’s a chimney that I know,
Since I am the kitchen maid.

5.
Yes, I would like to believe that,
I said, and quickly climbed up,          bis.
Through the lower chimney hole,
Until I reached the right height.

6.
When I sat above on the top,
I sang clearly from my throat;          bis.
And so I mocked the girl,
Who gladly had seduced me.

7.
If you want your chimney swept,
Girls, then consider carefully,          bis.
That you have it done when it suits you;
Be content for now.

label: work
translation:
1.  
I’m sitting here on the step, and wait,  
Surely the Maid will hear!  
Yes, even if she lies warm and soft,  
I must disturb her sleep;  
I’ll call again, it’s about time,  
I’d almost be annoyed,  
If I were to waste the time here  
I’d be stealing the day’s wage.

2.  
I still have so much to do,  
First the stairs and the small hall,  
Where I clean everything nicely,  
And then the porch,  
I still have to scrub wet and dry,  
Even the kitchen and cellar,  
And not just something for show,  
But thoroughly and clearly.

3.  
Miss won’t look over to me,  
But should I endure  
That when I leave  
She finds reason to complain,  
I’m rewarded well by her,  
And get a decent meal,  
Then it’s fair that one shows  
To even know one’s duty.

4.  
I sometimes think it’s really hard,  
Day after day to toil,  
If I had work for the whole year  
I might get through it,  
But what I earn in summer  
I must live on in winter,  
And then it’s difficult for  
Everyone to give their share.

5.  
But this does not hurt my courage,  
I am still content,  
Even if I have no abundance,  
I have healthy limbs,  
If my clothes are sometimes a bit poor,  
Old, and full of patches,  
It doesn’t matter, if everyone says,  
She is still pretty and neat.

6.  
I am healthy and quick and strong,  
I earn my bread with honor;  
If I am faithful in my work  
No one can harm me,  
Since no one envies my fate,  
I have nothing to fear;  
I’ll call again, where is the Maid,  
softly,... there she must be.

label: work
translation:
1.  
I must happily stretch my harness,  
Beating the leather while singing;  
May my elm not give way;  
Otherwise, I would have been finished with it.  
I have clogs here, I have shoes here,  
Mostly from girls in the neighborhood;  
If they are dirty, I must clean them,  
Yes, they could well be polished.

2.  
Sometimes I have to patch them,  
Although I barely earn anything,  
And wouldn’t earn a glass of beer,  
But I do it as I please;  
I certainly cut many little pieces of wood  
For the toddlers, to my detriment;  
Yet they speak rather boldly:  
I must have my shoes soon.

3.  
I also have to be reasonable,  
Because I carry for her lord,  
Otherwise I might have to fear  
That he would give me no more work.  
So I must carry myself happily  
With the old shoe repair;  
But if I did it every day,  
I would hardly benefit from it.

label: work
translation:
1.  
I must already be upstairs again,  
It's Kaatjen here and Kaatjen there,  
Then there must be fire in the stoves,  
And then it’s time to make coffee;  
Soon my Lord will come, then we must eat,  
The table must be ready,  
How will it still go! how will it still go!  
Everything is tossed about in confusion,  
What shall I start with? what shall I start with?  
This way I’ll never get my work done.  

2.  
Today I have to sweep two rooms,  
And first thing tomorrow, the stairs,  
Everything is going against me now,  
The bell rings like a slap of Lazarus,  
If only I could stay upstairs a while,  
At least I’d get some benefit,  
I am so disturbed! I am so disturbed!  
I still have so much good to rub,  
I am so disturbed, I am so disturbed,  
Kaatjen is always the other word.  

3.  
But I must stop that grumbling,  
Well, shame! that does not suit a good girl,  
It surely does not help the work,  
It looks much more like unwillingness,  
I’d rather start again with courage,  
Then the labor becomes so light for me;  
It is still my duty, it is still my duty  
I must earn my keep and wages with it,  
And it is my duty, and it is my duty,  
That I perform the work with pleasure.  

4.  
I certainly have no complaints,  
Madam is reasonable, good, and kind;  
She gave me a raise without asking,  
I have food and drink in abundance,  
She is not a woman who is proud and spiteful,  
Or snaps at or despises her maids;  
But she expects, but she expects,  
That one then is also faithful and diligent,  
With all one's might, with all one's might,  
Always with pleasure attends to her duty.  

5.  
I do manage quite well with the clothes here,  
I am well supplied with linen,  
Now I would still desire something,  
If only I served a little longer;  
If only I could buy a nice chintz jacket,  
A blue silk skirt in the fashion;  
And then a lace, a fine lace,  
But that would still cost me too much,  
No, with sense, no, with sense,  
I won’t spend all the money from my hand.  

6.  
Well, I have rubbed myself tired:  
But when Madam sees the chairs,  
She will surely give me a compliment;  
And what does it matter to me about glory,  
I often hear her talk about me,  
My Kaatjen, she says, oh that’s a girl!  
Of skillfulness, and good judgment,  
I dare to rely on her with confidence,  
She has judgment and honesty,  
Just think how this flatters my pride.  

7.  
That is why I am continually busy,  
So that I may give her pleasure,  
I take care whether she is home or absent,  
That I always live orderly,  
What could true happiness lack,  
From someone who does their duties,  
Is freedom sweet and abundant,  
I hear so much talk about wealth;  
But the free spirit, the peaceful spirit,  
And the honest heart is the best good.

label: work
translation:
I spend the whole day thinking.  
for only a penny four or five;  
I barely earn more than the room rent,  
a skirt and jacket on my body:  
However, this cannot bore me,  
my Miss provides a good meal,  
and she is as well as husband and children,  
as well as I am a patriot;  
as well as I am a patriot.

label: work
translation:
Damn, this waiting,
It's almost half past one again,
I’ve already fallen asleep three times in my sled,
That happens every night,
I say the sleeping is sorrow,
And the customers don’t count,
And, the customers don’t count:
Little sleep, little gain,
Night and day just quickly onward,
Whoever still likes such a life
Is certainly robbed of their senses,
If it were possible, just paint a little more,
I laid it down neatly,
And worked again,
And worked again.
And that is just a modest part,
Because a sleeper doesn’t give much,
They don’t give much
They don’t give much.

label: work
translation:
I must at least earn the bread,
Every person likes to move forward,
But if I were to start that too,
I certainly wouldn't win a single penny;
Backward is no way to win,
Forward, then, it's all ruined;
Backward is no way to win,
Forward, then, it's all ruined,
Hundreds of thousands of fathoms
I have certainly already worked off;
Hundreds of thousands of fathoms,
I have certainly already worked off.

label: work
translation:
God is good! I want to praise Him,  
Him who gives life to all.  
Nothing surpasses His grace,  
He is good to all His creatures.  
Sun and wind, and dew and rain  
Bring gentle abundance.  
Meadow and herd feel the blessing  
From Heaven. God is good.

See the little beasts grow lushly,  
Even heavy and slow from fatness,  
The udders swell, the buckets flow  
Over with rich cream.  
All that blessing comes from above,  
Awaken my heart to Your honor,  
Teach me to praise Your goodness,  
Teach me to be grateful, oh Lord!

label: work
translation:
There goes the sower up and down,  
And carries the seed vessel back and forth,  
And sprinkles with a swinging hand  
The seed on the stony land.  
How hard the man toils! How he sweats!  
It is indeed a heavy and difficult task!  

But what pleasure smiles upon him,  
When he once sees the precious grain  
Sprout, green, and bloom,  
And the harvest offers him reward for labor!  
How he rejoices at the blessed hour,  
When he may carry it into the barn!  
So I must never be discouraged,  
By care, fatigue, and pain,  
Though practicing virtue sometimes costs me  
A tear of displeasure;  
For the harvest time comes with joyfulness,  
This rejoicing is prepared for weeping.

label: work
translation:
I rejoice in my blessed fate:  
I asked for nothing;  
Apart from God, no one  
Has ever enough of rank and wealth.  
I have peace and daily sustenance,  
No enemy who schemes harm against me,  
Whose welfare is mine alike!  

It is true, I work from dawn  
Until evening;  
But work makes healthy, strong,  
And also happily tired.  
I am prepared to work,  
And call the vile idleness  
A plague upon Society.  
What joy my soul experiences  
When the work is done;  
Since I gain my merchant’s profit,  
Who watches over my well-being,  
While rewarding me according to merit,  
And shows himself a friend of man,  
Who fulfills his duty.  

How then do my spouse and children rejoice  
When I return home.  
Not one utters even a sigh:  
Each then praises the Almighty Lord,  
Who brings people to our welfare,  
And covers us with wings of love,  
And clothes and feeds and drinks us.  

I did not trade my humble state  
For earthly riches:  
Luxury and greedy excess  
Breed poverty and sorrow;  
But the labor that grants us benefit  
Elevates the heart that prayerfully reflects  
On Him who governs all.

label: work
translation:
I make a pipe, of fragile clay and earth,  
And learn how fragile life is,  
Since it still brings me sweet joy,  
It also adds to me the realization,  
That I may still work cheerfully,  
And earn my bread every day. (repeat)  

When we then sit together at the shop,  
Whether by day or by the lamp,  
Let us never forget in our work,  
That our life is like a vapor;  
The pipe that thus awakens our attention,  
Is the one that serves as a lesson to us. (repeat)  

Should we then love drunkenness?  
Or serve slanderous desire?  
No! friends, no! we will reflect,  
And oppose the evil example,  
Who works diligently, and lives frugally,  
Is the one who has food in winter. (repeat)  
Nothing will then hinder us in distress,  
One sees, steadily working,  
Take care, with full diligence, of our wife and children,  
So that each protects them from want;  
And chests, they tremble too,  
They guard against cracks and splits. (repeat)  

Let us together, in molding or in rolling,  
In straining, be attentive;  
Thus our work remains with the glass, and with the pipes,  
Renowned on the Y, and Maas, and Rhine,  
So it is that pipe-making,  
May be useful and beneficial to us.

label: work
translation:
Tryntjen, I am truly tired from working,  
But the haymaking must continue:  
Tomorrow I might not even notice;  
Food and rest are good for it.  
Have you prepared something for us?  
Ha! that is the proper fare,  
Boy! come, that will please us,  
Like pies at the Drost.

If we had now sat quietly,  
Just like lazy Piet,  
No one would eat so tastefully,  
The loafer does not eat tastefully.  
Now quickly, pour some in.  
Drink after food is medicine,  
I see the beer spark in the glass itself,  
Work turns the beer into wine.  
Fie! such unmannerly yawning,  
My mouth yawns like a hayloft;  
Whether I will also sleep properly..,  
Boys! Are you still wandering here,  
Hurry up and go upstairs. -  
Tryntjen! How are you looking at me,  
I will indeed promise a goodnight kiss..:  
Then quickly get it done.

Oh, how blessed is our life,  
Work makes everything sweet after all  
What can give so much blessing,  
Than a pure heart alongside it...  
Sometimes my Tryntje comes flying to me,  
And my children - truly a blessing...  
I could cry from joy.  
Dear Tryntje! How good is God!..

label: work
translation:
Where indeed does one find more joy  
Than with the inexperienced youth, bis.  
Fun, fun, always fun,  
Laughing, singing, dancing, jumping  
Fun, fun, always fun,  
Dancing they go to their bed.

Heintjen! play something quiet now!  
I think little sister wants to sleep, bis.  
In that little corner, lies a little book,  
There, there, Koosjen! there,  
Look at the pictures together.

Sleep now Jerjen! sleep softly now,  
Lotjen keeps watch over you, bis.  
Sleep, sleep, little one, sleep,  
Sleep my darling! heart thief!  
Sleep, sleep, Jerjen sleep,  
Oh! how the little lamb tosses!  
The airy little blanket covers the cradle,  
From the teasing of mosquito or fly.  
Fresh, fresh, cool and fresh,  
Lies my Jetjen on her little bed,  
Fresh, fresh, cool and fresh,  
Where it is filled with hay.

Well! what then bothers the dear child?  
Perhaps the sight troubles her; bis.  
Come, come, darling! come,  
I will stop it, I will move it,  
Come, come, darling! come,  
Lotjen shifts the cradle around.

It is true, a child’s maid  
Is continuously busy; bis.  
But, but, it is also true,  
I must admit, one can get used to it,  
But, but, it is also true,  
The burden is not equally heavy for everyone.

I also know that the little ones,  
Must be managed with understanding; bis.  
Neat, neat, just and precise,  
I must know how much to allow or prohibit,  
Neat, neat, just and precise,  
What I allow or deny.  
No! even though I have little rest  
Children are my joy and desire. bis.  
I know - I know no sorrow,  
If they are healthy, lively and cheerful,  
I know - I know no sorrow,  
I am always ready for them.

Always my maids are around me,  
Lotjen, Lotjen, is the only one. bis.  
Never, never, certainly never  
Do the maids play or quarrel,  
Never, never, certainly never  
Is anything done without me.

It is out of love, and with pleasure,  
That I watch over their well-being. bis.  
Sweet, sweet, soft and sweet,  
Laughing, caressing, talking, playing,  
Sweet, sweet, soft and sweet,  
I best lead their tender minds.

And this is not without result,  
I see it in Heintjen, and ape Koos; bis.  
See, see, don’t they  
Always act kindly, happily and skillfully,  
See, see, don’t they,  
Do everything they are told.  
But while I rock and sing,  
The little thing laughs and speaks; bis.  
Yes! Yes! I will soon,  
Take the dear Jetjen out of her bed;  
Yes; yes; I will soon take her—  
But—what joy; there is Mama.

label: work
translation:
Well, what a mess this is here;  
Shame! What would my wife say?  
If she saw it laid out so full here,  
Nothing is empty! Not even a chair;  
Everyone brings me something down,  
But then it gets thrown down:  
Storing is almost forgotten,  
That is the biggest plague for me.

Come! I'll quickly fix it up,  
One, two, three, well stored,  
I'll just ensure peace,  
There! Now it's like new again.  
If I can avoid  
Displeasure over a small matter,  
Then I can truly rejoice,  
Certainly, that is my pleasure.  
I have been very busy today;  
But that may be freely believed,  
Cooking, baking, roasting, stewing,  
I can do like the best cook;  
Look: it is my greatest joy,  
If I have a lot to prepare;  
I have also already  
Set several things ready in advance.

When my people have company,  
I make sure they eat well,  
Nonetheless, I will never forget,  
That 'frugality is lived'.  
I dare, Madam, at the butter tub,  
Even to peek with a request:  
I don't dig with holes or corners,  
No, it is as smooth as ice,

I do now burn a somewhat bigger fire;  
But I watch out to extinguish the coals;  
Tonight I must stew vegetables;  
And peat is terribly expensive:  
Those who store coal,  
Do not always have to burn hard,  
I must constantly boil water,  
The fire is always on the hearth.  
It is my duty, and I do it with pleasure,  
To manage something for the people,  
Although they do not say it often,  
They rely on my faithfulness.  
I also take it as my own affair;  
And if Madam wants proof,  
Let her look in all my cupboards,  
It is always neat and ready there.

I am proud that people know,  
Wherever I have roamed about,  
Nothing has ever spoiled for me;  
I ensure that the leftovers are eaten first,  
I know well, there is plenty here,  
But should it be forgiven for that?  
That I would live recklessly with it;  
No! It troubled my conscience.

What does Kee neighbor Mie think,  
If she constantly skims something?  
Sugar, tea, and spices collected,  
And gives some to others?  
It is the greatest theft;  
For she violates the good trust,  
It would grieve me all my life,  
If I ever did as she does.  
I have things wonderfully to my liking here!  
The people also consider us as humans!  
I would never wish for better food,  
The wage is fairly what I earn.  
And Madam! – oh! She is so good!  
I must respect and love her.  
I do not know what I will do,  
If I have to leave her.

But I have Koô, that faithful servant,  
Already kept waiting for five years;  
On his repeated complaints,  
I finally said yes.  
His merit is not great,  
But he is sincerely devoted to me;  
I think with diligence and Heaven’s blessing,  
One easily earns his daily bread.

label: work
translation:
I must truly suffer from above again,
It’s Kaatjen here and Kaatjen there,
Then there must be fire in the stoves,
And then it’s: make some coffee ready;
Soon my master will come, then we must eat,
The table must be set,
How will it still go! how will it still go!
Everything is thrown in disarray,
What do I start with? what do I start with?
This way I’ll never get my work done.

Today I have to sweep two rooms,
And tomorrow immediately the stairs.
Everything now seems to go against me,
The bell rings like a Lazarus blow.
If I could stay upstairs a while,
At least I would get some benefit;
I am so upset! I am so upset!
I still have so much to rub here,
I am so upset, I am so upset,
Kaatjen is always the other word.
But I must stop that grumbling,
Well shame! that doesn’t suit a good girl.
It can’t do any good to the work,
It looks much more like unwillingness.
I’d rather start again with courage,
Then labor falls somewhat lighter on me:
It is my duty, it is my duty,
I must earn my living and wages with it,
And it is my duty, and it is my duty,
That I perform the work with pleasure.

I certainly have nothing to complain about,
Madam is reasonable, good and kind,
She gave me a raise without asking,
I have food and drink in abundance.
She is not a woman who proudly and angrily,
Snaps at or despises her maids;
But she expects, but she expects,
That one is faithful and diligent then,
With all one’s might, with all one’s might,
Always performing one’s duty with pleasure.

I do wear out quite a bit here in the clothes,
Of linen I am well supplied,
Now I would still want something,
If I only served a bit longer;
If I could buy a nice chintz jacket,
A blue silk skirt in the style;
And then a lace, a fine lace,
But that would still cost me dearly,
No, with sense, no, with sense;
I won’t spend all the money from my hand.
Well I have rubbed myself enough,
But if Madam sees the chairs,
She will surely give me a prize;
And what then does it matter to me for glory.
I hear her so often speak of me.
My Kaatjen; she says, oh! that’s a girl!
Of skill, and good judgment -
I dare confidently rely on her,
She has judgment and honesty,
Just think how this flatters my pride!

That is why I am constantly busy,
So that I give her pleasure
I take care whether she is home or absent,
That I always live orderly,
What would true happiness lack
Of someone who does their duties,
Freedom is sweet, and abundance,
I hear so much about wealth,
But the free mind, the peaceful mind,
And the honest heart is the best treasure.

label: work
work
translation:
I faithfully keep my watch,  
According to the prescription of my duty;  
And if I hear someone, here or there,  
I go to see what he is doing;  
If I find him, driven by greed,  
Taking from his neighbor’s goods,  
I bring him to justice for his evil deed,  
There, where the thief belongs. –

I never seize, against law and right,  
My neighbor without cause;  
Which very often, for money alone,  
Is done by others;  
Our wage is indeed a modest part;  
But this gives no one the right  
To associate with wickedness  
And boldly fight innocence. –  
I warn everyone, in the evening hour,  
To carefully pay attention  
To the otherwise harmful light and fire,  
That shatters many a house.  
And if I perceive a fire somewhere,  
I call everyone together,  
So that no effort is spared in extinguishing it,  
But actual help is given.

My status is insignificant, regardless;  
Yet it gives me much pleasure,  
Though many a harsh night affects me,  
When I watch in winter;  
For in the silent darkness,  
When everyone sleeps and rests,  
What is hidden by day,  
Is at night my greatest joy. –

I often stand uplifted,  
When I behold the starry vault  
By the light of the silver moon,  
Thus speaking to myself, –  
‘How great must my Creator be,  
Who spreads so much majesty,  
For us, in the world’s realm of ruin,  
By day and night.’  
As soon as the dear dawn  
Appears to my eye,  
I thank my God that He has spared me,  
At night, from evil;  
Then I lie down a little time,  
On my resting place;  
And afterwards with pleasure and diligence,  
By day, I resume my labor.

label: work
translation:
Cheerful boys, go on!  
Do you not work there as you should?  
The sewing is not done as it ought to be,  
Look how badly the stitches run.  
Fortunately, it’s only a pair of trousers,  
From the beautiful Jewish quarter,  
Thus I serve everyone according to rank,  
Those who let me dress them.

The faded little black one that’s there,  
Is a Proponent’s dwelling,  
If the back is somewhat widened,  
It will fit him like a glove.  
A fat profession, or a wealthy wife,  
Is the point he and I rely on,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.  
But the coat that looks like gold,  
Seems to belong to a Baron,  
Is entrusted to a merchant,  
Now much richer than before,  
It suits me well, by good manners,  
To put the money to more work,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.

Jan Baas has brought me work, too!  
He will turn the brown coat,  
His work ethic shows day and night,  
What distinction among lazy gentlemen!  
Although he spends his goods yearly,  
His diligence always keeps me ready,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.

The straight thread has a fine strength,  
Who knows what I must resew?  
It is cleverly devised,  
To earn even more profit,  
For myself (I don’t do it),  
It goes back, whatever is left over,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.

But they say, (I know well)  
That in every tailor’s dwelling,  
There is a great patchwork,  
Yet I know this as an excuse,  
That I never steal a scrap,  
Unless there is too much,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.

Cheerful boys, keep working,  
Money satisfies all our desires,  
The one who does the most does it first,  
The one who steals the most must be the first to hang,  
Learn from me, listen to your boss,  
Who blabs everything is surely a fool,  
Thus I serve everyone; etc.

label: work
translation:
What new things does one not see  
A surgeon spends his time,  
To make himself beloved,  
With clothes after the first flourish,  
Now that these are also his matters.  
I waited for a good enema,  
My beard is still growing,  
You will still wait a day or two or three  
Then come to trim it.  
And he no longer found the work acceptable,  
He thought she had to give birth,  
Oh no! you had to be, in your practice,  
More experienced.

label: work
translation:
Come friends, stand still a little, ha ha  
And listen to what I want to sing, ha ha  
All about the Harvest of the Netherlands,  
And take up the weapons in hand, ha ha ha  

The Servant builds the Farmer’s land, ha ha  
And takes the plow handle in hand, ha ha  
There he then harnesses three horses,  
They pull along so happily, ha ha ha  

When the Servant has built it, ha ha  
Then it is considered the Farmer’s, ha ha  
What must stand on every land,  
According to what he thinks will go best, ha ha ha  

He sows it well to his liking, ha ha  
But he knows nothing of good gain, ha ha  
But our God who lives eternally,  
Who gives ripeness to the seed, ha ha ha  

The Farmer, with his strong hand, ha ha  
Lays the harrows on the land, ha ha  
There he then harnesses the horses,  
They pull right along so cheerfully, ha ha ha  

It is entrusted to the black clay, ha ha  
And is not rebuilt again, ha ha  
Before the fruits are also mowed,  
And then another crop is sown, ha ha ha  

The grain harvest is now coming, ha ha  
That we are going to mow the grain, ha ha  
Then we also mow very gladly,  
If our sight just cuts it, ha ha ha  
When it is mowed, it falls down, ha ha  
Another comes and binds it again, ha ha  
And when the binding is done,  
Then one must begin to stack, ha ha ha  

And our sheaves are placed, ha ha  
In straight rows through the field, ha ha  
To dry out by the wind,  
Then we also come very swiftly, ha ha ha  

And take it away to our barn, ha ha  
It also only lasts a short time, ha ha  
Then we take it down again,  
And lay it on the threshing floor, ha ha ha  

When one threshes the grain, ha ha  
Or separates the grain from the chaff, ha ha  
Or makes it cleaner with the winnowing basket,  
Or does some other work on it, ha ha ha  

When winter time arrives, ha ha  
That everyone starts threshing, ha ha  
And beats it with the flail,  
So that the seed flies around their head, ha ha ha  

There servants and maids have to help, ha ha  
Sometimes also the wife and the man, ha ha  
That beats and threshes then together  
At the soldiers’ fence fire, ha ha ha  

And when it has been beaten off, ha ha  
One shakes it up with the shovel, ha ha  
And throws the straw to the animals,  
They eat there so deliciously, ha ha ha  

Then it is kicked into a heap, ha ha  
The clock strikes eight, the woman calls, ha ha  
Come boys, come all of you,  
What eating, for the porridge is ready, ha ha ha  

Then everyone also washes up nicely, ha ha  
And quickly eats their fill of porridge, ha ha  
And then back to the work,  
And waters horse and ox and cow, ha ha ha  

The chaff mill is brought out before me, ha ha  
And separates the grain from the chaff, ha ha  
And prepares it with winnowing basket and sieve,  
So that nothing impure remains, ha ha  

Then it is neatly measured into the sack, ha ha  
The Farmer tightens the wagon, ha ha  
And loads it on according to his liking,  
And then drives with the steward, ha ha ha  

Then the Farmer is also well off, ha ha  
Then he comes with a sack full of money, ha ha  
Which he lays down in his cupboard,  
With the other money, or he has no more, ha ha ha

label: work
translation:
1.  
The sweet hardship rightly hits  
The wish of the Craftsman:  
Now that I am laid down in the shade;  
How sweet the rest tastes to me!

2.  
Today it was so terribly hot  
That since I began,  
A stream of sweat ran down my face,  
As if there were a spring in my head.

3.  
What the poor man must not suffer,  
For those who do nothing,  
Whom we call lazy in abundance,  
They see themselves resting tired!

4.  
I often thought (though I was wrong,  
Which I gladly admit,)  
Why am I not also as rich  
As such people are?

5.  
But it occurred to me: God deemed it good,  
That you are aware of this;  
And the piece of bread tastes sweet only  
To the one who rests after labor.

6.  
With everything, after a short time,  
It is done on earth, after all,  
Then begins, for joyful eternity,  
The holy evening.

7.  
There we are all equal again,  
There is no pressure nor cross,  
The work is finished, and poor and rich  
Go home to receive their wages.

label: work
translation:
1.  
How cheerful can the farmer live,  
When he considers his land and people,  
And can give each their due,  
And then trusts in God,  
The farmer who sows and mows the land,  
And then waits for blessings from God's hand.  

2.  
When he sees the morning clear up,  
Everyone starts with new strength,  
Threshing to gather the grain,  
And when winter is over,  
He drives the animals in the field;  
The farmer gives praise and thanks to God.  

3.  
How could the baker work,  
The dear bread that people like to eat,  
If God did not strengthen the farmer  
So that he prepares his land,  
How would one live in the village,  
If the farmer did not do the work.  

4.  
How could the sailor live,  
If the farmer did not do the work,  
It is true God must give growth,  
And made him strong through goodness,  
Therefore he shows his gratitude  
After his completed work.  

5.  
When you are seated at a table,  
Take a turn to look on your table,  
Then take a look at the dear food  
It all comes from the farmer,  
So, human, as often as you eat,  
Do not forget the farming profession.  

6.  
It is true the sailor brings many goods,  
From all kinds of countries,  
But if there were no farmers there,  
How would they exist there?  
It all comes from the farmer’s hand,  
But God is master of the land.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Pull the Pik thread,  
Draw it through, and show your strong shoes with dots.  
That is now the fashion!  
White is the leather, after all, darn expensive!  
Krispyn gives comfort, gives adventure,  
We never celebrate a Monday anymore.  
Although the leather remains costly,  
Pull the Pik thread, etc.  

2.  
Those foreign papers,  
They make it so that I have to work,  
For little wages, by night and day,  
Much harder than I used to.  
Pull the Pik thread, etc.  

3.  
Before, back then, it was a better time,  
Then we lost the shoes  
For a better price, the leather was cheap,  
But I live in good hope.  
Pull the Pik thread, etc.  

4.  
Come, citizens! come! come here and try  
My shoes, that is better leather,  
Look well, it is just as strong,  
And somewhat neater work.  
Pull the Pik thread, etc.  

5.  
Even if you buy well, for a lower price,  
What do you gain if it does not meet the demands  
Of beauty and strength?  
Buy here, my work is good.  
Pull the Pik thread, etc.

label: work
translation:
Way: How sweet it is where friendship lives!

I rejoice in my blessed fate:
I have what I asked for;
Regarding rank and wealth, besides God,
No one ever has enough.
I have rest and daily sustenance,
No enemy brewing harm against me.
Whose welfare is the same as mine!

It is true, I work from early dawn
Until evening;
But the work makes me strong, healthy,
And also happily tired.
I am prepared to work,
And call idle laziness
A plague on Society.

What joy my soul feels,
When the work is finished;
Since I gain my merchant’s profit,
Who guards my well-being,
As he rewards me according to merit,
And shows himself a friend to mankind
Who fulfills his duty.

How then my wife and children rejoice,
When I return home.
Not one releases a single sigh:
Everyone praises the Lord Almighty,
Who brings people to our welfare,
And covers us with wings of love,
And clothes and feeds and drinks us.

I traded my humble state,
For no earthly riches:
Luxury and greedy excess
Give rise to poverty and sorrow;
But the labor that grants us benefit,
Elevates the heart that meditates in prayer,
On Him who governs all.

D.B.

label: work
translation:
Way: Chloris who steals my little heart.

Oh, what a mess this is here;  
Fie! what would my wife say?  
If she saw it lying here so full,  
Nothing is empty! Not a single chair;  
Everyone brings me something to put away,  
But then it gets thrown down:  
Tidying up is almost forgotten,  
That is my greatest torment.

Come! I'll quickly fix it up,  
One, two, three, well put away,  
I'll just make sure there's peace,  
There! Now it's like new again.  
If I can avoid trouble  
Over a small matter,  
Then I can truly rejoice,  
Sure, that is my pleasure.

I have been very busy today;  
But one may freely believe that,  
Cooking, baking, roasting, stewing,  
I can do like the best cook;  
Look! It is my greatest delight  
When I have a lot to prepare;  
I have also already set  
Several things ready in advance.

When my people have company,  
I make sure they eat well,  
Nevertheless, I never forget  
That one lives frugally here.  
I dare to ask Mistress at the butter tub  
For even a look:  
I dig neither holes nor corners,  
No, it's as smooth as ice.

I now stoke a somewhat bigger fire;  
But I watch out to keep the coals alive;  
Tonight I must stew vegetables,  
And the turf is terribly expensive:  
Those who store coal well  
Do not always have to stoke hard,  
But I must boil water constantly,  
The fire is always on the hearth.

It is my duty, and I do it with pleasure,  
To arrange something for the people,  
Although they do not say it often,  
They rely on my loyalty.  
I also take it as my own property;  
And if Mistress desires proof,  
Let her look in all my cupboards,  
Everything is always ready and arranged.

I am proud that people know this;  
Wherever I have roamed around,  
Nothing has ever been lost to me;  
I make sure I eat the scraps first.  
I know there is abundance here,  
But was that a reason to forgive  
Recklessly living off it?  
No! It burdened my conscience.

What does Kee-buurs Mie think,  
If she takes something each time?  
Sugar, tea, and spices collected,  
And gives some to others?  
It is the greatest theft,  
For she violates good trust,  
It would grieve me all my life  
If I ever did the same as she.

Here, things are wonderfully to my liking!  
The people regard us as human too;  
I could never wish for better fare,  
The wage is fair that I earn.  
And Mistress! – oh! She is so good!  
I must respect and love her.  
I do not know what I will do  
If I ever have to leave her.

But I have Koô, that good servant,  
Who I have had wait now for five years;  
On his repeated complaints,  
At last once I said yes.  
His earnings are not great,  
But he is sincerely devoted to me;  
I think with diligence and Heaven’s blessing,  
One easily earns their daily bread.

Ma. V.H.

label: work
translation:
Tune: You who now are with me hunting.

I am again cheerfully at my work; ha! ha!  
And make the shoes neat and strong, ha! ha!  
Through which my boss wins many customers,  
And I steadily find work. ha! ha! ha!

A working man must be industrious, ha! ha!  
I don’t take Mondays or Krispijn off, ha! ha!  
But work the whole week diligently,  
And thus attend to my duty. ha! ha! ha!

Diligence, that beautiful virtue, ha! ha!  
Provides us money and grants us joy, ha! ha!  
Laziness is what causes suffering,  
Covering man with the garment of poverty. ha! ha! ha!

That he who likes it, makes Monday off. ha! ha!  
I have no taste for idling; ha! ha!  
Nor do I feel any self-reproach  
For wasting the precious time. ha! ha! ha!

My mates! who don’t work today, ha! ha!  
But now sit with fat Piet, ha! ha!  
Like gentlemen at their tavern,  
And think of no Saturday. ha! ha! ha!

They each have an inconvenience: ha! ha!  
That is; money dances in their pocket, ha! ha!  
And it must, down to the last penny,  
Be gone by Monday evening. ha! ha! ha!

By Tuesday their heads are destitute; ha! ha!  
Because they have drunk too much, ha! ha!  
Work has little appeal;  
They dridle and sleep and seem quite mad. ha! ha! ha!

Thus two days are already lost; ha! ha!  
But on Wednesday, oh then they work hard; ha! ha!  
Yet however they slave and toil,  
The week is short and doesn’t go well. ha! ha! ha!

On Saturday one then looks fearful, ha! ha!  
Since I then receive the most money. ha! ha!  
And because their week is rather meagre,  
I supply them to their annoyance. ha! ha! ha!

And when they come home to their wife, ha! ha!  
They tell her a story, ha! ha!  
As if the work didn’t go well;  
Thus the good worker is deceived. ha! ha! ha!

When the money received is then, ha! ha!  
Properly given to the wife; ha! ha!  
But no! she doesn’t get it all,  
They still slip some down their throat. ha! ha! ha!

The wife sighs, but it doesn’t help, ha! ha!  
The week is bad, as she sees. ha! ha!  
And while she grumbles and mumbles,  
She lacks a lot of money that week. ha! ha! ha!

Then where it was only this week, ha! ha!  
That still went reasonably well.  
But alas! most are even worse;  
So the wife ultimately knows no remedy. ha! ha! ha!

However she turns or twists the matter! ha! ha!  
It is poverty from beginning to end. ha! ha!  
Thus one works into their misfortune,  
Casting wife and children into hardship. ha! ha! ha!

A working man has enough work, ha! ha!  
Though he doesn’t waste money in the tavern. ha! ha!  
That he lives by the sweat of his brow.  
And everyone receives their due. ha! ha! ha!

J.H.

label: work
translation:
Way: Oh! how great how full of power, etc.

I must constantly sit sewing,
Early in the morning and late at night.
Should I keep running around,
Like others, along the street?
I would have to put my work down;
No, that would sadden me;
Because my customers would say:
Such a seamstress does not serve us.

When I come to collect new work,
It is always; oh! help me quickly!
And then I have to decide the time,
If it is the only little dress.
It is a weakness, not to be prevented,
If I did not do it well myself?
Yes, even if one had twenty clothes,
The new one must come first.

But what does it matter to me,
Everyone arranges it as they like.
Why should work bore me,
If I only earn my bread with it.
One penny (isn't it nice?)
That I earn through my diligence,
Is worth more to me than twenty,
Because I had to look into the eyes for it.

I live in my humble home,
Peaceful, contented, and free;
I am therefore richer than a king,
Because I envy no one's lot.
It can never lower me,
What I have to do for my bread;
To carry oneself well in one's station,
That makes a person truly great.

Work sometimes has its pleasures,
But there is effort and care attached to it.
It's like all things,
Everything has its joy and burden.
It can also please my pride.
It awakens my courage and zeal,
When they say: this one would be stolen,
Look! that dress fits like a doll.

I will then diligently sit sewing,
Eager, willing, early and late.
From idleness and dawdling,
Nothing comes but all evil,
I will once happily sing at work,
I am now truly glad at heart;
I know, the Creator of all things,
Care for everyone - so also for me.

Ma. V.H.

label: work
translation:
That free the farmer toils and sweats,  
For the benefit of society,  
But when he rests and drinks and eats,  
Then care must be gone.  
I like to see him in the dear circle  
Of the cheerful household,  
He then laughs, and cheers, and sings,  
For happiness is gain.  
Happiness is a blessing,  
For everything that exists.  
May that pleasure never leave us,  
Never leave us — never leave us.  
Where the rural folk with each other,  
Live so truly happy.  
When one at the joyful table,  
Sees his dearest treasures,  
What wonder that one is cheerful  
And enjoys such joy?  
Happiness is a blessing,  
For everything that exists,  
May that pleasure never leave us,  
Never leave us — never leave us.  
May that pleasure never leave us,  
Never leave us — never leave us.

label: work
translation:
1.  
Madam, do you want Geert, your noble maid,  
Your servant, whom I no longer please;  
But who longs for a quieter life,  
So the question is now whether you  
Would give testimony, as is required of me,  
About Geert?

2.  
The first question, madam, is:  
Is Geertje honest and faithful,  
And virtuous in her conduct?  
Does she have a sharp tongue?  
Does she give improper answers?  
But truly, is she sincere in her dealings?

3.  
Secondly, can she neatly and quickly  
Handle, manage the washing?  
Can she iron well and starch?  
Does she understand the housework thoroughly?  
Because see, the more skillfully this is done,  
The less I will argue.

4.  
Thirdly, according to your guess:  
Is she a night owl or a bat?  
Is she a chatterbox,  
Or does she have many suitors going wrong?  
Or does she sweetly flatter with honeyed words?  
That is a deceiver.

5.  
Does she also love playing the organ?  
How does she sing, is she at least decent?  
Is she reasonably passable?  
A maid is worth her keep  
Who keeps time at the musical instrument,  
Because that goes admirably.

6.  
Does Geertje sleep, if I may ask,  
Also once in a while during the day?  
I do not like lazy servants!  
Does she make the bed well and soft?  
And does she faithfully go hunting  
For the tormenting host of fleas?

7.  
How does she behave in the long run?  
Tell me, does our Geertje also bake,  
Using different pans?  
Madam, if this were so,  
Geertje would be expelled  
From my house at the very first step.

8.  
Again, madam, is this maid  
Properly trained  
In wool and in linen?  
My husband and I are very particular  
About people who are neat and tidy,  
But not about smudge goddesses.

9.  
Is she somewhat good-humored,  
Or does she, over a scrap or bargain,  
Also draw the knife too quickly?  
Is Geertje also from this breed,  
To know then from tick-tock, tick-tock,  
Or to be a chatterbox like Besje?

10.  
Does Geertje also run around too much,  
That is an accumulated pile  
Of night watchmen and nephews?  
Or old comrades of that sort,  
That ballast I threw overboard,  
Her passport was written.

11.  
Here I now expect a report,  
Whether in prose or in verse;  
If it pleases you best;  
But answer me well, complete, in good Zeelandic,  
And do not lure me with Hebrew,  
Or with a Jan van Leijen.

12.  
And if you can arrange it,  
Send Geertje to me tomorrow?  
I will let your master know in due time,  
But if it does not please you as then,  
I will give account of Geertje to  
Mietje the Housekeeper.

label: work
translation:
1.  
The dear rogue rightly strikes  
The wish of the Craftsman:  
Now laid down in the shade:  
How sweet then is my rest?  

2.  
Today it was terribly hot;  
Since I began,  
A stream of sweat ran down my face,  
As if there were a spring in my head.  

3.  
What the poor Man must not suffer,  
For those doing nothing,  
Whom we, despite an abundance of leisure,  
See rest wearily!  

4.  
I often thought, (then, I was wrong,  
Which I gladly admit,)  
Why am I not also so rich  
As such people are?  

5.  
But it occurred to me: God deemed it good.  
That you surely know;  
And the piece of bread tastes only sweet  
To those who rest after labor.  

6.  
With everything, after a short time,  
It is done on Earth,  
Then begins, for joyful eternity,  
The holy evening.  

7.  
There we are all equal again,  
There is no pressure nor cross,  
The work is finished, and poor and rich  
Go home to their reward.

label: work
translation:
Friends, listen all of you,  
What I am going to sing to you,  
About the sawmill workers here in Steê,  
How they know how to manage their mill:  
But the sailing all depends on the wind,  
In which they take great pleasure,  
Sawmill workers here in Steê,  
Keep your little mill on its course.  

2. Early in the morning we start the work:  
As soon as the wind begins to blow,  
We set our sails together,  
Let our little mill spin skillfully,  
Then we set our beam in place,  
We drink a nice cup of tea,  
Sawmill worker, tighten your saw correctly,  
And do your work with care.  

3. If a saw breaks down again,  
Take a wedge in your hands,  
Put it between the saws,  
So that they do not spoil the wood,  
Or they pull the saw somewhat out,  
They sing a little tune to conclude,  
Friends listen and hear it,  
How it must go with the sawmill workers.  

4. And if there is a small knot in the wood,  
You should quickly wedge it,  
So that you can work unhindered,  
And so that the wood breaks quickly,  
That’s how we do it with the hand bow,  
Sawmill workers, do not be lazy,  
Sawmill workers, secure your beam,  
Before cutting a warp or knot.  

5. And if there is a little gap,  
Let your planks shoot out freely,  
And lay your sled back carefully,  
Sawmill worker, do not let it upset you,  
Then they sharpen the saw slightly,  
So that it goes better through the wood,  
Porter takes away your planks,  
So you lay them properly on the stack.  

6. When the wind suddenly picks up,  
You should then ease half sail or high line,  
Just approve whatever is needed,  
Or how the wind may direct itself,  
Or one lays the sails along the rod,  
Sawmill worker, do not be afraid to ease,  
Sawmill worker, hold fast to your rope,  
So the little mill starts working again.  

7. Porter, if you have taken your planks away,  
Do you want to clear the sawdust from the frames,  
Loosen it so you can work continuously,  
It may be done accordingly,  
And put a brush on the edge,  
And then clean the sand thoroughly,  
And cut well the block,  
It is six and a half inches in the miller's pants.  

8. Friends, listen all,  
How the sawmill workers growl,  
When they are in the mill,  
They hear their saws or spikers rumble,  
And then they pull out the peg,  
And they growl as if half mad.  
They cut, they cut the spikers out,  
They say the work is spoiled after all.  

9. If we have a quiet day,  
We have to lay wood or sawdust,  
Because we always have the load,  
The millers know how to turn their boys,  
Now with one, and now with the other,  
Oh! Those millers are so strange,  
You millers sharpen your saws properly,  
So you can rest once in a while by the way.

label: work
translation:
There is the laundry, what now to do?  
And my hands are full;  
Shall I leave everything as it is now?  
My head is spinning;  
I am really angry at the Bleekers woman.  
Right in the middle of the week,  
She sends the laundry on me,  
It’s a wasted trick.  
Come on, cheerfully, sleeves rolled up,  
And off to the starcher!  
Yes! yes! I know that, Miss!  
Don’t tire my head;  
Well, human! how are you so rude?  
Who do you think you are?  
I have no desire for such reception,  
That cannot be allowed.  
Am I rude? I am not;  
Look, Miss! honestly;  
It is a great sorrow to me,  
That you growl and puff like that:  
You see I am not one to be silent,  
I do all that I can;  
And then that grumbling and nagging,  
I’ve had enough of it.  
Now come sit by me, girl!  
And listen to me;  
Thank you, Miss, I told you,  
I’m going straight to Mary:  
So bickering and talking like that, now!  
That would be a fine joke!  
I bet I’d fall asleep so quickly,  
When I go to bed in the evening.  
And even with that noise,  
One still does not hear well;  
My head is pounding too much now,  
I am disturbed in my mind.  
Because I must not see men laugh like that,  
And then be sad again;  
I always say: those who neglect their duties,  
Live in a sorrowful state.  
Marytje! are you my child?  
Yes, well, what is it, Wie?  
The laundry is here! come quickly,  
Go with me, one two three!  
Hey! is that running, I am so hot:  
My Miss does not keep quiet:  
I have already argued so much that I sweat:  
Do you also enjoy the chore?  
Arguing again, well the daw!  
You amuse yourself sweetly:  
Your head also often looks so messed up,  
Look, Wie! that is not good:  
Now you’re meek, like a lamb,  
Soon again a bunch of straw,  
A spark in it, it’s fire and flame,  
Say girl! isn’t it so?  
Yes I give you the beaten child,  
Right and wrong;  
Now, I’m running home like the wind,  
Because if I stand and look,  
My work will not get done:  
And I have no one who does it,  
Well what! I leave it on you,  
Till later, be greeted.

label: work
translation:
Gentlemen and Ladies, chimney sweeping  
I have been used to from a young age,  
If it’s a bit dirty, no problem,  
Money makes me clean and excellent,  
I wash myself, I pee, I drink once, I toast once,  
Thus I live steadily in joy,  
If I find a girl after my angel,  
Then I am green, then I am black.  
I give kiss after kiss, I live without sorrow,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart.  
Recently I came to a nice girl,  
Where I was supposed to sweep the chimney,  
I gave a kiss on her fair flesh,  
It made her dirty, she gave me a thrashing,  
She hit me, and chased me, and laid me down,  
A broomstick on my loins,  
But when I came to her on Sunday,  
Nicely dressed, she was like a lamb,  
Then I turned green, away was the black,  
I gave kiss after kiss, I live without sorrow,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart.  
Ladies, do you want to make use of me,  
Whether in black or in green,  
I live in the Man with seven wigs,  
Above my door lives Lijntje Loen,  
There I eat, there I feast, there I drink, there I toast,  
So I live steadily without worry,  
If you want to serve me,  
Black or green, just come to me,  
Whether in green, whether in black,  
I give kiss after kiss, I live without sorrow,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart.  
Is there then someone very embarrassed,  
Who finds his chimney too dirty,  
I will sweep it perfectly and nicely,  
If I don’t, then I am an owl,  
With pulling and stretching and hitting with balls,  
I will make it thoroughly clean,  
Who wants to serve me,  
In black or green, just come to me,  
I work in green, I work in black.  
I give kiss after kiss, I live without sorrow,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart,  
I drink once, I wash the black from my heart.

label: work
translation:
I have long been carrying peat,  
I also sometimes drink with a friend  
Yet I have never dared  
To drink more than suits me.  
Put it down, lift it up, put it down, keep working,  
Want to quickly empty the baskets,  
How heavy that carrying and lifting feels,  
In such hot days; but  
No one cares, oh what sorrow,  
When they see us occasionally drunk.  
No one cares, oh what sorrow.  

Some already come slightly intoxicated  
To the market, early in the day,  
Eventually become terribly drunk,  
Breaking glasses one after another,  
Put it down, lift it up, put it down, keep working,  
Want to quickly empty the baskets,  
While they stand high by the window,  
In great danger of falling down;  
No one cares, oh what sorrow,  
When they see them rarely sober.            (repeat)  

But the good sober people  
Among us are well regarded;  
Because since they avoid the taverns,  
Their duty is all the more respected,  
Put it down, lift it up, put it down, keep working,  
Want to quickly empty the months,  
No one cares, oh what sorrow,  
When they are rarely seen sober,  
Then, when the work is done,  
One may well have a little drink.            (repeat)

label: work
translation:
Friends all listen,
To what I am going to sing to you,
About the sawmillers here at Steê,
How they know how to control their mill,
But it all depends on the wind,
Since they find great pleasure in it,
Saw miller here at Steê,
Keep your mill on its course.
So that it can run unhindered,
And that the wood may quickly break,
That is how we do it with the hand saw,
Saw millers and you are not lazy,
Miller, fasten your beam firmly,
Before sawing a knot or branch.
When they are in the mill,
They hear their saws or nails humming,
And then they pull off the peg,
And they growl like half mad,
They chop, they chop the nails out,
They say the work is indeed spoiled.
