label: work
translation:
I must sit sewing steadily,  
Early in the morning and late in the evening,  
I would keep walking around constantly,  
Like others along the street,  
I would then have to lay down my work,  
No, this caused me sorrow,  
Because my customers would say:  
Such a seamstress does not serve us.  
Why should the work bore me,  
Since I earn my living with it alone.  
A little bit of money is not pleasant,  
That I earn through my diligence,  
It is worth more to me than twenty,  
Since I had to face the eyes for it.

label: work
translation:
1.
How happy can the farmer live  
When he looks at his land and people  
And can give each their due,  
And then trusts in God,  
The farmer who sows and tills the land,  
And awaits the blessing from God's hand.

2.
When they see the morning clear,  
Each begins with renewed strength,  
Threshing to gather the grain,  
And when the winter is over,  
He drives the animals into the fields,  
The farmer praises and thanks God.

3.
How could the baker work,  
The dear bread that people gladly eat,  
If God did not come to strengthen the farmer,  
So that he makes his land ready,  
How could one live in the village,  
If the farmer did not do the work?

4.
How could the seaman live,  
If the farmer did not do the work,  
It is true God must give the growth,  
And make him strong through goodness,  
For that he shows his gratitude,  
After his accomplished labor.

5.
When you are seated at the table,  
Tap a toast on the table,  
Then look at the dear food,  
It all comes from the farmer:  
So man, as often as you eat,  
Do not forget the farming class.

6.
It is true the seaman brings many goods,  
From various lands;  
But if there were no farmers,  
How would they fare with it?  
It all comes from the farmer’s hand,  
But God is master of the land.

label: work
translation:
1.  
The dear scoundrel strikes justly  
The wish of the Craftsman:  
And laid me down in the shade:  
How sweet is rest to me then!  

2.  
It was then so terribly hot  
From the moment I began  
A stream of sweat ran down my face,  
As if a spring were in my head.  

3.  
What the poor man must not suffer,  
Because of that idle lien,  
Whom we see rest in excess,  
While weary!  

4.  
I often thought, (then, I was wrong,  
Which I gladly admit,)  
Why am I not also as rich  
As such people?  

5.  
But it came to me: God deemed it good,  
That is surely conscious to you;  
And the piece of bread only tastes sweet  
To those who rest after labor.  

6.  
After a short time,  
Everything on earth is done,  
Then, to joyful eternity,  
A holy evening begins.  

7.  
There we are all equal again,  
There is no pressure nor hardship known,  
The work is finished and poor and rich  
Go home for their wages.

label: work
translation:
1.  
How joyfully can the Farmer live,  
When he looks upon his land and people,  
And can give to each what is theirs,  
And then trusts in God,  
The Farmer who sows and mows the land,  
And awaits the blessing from God's hand.

2.  
When he sees the morning clear,  
Everyone begins with new strength,  
Threshing the grain to gather it,  
And when winter is done,  
He drives the animals to the land,  
The Farmer praises and thanks God.

3.  
How could the Baker work,  
The dear bread that people gladly eat,  
If God did not strengthen the Farmer  
So that he makes his land ready,  
How would people live in the town,  
If the Farmer did not do the work.

4.  
How could the Seaman live,  
If the Farmer did not do the work,  
It is true, God must give the harvest,  
And made him strong through goodness.  
Therefore he shows his gratitude  
For his labor done.

5.  
If you are seated at a table,  
Take a look at your plate,  
Then consider the dear food,  
It all comes from the Farmer,  
So, person, as often as you eat,  
Do not forget the farming class.

6.  
It is true the Seaman brings many goods,  
From all kinds of countries,  
But if there were no Farmers,  
How would they stand there too?  
It all comes from the Farmer’s hand.  
But God is master of the land.

label: work
translation:
The dear toil hour strikes, justly  
The wish of the craftsman:  
Now laid down in the shade,  
How sweet the rest tastes to me!  
It was terribly hot today as well,  
From the moment I began,  
A stream of sweat ran down my face,  
As if there were a spring in my head.  
There we are all equal again,  
No pressure or burden is known there,  
The work is done, and rich and poor  
Go home for their wages.

label: work
translation:
You who take pleasure in flowers,
Enter my flower garden,
I have material for every wish,
And may boast of a selection of beauties.
On this well-planted ground,
One will celebrate their choice freely,
One will adorn the path of life
With flowers here below.

I give the fine touch-me-not herb,
These golden buds to the miser,
These blue bells to the fool,
Narcissi to those who want to shine beautifully.
The poplar to the talkative,
The sleep-bol to the slow,
The Judas money to the usurer,
The sunflower to those who ask for favors.

To heroes I give these laurels,
This white lily to virtue,
These thousand beauties to youth,
And the oak will adorn the heads of nobles.
The willows I give to diligence,
This blooming aloe to the gray-haired,
The sharp maple to envy,
The passion flower I give to the wise.
And to longing sentimentalists,
I give the maiden in the green,
Those who harbor amorous wishes,
I will grant with laurel and myrrh.
My beloved will always,
The longer, the more dearly captivate me,
For her I keep a rose,
On whose stem thorns grow.

label: work
translation:
Come, quickly out of bed! The rooster has crowed,  
Already the birds are singing, the morning air is true,  
I am going to the field, working the ground.  
Working is useful and keeps us healthy.  

Soon I will start sowing, I sow all kinds,  
Of plants and herbs, as needed, as is proper;  
I sow carrots and turnips, salad and radishes,  
I sow beans and peas, of all kinds.  

And when the harvest time comes, then I gather in,  
Then my labor gives me a double gain;  
Then I take my fruits from the farmland,  
And thank my Creator for what He has given me.

label: work
translation:
When my husband, early in the morning,  
Hurries to the field,  
I go to my cows,  
With a joyful spirit.

And if he takes vegetables to the market,  
Or the fattened pig,  
I bring cheese and butter,  
Which are also indispensable.

That is why I sing as well as he:  
Hail to the farming class!  
He provides us with food in abundance,  
The prosperity of the Fatherland.

label: work
translation:
I just polish the shoes smooth  
And this provides me with bread;  
Contentment is the greatest treasure,  
That any person has ever enjoyed!  
I do not yearn for earthly greatness:  
Money is the source of much sorrow.  

Oh, for a little that is offered,  
I serve every Master.  
Do not forget me, poor boy;  
That I polish your shoes:  
Then I carry my fate contentedly,  
And praise my Creator and my God.

label: work
translation:
Buy sulfur matches!  
I don't have many in my basket:  
For a few cents  
I offer them to you.  
It is a careless man  
Who dares to do without them;  
At night and in case of emergency  
They are often needed nonetheless.  

Buy sulfur matches!  
I have few in my basket;  
For a few cents  
I offer them to you.

label: work
translation:
I am a lackey or valets,
And serve my master faithfully;
I am attached to the good man,
And I respect his good wife.

label: work
work
translation:
Friends, come! Enter with me;  
I consider myself honored by your arrival;  
Those who continue to cherish company,  
And desire rest after work,  
Will here find to their liking,  
After labor follows rest.  

Come inside, choose a spot,  
By the stove, or by the fire,  
Smoke a pipe, and drink a glass  
Of my strong, tasty beer.  
Outside it is cold and bleak;  
Talk and chat a bit with your neighbor.

label: work
translation:
Buy, young ladies, buy, my gentlemen!  
Delicious apples, sweet pears!  
Buy! they melt in the mouth; } refrain.  
Fill your belly full and round! } refrain.  

Do you want little vegetables or gentlemen,  
Plums that delight the heart,  
Grapes, like sunshine, } refrain.  
Pure juice, and pure wine! } refrain.  

Peaches and apricots,  
Look how they blush invitingly,  
Fragrant, all the sweetest kiss } refrain.  
From Anna, the dear sister. } refrain.  

Yes, so a pimp made himself heard,  
I almost smothered a laugh;  
But pardon! it was well said: } refrain.  
Naâ is after all a sweet girl. } refrain.  

Buy cherries, cantaloupes!  
Everything prime! who lets it slip;  
Yes, so tender, so beautiful, so fresh, } refrain.  
No fruit shines on the King's dish. } refrain.  

Daily I must go trotting,  
Toiling, slaving with my baskets,  
For a small and pure profit } refrain.  
For my numerous household. } refrain.  

But my courage will not waver:  
Klaas also helps by polishing shoes.  
Thus, beneath the hardship } refrain.  
The reward of both honest sweats is gained. } refrain.  

But in the evening, when we rest,  
Nothing satisfies our desires either,  
We sit at the modest meal } refrain.  
Without remorse, without ailment. } refrain.

label: work
translation:
Oh poor woman, where shall I go!  
My work is done,  
Already before autumn, how full of weeping,  
Winter comes with harsh cries,  
Misery is coming. (repeat)  

My children, whom I have begotten,  
I cannot feed with bread,  
When clammy sweat covers their heads,  
They lie stretched out on their beds,  
What shall I do for them? (repeat)  

Oh man, do not be so anxious,  
Think of Him from above,  
He does not cast people into sorrow;  
That God, who rules the Universe,  
Also fixes His gaze on us. (repeat)  

God is faithful in all His actions,  
He grants strength after the cross,  
He will also feed your children,  
And help you in all your deeds,  
Are you really too weary? (repeat)  

Come, let us entrust it to Him,  
Who always orders everything best,  
According to His wisdom, power, and might;  
Also listen respectfully to His voice,  
He, who counts our days. (repeat)  

O great God of the Universe,  
Let us obey Your law,  
You who will save us in distress,  
Fulfill also what we need,  
Set poverty on stakes. (repeat)

label: work
translation:
O Woman, come now,  
Sing a light French song;  
Come have a dance with me,  
The dough lies in the trough,  
Help to weigh it then, } (repeat)  
To help your man, } (repeat)  
Then you can sweep the floors, } (repeat)  
Meanwhile build little Jan. } (repeat)  

Well man, what then,  
If I have weighed it?  
Why do you give me the look  
Against your wife;  
Or have I done something, } (repeat)  
That does not suit you, } (repeat)  
Now don’t make a fuss, } (repeat)  
Oh! How angry you are. } (repeat)  

Go away, go away,  
You interfere with my affairs;  
Can’t you make it easier,  
You weigh it far too heavy,  
I thought that in the oven, } (repeat)  
It would get lighter by the fire, } (repeat)  
You must not believe that, } (repeat)  
Even if Klaas neighbor says so. } (repeat)  

I will then,  
Squeeze my dough a little,  
If only you can understand,  
Just half an ounce,  
The yeast will make it rise, } (repeat)  
This is only for show, } (repeat)  
Who will even notice, } (repeat)  
If I also deceive them. } (repeat)  

Now woman, I want to  
Always have you weigh it;  
It is a hidden blessing,  
It gives profit in my pocket,  
But if the weighers come, } (repeat)  
Are we then not in danger, } (repeat)  
Don’t be ashamed of that } (repeat)  
That is all clear. } (repeat)

label: work
translation:
What gives steady work for me; the needle,  
What is used most by me; the needle!  
The needle that pricks, that stings, that glides;  
And what pleases the girl the most?  
The needle! the needle! the needle!

What can possibly be worse than the needle!  
How many people do not live from it, the needle!  
The needle that is hard to do without!  
In the household; certainly the woman!  
Beloved the needle! the needle!

What sews the most together, the needle!  
What does the helmsman observe the most, the needle!  
The compass that turns on the needle,  
By which the helmsman steers the ship,  
It is the needle! the needle!

A bookbinder also works with the needle!  
A saddler also pierces with it, the needle!  
The sailmaker certainly sews,  
The cloths together, no mistake:  
With his needle! the needle!

What does a corset maker work with, the needle,  
On a corset, neat and beautiful, the needle,  
Gives shape to the youthful body;  
He also always holds his needle firm.  
Oh needle! oh needle! oh needle!

Who can put things right in their place, the needle!  
With a fixed stitch, and firm then, oh needle!  
How indispensably great you are to us,  
Who is not a partaker  
Of the needle! of the needle! of the needle!

How many girls did not grieve, oh needle!  
When they missed it, what sorrow, oh needle!  
The needle is a charming thing,  
The needle brings much change;  
Long live the needle! the needle!

label: work
translation:
I rattle steadily by you this night,  
I also keep watch regularly, regularly:  
And then call out, and call out, ten strikes the clock,  
And then call out, and call out, ten strikes the clock,  

Make sure you are off the street,  
And go to your bed,  
Since the hour of rest  
Has begun for us.  
And then you sleep, I keep watch,  
For peaceful rest,  
For water, for fire,  
That it is extinguished.  

Endure also the cold, the rain, the snow,  
I am on the lookout like a young lion,  
For thieves and poachers I always keep watch:  
So that nothing can harm you, you rest sweetly and softly.  

A rattle watch is indeed a slavish job,  
One walks steadily along streets, along alleys, along sidewalks,  
Our view is then the reward, isn’t it?  
You do give a fair,  
A New Year’s;  
That then creates courage again  
To diligently carry out  
The work so that no one  
Accuses us of laziness.  

So now sleep peacefully  
In this dark night.  
I then keep regular  
Watch for you too,  
And call out, at my time,  
Ten strikes the clock,  
So crawl in calmly;  
But inside your nook!

label: work
translation:
I have long carried turf,  
I work from the break of day,  
And I still take great pleasure  
When I tuck my head into the cap.  
Shake on, give up, want to quickly empty the basket,  
But when I then lift the sack  
And bring it where my master wants,  
They don't notice, they don't notice,  
When they see us carry like this. (repeat)

Sweat runs from top to toe,  
When a hot day meets me,  
In the evening my legs nod,  
As I hurry home.  
Shake on, give up, want to quickly empty the basket,  
I quickly bring it back upstairs,  
I never get tired of carrying;  
They don't notice, they don't notice,  
When they see us carry like this. (repeat)

Now a glass poured,  
That gives new strength,  
And once cheerfully drunk,  
Set the bottle aside now;  
Shake on, give up, want to quickly empty the basket.  
Come Jaap, cheerfully move forward again,  
That way the turf comes quickly out of the boat,  
Then you get money, then you get money,  
Which will soon be counted for us. (repeat)

label: work
translation:
I am indeed a sailmaker,  
Always hustle happily hop sa sa,  
A sailmaker well known,  
Because all skippers are accustomed,  
To my sewing and to my cloth;  
That I always handle boldly,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la.  
But nowadays it’s la la,  
Always hustle, happily hop sa sa,  
Before I could work early and late,  
But now the steamboat causes me grief,  
And then the company appeared,  
Put sailmaking aside,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la.

Still always cheerful Gerrit, yes  
Always hustle, happily, hop sa sa.  
No courage lost, not oppressed;  
Even if Gerrit stood in his shirt.  
I still have courage, am young and strong:  
And continually still do my work,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la.

The tide can turn, oh yes  
Always happily, hop sa sa  
If I meet fortune once,  
Then I’ll gather new courage again,  
Then with greater diligence anew,  
The work that was assigned to me,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la.

I see Krijn coming there, yes,  
Always hustle, happily hop sa sa,  
Well, Krijn, you bring me some work now,  
You know I make it good and strong,  
This sail needs to be shortened,  
The gaff secured tighter,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la.  
I have work again, now I am, yes,  
Always happy, happily, hop sa sa,  
Happy again that I can work;  
For Krijn he is a good man.  
When I have finished my work,  
Then I can go after my pennies,  
Tra la la, tra la la, tra la la, tra la la!

label: work
translation:
You who take pleasure in flowers,  
Enter my flower garden,  
I have material for all wishes,  
And may boast of the choice of beauty;  
On this well-planted ground  
One can freely display their choice,  
One must adorn  
The path of life below with flowers.  
I gift the delicate "Touch-me-not" herb  
These bluebells to the fool,  
These golden buttons to the miser;  
Daffodils to the one who wishes to shine,  
The sharp maple to the envious,  
Sleep bulbs I give to the lazy,  
These willow branches to the diligent,  
The sunflower to those who seek favors.  
To the hero I give these laurels,  
These thousands-beauties to the youth,  
This white lily to virtue,  
And the oak will adorn the head of the citizens;  
The poplar to the chatterbox,  
The blooming aloe to the grey-haired,  
This Judas money to the usurer,  
That passionflower I give to the wise.  
To longing sentinels,  
I give the "Lady in Green";  
Those who nurture lost wishes  
I will bestow with myrtle and rose;  
My beloved object will always be  
The dearer and longer,  
To her I have dedicated a rose  
To which no thorns need be feared.  
To our good and dear king,  
All that is beautiful is offered;  
To his eldest brave son,  
I stretch palm and laurel as reward;  
Golden rain to our country,  
The olive to all powers,  
But every vile tyrant  
Should be content with devil's bread.

label: work
translation:
Madam, I come here to sell,  
All those kinds of Savonet,  
Even if I have to walk with a crutch,  
I don't even mind that;  
I have many kinds of waters,  
To wash the faces,  
I have beautiful Savonette,  
I have beautiful Savonet.  
Would you like orange water?  
Surely I have good ones,  
Or would you like German water?  
That one is clear as the sun;  
It is made in Paris,  
It fools all the girls,  
But even more my Savonette,  
But even more my Savonet.  
I have the queen’s water,  
Also Pomade for the ladies,  
To get a young son,  
I still have some in my stall;  
And that is so delicate  
It has no harm at all,  
And that is the Savonette,  
And that is the Savonet.  
Who wants my little mustaches,  
To put on the chin,  
Take that little box and open it,  
All the mustaches are inside,  
But you take nothing at all,  
That’s good—what would you like?  
But I swear on my Savonette,  
It is of that Savonet.  
There you have them all,  
The ones I have in my stall,  
I only have two of these halls,  
There you have them now, Madam,  
But I don’t use them too much,  
It causes sourness in my throat,  
Because it is the Savonette,  
Because it is the Savonet.

label: work
translation:
When I go out to sharpen scissors  
when I go out I call out loudly  
scissors scissors sharpen they come out  
can you understand this art  
here and there I go once again  
to the scissor sharpener fears no danger  
My little dog that makes me happy  
my little dog even if he is small  
he quietly walks towards a fountain  
where he drinks deeply  
then he wags his little tail happily  
he comes to rest at my side  
When it is noon I head to the pasture  
when it is noon hear what I am going to do  
I am going to rest in the youthful green  
there I will linger a bit  
I have meat and bread for hunger  
I set the table on my lap.  
The little morsels that roll from my small table  
the little morsels from my small table  
then he gets some meat as well  
then he still knows how to play  
hey hey cadé always too free  
how much fun I have with that.  
I play a tune on my lyre  
I play a tune with good spirit  
purring purring how well it goes  
on my little lyre  
come here gather it is melody  
all who want to sharpen scissors with me.

label: work
translation:
How cheerful can the Farmer not live,  
When he considers his land and people,  
And can give each his share,  
And that he then trusts in God,  
The Farmer who sows and mows the land,  
And waits for the blessing from God's hand.  
When he sees the morning clear,  
Everyone begins anew with fresh strength,  
Threshing and gathering the grain,  
And when winter is done,  
He drives the animals into the land,  
The Farmer gives praise and thanks to God.  
How could the Baker work,  
The dear bread that people gladly eat,  
If God did not come to strengthen the Farmer,  
So that he readies his land,  
How would one live in the village,  
If the Farmer did not do the work.  
How could the Sailor live,  
If the Farmer did not do the work,  
It is true, God must give the growth,  
And make him strong through goodness.  
For that he shows his gratitude,  
After his completed labor.  
When you sit at a table,  
Take a turn at your table,  
Then look at the dear food,  
It all comes from the Farmer;  
So, human, as often as you eat,  
Never forget the Farming class.  
It is true the Sailor brings many goods,  
From all kinds of countries,  
But if there were no Farmers,  
How would they exist there also,  
It all comes from the Farmer’s hand,  
But God is master of the land.

label: work
translation:
We farmers, men and women  
We work day and night,  
We plow and we spin,  
And we sing with all our might:  
Give, dear Lord, us food and clothes,  
The Kingdom of Heaven and then no more.  
We rarely set the table;  
A piece from the hand  
Tastes to us like a waffle,  
And we sing along the way:  
Give, dear Lord, etc.

label: christmas
translation:
The three kings came traveling from afar,  
All to bring their offerings,  
They saw a bright star in the East  
That led them to the lands of Bethlehem:  
But they lost it in Jerusalem,  
So they asked with a sorrowful voice:  
Where is born here that worthy child?  
King of the Jews who loves our souls as well as honor.

2. Herod was amazed and astonished,  
When he heard about this King,  
He was troubled and immediately hastened  
To summon the scripture scholars and priests,  
They quickly found in the prophecies  
That this King had already been born,  
And that in the stable of Bethlehem  
Herod immediately called the kings to him.

3. Go seek him in the stable of Bethlehem:  
But when you have found the child  
And have made your offering to him:  
Return to me and inform me of the place,  
So that I too, like you, will soon  
Go worship the child with humility.  
Thus the three kings parted from him,  
But they did not return to Jerusalem.

4. When they came outside Jerusalem,  
They saw the bright star again,  
It stood still over the stable of Bethlehem;  
Their hearts leapt together with joy,  
They entered the humble stable  
Where they found the greatest God of all  
Laid on hay and straw in a manger,  
They fell down before his high majesty.

5. They worshipped the child with great humility:  
For their hearts burned with love,  
They threw their crowns before the sweet child  
And with great respect made their offerings,  
They opened their boxes full of treasures,  
Offered myrrh, incense, and gold:  
Said, be welcome, you who love our salvation.

6. After the offerings, the child  
Gave them his blessing  
With his generous little hand,  
He looked at them, their hearts began to revive,  
Then he turned and grew thirsty,  
The mother pressed him to her dear breast,  
Seeing the kings the suckling child,  
They all joyfully exclaimed: see our King drinks.

7. The kings thanked the child greatly,  
Who thus gave them his blessing in return,  
The mother and Joseph, the guardian,  
Then they went their ways again  
Full of sweet heavenly consolation,  
A beautiful angel came to meet them,  
Who had shown them another path,  
With joy they each returned to their kingdoms.

label: christmas
translation:
I.  
What drives your journey after these corners,  
Say, Wise Men, over land and sea?  
Whom do you diligently seek here?  
Whom do you bring these gifts to?  

II.  
A Star with unusual rays  
Leads us, as guide, on this path,  
To Judah's King, after the story  
Born in Prince David's city.  

III.  
What is the use of this gold and myrrh-chest,  
With which you are so laden?  
What is the use of that incense, which as an offering  
Is dedicated to worship?  

IV.  
We bring Him this Gold as King:  
The incense, as a sign of worship,  
As God: the myrrh as a symbol  
Of the embalming anointing of His body.  

V.  
If I may meet the King.  
Yes, I still have something: that is my heart.  

VI.  
Would that be given to the King,  
So empty? In such poor condition?  
He will flaunt it with his own gifts:  
He accepts the will for the deed.  

VII.  
He will sell me Ophir's gold:  
And consecrate me with His offering's fragrance:  
The myrrh, dropped from His mouth,  
Will cause me to perceive favor.  

VIII.  
I will value the King's love more  
Than gold: and I will adorn my bosom  
With that myrrh bundle: I will honor Him  
With the incense-offering of prayer.  

IX.  
Come, soul, then go freely with us  
To Jesus' stable in Bethlehem:  
You will not waste effort in vain,  
But without doubt you will find Him.

label: christmas
translation:
I.  
In Bethlehem’s stable,  
When the three kings arrived,  
And according to custom offered their gifts  
To the Lord of All,  
They saw the sweet little child  
Suckle the virgin’s breast,  
All called out with joy:  
Behold the King drinks, long live.  

2. It is an old custom,  
That one goes to the king to pull,  
Pour, drink, and lick,  
And make good cheer,  
In the evening at the feast,  
Of the Three Kings’ day,  
So that everyone also happily in spirit  
Makes the best good laughter.  

3. There is one whom the king appoints,  
One councilor, one cupbearer,  
One fool, or jester, or stinker,  
Who also pleasantly drinks.  
Each one does their best  
To well play their part.  
When the king drinks, the rest  
Shout, the king drinks very happily.  

4. See what this means to us?  
It means human life,  
Everyone is given a part,  
Each also who falls here into something:  
One falls here a king,  
One a farmer, or beggar,  
Another a councilor, another monk,  
Merchant, craftsman, nobleman.  

5. A comedy, and a play  
Is here the whole of human life,  
Everyone is given a part.  
Who plays their part well,  
Whether the farmer or the king,  
Whether the master or the servant,  
Whether the priest or a monk,  
Receives at last their rightful reward.  

6. When the game has ended,  
When the game of life is over,  
He who was here very highly raised,  
Hereafter gains no advantage,  
Unless he has played  
His part well, small or great.  
All human equality  
Death makes alike.  

7. Farmer’s straw and the king’s crown,  
Farmer’s huts, the king’s courts,  
Death knocks on all unrelentingly,  
And brings everyone their reward.  
All kings of the world  
Are kings of a short kingdom,  
Whether mortal or limited,  
It is the same as the Three Kings’ day.

label: christmas
translation:
I.  
The Kings from the East  
Came into the stable  
To Jesus who comforts them  
Saw their misfortune.  
The sweet little Child thirsted,  
He threw himself to the breast,  
The Kings, that sounds,  
Shouted, the King drinks.  

2. The worldly people  
Refresh in this manner;  
But all who wish for Jesus  
Call out with pleasure,  
They call in spirit  
On this joyful feast:  
Behold our sweet King,  
Who drinks in our mind.  

3. See Jesus wants to give us  
His holy Flesh and Blood,  
So that we may drink Him,  
And He in our mind.  
Drink Jesus day and night,  
My youth and all my strength,  
I am prepared for you,  
Do with me as you please.  

4. You have given yourself to me entirely  
In the Holy Sacrament  
As nourishment for my life,  
You are fully impressed in me.  
Sweet King, give, give.  
And that my soul may drink you,  
So we call, that sounds:  
Long live the King drinks.  

5. The King gives to us today  
In the Holy Sacrament,  
His Blood and human limbs,  
And fully condescends to us.  
Long live, the King gives  
Long live, the man drinks him  
Long live, he gives his blood,  
And drinks in our mind.

label: christmas
translation:
You wise Kings, do you come traveling,
At the sight of a star;
And had you not pondered a thousand times,
The land of Judah is so far,
Could you afford to lose your sweet desire?
And go wandering,
To a foreign coast?
In cold and great unrest.

And had you no sorrowful thoughts?
When the star first left you?
How dared you watch Herod?
And did you not know his cruelty?
To seek a King,
To the rightful Lord,
Is to risk life,
The fight too fierce,
Against a Tyrant’s honor.

No, as soon as God’s light came to shine upon us,
We hurried to set out on the journey;
Who follows God’s call,
And cannot fail,
It is always peace and calm,
Should we fear in Jerusalem!
The Scribes lead us to Bethlehem,
For God’s word has a voice.

Oh, were you not completely overwhelmed,
When the Star stood above the Little Stable,
And that you in wind and Northern blasts,
Found a newborn child,
Could you bow to that tender child,
That you softly suckle,
Did you honor Him:
As your God and Lord.

No human tongue can express,
The joy that burst from our hearts,
Through all the pure signs of Love,
And what shone from the Divine being,
He gave us gifts,
Said the great Lord,
Behold your servants,
It is all our desire,
To die for your honor.

label: christmas
translation:
Make place, O little shepherds, come out of the stable,  
Behold from the East this great multitude,  
It is already walking to Bethlehem, already to the stable.  
Behold the three Kings with great numbers,  
The star on the manger remains still standing,  
And they are not ashamed to enter,  
Welcome, welcome, welcome all,  
Welcome, welcome into this stable,  
Extend offering hands and let incense burn for the sweet little child,  
It will reward you with the eternal crowns for the temporal goods,  
Welcome Kings, welcome all, welcome, welcome into this stable.  
They throw their crowns before the manger,  
They kiss the little feet of the child,  
And say welcome in the little stable,  
The little child who smiles at them,  
See how he grabs into the golden bowl,  
See how diligently his little eyes gaze,  
Welcome Kings, welcome all,  
Welcome, welcome into this stable:  
Extend offering hands and let incense be burned for the sweet little child,  
It will reward you with the eternal crowns for the temporal goods:  
Welcome Kings, welcome all, welcome, welcome into this stable.  
After the offering hand the little child looks,  
Gives his blessing to the Kings,  
With his blessed little hands,  
Afterwards he turns around, and he got thirsty,  
He throws himself onto his Mother’s breast,  
Draws a draught out for his thirst;  
The Kings seeing the suckling child,  
They call out the King, the King who drinks,  
The stable is full of joys, everyone sings, the King who drinks,  
Would you also rejoice, and sing with joy, the King who drinks,  
The King who drinks, the King who drinks, the King who drinks.

label: christmas
translation:
What do I see over there in the distance  
That shines so brightly,  
It seems like a thousand bright stars,  
I am very frightened,  
Oh, oh, I fear for our herds.  
Over there stands by me,  
The cattle of you and me,  
That we shall go to tend  
In another way.  
O Coridon, friend chosen  
On this night,  
The Messiah clearly has been born,  
Long awaited by us.  
I have heard in the first repose  
That you want to worship him,  
Little shepherds, let your sheep  
Hear a sweet angelic voice,  
Move towards Bethlehem.  
We took our milk pails  
First of all,  
And hurried together  
To the poor stable,  
Where we found  
The High Majesty,  
Laid in a manger,  
Wrapped in tender cloths  
For our salvation.  
The little angels sang together  
There Gloria,  
And also the little shepherds jumped for joy,  
All victorious,  
Coridon took his flute and the others  
Called, sa, sa, sa,  
Little shepherds hey viva,  
Let us now together  
Sing na, na, na.  
Three kings from far lands  
With all humility,  
Who came to bring their offerings  
To the sweet child,  
Mary was in thought  
At this very moment,  
She quickly thought of Herod  
Who would harm her child,  
Being angry about it.  
At first came Jasper and Melchior  
And Balthazar,  
Embraced the sweet child with joy –  
One after the other,  
They laid before their offering hands  
Myrrh, incense, gold,  
The child many faults  
Set aflame in love  
That were first described.  
The greatest princes of the world,  
With joyful sound,  
Came richly dressed, limited  
To the poor stable,  
But also they knew beforehand  
The reason and the outcome,  
That in this lowly place,  
Today was born  
Both man and God.  
The sweet child began to thirst  
With great desire,  
He cast himself upon his mother's dear breasts,  
And fulfilled his pleasure,  
The little shepherds, all without blame,  
Merrily celebrated so that it resounded,  
Their hearts leapt with joy,  
And all together cried out  
Our King drinks.  
Now shout viva for the young born  
The child Jesus:  
And give to the sweet chosen child,  
A peace kiss,  
Be welcome, o great valiant one,  
Prince of Israel,  
Greetings Emmanuel,  
Who comes to break the bonds  
Of eternal hell.

label: christmas
translation:
Truly a child full of holiness!  
Your royal might  
I present here with gratitude  
My offering hands, as those who lend  
Soul and body to you;  
For I believe with a pure heart  
That you are God, and man,  
And have brought all the world to desire.  
Receive my offering hands,  
And believe that in your hands  
You hold all things in power,  
As God does in the Trinity.

label: christmas
translation:
Greetings full of holiness!  
Your royal powers  
I hereby present with sincerity  
My offering hands, as those willing  
To lend my soul and body to you;  
For I believe with a pure heart  
That you are God, and man,  
And have brought all the world into existence.  
Accept my offering hands,  
And believe that you hold in your hands  
All things in power,  
As God does in the Trinity.

label: christmas
translation:
We come stepped with our star,  
Laureate the Cransio,  
We seek Lord Jesus, we would have him gladly,  
Laureate the knier  
His Charles' royal children,  
Father good Franselijn,  
Jeremiah.

label: christmas
translation:
We are three kings rich in power,  
and we go searching day and night  
all over mountain and valley,  
to find what we knew of,  
right over mountain and valley,  
to find the God of all.  
Jasper, Melchior, Balthazar  
came to this little child there;  
they knelt with humility,  
offered gifts, burned incense,  
they knelt with humility  
before this little sweet Jesus child.  
Through the stable that was full of peace,  
the little child and the little animals too.  
Then they shout so it resounds:  
vivat, vivat, vivat!  
Then they shout so it resounds:  
vivat, vivat! our king drinks!

label: christmas
translation:
Three kings great in power  
traveling by day and night  
through mountains and forest and valley,  
to search in the corners  
through mountains and forest and valley,  
to find the Lord of all.  
Gaspar, Melchior, Balthazar,  
three kings all eager,  
they all went with humility  
offering gifts, burning incense,  
they all went with humility  
with offerings for the sweet child.  
Sweet child, do you know,  
in this bitter winter cold  
who stands before the door?  
It is three kings with rewards.  
Who stands before the door?  
Say, let them come inside.  
Come inside, come in!  
It goes well according to the little Jesus’ will.  
The little Jesus who laughs and grimaces.  
See that little lamb, it eats its porridge well!  
The little Jesus who laughs and grimaces,  
the little Jesus is a sweet child.  
Joseph tidies your little kitchen,  
sweeps your floor, makes your bed,  
makes that sweet, sweet, sweet,  
hay and straw, vive le roi!  
makes that sweet, sweet, sweet,  
makes it beautiful for the sweet child.

label: christmas
translation:
There came three kings from distant lands,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
to offer a sacrifice to God.  
They were joyful.  
They came from the east, they came from afar,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
all through the brightness of a single star.  
They were joyful.  
But when they entered Jerusalem,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
they did not perceive the brightness of the star.  
They were sad.  
When they had sat at the table,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
then God’s angel came in secret.  
They were joyful.  
Gray lords and may no longer wait!  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
Herod has his horse prepared.  
They were sad.  
Well up, you lords, leave your food!  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
Herod is mounted on his horse.  
They were sad.  
When they left Jerusalem,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
they again perceived the brightness of the star.  
They were then happy.  
They followed the star in short journeys  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
to Bethlehem, where they found the little child.  
They were then happy.  
One went ahead, the other stayed,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
to come first to offer.  
They were joyful.  
There they fell together on the ground,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
they praised the king of greater worth.  
They were joyful.  
They offered myrrh, incense, and gold,  
now rock, now rock, now rock we -  
they praised that little child manyfold.  
They were joyful.

label: christmas
translation:
We come trudging with our star,  
Laureate of Cransio  
We seek Lord Jesus, we would have liked him.  
Laureate of the knee  
His Charles’ royal children,  
Father good Franselijn,  
Jeremiah.  
We had already come before King Herod's door,  
King Herod himself came forward.  
Herod spoke with a deceitful heart:  
“How does the youngest of three look so black?”  
Though he is somewhat dark, he is well known,  
he is the king from the Orient.  
We climbed the high mountain,  
there one saw the star standing so still.  
O star, you must not stand so still,  
you must go with us to Bethlehem.  
In Bethlehem, in the beautiful city,  
there Mary sat with her little child.  
The smaller the child, the greater the God,  
grant us a blessed New Year, God!

label: christmas
translation:
We come here with our star,  
we seek Lord Jesus, we wished so much.  
We already came to Herod’s door,  
Herod the king himself came out.  
Herod the king spoke with a false heart:  
“How is the youngest of the three so dark?”  
Though he is somewhat dark, he is well known, he is the youngest from the Orient,  
He is the youngest from foreign lands,  
where all those stars burn so quietly.  
Well star, stand still and do not move anymore,  
it is a sign from God the Lord.  
Well star, you must not stand so quietly,  
you must go with us to Bethlehem,  
To Bethlehem, that beautiful city,  
where Mary sat with her little child.  
The smaller the child, the greater the God,  
whom all the Jews have mocked.  
We have already sung before this house,  
give us the coin with a good cross!  
Though it is no cross, let it be a coin,  
give us the coin that you grant us!

label: christmas
translation:
Caspar, Melchior, and Balthazar,  
they all three went walking,  
but it was to go searching  
for the little child in the cloths  
lying in a forsaken stable  
for us great sinners all.  
The whole stable was full of joy,  
all the little animals knelt down there,  
the donkey and the ox  
they were free and loose,  
they warmed that little child  
already in his tender swaddling clothes.

label: christmas
translation:
1.  
We are three kings rich in power,  
and we go searching day and night  
all over mountain and over valley,  
to find  
what we knew,  
right over mountain and over valley,  
to find  
the God of all.

2.  
Jaspar, Melchior, Balthazar  
came to this little child there,  
they knelt with humility,  
offerings,  
burned incense,  
they knelt with humility,  
before this little child  
sweet Jesus.

3.  
The whole stable was full of peace,  
the little child and the animals too;  
then they shouted, so it sounds:  
‘vivat,  
vivat, vivat!’  
then they shouted, so it sounds:  
‘vivat, vivat,  
our king drinks!’  
hay and straw,  
vive la roy,  
makes that sweet, sweet, sweet,  
makes it beautiful for the little child sweet.  
‘make offerings,  
let incense burn!’  
and they knelt quickly  
before that sweet little child.

5.  
The whole stable was full of peace,  
the little child with the animals too.  
It was Mary with her little child,  
who lay in thirst,  
yes, at her breasts;  
and they shouted so it sounds:  
‘viva, our King drinks!’

label: christmas
translation: 1. Epiphany Epiphany Give me a new hat My old one is worn out My mother must not know Epiphany Epiphany Give me a new hat.

label: christmas
translation:
1. We come from the east, we come from far away  
A la Berliner postiljon  
We are three kings with a star  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus:  
From cher ami to the knee  
We are three royal children  
Sa pater went to Vendelo, from cher ami  

2. Oh star, you must not stand there so quietly  
A la Berliner postiljon  
You must come with us to Bethlehem  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus.  

3. In Bethlehem in that beautiful city  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Mary sat with her little child  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus.  

4. And the little child has lived so long there  
A la Berliner postiljon  
That he created heaven and earth  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus.  

5. Yes, heaven and earth and even more  
A la Berliner postiljon  
That is a sign from God the Lord  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus.  

6. We have already sung before this house  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Give us a coin and then we will go back home  
A la Berliner postiljon  
Chorus.

label: christmas
translation:
1. Three Kings saw a star  
A star of wondrous splendor  
The star of Jacob, the sign  
Long awaited in the sky  
They hastened away, guided  
By the star they had seen,  
To the land of the Jews, with gifts  
Of myrrh, of incense, and gold.  
[melody = the usual]

label: christmas
translation:
1. The Three Kings saw a star  
A star of wondrous splendor  
The star of Jacob, the sign  
Long awaited in the heavens  
They hastened under the guidance  
Of the star they had observed  
To the land of the Jews with gifts  
With myrrh, with incense, and gold

2. When they arrived at Jerusalem  
The star immediately vanished  
They went to King Herod  
And asked where should we go  
Where is the king born  
Whose star we have seen  
We have come to worship him  
With myrrh, with incense, and gold

3. When they had learned of it  
You must then go to Bethlehem  
The star appeared again  
And they traveled joyfully once more  
They found the royal child  
And knelt down in worship  
They now offered their gifts  
And returned full of gratitude

Text: G. de Bruijn  
Melody 1 unknown  
Melody 2 Adr. P. Hamers [Psalterke 1922/1929 no. 21]

Text in Psalterke:  
1:8 Of myrrh, of incense and gold  
2:1 When they came to Jerusalem  
3:1 And when they had learned there  
3:7 With gold and with incense and myrrh.

Melody 2 still generally known.

label: birthday
translation:
I.

Who wants to hear singing  
A new and farcical song?  
How the ear, its hearing,  
Almost lost by the singing;  
Oh! through music’s sorrow.  
Strings and viols stood ready:  
But thirsty throats were there with them.  
Who then wouldn’t want to hear? After basses, tenors,  
That dry sound:  
No, no; I stick with the flute;  
No, no; I stick with the flute.

II.  
I do not speak of organ pipes,  
Nor of where there was wine,  
Or tobacco or brandy,  
Or the manly kneeling-neck,  
Or the filler of the glass.  
An hour to feast, that does more good,  
Than nights of dancing with the idle youth;  
Then one hears the guts lament  
With a strange sound:  
No, no; &c.

III.  
Let Jenkins, Coleman, Taylor  
Run free in the meadow,  
And all that treasure  
Ever England had,  
I wipe on my ass.  
Then with allemandes, bourrées,  
Courantes, sarabandes, ut, re, mi, fa, sol,  
Also rounds and lords for pigs and piglets,  
Or such sounds:  
No, no; &c.

IV.  
Let the Frenchman cry freely,  
And sweat as he spins;  
So that in his song,  
Neither head, nor tail is seen,  
And first and last forgotten.  
Let the Italians, both rooster and capon,  
The Spanish Mores freely do their best;  
I wager they would never  
Leave me from the table,  
With their sound:  
No, no; &c.

V.  
Germany is all too stately for me,  
And gives but wind for money;  
With a stiff pastime,  
And not much substance:  
So that I find a torture rack.  
For weak memories, it is brain strain,  
Their little historical songs of six violins long.  
Where may those Germans sweat and dust themselves,  
One fart sound?  
No, no; &c.

VI.  
I love those chords very much,  
Where I am told:  
Sa, a vous, this jug  
Holds just half a buzz;  
Do take good care.  
I am skilled on this bean;  
And I hold it as worthy as those from the gown?  
Who can learn shorter to accord so neatly  
With quiet sound?  
Yes, yes; then I stick with the flute:  
Yes, yes; &c.

VII.  
Are you tired of hearing music?  
I have far higher stuff,  
From a hero whose power  
Has felled a dragon;  
It is true, though the voice is coarse.  
Of Maguelene or Palmerin,  
That brave figure or Bishop Tulpin,  
Of Adelaard, Ritserd, Reynouds and Witsard?  
That noble blood;  
And Don Quixote, full of courage,  
And Don, &c.

VIII.  
Malegijs should not be forgotten,  
That chivalrous man;  
Valiant, through his hand,  
Rosbayerd, triumphant,  
Vanquished in the mountain.  
Balkale and Bleccas from Lucifer’s lineage,  
And Roland, who was mad, with Folkerd of Leerdam,  
And Klaes of Kijten so rough in throwing,  
Or Monterbaen,  
And Job, that vengeful Neapolitan.  
And Job, &c.

IX.  
Pulcinel, Tijl Ulespiegel;  
Or Faustus with his page,  
Hillebrand or that Quand,  
Who at night stayed in the basket;  
Or also the Burgundian pope.  
Young Jakke, the lady of Meurs,  
Without eating or shitting or Fortunatus’ purse;  
The dwarf moon men, a terror for the little roosters,  
Or hollow Jas,  
The flying flat lice, Neel the shit cooker’s bag.  
The flying, &c.

X.  
Shut your mouth from all those snorers,  
So colorful in name;  
If I eat, you know this,  
I would not give a fart  
For all those heroes together.  
Go invent something from Kuwaert’s line.  
From Coppe the Hen horribly killed:  
Let us make their funeral stink, drink it out once;  
That goes first.  
So friends, follow my track:  
So friends, follow me, &c.

label: birthday
translation:
I.
Enough of idle reasoning,
That’s good for one who has eaten.
Now let us jingle a little tune,
That sticks to our ribs:
Drink once, it’s welcome,
No matter how much is brought to me.
I believe most poets fart with Apollo;
For none of them ever thought of drinking.

II.
The first shouts at us his head full of his desire;
Likewise; as if there were yet some mystery in it.
I prefer Bruno, who sang like a bard;
And ended each rhyme with a glass,
It’s fine amusement to hang his own business on the clock.
I used to get a lesson to keep silent;
Or else I was the scapegoat the next day.

III.
The second is struck in reason,
Who bluffs and enjoys Dullemans’ music;
And sings of things we do not ask about,
Of Pook, Stopmes, Cortelas and Piek;
Yes, he sings freely so that one hears
What you know of Wintbuyls,
Of knights, and of lances with all their circumstances;
Sing, for here stands no madhouse only about.

IV.
This one was worth being laughed at with farts;
Because here he spoils wounds from his garden.
It seems a paradise for those who do not know it;
But more fertile than Pier Wouter’s dune.
Here a tree bears cheese, milk, and cream,
Wine, beer, bread, meat, and fish.
Where else would one find a linden, covered with lindens,
Which is so rich, with various fruits!

V.
This one had to go live by a miller;
There the turning always comes in handy:
Where he points Saint Vechter to his crown,
Who sometimes grinds; and not half so fast.
He turns me blind with all his wind,
Makes my head confused.
It seems to me this spectacle vendor
Sat better and more comfortably,
Where the spectacles are the gable of the house.

VI.
Among all these crazy people raised,
Only the last speaks with reason;
Who likes a lot to lick spoons,
And to storm with knife and fork in hand.
Broth and pap is too weak for him;
That costs spooners;
He prefers turkeys
To kiss belly and anus:
And the fall of crowned storks.

VII.
Long live here a fresh glass, or a beaker;
It’s wine but all that is said;
The health of the apothecary,
Who today bravely stokes the white turf:
Well, now it sounds pretty out;
And if there is anyone,
Who finds a drop in it;
Beat me with a club,
That I out of fear again fill such a glass with piss.

VIII.
Now I also join in the refrains;
For whoever wants, it does not matter to me,
Think that I want to sit alone,
As if I were a jester or a sultan?
No, certainly not! That would be sadness:
I have consumed too much
With worthies and students,
And all those lively people,
Who lubricate more than wagon wheels.

IX.
The poems are a recreation;
They once make people’s hearts glad:
And shorten the already long span
Of the old unpleasant times;
Whether sonnet or ballet;
Or stately madrigal,
Or klinkers, or rondeels,
Or gentle table plays,
So long as it is in pure German tongue.

X.
Long live, long live our Signor Franciscus
Who often has delighted us,
That no panther beast, nor basilisk
Drag him to the grave before his due time.
His wine and tarts are ripe in March;
And that is our harvest;
Wherein we eagerly soak
Much more than the churches:
So that with us neither throat nor knife rusts.

label: birthday
translation:
Who prides himself on lofty thoughts,  
And braves on Augustus' throne,  
The fortune of the dehumanized Goon?  
Who endured through all inevitabilities  
Fire and water; lent storm and sword;  
And did not scorn feeling,  
When he now was plunged into his direst need,  
And saw no outcome except pale Death.

See, my Insensibility must be praised,  
Which loves much of Seafaring,  
Or trusts in his Rapier:  
Who quickly caught the flame of a swift arrow  
In his breast, by the dear Blind maid.  
(Whom no water can quench,  
Nor icy cold moisture appears so cool)  
Without cause scorn my Insensibility.

Who nonetheless obtained in his legs  
The Rich Man's Podagra,  
Or Gout, or the Cheiragra,  
Who though pain in his head; in his tooth;  
And for Insensibility wished the hellish Fire?  
Who has suffered so severely,  
That he rather would endure grief and evil;  
Than seek his grief's relief through Insensibility?

No Alcides, however great in power;  
Bound by Door and Fire,  
Has wished for Insensibility.  
No Apollo, who lost his charioteer,  
By the force of Saturnides' lightning glare,  
And with weeping eyes,  
And reason, was not fond of my Insensibility;  
And raised it above his divine being.

Let my Insensibility not sadden you,  
Now you joyfully in this time,  
Prince of honors, are once again aged;  
Although another, in place of your Tart,  
Might rather wish for Rodemont’s Halberd  
Let us rather enjoy  
Your generosity of tarts and fine Wine,  
Which will be insensitive to knife and tooth.  
Even Jove offered, from his golden hall,  
For your Father’s couchettes  
His Lightning bolt.  
No swift death was born from Pest for them,  
Through Apollo’s Arrow.  
Had Rome enjoyed that favor,  
Before Curtius bravely closed their Pool!  
Never sad was his end,  
Never sad, etc.

If, oh Prince, Love urged you  
For a lively Maiden,  
To tightly gird your Dagger and Sabre to your body,  
According to your decency;  
And that your hand rightly and curved,  
Smelled of fish, you are but without smell;  
Now that you have become once again aged,  
Now you again, etc.  
The hot Sun shone on his pale Face,  
Carthage had not cursed him through the Face:  
Had Oedipus the blindness of Tiresias,  
Had Oedipus enjoyed the blindness of Tiresias,  
Never was his downfall heard of.

No Love rivalry comes to us through the Sight;  
We do not see a Woman go to bed with another.  
The anger here vanished by Diana.  
Had Cadmus, after all, lost his Sight in the Cradle,  
No one saw Acteon's Crown.

Or although, oh Prince! our Eye saw no light,  
We see today your happy Birthday;  
Faith shows us that the Eye never understood:  
Therefore we wish that you may Blindly depart from this,  
Therefore we wish that you may Blindly depart from this,  
World as your years go to waste.  
In the Tastelessness of the finest feeling.  
Never does my brain stand strained  
Before the judgment seat of widowhood:  
But loves with the Splendor of Men  
The coarsest treatment, the roughest bustle:

Pleasant, still disarmed, still no one may call me a child correctly;  
I was never voracious afterwards:  
Nothing can upset me more,  
Than to pour Wine into the body;  
And to jerk like a Bear.  
I hate Wine with Ice;  
I despise the luxury  
In Food, and Drink, and Smoke, and Love, and Banquets.  
Away with Hares and Rabbits,  
With Pastry, or Tart or Pie,  
Away with French and Rhine Wines:  
Water can slake my thirst.

I do not spend money on Food or Smoke, or needless Drinks.  
I love Mustard yet Tobacco,  
Nor other sharp Smokes.  
I will lightly thank you in the morning  
For the Alsem, or Arak,  
I do not waste morning time  
With a voracious big breakfast  
Of Broths, or Pates de Mazarin:  
At midday I banish from my table  
All the fine Beer and Wine,  
And I set the Struff and Waffle,  
By the Taste of Flérécin.  
Life would lightly please me by Pythagoras,  
If it were less Spartan,  
I shall never lament Curtius,  
Nor ask for other necessities,  
Than the use of knife and glass.  
Away with Mark Antony,  
His Lust seems to us a mockery;  
Or even Cleopatra who seemed so stately,  
I shall not hitch after Nectar drink,  
Nor after other Divinity:  
Nor by filthy Ammelak  
Let all my Taste and desire be destroyed.

Prince, who through art can provide taste to Tastelessness,  
Let my Song be tasty to you:  
So I desire, all my life,  
To pursue no other Taste;  
Than Greek Ambrosia.  
Are you then aged,  
And create Taste in Tart,  
Or a Hare, or newly roasted Calico,  
Let the Taste of everything else be forgotten  
To hasten you to that dish,  
Where the Blind and Deaf crowds  
Take off their caps for the Taste.

In the tastelessness of the finest feeling.

Before the court of reproachful jealousy:
But love with the splendor of men
Treats it rudely, with the roughest turmoil:

Delightful, still uncloaked, no child may truly call me;

I shall not lean after nectar drink,
Nor after other divinity:
Nor at the filthy Ptolemy
Shall all my taste and desire be spent.

Prince, who through art can give taste to tastelessness,
Let my song be tasteful to you:
So I desire, all my life,
To pursue no other taste;
Than Greek ambrosia.
Are you then having your birthday,
And create taste in tart,
Or a hare, or newly roasted chicken,
Let the taste of all else be abandoned
To hasten you to that dish,
Where the Blind and Deaf gather
To take off their hats for the taste.

label: birthday
translation:
I who come out of the Realm of Wonders
Here without senses,
Buzz on my horn,
And through the echo of Wonders
Make Prince Astolph mute;
I who come out of the Realm of Wonders
Here without senses.

As Jupiter’s glances,
And thunder lean downwards,
Where everyone groans strongly,
I shit, instead of being frightened,
That air and earth resound.
As Jupiter’s glances, etc.

Let the fire appear,
Straight away my zeal becomes fierce:
Or if Vulcan came here,
I would not shrink from his glow;
But would even blacken his flame.
Let the fire, etc.

Let Aeolus unleash storms,
And roar on the sea;
I just drink a toast,
Though all his blasts I will still,
Even before Neptune does.
Let Aeolus, etc.

Or if one or another
Of the sea gods plays the rooster:
I shall, as before Vulcan,
The stormy Scamander,
Drive him to his den.
Or if one, etc.

Or Pluto would growl with me,
If he lost Proserpina:
The hellhound shall, in the guise
Of friendship with his claws,
Be humble and friendly to me.
Let Pluto then free, etc.

I wager Mars’s sword in the sheath
I will keep well for one,
Who seemed to follow my nature,
Or if I chased, like Diomedes,
Him before the rascal.
I wager Mars, etc.
Will keep it with care.

Let Bacchus bind his sweets,
And make no fuss;
For if I drink bottled beer
With sulky peasants,
I drive him through a fire.
Let Bacchus, etc.

I boasted with Lady Juno;
I boast more than men,
Away Venus with your shine,
And dancing love rays:
Love has no chance here.
I boasted with, etc.

Let Pallas never mix
Her pains with Gorgons’
Mingled poison
To make my head curly;
For if I once reach for the grin,
I grin more clever than the Thracians.
Let Pallas, etc.

I boasted with your hundred hands,
You Briatean ape;
For if I yawn once,
Straight you fly through my teeth,
So that no nail is spared.
I boasted with your, etc.

Let her calm the goddess,
And reign in her court;
For she who makes me too coarse
Of all those heavenly hosts,
I grind as small as dust.
Let her, etc.

Pan may silence his chatter,
And squeak on a reed;
For if he were mad in his domain
And led neighboring woods,
I would bring all his actions to nothing.
Pan may, etc.

Let satyrs ride and dance,
And roll in the grass;
For if there were one,
Who would dare oppose me,
I would wound him like Marsyas.
Let satyrs, etc.

If a nymph loved me,
And prayed for my grace,
To whom I was averse;
I gave her a box full of lindens,
And a shove for her backside,
If a nymph, etc.

The slyest of the enchantresses,
The most cursed hag,
Never makes me lose courage;
Or if she touches my shoes;
I press her testicles full.
The slyest, etc.

The smartest of the astrologers
Must flee from my wrath;
For whoever comes near my head,
(Although the foam of the stargazers,)
I let my globe be seen.
The smartest, etc.

Be silent about Achilles’ powers,
Of Peleus’ heroism;
For though he was swift of foot,
I am faster in thoughts,
In movement, hand, and courage.
Be silent of, etc.

Let Ninus found Babel,
Before he conquers the world;
I consider Cyrus a child;
Who had to yield to a woman,
I esteem much less than conquest.
Let Ninus, etc.

Let Alexander be silent,
And perish by poison;
Let Caesar be excluded,
Who after his civil wars
Was done away with by the sword.
Let Alexander, etc.

Do not speak now of any heroes,
Of whom one has composed poems;
I banish them from ear and light,
And do not want to mention their names;
Provided I yield to no one.
Do not speak now, etc.

I wish to give you the feeling,
That makes you flee deafness,
See eternally with eyes,
That your smell will never fail,
As long as you enjoy taste.
I wish, etc.

If you then want to enlighten the eye
Through the pure glow of wine;
Taste through ambrosia,
Feelings raised by tarts;
No one will be harmed by this.
If you want then, etc.

If you only think of the smell,
With sweetness gladly Greek:
(Whether for Pole, or Greek)
We offer pleasure for the ear,
And give you the music,
If you only, etc.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
On your pleasant birthday  
I would gladly  
Like the poets, try my hand,  
Compose this or that little verse  
And perform it,  
Thus linking my wish in rhyme.  

2.  
But I have never drunk  
From the spring water of the hoof,  
Whose splashing  
Lulls shady Phocis to sleep,  
And kindles the poetic fire;  
It flows for no insignificant lock.  

3.  
Nevertheless, I have decided  
Not to come  
To this annual feast at the table  
Without a rhyme; today  
These are my prayers  
That it be accepted as it is.  

4.  
But I must first mention  
That I rarely  
Read poets of recent flair:  
From this comes that my words,  
With accords,  
Are old-fashioned, in style.  

5.  
And since in these days  
All delight  
Comes from Antiquity-knowledge;  
My rhymes will please everyone,  
In whose ears,  
Everything old sounds dignified.  

6.  
So why should one bind oneself,  
Dear friends,  
To all the nuances of good German?  
Oh! He who first thought of this  
And reflected on it  
I consider head of the Guits.  

7.  
I will become a better rhetorician,  
And show  
That, in matters of rhyme,  
Hogeveen and Jan van Gyzen  
Are to be praised  
Above Vondel, or next to him.  

8.  
Just pay attention to those poets,  
Though forgotten;  
Who analyze a sad lament  
Or a rogue’s last words  
Bravely dissecting them  
On Friday before Court Day.  

9.  
Does one not see the crowds,  
Eagerly running  
After these works full of sense?  
Proof that they all enjoy  
And fully appreciate  
The cream of rhyme-artistry.  

10.  
On the other hand, the poems,  
Which were examined  
By the connoisseurs,  
Unintelligible and scorned,  
Are forgotten  
As happened in Zwanenburg.  

11.  
Therefore I shall, without fail,  
Determine myself  
To that clear heroic tone,  
And surely win the song prize,  
If I may invent.  
I cough once in Apollo’s crown.  

12.  
But to finally touch on the matters  
This entire song refers to;  
Namely your festive season,  
Where people joyfully  
And generously sing and jest.  

13.  
First, as we drink,  
Without lessening,  
Your healthiness;  
I wish you to live long,  
Without weakness,  
In health, with great content.  

14.  
Secondly, dear friend,  
Live full of love,  
With your spouse in comfort,  
For many years to come,  
Without fears;  
Before Death, that lean murderer.  

15.  
Now, thirdly; have, with grace,  
In the space  
Of your life much happiness;  
Thus you will, very gently,  
Always flourish  
In joyous song, without burden.  

16.  
Furthermore, fourthly; it grieves me,  
And I forget,  
That this little song lasts so long,  
Yet I hope, with effort,  
To manage it,  
Although it is sent to Peet Nieze.  

17.  
I say, fifthly, keep in mind:  
Great treasures  
Are not only prosperity,  
But spending it joyfully,  
And according to reason  
Is pompous, yet uncommon.  

18.  
May this happiness be given to you,  
And beside it  
To your virtuous descendants,  
The same fate bestowed!  
Thus it may shine proudly  
As the stars shine at night.  

19.  
That your children: sixthly,  
And lastly,  
Stand before you as plantings,  
And through loving handling,  
Raise much increase  
To your very true joy.  

20.  
See, from these your good shoots  
Sprout forth  
Other branches, whose virtue  
Makes your heart glow with joy,  
And delight,  
Through that sign of your youth.  

21.  
Now I stop my singing  
With restraint;  
They weary you enough!  
I will reveal to you very shortly,  
Please remember:  
That it is sung to this tune:  

22.  
Polyphemus on the shores  
Felt burning  
His young heart with hot love.  
Born with the head of a pig,  
Who wants to hear?  
With his one eye facing the sea.

label: birthday
translation:
1.  
Happy New Year!  
Pull the cat by the hair,  
Pull the dog by the tail,  
Then you will have something all year round.  

2.  
Much happiness in the new year!  
Father and mother sleep together,  
And I sleep in the crib.  
May I have a little ear?  

3.  
I wish you much happiness and blessing,  
On dikes and roads,  
With curls in your hair.  
I wish you happiness and blessing in the new year.  

4.  
Hello woman, hello man, hello everyone,  
I come to wish you a good new year.  
Through thick and thin, I walk along;  
Do you have a waffle or two? I will not sell them.  
It is a good woman who gives something;  
I wish she lives through the next year.  
Good woman! Good woman!  
Do you have a waffle or two? I will put them right in my sleeve.  

5.  
It is New Year, I stand here outside  
I have a little basket, and it will not close;  
I have a little knife, and it will not cut;  
I do have butter, but it will not fry;  
I see a cookie and cannot buy it;  
Give me a penny, and let me go.  

6.  
New Year is coming to the land;  
The pig burned its tail,  
At the back of its little gate.  
Mother, give me a little ear;  
If it is not an ear, then it is a penny,  
Tomorrow New Year will be over.  

7.  
Sweet little New Year,  
The piglet has four feet,  
Four feet and one tail,  
Is that not worth a cookie?  
It did not stand long in the stable,  
Janneef has killed it,  
Give me a cookie and let me go.  

2.  
Palm, palm Easter;  
A little rooster on a stick,  
A hole in its coat,  
A piece of its left foot;  
Now the whole little rooster is dead.  

3.  
Palm, palm Easter, hey, cow’s milk;  
There is still one more Sunday,  
Then we will go to the Easter pasture.  

4.  
Palm, palm Sunday,  
When the farmer was in the water,  
I was there, and you were there,  
Wasn't that a beautiful painting?  
Woman, woman, give us an egg,  
That the black hens laid,  
Whether they see black or are red,  
Therefore, they do not lay under duress.  
A, B, C, D. give us a clerk with us,  
Bake us a pancake,  
Give us eggs and enough money.  
Woman, woman, at last,  
Give us the laid eggs,  
Laid and picked,  
Let us stay a little longer.  
Here is still a good woman,  

Who will give us something.

label: birthday
translation:
MB tries the Surinamese song she had learned: A diris deri jufiarie A sofie sofie sofie sofie. A diris deri jufiarie And here comes vesterioe: Hatsjoe. Open zim zim p. 125: In the glory! Michelle (15 years old) attends the De Bruijne Lyceum, in a class with students of very different backgrounds. At the Sint Dominicus School in Oog-in-Al, where she completed primary education, according to her, there were only Dutch children, including a boy of Surinamese descent. Especially for him, the following birthday song was practiced in her class:  
Di mi jere joe verjari  
1. Di mi jere joe verjari  
   Because I heard that you have a birthday  
Di mi jere joe verjari  
Di mi jere joe verjari  
   Because I heard that you have a birthday  
Ne mi kom versteri joe  
   I come to congratulate you  
2. A nof joe koek a nof joe sopie  
   Not for the cake, not for the drink  
A nof joe koek a nof joe sopie  
A nof joe koek a nof joe sopie  
   Not for the cake, not for the drink  
Ma mi kom versteri joe  
   But I come to congratulate you.  
Hatsjoe!

Sranan, also called Surinamese, is a creole language. We see that clearly in the first line. In the word 'jere' the English word for 'hear' (to hear) can be recognized, in 'joe' the English equivalent (you) for ‘je’ and ‘jij’ (the Dutch informal ‘you’). Verjari comes from ‘verjaren’, an old-fashioned term for ‘to have a birthday’. Versteri, always in the last line, is a contraction of ‘feliciteren’ (to congratulate), a Dutch verb derived from French. Sopie, in the (outdated) Dutch spelling written as ‘zoopie’ or ‘zoopje’, means a sip or a gulp of (strong) drink. The word thus refers both to the act of drinking heavily as well as the drink itself. ‘Koek-en-zoopie’ originally did not primarily mean cake and drink but rather the stalls on the ice where these refreshments could be bought. Hatsjoe is a rhyme word without meaning, added by Michelle and her classmates. It functions as an accent, as confirmation or reinforcement of the preceding line. Sometimes the girls invented another word; it did not matter which, as long as it rhymed.

The melody of this song is the same as that of John Brown’s Body, a soldiers’ marching song from the American Civil War. The refrain text was as follows:  
Glory, glory, hallelujah  
Glory, glory, hallelujah  
Glory, glory, hallelujah  
His soul is marching on.

As a soldiers’ song, with adjusted, translated, or entirely new text where needed, but apparently also as a birthday song, this melody has conquered roughly half the world. In the Netherlands, (marching) soldiers and other ‘stappers’ or avid walkers sang, among others, the following text to this tune:  
Glori-, glori- glori- gloria  
The girls of Batavia are black  
Glori-, glori- glori- gloria  
And the girls of Batavia are black  
Pitch black!

In this version too an extra word, an intensifier of the final word, is added.

The girls in Batavia are of course not black, let alone pitch black, but nobody ever cared about that. Even if they had been every color of the rainbow, as long as you could march nicely to the tune, that would have been no problem at all.

label: funeral
translation:
I pray to you Batavians  
You Hollanders of noble names  
Who are also esteemed as Banners,  
And will you not also be ashamed  
Of your Prince, highly honorable,  
Though he has fallen,  
God will restore us all  
A Hero once again.

Already from that very Stemma  
From where he sprang forth,  
There is now once again  
A new shoot emerging,  
No one has yet regretted,  
His princely state,  
God will measure his power  
Like that of his brothers.

For his praise at least  
I must recount here,  
For women and men,  
Young and old in general,  
How you  
Are princely,  
His deeds highly esteemed,  
His enemies all  
Have been so defeated.

But is there ever such a Roman,  
Born of noble deeds,  
As Maurice Prince alone,  
As you may hear here,  
Though God has chosen him  
And called him home in the end,  
Nevertheless I will beforehand  
Praise his deeds anew.

Victorious he was,  
It has become clear indeed,  
He has bested so many forces  
Of his enemy,  
There many gave their lives,  
For the Pious Hero,  
As you are described,  
Mentioned in the chronicle.

As it is well known,  
When he took up the Sword,  
Gheertruyden-Berg bears witness,  
By order of the Lords of the States,  
Who came to his aid,  
With very brave men recruited  
And did not leave it,  
Until it was in his hand.

Next, an assault  
Upon the City of Breda began  
Which I can well say,  
It was also very cleverly accomplished  
With a peat barge,  
By the order of our Prince,  
Where it all began,  
We proceeded according to our wish.

Thereafter marched to battle,  
Zutphen,  
Deventer very bold,  
That was taken with force,  
Nassau then not yet extinct,  
But marched to the distress  
Of Steenwijck excellent,  
Which observed with the Prince,  
To him honor was due.

And Coeverden there beside,  
That had also to be taken,  
Many a force came there,  
Who left their lives there,  
The city Groningen likewise  
Must be subdued by him,  
So he went about pursuing them,  
For nine weeks long,

I have not yet recounted  
All his deeds together,  
How the battle went,  
To Turnhout highly renowned,  
Nor in one year did he  
Win twelve cities,  
What do you think of this reckoning,  
Our God has granted all this.

And yet I must especially  
Tell of Flanders,  
Where he was in the trap  
Of Spaniards and Walloons,  
And many tongues,  
Who came into battle there,  
To capture him so,  
With all their great forces.

But God has spared him  
The battle began there,  
No one failure,  
The Prince had conceived it,  
By day and by sun,  
To probe near Nieuwpoort,  
The Prince won the battle,  
And withdrew again from there.

Yet I would tell more  
Of all his pious deeds,  
The time is too short again,  
Of his great mercies,  
Which he has by grace  
Dispensed for the country,  
You Men want early and late  
To praise our Prince, because.

The time of Arminians  
Where the Prince succumbed,  
He dared stand alone,  
Though they preached,  
On Sundays and throughout the weeks,  
They set themselves against him,  
But he did not avenge on them,  
Was not a good Lord.

Nevertheless the Romanist  
Has so many wiles,  
Against this Prince conspired,  
Caused him to die by treason,  
According to his father’s inheritance,  
Just as he was killed,  
But it was all her ruin,  
They themselves remained in need.

Thereupon notice might be taken  
That God the Lord Almighty  
Has helped the Prince with his counsel,  
Very powerfully,  
All through his hand united,  
That he did not die,  
Like his Father truly,  
At Delft all in one shot.

But on his bed, rested,  
Described with good understanding,  
All according to his heart’s desire,  
Each according to his will given,  
And that all during his life,  
With such good understanding,  
Which among mankind,  
Will remain in our land.

He who had so devoutly  
And vigilantly guarded his young life  
He is now already gone,  
It is worth to be recorded  
How he conducted himself  
At the end and at death,  
So wise he strove there  
In his last distress.

This was his lesson there  
Which the Prince commended,  
Against them all  
Who stood there about,  
Oh if with heart and ground  
The fatherland be defended  
Thus spoke the Prince around  
And do not let yourself be betrayed.

Look indeed at this Figure,  
As he is buried,  
The day, time and hour,  
Of Princes and Counts,  
Who came after him,  
Before and behind the Corpse  
Still were there his servants,  
Who went along likewise.

His Scepter with the Crown,  
They also saw them carry,  
With many fine weapons,  
But listen to my reports,  
Everyone seemed well accounted,  
With sorrow and misery,  
One heard laments there,  
The Prince’s death announced.

I would tell more,  
But my heart is broken,  
That the pious warlord  
Has thus stricken from here,  
Oh might we still avenge,  
His death to bring back,  
It would not be lacking,  
In people nor in weapon.

Already inside Delft the City,  
There men saw this happen,  
Many people saw that,  
Of poor and rich people,  
Many people walked by that side,  
Before and behind strong,  
One could not avoid each other,  
Before he was in the Church.

In short this is done  
If you would take it in thanks,  
It is to no one’s disdain,  
But to everyone’s benefit,  
How rich and powerful states  
You must deal with all,  
And must leave them here,  
Even if it outwardly seems otherwise.

label: funeral
translation:
O Highly Famous Netherlands,  
Lay down your splendor, clothe yourself in mourning,  
Our Noble Prince brave of mind,  
Who faithfully for our Fatherland,  
His body and goods, and noble blood,  
So liberally has risked,  
Against our enemies, was never defeated.

He who fought for our freedom,  
Who has trampled our lofty enemies completely,  
And brought them all underfoot,  
Whom the Lord, according to His desire,  
Has taken from us into His kingdom,  
Where he lives in joy eternally.  
His soul shall live in joy,  
His body here in the earthly valley  
Elevated highly by his Lord Father,  
And shall rest with his Lord Brother,  
To whom now, with great mourning,  
With greater honor and dignity,  
But accompanied by many tears.

The mourning cannot be described,  
Of our sorrowful fatherland,  
Everyone witnesses it,  
Over this very triumphant Hero  
Whose bravery and well-conducted,  
And all his deeds wonderous great  
Are known throughout the entire world.

Oh, how grieved is the Noble Princess,  
Oh, how grieved is that young Hero,  
Who, to follow his Father’s lessons,  
Has placed his foot in his Father’s place,  
Oh, how grieved, as is indeed necessary,  
Are our high-born Princesses,  
And all that Orange has ever gained.

How could the human heart contemplate,  
The praise that this Prince is highly honored,  
There is caused with the funeral to bestow,  
For what his praise well merits,  
No man, however wise, who according to his price,  
Can raise his praise as high  
As that well deserved by the Orange family.

Yet honor him, you Batavians,  
And help honor his high praise,  
Also follow the body in good manners,  
O The Hague! O High Court!  
For the last accompanied, but his Highness,  
Though his soul rests with the Lord,  
His fame and praise shall never die.

In the Binnenhof in The Hague,  
Laid in a tomb in the carriage,  
Completely covered with black velvet,  
And also eight horses so clothed,  
Therewith, although his person,  
Was carried very mournfully,  
And also as it should be.

The entire citizenry very loyal,  
Of The Hague strongly armed,  
All very dignified in mourning,  
With their weapons very triumphant,  
They went along halfway,  
Where the citizenry of Delft came,  
Also meeting the Orange family.

There it was very well intended,  
Three times so honored  
Salvo shot with reverence:  
Those from The Hague returned again,  
The citizenry of Delft free,  
Also all together in mourning,  
Showed themselves there also very faithful.

To now increase his honor highly,  
Who remains forever in thought,  
Many Kings, Princes and Lords,  
With embassies according to the custom,  
Honor his departure well,  
To show the affection indeed,  
That they bear to his person.

For outward display very beautifully,  
Nineteen horses, all in black,  
With velvet very beautiful and fine,  
That could delight human hearts,  
They all bore there,  
The arms of our cities clear,  
To the glory and praise of his person.

For the body they also laid out,  
One horse according to the old Roman custom,  
Armed, and all in various  
Colors, and plumes not less,  
Very triumphant on all sides,  
Fame shall proclaim his praise,  
To the ends of the world.

Behind the body in great mourning,  
William of Orange came,  
A young flower of the house of Nassau,  
A sprout of the Orange family,  
Further also according, every pair and pair,  
To the noble lords nearest in blood,  
Our Lord’s State, very wise and prudent.

Now let us all with faithful hearts,  
Fall down before the Lord of Lords,  
Pray for the noble house of Nassau,  
And for the High Noble Orange Blood,  
For long respite and lifetime,  
For good governance with understanding,  
And faithful prudence for our Fatherland.  
To the joy of our united lands,  
O Lord hear our sweet supplication,  
Give our Prince wisdom and understanding.  
In his government good prosperity,  
And will his seed also thereby,  
Let increase abundantly,  
For comfort and hope of our Fatherland.

Farewell you pious Dutchmen,  
Remain faithful to Orange until death,  
They have always been loyal supporters,  
Been at our greatest distress,  
William was the first breed,  
Who against Spain’s tyranny,  
First protected us and made us free.

Maurice very young in years,  
But old in wisdom and knowledge,  
Frederick Henry without hesitation,  
Who did many fine deeds for our land,  
Rest all together in the Lord’s name,  
William is he who now lives again,  
O Lord give him Solomon’s wisdom.

In print, I seek my fortune.
