label: st_nicholas
translation:
Saint Nicholas Bishop, good holy man,  
will you give something in my little shoe, God will reward you then.  
Give me a purse with bells,  
so I will not suffer anymore.  
As long as God allows,  
I love Saint Nicholas.  

Saint Nicholas, good holy man,  
put on your best robe  
and ride it to Spain,  
bring apples from Oranges,  
plums from Damascus,  
give the little children something.  

Saint Nicholas, that good lord,  
comes every year again,  
with his little horses before his wagon,  
thus Saint Nicholas rushes in.  

3.  
You grew in virtue with the years,  
atoned for the sins of your youthful days,  
diligently seeking knowledge,  
you became proficient in it in a short time.  

4.  
During fasting you disciplined your body,  
curbed the flesh's urges with a hairy garment;  
your soul, a temple of the Holy Spirit,  
was attached to God day and night.  

5.  
After you were ordained a priest,  
you further cultivated virtue,  
engaged the body in a hard warfare,  
so that it would be more subject to the spirit.  

6.  
You slept and ate much less than before,  
and your drink was only clear water,  
your clothes were poor, yet neat,  
the spirit always cared for in prayer.  

7.  
When both your parents were dead,  
you received from their estate a very large treasure,  
which you did not squander uselessly  
or lock away in an iron chest.  

8.  
You gave it generously to the poor,  
as you did not consider it your own,  
but as administrator appointed by the Lord,  
through which this deed of yours is also told:  

9.  
In Patara there was a poor nobleman,  
who with his wife had three beautiful daughters;  
but now that they had grown old,  
he could no longer provide for their support.  

10.  
So he gave them a counsel,  
which was deadly evil for their souls,  
that for a piece of money they would stain  
their purity with dishonor.  

11.  
As soon as Saint Nicholas understood this,  
he went by night to the same house,  
threw the window in, a cloth bundle with gold,  
with which one daughter was helped.  

12.  
Again he returned to the household,  
and threw gold again through the window;  
through which the second daughter got married,  
such great power this hidden gold had.  

13.  
He remembered the third daughter,  
and brought money into the house one night,  
through which he saved her body from need  
and her soul from death.  

14.  
O wise and merciful prelate,  
you are aware of our slippery state,  
through which our thoughtless youth  
is quickly carried away from her innocent virtue.  

15.  
Grant us from God a devout mind,  
that always purely does His will;  
give us the gold of love in our shoes,  
so that we escape the punishment of God's rod.  

For him, whether of money or delicacy,  
has suffered the night before Christmas:  
there is then found some sugar in it,  
some money, some gifts, some more or less,  
according to what it has received earlier  
according to the will of fathers and mothers:  
for if it has shaved the beast too much,  
and has been too bad,  
Saint Nicholas will put, in his shoe,  
instead of money, a rod.

label: work_sea
translation:
Many brave sea heroes,
With ships and cannon,
Quickly went into battle,
Before it fiercely began;
As soon as the wind shifts,
The fleet is turned around,
To find good winds,
When it blows.

Whoever uses it to his advantage,
Has already won much,
After much sea voyage judgment;
Then the smoke of them swirls;
Through which everyone sees,
What happens in the fleet;
Through the smoke, the people do not escape many calamities.

They now begin cannonading,
With the heaviest artillery;
They first do this from afar;
And if the night does not stop it,
Then the fight goes fiercely on,
With shooting and fierce striking,
Until many ships, through wrecked ships,
Go down.

The admiral wants to fight,
With much prudence;
He encourages his war servants,
To the fight.
The cannon kills many sailors.
Many ships are in trouble;
Those who jump off, perish,
By their fleet.

One sees hands and feet,
Flying in the air;
The people who fall off must
Struggle for breath;
With dreadful noise,
The swelling house flies apart;
Those who drown, the sinking
Is a short cross.

There goes a keel to the bottom,
There many sailors sit;
Death sits on their mouths;
Those who do not drown bite,
For rescue, as a boat
Saves them from their distress.
By the dead, death's needs
Shoot shot by shot.

One hears the cannon fiercely roar,
The fight not yet slackened;
You have seen it ten times,
With bravery.
The people are still full of courage;
They fight very wildly.
Oh! the glory of victory,
It is with them.
Your admirals do not shoot,
With all their heavy cannon,
Until one could closely
Shelled them in the fleets;
However fiercely the enemy shoots,
They do not groan at this;
According to their judgment, to their advantage,
This happens.

They now fiercely attack him,
With all their firearms;
This hits many people; many masts
Are also hit;
Many ships by such blows
Must go to the abyss;
It almost failed, or they sailed
Away from there.

Their fight is lost.
The enemy is trapped,
From behind and from the front;
But light was there appointed,
To be loyal to each other,
And not to fight,
Until glory would
Give them victory.

The admiral sends ships
To their aid; but the fight
Gives them heavy blows,
Through the enemy's bravery.
The breakers come along,
To attack them;
Through the smoke, they do not escape
The pursuit.

The people are full of glory.
Also full to board.
We now gain victory,
And many ships of goods;
Like bullets, gunpowder, the cannon;
What the admiral now won,
From those who would not avoid fighting;
But began.

Now his enemies flee.
He sails after them.
We burn many ships.
He causes them great harm.
He also drags away,
What he encounters,
Without masts; the war guests
Act quickly.

His enemy without glory
Sails into his harbors;
But he comes with victory,
And with a great gain,
Rejoicing to the land:
To the damage of his enemy;
Who had to retreat and fight,
To his shame.

If everyone were content with theirs,
Thereby also yielding,
The war would disappear,
Just as smoke does;
If everyone were content with what
God lent him,
All the fighting and much suffering,
Would soon vanish.

label: work_sea
translation:
Belongs together Sailors all as one,
Who were by the Sea,
Are you caught by storm or distress
God helps you on a reef:
For many a Boat crew,
Must risk their lives,
All on the Sea rebel
By nights and by days.
The Sea by God's ordinance,
Consumes so many Men,
Who all together from the Fish,
Are then devoured,
But read! what depends on that,
Where our Bodies sail,
If God the soul then
Wants to preserve eternally.
What grief the Sailor suffers,
By nights and by day,
When he sees death before his eyes,
And may not escape:
Through storm and great thunder,
And the violence of the waves,
That brings us all the more
Into fear and great troubles.
Also great violence and adversity
The Sailor suffers valiantly,
From Turk and Pirates very fierce
They are assaulted,
And many more Enemies,
Who seek to harm them,
Who sail by the Sea,
May well think of that.
If they also on a lee shore
Are driven forward by storms,
Very quickly they cut away the Masts,
Then cut overboard,
Then we again see death,
Before our eyes with dread;
In short the care is great,
That the Sailor must bear,
God then helps her out of the distress,
Who calls to him deeply;
He does not want the Sinner's death,
But that he repents,
For by his strong hand,
He will revive the man again,
And brings her back to Land,
In a desired Harbor.
When they come ashore fine,
Their first work is,
Daily in brandy
But little in the Church,
It is quickly forgotten,
That the Lord has preserved her,
Faithfully helped by his Grace,
From fear and great terror.
When they receive their money in hand,
It is quickly consumed,
They buy therefore neither land nor sand,
The Landlady and Land,
It is as if he finds it somewhere,
So he goes dominating,
That one so easily consumes,
One sees so easily consume.
With Ladies of the loose kind,
They take to the wild,
They must again set sail,
When the money is all spent,
It is no great honor to them,
Nor less for the women:
For they according to God's teaching,
Must keep themselves chaste.
But when, ah I do speak of,
What much display is seen,
Daily along the streets go,
Of daughters old and young,
In Holland everywhere,
One observes them flaunting,
It comes from misfortune,
They are no daughters with honor.
The Zeeland Girls, what a talk,
They know very well too,
At night to dance along the street,
Already with a Boat crew member,
And make a great commotion,
Wanting to know from the young men,
They are daughters of two houses,
Or I have forgotten it.
But it is with a foolish face,
Of these daughters here?
At night to dance over the street,
The musician leads the way,
It is no great honor to them,
They come to carry it lightly,
For in the East Indies far,
One hears about that.
The young men consider it no harm,
They say, it is pleasure,
To dance sometimes along the street,
Already with a Venus-animal,
But as the Scripture mentions
It is no honor to them,
That they their fair money,
So recklessly consume.
These young men and daughters fine,
They are very well known,
They live by the Turf quay,
Or somewhere nearby,
Because they are honest,
I do not want to blame them here,
For if the thread is not fine,
One would make it coarser.
You young men hear with good counsel,
Keep temperance in drinking,
For drunkenness, as it is said,
Is the mother of all evil,
Live free according to God's Teaching;
Want evil company hate,
So will God the Lord,
Let you sail in peace.
Farewell Sailor hear my admonition,
When you are in distress,
Call God the Lord from your heart,
Confess your great sins;
For he who from the heart truly
Repents of his sins,
Is healed by God the Lord
In all his wounds.
When we first found this new Song,
We sailed over Sea,
From East Indies to the Fatherland
To Middelburg the place,
With the Ship Prins Hendrik ree,
In the Year of thirty-nine,
And sixteen hundred plus,
Written the tenth of March.

label: work_sea
translation:
Hear, sailors all together,
Who sail by the sea,
Whether you face storm or distress,
God will help you on a course:
For many a ship's mate,
Must risk their lives,
Alone on the sea's swell,
By nights and by days.
The sea, by God's will,
Devours so many men,
Who all together from the fish
Are then swallowed:
But read! What depends on that,
Where our bodies sail,
If God will then
Eternally preserve the soul.
What sorrow the seaman suffers,
By nights and by days,
When he sees death before his eyes,
And cannot escape it,
From storm and great thunder,
And the power of the waves,
That brings us thus all the more
Into fear and great distress.
Also great violence and adversity,
The seaman bravely suffers,
From Turks, ravaging pirates fierce,
They are attacked:
And many more enemies,
Who seek to harm,
Who sail by the sea
May well think of this.
Even when they are driven aground
By storms,
Very hastily they hew the masts,
Then cut overboard:
Then they see death again,
Before their eyes with dread,
Sometimes the sorrow is great,
That the seaman must bear.
God helps them then out of distress,
Who call to Him fervently,
He does not want the sinner's death,
But that he repents:
For by His strong hand,
He revives the man again,
And brings them again to land
In a desired haven.
When they come ashore well,
Their first work is,
Immediately in brandy,
But little in church:
They quickly forget
That the Lord has blessed them,
Faith helped by His grace,
Out of fear and great dread.
When they get their money in hand,
It is quickly spent,
They buy there land or sand,
It is for women and the men,
As if he finds it somewhere,
So he goes to dominate:
That one so sourly wins,
One sees so easily consumed
With girls of a light nature,
They go wild,
They must again set sail
When the money is spent,
It is no great honor to them,
Nor less for the women,
For they according to God's teachings
Must keep themselves chaste.
But what can I speak of
When one sees much display
Daily along the streets going,
Of daughters old and young:
In Holland everywhere
You see them boasting,
It comes from misfortune,
They are no daughters with honor.
The Zeeland girls, what a talk,
They also know very well,
By night dancing along the street,
All with a ship's mate:
And made great noise
With the young men wanting to know,
They are daughters of this place,
Or I have forgotten,
But is it not a foolish appearance,
Of these daughters, hear?
By night dancing over the street,
The player that goes before:
It is no great honor to them,
They can easily bear it,
For in East India afar
It is spoken of,
The young men regard it as no evil,
They say, it is pleasure,
To dance sometimes along the street,
All with a Venus creature:
But if the scripture relates,
It is no honor to them,
That they their fair money
So recklessly consume.
These young men and daughters fair,
They are very well known,
They live near the Turf quay,
Or somewhere nearby:
Because they are honest,
So I will not blame them here,
For if the thread is not fine,
One would make it rougher.
You young men hear with good counsel,
Keep measure in drinking:
For drunkenness is (so they say),
The mother of evil:
Live free according to God's teaching,
Wish to hate bad company:
Then God the Lord,
Will let you sail in peace.
Farewell sailor, hear my admonition,
When you are in distress:
Call God the Lord from the heart,
Confess your great sins:
For he who sincerely
Repents of his sins,
Will be by God the Lord,
Healed of his wounds.
When this new song was first made,
We sailed over the sea:
From East India to the Fatherland,
To Middelburg the place:
With the ship of Prince Hendrick's squadron,
In the year thirty-nine,
And sixteen hundred more,
Written on the tenth of March.

label: work_sea
translation:
Who wants to hear a New Song
Exalted from our Fishermen,
Listen well to my message,
Those who venture on the North Sea,
Without fear or trembling,
With brave courage,
And stake their lives,
In God's keeping,
On the open Sea,
To do each according to his diligence
And very gladly,
Return home.
Friends, our Ship is already prepared,
If God will but give a good Wind,
We sail all guided by God,
Uplifted in our trade,
To live together,
Holy according to God's Word,
As it may please,
Always forward and onward,
To God's delight
With courage always,
To do our diligence,
Our days of life.
Farewell now, dear Friends,
Who inhabit our City,
We commend you all to God,
Young and old People,
As we now part,
For a short time,
May the Lord lead you,
And bless you with health;
Farewell Father and
Mother,
Sisters and Brothers all,
Trust indeed,
In God our Keeper.
We sail all in good spirits,
With a good Wind uplifted,
We hope in God's keeping,
To set out for that purpose,
Like the honorable Apostles,
Together following Christ's words,
To Fish on the Sea,
As they have done,
According to Matthew's teaching,
Casting the Net to the right side,
With Peter joyfully,
On the Word of the Lord.
When we come to the northern Sand,
We shoot our Herring-net,
We then put it in God's hand,
To enjoy the Herring,
Listen to my testimony,
We get a good Catch,
We shoot with pleasure,
Our Corner-net quickly,
Without delay,
Each doing his diligence,
With glad courage,
To our liking.
If we get a good catch of Fish,
Then we rejoice,
We thank the Lord for sure,
And put pressure aside,
Without long hesitation,
If they then drop anchor,
Then we prepare ourselves,
With a very cheerful heart,
To sleep a while,
And that does not last very long,
We move along,
Again with the Boys.
Thus we continue our Trade,
Until the Voyage ends,
We live according to God's Holy Word,
May He send us His blessing,
But if we do otherwise,
God will withdraw His hand,
And His gentle blessing,
From us for
He will reveal
Whoever acts against His will,
It is not good,
To sail this way.
If we get Storm and also Thunder,
As often happens,
Protect us then,
O good Lord:
That no one regrets it,
In the great Sea distress,
Punish, the Waves ward off,
So that we do not as dead,
Discouraged Hearts stand,
But strengthen us,
As your good Disciples,
In the Sea-flood
They could endure.
When God then calms the Waves again,
And brings good Weather,
Very soon we take our turn,
With joy taken out
To our Trade once more,
With a peaceful life,
Until we return,
Again to reckon,
To our Friends in the Dutch valley,
With joyful cheer,
Come together.
When we then come ashore again,
With abundant blessing,
Everyone offers us their hand,
Speak kindheartedly,
Be welcome Friend with peace,
And honorable Ladies,
Do the same likewise,
To their Husbands truly,
The kind Parents,
To their Children at this time
Welcome joyfully,
They quickly shout.
Farewell then all you Fishermen together,
Who cultivate the North Sea,
I commend you in God's name,
Always trust in Him,
He will help you well,
Out of every heavy need,
Stay the Flood surely,
Although He makes a great clamor,
Without wavering
Always firmly hold to His Doctrine,
Then the Lord sends
His rich blessing.
Who first sang us this little Song,
It was a young man done,
Out of love for many young Fishermen,
He went to present this,
Sail well now uplifted,
You all Fishermen,
On the North Sea written,
I tell you truly,
The Year Sixteen Hundred,
And six times ten capable,
Farewell all together,
None excluded.

label: work_sea
translation:
Wonderful are the works of the Lord!  
That He shows on Sea or Land:  
To observe in every way,  
As great powers of His hand:  
One sails away with great joy,  
To Java's fatherland coast,  
And suffers through misfortune in many pains,  
That many Widows and Orphans are disturbed.  
I have heard it from the Crand,  
The sixteenth of this month;  
It has seemed sad to me!  
To my understanding where tears fall,  
And keeps his eyes on Heaven,  
With much dejection:  
God's wonders are many in the swarm,  
That He works out in ways and time.  
A lightning flame that can hurt much,  
Set Pylswaert flying in the fire,  
Many hundreds of souls and companions;  
Are by that sad flame lost,  
The powder that everyone must watch out for:  
Brought Captain Eggers into distress,  
His helmsman struck by the lightning's force,  
And they are both dead.  
Of one hundred thirty-three souls;  
Are alas consumed by the flame!  
The great danger with mast and keel;  
Is all changed to dust and ash,  
The engineer is still alive,  
Second helmsman and assistant,  
Ship-navigator and ship's mate nearby,  
Kept life amid misery.  
Remarkable is God's work exalted,  
While the engineer has still been saved!  
And driven onto a plank,  
And set on the solid land,  
I hope he thanks Heaven for it,  
Who mercifully has guarded him,  
That he was saved by wood and boards,  
Always doing the Lord's will.  
Constable, quartermaster both,  
Two butlers, one butcher's mate:  
And boatswain's mate are outside hurts,  
Remained in healthy state;  
And twice seventeen and even  
Sailors besides this case,  
And one soldier is still alive  
Preserved, and lands on Java’s shore.  
The damage that the companies,  
Thereby have suffered is great,  
But widows and orphans are in sorrow,  
And deprived of their support!  
Who knows how it is complaining,  
And full of care and grief!  
Great is the Lord's pleasure,  
When everyone looks at his works.  
From such sad misfortunes,  
Thus the seaman had to learn:  
That God is angry at us all,  
Can strike us with fire or lightning,  
The ship Pylswaert reminds us,  
Of God's feared Almighty power;  
Who can swiftly change our life,  
And bring it down by His might.  
Obdam, an admiral before this,  
In the defense against the enemy's power,  
Also had the misfortune, as we read:  
That he was killed by the powder,  
His ship exploded in the air,  
The powder is a great plague,  
That hero perished with young and old;  
And rests with one in The Hague.  
Does God not have people in His hands,  
The lightning, thunder, and storm!  
Whenever He wills, it can all burn,  
No one resists the Supreme Lord:  
He plays the scenes of punishment on Earth,  
And can melt it in one glow,  
And destroy four continents,  
Due to mankind's evil wickedness.  
Teach men who build the sea,  
That God is the helmsman and the rudder,  
He can keep you together in pressure!  
And if your need returns once more:  
Think of His omnipotence!  
Keep His omniscience in sight,  
And if your need returns once more:  
He reigns on the earthly round below,  
As well as at the heavenly bow.  
All those who sail the great sea,  
And steer keels from Neyphtuyn,  
He wants to keep them safely sailing;  
And strengthen their life's crown,  
So that after long wandering they come,  
And have seen wonders at sea;  
Once anchor after wind and storms,  
In the homeland on good anchorage.  
As high as lower officers,  
Who sail on wooden keels,  
Each strives to steer his duties,  
That he acts bravely and calmly;  
The ship's work may be performed with pleasure,  
And keep God steadily in sight,  
Beware of lightning flashes,  
And grant you the good from above.

label: work_sea
translation:
Oh, what has happened to me,
On my last journey that I took,
Every person would shudder at it,
If I shared the story with him:
They had barely set sail,
Three days out at sea,
When a storm on the waves,
Broke the great mast in two;
The ship was rising, the ship was falling;
Finally it got such a blow,
That it ran aground on a rock,
And broke into a thousand pieces.
Then it was time for men to save themselves,
Swimming was the very best:
I had to move like a duck,
Disturbed in its nest:
But when I was swimming in the water,
Ah, I will never forget,
That almost a shark with its snout,
Bit me in the arm:
I swam all the way to the shore,
And thus escaped danger;
Soon I climbed ashore,
And walked on up to here.
What shall I do now,
Wet and cold in this state,
Sickly and bruised inside:
Ah, how do I get off this street:
If the innkeeper now would vouch,
And tap on credit,
Then I would be free of worries,
And I think of no sorrow;
Although I have no money for this,
I have spent my coins there,
I bet he will not be so angry,
That he won’t give credit now.
I have not yet lost courage:
That spirit is alive,
Said the innkeeper might hear it,
Who certainly sang the babok song,
If you want to leave here right away,
Or I will cover you on your lap,
With a broomstick,
So you will look like no human:
There is the woman who is much softer,
She has blood running from my nose,
I bet she calls me soon behind,
There she already gives the signal.
Well friend, what is your desire,
Well friend, what is your desire,
Make me say it quickly;
Because the people inside are waiting for me there,
Also I don’t have much time, my father,
Why do you look so sad and sour,
Come, just tell me.
Well, how now Miss Griet,
Well, how now Miss Griet,
Have you forgotten me so,
Tell me honestly, don’t you know me,
I am your old customer Piet,
Who spent and lent a lot of money here.
You, Piet, I have never seen,
You, Piet, I have never seen,
So poor and so poor;
I praise your money and goods,
Wasted, you’ll be punished if you do more,
Therefore just go from here quietly,
You naked louse.
Well Griet, what talk is that,
Well Griet, what talk is that,
Is it serious or is it madness:
I say that I do not want to go from here,
Before I speak your man or boy,
Go tap a jug of old beer immediately,
And call her here.
Well scoundrel, now I don’t want to,
Well scoundrel, now I don’t want to,
Call my man or boy,
You will not come into my house,
I see you have neither coin nor cross,
Therefore make no noise here:
Away naked louse.
Don’t be so hasty,
Don’t be so hasty,
I will tell you my story:
But she has gone on very angrily,
And left me standing here on the street;
But who comes there in rich attire,
Ah, it is my mate.
His mate gives him money, and they go together
To the inn, where Piet was then welcome,
And sang with a glass of wine the conclusion.
Let us be merry,
With the girls and the fresh wine;
Hey, quickly grab the glass,
Drink cheerfully, let the wine go down,
Come pour again,
And let us toast with joy in mind.

label: work_sea
translation:
What joy and glory does one now see,
In our dear Dutch valley,
It gives us again victory,
In the year sixty-six already:
A fine fleet of 24 ships,
Heaven protects them from storm and thunderclap,
That with joy Florizant,
They safely return to the fatherland.
Who will be our commander,
Mr. Marcus Tetting very capable,
He commands the ship Lapienenburg very well,
It is a joy for us together,
Captain Ian van Daelen comes many miles,
With Keukenhof sailing through the sea,
Tulpenburg, with Pieter Klement, sailed along,
All three with glory for Amsterdam.
For Zeeland one can see arriving,
The ship Zuydbeveland quite capable,
Captain Huybert van der Kneu, without hesitation,
God is their guide together,
The Admiral de Ruyter very pleased in spirit,
Sails with joy into Zeeland,
God is their leader at sea,
That they arrive safely at a good anchorage.
Captain Ian Moorland is seen coming for Delft,
With the ship Schravezande, very capable,
Who fears no storm or wind,
God is their guide together,
The Lady Elisabeth shows favor to the wind,
Captain Pieter Beugels arrives for Rotterdam,
Thus the fleet follows one after another,
God is their guide on every route.
The ship De Barrezande is seen approaching powerfully,
With full vigor through the sea,
With Captain Bartels for the Chamber of Enkhuizen,
God protects them at a good anchorage;
Captain Wolhart Groenweg comes many thousand miles;
For Amsterdam, not by sailing through the sea,
With the ship Schoonsigt very capable,
God is their guide together.
Zeeland also receives the joy to hear,
Of the ship Neijenburg as it is said,
Which was almost lost due to mutiny,
Captain Christoffel Hars now sets it right,
One sees that God's hand wishes to preserve,
Though the mutiny was on the wild waves,
The captain with many crew who sided,
Nevertheless, the ship remained intact.
From China one sees with joy coming,
Captain Sybrantse Flout,
With the ship Bossenhoven without hesitation,
Sailors all trusting in God,
The ship Damsigt is seen approaching with joy,
All the way from Ceylon without hesitation:
With Captain Lodewyk Tomazyn all through the sea,
God protects all at a good anchorage.
The Barbara Theodora is seen coming with joy,
From Ceylon with great eagerness,
With Captain Albert Aelders God grants a good rest,
The Lymuyde is seen coming with joy,
From Ceylon without hesitation,
With Captain Antonie de Iong very capable,
God is their guide all together.
For Amsterdam one sees with joy arriving,
The ship Scholtenborg with great joy,
With Captain Iacob Meijer over Bengal,
Which many rejoice in,
Leendert van de Linde is seen favoring the wind,
That he comes into Zeeland from Ceylon now:
With the ship Kattendyk,
It is a joy for young, old, poor and rich.
For Delft one sees with joy coming,
The ship Blyswyk very capable,
Over Bengal without hesitation,
Captain Klaes Stuyver pleasantly,
Captain Klaes Aerens sails eagerly ahead,
Over Bengal for the Chamber of Hoorn,
With the ship Schage well manned,
God assists all in need.
Uredenhof is seen eagerly favoring the wind,
Comes from China with great splendor,
With Captain Willem van Braem will win the battle,
His people work day and night,
Noort-Beveland seen carrying the load,
All the way from China with great pleasure:
With Captain Simon Both all through the sea,
God protects all at a good anchorage.
The ship De Pallas is seen coming with joy,
From China with great eagerness,
With Captain Iacob Boekhout without hesitation,
To bring it to safe rest.
The ship Walenburg is also seen coming with joy;
All the way from China without hesitation,
With Captain Erasmus de Uries very capable:
God is their guide altogether.
At last I want to conclude my poem,
God is their assistance in need,
I will not leave out the ship Lycogton,
Bringing us the happiness of the fleet,
With Captain Daniel Nieuman for the Chamber of Hoorn,
It sounded many lasses and sweethearts in the ears:
And also many whores from the lane;
Who with the sailors go swinging.

label: work_sea
translation:
Wonderful are the Lord’s works,  
Which He shows on Sea or Land:  
To note in every way,  
As great powers of His hand:  
One sets off with great joy,  
To Java’s fatherland coast,  
And suffers through misfortune in much pain,  
That many Widows and Orphans are disturbed.  
I have heard it from the News,  
The sixteenth of this Month:  
It struck me sadly!  
For my understanding tears fell,  
And kept its eyes toward Heaven,  
With much dejection:  
God’s wonders are many among the throng,  
Which He works out in wisdom and time.  

A lightning flame that can strike hard  
Set Pylswaert flying into the fire,  
Many hundreds of souls and companions;  
Are by that sorrowful flame lost.  
The powder, before which all must be cautious:  
Brought Skipper Eggers into distress,  
His Helmsman struck by lightning’s force,  
And both are dead.  
Of one hundred eighty-three souls;  
Are by the flame alas consumed!  
The great Danger with Masts and Keels;  
Is all turned into horror and ash,  
The Engineer is still alive,  
The First Mate and Assistants,  
Boatswain and Boatswain's Mate beside,  
Endured life through misery.  
Remarkable is God’s work exalted  
While the Engineer is yet saved!  
And by a Table driven forward,  
And set on the solid Land.  
I hope he thanks Heaven for that  
Who has graciously preserved him,  
That he, saved by Wood and Planks,  
Always does the Lord’s will.  
Constable, Quartermaster both,  
Two Butlers, one Butler’s Mate:  
And Boatswain’s Mate outside suffering  
Remained in healthy state;  
And twice seventeen even  
Sailors outside of this case,  
And one Soldier still alive  
Preserved, and lands at Java’s Shore.  
The damage that the Companies  
Thereby suffered is great,  

But Widows and Orphans are in sorrow  
And deprived of their support!  
Who knows how it is to lament,  
And full of grief and sorrow!  
Fierce is the Heart’s pleasure,  
When each looks upon his Works.  
From such sad accidents,  
Thus the Seaman had to learn:  
That God is angry with us all:  
He can strike us with Fire or Lightning.  
The ship Pylswaerd makes us think  
Of God’s feared Supreme Power.  
Who swiftly can change our life,  
And bring it under His might.  
Obdam an Admiral before this,  
In resisting the Enemy’s power,  
Also had the misfortune as we read:  
That he was killed by the powder  
His Ship has blown up into the Air,  
The Powder that is a great plague,  
That hero perished with old and young;  
And rests with them in The Hague.  
Does God not have men in His hands  
The Lightning, thunder and storm!  
When He wills, He can burn it all,  
No one resists the Supreme Lord:  
He plays on earth the scenes of Punishment,  
And can melt it into a glow,  
And cause four continents to perish,  
Because of mankind’s evil temper.  
Teach men who navigate the Sea,  
That God is the Helmsman and the Rudder,  
He can keep you safe in distress!  
And if your need arises again:  

Then think of His Almightiness!  
Have His Omniscience in view,  
Reigns on the terrestrial sphere below,  
As well as on the Heavenly arch.  
All who sail the great Sea,  
And steer keels of Neptune,  
He wishes to keep safe the voyage;  
And strengthen their crown of Life.  
So that after long wandering they come,  
And have seen wonders at Sea;  
Once anchor after Wind and Storms,  
In the Fatherland on good roadstead.  
As high as lesser Officers,  
Who sail on wooden keels,  
Each strives to steer his office,  
That he bravely and calmly;  
May perform the ship’s work with pleasure  
And keep God steadily in sight,  
Beware of lightning strikes,  
And grant you good from above.

label: work_sea
translation:
Listen Annaatje, I will tell a story,
What I recently have heard,
England will get the devil,
If it disrupts the Sea voyage again,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

2. I now have my pocket full of ducats,
Henrikaters on my shoe,
I will no longer just talk.
I will make the journey with joy,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

3. It is said now that the Danes,
With the Russians and the Swedes,
United under our flag,
And are ready to support.
And our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

4. If there is no Tromp alive,
No Rider known anymore,
Our courage will never fail us,
Water is our element,
Where our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

5. Our naval power still has heroes,
Who wear sailor's pride.
Those who gave their lives for the country,
Daedel weighed his head,
And our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

6. Would Hartsink and Kingsbergen,
From the English brood,
Allow themselves to be taunted unpunished,
No, that does not show heroic courage.
Where our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

7. Yes, I could name more,
Whoever it suited,
Many a brave sailor would boast,
If only there was occasion,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

8. It will no longer happen as last time,
That despite the convoy,
Our ships are interfered with,
And they make themselves prey to her,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

9. Was it really no shame,
That a skipper from this city,
Was bound at feet and hands,
And was Britted for his backside,
That’s over now, because the Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

10. Our flags will wave,
For the dear Fatherland,
Our rooster will crow the king,
To the terror of England,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

11. What prudence of those gentlemen,
Worth being loved by everyone,
Who govern our Amsterdam,
Where the seaman gains tribute,
Therefore our Admiral,
Is also neutral with city and country.

12. If one might tell you once,
That they had done something wrong,
To stir the country,
There is no harm done there,
Because you see our Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

13. Would one still seek unrest,
In a time of prosperity,
Whoever did that deserves to be cursed,
And that never suits Dutch blood,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

14. That godless fellows,
Blinded by English gold,
Want to close our prosperity,
See that Unity prevails,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

15. Now we are truly Lords,
Now is the first a beautiful time,
I will treat you to rice and wine,
Because my heart is truly glad,
Where our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

16. Come Annaatje, let us drink,
Do you want red or do you want clear,
Let us cheerfully clink the glass,
To the well-being of the Prince,
Because that brave Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

17. Now to the well-being of the States,
Of the States of our Country,
Who never abandon us in distress,
And govern with wisdom,
And who with our Admiral,
Are neutral with city and country.

18. Amsterdam must also have a glass,
To its zeal and policy.
Because of that I am such a boss,
Don't you think so too, dear girl,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

19. Look girl, I have received,
Forty guilders in hand,
Who would now not desire,
To serve for the country,
Where our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

20. What do we see, the dear girls,
Now with friendly little eyes.
Now I can take as many trips,
As I want to roam,
Because our Supreme Admiral
Is neutral with city and country.

21. I will give you a few ducats,
Hear you, dear girl, as a tip,
That’s just a gentleman’s life,
I will calmly go courting with you,
Because our Supreme Admiral,
Is neutral with city and country.

label: work_sea
translation:
Adi my wife; come, be courageous.
I am calmly going to sea;
And Piet goes, (he is a good boy)
Also cheerfully with us.
Come cheerfully, then climb into the boat,
And bring me to the big Ship...
What nonsense! I am not going to my death;
You have a strange notion!
I truly do not know why you are crying.
Go, you have no sense!
If death is my fate,
Then I will also die on land.
I certainly have no drowned body!
I am not discouraged.
I have always been with you, my wife,
In honor and virtue.
I am not anxious about the trip;
Let Klaas be embarrassed;
He who loves Tryntje and the wine a lot;
That uncouth swine.
Am I not as fresh as a chicken?
By Joost, I am a man
As strong as any sea lion;
Thank God, I know nothing of it.

I know nothing of cold or heat;
Send me around the world;
Whoever sees me sick or sighing,
I am always healthy:
There you have Piet, yes look at him;
How well that cap suits him!
He dares to go to Janstinje;
He does not fear the Plas...
Always complaining again! that behavior
Really annoys me:
There is the bottle, come, drink, Trui!
What's wrong, are you crazy?

I am not leaving the country because of my vice,
I myself desired it:
Sailing, yes that is my way!
I have not learned otherwise.
I sailed from my eighth year;
My father always said:
‘My boys must go to sea;
That way I lose them all;
That way they earn their own bread,
Just as I have done’.
Come, come my wife, I have no worries,
It will all go splendidly.
I do not curse or swear for pleasure,
You have never heard that,
Though I speak, that’s the custom here,
Sometimes a sailor’s word,
And you really are not delicate either;
But good for your sake.
Pour again; honestly, that wine
Is just to my taste.
Now another scoop. Listen, Piet; Do you like red Bay?
Oh I see already, you don’t like it;
Well boy, that is fine!

The Comforter of the sick on our ship
Is also a lively guy;
He is quite understanding,
Who watches over his things.
He is no hypocrite,
Who drinks and prays alternately;
He takes his duties eagerly
When he is with us.
‘He whom God protects is well protected’;
So said the good man;
Even if a storm rages, I remain calm
While God can help us.

Now, after maybe five years,
Oh they pass quickly!
Will you see us both again,
Then it is, ‘welcome’.
Then I will come with a big bundle
Of thyme again to you;
Who knows what you will then receive
Of nettle cloth and silk!
Of chintz, fine as hair,
And costly tea;
And many other fine things;
Listen wife, I will bring everything.
Now, give my regards
To everyone who asks for me;
To Boudewyn, to Leen, and Kris,
And a kiss for our Aagt;
The child knows little of his father...
Now wife, do not cry,
Because your husband goes to India;
It is already done, after all?
It is best that I remain a sailor,
What am I doing ashore?
Kiss my mother too, will you wife?
She will comfort you well.

Live cheerfully, oh that’s my pleasure!
I write a letter from the Cape.
And if I come, after three or four years,
To the dear Fatherland,
Who knows if you then recognize Piet;
At sea I grow like a cabbage.
Come, Jasper, come, brave like a man,
Once more on your fiddle:
‘And that goes to Batavia;
All the way to the East Indies.
And yes, yes; and yes yes,
I am not tired of sailing yet’.

You have really done well, comrade!
See, the bottle is empty;
But I want no more drops,
Even if I got them...
The dizziness, my wife! the wind changes.
Did a tear escape me there?
That’s strange. How! does the man become a child?
My dear wife, I have to go...
May the good God help you and me,
And give us salvation and peace!
Adi my wife, there we sail.
Hooray! Hooray! Hooray!

label: work_sea
translation:
Come, swift young lads,
Sailors, come aboard.
We are about to set out as privateers;
There beats the drum! Onward, onward.
The little ship is already seaworthy;
It is right before the wind and tide.
Are you still worthy of the name
Of Vlissingers, follow me!

The letters have arrived,
Look there, now we go aboard;
Now there is no shirking,
That we seize you like pirates.
Now we will show those British
Who we really are;
It will not stay like this!
Here’s a toast of wine for everyone.

To the health of the Lords of the Land,
And the Admiral-in-Chief;
May they rule us for a long time!..
I bring it to you once again.
The wine gives fire and life!
So we set sail.
Now let the ship’s bow vibrate freely
With a repeated Huzzah!

But when we get prizes,
And look, there we go for it,
We will not keep quiet about it;
Live in communion with the loot.
Treat the shipmates
As sailors should:
I forbid you to torment them,
And if you do; stand firm!

If anyone dares to exact revenge
On Scots or Englishmen,
I will speak to them
In a way they will remember; I can.
I want to repay you,
See there, there is my hand:
No cruelty adds to heroes;
He who fights, fights for his country.

Listen, I will live with you
As brothers; do not forget
That to me was given
The highest command.
The advance payment has been received,
You were satisfied with that,
It was after all your desire?
You admitted that yourself.

Cursing, swearing, and roaring,
I also dislike;
He must learn to give that up,
Who wants to stand with me.
Whoever loses themselves in drink,
Or is of bad nature,
Behold, this rope with knots
Is kept here for them.

Be cheerful, merry, jocular,
Sing so it resounds; that’s well.
Be active, orderly, lively,
This suits a shipmate.
And if it comes to fighting,
Look there, this is the man
Who will judge you,
If you get the worse of it.

You will not lack courage
When it comes to striking:
I do not want to speak a word of it;
I see you as men.
But now that we speak of it,
In round sailor’s speech:
Will you not abandon me?
What do you all say?
Captain! I speak for all:
Whatever we encounter on the voyage,
Wherever our fate may fall,
We will shed all our blood
With you, if necessary;
We are ready for that.
Never want to dishonor our honor:
Our word, see, that is our oath.

And are we rough men?
It is less in deed than appearance.
See there, Captain, we wish
To be at the dance already.
The blood boils in our veins
Remembering the insult...
Those pirates, those barbarians!
Now they get their due!

See there, there is mate Steven,
He sailed for Amsterdam;
Is that ever to be forgiven,
They beat him half senseless.
They bound him with ropes
To the leg; one can
Clearly see the bruises;
Who ever heard of that!

Here, Kees, the sweet boy,
Also knows that scum of a people;
They forced him,
Threatened with a bare dagger;
Because he did not want to talk,
And the boy knew no evil:
“You can cut me into pieces,
But I know of no betrayal.”

So the lad let himself be heard:
Isn’t that true, my curly-haired one?
He was born for the sea,
And goes to the ship like a madman.
There is much to expect from him.
He has a fine understanding.
If he keeps his duty,
He will get a ship someday.
Now mates, I am satisfied,
You speak as sailors should.
And Kees I take today
With me. Come, sailor guest,
I see what I can make of you;
The Rider had to watch the cabin first, like you;
And what a man he became!

label: work_sea
translation:
There, the journey is done;
The Prize costs us dearly.
It went terribly;
It was nothing but blood, and fire,
And smoke, and dreadful shouting:
What a guest we became!
But just so you know, the brave sailor
Also took some hits!
The Captain led us constantly,
In the heat of the fight,
We all flew in his footsteps;
Just as the man says.
Everything really went well:
And if it had to be this way,
Not a day were we at sea,
When look, a Brigantine,
Came boldly towards us;
How happy we were then!
Although it gave us full force,
We held firm;
And gave back as well as we could.
The bullets whizzed by;
They beat fiercely on the drum
By them, and on our board.

What did we have standing?
Our rudder was theirs.
We shouted: "Long live the Fatherland,
The Devil take the Brit."
They shouted nothing but: dam you, dam!
Dutch Devils! bless the King.
And I hardly know how it happened
That I sustained a wound,
Somewhere at the side of my neck;
The blood dripped long on my vest:
Well, tin box, how faint I grew!
And Flipmaat got the rest.

He fell dead as a doornail, that good fellow.
Well, that's how it was! See there,
His bravery is known to us,
It troubled us all:
But, come, he died on the bed of honor,
As the proverb goes:
A brave Sailor asks for no more.
Once we all must get out.
But it worries me terribly about his wife;
Who knows how she suffers!
There she sits with a child four or five;
She has lost her husband after all.

We still have many wounded;
But only Flip is dead.
The Brigantine got its share
Of bullets, powder, and scrap.
Then we all boarded,
Like Lions at board;
My wound did not stop me there,
I just kept on.
They then risked one chance,
We became somewhat wiser!
And so we ended that dance:
"Hooray! The ship is Prize."
We were in no good mood;
We received bad news:
How gladly would we have fought
In the hold again over news!
I gave one such a good hit,
He crashed against the deck.
And if mate Hein had not prevented it,
I would have given him more on his face!
What did he have
To curse at a sailor?
Is fighting not free for us too?
Look, I was furious angry.

Then the Captain gave a new order
That no one should harm them:
The man undoubtedly meant well,
Although I regretted it at the time.
But, while I walk up and down,
And fly front and back,
I see a Pea Coat in the crowd,
Who, if I am not mistaken,
Is my old faithful schoolmate.
And it was just right;
I called to him: "Were you there, Kris?
That went well."

"For sure!" he shouted, "now that's good,
The Prize is well deserved;
The Zeeland privateer fought fiercely!"
"But what are you doing here, friend?"
I asked again. "Say, what happened?
You were such a good man;
But if you side with the Englishman,
Then just stay where you are."
"Hey, do you dare ask me that again?"
He shouted; "I am enlightened!
But know, I am a man of honor,
I did nothing against duty."

Then I immediately gave him my hand,
We took a sip together:
And about the dear Fatherland,
He was very busy with.
Yes, yes, it was still the same Cris
As over a year and a day;
That I saw clearly and surely.
I reported to the Captain,
That I had found an old mate here again;
Who showed me a happy face,
And immediately he got something.
The Brigantine is good Prize.
We share that too.
How our flags flutter,
How the Sea roars, how it waves!
How pleasant it is all around here;
What heavenly weather!
How the big drum now rolls
To honor the boatswain!
How well organized and clever it is here!
What a Captain we have!
What good luck we get here!
How clean the air is here!

There I see Vlissingen’s towers already.
Ha! It goes like a flash.
What Women stand on the shore;
You know how Women are.
Curious! well, it can happen,
Everyone waits for their own at home,
Or follows a Neighbor Woman for the gossip.
What noise they make!
They wave us with cloths and hats,
And all are equally happy.
Come, come, that's just how it is...
I see children there too.

They point to the Brigantine,
And hope for much Booty.
Whether we are also welcome in the evening!
Even if it is not completely safe;
That did not say little sand, come, a pirate knows little about that.
It still pains me about Flipmaat,
He was such a good man.
The wife will get her share, that’s true,
And also pulls widow’s money;
But I don’t know, it was such a nice couple:
Who will tell her?

Oh Vlissingen! Renowned since ancient times,
For free Piracy,
Isn’t that cleared up by us?
We will come to you quickly;
But soon go back to Sea,
As soon as it suits us.
And if we bring no Prizes,
Then it goes beyond guess...
Now we fire, to honor the City,
Once more with all the Cannon...
There we get the Salvo back,
As beautiful as it could be had.

label: work_sea
translation:
1  
Here come East Indies sailors already,  
I must tell you something,  
How recently in this situation,  
I have become so sorrowful,  
From the Cape of Good Hope,  
With our six ships fully loaded,  
We were to sail home,  
Twenty-one hundred miles.

2  
We sailed quietly onward,  
At the island of Saint Helena;  
In the sweet season arriving,  
Everything appeared to us,  
First past the wind, so to starboard,  
And it seemed joyfully,  
We passed the line,  
Without anyone harming us.

3  
From there we sailed on,  
To the coral meadows,  
In the South American Sea,  
Who would not rejoice,  
But that joy did not last long,  
For shortly thereafter,  
They quickly changed their course,  
Into sadness and misery.

4  
We captured a Portuguese  
Who informed us,  
How Holland and England,  
Were fighting together,  
But he did not disturb us,  
Until corpses and flowers were heard,  
There we sailed very cheerfully,  
That made us restless.

5  
Because of a lower wall,  
At night we began to turn,  
In the morning in this situation  
An English ship came to chase us,  
It was morning around mealtime  
When he came to pursue us diligently,  
With six or seven glasses,  
Which amazed us.

6  
He shouted, “Where do you sail from?”  
Our captain spoke very nobly,  
“We have left the Cape,  
With our six ships ready.”  
Then the enemy immediately answered,  
“Strike your flag or I shoot you in the ground,”  
“If you do not strike your flag,”  
He shot to prove it.

7  
Our brass and our small arms  
Were all shot to pieces,  
So that in haste,  
We could not increase our help.  
We were the last of the fleet  
When he immediately shot us through,  
And attacked us,  
So that the water overwhelmed us.

8  
Instead of our commander,  
Coming to our aid,  
He made more sail to the side,  
Because he was in a hurry,  
But two ships of the fleet,  
Also shot bravely at him,  
But one was afraid,  
That one was not praised.

9  
But when he saw that our support,  
Of ships increased,  
Then he immediately steered off,  
And fired no more shots,  
But when he was close to the fleet,  
Which upset us the most,  
He dared not trust,  
It was to keep the advantage.

10  
Then he left us,  
To the praise of these ships,  
We saw that he signaled,  
As much as we understood,  
His boat went to set outside board,  
Which we saw and heard,  
Our commander wanted to speak,  
As it became clear.

11  
On starboard side they came aboard,  
We were very angry,  
Then the rogue very disturbed,  
Spoke to our commander,  
“You will immediately give me,  
One of your ships,  
Otherwise I will rejoice,  
I must fight you tomorrow.”

12  
And take you as my prize,  
Our commander, trembling,  
Was very upset and wanted in his way,  
To have him presented,  
And immediately went to him,  
Because the Englishman was about to leave,  
He gave him a box of tea,  
Full of anxiety and trembling.

13  
Our little ship sank every moment,  
The pumps could no longer help,  
We sent signals for support,  
And they helped us,  
The boats came aboard us,  
Our ship was going under,  
They made us happy,  
That they nevertheless freed us.

14  
Then we did our best there,  
To get off board,  
No one wanted to be last,  
We feared the currents,  
Then we were immediately divided,  
Around the Concordia was destroyed,  
On remaining keels,  
Where he took us aboard.

15  
Everyone stayed there on board,  
And one was shot dead,  
During the night we continued our course  
To Cadiz undaunted,  
But shortly thereafter,  
We met a Spanish fleet there,  
We passed Cape Vincent,  
And there they confiscated from us.

16  
Then we were all happy,  
And no one feared anymore,  
Immediately we were told,  
Where we had to be,  
The loot came aboard,  
And we sailed quietly on,  
To Cadiz on the roadstead,  
Where we lay in peace.

17  
But when we get away from here,  
I cannot describe,  
I expect to leave soon,  
I hope God stays with us.  
Furthermore, I part from you,  
And now make my decision here,  
My pen no longer wants to write,  
I will stop here.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
What is His Majesty of Heaven,  
Just in His deeds,  
Who punishes rebellion in its time,  
To all those who dare  
To undertake their boldness for evil;  
And who rise against His Power,  
Which He has given in this life:  
To the Authority.  

2.  
To carry ourselves respectfully  
According to their high counsel;  
And never oppose the helm of state,  
But to carry out their will diligently,  
Before the sword has trained them;  
Out of rage from its sheath:  
In anger spreading upon us.  

3.  
As recently happened on the land's ship Venus,  
To mutineers who surely,  
Regretted their crimes;  
Which they had committed boldly,  
In large numbers undertaking,  
Threatening their souls  
With powder, as if to destroy.  

4.  
And causing the ship to explode in the air!  
If the tyranny,  
Of their vain desire!  
Did not go according to their wish,  
Which with much gentleness,  
After a very long time  
They continually confessed,  
Was soon quickly breached.  

5.  
His Highness then gave the order  
To attack them;  
Fiercely besieged a war council:  
Of captains and lords,  
Of the High Admiralty;  
So that through wise policy,  
By their council's decisions,  
These atrocities could be stopped.  

6.  
Kinsbergen, a brave knight,  
In all matters of sea power,  
Denied their intentions,  
By his very diligent watchfulness,  
Which he did day and night,  
And was very close upon them,  
To compel by his power  
If they committed anything.  

7.  
Everyone performed this brave deed,  
Clearly as if shaking,  
So that all the people rose,  
In fear that they also might suffer:  
This hero with his ship's crew;  
If this evil turned worse,  
Their plan would come  
Which did not deter them at all.  

8.  
Shortly thereafter, a writer was sent  
By the war council;  
To the rebels; who however wicked,  
Stood in their orders,  
Were received with leniency,  
And were courageously read a letter from His Highness,  
Which brought them to fear.  

9.  
In which His Highness promised them:  
To show his favor,  
If their supreme leader  
Would respectfully be restored;  
And granted pardon to the sailors,  
So that they might be distributed aboard the surrounding ships,  
And continue in their service.  

10.  
But the non-commissioned officers,  
At the war council, as desired,  
Had to appear as required,  
To show them in all honor,  
With respect,  
Why they committed this bold behavior:  
As if they undertook it  
And came to do so.  

11.  
Not even twenty-four hours were given  
For their deliberation;  
To then willingly,  
Without resistance,  
Obey their command,  
Without any further delay,  
To conduct themselves accordingly,  
Either as pleased.  

12.  
By harsh means, however severe,  
To compel them for their sin;  
So that in time to avoid danger,  
They might not be surrounded,  
They had to be inclined to  
Give their lawful sovereign  
The reason why  
They behaved thus.  

13.  
They sent in response  
By a letter to be known,  
That everything would be as required,  
Forgiven and forgotten;  
But that they might again have:  
Their officers, as requested,  
Aboard, to remain faithful in service.  

14.  
Whereupon a lord vice-admiral,  
Answered them again,  
That they disturbed neither themselves  
Nor any story however named,  
And accepted no conditions,  
But that the wise counsel  
Was to be done with His Highness;  
According to what his power commanded.  

15.  
After a little time of deliberation,  
They further resolved  
To honor the deed  
Of His Highness’s will,  
Whereupon a signal was given  
To all the ships at anchor,  
Which spread on all sides,  
By boats.  

16.  
And so together aboard,  
They came to fetch them away,  
The non-commissioned officers,  
Felt and, without hesitation,  
Were placed on various ships;  
And the people who had been pardoned,  
Given their freedom,  
The rest remained on the ship.  

17.  
Now those who are guilty stand  
To receive their punishments,  
Who rose against their sovereign  
Through their evil ways,  
Yet God who governs the universe  
Here on earth,  
Gives each reward according to their deeds,  
As can be observed here.  

18.  
The war council, shortly thereafter,  
Sentenced three of the most severe rebels  
To be slain by a bullet there!  
The corporal, boatswain, gunner,  
Were the foremost among them,  
To whom this was read,  
But praised for their mercy.  

19.  
By His Highness’s humanity;  
This matter was alleviated.  
Before the punishment at the time,  
Had been carried out on them,  
So that with thrice beating with the cane,  
And whipping, would suffice;  
Their wicked atrocities  
Boldly confessed.  

20.  
Thus the others were also  
Punished for misdeeds,  
Keep God in mind and in peace,  
When He in His mercy,  
Sees all this to each one,  
To not fall into weeping  
From punishments in judgment,  
Before which no one can acquit themselves.

label: work_sea
translation:
1.  
O God, who governs the entire universe,  
Through whose terrible power  
The sea is turned to its wildness,  
Raises its waves,  
And what gave our land its appearance  
Turns into a swelling grave  
For thousands of souls  
Who fell into its abyss.  

2.  
You showed your Almighty power very fiercely  
Also in these days.  
Briefly, one sees a disturbed sky  
Carrying its water vortices,  
There the wind bursts with terrible sound  
At your awe-inspiring command,  
Shattering today  
Our Dutch ship the Vreede.  

3.  
At Sorlings it struck the shore,  
Many goods, rigging, and barrels,  
Driven by the untamed sea,  
Had to be left drifting there,  
Or washed ashore, reached land,  
A young woman completely naked,  
Who can describe that disaster,  
When one first saw it drift.  

4.  
Not long after, the dreadful scene  
Was seen to grow again.  
The wild sea became a greater part,  
The ship was smashed to pieces  
Where all help and courage were lacking,  
Drifting like a completely shattered wreck,  
Countless corpses were seen,  
Nothing could avoid that storm.  

5.  
Up to one hundred and twenty in number,  
Those first discovered,  
Tossed ashore by the flood,  
Which caused new fear.  
Eight women were carried on land,  
In hope of their survival.  
Then, by the dreadful fall  
Of the mast, they all drowned.  

6.  
At Folkstone or around there  
Three dear children were found,  
Still unaware of their fate,  
Nothing could prevent this misfortune.  
A tender child, eight months old,  
Another sucked at mother’s breast,  
Were seen to drift there lifeless,  
Nothing could be saved.  

7.  
No more than eighteen were saved,  
A quarter of a hundred souls  
Crushed by waves into the sea,  
Who fell victim to it.  
The ship completely trampled,  
The cargo turned to the ground,  
By storm and icy fury  
Swallowed in its floods.  

8.  
The captain’s little son  
Had to be part of this sad event  
In his early days,  
And was honorably carried  
To the silent and devouring grave.  
Death cut off the youthful blossom,  
And renews at the mother’s heart  
All the pain once more.  

9.  
How many mothers cry and sigh  
For the child given to them,  
Expect the news, so dreadful:  
“Your son has drowned with him!”  
How many households in mourning!  
The husband, given by God in true marriage  
For help and comfort  
Lost his life hereby.  

10.  
Who does not mourn the mournful cries  
That filled air and sea,  
Who can endure  
The great distress call here  
In heart and ears.  
What pain befalls you through the Highest’s hand,  
O dear and beloved Fatherland!  
And makes you wear  
The mourning garment again these days.  

11.  
Yes, Dutch people! kneel humbly down,  
Worship God’s great power.  
The Almighty of the Lord Most High  
Brings down, lifts up.  
O crew, learn from this report  
Also at all times your dear duty  
To live according to God’s law,  
Give Him the glory alone.  

12.  
Protect, O God! us at all times,  
Make the wide sea and waves,  
Prepared for blessing and for punishment,  
Be navigated safely.  
Restrict the terrible weather and wind,  
That they do not devour the keels,  
But bring to Holland’s shores  
From rich and distant lands.

label: work_sea
translation:
Have you not heard of the Dutch sailors,  
Would you then like to rest awhile with my song;  
We are boys as quick and as fast as an eel,  
Not rich in money and the purse somewhat lean,  
But open and honest and competent at hand,  
And loyal to the King and the Fatherland.  

When we go ashore to take a stroll,  
We know well how to dress up;  
We always behave as is proper,  
And have the highest standing with the girls,  
Very neatly dressed in sailor’s attire  
With the uniform of the Fatherland.  
A blue linen jacket with anchor buttons,  
A shirt, as open as possible on the chest,  
A blue collar, and a loose tie,  
A shiny shoe as if freshly polished,  
A hat with a ribbon and a broad brim  
With the name of the Navy of the Fatherland.  

Trousers on the hips with wide legs,  
A knife with a belt, which we sharpen daily,  
A sock on the foot as white as snow,  
The beard and the hair like the mane of a lion,  
Thus we are in the harbor of our land,  
And cheerful in the service of our Fatherland.  

On board the ship in our duties,  
Then we must dress differently again,  
Then we wear at night in the cold climate  
A blue jacket shirt of Leiden manufacture,  
A cap on the head with a blue border  
With the colors of the King and the Fatherland.  

As long as we are in the cold regions,  
We sleep in a bunk with a woolen blanket,  
This sometimes lasts a bit too short,  
Because when reveille is sounded,  
That rocking bed  
Moves us even more than the King and the Fatherland.  
Before we were skilled sailors  
We first had to sail for quite some time,  
Because when you start on board your hands are awkward,  
And before you have learned everything there,  
You sometimes feel a loose hand  
That teaches lessons in the service of the Fatherland.  

If you want to keep the name of sailor,  
You must know all the ropes,  
You stand at the helm, then again at the lead line,  
You sit down below and then again on the yardarm,  
As quick and agile as a monkey in the rigging,  
Even quicker than the King in the Fatherland.  

You must be able to place the toggle on the ridge,  
Cast ropes on anchors and grapnels;  
Skilled with the needle when the sail is torn,  
You use the marline spike and the tar in turn;  
Row as the boom of the old man is manned,  
If that makes the King water with envy.  

Then you must still regard this as a rule,  
To keep the ship clean from top to bottom;  
Every day you hear from the front,  
Scrubbing, scraping, polishing as hard as possible  
Twice a week with water and sand,  
As can only be done in our watery land.  
Then we also have to exercise with the cannon,  
And maneuver with the rifle as soldiers;  
With saber and axe, with pistol and pike  
You must also be skillful and quick,  
So that the enemy does not overpower you,  
Who lurks against the King and the Fatherland.  

But before we know all these matters,  
You have eaten some bacon and peas,  
You are also familiar with Dutch barley,  
And when the drum is beaten,  
You bite it off with a loose tooth,  
To the well-being of the King and the Fatherland.  

If there is one among us who wants to desert the ship,  
Or go to the doctor to chew raisins,  
He is soon known among his comrades,  
Because we have a disdain for such a lame fellow;  
Because a deserter in our ranks  
Is useless for the service of the Fatherland.  

Or if there is also someone incompetent at his tasks,  
The skipper knows well how to handle him,  
Because although Miss Strictness is retired,  
That lazy idler will soon be told  
What means there are to sharpen his mind,  
To work for the benefit of the Fatherland.  
But if we then are capable in all these matters,  
Let the enemy then make war  
We are the boys of Jan de Witt.  
Everything perfectly neat and full of spirit;  
We thrash about in the old style,  
To the glory of the King and the Fatherland.  

And if sometimes there is talk of robbers,  
Then quick, we escape with the boat from the ship.  
We are there within a quarter of an hour,  
Paying them for that extortion;  
Because a robber and a thief who murders and burns,  
Is a plague for the King and the Fatherland.  

But have you conducted yourself well in all this  
Then you will receive from the King, you can trust that,  
So good, so honest, so just, and royal,  
On your chest the Royal honorary medal,  
Then we, proud of our rank,  
Return again to our dear Fatherland.  

Or has your service time then elapsed,  
Then you take a look at your family,  
And if they have been spared by heaven,  
And you have saved your pennies,  
Then you live a month like a lord on the land,  
Even better than the King in the Fatherland.  
But if then there is again room in the purse,  
Then service is taken again.  
When you are done and old, and can do no more,  
Then the King gives you a good pension,  
With a letter of dismissal, with a weapon and a band,  
In thanks for services to the Fatherland.  

j.o.h. arntzenius.

label: work_soldier
translation:
Come friends! Stay a little while,  
I will tell you a strange case,  
How love, through wrongdoing,  
Must descend early into the dark grave.  
Yes friends! Listen to this song,  
And engrave it in your dear children's hearts,  
If you offer love no resistance,  
It often becomes the cause of much suffering. (repeat)  

A farmer lived in Brabant,  
Had fields and cattle and servants and maidens,  
And also a son, a precious possession,  
Who was going to express his love,  
To a young and fresh maidservant,  
He swore to love her forever,  
If only he could win her love,  
To conquer her for his young heart. (repeat)  

He was barely eighteen years old,  
He had to fulfill his duties,  
To go to the draft without fault,  
To avoid any grief,  
He drew a number, but very low,  
Returned sadly to his parents,  
And spoke to this tender maiden:  
Oh love your lover forever tenderly. (repeat)  

The hour of his departure was there,  
That he had to march to the garrison,  
Everyone who cared for him,  
Oh how these bonds tore me apart,  
How hard leaving had to wound him,  
His eyes were full of tears,  
He also said: my dear possessions!  
May God be with you all. (repeat)  

Shortly after, she wrote him a letter,  
And revealed her misfortune;  
Oh come soon beloved,  
You will hardly recognize me,  
Sorrow has eaten through my heart,  
A young son was born to me;  
So wrote this tender maiden,  
To hear of my word of faithfulness. (repeat)  

He read the letter repeatedly,  
And resolved to ask for leave,  
And spoke to his officer,  
For a pass of 14 days.  
He showed the letter to his captain,  
What sorrow his heart had to bear,  
Oh no, said he, that cannot be,  
Wait, your turn will also come. (repeat)  

The young man now became desperate,  
And resolved to desert,  
Come what may,  
I want to honor my beloved,  
Posted at the powder magazine,  
He laid down his rifle and weapons,  
He deserted in the dead of night,  
Ha! thought he, I will soon see my loved one again. (repeat)  

He deserted until dawn,  
When two gendarmes met him,  
One said, show me your pass immediately,  
The young man stood embarrassed.  
They tied him with a cord,  
To transport him to his corps,  
They brought him straight to prison,  
To teach him a lesson for his audacity. (repeat)  

The court-martial was convened immediately,  
And he was sentenced to death,  
His parents undertook the journey,  
To obtain clemency for him,  
His girl, crazed with despair,  
Fell down on her knees there,  
She crawled to the general's feet,  
And sobbed: give me back my lover. (repeat)  

He is the father of my child,  
Show him mercy, have pity on me!  
He is his parents' only child,  
Oh! Bring him back into my arms,  
But the cruel man turned her away,  
And said: no matter how you sigh and cry,  
He who leaves his post in disgrace,  
Is never shown mercy. (repeat)  

The day of punishment came quickly,  
He slowly approached his place of execution,  
A preacher who was his guide,  
Pointed him to our Lord's Prayer,  
He knelt down on the ground,  
And cried: O God! Be my Guardian,  
Be my girl's support and Lord,  
Oh! Strengthen my father and my mother. (repeat)  

The buzzing bullet pierced his heart,  
Put an end to his suffering,  
Everyone shared in his sorrow,  
Many tears were seen flowing,  
Come, youth! Listen to this song,  
And take this sad story to heart,  
If you give love no resistance,  
It early becomes the portion of much suffering. (repeat)

label: work_soldier
translation:
In Vienna, in Vienna, oh yes, that beautiful city,
Which contains thousands of little soldiers,
There was a sweet girl who loved faithfully,
She had a little soldier as her best friend.
He went to desert to see his bride,
Because he knew very well it was not allowed.
They imprisoned him, oh what great sorrow,
His sentence was passed, oh yes it was: death!

The girl became furious, she immediately ran,
And threw herself on the ground before the Captain.
Oh sir Captain, free my little soldier,
Then you will make my heart joyful and happy again,
Oh sir Captain, I will give you some money,
If at least you care about that,
Oh my misfortune is great,
Spare my little soldier from the bitter death.

Your little soldier must die, that is the law,
That is what I have fixed now,
But I will give you three riddles,
If you know them, you save his life,
If you guess them, then he is saved,
If you listen to my words,
Otherwise it is over for him,
If you don't solve them, he must die.

Riddle. Tell me, does there exist a king without land?
Where is there water without sand?
What is more than all the money on earth?
What is worth millions to everyone?
How does one travel around the earth in a day?
Now, dear girl, tell me at once,
You save his life, I am listening,
Just guess it, otherwise it is over.

She. A king on a map has no land,
Water in the eyes has no sand,
Being content is the greatest treasure on earth,
Health is worth everything to people,
Stand on the sun and you certainly get there,
Because it is surely there at its appointed time.
If this answer is to your liking.
Oh save him then, I beg you sir.
He. Tell me, is there great water without fish?
Tell me what is worse than death?
Tell me who is the greatest power in every case?
Who forces itself everywhere and into everything?
Where is nothing that everyone must always have?
Be careful and guess this well again,
Oh German girl, his life depends on it,
Otherwise he undergoes his deserved punishment.

She. In the rain barrel one finds no fish.
An evil woman who is worse than death,
God is the power, which does not please everyone,
The little fly forces itself everywhere.
One must have patience as long as one lives,
No one can exist without it.
My answer is now certainly right with that,
Oh sir captain! let my little soldier free.

He. Where is a mirror, girl, without glass?
What is most useful in the world?
Where is the key that fits all locks?
What can’t be grasped, even when you try hard?
What does man not lose before his death?
Guess these now, they are very great,
If you know them, he is free.
So be well and be joyful and happy.

She. The mirror of the heart has no glass,
Tolerance is the most useful,
Money certainly opens all locks,
The mist, I believe, cannot be grasped.
Hope leaves no one as long as he lives,
Now your word of honor that you give me,
Oh sir! I have now done my duty,
Let me now happily leave you.

Both. Well then, he has mercy, he is free.
Make yourself happy, joyful and glad;
We will now marry together,
And celebrate a happy wedding,
That is according to our wish and desire,
As a reward for our tender love,
Thus a clever girl saves her lover from death,
Is such love not truly great?

label: work_soldier
translation:
Hop, hop, Wilekin, hop, Wilekin,
England is my woe.
When they wanted to go to Brussels,
and the gentlemen came out,
they found my lord Sir Jacob
shot through his body.
He said: ‘My dear companions,
I must leave the country
and go to foreign lands;
my Ghent has betrayed me.

My Ghent, that gave me birth,
my Flanders for which I fought,
and that I have freed
from enemy evil and harm.
Be grieved
for Christ’s honor!
For God the Lord,
let those sins be at all times!
Strike hard,
with pitch they extinguish it.
That’s how we live, if we have heart.
Help us, Mary Queen,
so that we may earn your child’s favor.
Jesus Christ was captured,
hung on a cross;
that cross was red with blood.
We mourn His suffering and His death.
‘Sinner, why do you want to praise me?
Three nails and a crown of thorns,
that cross, the spear and the wound,
sinner, I lay that upon you.
What will you now suffer because of me?’
So we cry, Lord, with loud voices:
give us the reward for our service;
protect us from hell’s need;
we pray to you because of your death.
By God, we offer our blood;
that is for us the good of sins.
Mary, mother queen,
through the love of your living child,
all our distress we have lamented to you;
so help us, mother, pure maiden.
The earth trembles, oh the stones split.
Dear hearts, you shall weep.
We weep tears with our eyes
and have such good praise
with our minds and with hearts.
Christ has laid upon us many pains.
Now grieve you sorely
for Christ’s honor;
now God lets sins go.
So may God have mercy on us.
Mary stood in great distress,
when she saw her dear child die
and a sword pierce through her soul.
Sinner, that may be your sorrow.
In a short time
God is angry.
Jesus was anointed with gall;
therefore we fall on a cross;
raise yourselves with your arms,
so that God may have mercy on us.
Jesus, through your three names,
now make us here free from sin.
Jesus, through your red wounds,
save us from eternal death.
That He send His Spirit
and give us the corporal life.
The woman and the man to break off their evil;
God Himself wants to avenge that.
Nails, pitch, and also the gall,
that the devil tastes in all these:
therefore they are the devil’s mockery;
therefore protect us, Lord God.
He who is pure life;
God Himself has given us that.
I advise also women and men:
guard yourselves for God;
therefore pray, you poor soul.
Through God now let guard be more,
through God now let guard go.
So may God have mercy on us.
Christ reigns in heavenly kingdom
over all His angels alike:
‘Christianity wants to escape me;
therefore I want also to send it ahead.’
Mary prayed to her child so fervently:
‘Dear child, let them repent;
that is what I will create, that they must
convert themselves;
therefore I pray to you.’ -
You swear lies,
you swear my oath,
you confess pure and confess the sins,
so God will forgive you again.
Oh woe, you poor awakener,
who fiddles crooked and light on a point;
that sinks you to hell’s bottom.
You murderers and your street robbers,
you are without mercy for the living God;
you do not want to show mercy over yourselves;
therefore you are forever lost.
Had not this message come,
Christianity would have been entirely lost;
the lead devil had bound it.
Mary had loosened our bond.
Sinner, I say you dear more:
Saint Peter is the doorkeeper;
turn to him, he lets you in;
he brings you before the Queen.
Dear Lord Saint Michael,
you are a advocate of every soul;
protect us from hell’s need;
that at your Creator’s death.

label: work
translation:
I have described the worldly cares here,
How everyone strives after money and goods,
As if one had to live for a hundred years,
Which, when one dies, alas, must all remain here.
Everyone sees worries, always for tomorrow,
No one lives here without having some worry.

One worries and strives for high status,
Another to be beautiful and rich,
The third wants to leave worldly goods,
The fourth to have much showiness,
Others strive for great splendor,
Some for a woman who is beautiful and faithful.

There are many who worry about being rich,
Or strive after a beautiful creature,
All those devout try to read,
Others to be a young lady or gentleman,
A farmer for animals, tanners for leathers,
A weaver cool, after the loom and shuttle.

The tailors worry about sewing,
The barbers about having a patient,
The farmer about sowing his land,
The painters about working diligently,
Brewers for brewing, daughters for marrying,
Bakers for bread, to bake in times of need.

Lawyers to deceive the farmers,
Therefore the wise advocate also worries,
Singers sometimes to lie a little,
To make ends meet the soldier worries,
The smiths for forging, commanders for cities,
The innkeeper, to sell wine and beer.

The drunkards always worry about drinking
And the priests about sobriety,
The waiters to pour full glasses,
The nuns about their salvation,
Printers for printing, pickers for picking,
A skipper’s child worries about a good wind.

The millers worry about grinding,
Carpenters to make a good work,
Porters to carry the merchant’s goods,
Pastors and sextons worry about the church,
Writers for writing, men for women,
Hunters for game, and are almost never satisfied.

Fishermen worry about catching fish,
The beggars to get their food,
A thief steals until he is hanged,
Smooth-tongued flatterers to grease their chests,
The smock brothers, care for the prostitutes,
A kettle maker, after a good purse heavy.

The gardeners to sow and plant,
The shipbuilders make their ships ready,
The shopkeepers care for their customers,
The privateers to prey on the sea,
Sawyers for sawing, tormentors for tormenting,
Always worrying, everyone for their profit.

Barbers worry about selling,
Hat makers to make a hat!
Judges to hang the thieves,
And lovers for their sweetest beloved,
The cook for cooking, ghost hunters for ghosts,
Coopers for wine, people’s medicine.

The masons worry about masonry,
Chair turners to make a chair,
Players for cards or for playing ball,
The shooters to shoot at the target,
Glassmakers, blowers to blow,
The apothecary, for many heavy sicknesses.

Doctors to ordain good prescriptions,
To get money worries the earner,
Schoolmasters to teach the children well,
The dyers to dye beautiful color,
Peddlers for stalls, blamers for blaming,
Always temporary, but not for the kingdom of heaven.

label: work
translation:
When Father Adam dug, and Mother Eve spun,  
Where then did the Lords, or also the Nobleman come from?  
Hear Popes and Prelates, who are wondrously esteemed:  
Hear great Potentates who are of noble birth,  
Hear all people together,  
For Adam is your Father,  
And why then are the Farmers so wondrously despised?  
The Farmers and the Lords all come from Adam's seed.  

2. The first Farmer was Adam, and also the first Lord,  
What then do the Lords want if Farmers are to be more?  
Come, Farmer, give hands and fall to foot,  
For from the Farmer’s lands all our goods come,  
The Farmer must give,  
So that everyone may live:  
And if the Farmer were not busy from early morning onward,  
With digging and with spading, we would soon perish.  

3. The Pope and Cardinals with all their council,  
The Bishop, and Prelates, the Abbot with his state,  
The Priests and Plebeians, Doctors generally,  
Pastors and Chaplains, the large and small Cloisters;  
With all the Canons,  
And so many thousands of Monks,  
It all comes from the Tithes, from Rents and from the bee,  
That they all live on and maintain themselves.  

4. How could the King maintain his lands,  
And so boldly go to war against his enemies?  
How could he pay his soldiers at all times?  
The Germans and Croats also keep their hold in battle,  
The Farmer bears the burdens,  
Though sometimes he must fast,  
With tolls and excises so the Farmer is harassed;  
That they wage war, that comes from the Farmer’s money.  

5. How would great Princes, and all the Nobility,  
So merrily banquet, always with full cheer,  
With Chickens and Capons, with delicate food,  
With Wine and Lemons, and with the finest must,  
The Farmer must provide it,  
To give to my Lord,  
He must plant the Vineyard, and also press the Wine,
