And fatten the Capons, with which they are cheerful.  

6. How would the bald Young Nobleman carry out his status,  
With Leathers and with Spurs sneaking along the street?  
How could he ride all fashionable tours,  
How would he adorn maidens with pearl necklaces,  
The Farmer must bring the coins,  
Or the Young Nobleman would quarrel,  
It all comes from a small estate that he leases too dearly,  
And upon that he dominates with all this splendor.  

7. What would the Merchant manage, and also the Shopkeeper,  
How would he sell his wares all too dearly,  
How would he deceive the poor craftsman,  
With wondrous lies as often as he can:  
From where must the Farmers,  
All to the towns bring,  
Butter and Cheese, various grains,  
Of Corn, and of Oats, of Barley and Buckwheat.  

8. The Miller with his Servants would soon perish,  
If he had to be without Grain for three months at once,  
How could he dare to reach so deep into everyone’s sack?  
He would indeed have to fast that the Farmer deprived him,  
The Farmer must supply it,  
So that the Miller can grind,  
The Miller must honor the Farmer, and also the wind,  
The wind and also the Farmers they are the Miller’s friends.  

9. The Brewer would become as skinny as a stick,  
If he could not buy Barley and Malt from the Farmers:  
How could he brew that gentle and fine drink,  
By which many Men and Women so often become satisfied,  
The Brewer and the Baker  
Live off the Farmer’s Acre;  
The Baker would become very poor without the Farmer,  
He would have to sell the Oven and his Peel.  

10. How would young Children grow so quickly,  
If they could not eat the sweet milk porridge?  
How would sick people so soon be healthy again,  
For what they could wish for to the taste of their mouth,  
Fresh Eggs, or a Chicken,  
A Pea, or a Bean;  
A well-roasted Chicken, and also some small bones,  
A Finch, or a Thrush that the Farmer provides them.  

11. How could the old greybeard in the corner of the stove,  
Warm his little feet: he would perish from the cold,  
How could old Women, who are now grown old,  
Live so long in life that they are not helpless,  
And had Buckwheat cakes,  
Where shall we seek them?  
It all comes from the Farmers, the Farmer gathers the wood,  
The Farmer cuts the peat from the moors, quite a lot.  

12. With your permission you Farmers, and all Farmers indeed,  
Though I have praised you, it's only in general  
Although you are deserving of praise, although you must be esteemed,  
Be therefore not arrogant, but keep yourself without splendor,  
Humility will cling to you,  
Always live in virtue,  
Be thankful to the Creator, and praise Him at this moment,  
And also say every day once Deo gratias.

label: work
translation:
Listen, Friends and understand well,  
Hear this Song,  
Always abundant from the Fisherman,  
And also its use,  
For no City in the Netherlands  
Can do without it.  
There is scarcely a trade common,  
Large or small,  
That the Fisherman cannot see suffer,  
Few merchants praised,  
Unless goods from it  
Are found on the Ship.  
The Merchant ship brings Chalk and Grease,  
Also Pitch and Tar,  
Which the Fisherman first needs  
For his Ship without fail,  
Many a Skipper also goes away  
To fetch hemp.  
From this one makes Ropes and Cords  
Very abundantly,  
Many ships certainly sail to Spain,  
To get Salt from there,  
Wherewith he carries salt, Herring and Fish,  
Without fail,  
He addresses the Shipbuilder  
Before all men,  
Who makes his Ship masts well,  
To build for the Sea,  
Because he wants to complete his Journey  
With speed.  
The Smith generally makes Anchors,  
Builds large and small,  
The Block-maker also comes in handy,  
In various affairs,  
He must skillfully make Blocks, Pumps, and Compasses.  
The Line carriers also in the trade,  
Make many Ropes,  
And Cables for the Fisherman's Ship,  
So that it does not get caught  
In Storm and distress, on Sand or Rock,  
In these matters.  
The Sailmaker also makes  
His Sails ready;  
And the Cooper makes from split wood  
Large and small Barrels,  
In which he stores Herring and Fish in salt  
For his benefit.  
The Basket maker very pushy  
Makes from Rushes,  
Baskets, Traps, and good Holders,  
In which the Fisherman proudly  
Puts various fresh Fish,  
Skewers to keep alive.  
The Baker, Brewer, Grocer  
Know very well,  
That they have to do with the Fisherman,  
And the Shoemaker nicely  
Makes the Fisherman Clauses and Shoes,  
Worthy of praise.  
To the Cloth merchant in the season,  
He has much to do,  
Then the Tailor is addressed,  
Could I even imagine it all,  
How many a Workman  
Must earn his living.  
Few can harm the Fisherman,  
Even the Merchant,  
He buys Herring and Fish boldly,  
Sends them to various Countries,  
Then goods are obtained abundantly,  
In many ways.  
Also the Fisherman certainly brings you,  
Various kinds of Fish,  
With which many of their livelihood go,  
Skippers, Merchants, well-wishers,  
Simply the Fisherman is therefore loved  
By all People.  
He brings you the noble fine Herring,  
Cod done well,  
Halibut, Haddock, and Plaice,  
Whiting, Ling, Mackerel good,  
Also Turbot and Aberdeen,  
Abundantly.  
And still more other Fish,  
That is certain,  
The Fisherman brings you very boldly,  
To the Countryman's enjoyment,  
At his Table very abundantly,  
Good in flavor.  
Therefore do not despise the Fisherman,  
For much sorrow  
He suffers in Storms and distress,  
While the Countryman sleeps in peace,  
With his Wife and Children dear,  
As he wishes.  
See Friends this difference is great,  
Who can now earn his Bread  
Here on Land,  
Without having to sail out to Sea,  
Suffering the sorrow of Winter  
Storm and waves.  
Also he is often heavily burdened  
With a whole Journey,  
That he nevertheless enjoys no Profit,  
Yes whole Years,  
Tell me, is that not heavy sorrow,  
To sail like that.  
Mark once what gratitude you owe  
To God with diligence,  
Who lives here on Land in peace,  
Like blessed People,  
With Wife and Children to his liking  
And desire.  
Farewell Fishermen Young and Old,  
Trust in God;  
Even if you often have adversity,  
Do not therefore despair,  
You will gain Heaven's reward,  
But keep it.  
Who composed and established us this New Song,  
Was a good Fisherman from Vlaardingen,  
He was still Young in years,

The sculptor lets his claws,  
Stand still some time now as well,  
To empty the glass,  
He thinks duly  
Now I have done my work.  
The painter also goes to amuse himself,  
As summer now comes to an end,  
One glass, two, three can at least not harm her  
And anyone also buys to drink once in a while;  
With full glasses, like great masters.  
Are these not fools, that this goes on like this.  
The cooper knows no block,  
The dry-shearer no sheet.  
They all stop,  
The hat maker reckons,  
This is well ingrained.  
One already sees it swarming,  
The weaver knows no shuttle.  
It all wants to be cheerful,  
One drinks good beer and wine.  
It all runs into the hundred,  
Everyone speaks in full amazement.  
The fair is ready,  
It only comes once a year.  
Stonecutters who with heaps,  
And sawyers also with one,  
Will buy a glass well.  
Because that is its custom,  
Joy risen,  
To the very highest peak,  
There is no fear with it.  
Even if all is spent.  
The tailor must also swing,  
Though he has no shirt on his back.  
So with raging and with ranting,  
Often drunk or half drunk,  
Like boatmen’s mates know,  
This often done as well.  
Even if in the house, no coin or cross,  
Nor food to feed oneself.  
Those pleasures, are but follies.  
For those who live in joy here.  
But that running,  
And that fooling,  
Gives my day no profit at all.

label: work
translation:
What one hears and sees today,  
The very best Fools;  
Who late at night, across the street;  
With girls making faces,  
This great joy, now fully delights,  
They know no work;  
No craftsman leaves the jug,  
To strengthen the thirsty heart.  
I will now here, with much pleasure,  
Let you once hear,  
What a craftsman, mostly can,  
At this time delight,  
One who swears, another who rages,  
With curses swearing fiercely,  
And on this path I quickly tell you  
That his noble masters.  
The printers and the typesetters,  
Who are famous everywhere;  
One at the press, and the other with the letters,  
Each mastering the art of the desk,  
But they know well enough,  
Also to empty their glass once in a while,  
Because the sweet fair delights.  
Everyone’s heart is joyful.  
See the barber’s boys raging once,  
With their fair coins at hand.  
They go home and swing merrily;  
It’s as if they are grown men,  
One sees their turns,  
With girls and nags.  
Is that dirty? Is that not?  
Although they do curl hair,  
Because there are also good ones.  
Masons and carpenters,  
Do not forbid themselves this.  
Being joyful once,  
And going to strum the strings,  
All to have a glass to drink  
At this joyful fair delight.  
And the bookbinders,  
Do not like swearing at all.  
But they are excellent,  
Lovers of cleaning their throats,  
When they empty a goblet.  
That is what they are known for.  
Many grinders of diamonds are found,  
Who are coopers and like to dress up.  
With many a young girl,  
With whom they once, properly,  
Go to carouse.  
And play very gallantly as great gentlemen.  
Here also,  
Many wigmakers;  
Who weave and prepare hair,  
They also know well enough,  
To nicely clear their throat.  
With the noble grape’s juice,  
Tapped from the bagass barrel,  
They drink it full and drunk;  
The shoemakers’ journeymen shine in all,  
To run quickly from one to another hole.  
To drink under one, swing near the peat  
Like the wet one wants, the barrel is very mean.  
The smith finds the fire too hot,  
Therefore he does not want to work,  
And says I am already clothed,  
I must strengthen my heart,  
Even with a fresh Rhenish wine,  
At the fair one must be merry.  
Who could blame that,  
To stop working now.  
The bell founder by his pot,  
Does not want to be foolish now.  
But watches where he pleases,  
In the playhouse or in the stuff,  
Because it is considered mere nonsense,  
Those who now do not join,                     bis.  
Joy persists in this fair time.  
The sculptor lets his claws,  
Stand still for a while,  
To empty the glass,  
He thinks properly  
Now I have done my work.  
The painter also goes to entertain himself,  
As summer now comes to an end,  
One glass two three would at least not harm him,  
And each buys one to drink sometimes;  
With full glasses, like great masters.  
Are these not fools, when it goes like this?  
The cooper knows no block,  
The dry shearer no cloth.  
They let it all stop,  
The hat maker dances,  
This is also imprinted.  
One sees it all bustling,  
The weaver knows no reels.  
It all wants to be merry,  
They drink beer and wine bravely.  
It all runs into hundreds,  
Everyone speaks quite amazed.  
The fair is clear,  
It comes but once a year.  
Stonecutters with heaps,  
And sawyers also with one.  
Also buy a glass.  
Because that is common,  
The cheerfulness rises,  
To the highest peak,  
There is no fear with it.  
Even if everything was gone.  
The tailor must also dance,  
Even if he has no shirt at the hole.  
So with raging and with swearing,  
Often drunk or half drunk,  
Like boatmen’s journeymen strong,  
Often do this as well.  
Although there is no coin or cross in the house,  
Nor food to feed themselves.  
Those enjoyments are just foolishness,  
For those who live here in joy.  
But that running around,  
And that fooling,

label: work
translation:
Whoever ever performs his work with joy,  
My work is my delight;  
And although the days  
Slip away with the light.

People consider my position low;  
I see with different eyes:  
It's true, I am but a gardener's servant;  
I have no status nor means.

What does it matter to me what others say?  
I am content.  
I must work; I am capable of it,  
Healthy and fresh of body.  
My father was a clever man,  
He taught me everything.  
Though not rich, that was necessary.  
So I got what I desired.

He also left me free to choose  
What I wanted to begin with;  
And I chose gardening,  
To one day earn my bread.

Gardening is truly my business;  
That I dare to boast:  
Even as a boy, I already took pleasure  
In trees, plants, flowers.

How helpful is my father’s care!  
He knew that reading, arithmetic,  
Writing was necessary for me,  
But also all the drawing.  
He placed me with a master,  
Experienced in his craft:  
How swiftly time has flown,  
The time of seven years!

What have I not noticed,  
Since I came out into the world:  
I do not work as a lazy fellow works,  
Only for the money.

This brings me into the master’s favor;  
I often hear him say:  
“Gardening is an art  
Not easily explained.”

“Klaas,” he often says, “listen, Klaas:  
Take diligent care of the matters.  
I too was once a servant, now I am master;  
You can reach that too.”  
And if I have an hour free,  
I go to reading;  
And reflect with desire and diligence  
On all that might be useful to me.

Or if there is little to do,  
On Sundays and holy days,  
I sit with the master in the greenery,  
And ask him a thousand questions.

Even if I were his own child;  
Even if the man were my father,  
I would not be loved more by him:  
Oh, may no one be worse off!

It’s Klaasje before, it’s Klaasje after;  
Klaas must fix everything:  
And everything I observe,  
Usually succeeds well.  
That floral arrangement I made myself;  
Madam praised it:  
She was amazed that a servant  
Was so well educated.

She gave me a ducat as a reward;  
And pointed me to other gentlemen.  
That is a jewel on my crown!  
I will learn much more now.

How wonderfully beautiful the fruits stand!  
Oh look at those cantaloupes.  
If I might; but it is forbidden:  
The garden servant must never taste.

How lovely grows the flower crop!  
How the green soothes my eyes!  
The pond is as clear as glass,  
Swayed gently by the breeze!  
How wonderful is this morning hour;  
Hear how the birds sing:  
What blessings do You give, O Nature,  
In silent blessings!

O gracious God, through Your hand  
We are granted this all.  
You adorn the kitchen garden, orchard and land,  
With so much beauty.

There the sun rises in the east,  
Who can tell its brilliance!  
It already shines on the dune tops,  
And soon on these fields.

I once read that the Persian  
Worshiped the sun;  
This did not surprise me:  
I thought it had its reason.  
But I, since I am a Christian,  
Worship its Wise Maker;  
Whom I recognize as my God;  
I shudder at the godless.

I hear, but I do not believe,  
That there can be those:  
Who never see God in his works,  
Nor have ever praised Him.

I predict again a beautiful day,  
And on no weak grounds.  
I go now, and report to the master  
How things have stood.

That will first be his will;  
He will praise my concerns:  
But I will step once more into the flower garden;  
My Styntje loves the flowers.  
How wonderfully tender the flowers are;  
I touch her with modesty:  
I gently lay them down in the basket,  
That I brought along.

How rich, how beautiful each color;  
Oh look at those thousands of beauties:  
What a sweet scent they have;  
Those are the first anemones!

I pick lilies, I pick auriculas,  
I pick dew-wet roses;  
My beloved, now see what Klaas  
Has chosen for you.

Now cut some periwinkle,  
To bind everything together:  
And then, with pace, home again;  
There I will find my love.  
O love to whom I pay heed,  
She will not hate me, will she?  
She sometimes smiles kindly at me,  
When we talk together.

She has more money than I; that is true!  
But can that matter so much?  
If I were rich, I would, see there,  
Share all mine with her.

I know she has money and goods  
Waiting for Jan-Master’s death;  
I do not love her for abundance;  
It never crossed my thoughts.

I love her for her sweet nature;  
Oh, how lovely she is!  
What this girl is worth to me;  
And charming in my eyes!  
Her eyes shine like the light,  
That awakens me to love:  
How shy her gentle face blushes!  
What lovely traits she has!

I love all girls much;  
That suits my years.  
But I will not pair with Knier, or Aaf, or Neel,  
No matter how beautiful they are.

I will occasionally give her,  
A pretty little flower;  
But if I do not become Styntje’s man,  
Then I will live as a bachelor.

Oh why do I not dare to ask the master  
For his daughter?  
I often say to myself: “come Klaas,  
Now you must finally take the risk.  
Who knows what will yet happen?  
You will never get her by staying silent;”  
But if he says: “I won’t give her to you;  
She may get others.”

Then what to do? Then day and counsel.  
I will perhaps study him!  
He does not expect high status;  
I have nothing to fear from that.

And if the girl becomes my bride...  
He will give her to me...  
Now the final word will be spoken.  
But oh, how I will tremble!

I wish I were already with him!  
How shall I best begin?  
If it does not fit, I will make it fit;  
Love sharpens the senses.

label: work
translation:
1.
Friends come all together,
And listen to my song:
I have no reason to be ashamed,
That I am going to sing this:
It is how I sharpen scissors and knives,
For all people and cares,
Tiereliere, Tierela,
My stone turns left and right,
By stirring all of my leg.

2.
As long as my stone can sharpen,
So I earn pennies and shillings;
I hear no children whine,
From hunger in my house:
They have no discomfort,
Always going with the beggar's bag,
Tiereliere, etc.

3.
I take it all with thanks,
That comes here by chance,
Even if it is just a penny,
That I will earn:
Two pennies for a rusty knife,
An elle* is also a penny,
Tiereliere, etc.

(*an elle is a Dutch unit of length)

4.
Let the weavers keep weaving,
Until they become weak,
It is a lot for those who can give,
Twelve stuivers to his wife;
I earn with my grinding stone,
Often in just one hour,
Tiereliere, etc.

5.
The smith must work hard,
Continuously at the fire,
He may not strengthen it,
With a pot of beer;
Because of their small gain,
But I often play in there,
Tiereliere, etc.

6.
The miller who winnows much,
But receives it with his shovel,
The baker can also steal,
Because he also keeps a pinch:
But I with my grinding stone said,
Earn money always in fairness,
Tiereliere, etc.

7.
The tailor makes a show,
Already for ten stuivers a day,
When winter is at the door,
Then he almost cracks,
I do not fear the winter,
Provided everyone offers me something to sharpen,
Tiereliere, etc.

8.
The shoemakers storm,
Already for a small gain,
Beat on their awl and lasts,
They still have the curls in it;
Must I from the beginning of the week,
With my wagon on the route,
Tiereliere, etc.

9.
I know there is still a pair of scissors,
That must be sharpened,
At the house of my strings,
That is already profit for me;
Then I go home with pleasure,
And go drink a jug of beer,
Tiereliere, etc.

10.
Ship carpenters boys,
Your work now goes well,
Through the swinging and little sleeping,
You make many a strong man;
Although I also kiss the girl,
My stone still turns on its time,
Tiereliere, etc.

11.
House carpenters belong,
Under St. Joseph’s Guild,
Your work can please much,
But you would not have wished;
That you could sharpen scissors,
I know that you never walked empty,
Tiereliere, etc.

12.
The masons, you know,
They lay much on the roof,
The foreman looks through a toy,
But then Jan gets the sack:
Yet I look no one in the eye;
I am my own boss and guardian,
Tiereliere, etc.

13.
The printers' loose spirits,
They print all that is much,
Most of what they earn,
They drink it down their throat;
Because many wear patch upon patch,
Where I am always equally skilled,
Tiereliere, etc.

14.
Surgeons are seen walking,
Scraping many beards,
They could buy no bread,
If I were not there,
Because I must sharpen their fine knives,
Lancets big and small,
Tiereliere, etc.

15.
For do you think of the hairdressers,
They seem great gentlemen,
But if you don't count the show,
It is all for the bread:
They cut the ladies nicely in the hair,
But mostly decency makes the scissors,
Tiereliere, etc.

16.
The butchers great balls,
When they are in the hall,
Most they may fool around,
But that is all;
Because I do not sharpen their knives finely,
How would they then get the beast small?
Tiereliere, etc.

17.
The seamstresses go out sewing,
And that for small money,
To make jackets, cabayes*,
Or otherwise something fixed;
But if I did not sharpen the scissors sharp,
They would never be ready among the people,
Tiereliere, etc.

(*cabaye is probably a type of garment)

18.
I also sharpen my little knife,
Already with a dear girl,
Who likes a little chat,
But only in silence;
They also like me to do it so,
And do it carefully with decency,
Tiereliere, etc.

19.
Diamond grinders light workers,
What do you think of those,
They grind diamonds,
And that also with a stone,
But the girl goes with the gain,
Where I feed my bee as I please,
Tiereliere, etc.

20.
The song singers walk,
And that from March to March,
To sell songs,
And scream like a hart*;
For small gain remain in the city,
I call scissors sharpening, then I win something,
Tiereliere, etc.

(*hart means "deer" but likely "heart" is intended here)

21.
I say for the last,
Many trades are good,
But mine is the very best,
Although I often must:
Sleep in hay and straw in the stable,
I have the livelihood for nothing,
Tiereliere, etc.

label: work
translation:
When friends come together,
And hear my little song,
I need not be ashamed,
That I go singing this,
It’s how I sharpen scissors and knives,
For all people and cares,
Tiereliere, Tierela,
My stone turns from left to right,
By the stirring of my leg.

2. As long as my stone can sharpen,
I earn coin and cross,
I don’t hear any children whimper,
From hunger in my house,
They have no hardship yet,
Though they go about with the beggar’s bag.
Tiereliere, etc.

3. I take it all in thanks,
That here comes by chance,
As long as it is a white coin,
That I will earn,
Two pennies, a rusty knife,
An ells’ measure, also a penny,
Tiereliere, etc.

4. Let the weavers weave,
Until they become weak,
It’s much for those who can give,
Twelve stivers to his wife;
I earn with my sharpening stone,
Often in just one hour,
Tiereliere, etc.

5. The smith must work hard,
Constantly at the furnace,
He must not reinforce it,
Even with a pot of beer,
Because of their small profit;
But I speak often about this,
Tiereliere, etc.

6. The miller earns a lot,
But gets it with his shovel,
The baker can also steal,
Because he also loves a trick,
But I with my sharpening stone said,
Earn money in all fairness,
Tiereliere, etc.

7. The tailor makes figures,
Already for ten stivers a day,
When winter is at the doors,
He is soon cracked,
Winter does not frighten me,
As long as everyone offers me something to sharpen,
Tiereliere, etc.

8. The shoemakers grumble,
Even for a small profit,
Beat on their awls and lasts,
Yet they still have the curls,
I must from the beginning of the week,
Begin with my cart on the street,

9. I know a little pair of scissors there,
That must be sharpened;
At the house of my strings,
That is already profit for me:
Then I go home with pleasure,
And go drink a jug of beer,
Tiereliere, etc.

10. Ship carpenters work hard,
Your work goes well now,
Through swinging and little sleep,
Thus you finish many things,
However much I also court the girl,
My stone still turns in time,
Tiereliere, etc.

11. House carpenters belong,
Under the St. Joseph’s guild,
Your work pleases all,
But you would not have wanted,
That you could go to the scissors sharpener,
I know that you never go empty-handed,
Tiereliere, etc.

12. The bricklayers, you know,
They lay a lot on the roof,
The foreman peeks through a crack,
But then Jan gets the sack,
Yet I see no one as a boss,
I am my own boss and guardian,
Tiereliere, etc.

13. The printers loose souls,
They print all that is much,
What they earn most,
They drink down their throats,
For see they carry layer upon layer,
Where I am always just as skillful,
Tiereliere, etc.

14. Surgeons are seen running,
To scrape a beard;
They cannot buy bread,
If I weren’t there,
For I must sharpen their knives fine,
Lancelets large and small,
Tiereliere, etc.

15. What do you think of the hairdressers,
Who seem great gentlemen,
But would not like to count the spin,
It’s all about bread,
They cut the ladies pretty in their hair,
But mostly the scissors make the style,
Tiereliere, etc.

16. The butchers’ big balls,
When they are in the hall,
Mostly they may fool around,
But that is all,
Because if I don’t sharpen their knives fine,
How would they get the beast small,
Tiereliere, etc.

17. The seamstresses go out to sew,
And that for little money,
To make jackets, capes,
Or other tailoring,
But if I had not sharpened their scissors,
They would never get ready among the lazy,
Tiereliere, Tierela,
From left to right my stone turns,
Tiereliere, etc.

18. I also sharpen a little knife,
Already by a dear girl,
Who likes a little drink,
But only in quietness,
They also gladly let me do this,
I do it precisely and with care,
Tiereliere, etc.

19. Diamond cutters light quanta,
What do you think of those,
They sharpen diamonds,
And that also with a stone,
But the girl goes with the profit,
Where I place my leg as I like,
Tiereliere, etc.

20. The ballad singers roam,
From market to market,
To sell songs,
And shout like a heart,
For small profit I stay in town,
I call scissor sharpening, then I earn something,
Tiereliere, etc.

21. I say for the last,
Many crafts are good,
But mine is the very best;
Although I often have to,
Sleep in hay and straw and stable,
I have food for nothing at all,
Tiereliere, etc.

label: christmas
translation:
1.  
Happy New Year!  
Pull the cat by the hair,  
Pull the dog by the tail,  
Then you will have something all year long.  

2.  
Good luck in the new year!  
Father and mother sleep together,  
And I sleep in the crib.  
May I have an ear (a small coin)?  

3.  
I wish you much luck and blessing,  
On dikes and roads,  
With curls in your hair.  
I wish you luck and blessing in the new year.  

4.  
Hello woman, hello man, hello everyone,  
I come to wish you a happy new year.  
Through thick and thin, I come walking;  
Do you have a waffle or two? I won’t sell them.  
She is a good woman, the one who gives;  
I hope she lives for the coming year.  
Good woman! Good woman!  
Don’t you have a waffle or two? I’ll put them right in my sleeve.  

5.  
It’s New Year, I stand outside here,  
I have a basket, and it won’t close;  
I have a knife, and it won’t cut;  
I have butter, but it won’t fry;  
I see a cookie and can’t buy it;  
Give me a penny, and let me go.  

6.  
New Year comes into the land;  
The pig has burned its hole,  
From behind at its gate.  
Mother, give me a small coin;  
If it’s not a small coin, then it’s a penny,  
Tomorrow the New Year will be over.  

7.  
Sweet New Year,  
The piglet has four feet,  
Four feet and one tail,  
Isn’t that worth a cookie?  
It hasn’t stood long in the stable,  
Janneef has put it down,  
Give me a cookie and let me go.  

---

2.  
Palm-, palm-Easter;  
A little rooster on a stick,  
A hole through its skirt,  
A piece of its left foot;  
Now the whole little rooster is dead.  

3.  
Palm-, palm-Easter, hey, cowrie;  
It lasts one more Sunday,  
Then we’ll go to the Easter meadow.  

4.  
Palm-, palm-Sunday,  
When the farmer lay in the water,  
I was there, and you were there,  
Wasn’t that a beautiful picture?  
Woman, woman give us an egg,  
That the black hens laid,  
Are they black or are they red?  
That’s why they don’t lay under duress.  
A, B, C, D. give us a clerk along,  
Bake us a pancake,  
Give us eggs and enough money.  
Woman, woman at last,  
Give us the laid eggs,  
Laid and given,  
Let us stay a little longer.  
Here is still a good woman,  
Who will still give us some.  
How long will she give!  
How long will she live!  
The first year a penny,  
The second year a shilling,  
The third year a pound of cake,  
Give us eggs and enough money.  
Reach deeper into the nest,  
The black hens lay best,  
The red (notwithstanding),  
Reach a little deeper in,  
Master and wife stay healthy.  

---

11.  
Saint Nicholas Nikkel,  
Three pounds of speckled (something),  
Three pounds of bacon,  
Saint Nicholas is already crazy.  
The cows carry horns,  
The churches carry towers,  
The towers carry bells,  
Pretty girls wear skirts,  
The boys wear trousers,  
They crawl into all corners.  
Here we come to a rich man’s door,  
There hangs a bag with bran before,  
So many brans, so many pennies,  
Here hangs the good angel out.  

10.  
Chicken, cap, bullet,  
Saint Martin’s bird  
Flew over the dike, flew over the dam,  
A ship came with apples on it.  
Sweet, sour, can last longest.  
Here lives a rich man,  
Who can give us something,  
He will give a lot, live long,  
Blessed will he die,  
Inheriting the kingdom.  
Give something too, an apple or a pear.  
Come not again in the coming year.  
Above lives the rich man,  
Who can give us some too.  
Rich man in pari,  
Our dear Lord  
He lets grow  
Good grain and good bottles,  
Good grain and good flaxseed.  
Dried, isn’t that good household stuff?

Mother, give me a little ear;  
If it’s not an ear, then it’s a coin,  
Tomorrow the little New Year is gone.

7.  
Little sweet New Year,  
The piglet has four feet,  
Four feet and one tail,  
Isn’t that worth a cookie?

Janneef has killed it,  
Give me a cookie and let me go.

2.  
Palm, palm Easter;

A piece of his left foot;

3.  
Palm, palm Easter, hey, little cow;  
It will still be one Sunday,  
Then we go to the Easter meadow.

4.  
Palm, palm Sunday,  
When the farmer was in the water,  
I was there, and you were there,  
Wasn’t that a nice picture?  
Woman, woman, give us an egg,  
That the black hens laid,

A, B, C, D. give us a clerk along,  
Bake us a pancake,  
Give us eggs and enough money.  
Woman, woman at last,  
Give us the eggs well laid,  
Laid and counted,  
Let us still stay a while.  
Here is still a good woman,  

Who will still give us something.  
How long will she give!  
How long will she live!  
The first year a penny,  
The second year a shilling,  
The third year a pound of cookies,  
Give us eggs and enough money.  
Dig a little deeper into the nest,  
The black hens lay best,  
The red ones nonetheless,  
Dig a little deeper in,  
Master and wife remain healthy.

11.  
Saint Nicholas Nikkel,  
Three pounds of sprinkles,  
Three pounds of bacon,  
Saint Nicholas is all too crazy.  
The cows wear horns,  
The churches have towers,  
The towers have bells,  
Pretty girls wear skirts,  
The boys wear trousers,  
They crawl into every corner.  
Here we come to a rich man’s door,  
There hangs a bag with bran before,  
So many bran, so many coins,  
Here the good Angel hangs out.

10.  
Chick, cap, bullet,  
Saint Martin’s bird  
Flew over dike, flew over dam,  
A ship with apples came along.  
Sweet, sour, can last the longest.  
Here lives a rich man,  
Who can give us something,  
He will give a lot, live long,  
Will die blessed,  
Inherit the kingdom.  
Give something too, an apple or a pear.  
Don’t come again for the coming year.  
Above lives the rich man,  
The one who can give us something.  
Rich man at pear,  
Our dear Lord  
He watches over  
Good grain and good bottles,  
Good grain and good flax seed.  
Dry, isn’t that good household goods?

Happy New Year!  
Pull the cat by the hair,  
Pull the dog at the start,  
Then you have something all year long.

Father and mother sleep together,  
And I sleep in the crib.  
May I have a little ear?

3.

With curls in your hair.  
I wish you luck and blessing in the new year.

4.

I come to wish you a good new year.  
Through thick and thin, I come walking;  
Do you have a waffle or two? I will not sell them.  
She is a good woman, who gives something;  
I wish she lives the next year still.  
Good woman! good woman!  
Do you have a waffle or two? I’ll already put them in my sleeve.

5.
